FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATES-GLOBALAcademic journals do not favour left-leaning views – StudyNathan M Greenfield19 September 2025TweetA recent study of tens of thousands of journal articles challenges the view that academic publishing is heavily biased towards certain political leanings – a key concern of the Trump administration, which has announced an investigation into theHarvard Law Reviewover what the administration claims is its race-based selection process.The findings of thestudy, “The Gatekeepers of Academia: Investigating Bias in Journal Publication Across Topics, Author Backgrounds, and Institutions” (Gatekeepers study), published last month inLearned Publishing, also challenge the notion that“faculty have to self-censorin order to successfully publish in well-known journals with strong citations (which ultimately support their case for tenure)”.In keeping with academic practice in articles reporting statistical research results, Dr Steven Zhou, who teaches psychology at Claremont McKenna College (Claremont, California), and three other researchers refer to the present American context in which academics and academic publishers are under attack by United States President Donald Trump’s supporters and the administration itself obliquely.The Gatekeepers study’s argument that “other recent controversies such as federal government influence on universities may also be changing the degree to which faculty can exercise their academic freedom in their publications” is followed by a note to a 15 April press release by FIRE (the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression, which in 2022 provided a grant for the study).Given the lead-up time in academic publishing, FIRE’s Aprilpress release– “Harvard stands firm, rejects Trump administration’s unconstitutional demands” – was likely the last one that Zhou’s team could include; nevertheless, it serves to place the study in opposition to unprecedented attacks on academic publishing and, indeed, against academics themselves by both President Donald J Trump and his supporters.During his campaign for the presidency and as president, Trump has attacked academics; just last week, in the wake of the assassination of Charlie Kirk, the presidentbranded professors“Marxist maniacs and lunatics” seeking to undermine American youth.In March, two weeks after Trump returned to the Oval Office, the American Libraries Association reported that the Department of Government Efficiency, led by billionaire and Trump supporter Elon Musk, hadcancelled 400of the US Department of Agriculture’s 2,000 journal subscriptions.Three weeks later, Trump’s son, Donald Jr, whose social media postings are a reliable guide to his father’s thinking,posted on X: “No more taxpayer money for woke publishers! European publisher @SpringerNature . . . pushed gender insanity, publishing a piece last year titled ‘Beyond the trans/cis binary’,” – “cis” denoting an individual whose sexual orientation corresponds with the sex registered at birth.In late April, following an article in theWashington Free Beacon, a conservative-leaning online publication, that claimed that theHarvard Law Review“incorporated race into nearly every stage of its article selection process”, which as a matter of policy considers ‘both substantive and DEI factors’, Secretary of Education Linda McMahon, whose department had already launched a number of investigations of Harvard and Columbia,announced onXthat the government had “launched [Civil Rights] investigations into [the]Harvard Law Review.In a press release, the Department of Education’s Acting Secretary for Civil Rights Craig Trainor said, “Harvard Law Review’s article selection process appears to pick winners and losers on the basis of race, employing a spoils system in which the race of the legal scholar is as, if not more, important than the merit of the submission.”By way of example of the journal’s violation of civil rights regulations, Trainor pointed to areport by theWashington Free Beaconabout an exchange between editors on Slack in which one “suggested ‘that a piece should be subject to expedited review because the author was a minority’.”Jeff Neal, spokesperson for theHarvard Law Review, toldThe Crimson, Harvard’s venerable student newspaper that the Harvard journal “is committed to ensuring that the programmes and activities it oversees are in compliance with all applicable laws and to investigating any credibly alleged violations”.Neal also noted that in 2018 a federal district court dismissed a case that alleged that the law review discriminated in its selection of articles and personnel.Then, in June, the Trump administrationcancelled US$25 million in subscriptionsfrom SpringerNature, the publisher of 290 peer-reviewed journals, includingNatureandScientific American.Proof of a robust systemTogether with Zhou, Amy Lebrecht, an assessment planner at George Mason University (GMU, Fairfax, Virginia), Pawinee Pithayarungsarit, a graduate student at GMU, and Connor Monke, an undergraduate at GMU, examined the abstracts of 20,200 journal articles published between 2017 and 2022 for controversial topics.Among these topics are free speech, labour unions, universal health care, gun control, social media and mental health, diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) training, and the gender wage gap. More than one-third of the authors were based on American campuses.To produce the data used to train ChatGPT’s 3.5 turbo framework, a team of coders examined 1% (237) randomly selected articles across the 12 controversial topics drawn from the National Speech and Debate Association’s list of debate topics. The abstracts were rated on a scale of 1 (strong liberal bias) to 5 (strong conservative bias).“A rating of 3 implied the article was neutral or presented both sides of the issue. For example, on the topic of labour unions, the classical liberal stance in the US leans in favour of labour unions, whereas the classical conservative stance in the US leans against labour unions,” wrote Zhou.Zhou’s team found that the arithmetic mean of all the articles was 2.71, meaning that there was a slight liberal bias among them. The modal average was 2 out of 5, which also indicated a slight liberal bias. This was followed by 3 out of 5, indicating there was no bias in the articles.The score for articles on free speech was 2.97, for the gender wage gap 2.16, for labour unions 2.84 and for universal health care 2.57.“Only 1.8% of the articles were rated as having an extreme liberal bias (1 out of 5), and only 0.9% of the articles were rated as having [an] extreme conservative bias (5 out of 5),” reports the Gatekeepers study.Perhaps not surprisingly, articles that were rated as most liberal, with a 1.9 rating, concerned DEI training. Those rated most conservative, with a rating of 3.79, were about social media and mental health.The weak predictive link between an author’s personal metrics and the political tilt of their articles indicates that authors did not, on the whole, self-censor.According to Zhou, “political leaning was not strongly related to key article metrics such as citations, journal impact factor, or characteristics of the lead author’s institution nor educational background … These findings challenge the notion that academic publishing is heavily biased toward certain political leanings and that faculty have to self-censor to successfully publish in well-known journals with strong citations (which ultimately support their case for tenure).”The Gatekeepers study’s findings also “underscore the robustness of the peer review system and its commitment to uphold the principles of academic integrity and objectivity,” writes Zhou.Journals under attackThe study by Zhou,et alstands, therefore, in sharp contrast to assertions made by Secretary of Health and Human Services Robert F Kennedy Jr in a podcast last May in which he announced plans to bar government scientists from publishing in the world’s most respected medical journals, including the more than two-century-old British journal,The Lancet– and to set up in-house journals to be edited by government employees.“We are probably going to stop publishing inThe Lancet, The New England Journal of Medicine (NEJM), theJournal of the American Medical Association (JAMA)and those other journals because they are all corrupt,” Kennedy said onThe Ultimate Humanon 27 May.The secretary went on to say that the as-yet-not-launched in-house journals will “become the preeminent journals, because if you get National Institutes of Health (NIH) funding[,] it is anointing you as a good legitimate scientist”. Left unsaid on this podcast is the fact that as of the end of May, he had presided over almostUS$2 billion in cutsto the NIH and that thousands of scientists and other employees had been fired.Kennedy’s criticism centres on 5% of 522 citations in which he claims that researchers were biased because the studies were funded by the sugar industry.Kennedy’s critics pounced on the inconsistency of the secretary calling the world’s most respected medical journals corrupt and the fact that this “Make America Healthy Again”cites the very medical journals he attacked.“Anyone with experience and knowledge about medicine and public health knows thatJAMA,The Lancet, andNEJMare highly reputable journals that publish high-quality science under rigorous peer review,” Lawrence Gostin, JD, of Georgetown Law School in Washington, DC, and director of the WHO Collaborating Center for National and Global Health Law, toldMedPage Today."Criticism of these journals is part and parcel of a broad campaign to undermine public trust in science and evidence,” added Gostin.Shortly after Kennedy attackedNEJMand the other medical journals, a spokesperson for theNEJM,told the online political newsletter,The Hill, that the journal has “an impeccable record of scientific rigour and independence”.The spokesperson for theNEJM, which, having been founded in 1812, was America’s first medical journal, further said: “We use rigorous peer review and editorial processes to ensure the objective and reliability of the research we publish.”In early August, Kennedy set his sights outside the US when he tried to jawbone the highly-cited journal,Annals of Internal Medicine (AIM), into withdrawing astudy of more than 1.2 million two-year-oldsliving in Denmark that “did not find evidence supporting an increased risk for autoimmune, atopic or allergic, or neurodevelopmental disorders associated with early childhood exposure to aluminum-adsorbed vaccines”.For more than a century, some vaccines have contained very small amounts of aluminium, which boosts the immune response and improves the vaccine’s efficiency.Kennedy, a long-time vaccine sceptic,criticised the studyfor, among other reasons, excluding children who died before the age of two, which, he asserted (without evidence), were “the very children most likely to reveal injuries associated with higher exposures to aluminium adjuvants in childhood vaccines”.The editor-in-chief ofAIM, Christine Laine, who is a professor of medicine at the Kimmel Medical College at Thomas Jefferson University (Philadelphia, Pennsylvania),refused to withdraw the studyand said in a comment onAIM’s webpage, “retraction is warranted only when serious errors invalidate findings or there is documented scientific misconduct, neither of which occurred here.”Word of the yearIn 2022,Voguemagazine chose “gatekeeping” as one of its three “Words of the Year” (the other two being “Nepo baby” and “gaslighting”). From its use onTikTok,Voguedefines gatekeeping as a pejorative: “anyone who withholds information or access”.In his executive order on college accreditation, Trump decried accreditors as “gatekeepers that decide which colleges and universities American students can spend the more than US$100 billion in Federal student loans and Pell Grants dispersed each year” who have “abused their enormous authority” by requiring “unlawfully discriminatory practices” such as accessing Federal aid through diversity, equity, and inclusion-based standards of accreditation.In Canada, conservative leader of the opposition in the federal parliament Pierre Poilievre has been using “gatekeepers” as an invective for several years.In his successful race (February to September 2022) to become the Conservative Party leader, 84% of his 139 tweets used the term “gatekeeper,” usually aimed at the governing Liberal Party, then led by Prime Minister Justin Trudeau.In the following year he used the term in more than 43% of his tweets – again accusing the governing Liberals of being gatekeepers.“Fire the gatekeepers” was a leitmotif in his stump speeches during the federal election last spring that he lost to Liberal Prime Minister Mark Carney.“A policy document on the Conservative Party website that blames the federal government’s immigration rules for Canada’s perennial doctor and nursing shortage ends with the pejorative: “It’s time to remove the gatekeepers, fix our broken healthcare system and bring home doctors and nurses.”Against this background, the title of Zhouet al’s study lays down a marker about the strength and integrity of academic publishing – and, thus, goes some way towards undercutting the myth of “gatekeepers” in the academic publishing enterprise.As Angel Calderon, director of strategic insights at Australia’s largest university, RMIT University (Melbourne), toldUniversity World News, the study “highlights the importance for editors and reviewers to assess the relevance and significance of papers on their merit and content relevance, and not to be driven by their own perceptions of which topics [their publications] should cover.”Wagdy Sawahel contributed to the research for this article.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & Open Rank Faculty PositionsBANGLADESHStudent poll upsets may signal shifts at national levelMohiuddin Alamgir19 September 2025TweetIn what is being seen as a seismic shift in the political landscape on university campuses that may herald changes at national level, student groups linked to Bangladesh’s largest Islamist political party won overwhelming victories in student union elections at two of the nation’s flagship campuses.Political scientists describe the wins at Dhaka University (DU) and Jahangirnagar University by Islami Chhatra Shibir (Shibir), the youth wing of the Islamist Jamaat-e-Islami party, which was banned by the previous government, as a dramatic reconfiguration of campus politics, with Islamist forces rising to challenge the decades-long dominance of secular and progressive student groups on these campuses.These youth elections, the first to take place in the country since a student-led uprising led to the ouster of former prime minister Sheikh Hasina in August 2024 – and in many cases, the first student elections to take place in several years – could also have far-reaching implications for Bangladesh parliamentary elections, as experts noted that campus politics, in particular at DU, is seen as an indicator of broader political trends.The country’s Election Commission has yet to announce the election date, but the interim government’s Chief Adviser, Professor Muhammad Yunus, has said the next national election will take place in the first half of February 2026.Year-long preparationDhaka University Central Student’s Union (DUCSU) elections were held on 9 September after a six-year hiatus.“Shibir has won the student union elections at Dhaka University and Jahangirnagar University, and it is being predicted that they might also win at Chittagong University and Rajshahi University. To some extent, this indicates a rise in Islamic ideology,” Dhaka University political science professor Farid Uddin Ahmed toldUniversity World News.He added the result “was a bit surprising”, as Shibir has never had such a result, noting that after victories at Dhaka University and Jahangirnagar University, if Shibir clinches the student vote at Chittagong and Rajshahi University, its successes could have a significant impact on the upcoming national elections.He also said that Shibir had been preparing for the union elections at the two universities in an organised way since the July 2024 uprising. “Their preparation lasted nearly a year, and during that time, they built a mostly positive image among students,” he noted.Elections to the Rajshahi University Central Student’s Union and Chittagong University Central Student’s Union are scheduled for 25 September and 12 October, respectively, marking the first such polls in over three decades at both institutions.According to insiders, concerns about potential campus violence and the possibility of ruling party-backed student organisations doing badly at universities like Chittagong and Rajshahi meant universities had little interest in holding student union elections.For over two decades, Chittagong and Rajshahi have been longstanding bastions of Islamic student organisations.The vice-chancellors of the respective universities serve asex officiopresidents of their student unions.Active participants in student-led uprisingThe Shibir-backed panel or group of candidates for all the union seats at DU was led by Abu Shadik Kayem, standing for the vice-president post, and SM Farhad for general secretary. The panel won 23 out of 28 central posts in the DUCSU election.Both Kayem and Farhad were active participants in the student-led mass uprising last year.Newly elected DUCSU vice-president Kayem toldUniversity World Newsthis week: “We have carried forward the aspirations of the July mass uprising. Throughout our campaign, we organised numerous programmes and stood firmly beside students, guided by the honesty, integrity, and dedication of our leadership.“Our panel members have demonstrated strong academic performance and active involvement in social initiatives. We believe that meaningful change comes through action, and we are committed to delivering results that reflect the trust students have placed in us.”After the fall of Bangladesh President General Hussain Muhammad Ershad’s government in 1990, Paribesh Parishad – a platform of DU authorities and student representatives – banned Shibir activities on campus.Jamaat-e-Islami had opposed Bangladesh’s independence from Pakistan and sided with the Pakistan army during Bangladesh’s war of independence in 1971. The party and Shibir had also been banned by Hasina’s government. But last year the interim government set up under the leadership of Nobel laureate Muhammed Yunus reversed the ban.During the 16 years of Awami League rule, incidents of assault on individuals suspected of being affiliated with Shibir were frequent in various student residential halls.Political scientist Ahmed said Shibir operated quietly on campus during the last several years due to opposition from the ruling party but managed to establish a strong presence in halls, faculties, and nearby hostels.“Compared to other panels, their activists were more skilled in organising, networking, and carrying out their plans effectively. All these [activities] helped them to win in [union] polls,” he said.Shibir-linked students began appearing openly on campus just a month after last year’s July-August uprising. Kayem and Farhad, who had previously operated on campus presenting themselves as activists for the Chhatra League linked to Hasina’s Awami League Party, publicly revealed themselves as the president and general secretary of Shibir’s DU unit.Kayem, who is also a central leader of Shibir, said he was confident about the group’s success in the student union elections at Rajshahi and Chittagong universities.Jahangirnagar University unionAt Jahangirnagar University Central Student’s Union, the Shibir-backed panel swept 20 of the 25 posts, including that of general secretary, filled by Mazharul Islam, in elections held on 11 September.According to former Jahangirnagar student Luna Noor, a former president of the left-leaning Bangladesh Chhatra Union, no Shibir-backed panel has won the student union election since the university was established in 1970.She toldUniversity World Newspanels from leftist organisations that the Chhatra League and Chhatra Dal had always prevailed in the past.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand 孟加拉國學生選舉引發政治變革的信號 近日，孟加拉國的兩所頂尖大學，達卡大學和賈漢基爾納大學的學生會選舉中，與伊斯蘭政黨有關的學生組織伊斯蘭青年團（Shibir）取得壓倒性勝利，這被視為校園政治格局的重大變化。這次選舉是自2024年學生運動推翻前總理哈西娜以來的首次選舉，專家認為，這一結果可能預示著即將到來的國會選舉的變化。新當選的學生領導人承諾將致力於改善學生的福祉，並強調他們的當選反映了學生的信任。隨著即將舉行的其他大學選舉，Shibir的成功可能會進一步改變孟加拉國的政治格局。

Open Rank Faculty PositionsDENMARKUniversity chair quits after PM attacks Bangladesh intakeJan Petter Myklebust18 September 2025TweetThe chairman of the board of Roskilde University (RUC) has stepped down from his position, and a masters programme in business administration and leadership at the institution has been shut down amid a political row over the recruitment of Bangladeshi students.Carsten Toft Boesen’s resignation comes in the wake of public criticism of the university by PM Mette Fredriksen for accepting so many students from Bangladesh, many of whom did not have the requisite qualifications to succeed in the programme,as reported byUniversity World News.“Five years ago there were nine masters degree students from Bangladesh at RUC. While last year every six new masters degree students at RUC were from Bangladesh. Every six! And they are here to study? Hardly,” she told the annual meeting of the Social Democratic Party, which had met to discuss strategy before next year’s general election.Union investigationFrederiksen said an investigation by Denmark’s largest trade union, 3F, recently found that over a few years there has been a tripling of people from Bangladesh working within the traditional 3F work sectors (hotels, restaurants, and services). “For me it is evident that this is a clear abuse of our study programme,” she said. “We, as a government, are going to put a stop to this,” Fredriksen said.In her firststatement, RUC’s Acting Chair Dea Forchhammer is reported to have said: “I agree with the prime minister that we lost control of the intake of foreign students, and that we should have done more.”She added: “Roskilde University should primarily be for young people from Zealand and Denmark, not for Bangladesh or other places.”Days earlier, on 15 September, both the Minister of Migration Kaare Dybvad Bek and the Minister of Higher Education and Science Christina Egelund faced a series of questions from the right-wing political parties during a lengthy meeting in parliament of the Committee for Foreigners and Integration, which requested information about international students in Denmark over the 2019 to 2024 period.As a result of the recent developments, RUC is now introducing new measures to curb admissions to masters programmes characterised by a high influx of students from Bangladesh.This means in concrete terms that the university will close its Masters in Business Administration and Leadership from 2026. Only students who are already enrolled in the programme and have met the legal requirements can complete it.University initiativesRoskilde University Rector Hanne Leth Andersen said in a statement the university “faces a significant challenge, which we are aware of and which we have tried to solve with various initiatives over the past three years”.“Most recently, the university closed down the winter intake, and now it is closing down Business Administration and Leadership completely. In addition, the university is initiating a study with a view to further initiatives in the education programme with a large intake of foreign self-payers,” she said in astatement.“The decision follows a discussion with the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science and is intended to ensure peace of mind and a more balanced development in the admission of international students,” Leth Andersen noted.She described the decision as “an extension of a number of initiatives that we have already implemented”, including the tightening of language requirements, the control of diplomas, and the raising of fees for programmes with high international demand.“I understand why some are questioning the situation, and it is my clear expectation that with these initiatives we will see a significantly changed admissions in the future,” Leth Andersen said.Christoffer Rosenkvist, president of the National Union of Students in Denmark, said the union agreed that RUC has faced a significant issue with the sharp increase in applicants from Bangladesh who lack either the academic level or the language skills necessary to participate in teaching on equal terms with other students – a conclusion shared by both the university and the ministry, based on the findings of an investigation commissioned by the minister in March.In a statement, Rosenkvist said: “While we do not believe that challenges related to academic and language proficiency apply to all students from Bangladesh, the fact remains that when a large number of students from a single country struggle to keep up academically and linguistically – and when many appear to have labour as the main purpose of their residence in Denmark, and not studying – we agree that action to address the issue must be taken.”However, he said the union did not believe that the solution should involve “blacklisting applicants from specific countries or dramatically increasing tuition fees”.He noted: “Instead, we propose that the admissions system for international students be reformed through necessary legislative changes and that universities be provided with the appropriate tools in the admissions process to ensure that all admitted students possess the required qualifications and skills to fully participate in their studies.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Race bias row erupts as universities reject Chinese student
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Race bias row erupts as universities reject Chinese studentGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksMALAYSIA-GLOBALMalaysia to stop sending its scholarship students to the USMALAYSIAMinistry steps up efforts to bring back overseas academicsCHINA-MALAYSIAMore students from China are choosing to study in MalaysiaFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMALAYSIARace bias row erupts as universities reject Chinese studentKalinga Seneviratne18 September 2025TweetA political controversy over the highly sensitive issue of racial discrimination has erupted in Malaysia, a multiethnic country, after a Malaysian Chinese student who sought to study accountancy was rejected by five of the country’s top universities, even though he achieved an almost perfect score in the university entrance exams.Though complaints by ethnic Chinese students of discrimination at the university entrance level are not new in Malaysia, the case gained added traction and publicity after Dr Wee Ko Siong, the president of the Malaysian Chinese Association (MCA) – a party of the ruling coalition government – held a press conference on 8 September with the 20-year-old student, Edward Wong.Wong, with Wee’s backing, raised an issue that is very sensitive in Malaysia: quotas that favourBumiputras(sons of the soil), a term that refers to the majority Muslim Malays who constitute roughly 62% of the population, while Chinese Malaysians make up about 21%, according to government statistics.Wee called the current university admissions process “flawed” and “unfair”.Wong achieved a perfect cumulative grade point average of 4.0, top marks in all subjects, and scored 9.9 out of 10 for co-curricular activities in the Malaysian Higher School Certificate (STPM), the national pre-university examination. The exam is internationally recognised and widely regarded as more academically rigorous than other pathways into public universities in the country.Despite this, five of Malaysia’s leading universities rejected his application to study accountancy. Instead, he was offered a place on a management programme at Universiti Sains Malaya in his hometown, Penang, a course of study Wong said was “neither my passion nor my dream”.The Higher Education Ministry insisted the admissions system was merit-based.In a statement released on 9 September, the ministry said in assessing candidates for admission, 90% of the assessment is based on academic achievements and 10% on co-curricular activities. The ministry added that 1,255 candidates from the STMP system had a perfect cumulative grade point average of 4.00, and Wong was among them.This was seized upon by Dr Akmal Saleh, the chief of the youth wing of the Malay nationalist party (UMNO), who said: “In the end, the truth is not that he [Wong] is not smart, but there are more who are smarter and more qualified than him”.Saleh demanded that Wee apologise to the higher education minister, an indication of the sensitivity of raising the issue of possible discrimination. But Wee refused and called upon the government to review the so-called open-channel (matriculation) system.Since the New Economic Policy (NEP) was introduced in 1971 under Article 153 of the Federal Constitution, theBumiputrapolicy has been designed to provide preferential treatment to Malays and Indigenous groups to ensure their economic empowerment, as they were marginalised during British colonial rule. The policy provides guaranteed quota placements in public universities, jobs in public institutions and business ownership.“As the system protects the interests and privileges of the majority ethnic group in the country, it is an extremely sensitive issue to be brought into discussion,” Tim (who preferred to use only his first name), an ethnic Chinese student who has just graduated with a masters degree from a foreign Asian university, toldUniversity World News.“Touching on or questioning these matters may lead to losing political support or even disharmony [in the community]. Constitutional reform on this matter is an arduous task for any ruling coalition,” he said.Two pathways to university entranceThe current debate spotlights the existence of two parallel university entrance pathways – STPM, which Wong went through, and the matriculation system, usually a one-year programme considered a fast-track to local universities and, in practice, reserved forBumiputras.“At least 80% of the places are given to matriculation graduates. So that is where the unhappiness comes from, and it is a form of institutional racism. While officially it doesn't exist, racial discrimination does exist,” explained Malaysian professor James Chin, currently the director of the Asia Institute at the University of Tasmania in Australia.“The Chinese are really unhappy because they understand the matriculation system is a backdoor entry to universities,” he toldUniversity World News.Chin noted that STPM is an 18-month programme, while matriculation takes just nine months. “If you go through the STPM path, by the time you get to university, it could be two years.”Application and admission to local universities is managed by a centralised university admission system called UPU, and the traditional quota reserves 90% of university places forBumiputrastudents and the remaining 10% for the non-Bumiputrasplaced through the matriculation system.The debate triggered by the Wong saga has brought into public scrutiny the lack of transparency in the system.“Some argue and allege that theBumiputraapplicants are given a significant advantage to enter local universities despite the government’s insistence that the system evaluates all applications based on merit before making the offers,” said Tim.“Furthermore, Article 153 provides the legal framework to safeguard the special position of theBumiputras(and it is) stipulated as the responsibilities and functions of the King which are not [able to be questioned].”Calls for a unified examAfter Wee went public and urged the Ministry of Higher Education to address the issue, former Economy Minister Rafizi Ramli also questioned the transparency of the system and advocated a restructuring of the programmes and a more equitable admission process.The University Malaya Association of New Youth (UMANY), a student organisation at the university comprising mainly Chinese students, called for a unified exam to ensure a fair comparison of all outstanding students.Responding to UMANY’s demand for the abolition of the matriculation system, University of Malaya Vice-Chancellor Professor Noor Azuan Abu Osman was quoted by Malaysian news agencyBernamaas saying in astatement: “The matriculation system is part of the government’s ongoing efforts to ensure more inclusive and equitable educational opportunities for all.”He noted that the university was committed to supporting the national education policy.However, in a veiled criticism of UMANY for raising the issue, he said UM had always supported freedom of expression among campus residents in line with the spirit of openness and intellectualism that forms the foundation of an institution of higher learning.“This freedom, however, must come with responsibility and due consideration of the sensitivity and implications of statements made, especially those involving public policy.“The government and society needed to continue to dialogue and evaluate policies such as matriculation so that educational justice can truly be achieved without eroding quality or paving the way for new injustices,” the vice-chancellor said.The National Student Consultative Council (MPPK), a student consultative body set up by the Ministry of Education to consult on student affairs, said in its own statement: “MPPK condemns any action, call or approach that may affect the wellbeing of students and cause public unrest. Every education issue must be discussed rationally and professionally and not based solely on emotions or specific interests.”International studentsWee has also suggested separately that Malaysians should be prioritised in university admissions, as international student enrolment continues to rise. He noted this applied to all Malaysians, regardless of race.The success of Malaysia’s higher education internationalisation has meant that universities in the country attract a large number of students from China. Kedah state assemblyman Dr Hain Hilman Abdullah claimed in parliament that 27% of students in Malaysia’s five top universities – the ones rejecting Wong’s application – were from overseas.“These universities are funded by taxpayers, and professors are paid by public money. Yet, 27% of the students are not Malaysians,” he told the state assembly. “We must safeguard higher education facilities for our children, while still balancing the need for internationalisation.”However, Higher Education Director General Azlinda Azman said in a statement that international students make up 21.3% at these top universities, not 27%.Chin said: “The internationalisation of Malaysian universities doesn't mean that it is more difficult for local students.“The federal Malaysian government funds a certain number of spaces, and the universities are free in terms of selling the access space to international students,” he said, adding: “Malaysia has been very successful in recruiting international students from China.”The issue of quotas and fair access relates to higher education's contribution to several United Nations Development Goals (SDGs): SDG 4: Ensure inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning opportunities for all; SDG 10: Reduce inequality within and among countries; and DG 16: Promote peaceful and inclusive societies for sustainable development, provide access to justice for all and build effective, accountable and inclusive institutions at all levels.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New documentary digs into the shadow scholar industry
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New documentary digs into the shadow scholar industryGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: Joburg Film FestivalJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksKENYA-AFRICAWhite-collar hustle a growing threat to academic integrityGLOBALChallenges to eradicating academic corruptionKENYA‘Academic writers’ set to lose lucrative global marketFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsKENYA-GLOBALNew documentary digs into the shadow scholar industryNic Mitchell17 September 2025TweetEssay mills may have beenoutlawedby some governments, including in Australia and the United Kingdom, but that has not stopped a booming industry from ‘employing’ an estimated 40,000 bright young Kenyans in the country’s capital, Nairobi, who rely on academic contract cheating to survive and pay essential bills.Recent research investigating the shadowy essay-writing industry from an African perspective, rather than from the Global North, has been turned into an award-winning documentary film.The research inThe Shadow Scholarsdocumentary comes from Patricia Kingori, a British Kenyan sociologist and the University of Oxford’s youngest black female professor in its 925-year history.Now professor of global health ethics at Oxford’s Population Health Ethox Centre, Kingori travelled to Nairobi with filmmakers to explore the exploitative global relations between students struggling academically in the Global North, who are increasingly relying on more talented students and unemployed graduates in Global South countries, such as Kenya, to ghost write their academic assignments.In the documentary, Kingori sought out and interviewed many of the brilliant young ‘contract writers’, who can turn around a masters essay overnight for little more than US$100 in a subject they may have barely studied before.Millions used essay millsShe wanted to understand their motivations and the questionable power dynamics of the essay mills which, according to one estimate, may have been used by 37 million students globally, primarily in the United States, UK and Australia.Kingori, who spent her first three years in Kenya, before moving, first, to St Kitts in the Caribbean and then to the UK, calls the contract writers ‘shadow scholars’ because they are invisible most of the time and often hide their real identities by using Virtual Private Networks (VPNs) to appear to be from the Global North.“We use white names because it convinces the cheats that we are well able to complete the task,” says one of the Kenyan ghostwriters in the film, who explains that the Western stereotype of African students and academics might put off their customers in the Global North from using the service.Kingori says: “Power makes itself invisible, so we don’t question whether things should be the way they are.“This film, based on my research, investigates the global structures that allow an industry of brilliant young Kenyans, who cannot get other work, to support others who buy their degrees rather than doing their own work.“It enrages me! This should not be why Kenya is on the map, and if the world was fair, these scholars would be able to operate on the world stage as themselves.”Degrees can be boughtThe film questions the true value of academic qualifications in a world where degrees can be bought and suggests there may be as many 50,000 highly educated Kenyans earning their living by writing academic papers for less able students in the Global North, with most of the shadow scholars based in Nairobi, which has become an ‘online labour’ hot spot.The Shadow Scholarsis currently doing a short tour of cinemas in London, Oxford and Leeds and will be screened on Channel 4 television in the UK on 24 September 2025. The names of the contract essay-writers have been changed and their identities disguised in the interviews in the film using artificial intelligence.Exploitative global relationsRachel Brooks, professor of higher education at the University of Oxford and the president of the British Sociological Association, toldUniversity World News: “The film provides appalling evidence of highly exploitative global relations.”Brooks said: “Although staff in the UK and elsewhere have been aware of the existence of essay mills for some time, the scale of the activity revealed in this film is shocking.”She suggested that “the pervasiveness of Generative AI may help bring such practices to an end – with lecturers now thinking more creatively about the assessments they set, to ensure the work students submit is their own”.However, IT experts are not so sure that AI will spell the end to the contract essays, despite acknowledging that some students have moved to using GenAI systems like ChatGPT to write their assignments.Dr Thomas Lancaster, a computer scientist and expert on contract cheating at Imperial College London, said he is aware of a new market for students who use GenAI to create a first draft, but then hire a writer to check the content and to rewrite it so that it is not detected as AI generated.Warning to Global North studentsThe film shows Lancaster warning Global North students that, once they have given their name, e-mail address and credit card details, and maybe even agreed to send copies of their ID, the essay mill owners may keep demanding payments with the threat of exposing the cheats to their university.The film also interviewed one of the Kenyan contract essay-writers who has been in touch with the International Labour Organisation to try to set up a union to represent the writers to get a better share of the money paid for the fake essays. Many of the contract writers only get a third of what clients in the Global North pay for the fake essays.Most of the Kenyans involved in the industry don’t see anything wrong with what they are doing to earn a living and say the ethical questions should be directed to students in the US, UK and Australia who are paying for these services.Kingori and the filmmakers leave audiences pondering just how many students from top universities in the West who have graduated with flying colours and are now doctors, engineers, pilots, nurses or whatever, used the contract cheating services – and how many of these ‘graduates’ may now be at the mercy of the essay mills if they stop paying.It is a dangerous game, but about 37 million students have benefited from the shadow scholars’ services, according to an estimate by Professor Phil Newton, an expert in education fraud at Swansea University in Wales, whose research is mentioned in the film.Clampdowns on advertisersEfforts to clamp down on the essay mills have included specific legislation in the United Kingdom to crack down on internet service platforms advertising contract cheating services, such as a move adopted by the last Conservative government in 2022. Pay Pal has also restricted payments to known cheating services, but these are often disguised as research centres or the like.Australia has also attempted to block access to websites offering contract cheating, but the business is still flourishing in Kenya and elsewhere.The Shadow Scholarsdocumentary has already been screened at 20 festivals, including BFI London Film Festival, where it had its world premiere in the Best Documentary Feature competition and was also awarded a Special Jury Mention in 2024. It also won Best Documentary Special Jury Mention at the Johannesburg Film Festival. It will also be screened at the Nairobi Film Festival from 16-26 October.The film was directed by Eloïse King and Sir Steve McQueen is the executive producer.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
新紀錄片揭露「影子學者」產業

一部新紀錄片《影子學者》深入探討了肯尼亞娜伊羅比的學術合同作弊產業，這一行業雇用約4萬名年輕人，幫助全球學生完成學業作業。該片由牛津大學社會學家帕特里夏·金戈里主導，揭示了全球南北之間的剝削性關係，並訪問了多位「影子學者」，探討他們的動機及其在學術界的隱秘角色。金戈里指出，這一行業使得學位的真正價值受到質疑，並引發了對學術誠信的深刻反思。目前該片已在英國的多個影展上放映，並將於9月24日在Channel 4播出。

新紀錄片揭露「影子學者」產業

一部新紀錄片《影子學者》深入探討了肯尼亞娜伊羅比的學術合同作弊產業，這一行業雇用約4萬名年輕人，幫助全球學生完成學業作業。該片由牛津大學社會學家帕特里夏·金戈里主導，揭示了全球南北之間的剝削性關係，並訪問了多位「影子學者」，探討他們的動機及其在學術界的隱秘角色。金戈里指出，這一行業使得學位的真正價值受到質疑，並引發了對學術誠信的深刻反思。目前該片已在英國的多個影展上放映，並將於9月24日在Channel 4播出。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Cap on non-local undergrads raised to 50% of student body

Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHONG KONGCap on non-local undergrads raised to 50% of student bodyYojana Sharma17 September 2025TweetHong Kong will again raise the proportion of non-local students permitted to study at its publicly funded universities – this time to 50% of the student body – according to an announcement madejust one year after the city doubled its quota of international studentsfrom 20% to 40%.The announcement to raise the cap on non-local undergraduate enrolment with effect from the 2026 to 2027 academic year came in a policy address by Hong Kong’s Chief Executive John Lee on 17 September, as part of his vision, first mooted in 2024, to turn Hong Kong into a higher education hub.He also announced policies to promote Hong Kong as a hub for innovation and technology and to strengthen industry-university research collaboration.Lee said that universities can also raise the number of self-financing places in funded postgraduate research programmes, increasing the ceiling from 100% of existing places to 120%.“Universities in Hong Kong are highly popular, with a double-digit year-on-year increase in the number of self-financing non-local applicants,” Lee said, explaining the increase in the undergraduate cap. He added that 72% of non-local undergraduate students are mainland Chinese.Hong Kong’s highly ranked universities area major drawfor mainland students and cheaper than universities in the United States and the United Kingdom.“After increasing the ratio from 20% to 40%, the academic standards of admitted students continued to rise rather than decline. A further increase to 50% is acceptable,” lawmaker Lau Chi Pang, who is also a special adviser to the president of Lingnan University, told local media just days before the announcement. He suggested further increases in the cap were possible in the future.Record applicationsHong Kong’s Secterary for Education Christine Choi said earlier this month the city’s eight publicly funded universities had the capacity to enrol more students. According to Choi, some universities had already reached the 40% threshold set for the 2024 to 2025 academic year.Hong Kong University (HKU), Hong Kong’s highest-ranked university, said it received a record 25,000 non-local applications for undergraduate programmes for the academic year beginning this month, 17 times more applications than available places. They include applications from students impacted by shifts in US education policies.“For universities, increasing the number of higher fee-paying students means more income at a time when the government hasreduced the amounts universities are allowed to hold in reserves,” an academic at the Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK) toldUniversity World News.However, some parent groups have criticised the raising of the undergraduate cap, saying it could affect thequality of teachingif the student-lecturer ratio worsens as a result.Xiang Zhang, vice-chancellor of the HKU, said key measures announced in the policy address, including raising the non-local student ratio and promotion of Hong Kong as an education hub, would further attract global talent.Nancy Ip, president of the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, while not commenting directly on the non-local cap, welcomed the focus on research and innovation in Lee’s speech. “Amid increasingly intense global competition for talent, Hong Kong must continue to enhance its academic environment and research resources to strengthen its distinctive edge,” she said in a statement.Dennis Lo Yuk-ming, vice-chancellor of CUHK, said in a statement the increases in non-local students at the undergraduate level and self-financing places at the postgraduate research level “will help attract more outstanding students to pursue their studies in Hong Kong”.Accommodation shortageBut Simon Ho, president of Hong Kong’s private Hang Seng University, was quoted by localRTHKradio as saying the plan to raise the cap was “too drastic” and advocated a more gradual approach that took into account the student intake capacity of each institution, along with improvements to support measures.He said “societal impacts” should also be considered before the change is implemented.Universities arestruggling to provide adequate accommodationfor students at reasonable prices in the high-cost, densely packed city.Lee last year gave the go-ahead for commercial buildings, including hotels, to be converted into student accommodation, with several universities recently acquiring properties under this scheme.Lee announced on Wednesday 17 September that the government will “earmark new sites, including commercial ones” to construct university hostels to increase supply.Kathy Lee, head of research at Colliers Hong Kong, a property firm which has held consultations with the government on land use to promote Hong Kong as an education hub, toldUniversity World Newsthe announcement was a major step forward, given Hong Kong’s strict zoning and land controls.“They’re expanding the scheme, allowing student accommodation projects to come from redevelopment and new development on commercial land in Hong Kong,” she said, noting the new rules would allow commercial companies to respond swiftly.“It is the first time we are seeing this in Hong Kong,” she said. “Although the government [are] not saying they are going to provide land for commercial purpose-built student accommodation, they are allowing office buildings currently under construction or planning to change their use to student accommodation.”“Previously in Hong Kong’s land-use or development scheme, there was no such thing as student accommodation, [which] they count as an ancillary facility of the university, and provided funding to the university to provide their own facilities.”In a report titledBuilding Hong Kong into an International Education Hubreleased on 11 September, Colliers Hong Kong estimated the shortfall in purpose-built student accommodation at approximately 94,000 in the ongoing 2024 to 2025 academic year and projected that the gap would widen significantly to a shortfall of around 120,000 beds by 2028.“These were conservative estimates,” Kathy Lee acknowledged, with projections to 2028 based on local student enrolments staying at current levels and non-local enrolment growth at 10% per year from 2024 onwards.“Actually, the growth [from] 2024 to 2025 was 24%,” she said. “Now, with the government increasing the [non-local] cap from 40% to 50%, growth in demand will be much greater than we projected.“The [Hong Kong] government is adopting new ideas quite quickly and is pushing out the student accommodation scheme as fast as they can because they see the market potential, especially with the geopolitical situation.”She pointed to US visa policies and statements on restricting student visas for mainland Chinese, so the government “sees that there’s a huge opportunity for Hong Kong to replace or at least become the preferred location for those students”.Definition of non-localThere has been somedisquiet from Hong Kong parentsabout rising “competition” from the mainland for places at Hong Kong’s universities, and particularly from children of parents who entered Hong Kong via the city’sTop Talent schemefor graduates of top overseas universities.Around 95% of people who come to Hong Kong on the talent scheme are from the mainland.Some Hong Kong concern groups have lobbied the government, saying the talent scheme was abused by parents who did not necessarily want to work in Hong Kong but wanted their children to qualify for the local student quota at Hong Kong’s universities, which also qualified them for cheaper fees for locals set at around HK$44,500 (US$5,700) compared to three times as much for international students.After an outcry earlier this year, Hong Kong’s Education Bureau announced in late July that children of non-locals will be required to have been resident in Hong Kong for a minimum of two years before they become eligible to enrol as local students.Thenew requirementwill take effect from the 2027 to 2028 academic year, with a transitional period in the first year requiring only one year of residency to be eligible.Schools offering HK qualificationsThe number of schools and private tutoring companies in mainland China offering the Hong Kong school-leaving exam known as the Diploma of Secondary Education, or DSE, has mushroomed in the past two years as a way to secure admission to Hong Kong institutions.In his policy address on Wednesday, John Lee announced the government would work to widen global recognition of the DSE while stressing that “measures will be taken to crack down on unauthorised provision” at so-called “shell schools”, or unauthorised, unregulated schools that work with agencies to help non-locals become eligible for subsidised university places.A private Hong Kong secondary school recently had its registration revoked by the authorities for allegedly offering “Hong Kong student status” to students who had never studied in Hong Kong, allowing them to take the DSE exam in the mainland city of Shenzhen across the border from Hong Kong.Other schools have reportedly offered DSE courses for students as far away as Beijing.The DSE has been reformed in recent years, including the scrapping of “Listening and Comprehensive Skills” and “Speaking Skills” papers in Cantonese – the Chinese dialect in use in Hong Kong – making it easier for non-Cantonese speakers on the mainland, who speak Mandarin, to pass the exam.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 香港將非本地本科生名額上限提高至50%。根據香港特首李家超於2025年9月17日的政策發表，這一新政策將於2026至2027學年生效，旨在將香港打造成高等教育中心。此舉緊接著去年將國際學生名額從20%提高至40%。香港的八所公立大學已具備接納更多學生的能力，目前已收到創紀錄的2.5萬份非本地本科申請。儘管學校對增加非本地學生持正面看法，部分家長則擔心這可能影響教學質量。政府也計畫增加學生住宿設施，以應對日益嚴重的住宿短缺問題。
