https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Commonwealth universities chart waters of global change
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Commonwealth universities chart waters of global changeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsProfessor Colin Riordan, secretary general of the Association of Commonwealth Universities. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALAmerican isolationism brings HE opportunities elsewhereGLOBALCommonwealth has a key role in mitigating HE funding crisisGLOBALACU launches toolkit for equitable research partnershipsGLOBALStark inequalities across Commonwealth research ecosystemsGLOBALHow to help researchers make the connections that matterAFRICADigital education should be a sustainable development goalUNITED KINGDOMSave Institute of Commonwealth Studies from the axeCHINA-UNITED STATESIsolationism is a loser’s game and devastating for HEAFRICA-GLOBALSDGs at centre of new Commonwealth universities' strategyUNITED KINGDOM‘Strengthen Commonwealth links’ to stay internationalFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredGLOBALCommonwealth universities chart waters of global changeBrendan O’Malley12 September 2025TweetUniversity World NewsEditor-in-Chief Brendan O’Malley sat down with Professor Colin Riordan, secretary general of the Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU) and former vice-chancellor of Cardiff University, ahead ofACU Congress 2025in Nairobi in November on the theme of “Connected and resilient: Commonwealth universities charting global change”.The two discussed the vital importance of higher education to the whole education ecosystem, the funding problems that many universities are facing worldwide and the opportunities that American isolationism and tariffs may offer for other countries – in particular the Commonwealth, as an organisation that brings countries together.In the exclusive interview, Riordan said that the ACU has conducted a study* to make the case for the many benefits investment in higher education brings. He also covered increasing public scepticism about science and research and spoke about the importance of organisations like the ACU for sharing knowledge and best practice.He said that while the ACU acknowledges its past, it is moving forward with its sights set on providing solutions and optimism for the future, in line with the modern Commonwealth’scharter.This article is published with the support of theAssociation of Commonwealth Universities.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.This is a transcript of the interview. It has been edited for length and accuracy.UWN:We’re looking forward to your 2025 Congress, coming up in Nairobi in November with the theme ‘Connected and resilient: Commonwealth universities charting global change’. This event will bring together university vice-chancellors and leading voices in higher education from across the Commonwealth and, over two days, you'll be discussing critical challenges – and there are a lot of critical challenges facing higher education today. What do you see as the most pressing and significant changes and challenges that universities are facing right now?CR:Well, it always starts with funding, I’m afraid. Universities around the world and certainly in the Commonwealth are all under pressure for funding, and this is partly a result of, I think, the enormous pressure on the public finances which has arisen out of the COVID pandemic, which really drained the coffers of universities and indeed governments for that matter, and has made it more difficult to support the sector[JW1].Another issue is public scepticism about science, research, and universities in general. There’s been a real change in attitude over the last 10 or 15 years. I’ve worked in higher education for almost 40 years – 18 of those as a university leader in one form or another – and I actually saw that change happening.Previously universities were seen generally as a force for good in society and now – through the culture wars, through the polarisation, particularly after the 2008 financial crisis, which brought many things into sharp focus – universities have found themselves having to absolutely justify their position. And frankly, there's nothing wrong with that as it's important that universities should make those arguments and demonstrate why they are relevant.I know that our members are very concerned to do that and spend considerable time and effort on ensuring that the public, and governments, and all the relevant audiences – particularly students and parents right around the Commonwealth – understand that universities are important for their personal futures and for the future of the world.UWN:There's been a real decline in trust in higher education and in higher education institutions across society, especially in certain countries. But what about across the Commonwealth? Given the range of countries – high income, low income – is there a decline in trust across the board, or does this vary?CR:I would say there is a differentiation. I think the bigger decline in trust has happened more in the high income countries than in the low to medium income countries. And in those cases, the aspiration, particularly of young people, but also in terms of lifelong learning of the general population, can often be very high; an extremely powerful motivation to go to university and to get qualified and to get a degree and to build a better life in that way.And there’s also, I notice in many Commonwealth countries that are low or middle income countries, a commitment to national development, to building their country and taking the country forward. So it is differentiated.The issue that is common to all of them is competition for resources.It is quite understandable that education ministers and, for that matter, prime ministers and treasury ministers across the Commonwealth, see early years education as the big priority, which is understandable when 60% of children of that age in sub-Saharan Africa don’t go to school.That’s extremely important, and all of our members understand that. But higher education is a critical part of the education ecosystem, and you can’t just have early years on its own.In the best systems, early years, secondary, and tertiary education all work together. In the higher education sector, you’ll be educating the teachers and looking at how to craft the best policies to foster primary education, and how to make resources go further and support government in that respect.The argument that we want to make to Commonwealth governments is that higher education is not just an enormously important good in itself but it is also very important for the rest of the education ecosystem.UWN:Are there lessons to be learned from the painful experience of advanced countries, that perhaps medium and low income countries, as they’re investing more in higher education, can benefit from?CR:Well, there are plenty of instances where you can learn what not to do, because things have been tried and they haven't worked, or they've had unintended consequences that are then amended by new governments.And frankly, how to structure higher education to make it sustainable is a very knotty problem. I don't think there's any country in the world that's really cracked that one.It is essentially about getting the interests of the individual in the correct balance with the interests of the taxpayer, because there's a benefit to both. There's a benefit to any individual that goes to university. And it’s not just about the crude calculations of income once you've got a particular degree, but the intangible knock-on effects in communities that arise from a university education.You know, the taxpayer almost always does put money into universities. It's rarely possible to avoid that – although, of course, it does happen; you do get private universities with immensely wealthy benefactors, and there's nothing wrong with that, that’s all part of the panoply. But the state does have a role to play, and the taxpayer has a role to play. So getting that balance right is critical, and there is no right answer to it.But what's very helpful about the Association of Commonwealth Universities is that we can share that learning. And in fact, I've just come from a meeting – an online meeting, of course – with our Higher Education Task Force, which has members from right across the Commonwealth. What we're doing [with the task force] is developing policy proposals and options that will be useful to education ministers in the Commonwealth, that they can then take to their finance ministers and prime ministers to get support for their policies.We have conducted a study* which delves into the relationship between investment in tertiary education, and prosperity and economic growth, for each Commonwealth country. And that is not information that has hitherto been available. It might already be available in some countries: the UK and Canada, Australia, New Zealand. But it’s useful to have it on the same basis for the other Commonwealth countries; there will be many of our members that have never had that information available. It will be useful for them to have so they can make those arguments.And we do talk about what works, and what hasn’t worked.UWN:Are there some surprises? Do you find that there are some things that lower income countries are doing with fewer resources that higher income countries could learn from?CR:Oh yes, absolutely. And I think it is very much that way round, actually. One of our members, a vice-chancellor, is fond of pointing out that the Indian space programme can put somebody in space for less money than it takes to create a Hollywood blockbuster about going into space.And there is certainly something there that you see in universities across the Commonwealth; people doing an enormous amount with relatively inadequate resources.UWN:Is anyone advocating a path that veers away from what we're seeing in many Western countries of offloading some of the cost onto students through tuition fees? Is there any new thinking on that?CR:I wouldn't say that. In many of these countries, you know, university education just can't be afforded. Of course, there are certain countries like India and Nigeria, in particular, where there is a significantly wealthy portion of society that can afford that sort of thing – although it's a relatively small proportion.But what we are seeing is Commonwealth countries wanting to focus the resource in their own country, rather than financing students to go and study abroad, often in the UK or Australia or somewhere like that. What they’re doing is refocusing resources internally.And so you see, in India, for instance, the opportunity for international universities – and there are about a dozen UK universities doing this – setting up campuses in India, and they've been trying and debating this question for 15 years or more.UWN:And they’ve only just opened it up.CR:Yes, and I remember sitting in a meeting back in, I suppose, 2010, which included universities in India, with the then minister of human resource development, Kapil Sibal, where he was absolutely confident that this would be happening within the next year or so. And that was 15 years ago. I’m sure it wasn’t his fault. The politics are very complicated.But they are there now. And it’s an interesting development, because it’s part of the 2047 programme – a century after independence – of getting India to a full state of development. And partly doing that by opening things up to, as it were, international competition – not uncontroversial, but I think it’ll probably work.And the other trend that we’re seeing is a desire to spend domestic resources – in the case of Nigeria, for example – on transnational education, or TNE, that isn’t just about campuses, but also online education supplied by universities outside Nigeria, rather than financing their students to go and study at overseas universities.And the reason for that, I think, is partly to make sure the resources, of course, are deployed within the country, which is helpful, but also just because of the sheer economics, because the crisis in the naira, the currency in Nigeria, has made it really difficult to get value for money abroad. So we’re seeing that kind of trend.UWN:Is that being adapted to the dual degree idea? Because we’ve had that on some campuses where people will study at a domestic university and then spend some time either at a university abroad or at the campus in their country? Is there a transnational [TNE] equivalent that would work?CR:I think it's there, but it adds a lot of complexity. And I know that, as a former vice-chancellor – in fact, as a former head of school involved in these things – dual degrees take a lot of work. Because of the regulatory aspects, you've got to align two systems. It certainly does exist, but I certainly haven't noticed that it’s particularly on the rise.But a tendency more towards TNE and cooperationincountry is definitely there. And certainly one of the principles that we try to work by is to support our universities in their country as much as we can.So we have a new scholarship programme called the King’s Commonwealth Fellowship Programme, which His Majesty actually asked us to put on, and he mentioned it in his speech at the Commonwealth Heads of Government Meeting, CHOGM, in Samoa last October. We’re raising funds philanthropically for that. The King himself gave a generous personal donation and others have also donated.The idea is that those resources flow to the country, and the education, the scholarships, actually take place in the country. And in this case, it's the Small Island Developing States in the Pacific, the Caribbean and the Indian Ocean.So I think the emphasis is that the development takes place in the country – which has not traditionally been the case with international higher education. International higher education has tended to mean that you travel to a very well-established university, and undertake your studies there, and sometimes don't go home – and that's the issue, and that's quite understandably an issue. Obviously, there’s a place for both approaches, and we're supportive of both, but we're trying to shift the balance a little bit.UWN:Isn’t part of the problem with international higher education also that – especially because international student recruitment is often used as a means of raising income by charging much higher fees – there’s a sense in which sometimes only the international students who have a lot of money can afford to participate, in which case it’s tending to reinforce existing social power structures? Generally, one area in which higher education is open to criticism is if it hasn't done enough to address that point; that higher education should play its role in addressing inequities around the world, and it's doing the opposite if it simply reinforces elites.CR:I wouldn’t go as far as to say it's doing the opposite. And let’s remember not all international students are wealthy. But the fundamental point, which I think you are correctly suggesting, is that there is anextractiveelement to this process, and we really need to turn things around, or at least to address the balance as much as we reasonably can.However, I think it’s very important to stress that this isn't somehow a choice that universities in Australia and Canada and New Zealand have made because they thought that would be a great idea. It’s a system that has been created by governments that universities have to operate within. And that’s certainly what's happened in the UK and in the other wealthy countries in the Commonwealth where higher education is, to a significant degree, financed by international fees.Now there is an argument to say that international studentsshouldpay more because their families haven’t been paying taxes all their life into the system that they are, in part, benefitting from. So there does need to be some redressing of that, or at least it's arguable that there should, on a moral basis, be some redressing of that balance.The question is whether fees should be as high as they are. Having said that, nobody iscompelledto come and study in Australia, for instance; you know, that’s a choice that the students across the world can make if they want to, and if they're able to, and we have to accept that part. But you can't expect one country to be necessarily, as it were, responsible for any inequalities that might exist in another country.UWN:And, of course, we look at the whole issue from a global perspective. It is of value for the world that you get the best minds together from any discipline to work on particular challenges in the best places to do that. So there’s an argument to be made – purely in terms of expanding education and expanding knowledge for humanity’s sake – that bringing the best people from different places together intrinsically has good value in itself.CR:Absolutely. And the bigger the pool you can fish in, the more likely it is that you will get the best people together. And of course, having different perspectives really does make a difference.There’s a fantastic piece of research done some years ago by Jonathan Adams, who looked at 25 million records on international research collaboration and found that papers that have authors from more than one country are, on average, more highly cited than papers that don’t.Now, we often generally take levels of citation as a proxy for quality. It’s not a perfect analysis, in the sense that, of course, it could be just that they’re being cited in two countries or more. But it’s pretty commonly accepted that international collaboration produces better research – and it seems reasonable to assume that that’s partly because you’ve got the best people from a bigger pool, and therefore the quality is likely to be higher.And also the diverse viewpoints, backgrounds and education systems create a more fertile ground for ideas to flourish.UWN:One big stride forward in terms of equity was Agenda 2030 and the SDGs, but these are now under attack from various quarters. Could universities do more to meet them?CR:Well, my experience is that certainly the universities in our membership are extremely committed to the SDGs, and in certain countries universities are obliged to report annually on them. So, for instance, our members in India have to produce an annual report on progress on their contribution to the SDGs and it’s the same in other countries.We don’t have a directive system in the UK. But I certainly know that in all the Commonwealth countries I visit as part of my role as ACU secretary general, there is a real commitment to the SDGs.But coming back to the previous point about whether enough is being done. The goals are so ambitious that, frankly, ‘no’ is the answer. Enough isn’t being done, because politics kind of gets in the way. Politics is the art of the possible, and even if you didn’t have the wealthiest nation in the world not only rejecting but actually denouncing the SDGs – they would still be difficult to achieve.If you look at the progress, it is slow. But, of course, the whole system is under review. And one assumes that there will be a kind of revision of the SDGs; perhaps having fewer of them, reducing the range of what’s aimed at and what’s to be achieved, to make the initiative more focused.I think that probably would be helpful because the initiative covers such a broad spectrum, and there’s an SDG for really everything, which makes it very difficult to prioritise. And any government, any country, that doesn’t prioritise is not going to make progress in the direction they want to go.I think universities were really an important part of the development of the SDGs in the first place, and I think we’ll stay part of that as they’re revised.UWN:Well, it’s interesting you say that because higher education is barely mentioned in Agenda 2030 and there’s a lot of debate about whether its role is recognised at all, and why not. Whereas, higher education institutions have a key enabling role to play, as does education as a whole, for every single SDG. What would you like to see change in that regard, when they try to develop the successors to the 2030 goals?CR:Well, I think we need to do a much better job of explaining what the role of universities and researchers and educators of that level actually is and has been.In the case of many of the SDGs, without research you wouldn’t even be able to measure them. You wouldn’t be able to identify them, you wouldn’t be able to formulate them, let alone quantify them, without research.And much of that research – most of it really – takes place in universities, because universities are there for the public good. So if you look at the SDGs, you couldn't do the analyses or measure progress or any of it, really, in my view, without universities.So what we actually need to do is get that message across more clearly; that our members in the Association of Commonwealth Universities are absolutely fundamental to all these areas of education: climate resilience, oceans – pretty well all the SDGs.We have to focus somewhere, and that's where we tend to focus. If you take the Commonwealth, for example, there are 2.7 billion people, 60% of them under 30; that is the future of the world. They are the people who are going to make all this happen. So you know, education, including university education, plays a key role.But you’re right in saying that that is not really apparent at the moment. And I think the challenge for us is to make sure that our critical involvement in the development of the new SDGs or the revision of them is recognised.UWN:There is an ongoing debate about the role of universities in promoting skills, as opposed to knowledge alone, and there seems to be constant pressure from governments for universities to be doing more skills development. Is there not a danger that such governments will slip into thinking that TVET – technical and vocational education and training – rather than higher education, is the answer? And how do you see the role of higher education adapting to meet that challenge?CR:Well, I think one thing we can do is to make it clear that universities actually already do a lot of TVET. You know, a lot of what universities do is vocational type training. And a simple example of this is medicine. One doesn't think of that as a kind of vocational training, but it really is. You don't want a doctor treating you who's only done this theoretically and read it in a book. It's like Thoreau claimed to have learned to swim by reading a book; and I'm not quite sure I believe that. I certainly don't want to have a doctor or a dentist treat me that's only learned from reading a book.And there are many, many other examples in universities, whether it’s law or engineering – whole varieties of elements of the sciences and life sciences – that, when you look at them, are vocational. So there's that point to make; that it's already there and it's just that people tend not to recognise it.But there is also an important obligation, which I know our members recognise because we talk about it – we've just been talking about it in our Higher Education Task Force – which is our obligation, as universities, to support our governments, to support our national development, and to play our role in filling skills gaps and taking part in skills audits, finding out what the issues are, and coming up with programmes that can help meet those needs.I think that that is important to every single one of our members, including the ones you wouldn't necessarily expect. You know, the very well-established, old – possibly even ancient – universities are much more involved in that than people would think.And certainly all of our members in the developing countries have a very, very clear mandate from their governments to ensure that national goals are supported. And skills development is a really key element of that.One point I’d make though about universities and skills is that often the focus is on universities: ‘Why aren't you educating students that have the right skills for industry?’ Well, actually, it is a three-way process, because you also need industry and employers – those that require the skills that our graduates have – and you need them to be really clear about what they want and how they want it, and you need to work closely with them.And you also have to have, as it were, students or applicants whowantto do the programme that you develop. You know, it’s no good coming up with a wonderful programme that no student wants to study, because that doesn't get you very far.And that’s the real challenge in this, and something that we are looking at with our members; how to make sure that that three-way process really works, so that we can give sensible advice and support to governments across the Commonwealth, and universities and, for that matter, employers. We work with other Commonwealth organisations that represent employers, for example, to ensure that the supply of skills is appropriate.UWN:There seems to be a common theme here, throughout these issues that we've talked about, of the challenge of communication. If there's declining trust in universities among certain groups in the population, is it partly because universities aren't communicating a lot of the good work that they're doing? Or perhaps they're not communicating enough with government and employees and vice versa to get the mix right? So communication seems to be a big challenge.CR:Yes, it is, and there is no easy answer to that. It is difficult for universities because we’re not really doorstep issues in the sense that the first thing you ask your representative is, you know, ‘What’s going on with my university?’ It’s not about that. People are much more interested in the standard of living and job prospects, and prosperity and health care – or schooling, for that matter.So it is difficult to make your voice heard. And it’s made, of course, a lot more difficult because the communication channels now are so prolific; there are so many voices out there and so many channels, that it is hard to make a mark.So that’s really one of the issues that we face as universities. But it’s a big role for us in the ACU: to be the voice of universities in the Commonwealth. We are the accredited voice, the official voice of universities, and we speak for all universities in the Commonwealth in our activities. But as I say, it’s a crowded field.Everybody agrees we need to do better, and people are really trying, but there are a lot of demands, I suppose, on their time.UWN:What’s the message you’d share about how universities should respond to both the denigration of higher education through the culture wars and the tensions caused by current geopolitical shifts?CR:Well, there are different views on this. I heard at a conference a year or so ago, some vice-chancellors arguing that they should not make any pronouncement on anything that could be deemed political or controversial, that it’s not the job of a university to take a position on things like that – which feels as though itoughtto be a good idea, but is actually very difficult to uphold in practice.If you’ve got, for instance, a couple of 100 students either camped outside your window or shouting through it, or actually invading and occupying your office, it’s difficult to ignore – and difficult to say that we shouldn’t be saying something about whatever the particular issue may be.Also there’s a sense in which onedoeshave an obligation to get involved in things like climate change and carbon emissions and so on. So again, there’s no easy answer.But I think what’s important is that you don’t allow the culture wars to become such a distraction that you cannot focus on your core mission, and your core mission is the creation and dissemination of knowledge for the benefit of society – through education.That’s what you really have to focus on. And remembering that sometimes there are some very loud voices out there, but actually there are a lot of people who really do want to get in the lab, get in the library, attend their lectures, get their degree and go on to get a good job.So making sure that you balance those things is really an important part of all of this.But unfortunately, universities over the last 15 or so years have become heavily politicised in ways that they just weren’t. I mean, I started as a vice-chancellor in 2007 and yes, you know, there was politics, but it wasn’t really controversial in the way it is now; you’d have little battles over funding and so on.But now it is absolutely fundamental issues that people deeply disagree on, and you’re not going to be able to get them to agree. And you’re kind of caught in the middle, and you’re damned if you do, and you’re damned if you don’t.So I think the job of universities, and particularly university leadership and vice-chancellors, has become much more difficult, because you’re having to steer a course between really polarised views. There are big challenges there but somehow universities do seem to continue to function extremely well and do a great job.UWN:How are you negotiating the challenge of increasing hostility between some countries? Is it creating a problem for the ACU in terms of how you collaborate with partners, or is it perhaps an opportunity to do more?CR:Well, I think there are two aspects to this.One of them is within the Commonwealth. You know, recently we've seen some extremely high tension between two Commonwealth countries, India and Pakistan, which thankfully has now abated somewhat – although the fundamental issue, of course, remains, and has remained for some decades, and requires enormous sensitivity and tact.But the other issue, which really, I think, does affect our members, is the split between the United States and Canada. Now Canada is a Commonwealth country with a new prime minister who – I don't think anybody would deny – won that election in great measure because of the issue of the Trump decision to dismantle the traditional relationship and replace it with a much more transactional one. The new prime minister, Mark Carney, himself a Commonwealth scholar, understands the Commonwealth, and there may well be opportunities for Commonwealth countries to work together and collaborate.That’s very much what the Association of Commonwealth Universities has always done since our foundation in 1913; we were set up to allow universities to collaborate, and that's what we still do. And I think the isolationism that you now see in the US, and the tariff wars and so on, could provide an opportunity.Naturally, one would much rather these disruptions weren't happening, put it that way, but they could provide an opportunity within the Commonwealth for greater collaboration.And the final point on this is that the new Commonwealth of Nations secretary general, Shirley Botchwey, has put trade as one of her key strategic pillars for her term as secretary general, which is quite a big shift.If you look at the Commonwealth charter, there’s a paragraph on supporting multilateral, fair, open-access trade between Commonwealth members. So that's there in the principles. And I think that would certainly involve higher education if we want it to.UWN:Could you elaborate on that, how it concerns higher education? Are we talking about higher education as an export, or something else?CR:Yes. The reality is that when students move from one country to another, it does actually count as an export. This offers the opportunity, in line with the Commonwealth charter, to really look at how we reduce inequality and introduce more equity within the Commonwealth. So I think that there could be opportunities there.But also, of course, universities are heavily involved in innovation, in coming up with solutions to climate change – or at least helping to develop climate resilience right across the piece.And that is a massive strength of the ACU, because we are able to forge collaborations between relatively small, relatively new universities, that are on the absolute frontline of climate change, with well-established universities elsewhere in the network.And we see that as a real opportunity and a real advantage for the ACU compared with other international university associations.UWN:You have this great coming together of university leaders in Nairobi in November at the ACU Congress 2025. What makes the Commonwealth so relevant that they should come together as a bloc? Because people might have a vague idea that the Commonwealth is to do with history rather than with today. What do the university leaders want to get out of a meeting like this?CR:Well, I think the university leaders always benefit from coming together from time to time, particularly to talk to their counterparts from very different countries. And you do find that that diversity is really important and really fertile ground, because you can learn from one another. That is our experience.We held a smaller meeting in London in May 2024, where we brought vice-chancellors together from 100 Commonwealth universities from our membership, and we had some very, very lively discussions and were able to learn from one another. So that will be one more general reason for coming together.But why the Commonwealth? It is because we have so much in common. You know, there's a high degree of commonality there – not least the English language but also the heritage, traditions and structures.However unhappy the origins are, we’re now in the 21st century, and looking to the future. We're very aware of the past, and we don't deny where the Commonwealth came from. But let’s not forget, the Commonwealth is a voluntary association of nations. Nobodyhasto be a member and you have to choose to join. It’s entirely voluntary.And that goes as well, of course, for our membership. People choose to be a member of an organisation that brings people together from an extraordinarily diverse set of countries, but with a common basis on which to have a discussion and a common language to do it in, and that is highly beneficial.To find out more about the ACU Congress 2025click here.*The Impact of Investment in Higher Education on Economic Growth:Final report of the Association of Commonwealth Universities, which is availablehere.Brendan O’Malley is the editor-in-chief ofUniversity World News.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

``` 
英聯邦大學應對全球變革挑戰 近日，英聯邦大學協會（ACU）秘書長科林·里奧丹（Colin Riordan）接受專訪，討論了高等教育在教育生態系統中的重要性及面臨的資金壓力。里奧丹指出，美國的孤立主義為其他國家，特別是英聯邦國家提供了機會。他強調，投資高等教育對經濟增長至關重要，並呼籲各國重視高等教育的角色，以促進社會發展和技能培訓，並強調ACU在共享知識和最佳實踐中的重要作用。預計2025年11月在奈洛比召開的ACU大會，將進一步探討這些關鍵挑戰。 
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: American isolationism brings HE opportunities elsewhere
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: American isolationism brings HE opportunities elsewhereGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALCommonwealth universities chart waters of global changeGLOBALCommonwealth has a key role in mitigating HE funding crisisGLOBALACU launches toolkit for equitable research partnershipsGLOBALStark inequalities across Commonwealth research ecosystemsGLOBALHow to help researchers make the connections that matterAFRICADigital education should be a sustainable development goalUNITED KINGDOMSave Institute of Commonwealth Studies from the axeCHINA-UNITED STATESIsolationism is a loser’s game and devastating for HEAFRICA-GLOBALSDGs at centre of new Commonwealth universities' strategyUNITED KINGDOM‘Strengthen Commonwealth links’ to stay internationalFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALAmerican isolationism brings HE opportunities elsewhereDorothy Lepkowska12 September 2025TweetAmerican isolationism and tariff wars could pave the way for greater collaboration between universities in Commonwealth countries to reduce inequality, according to Professor Colin Riordan, secretary general of the Association of Commonwealth Universities (ACU).Speaking in anexclusive interviewwithUniversity World NewsEditor-in-Chief Brendan O’Malley, he said changes to the world order and a move by the United States away from international cooperation offered new opportunities for Commonwealth countries to work together in higher education.Riordan referred to the breakdown in relations between the United States and Canada following the election of Donald Trump, who has “dismantled the traditional relationship and replaced it with a much more transactional one”.“Mark Carney, the new [Canadian] prime minister, who is a Commonwealth scholar, understands the Commonwealth, and there may well be opportunities for Commonwealth countries to work together and collaborate,” Riordan said.This article is published with the support of theAssociation of Commonwealth Universities.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.“That’s very much what the ACU has always done since our foundation in 1913. The new Commonwealth secretary general, Shirley Botchwey, has put trade as one of her key strategic pillars, so the principles are there.“If you look at the Commonwealth charter, there is a paragraph supporting multilateral, fair, open access trade between Commonwealth members. So that’s there in the principles. And I think it would certainly involve higher education if we want it to.”In higher education, trade includes the recruitment of international students, because when students move from one country to another, it counts as an export.Regarding the impact on international student flows of changes in higher education implemented by the Trump administration, he said there is an “opportunity, in line with the Commonwealth charter, to really look at how we reduce inequality and introduce more equity within the Commonwealth”.Riordan was speaking ahead of the organisation’s 2025 Congress, which is taking place in Nairobi in November with the theme, ‘Connected and resilient: Commonwealth universities charting global change’. The event will bring together university vice-chancellors and leading voices in higher education from around the world to discuss critical challenges facing the sector.He said one way of bringing trading principles into the sector globally was to help to reduce inequality and to innovate in finding solutions to climate change.One of the advantages that the ACU had over other international university associations was its mix of well-established universities and smaller, relatively new institutions which were at the frontline of climate change. This enabled members to work together to innovate solutions to climate change and to help develop resilience globally.Funding pressuresHowever, universities globally continued to face many challenges, with funding being the most pressing.“Universities around the world, and certainly in the Commonwealth, are all under pressure for funding, and this is partly a result of, I think, the enormous pressure on the public finances which has arisen out of the COVID pandemic,” Riordan said.“This really drained the coffers of universities, and indeed governments, and has made it more difficult to support the sector,” he said.“Another issue is public scepticism about science, research, [and] universities in general. There's been a real change in attitude over the last 10 or 15 years. Universities, once seen generally as a force for good in society, through the culture wars and the polarisation, particularly after the 2008 financial crash, which brought many things into sharp focus, now find themselves having to justify their position. And, frankly, there’s nothing wrong with that, as it’s important that universities should make those arguments and demonstrate why they are relevant.“I know that our members spend considerable time and effort on ensuring that the public and governments understand, and all the relevant audiences, particularly students and parents right around the Commonwealth, understand that universities are important for their personal futures and for the future of the world.”A sector in need of government supportThe biggest decline in higher education in recent decades has occurred in high-income countries, Riordan said, compared with low- to medium-income countries, where universities are a vital part of the commitment to national development, to building a country and taking it forward.“The issue that is common to all of them is a lack of, and competition for, resources,” he added. “It is quite understandable that education ministers, and for that matter prime ministers and treasury ministers across the Commonwealth, see early years education as the big priority, which is understandable when 60% of children in Sub-Saharan Africa don’t go to school.“But higher education is a critical part of the education ecosystem, and you can’t just have early years on its own. In the best systems, early years, secondary, and tertiary education all work together.“In the higher education sector, you’ll be educating the teachers and looking at how to craft the best policies to foster primary education and how to make resources go further and support government in that respect.“The argument that we want to make to Commonwealth governments is that higher education is not just an enormously important good in itself, but it is also very important for the rest of the education ecosystem.”However, trying to structure higher education so it is sustainable was “a very knotty problem”, and developing countries did not necessarily have lessons to learn from more established systems.“It is essentially about getting the interests of the individual in the correct balance with the interests of the taxpayer, because there’s a benefit to both,” Riordan added. “And it is not just about the crude calculations of income but the intangible knock-on effects in communities that arise from a university education.”Strategy for engaging governmentsA recent meeting of the ACU’s Higher Education Task Force, attended by Riordan and other higher education leaders, discussed how to get governments more engaged with the higher education sectors in their respective countries.“What is very helpful about the ACU is that we can share that learning,” he said. “What we’re doing [with the task force] is developing policy proposals and options that will be useful to education ministers in the Commonwealth, that they can then take to their finance ministers and prime ministers to get support for their policies.“We have conducted a study* which delves into the relationship between investment in tertiary education and prosperity and economic growth for each Commonwealth country,” he added, stating that while this information might already be available in some countries there will be many ACU members who have never had that information available. “It will be useful for them to have so they can make those arguments,” he said.Fertile ground for ideasLooking ahead to the coming together of university leaders in Kenya in November, Riordan said the spirit of collaboration and diversity was a “fertile ground” for ideas and mutual discovery.“We have so much in common,” he said. “There is a high degree of commonality there, not least the English language, but also, you know, the sort of heritage and traditions and structures.“However unhappy the origins are, we’re now in the 21st century and looking to the future. We’re very aware of the past, and we don't deny where the Commonwealth came from. But let’s not forget, the Commonwealth is a voluntary association of nations. Nobodyhasto be a member, and you have to choose to join. It’s entirely voluntary.“And that goes as well, of course, for our membership. People choose to be a member of an organisation that brings people together from an extraordinarily diverse set of countries but with a common basis on which to have a discussion and a common language to do it in, and that is highly beneficial.”Colin Riordan was interviewed byUniversity World NewsEditor-in-Chief Brendan O’Malley. You can read the full interviewhere.To find out more about the ACU Congress 2025click here.*The Impact of Investment in Higher Education on Economic Growth: Final report of the Association of Commonwealth Universities, which is availablehereReceive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 美國孤立主義為英聯邦高等教育帶來新機遇。根據英聯邦大學協會（ACU）秘書長科林·里奧丹教授的說法，隨著美國日益遠離國際合作，英聯邦國家間的合作機會增加，有助於減少不平等。他提到，與加拿大的關係變化可能促進英聯邦國家在高等教育方面的合作，尤其在招募國際學生和應對氣候變化方面。里奧丹強調，雖然全球大學面臨資金壓力，但英聯邦的合作精神為創新解決方案提供了良好的基礎。這一趨勢在即將舉行的2025年ACU大會上將成為熱議主題。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Is Greenwich-Kent multi-university a merger or a takeover?
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Is Greenwich-Kent multi-university a merger or a takeover?GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED KINGDOMGreenwich-Kent merger is no tidy governance rearrangementUNITED KINGDOMAs strikes over HE cuts loom, two reports offer fresh ideasUNITED KINGDOMUniversities blueprint seeks new HE-government compactUNITED KINGDOM50+ universities cut jobs as overseas students stay awayUNITED KINGDOM-EUROPEDismayed students’ bid to save University of Kent’s Brussels hubUNITED KINGDOMIs anyone listening? International HE is in a parlous stateFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED KINGDOMIs Greenwich-Kent multi-university a merger or a takeover?Nic Mitchell12 September 2025TweetExperts are divided over the surprise announcement this week that the University of Greenwich in London and the University of Kent plan to create a new ‘trailblazing’ multi-university, with both institutions coming together under one structure and one vice-chancellor – but keeping their names and local presence.Some simply see it as an over-hyped merger by another name, with media coverage jumping on the partners’ claim that they want to create a new ‘super-university’ to serve South London and South East England from the start of the 2026 to 2027 academic year.Others suggest it is really a takeover by Greenwich, an ex-polytechnic which became a university in 1992 and one of the few higher education institutions in the United Kingdom still turning in a profit.Turbulent timesThe University of Kent, based in Canterbury, has faced turbulent times in recent years, with large-scale job cuts and course closures as it battles for financial survival.Kent’s vice-chancellor, Professor Karen Cox, was accused by University and College Union leader Jo Grady of behaving “like an arsonist trying to flee the scene” after her resignation last year, asUniversity World Newsreported.Amongst the cutbacks at the 60-year-old University of Kent, which billed itself as the “UK’s European University”, was the closure of its outpost in Brussels, whichUniversity World Newscovered in 2023.The University of Kent had 17,190 students in 2024 when it had a £31 million (US$42 million) deficit, up from £12 million the year before.The University of Greenwich had 29,695 students in 2024 and made a pre-tax surplus that year of £33 million, up from £14 million in 2023.The two universities already share a campus in Medway in the former Chatham Royal Navy base, where Nelson’s flagship HMS Victory was built.Mixed reactionMergers of institutions are rare in UK higher education compared to elsewhere in the world.Branding and higher education experts gave a mixed reaction to the announcement that Greenwich and Kent intend to establish theLondon and South East University Groupto create the new multi-university model, “bringing both institutions under one structure whilst enabling each university to retain its name, identity and local presence”.Vivienne Stern, chief executive of Universities UK, described the move as the “perfect example of creative thinking” needed to work “differently in response to sustained financial pressures” facing higher education in Britain.Universities UK is the umbrella body for British vice-chancellors and has established aTransformation and Efficiency Taskforceto encourage universities to collaborate more closely to achieve greater efficiency.However, others are questioning the motive behind the Greenwich and Kent move, with Michael Beverland, a professor of brand strategy and marketing at the University of Sussex Business School, saying: “What’s missing is any clear substance.”Beverland toldUniversity World News: “Simply calling it a ‘super-university’ is kind of meaningless.“Both brands are fairly average after all; Kent is really struggling, while Greenwich is more invested in its local area and very focused on graduate outcomes. And Greenwich is larger and more viable.”Beverland suggested Greenwich may be hoping to buy some additional research capability and prestige through the link-up with higher-ranked Kent, but it may also end up stripping it back “much like BMW did when they purchased Rover for Mini”.Greenwich also has the historical location of its main campus at the old Royal Naval College on the south banks of the River Thames, offering “a heritage one can work with”, said Beverland, who wondered whether the multi-university “mega merger” might be more successful trading in Kent along the lines of “Greenwich at Canterbury” rather than trying to market the new institution under two university brand titles.Educational rationaleA former University of Kent senior academic, who asked not to be identified, toldUniversity World News: “I think the challenge is less about creating a new brand and more about how the curriculum or wider portfolio of activity and mission is rationalised, differentiated, and presented.“In the case of students, they are primarily concerned with the clarity of the offer, the strength of teaching, and the credibility of the degree, so any marketing narrative has to be underpinned by a real educational rationale.“Without that, brand consolidation risks feeling cosmetic.”The ex-Kent academic said the UK had much less experience of two universities in the same geographic region and with overlapping portfolios coming together.However, European precedents, exemplified by the University of Lille in France, could prove useful. There, several institutions were brought under a single identity with the explicit goal of streamlining resources, boosting research visibility, and presenting a clearer message internationally.“At Lille, the internal reorganisation gave external marketing greater credibility, and the offer really was restructured, not just rebadged.“Kent has been through a succession of challenging restructures in recent years, and there is a risk that these next steps could prolong disruption to working culture and student experience in the short term.“Avoiding that outcome will be critical for the benefit of all stakeholders. The key is to avoid the perception that this is simply about cost savings or administrative efficiency,” said the former Kent academic.A move likely to ‘resonate’ with governmentDr Diana Beech, a former higher education government adviser who was chief executive at London Higher before taking up a new role as director of the Finsbury Institute at the recently merged City St George's University in London, toldUniversity World Newsshe thought the “new initiative will resonate with the current Labour government”.Beech said: “With limited public funding available and a clear message that the sector needs to take responsibility for its own governance and sustainability, partnerships like this show the sector is not just thinking differently but willing to do something about it.”With the government having ruled out bailouts for cash-strapped universities in England, Beech said: “Any government will be pleased to see the sector building resilience from within.” However, she warned that the Greenwich-Kent model might not work in regions without a high concentration of provision.Beech said it had appeared that “most universities have been playing a bit of a waiting game, thinking that help will come if they sit pretty long enough ... but I don’t see a lifeboat coming to save us anytime soon”.Turning to the recent merger at her institution to create City St George’s, University of London, she toldUniversity World News: “It was never built on a financial rescue, but an opportunity to join forces to create a much more powerful university with greater scale, resilience and interdisciplinary potential.”Beech said it hadn’t been without its challenges, and “while carefully planned, much of the integration work was left until after the formal merger date, so the process of integrating teams and working out new operating models is still ongoing”.Greenwich to hold new VC roleIn theannouncementabout the new multi-university, Greenwich and Kent universities said they had agreed that the new ‘super university’ will have one unified governing body, academic board and executive team, and one vice-chancellor.The first vice-chancellor will be Professor Jane Harrington, currently vice-chancellor of the University of Greenwich.“Students will continue to apply to, study at and graduate from their chosen university, preserving each institution's identity whilst benefitting from the scale and opportunities of the new group,” the announcement noted.The announcement added that “the two universities are working closely with the Department for Education and the Office for Students, both of whom support the sector’s need to evolve to ensure future sustainability and impact and deliver positive outcomes for students”.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 英國格林威治大學與肯特大學計劃合併為新型「多大學」模式，將於2026至2027學年啟動。該模式將兩所大學置於同一結構與副校長領導下，卻保留各自名稱與地區影響力。專家對此反應不一，有人認為這是一場合併的過度炒作，另一些則質疑格林威治大學是否在進行接管。肯特大學面臨財務困境，而格林威治則表現穩健。此舉被視為應對高等教育資金壓力的創新思路，可能對學生提供更豐富的學習機會。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New plan to speed up HE alignment with national priorities
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New plan to speed up HE alignment with national prioritiesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINATariff crisis speeds up approval of new ‘strategic’ coursesCHINAFudan’s cuts fuel fears for future of humanities in ChinaCHINAPush to speed up open-source AI adoption by universitiesCHINAUniversity disciplines revamp to link closely to industryCHINA‘Low employment’ university courses not approvedFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredCHINANew plan to speed up HE alignment with national prioritiesAmber Wang12 September 2025TweetChina has unveiled a new plan for 2025 to 2027 that goes further and faster in aligning higher education with national economic and strategic priorities. Its issue by the Central Education Work Leading Group – the Communist Party of China’s top body for education policy – signals that its rollout is considered a priority at the highest political level.But accelerating measures to ensure the university system serves national plans and employment needs has also met with criticism among academics.They warn that an overemphasis on immediate employment outcomes risks narrowing universities’ broader educational mission.Students are also concerned about uncertainty because implementation of the plans means that programmes tend to be cancelled or restructured at short notice.The Ministry of Education has already said its2023 reform targetrequiring 20% of university majors to be overhauled by the end of this year was successfully met ahead of schedule.Universities added 3,715 new undergraduate programmes, while 6,638 were cancelled or suspended, according to Luan Zongtao, deputy director of the Department of Degree Management and Postgraduate Education at the ministry, speaking to the media on 28 August.By comparison,a decade agoin 2015, only 2,342 programmes were “adjusted”, according to official figures.Vice-minister of Education Wu Yan acknowledged at a recent press conference that the “intensity of the adjustment is unprecedented”.Forecasting platformA noteworthy development in the new plan made public on 28 August is the creation of an artificial intelligence-powered big data platform to track talent supply and demand with “precision”, according to the official announcement.The forecasting platform will analyse workforce demand nationally in key sectors, flag misalignments with university programmes, and provide early warnings on university degree majors that fall short of industry needs.In its announcement at the end of August the government said the platform is already being piloted at several universities.Zhang Duanhong, deputy director of the Institute of Higher Education at Shanghai’s Tongji University, said it signaled a “more dynamic mechanism” to adjust higher education alignment to national goals.To put it more bluntly, future decisions about which disciplines and new majors universities will establish will rely not only on expert judgment but also on real-time data analysis,” Zhang told local Shanghai media.The system would also build on the existing Red-Yellow Card system implemented recently to hold universities directly accountable for subpar employment of their graduates. Under the new rules, programmes with graduate employment rates below 50% for two consecutive years will receive a yellow card warning, while those that fail to improve will be issued a red card, risking suspension or closure.In June, Beijing also introduced an “extraordinary mechanism” tofast-track approval of majors deemed strategically important. Recent approvals centred on STEM fields, with majors such as low-altitude technology, and engineering granted priority approval.This means they can leapfrog the centralised application system, which normally begins at least one year in advance of programmes being launched for enrolment. According to a Ministry of Education notice on 14 August, 120 universities have applied under the fast-track system to offer low-altitude technology and engineering programmes in 2026.The government has hinted at even shorter approval periods under the new plan with a stated aim to further shorten the process to “innovate” the annual catalogue of approved undergraduate majors over the next three years.Debate about ‘unbalanced’ changeBut the rapid pace of restructuring under the plan has also sparked debate within China’s higher education community, including about whether the all-out drive towards science and technology and the high number of engineering degrees being brought in, while humanities and arts courses are culled, has gone too far at the expense of some traditional disciplines.In April, drasticplans to halve the intake of humanities studentsat one of China’s most prestigious institutions, Fudan University, divided opinions among scholars.Professor Joshua Ka-ho Mok, provost and vice-president (academic and research) at Hang Seng University of Hong Kong, acknowledged criticism that the system could become unbalanced in the long term as “more emphasis is being placed on practical and applied needs with employment considerations”.“But I am sure the Chinese government has realised the impact of this shift and some universities with a strong humanities orientation are also supported to keep up their tradition,” Mok said.After the release of the latest plan, many people also expressed concern that intensified closure, suspension, and transfer of majors could shut students out of their chosen fields.Que Mingkun, executive director of the National High-End Think Tank Centre at Zhejiang University’s School of Education, was quoted by Shanghai’s officialWenhui Baonewspaper as saying: “In the face of rapid external changes, universities must maintain their composure, neither rushing to add new majors nor blindly closing down traditional, long-established majors without considering the university's specific circumstances.”Jiabin Zhu, associate professor in the School of Education at Shanghai Jiao Tong University, toldUniversity World News, over-emphasising employment outcomes should be avoided as it can “undermine the development of talent needed for long-term technological innovation”.“This critical issue should be approached holistically – by aligning with macro-level factors such as national strategy, industrial restructuring, and regional economic development,” said Zhu.“[It should be] supported by middle-level [organisational level] elements including institutional discipline development and faculty capacity building, and take into account the ‘micro-level’ considerations, particularly students’ individual growth needs and the fundamental principles of talent cultivation,” she noted.Criticism from studentsStudents turned to social media to express their growing anxiety over the uncertainty caused by the speedy overhaul, with many programmes having been cancelled, merged or restructured at short notice.For example, the speed at which AI courses are being expanded has led tocriticisms of low quality courses. Some students complained of faculty shortages and incomplete curricula for many of the new programmes.Zhang suggested better career guidance for students, noting if the government’s new forecasting centre “can be connected to university career development centres, helping students understand industry trends, job opportunities and risks in real time, it will alleviate the uncertainty students experience as they adjust their majors”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
中國近日公布了一項2025至2027年高等教育新計劃，旨在加速高等教育與國家經濟及戰略優先事項的對接。該計劃由中央教育工作領導小組推出，強調大學系統需符合國家規劃和就業需求，然而，學者們對此表示擔憂，認為過度關注就業結果可能會削弱大學的廣泛教育使命。

教育部已提前完成2023年改革目標，新增3715個本科專業的同時，取消或暫停6638個專業。新計劃中包括一個基於人工智慧的數據平台，用以精確追蹤人才供需，並及時調整大學課程，這一措施將可能改變未來學科設置的決策方式。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Steep drop in overseas students leaves universities reeling
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Steep drop in overseas students leaves universities reelingGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative
