https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: HE groups welcome court ruling on Harvard funding cuts
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: HE groups welcome court ruling on Harvard funding cutsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsHarvard University. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATES-GLOBALWhy the world can’t afford to lose Harvard’s global missionUNITED STATESHarvard’s lawsuit offers an opening for ethical renewalUNITED STATESHarvard stands firm against Trump’s ‘retaliatory’ threatsUNITED STATESTrump cuts US$2.2 billion and threatens rebellious HarvardUNITED STATESHarvard’s turn in the crosshairs of Trump’s authoritarianismUNITED STATESTrump pressurises universities to stamp out antisemitismFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED STATESHE groups welcome court ruling on Harvard funding cutsNathan M Greenfield05 September 2025TweetIn a landmark ruling likely to affect other university-related cases, the United States district judge of the US District Court for the District of Massachusetts, Allison D Burroughs, ruled that the freezing of billions of dollars of research grants to Harvard University by the Trump administration was unlawful and had nothing to do with any of the administration’s purported fight against antisemitism at universities.“A review of the administrative record makes it difficult to conclude anything other than that Defendants [the Department of Education, DoE, and the Department of Justice, DoJ] used antisemitism as a smokescreen for a targeted, ideologically motivated assault on this country’s premier universities and did so in a way that runs afoul of the Administrative Procedures Act [APA], the First Amendment [to the Constitution, which bans ‘viewpoint’-based discrimination by the government] and Title VI [of the Civil Rights Act of 1964, which prohibits discrimination on the basis of race, colour, national origin, sex, religion, shared ancestry and several other grounds],” the ruling stated.Burroughs’84-page decisionchastised the Department of Health and Human Services (DHHS), which made the medical grants and froze the medical research grants, and the freezing of others by the Defence Department when “there was no obvious link between the affected projects and antisemitism.”In addition to repeating the claim – made by the original case brought by both the Harvard branch of the American Association of University Professors (AAUP) and the national organisation, and Harvard’s case, which was joined with the AAUP’s – that the freezing of medical grants meant the loss of “life-saving medical research”, Burroughs slammed the government for claiming that it was motivated by the imperative to root out antisemitism and for not taking into account how the wholesale freezing of grants would affect Jewish faculty and students.“[R]esearch was frozen without any sort of investigation into whether particular labs were engaging in antisemitic behaviour, were employing Jews, were run by Jewish scientists, or were investigating issues or diseases particularly pertinent to Jews (such as, for example, Tay-Sachs disease), meaning that the funding freezes could and likely will harm the very people Defendants professed to be protecting.“And it is unlikely that any Jew, even one directly impacted by antisemitism, would be in favour of stopping research on, for example, Alzheimer’s disease, heart disease, or autism, to name a few, as a means of redressing their unrelated harm,” wrote Burroughs.A win for higher educationThe ruling was welcomed by Todd Wolfson, national AAUP president, who said in a press release: “This is a huge win for all of American higher education, for science, and for free and critical thought in this country. Time and again, Trump has tried to restrict speech and cripple lifesaving university research. As today’s victory shows, Trump’s war on higher education is unconstitutional.“We will continue to stand up and fight back against these attempts to dismantle our universities, terrify students and faculty, and punish hospitals and scientific research for not bowing to authoritarianism.”At an online press conference, Veena Dubal, the National AAUP general counsel, said: “We have never seen or faced a crisis like we see today. Unlike during the Cold War, when ‘communists’ alone were targeted, today entire universities are being targeted. The Trump administration is trying to dismantle the system of higher education that has made the US the leading nation in science, critical thought and upward mobility.“The story here is not that university leadership stood valiantly alone and fought back against the Trump administration … [F]aculty and graduate students, in their individual capacities, put themselves at risk, subject to potential retaliation by an unpredictable federal government, by writing declarations, sharing their information and having their harms delineated in our filings. And it was those harms, the spectacularity of those harms [that were quoted by Judge Burroughs], that won this case for us.”In an email toUniversity World News, Lynn Pasquerella, president of the American Association of Colleges and Universities, warmly welcomed the decision.“Judge Burrough’s ruling is both a decisive affirmation of academic freedom, institutional autonomy, constitutional integrity, foundational to the strength of American higher education and a rejection of the weaponisation of antisemitism as a means of political retaliation aimed at ideological conformity.”White House to appealThe White House was not pleased and immediately announced it would appeal the ruling.Speaking for the president, White House Assistant Press Secretary Liz Huston said: “Just as President Trump correctly predicted on the day of the hearing, this activist Obama-appointed judge was always going to rule in Harvard’s favour, regardless of the facts.”Her statement,reported byMSN.com, continued: “To any fair-minded observer, it is clear that Harvard University failed to protect their students from harassment and allowed discrimination to plague their campus for years. Harvard does not have a constitutional right to taxpayer dollars and remains ineligible for grants in the future.”While the last part of Huston’s statement will play well with Trump’s MAGA (Make America Great Again) supporters, it is factually inaccurate – and was likely anticipated by Judge Burroughs.As Andrew Crespo, a prominent law professor at Harvard, told the press briefing: “The ruling provides broad and powerful prospective relief”. In other words, it is designed to prevent future harm.“Not only does she order the Trump administration to immediately stop withholding the funds that it has illegally held hostage . . . she putin place a permanent injunction barring it [the Trump administration] from ever trying to use these same unlawful tactics again.“It cannot freeze funds or deny future grant applications to try to coerce [us] into toeing the line of what it wants said on our campus.A central part of Judge Burroughs’ decision concerns theFirst Amendment, which reads, in part: “Congress shall make no law . . . abridging the freedom of speech.”In a number of cases dating back to the 1950s, the Supreme Court of the United States used the First Amendment to fashion a four-part definition of academic freedom, which grants the college or university the right to determine “who may teach, what may be taught, how it shall be taught, and who may be admitted to study” and the doctrine that the government cannot engage in viewpoint discrimination by either favouring or banning content.Burroughs found that the demands the DoE made in its 11 April letter to Harvard contravened these constitutional guardrails.In part, that letter demanded that Harvard “commission an external party . . . to audit the student body, faculty, staff, and leadership for viewpoint diversity, such that each department, field, [and] teaching unit must be individually viewpoint diverse”.Since the administration’s claim was that Harvard is, as Crespo put it, “a bastion of leftists” or a “den of Marxism”, the only way to achieve “viewpoint diversity” would be to impose the hiring and recruiting of conservatives.Incidentally, Crespo pointed out that Harvard’s chief academic officer is “one of the leading conservative legal scholars in the country” and that about 80% of Harvard Law School graduates work in large corporate law firms.Burroughs focused on another part of the 11 April letter by saying that it “require[d] Harvard to overhaul its governance, hiring, and academic programs to comport with the government’s ideology and prescribed viewpoint”.Burroughs accepted the argument advanced by the AAUP that “the Defendants’ [that is, the Trump administration’s] viewpoint-based demands have resulted in the Organisational Plaintiff’s members [that is, AAUP members] being well beyond ‘subjectively chilled’. Rather, the Organisational Plaintiffs have established that their members have been and likely will continue to be compelled to forgo certain research and discussion topics or at least parse their words carefully to maximise any chance of maintaining funding.”Affected research projectsWhile Harvard and its supporters, as well as supporters of other universities, like Johns Hopkins, often speak of life-saving research that will be lost, Burroughs’ decision is the first decision to provide a more complete list of this research.In addition to the research on Tay-Sachs, the freeze in funds meant that “immunologists overseeing a multi-school tuberculosis consortium” were told to halt their research. Research in Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis (ALS), a degenerative nerve disease that killed Lou Gehrig, the great New York Yankee player, was halted.The Department of Veterans Affairs was readying to terminate a grant for the development of “a predicative model to help VA emergency room physicians decide whether suicidal veterans should be hospitalised.” Another researcher was told to “halt his development of an advanced chip to measure NASA astronauts’ radiation exposure during the upcoming Artemis II mission to the moon.”Exactly how a Department of Defense programme was spared, Burroughs did not appear to know. But she included the effort to shut it down as one more example of research that was endangered and had no connection to antisemitism on Harvard’s campus.That was the Assured Microbial Preservation in Harsh or Remote Areas (AMPHORA) programme at Harvard, which is researching the [d]evelopment of critical technologies that enables biosurveillance and biocollection in austere, field-forward locations.”This knowledge “bolsters national security and warfighter safety and lethality by enabling medical countermeasure development of new and emerging threats and provides biological threat intelligence to the deployed warfighter”, quotes in the judgement noted.Nor was Burroughs swayed by the government’s defence that what this case is really about is contract law, because in its view, grants are simply contracts by another name.Burroughs rejected that argument, which would have meant that the case against the government should have been brought in the Court of Federal Claims and not Federal District Court – an argument supported by the Supreme Court (SCOTUS) just last week when it greenlighted more than three-quarters of a billion dollars in cuts at the National Institutes of Health.Burroughs upped the constitutional ante by writing: “The First Amendment claims here are about speech and whether the federal government is improperly infringing on the free speech of an academic institution and its employees [that is, by reacting to the refusal of its viewpoint diversity demands by cutting research dollars as punishment].“The resolution of those claims might result in money changing hands [that is, the grants being unfrozen], but what is fundamentally at issue is a bedrock constitutional principle rather than the interpretation of contract terms.”In ruling against the government’s defence of its termination of the grants to Harvard, Burroughs determined that Harvard itself was not really party to the grantor-grantee relationship. Rather, it was the researchers “names that appear directly on the face of the grant agreements, their labs that receive the funding, and their research that is eliminated when funding is discontinued.”In other words, by terminating the grants for something Harvard did or did not do, the government was punishing not the Corporation of Harvard but, rather, researchers with whom the government had an independent relationship.No reasoned explanationWhile somewhat esoteric to those outside the United States, Burroughs’ finding that the government violated the APA and Title VI should be noted. In both cases, the issue is that the government did not follow established legal procedures for terminating the grants.Neither procedure allows for cancellation of grants based upon the host institution’s failure to combat antisemitism, and each lays out a number of steps and the requirement for hearings and rebuttals – none of which preceded the termination order.Burroughs ruled that the government cannot use fighting antisemitism, however “laudable and important” that goal is “to change the course of decades of federal funding for critical research without providing a reasoned explanation as to how the agency determined that freezing funding would advance that goal, or, in other words, help combat antisemitism.“Defendants have not pointed to a single document, nor has the Court’s review of the record located one, that indicates that they weighed the value of the research funded by a particular grant against the goal of combating antisemitism at Harvard. Several documents, in fact, indicated that such a weighing likely did not occur before the assault on Harvard began or even by the time of the eventual Termination Letters.”Burroughs noted that the government did not take into account various actions Harvard took to combat antisemitism on campus before the 11 April letter that announced the termination of the research grants.Peter McDonough, the general counsel for the American Council on Education (ACE), also welcomed the decision.In a statement made available toUniversity World News, he said that the “federal court affirmed what we always knew to be true: the Trump administration has ignored the law in pursuing politically motivated attacks on Harvard and other institutions” – and noted that the ACE’samicus curiaebrief, filed on behalf of 28 higher education associations, was reflected in the judge’s finding about “antisemitism [being] used as a smokescreen for a targeted, ideologically motivated assault on [the] country’s premier universities”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ZEALANDReport warns of fundamental weakness in university systemJohn Gerritsen05 September 2025TweetA top-level report has warned of fundamental weakness in New Zealand’s university system, questioned whether nursing and other caring professions belong in universities, and queried the value of the sector’s ambitious building projects.The government-convened University Advisory Group’s long-awaited report urged the government to start treating the eight universities as a system and warned that increased funding would be needed.The report said the universities did a good job but faced “significant challenges and uncertainties”.‘Support greater differentiation’“There is a strong rationale to treat the eight universities as a system and to support greater differentiation between them.“The lack of effective policy consideration of the universities as a system operating in the national interest is a fundamental weakness,” it stated.The report recommended the creation of a national strategy to fund and administer universities as a distinct system under a Higher Education Council, which should be established as the “highest priority”.But Universities Minister Dr Shane Reti said that would be too expensive and complicated.Instead, the government would create a University Strategy Group to guide policy and encourage the universities to differentiate from one another at the postgraduate level.Reti said he would chair the group, and its members would include representatives from universities and from the Education Ministry and funding body, the Tertiary Education Commission.He said the government would develop a new Tertiary Education Strategy aimed at lifting student achievement, maximising economic impact and innovation, and boosting international enrolments.Reti said the government would also simplify the NZ$315 million-a-year (US$185.724 million) Performance-Based Research Fund by replacing a six-yearly assessment of individual academics’ research with a metrics-based system.“These changes are about working together, simplifying where it makes sense, and strengthening leadership and quality,” Reti said.“Collectively, they will make sure our universities continue to deliver opportunities for students, impact for businesses, and growth for New Zealand’s economy.”Underfunding ‘not addressed’The Tertiary Education Union said the government’s responses were tweaks that did not address the core problem of underfunding.Sandra Grey, national secretary, said the union agreed with the need for a more coordinated and strategic approach to the university system as a whole.“It’s sensible and appropriate for a country and economy our size to encourage collaboration and coordination instead of competition and duplication,” she said.“However, it’s disappointing that the proposed ‘University Strategy Group’ is unlikely to have any educators at the decision-making table, and we are once again troubled by an initiative from this government that, like too many others, places more power in the hands of a minister.”The chair of Universities New Zealand, Lincoln University Vice-Chancellor Grant Edwards, said the sector needed more funding.“Better strategies and different measures will not be enough by themselves to help universities deliver the teaching and research that underpins economic growth and prosperity for this country,” he said.“We are continuing to find funding challenging, in particular funding for the numbers of domestic students who want to attend our universities.“Since 2018, during a period when inflation has risen 29%, funding subsidies for teaching have increased only 18%, and funding for research has not increased at all, meaning a real decline of 29% in the activity that the government agreed was core to economic growth with the release of the Science System Advisory Group Review report earlier this year.”New Zealand Association of Scientists co-president, Troy Baisden, said there would be little real change for universities.“The problem is that the success of our universities is the product of the many successes of individual researchers and fields of study. This reality has been buried in efforts not to disrupt the ministry and institutions, so actions being taken up by the government will bury the strategic recommendations that would have led to major improvements,” he said.Call to raise entry standardsThe University Advisory Group report said funding for New Zealand’s universities encouraged them to compete for students, rather than strive to provide the best quality education.It suggested universities could raise their entry standards.“One relatively compelling argument is that the easiest way for universities to advance their standards and reputation is to limit their entry.“The higher the entry standard, the more likely that the university’s reputation is enhanced, with flow-on effects for the marketability of graduates, the retention of high-quality faculty, and research intensity,” it said.The report said universities should continue to provide a broad range of courses, but at the graduate level differentiation “becomes essential”.The report noted: “Quality is more likely to be assured where a critical mass of academics and researchers are co-located.”It stated that the lack of strategic coordination between the eight universities was causing problems.“The relative lack of strategic oversight at the national level has led to some less-than-optimal outcomes when viewed by the national lens rather than by institutions.“For example, there is little logic in two universities in Auckland offering professional entry-level courses in physiotherapy, which is an intensive practicum-based programme. Similarly, there are no compelling reasons for Wellington to have two expensive campuses of two different universities offering numerous overlapping courses,” it noted.National approach on AI neededThe report called for a national approach to the use of Artificial Intelligence in universities.“It needs to be recognised that while the future is not yet clear, the probability is that AI will drastically change the shape and role of many educational institutions, including universities. Strategic oversight will be needed to ensure that government and institutional governance [are] able and ready to adapt as circumstances demand,” it said.The report said that some qualifications did not belong in a university, observing that some appeared to have been developed in order to access higher levels of government subsidy.“There needs to be closer examination of the qualification paths for several vocations, especially those where there is a large workforce requirement.“For example, what is the role of university versus polytechnic training for nurses? Several caring professions and their professional bodies are requiring university graduates when shorter forms of education may be more appropriate,” it noted.The group said universities should be free to address concerns about educational disadvantage, including ensuring representation on an ethnic, rural or disability basis.It also queried the wisdom of building programmes “that have comparatively high construction costs arising from their generous architecture”.“In the current and future climate of tight economic conditions, there are opportunities to better coordinate university capital programmes within a national strategy for investment in New Zealand’s university system.“The combined university capital plans currently indicate a desire to invest over NZ$8 billion over the next decade. This figure alone suggests the need for greater coordination and strategic clarity,” it stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank FacultyDENMARKMinister to close masters student ‘backdoor’ to Danish jobsJan Petter Myklebust05 September 2025TweetDanish Minister of Higher Education and Science Christina Egelund is considering several measures to address a spike in the number of international masters students entering the country from two Asian countries over the past five years, a development characterised by various media outlets as a “backdoor into the Danish labour market”.These include raising the cost of tuition fees and curbs on access for spouses.Egelund was grilled for more than an hour on the issue – particularly as it related to students from Bangladesh – during afilmed parliamentary education committeeconsultation on 2 September, fielding questions from representatives of the Danish People’s Party (DF), the Danish Democrats and the Liberal Alliance.According toOmnibus, the official newspaper of Aarhus University (AU) in Denmark, Bangladeshi students are now the largest group of international students at AU.According to online newspaperPio, concerns that students from Bangladesh as well as Nepal were primarily looking to gain access to the Danish labour market, rather than to study, were confirmed by apreliminary studyconducted by the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science, which sought input from all Danish universities and was published at the end of August 2025.Relatively low pass rateAccording toOmnibus, feedback from universities contained in the report show the pass rate is between 55 and 65% for Bangladeshi students at AU, while it is around 90% for other nationalities. Students from Bangladesh at AU had a first-year dropout rate of 13% in 2023 and 2024, compared with 4% for other international students.In separate news reports,Berlingskereported it had uncovered through access to documents that several universities warned the Danish Agency for Higher Education and Science as early as the beginning of 2023 that the number of students from Bangladesh and Nepal had almost exploded.In their questions during this week’s consultation, Mikkel Bjørn (DF) and Sandra Elisabeth Skalvig (Liberal Alliance) asked how Denmark had been left “wide open as a gateway for a massive influx of students – as well as their spouses and children – from the predominantly Muslim country of Bangladesh in recent years” when it was “blatantly clear” that the student visa scheme was being “systematically exploited as a migration channel into the Danish welfare society”.Bjørn also asked the minister to respond to concerns raised by universities themselves about “linguistic and academic deficiencies among these foreign students” – concerns that had not been addressed by the government.Bjørn asked the minister to explain why the government accepts that agencies in both Odense in Denmark and Dhaka in Bangladesh openly advertise Denmark as the “easiest path to a new life in Europe – and to the Danish labour market”.While not directly addressing the issue of when she became aware of the influx of students with suspect motives, Egelund said she received concrete facts about the problem in “early summer 2025”.She said preliminary numbers from this year’s admission indicate that in spite of the increasing numbers of applications, there has been a fall of 29% in the number of admitted students from Bangladesh at Danish universities.“This demonstrates that universities now are aware of the challenges and are working actively to solve it. With the additional measures we are introducing from the government, I am sure that we, with our joint efforts, are going to solve this problem,” she said.An attractive destinationEgelund said giving students the right to work and study, the lower financial costs associated with degrees in Denmark, and the fact that students could bring a spouse with them all contributed towards making Denmark an attractive destination for applicants from Bangladesh and Nepal compared with other European countries.She said that corrective measures would be implemented for the 2026 intake of students. However, these measures would affect all international students and not only those from countries considered to be problematic.Egelund said she will consider taking action on several fronts. These include raising the costs of studies; increasing the upfront payment required to secure a longer-term visa; limiting access for spouses; improving the methods used to assess qualifications, including English skills; and greater coordination between the country’s eight universities on masters-level intake.Student union reservationsChristoffer Rosenkvist, chair of the National Union of Students in Denmark, toldUniversity World Newshis union welcomed political action on the issue but had some reservations about the proposed solutions.“We have closely followed both the coverage in the Danish media of the recent rise in applications from students from Bangladesh and Nepal to Danish higher education institutions, as well as the consultation meeting with the minister about the issue.“We are deeply critical of the fact that recruitment agencies advertise Danish study visas as a way to access the Danish labour market and the fact that Bangladeshi students have been exploited in the process.”Rosenkvist said his union was “troubled by the practice of social dumping in Denmark, where it can be argued that gig-economy delivery platforms and employers offering low-paying service jobs that are not covered by collective-bargaining agreements profit off of Bangladeshi and Nepalese students in precarious situations”.He said the gap between the linguistic and academic requirements of international masters-programmes and the academic level of certain students must be addressed.Concerns over fee increases“However, we are also negatively disposed to several of the changes proposed by the minister,” he said.“We are particularly worried about the proposal to raise tuition fees for non-EU/EEA students. In our view, international students and the internationalisation of our higher education [are] a huge asset whose value cannot be understated.“The only logical consequence of a raise in tuition fees across the board will be that Danish higher education will become far less accessible for international students from less privileged backgrounds than it already is.“Furthermore, we reject the proposals of certain political parties to blacklist students from certain non-EU/EEA countries from applying to Danish higher education institutions.”Rosenkvist suggested instead that the minister and politicians work with the higher education institutions and students to create a system of admission that ensures that only the most motivated and qualified international applicants are admitted.“In any case, if changes to the admissions system are implemented, it is essential that the universities and higher education institutions receive sufficient funding to ensure high quality in the administration of the changes, such that students’ and applicants’ rights are always respected,” Rosenkvist said.Earlier this year,University World Newsreportedthat the Bangladesh Association of Students and Alumni (BASAD) in Denmark was “deeply concerned by the tone and framing” of the media coverage of reports of increasing numbers of students coming to Denmark from Bangladesh.Receive email universities in GazaMichael Gardner05 September 2025TweetThe Alexander von Humboldt Foundation (AvH) and the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD) have pledged their support in restoring universities in Gaza after the war.Meanwhile, the German government has rejected a proposal by the European Commission to partially suspend cooperation with Israel in the context of the EU’s Horizon Europe programme.In their joint statement, AvH President Robert Schlögl and DAAD President Joybrato Mukherjee call for determined steps to address the humanitarian crisis in Gaza but also stress that they are “deeply concerned about the long-term consequences of the war for the region, particularly with regard to education and science”.Schlögl and Mukherjee state that the Palestinian territories need prospects for the future and better educational opportunities for the younger generation.“Following the urgently needed end to the war, we in Germany must actively support the reconstruction of Gaza, especially of schools and universities as well as the research infrastructure,” they said.Academy of Sciences appealThe two science organisations support an appeal from the President of the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities, David Harel, to end hunger and war in Gaza and to free the Hamas hostages through negotiations.Harel warned in his appeal, issued in July, that measures to be taken by the European Union could lead to Israel being excluded from the Horizon Europe programme because of its not complying with clauses relating to human rights.Harel noted that this would severely damage Israeli research, development and innovation budgets as well as its scientific standing in the world.“The State of Israel, which views the sanctity of life as a fundamental value and scientific excellence as a source of national pride, has a moral duty and also a clear security interest in preventing a large-scale health disaster whose consequences will extend for generations,” Harel stressed.His appeal has received the full support of the European Federation of Academies of Sciences and Humanities.The AvH/DAAD statement, the text of which has been adopted by various German universities, emphasises that the two organisations “stand in unwavering solidarity with the state, the people and the scientific community in Israel”.The statement adds: “We therefore continue to clearly oppose calls for a boycott of Israeli scientific organisations, universities and scientists and advocate scientific exchange both with Israeli and with Palestinian universities and scientific institutions.“Scientific cooperation can – despite all conflicts – point the way to a more peaceful future for both peoples. Science is a voice of reason that should be strengthened."Not backing sanctionsMeanwhile, Germany has announced that it is not backing the proposed EU sanctions for the time being.In order to put pressure on Israel to improve the humanitarian situation of people in Gaza, the EU Commission had proposed an immediate partial suspension of the country’s participation in the Horizon Europe research funding programme.Such a measure could have denied Israeli businesses considerable amounts of subsidies. It would, above all, have excluded Israeli institutions from projects run via the European Innovation Council.This measure would have affected companies, for example, in the fields of cyber-security, drones and artificial intelligence, whereas there would have been no impact on Israeli universities and researchers participating in collaborative projects and research activities in the context of the Horizon Europe programme.German Foreign Secretary Johann Wadephul argued that suspending research funding in the context of Horizon Europe would probably not have any impact on Israel’s Gaza campaign.Wadephul said that the German government’s restrictions of arms supplies to Israel were a “far more effective and really necessary measure” which targets Israel’s military engagement, whereas “academic cooperation makes sense”.However, the restrictions do not cover Israel’s navy. Orders here include submarines, including those that can be equipped with nuclear warheads.EU incomprehensionGermany’s stance has met with incomprehension among representatives of some other EU states, with Spain’s foreign minister emphasising that the EU could only maintain its relationship with Israel on the basis of human rights, whereas the EU Commission had confirmed Israel’s massive violations of these rights.EU Representative for Foreign Affairs Kaja Kallas warned that a “fairly mild” commission proposal failing to gain acceptance could leave the EU without a common voice in the international arena.Michael Gardner can be contacted via: michael.gardner@uw-news.comReceive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: India rankings penalise retractions for the first timeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIA‘At risk’ universities point to need for systemic changeGLOBALNew tool aims to deter gaming in global university rankingsINDIAIndia’s ranking framework is at odds with national policyFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredINDIAIndia rankings penalise retractions for the first timeShuriah Niazi05 September 2025TweetIndia’s national university rankings, released by the Ministry of Education this week, for the first time introduced penalties for research paper retractions – the first ranking system, national or global, to do so.Although no specific institutions were publicly named as having been penalised in the latest ranking, the move signals a decisive shift in how India evaluates its universities and colleges, experts said. However, experts closely watching past and present rankings said the impact of the penalties appeared limited in the latest rankings.India’s National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF) 2025, released on 4 September, introduced negative scoring under its research and professional practices parameter if works published under an institutional affiliation were withdrawn from academic journals in the past three years due to plagiarism, data manipulation, or other ethical breaches.Vineet Joshi, secretary of the Department of Higher Education, said: “Noteworthy changes in India Ranking 2025 included the introduction of negative marking for retracted articles. Moreover, the removal of self-citations under research and professional practices, applied across all categories and subject domains last year, was continued this year as well.”Though the exact deductions have not been disclosed, the deduction for 2025 is intentionally modest, according to the ministry, which has described it as “symbolic” this time. It has made it clear penalties will be harsher from 2026, ensuring academic misconduct carries real reputational costs.Negative scoring is intended to signal that rankings in India are not only determined by teaching standards, infrastructure, or research volume, but also by the integrity and credibility of scholarship, officials said.Erratic impactAchal Agrawal ofIndia Research Watch, an independent watchdog, noted that the impact of the newly introduced penalty remains unclear in respect of the 2025 rankings.“Some universities with among the world’s highest retraction rates have still seen their research scores rise, while others with similar records have suffered significant declines,” he toldUniversity World News.“If these institutes, despite high retraction rates, are showing an increase in scores, it suggests the penalty may be too negligible. It probably needs to be scaled up at least 10-fold to make a real impact,” Agrawal said.“Even when we look at Indian universities that figure among the top 100 institutions globally for retraction rates, the average change in their research scores is around 0.32 points – in percentage terms, that is effectively no penalty at all,” he added.Agrawal explained that while some institutions saw their research scores fall by 4-5 points, others, such as Chandigarh University, gained as many as 8-9 points.“The impact is completely erratic. If the penalty were really effective, we should see a clear negative trend for institutions with higher retraction rates. Instead, the average movement is positive,” he said.Comparing India’s approach with global practices, he added: “NIRF is the first ranking system in the world to impose a penalty for retractions. Even QS rankings do not have this. In that sense, India is pioneering.“But abroad, individual cases of misconduct often lead to strong action – professors have lost positions at Harvard and Stanford. In India, even proven plagiarism has rarely led to consequences. So the penalty is a step forward, but it must be enforced more rigorously.”Need for greater transparencyAgrawal stressed the need for greater transparency from the NIRF. “It would be better if NIRF disclosed how much [of a] penalty was applied to each university. Right now, it is confusing for both observers and institutions to understand why some scores have gone up and others down.”The current lack of clarity may stem from the NIRF’s approach of verifying retractions through individual journals rather than relying on a standardised global database, according to Agrawal.“This could explain the erratic results. A free, open-source resource like theRetraction Watchdatabase would be more transparent and reliable, while also helping to avoid accusations of bias,” he said.He underlined that the penalties are intended to create a top-down effect: “If universities are penalised for retractions, they will in turn push their researchers to uphold higher standards —whether avoiding predatory journals, ghost authorship, or data manipulation.“Just as the earlier NIRF’s focus on publication numbers drove more research output, this can drive stronger ethics. But for that, the penalty must be fair, transparent, and strong enough to matter.”Alarming study on research integrityA recent global study raised alarm over the state of research integrity in Indian higher education. The Research Integrity Risk Index (RI²), developed by Professor Lokman Meho of the American University of Beirut,flagged 32 Indian universitiesas being among the most “at risk” institutions worldwide.The RI² index was designed as an early warning tool rather than a punitive measure, according to Meho. It draws on two clear, well-documented indicators: the rate of retractions per 1,000 publications (2022 to 2023) and the share of papers published in journals delisted from Scopus and Web of Science (2023 to 2024).However, the impact of this penalty does not appear to be strongly reflected in this year’s rankings. For instance, Graphic Era University, ranked 79th in India last year, ranked at 72 this year, despite coming first in the RI² index retraction rate ranking.Some universities have even shown significant improvement. Osmania University, Hyderabad, which also featured in the retractions list, rose to 53rd position in the NIRF ranking, from 70th last year.Another “at risk” university, Anna University in Chennai, Tamil Nadu, which was in top position in the NIRF’s State University category first introduced last year, came second in that category this year.Familiar domination of IITsDespite the reform, the overall institutional leaderboard remains familiar, dominated in the overall rankings by Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs), which take seven out of the top 10 places.They also dominate in the engineering institutes and research institutes categories, taking six out of the top 10 places in the latter. IITs almost sweep the field in the new ranking category of ‘innovation’ added this year, which has IITs in nine out of the top 10 positions.A record number of 7,692 unique institutions responded and offered themselves for ranking under ‘overall’, category-specific or domain-specific ranking.In all, 14,163 applications for ranking were made by these 7,692 institutions, including 4,045 in the ‘overall’ category, 1,584 in engineering, and 4,030 in general degree colleges, said a press statement by the Ministry of Education.Apart from the ‘overall’ list, the NIRF produced rankings lists for 16 categories, including engineering, management, medical education, pharmacy, dental, architecture and planning, law, agriculture and research institutions.A new category added this year was sustainability, with institutions assessed on contributions to the United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).This reflects a broader attempt to align Indian higher education with global academic benchmarks that emphasise not only research and teaching but also social responsibility, according to the NIRF.The Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Madras retained its crown as India’s top institute for the seventh consecutive year in the overall category, followed by the Indian Institute of Sciences (IISc) Bengaluru and IIT Bombay. IIT Madras also topped the first SDG ranking list.“Being a topper consistently is a result of a collective, cohesive and focused team effort,” said Professor V Kamakoti, director of IIT Madras, in a statement.In management education, the Indian Institute of Management (IIM) Ahmedabad led the rankings, followed by IIM Bangalore, IIM Kozhikode, IIT Delhi, and IIM Lucknow.The top (non-IIT) universities list was headed by IISc in Bengaluru, Jawaharlal Nehru University (JNU) in New Delhi, Manipal Academy of Higher Education in Manipal, Jamia Millia Islamia in New Delhi and the University of Delhi, with Banaras Hindu University (BHU) in sixth place.JNU and BHU were also in the top 10 overall category, with Jamia Millia Islamia also taking third place in the new SDGs category.The parameters used for ranking broadly cover teaching, learning and resources, research and professional practices, graduation outcome, outreach and inclusivity, and perception (reputation).Receive email Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED KINGDOMHopes rise that UK is edging towards rejoining Erasmus+Nic Mitchell04 September 2025TweetHigher education stakeholders have given a cautious welcome to an announcement by the United Kingdom government that it is exploring options to rejoin the Erasmus+ student mobility scheme before the end of the current European Union programme, which runs until the beginning of 2028.Aformal noticeon the Department for Education (DfE) website said it was looking for potential contractors interested in helping it “understand how we may structure our requirements and develop our strategy” following the EU-UK summit in May 2025, at which the British government “agreed to work towards association to the Erasmus+ Programme”.However, there are still plenty of hurdles to overcome, such as the price the UK is willing to pay the European Commission for the privilege of rejoining the world's premier student and youth mobility programme.After a burst of excitement at the news, first reported by theFinancial Timeson 30 August 2025, that the British government appeared to be “laying groundwork” to rejoin the EU’s flagship student exchange programme by advertising for interest in a potential future contract worth up to £24 million (US$32 million), a UK government spokesperson toldUniversity World News: “We are not going to pre-empt our ongoing conversations with the EU regarding summit outcomes, including discussions regarding association to Erasmus+.”Implications for TuringThe spokesperson went on to stress that “any deal with the EU would not include paying into the overall EU budget”, and they would need to consider the implications for other programmes, such as the UK’s Turing Scheme, which was set up as an alternative to fund young British people studying or working abroad after the UK left Erasmus+ in 2020 and covers mobilities outside, as well as inside, the EU.The year-old UK Labour government hasalready pledged to continue fundingthe Turing programme for 2025 to 2026, albeit with a slimmed-down budget of £78 million, to pay towards travel and living costs and organisational support for the Turing student and young person's mobility scheme.The Turing Schemeis limited to supporting outward mobility from the UK. In contrast, Erasmus+ also paid for reciprocal arrangements and funded both inward and outward mobility of young people between the UK and EU before the Boris Johnson government decided to leave the EU scheme as part of the EU withdrawal agreement.National agency roleThe latest bout of excitement about the possibility of the UK rejoining Erasmus+ followed the DfE holding a ‘virtual supplier event’ on 27 August 2027 to outline the role that a national agency would play should negotiations with the EU be successfully concluded.Before the UK left the EU, the British Council acted as the UK national agency for Erasmus+, but asUniversity World Newsreportedin December 2021, there was dismay when the Conservative government handed the contract to run the alternative Turing scheme to private outsourcing company Capita.It is unclear whether the British Council would be considered as a frontrunner to become the national agency again, as theFinancial Timestalked about the government looking for a private contractor.However, the actual DfE notice says it is “keen to maximise opportunities to participate in procurement for Small to Medium-sized Enterprises and Voluntary, Community and Social Enterprises”. It went on to say: “Consideration will also be given to consortia and collaborative bids from all forms of organisations.”The British Council was outbid on price – as well as politics – to run theTuring Schemeby the Johnson Tory government in 2021.Since then Labour has taken over the UK government. However, it is in the middle of trying to cut government spending.A British Council insider toldUniversity World Newsthat they are interested in being the national agency again, but they understand the DfE is in the early stages of running a procurement exercise, and part of that includes working through definitions of scope, including the eligibility of organisations to bid.Positive reactionReaction from the higher education sector in both the UK and EU is positive about the chances of the UK rejoining Erasmus+, which also funds a number of EU initiatives such as the European University Alliances.Anne Corbett, a senior associate with LSE Consulting at the London School of Economics and Political Science, said despite the somewhat “Kafkaesque official statement” and “proceduralist” approach by the UK government, she thought it unlikely that the government would say it is looking for a bidder to run the Erasmus+ programme unless there is already a draft UK-EU agreement in the pipeline.Corbett, who is a close watcher and commentator on Britain’s relations with the EU, toldUniversity World Newsit was promising that the UK government was looking for an agency to run the UK’s participation for the last 18 months of the current Erasmus+ programme, rather than waiting to join the new version of Erasmus+ coming on stream in 2028.“The UK rejoined the EU’s Horizon Europe research and innovation programme partway into the current European Union Multi-annual Financial Framework (MFF). So, not unprecedented,” she said.However, Corbett noted that different parts of the Labour cabinet, such as the Treasury, might be overly cautious in EU negotiations about spending more on Erasmus than they are currently paying for the Turing outward mobility scheme, which had a smaller budget this year than under the Tories.Cross-party supportCorbett claimed there was overwhelming cross-party support for the UK rejoining Erasmus+, despite Labour appearing to downplay chances of creating a youth mobility scheme in the past, asUniversity World Newsreportedin May 2025.Jamie Arrowsmith, director of Universities UK International, toldUniversity World News: “It’s positive to see the government commit to exploring re-association to Erasmus+, subject to a fair financial agreement.“The scheme offers a broad range of opportunities for learners and staff across the education, training, youth, and sport sectors.”Thomas Jørgensen, director of policy coordination and foresight at the European University Association (EUA), toldUniversity World News: “This is certainly good news; there would be a lot to say in favour of full association of the UK to the programme.“We might be on the last leg of the current version of Erasmus, but projects selected within the next two years will become common activities until the end of the decade, so it is by no means too late.“Moreover, the proposal for the next Erasmus programme contains provisions similar to the last one, and the Trade and Cooperation Agreement foresees that programmes can be continued from one generation to the next: any timing is good timing.”Money mattersJørgensen noted: “On the money, we have seen that particularly when it comes to cooperation between institutions to share and develop good practice, you can go very far with fairly moderate amounts. There is a lot of value to be had from a relatively small investment.”However, amidst all the positive signals, one international higher education professional based at a leading British university (who asked not to be named) toldUniversity World News: “It is difficult to see in the current climate around immigration and budgetary constraints (in the UK) that rejoining Erasmus+ would be taken forward before the end of the current EU programme.“I'm not convinced that the UK will be able to or want to commit to funding this fully, and there also remain questions about how being back in Erasmus+ would impact non-EU placements alongside Turing, if that is being retained.“Despite reservations about Turing, it has played a huge role in supporting widening participation of students, particularly for non-EU placements.”Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.cn_summary: 英國政府探索重新加入Erasmus+計劃的可能性，為高等教育界帶來希望。根據教育部的公告，英國正尋求合約商協助制定策略，以便在2028年前重新參與該學生流動計劃。雖然目前面臨價格和預算等挑戰，但教育界對此表示積極反應，認為這將為學生提供更多的學習和交流機會。Turing計劃的預算雖然繼續支持英國學生的國外流動，但Erasmus+的雙向流動特性仍然被視為重要優勢。專家呼籲政府在與歐盟的談判中保持開放態度，尋求公平的財務協議。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Police criticised for response to protests near campusesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDONESIAStudents alarmed, feel ‘threatened’ by army’s campus visitsINDONESIAStudents protest military bill, condemn lack of transparencyINDONESIAStudent protests against government policies and budget cutsINDONESIAStudent pressure helps to thwart political ‘manoeuvring’INDONESIASteep tuition fee hikes ‘reversed’ after student backlashINDONESIAProtesting students urge clarity on presidential term bidFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackINDONESIAPolice criticised for response to protests near campusesKafil Yamin03 September 2025TweetPolice used tear gas on crowds of protesters near two universities in Bandung, the capital of Indonesia’s West Java province, this week, according to authorities and student groups, amid ongoing nationwide protests over government spending. Around 10 people have been killed since the unrest erupted last week.On Monday 1 September, police fired tear gas near the campuses of the Islamic University of Bandung (UNISBA) and nearby Pasundan University.According to police official Hendra Rochmawan, the authorities did not enter either campus but tried to break up crowds of non-student protesters who had been seeking protection within the university grounds as crowds blocked the roads in the area.Nevertheless, the UNISBA student body accused security forces of seeking to silence dissent, saying they “brutally attacked” the campus, noting the tear gas had caused breathing problems for some students.UNISBA Rector Harits Nu’man denied the chaos was caused by students,Antaranews agency reported, and students and others have condemned the actions of the police. The Student Executive Body (BEM) of the University of Indonesia (UI), in a statement on Tuesday 2 September deplored what it called “police repressive action”.“We condemn the security forces’ brutal attack on the Bandung Islamic University and Pasundan University campuses. The attack is a gross violation of democracy and human rights as guaranteed by our constitution,” said the statement as read out by BEM UI chairperson Atan Zayid.Indonesia’s Minister of Higher Education, Science and Technological Innovation Brian Yuliarto also condemned the actions of the police, saying in a written statement on 2 September they could “be interpreted as an attack on the campus’ safe space” and expressing his regret over the incidents.“Student demonstrations are a peaceful movement to monitor the government's work. Anarchy is not in their DNA. Therefore, the academic realm must be protected from repressive actions and infiltration by outside parties,” he said.He added his ministry was committed to safeguarding universities as safe academic spaces. Campuses must remain free from repressive measures, with dialogue and persuasive approaches prioritised, he said.Yuliarto said medical and psychological assistance would be provided if needed, and coordination with security forces would be enhanced to protect universities. A ministry team would also liaise with university leaders to assess the impact of recent unrest on students, staff and facilities, he said.Student rallyThe countrywide unrest, which has seen over 1,200 rioters arrested and extensive damage to infrastructure, particularly in the capital Jakarta, began after students held a rally on 28 August in front of the House of Representatives building in Jakarta to demand a halt to lawmakers’ rising allowances. The rally turned violent when police dispersed demonstrators trying to enter the gate of the parliament building.The protests escalated into chaos after police fired tear gas, turning nearby roads into smoke-filled zones. As seen on television, a police car suddenly stormed into the crowd of demonstrators and hit a man known to be anojeg(motorcycle taxi delivery service) driver. As the man fell, the police car ran over him.Theojegdriver, later named as Affan Kurniawan, was delivering food when he was caught up in the confrontation between students and police. His death, which gained wide public sympathy around the country, added to the intensity of the protests, which then spread to non-student groups.An angry mob late on Friday 29 August set fire to a local parliament building in Makassar in Sulawesi, trapping several people in the blaze, which left three dead and five others hospitalised. In a separate riot in the city, a man who was thought to be police intelligence disguised as a delivery rider was beaten to death by a mob.Authorities in Yogyakarta said a university student was killed in Friday’s clashes between rock-throwing demonstrators and riot police.Thousands of UI students staged a protest at the Jakarta Regional Police headquarters on 29 August in solidarity with theojegdriver. The same day, the university announced all academic activities would be shifted online till 4 September.UI emphasised that the scaled-back work arrangements for staff and others were solely to ensure the safety of the academic community amid a perceived risky situation.A circular signed by UI Rector Heri Hamansyah said: “Practical activities, laboratory usage, and other activities that cannot be conducted online will be rescheduled, taking into account the principles of safety and security.”Sigit Rochadi, a sociology expert at the National University (Unas), did not believe rioting and alleged looting had been committed by students and workers, who, he said, only wanted their voices to be heard.“Rioting, looting must have been committed by non-students,” Sigit toldUniversity World Newson 2 September.“The demonstration included only two main groups: students and workers. The students demand the passing of the confiscation of corruption wealth bill into law soon and a halt to rises in lawmakers’ lavish allowances,” he added, referring to a proposed law on asset forfeiture, which covers proceeds from corruption.Student concernsStudents called for the dissolution of the House of Representatives after media reports in late August revealed all 580 lawmakers have been receiving a monthly housing allowance of IDR50 million (US$3,075) since September 2024 in addition to their monthly salaries, which have surged to around IDR120 million (US$7,300) and comprise housing and a string of other generous perks, from honoraria to family and rice allowances, as well as taxes.These allowances were raised amid mass layoffs in the country, rising taxes and strict austerity measures imposed by the government of President Prabowo Subianto.The “budget efficiency” measures, as Prabowo dubbed them, include cuts to public works, health and education. Critics said the new allowance is not only excessive but also insensitive at a time when most people are grappling with soaring living costs and taxes, rising unemployment and weakened purchasing power.Protests intensified after Ahmad Sahroni, a member of the House of Representatives (DPR) from the National Democratic Party (Nasdem), asserted: “Those who demand the House be dissolved must be the dumbest people on the planet.”Sigit, who observed the Jakarta demonstration, said: “While the students are pressing for a halt to House member allowance hikes and immediate passing of the asset forfeiture law, the workers are demanding a 8.5% wage hike and the removal of outsourcing practices” – a reference to a system which places the agents that provide workers to companies, rather than the companies themselves, in the position of employer, with a consequent loss of rights and benefits for workers.He noted students’ demands were genuine and were made to the House peacefully. “The riot happened after they left the House building.”During a meeting with the House of Representatives in Jakarta on 2 September, student representatives from the University of Indonesia (UI) and Trisakti University in Jakarta said they had nothing to do with the rioting and looting. “It did not cross our minds to voice our demands through anarchy,” a student spokesperson from Trisakti University said.Rector of IPB University Professor Arif Satria said in astatement: “We respect the freedom of every citizen to express their opinions and aspirations as guaranteed by the constitution, and we ask the Indonesian House of Representatives and the government to be more sensitive and empathetic to the problems of the people, to prioritise dialogue and deliberation in finding solutions to various national issues, particularly those related to poverty, unemployment, food security, economic inequality, investment certainty, social injustice, agrarian conflicts, and corruption …”.Authorities detained 1,240 rioters afterfive days of protestsin Jakarta, which caused losses of up to IDR55 billion (US$3.3 million) as rioters burnt buses and subway shelters and damaged other infrastructure, said Jakarta Governor Pramono Anung.Flanked by leaders of eight Indonesian political parties, Prabowo told a news conference in Jakarta on 31 August the government would cut lawmakers’ perks and privileges, including the controversial housing allowance, and suspend overseas trips for members of parliament.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 印尼警方因在校園附近的抗議活動中使用催淚瓦斯而受到批評。事件發生在萬丹省的兩所大學附近，抗議者主要針對政府的開支政策。據報導，自全國抗議活動爆發以來，已有10人遇害。學生團體指責警方試圖鎮壓異議聲音，並強調催淚瓦斯對學生造成的健康影響。高等教育部長也譴責這一行為，強調應保護校園的安全空間，並承諾提供必要的醫療和心理支持。此次抗議活動反映了學生和工人對政府官員津貼上漲的強烈不滿，並要求終止不當的政策。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities tap into demand for Mandarin-taught degrees
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities tap into demand for Mandarin-taught degreesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksASIACan Asia become a ‘sanctuary’ for global student talent?ASIAMixed results show need to strengthen internationalisationCHINAStudy abroad students no longer just the elite – ReportFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSINGAPOREUniversities tap into demand for Mandarin-taught degreesYojana Sharma03 September 2025TweetSingapore’s universities have long been a magnet for international students, with their high-quality degrees taught in English. Now, as large numbers of Chinese students seek to study nearer to home, several universities are launching new programmes taught in Mandarin Chinese to attract students able to pay higher fees.The move has sparked concerns from students and academics that it could skew the international student profile to the detriment of a more diverse student population or that students on these programmes may not be as well integrated into the student body as other international students in Singapore.Nanyang Technological University (NTU), Singapore Management University (SMU), Singapore University of Technology and Design (SUTD), and Singapore University of Social Sciences (SUSS) are among those launching new programmes taught in Mandarin Chinese.NTU’s Nanyang Business School (NBS), which has 10 existing postgraduate programmes in English, is offering a new Executive Master of Business Administration (EMBA) programme starting this October called the Nanyang EMBA (Chinese).In a statement toUniversity World News, NTU’s vice president (lifelong learning and alumni engagement), Professor Boh Wai Fong, said this was in “response to the growing interest for Chinese management programmes among local and regional business leaders”.The Nanyang EMBA (Chinese) focuses on “cutting-edge business and technological innovation”, he said. “Beyond classroom learning, the programme includes dialogues and seminars with leading industry partners and academics, innovative laboratory visits, and cross-cultural exchanges.”NTU and China’s Shanghai Jiao Tong University (SJTU) also jointly offer the Nanyang-SJTU Executive Master of Business Administration (Chinese) programme. About 50 students graduate from this programme each year, Boh said.‘First foray’SMU is also preparing to launch its first Chinese-taught programme, an EMBA, beginning in January.“It’s our first foray into [Chinese-taught programmes], although we’ve had joint programmes with Chinese universities which we’ve taught on and which have a Chinese component,” SMU president Lily Kong toldUniversity World News.She said in the joint programmes “where we have taught in English, we’ve had translators in the classroom,” rather than teaching directly in Chinese.Kong added the new development was partly for pragmatic reasons, recognising the “very large market looking for higher education from China”. To be effective in delivering such education using a Chinese language was important, she said.“Does it mean that we will eventually offer programmes in Japanese, or Spanish, and in European languages, etcetera? Highly unlikely, because we don’t have a comparative advantage in these areas,” Kong said, adding: “Singapore, being a largely Chinese population, means that many of us have at least some facility with the Mandarin language.”Nonetheless, she emphasised the move towards Chinese-taught programmes was limited. “We don't see ourselves becoming an equally balanced English language-Chinese language university. We see ourselves as offering some select programmes on the margins in Chinese.”Experts noted overseas EMBAs are highly popular among Chinese, who see them not just in terms of education but as opportunities for networking and building up overseas contacts for their future businesses. For all of this, they are willing to pay high fees.The UK’sFinancial Timesnewspaper, which publishes an annual global EMBA ranking, noted recently that business schools in East Asia were emerging as leading global providers of EMBAs, with very strong demand from China.In an analysis of its 2024 EMBA rankings, it found that the share of EMBA alumni who created their own business startups was increasing. One academic in Singapore pointed out that recent US tariffs have hit the region hard, increasing demand for EMBAs as a means to change employment or self-employment prospects.Changing demographicOne of the advantages of the Chinese-taught programmes is they do not require TOEFL or IELTS English proficiency exams. But this has sparked heated debate via social media in Singapore.“English is the language of business and commerce in Singapore. Why are we pandering to PRC [People’s Republic of China] students?” asked one netizen.Another wrote: “If the Unis want to start teaching in Mandarin, then I believe they should also start teaching courses in Malay and Tamil too, as these languages are our [Singapore’s] national language(s).”But Chinese-taught programmes also cater for a different Chinese demographic than those who mainly opt for study abroad in Western countries.Jason Tan, associate professor at NTU’s National Institute of Education, Policy, Curriculum and Leadership, told Singapore’sCNA938radio show: “The demographic of Chinese families who wish to send their children overseas to study has been changing gradually over time.“In other words, the choice of studying overseas is no longer a privilege only for richer people. We're getting a much broader spectrum now of families in China who are thinking of a postgraduate overseas degree for their children.”He added: “Now we are seeing a wider range of students, and some of these students, not all, of course, may find language barriers in terms of accessing a postgraduate degree in what are traditionally English-speaking countries.”Need for diversitySMU’s Kong pointed out that “because we're targeting our Mandarin-speaking programme at the EMBA level, [and] we're not doing undergraduate Mandarin programmes and so forth, the numbers are actually relatively small.”But she acknowledged that the cultural diversity of international students coming to Singapore was important. “We’re only looking at 50 students [for the EMBA]. But for all our other programmes, we’re very mindful of this [need for diversity],” she said.While SMU gets a lot of applications from China, she said: “We have been stepping up our recruitment from Southeast Asia, and this year we've seen many more Indonesian students and Vietnamese students coming to us than was the case in the past. And we're really happy with that.”“It may not necessarily be a case of needing to reduce the numbers from any one particular country, but about increasing the numbers from other countries.”“We want to do more with Southeast Asia, this being our backyard. And if our neighbouring countries do well, the rising tide raises all boats.”She also pointed to India, another big student market for Singapore’s English-taught degrees.Explaining NTU’s approach to diversity, Boh said: “To bring together NTU’s diverse community, NTU organises various events and celebrations throughout the year for students, staff and faculty from over 80 countries. These include the [Singapore] National Day Observance ceremony, the (Chinese) Mid-Autumn Festival, Lunar New Year, a mass Iftar session during Ramadan, and [Hindu festival] Deepavali.”NTU has around 250 enrolled in the Chinese-taught executive masters and masters programmes in managerial economics and public administration at the Nanyang Centre for Public Administration.In addition, the NTU Entrepreneurship Academy offers a Master of Science in Technopreneurship and Innovation programme in English (MSc TIP-English) and in Chinese (MSc TIP-Chinese). The Chinese-taught programme, which was launched in 2005, has about 50 students on average per academic year.“We continue to see strong interest in postgraduate courses from Chinese-speaking applicants, including those from Singapore and China,” Boh said.NTU’s only undergraduate courses taught in Chinese are within its Bachelor of Chinese Medicine degree.Chinese-speaking facultyTan noted local universities need to have thought through the logistical issues, such as having enough Chinese-speaking faculty, before launching these programmes, as well as the “larger task of getting these students to move beyond their classmates in that programme and to mix around with students from other countries as well as Singaporean students”.Although a proportion of faculty members in Singapore’s government-funded universities have conversational Chinese, some of them admit that teaching subject-specific material in Mandarin would be “a stretch”, which may limit the expansion of Chinese-taught programmes, particularly in non-STEM disciplines.Kong is frank about this: “I use myself as an example. Conversational Mandarin, yes. Technical, professional [Mandarin] – I’ll need some help with that.”However, she noted: “I have done it before in my own discipline, when I've gone to China. I've actually been able to deliver a Mandarin lecture off the cuff if I know my work well enough.”The reality, she emphasised, was that at her university there were already people “in our midst” who were not native Singaporeans and could also deliver programmes in Chinese. “They may be mainland Chinese or may even have naturalised to Singapore citizenship or hold US citizenship or whatever else. But they're living and working in Singapore, and they have that [Mandarin language] ability.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 新加坡大學推出中文授課學位以吸引國際學生 隨著大量中國學生希望在家鄉附近學習，新加坡的幾所大學開始推出中文授課的課程，以滿足需求。南洋理工大學、香港中文大學、以及新加坡管理大學等均計劃在2025年推出新的中文授課項目，包括高管工商管理碩士（EMBA）課程。這一舉措引發了對學校國際學生多樣性影響的擔憂，因為中文授課可能導致學生群體的單一化。然而，專家指出，這些課程不再僅限於富裕家庭，也能吸引更廣泛的學生群體。大學強調在提供中文課程的同時，仍會重視文化多樣性和其他語言的課程。
