Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackHONG KONGNon-local student influx ramps up accommodation pressureYojana Sharma01 September 2025TweetA recent major influx of international students to Hong Kong – mostly from mainland China – has led to new policies to house them, reversing previous policy that restricted non-local student numbers due to the densely populated city’s chronic housing and land shortages.Hong Kong’s universities are also expanding academic departments and setting up new centres of research to align with Beijing’s research policies, with universities recently allowed to acquire and repurpose commercial buildings in Hong Kong to facilitate this.Hong Kong is seeing some success in its drive to attract more international academics to teach at the city’s top universities. It comes after a sustainedexodus, spanning pandemic and post-pandemic years, of both local students and academics.Accommodating incoming students has become a “number one topic” at education conferences in Hong Kong, according to Ryan Allen, associate professor of international education at Soka University of America in the United States, who runs theCollege Townsnewsletter on urbanism and higher education.“It is all about how to accommodate international students, but when they say ‘international students’ they mean Chinese that are coming over [from the mainland], and so many of these are coming for university,” he toldUniversity World Newsafter a recent visit to Hong Kong.While accommodation challenges are common in other countries – notably Canada and Australia, which have used student accommodation shortages as a reason to restrict numbers of international students – Allen noted that “in Hong Kong there is just so little space, especially around the universities, for new construction”.“It's not that simple for a university, especially in a place that is, per square foot, the most expensive real estate on the planet.”Swift rise in student numbersThe rise in numbers of students has been swift, mainly over the past year, but the number of academics and researchers is also rising.Hong Kong Education Secretary Christine Choi Yuk-lin said in April that “world-renowned professors from US institutions are relocating to Hong Kong”, adding they were driven by tighter visa policies and geopolitical tensions affecting traditional Western study destinations.Choi recently revealed that applications from non-local students had increased significantly this year, with the numbers almost doubling in some publicly funded institutions. The expansion is part of a government policy unveiled last year to make Hong Kong an international higher education hub.Notably, the Education University of Hong Kong (EduHK) will increase its intake from 2,700 to 5,000 in the coming academic year, which begins this month, September.Mainland Chinese students accounted for 74% of non-local first-year students in the 2024/2025 academic year afterthe cap on non-locals was raisedfrom 20% of the undergraduate student body to 40% from September 2024 for both publicly funded and private universities, sparking considerable expansion in private university admissions as well.According to the education department’s figures, some 8,000 mainland Chinese students were approved to study in Hong Kong in the first quarter of 2025, compared to 3,000 over the same period last year.In July, the University of Hong Kong (HKU) reported a historic high of over 25,000 non-local applications for undergraduate programmes in the coming academic year – 17 times more than available places – including more than 20 international students impacted by sudden shifts in US higher education policies. Of these, 21,000 were from mainland China.The influx has caused prices for rental accommodation to rise by as much as 12% in neighbourhoods near universities, according to Centaline, a major chain of estate agents in the city.The big rise in Hong Kong students tends to be seen in higher-ranked universities in Hong Kong such as HKU, noted Martin Wong, provost of Hong Kong Baptist University (HKBU), a smaller liberal arts college. “But I would say there is some trickle down, so every [university] in Hong Kong will get more [students].”He toldUniversity World Newsmainland student demand at HKBU was particularly high for masters programmes which are not subject to the non-local cap.There is “a lot of room to expand – it can bring in a lot of resources, and demand is high. For example, in Chinese medicine, we are only admitting 15% of applicants. In computer science it is 10%, so there is a lot of room to admit more students.”However, he acknowledged that the shortage of student housing was a constraint.Converting commercial buildingsIn the past, universities were restricted in their increase of non-local student numbers due to the lack of space to expand student dormitories and the very high price of accommodation in Hong Kong’s private rental market.But in a change of policy, Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee last year announced the “Study in Hong Kong” campaign to attract more overseas students, especially those from the Association of Southeast Asian Nations (ASEAN) and other Belt and Road countries.At the same time, he announced the city would increase the supply of student hostels, introducing apilot schemeto allow the conversion of hotels or commercial buildings into student hostels “on a self-financing and privately funded basis”.Universities have been acquiring assets at attractive prices as commercial prices have dropped amid a tourism slump in the city.“It seems the government really wants to attract students to come [to Hong Kong], and they have to settle them, so this is the fastest way to get housing,” said Michelle Cheng Wing Tung, assistant professor at EduHK, who has conducted research on student housing in the city.She toldUniversity World News: “Around 2017-2018 the Hong Kong government spent a lot of money on expanding university housing, but those hostels are still being built, and the target for building all of them is 2027, so it’s a few years from getting all of them done.”She was referring to the government’s plan for an extra 13,470 student hostel places, currently being implemented. But Cheng noted that these plans were targeted towards housing undergraduate students.“Even if they would have [already] been built, they would not be sufficient for the self-financed masters’ students that universities are currently drawing in. So they would still need new spaces.”Growth is likely to continue apace. In early August Choi said the non-local student quota was still not fully reached and that some institutions were not yet ready to increase their intake in terms of staffing and facilities.Choi toldbroadcast mediaon 3 August that the number of non-local students at public universities in 2024-25 accounted for about 23% of student places, still short of the 40% cap.Hotel conversionsCheng said university-endorsed housing was important for international students unfamiliar with the city’s expensive housing market.One approach is to buy a hotel – Hong Kong’s hospitality sector is in the doldrums due to a big drop-off in inbound visitors to Hong Kong after the pandemic – to convert into a student hostel operated by the university, she said.Another approach adopted by most Hong Kong universities is to work with property owners, as “university endorsed housing”.HKU is working with nearby hotels and is in the process of finalising agreements to ensure all first-year non-local undergraduates are provided with student accommodation, Bennett Yim, HKU’s director of undergraduate admissions and international student exchanges, told local media. Yim added HKU would consider cooperating with private developers to build student hostels.“The government’s [non-local] quota expansion has been a crucial factor. Previously, many students hesitated to apply, thinking admission chances were slim due to limited spots,” he was quoted as saying.There are also commercial initiatives. Centaline is converting 10 two- to three-star hotels to provide 3,000 beds in the next three years, the estate agency said.But Cheng warned such commercial conversions may seek to maximise occupancy through subdivided units, sacrificing communal spaces and reducing them to “just a place to sleep” rather than environments supporting student wellbeing and development.Space for expansionSome university campuses in outer areas are looking to set up satellite centres for part-time students and those enrolled in external programmes. Estate agents said this has become possible with Hong Kong office property prices and retail property falling 43% and 35%, respectively, since mid-2019.The School of Business and Management at Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, situated in the scenic outskirts, recently agreed to a purchase in the central commercial area of Admiralty – one of Hong Kong’s most expensive commercial districts. The site, previously occupied by a large and popular restaurant, will be used for teaching.City University of Hong Kong acquired part of a shopping centre in another upmarket commercial district — Tsim Sha Tsui.Lingnan University reportedly paid HK$120 million (US$15.3 million) this year to purchase a shopping arcade, ground floor shops and a car park of the residential tower close to its current campus to convert into classrooms, agents said.Looking across the borderSome universities have more space across the border in China, which could be utilised. For example, the Shenzhen campus of the Chinese University of Hong Kong is now larger than its Hong Kong campus.HKBU’s Wong pointed to HKBU’s campus across the border in Zhuhai, about two hours from Hong Kong, operated jointly with Beijing Normal University, whose mainland students receive a joint China and Hong Kong degree. “The campus is 10 times larger than here [in Hong Kong],” he noted.“The campus here is very crowded. We need to look into models that enable the two campuses to work closely together so that we can take full advantage of the space over there [in China].“When we come to expanding our research programme, we could have labs there, and that would work to integrate the two sides together closely,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New director general for Irish Universities Association
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New director general for Irish Universities AssociationGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksIRELANDIrish government split on increase in HE tuition feesIRELANDGovernment launches bid to lure US researchers to IrelandIRELANDUniversities receive additional funding in giveaway budgetIRELANDUniversities set to run deficit of €15 million in 2024IRELANDUnified tertiary education and research system a ‘game changer’Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyIRELANDNew director general for Irish Universities AssociationJohn Walshe29 August 2025TweetThe outgoing British ambassador to Ireland, Paul Johnson, is the surprise but popular choice as the new director general of the Irish Universities Association (IUA).The high-profile diplomat has long championed the value of higher education. Commenting on his appointment, he said that: “Yeats called education ‘the lighting of a fire’, and I know from my time in this wonderful country how important high-quality education has been to its development and will be to its future. I look forward to playing my part in the exciting and important years ahead.”Born in Edinburgh, Johnson held a number of diplomatic posts before being appointed ambassador to Ireland in September 2020. He quickly endeared himself to the Irish by revealing that Ireland was “high up” on the list to receive surplus UK COVID-19 vaccines. He further strengthened good Irish-UK relations by handling the post-Brexit situation sensitively.In June he announced that he and his wife had fallen in love with Dublin and would remain in Ireland after his ambassadorial term ended.In November he takes up the new post as head of the IUA, which represents the majority of Irish universities. Professor Eeva Leinonen, President of Maynooth University and Chair of IUA, said: “Over his five years in Ireland, Paul not only played a key role during a time of complex geopolitical change but also formed a deep and lasting connection to the people and communities around him. As Paul takes up this new role, he does so at a time of great strategic importance for our universities.”Trump and state funding for HEAlthough Ireland's economy is still very healthy, it is sailing into troubled waters mainly because of the effects of Trump’s tariffs.This is causing concern over future state funding of the universities at a time when applications for places are at a record high. They increased by 7% this year because of rising rolls in secondary schools; the figure also included an increase in the number of applicants from the UK and the EU.The funding challenge will be a major issue for the new director general. The other big challenge he must face is grade inflation in secondary school Leaving Certificate exams, which are used to select applicants for university.Other countries have unwound the special measures taken during COVID, but Ireland is on a very slow ‘glide path’ to pre-pandemic distribution of grades. This year the results are 5.9% higher than they were in 2019 – that’s down from slightly over 7% last year.The universities say they still have difficulties differentiating between very highly qualified applicants for some courses such as medicine. They have to resort to random selection, which is a form of lottery and inevitably leaves some disappointed students.The Vice-Provost of Trinity College Dublin, Professor Orla Sheils, said: “It’s great that we are reducing the ‘uplift’ in Leaving Cert grades, but it is regrettable that the effects of this gradual unwinding will continue for a number of years to come.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Global|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities’ policies mostly silent on gender identity equity
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities’ policies mostly silent on gender identity equityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESInclusive labs: Safe spaces for LGBTQ2S+ STEM studentsUNITED KINGDOMOxford University appoints first LGBTQ+ history professorUNITED STATESSupreme Court allows Jewish university to deny LGBTQ groupFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFRICAUniversities’ policies mostly silent on gender identity equityWachira Kigotho28 August 2025TweetHow do universities in Sub-Saharan Africa address issues of gender identity and sexual orientation within LGBTQ+ student communities? What challenges do these students face, often living in hostile environments and experiencing stigma and discrimination on university campuses across the region?These are some of the questions that Dr Theresia Joakim Kanyopa, a lecturer in educational psychology at the University of the Free State in South Africa, is trying to answer in her contribution to a newbook,Internationalisation of Higher Education for Sustainable Development: A Southern African perspective, which was recently released by Brill, an international publisher of academic books in humanities, social sciences and law.Edited by Kudzayi Savious Tarisayi, an associate professor at the school of mathematics, sciences and technology education at North-West University in South Africa, the book focuses on how multicultural diversity and inclusion in higher education in Sub-Saharan Africa can align with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) to enhance equitable futures in the region.It is against this background that Kanyopa is debating the issue of gender identity and inclusivity in universities in Southern Africa and Sub-Saharan Africa.Ongoing violationsIn a chapter titled ‘Unconditional Love and Support for Humanity: Nurturing Gender Identity Equity and Inclusivity in Higher Education Institutions in Southern Africa’, Kanyopa argues that, despite comprehensive human rights frameworks that include rights to sexual orientation and gender identity, violations targeting LGBTQ+ communities persist in Africa.This is the case, despite the World Health Organization pointing out to countries and governments thatdiscriminationbased on sexual orientation or gender identity contravenes the United Nations human rights standards, negatively affects individuals, communities, societies, and undermines the achievement of the SDGs.Nevertheless, the issue, according to Kanyopa, is that, in most African countries, gender is divided into two groups, in which people are either male or female. “The system, which is known as ‘gender binary’, creates no room for an individual outside the two binary categories,” according to the researcher.AttacksShe noted that, even in South Africa and Botswana, the two countries in Southern Africa that have enacted policies on sexual orientation and gender identity and outlawed discrimination based on sexual inclination, attacks against the LGBTQ+ community still happen regularly at universities.Drawing insights from research by Floretta Boonzaier, a professor of psychology at the University of Cape Town, as well as other researchers on the issue, attacks and discrimination against the LGBTQ+ student communities are common in higher education institutions in Southern Africa.In this regard, there are indicators that many LGBTQ+ students in universities in Southern Africa, as elsewhere in Sub-Saharan Africa, are struggling to ‘come out’ with their hidden gender identities because of severe discrimination and stigma.‘Treated like animals’The academic noted that, in Eswatini, Lesotho and Zimbabwe, African homosexual students are treated as criminals, and they are at risk of imprisonment, torture and death. In addition to facing cruel acts and widespread discrimination, those students, according to Kanyopa, suffer from psychosocial challenges that include feelings of isolation and suicide.Indeed, the plight of LGBTQ+ student communities in Sub-Saharan Africa has been enhanced by the inherited legacy from colonial laws that criminalised same-sex relationships, and which continue to exist in many African countries. In effect, this has resulted in some African governments forming legal frameworks and legislation directed against the LGBTQ+ communities.For instance, Nigeria has enacted legislation that prohibits same-sex marriages and subsequently imposes harsh penalties on the LGBTQ+ community, while Uganda’s Anti-Homosexuality Act, which becamelawin 2023, proposes life imprisonment for same-sex relations.Other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa which have enacted laws that restrict the LGBTQ+ community’s fundamental rights and freedom include Tanzania, Kenya, Ghana and the Democratic Republic of the Congo.In this context, whereas the rights and freedoms of higher education students who are marginalised by their sexual orientation and gender identity have improved in many countries in other global regions, they have more or less stagnated in Sub-Saharan Africa, or are invariably getting worse.Are any changes on the horizon?However, Kanyopa’s advocacy against unfair and harsh treatment of LGBTQ+ students in universities is invariably raising questions as to how long those students should suffer oppression, discrimination, and exclusion before they can achieve equal recognition, fair treatment and above all, enjoy fundamental rights and freedom as their peers.For Kanyopa, universities should take a leading role in raising awareness and addressing the discrimination and stigma of the LGBTQ+ student community. She urges universities’ managements to reduce discrimination and disapproval by creating programmes that open up positive conversations about the LGBTQ+ community, as well as introducing support systems that students might need.She noted that individuals working in programmes for the LGBTQ+ students in the universities should be conversant with the self-determination theory, the psychological approach that focuses on the innate human needs for independence, competence, relatedness and well-being. It also explores the motivational factors driving living experiences, human behaviour, performance and personal growth.Nevertheless, Kanyopa highlighted that the pathway towards achieving gender identity equity and inclusivity in universities in Southern Africa and elsewhere in Sub-Saharan Africa is not an easy one, considering the identity crisis, distress, abuse and prejudice that most of those students encounter.In addition to raising awareness and recognition of LGBTQ+ students in the universities, Kanyopa advocated for fair representation and channels to redress abuse and other forms of marginalisation.Considering the well-being of all studentsAt institutions, the primary focus should be to overcome injustice by opening up spaces for all students, irrespective of their social group, race or gender identity. “Universities should show that they care about the well-being and success of all students, including those of the LGBTQ+ community,” stated Kanyopa.However, considering the multiple and diverse perspectives and varied students’ personal experiences, Kanyopa said universities in countries in Sub-Saharan Africa should enhance the right to freedom of expression and academic freedom. She noted that this could be achieved if the universities nurtured diverse perspectives that contribute towards the educational issues on campus.For further success in achieving acceptance and respect of LGBTQ+ students at universities, Kanyopa pointed out that there is a need to empower staff to conduct continuous surveys and assessments to determine the well-being and stress levels of students.According to Kanyopa, LGBTQ+ students in African universities are stressed as a result of biases and stigma surrounding their genders.In effect, the merit of Kanyopa’s study is that it has pointed out the need to improve the learning environment in universities in Southern Africa and elsewhere in Sub-Saharan Africa, where there is no academic motivation for students who identify with different genders outside the binary branding.In this regard, Kanyopa also pointed out that, without promoting equity and inclusivity in the learning environment, regardless of race, ethnic group, language or sexual orientation, it would be hard to realise the SDGs, which insist on the provision of quality education for all and gender identity equality.The study also highlights that advancing gender equity and inclusivity in the learning environment does not just support students in their academic performance, but also in their emotional challenges.The truth, according to Kanyopa, is that LGBTQ+ students in most African universities are currently living in fear, hiding their gender identities from others, such as teachers, parents and the community in general.In effect, in most countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, LGBTQ+ communities are rarely mentioned in university policies that safeguard student interests and protection.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica 大學在性別認同平等政策上多數沉默

``` 
在南部非洲，大學如何處理LGBTQ+學生社群的性別認同和性取向問題引發關注。南非自由國立大學的教育心理學講師Dr. Theresia Joakim Kanyopa在新書《可持續發展的高等教育國際化：南部非洲視角》中探討了這一議題。她指出，儘管存在全面的人權框架，LGBTQ+社群仍遭受歧視和暴力。許多學生因害怕歧視而無法公開自己的性別認同，身處壓迫環境中。Kanyopa呼籲大學應該主動提升對LGBTQ+學生的認識，並創建支持系統，以促進性別認同的平等與包容性，這對於實現聯合國可持續發展目標至關重要。
```

``` 
大學在性別認同平等政策上多數沉默

在南部非洲，大學如何處理LGBTQ+學生社群的性別認同和性取向問題引發關注。南非自由國立大學的教育心理學講師Dr. Theresia Joakim Kanyopa在新書《可持續發展的高等教育國際化：南部非洲視角》中探討了這一議題。她指出，儘管存在全面的人權框架，LGBTQ+社群仍遭受歧視和暴力。許多學生因害怕歧視而無法公開自己的性別認同，身處壓迫環境中。Kanyopa呼籲大學應該主動提升對LGBTQ+學生的認識，並創建支持系統，以促進性別認同的平等與包容性，這對於實現聯合國可持續發展目標至關重要。
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Africa, Japan interrogate building of equal HE partnerships
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Africa, Japan interrogate building of equal HE partnershipsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksJAPAN-AFRICAAfrican universities to benefit from research partnershipAFRICA-JAPANJapan-backed science hub to involve 10 African universitiesAFRICA-JAPANJapan unveils ‘relevant and timely’ HE initiativesFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFRICA-JAPANAfrica, Japan interrogate building of equal HE partnershipsWagdy Sawahel28 August 2025Tweet“Until now, cooperation in higher education between Japan and Africa has mainly been centred on a support-type framework but, in recent years, there has been a demand for the transfer of knowledge and technology and for equal partnerships so that African universities can conduct education and research independently.”This is how theGlobal Innovation Japan-Africa UniversitySymposium, ‘Imagining the Future of Human Resource Development and Science and Technology Cooperation between Japan and Africa’ was introduced in material about the event.It took place during the 9th Tokyo International Conference on African Development (TICAD9) held under the theme, ‘Co-create Innovative Solutions with Africa’, and ended on 22 August in Japan.The symposium was set against a background in which Africa’s international importance is increasing, given its growing youth population and potential for economic growth and strengthened by the African Union’s positioning of 2025 as the ‘Year of Youth and Innovation in Africa’. In this context, human resource development and science and technology cooperation between Japan and Africa were becoming even more important, according to the symposium’s organisers.Echoing these statements, Selma Malika Haddadi, the African Union Commission deputy chairperson,saidat the TICAD ministerial meeting: “Our continent offers many opportunities for the mutual benefit of its people and those who seek to partner with us on an equal footing.“While we seek technological and skills transfer, we also offer abundant natural resources, a youthful and dynamic workforce, and an increasingly integrated market.“I encourage us to work collaboratively and in synergy to ensure that strategic investments in critical infrastructure, as well as strong policies for resilient health systems and social stability, are in place to realise the prosperous, integrated and peaceful Africa we envision,” she said.Developing cultural understandingThe university symposium enabled a further exploration of what an equal partnership between Japan and Africa – centred on human resource development, science and technology cooperation – would look like. Various speakers and commentators highlighted the areas of collaboration that should be advanced, with several foregrounding shared PhD programmes and infrastructure development as areas for partnership-building. Africa’s ownership of its research agenda when it collaborated with the Global North was another key point that was raised.Professor Mitsuo Ochi, the president of the Hiroshima University in Japan, who delivered a keynote speech at the symposium, emphasised the importance of cultural understanding as a starting point for collaboration.He toldUniversity World News: “Africa is a region of profound diversity. It is essential that we first develop a nuanced understanding of each nation’s systems and cultural context before embarking upon collaborative research, complementing online engagement with on-site visits when necessary.”“The considerable geographical distances present significant challenges to travel, while the unique cultures, institutional frameworks, and often protracted decision-making processes of each country add further complexity,” Ochi said, reflecting on factors that affect partnership-building.He said meaningful collaboration required the active involvement of a wide range of stakeholders, particularly those from industry and the business sector.Prioritising capacity in African universitiesYoko Shimpuku, a professor in global health nursing at Hiroshima University, the vice-chair of the Young Academy of Japan and a panellist at the symposium, toldUniversity World Newsthat, to promote equal partnerships, it was essential to prioritise capacity strengthening in African universities rather than dependency.“This means investing in the long-term training of African researchers and educators, supporting infrastructure development, and co-designing projects that respond to locally identified needs.“Japan can play a crucial role by sharing advanced expertise in health systems, digital health, and pedagogy, while respecting African leadership in defining priorities,” Shimpuku added.“Exchange programmes should be two-way, enabling African and Japanese scholars to learn from one another and build sustainable collaborations,” she suggested.A research for development approachProfessor Patrick Okori, the executive secretary of the Uganda-based Regional Universities Forum for Capacity Building in Agriculture, or RUFORUM, who was also a panellist at the event, said that, to enhance Africa’s independent growth, Japan-Africa partnerships must be based on co-created initiatives that boost the development of local capability – especially in emerging areas of science, technology and innovation.“A focus on shared PhD degree programmes is a suitable approach, shared research-for-development, which emphasises practical, context-specific knowledge and innovations that can be applied to real-African problems, involving staff and student mobility for cross-learning and inter-regional partnerships, along with supporting Africa to build ecosystems for industry-university engagements,” Okori said, referring to areas of collaboration.Building human resourcesHassan Virji, a visiting professor at Hiroshima University and the honorary executive director of START International, said in his keynote address at the symposium that equal partnership involved a clear understanding of well-defined goals and objectives and factors that impact a partnership.“This includes focusing on emerging opportunities for collaborative research on pressing global challenges of climate or environmental changes and related adaptation and resilience issues, challenges of ensuring human and ecosystem health, and challenges of rapidly developing new technologies of artificial intelligence applications to analyse, manage, and manipulate large data bases from a wide variety of sources.“[A] clear focus of collaborative programmes and projects should be on building human resources and institutional strengthening,” said Virji.STARTis an international non-governmental non-profit organisation dedicated to building capacity in developing countries to deal with global environmental changes and sustainable development.ChallengesThe panellists also listed obstacles that could hinder effective partnerships. Shimpuku said one of the main challenges to establish equal Japan-Africa partnerships in higher education and in science, technology and innovation was structural inequality in resources, research funding, and publication opportunities.“Too often, African institutions are placed in a subordinate position, with Japan or other high-income partners leading project design and decision-making. Language barriers, differences in academic systems, and limited opportunities for African universities to access cutting-edge technologies further widen the gap.“Moreover, short-term project cycles, donor-driven agendas, and lack of continuity make it difficult to sustain equal and independent research environments,” Shimpuku said.These aspects – co-development, co-implementation and continuous programme assessments – were also highlighted by Virji as challenges.On a practical level, Virji added: “A significant challenge is the wide variety of cultures and languages across nations of Africa.“While regional groupings, economic or cultural, exist, it is often difficult to perform effective collaborative partnerships over long time periods.”Okori identified a partnership challenge as the “dynamic funding environment”, signalling, according to him, the need to rethink how the resourcing of partnerships should be done. For him, this aspect, together with a science, technology and innovation divide between developing and developed economies, were hurdles en route to more equal partnerships.SolutionsTo deal with challenges facing equal Japan-Africa partnerships, Okori said collaborative initiatives that build local capacity must be emphasised, especially in the Global South.This could also include initiatives such as shared PhD programmes and support for industry-education partnerships that are common in Japan, but weak in Africa, using co-designed models.Virji added that Japanese institutions could assume various impactful roles, including hosting early-career fellows, developing joint proposals for projects and supporting research communications and career development.“[In addition, they could] create twinning relationship agreements with African universities, enabling the exchange of researchers and shared governance.“Also, it is very important to provide writing mentorship, outreach training, [and] guidance in policy and practice briefings to support visibility – as well as emphasise impacts,” Virji said.Expanding further, Shimpuku said a partnership model based on co-creation, in which agendas are jointly set and benefits are shared, should be adopted.Funding mechanisms should allocate resources directly to African universities, ensuring they have ownership over research activities, she added.Like Okori, Shimpuku also proposed joint PhD training programmes as an example of collaboration. She also said faculty exchanges, and open-access platforms for publications, could reduce inequalities in knowledge production.According to him, building digital infrastructure and supporting African-led innovations will strengthen sustainability.“Japan should also invest in South-South collaborations, connecting African institutions with one another as well as with Japan. [But] equal partnerships require humility, mutual respect and recognition of diverse knowledge systems,” Shimpuku stressed.Other science experts weigh inProfessor Ahmed El-Gohary, the former president of the Egypt-Japan University of Science and Technology, toldUniversity World Newsequal partnerships could be promoted through the establishment of common research funding accessible to African universities and support for the development of needed infrastructure at the African universities, such as labs, digital libraries and knowledge exchanges through academic networks.“[Partnerships] should all be based on the co-creation of research agendas involving key African leaders in science and technology,” El-Gohary said.Professor Mohamed Hassan, the president of the Sudanese National Academy of Sciences, toldUniversity World Newsthat Japanese universities have a significant opportunity to expand partnerships with top universities in Africa’s 33 least-developed countries, focusing on frontier science and technology and their applications to real-life problems.“Key sectors for collaboration include renewable energy, agriculture, health, climate change adaptation, and biodiversity. To foster effective partnerships, programmes should be designed to train talented students at Japanese universities, particularly in STEM fields, artificial intelligence, and digital technologies,” Hassan added.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

``` 
日非高等教育夥伴關係新探討 在日前於日本舉行的第九屆東京國際非洲發展會議(TICAD9)上，非洲與日本的高等教育夥伴關係受到了重視。與會者強調，過去的合作多為支持性質，但隨著非洲在全球舞台上的重要性提升，雙方應推動平等的知識與技術轉移。非洲聯盟副主席Selma Malika Haddadi指出，非洲擁有豐富的自然資源和年輕的勞動力，應該與日本在教育和技術領域建立更緊密的合作。專家建議，應優先加強非洲大學的能力，推動共建博士學位計劃，並強調文化理解在合作中的重要性。這些舉措有望促進非洲的獨立發展及創新能力提升。
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Will the reshuffle affect internationalisation work?
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Will the reshuffle affect internationalisation work?GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsCaspar Veldkamp, one of several Dutch ministers who resigned from the government last week. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNETHERLANDSUniversities hope for better days after coalition’s collapseNETHERLANDSUniversities go to court after Senate supports €500 million cutbacksNETHERLANDSUniversities plan strike action over austerity cutsNETHERLANDSCoalition parties back plan to keep international studentsNETHERLANDSProvince warns of dire impact of foreign student numbers capFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNETHERLANDSWill the reshuffle affect internationalisation work?Jan Petter Myklebust27 August 2025TweetOn Friday 22 August, the Dutch Foreign Minister Caspar Veldkamp resigned after failing to secure sanctions against Israel at a cabinet meeting. All nine ministers from the centrist New Social Contract (NSC) party, including Eppo Bruins, minister of education, culture and science, resigned from the caretaker government the same day.In the interim, Climate Minister Sophie Hermans (VVD – the centre-right People’s Party for Freedom and Democracy) will become responsible minister of climate policy and green growth, minister of education, culture and science, and deputy prime minister. A general election will take place shortly, and experts estimate it could take up to six months until a new government is established.VVD and BBB (the right-wing populist Farmer-Citizen Movement) put forward new ministers on 23 August. Bruins’ successor Sophie Hermans was number two on the VVD list in the previous elections, but this time she is eligible. She said she is available as a minister should the VVD return to government.For years, Hermans was political assistant to VVD leader Mark Rutte. In 2017 she entered the House of Representatives for the Liberals and five years later became parliamentary leader. She is the daughter of Loek Hermans, former minister of education, culture and science, in the second ‘purple’ cabinet (1998 to 2002).The acting ministers have been appointed until successors are found.The announcement speaks of a ‘temporary management’. It does not say how quickly the two remaining coalition parties expect to find new ministers. The elections are on 29 October, and after that a new coalition must be formed. The Rutte IV cabinet continued in a caretaker capacity for almost a year, according to ERASMUS magazine.Outgoing Minister of Education, Culture and Science Eppo Bruins planned to remove the assessment of non-Dutch-taught programmes (Toets Anderstalig Onderwijs, TAO)for existing degree programmes from the Balanced Internationalisation Bill(Wetsvoorstel Internationalisering in Balance, WIB).In a letter to the House of Representatives published on 3 July, Bruins expressed support for the universities’ plans to manage internationalisation through self-governance, a position also supported by a broad majority in the House. His plan was to send a new version of the bill to parliament at the end of this year. It is unclear whether his resignation will have any further impact on this schedule.Universities of the Netherlands (UNL) chairman Caspar van den Bergsaid in a press release: “It is very good news for universities and for the Netherlands that the bill is being amended. The TAO would have been disastrous for our education system, the labour market and the vitality of borders and shrinking regions.”In the letter to parliament, the minister outlined how he will address the motions submitted regarding the WIB (Dutch Academic Exchange Act), including the motion by Krul et al, which requested the minister drop the TAO (General Education, Teaching and Learning) from the existing curriculum.Although the minister advised against the motion, he said he would implement it and amend the WIB. He stipulated several preconditions: for example, an inventory must be made of existing programmes that are taught in a foreign language according to the WIB definition. The minister indicated in July that he expected to submit the WIB amendment to Parliament by the end of 2025.“The expectation is that the intake of international students at universities will decline for the third year in a row. This is evident from the application figures for the coming academic year.“Applications from students from the European Economic Area (EEA) are currently 4.5 per cent lower than last year and even 11 per cent lower than in 2023 to 2024. For all international students, enrolments are 3.0 per cent lower than last year.“The decline is particularly noticeable in the Language and Culture sectors (-28.5 per cent), Law (-25.0 per cent), Science (-12.5 per cent), Behaviour & Society (-16 per cent), and Economics (-4.0 per cent). Growth is observed in the Technology sector (+26 per cent). In 2023 to 2024, a total of 18,500 international students enrolled in university bachelor's programmes,”UNLreported.Caspar Van den Berg said: “These figures show that, thanks to the measures taken, universities are increasingly gaining control over the influx of international students.But we also see that the Netherlands' image as an attractive place to work and study has suffered significant damage. It is therefore essential that the Netherlands develops a talent strategy so that we can develop, attract and retain talent, including from abroad.“Our self-management plan aligns with this by focusing on strategic growth. We will ensure that the number of international students is reduced in a targeted manner so that we maintain room for growth in sectors where we specifically need more students.”The preliminary intake figures will be released at the beginning of November. They will reveal how many Dutch and international students started a programme in the 2025 to 2026 academic year.Political signals in the election campaignThe VVD wants to use tuition fees to influence young people’s study choices. The fee should be lowered for “shortage programmes” in technology, healthcare, education, security and climate, it says. The VVD also wants some programmes to become less accessible. It says: “We’re limiting the number of places available for programmes with poor job prospects.”Student numbers are declining, and a significant portion of Parliament wants to reduce the influx of students from abroad. However, universities and universities of applied sciences receive funding per student. There are concerns about intense competition for students. Many parties suggest that educational institutions should receive more “stable” funding.Even the VVD party, which generally supports competition, is in favour of more stable funding. The liberals want more control over the “capacity” of programmes. In other words, some programmes would have to admit fewer students, and the VVD wants to enforce this through funding. The idea is that this would allow higher education to better align with the labour market.The centrist Christian Union also wants “a better and fairer system for funding higher education institutions”. The number of (international) students will become less important, the Christians promise.An insecure situationUniversity World Newsasked Dutch academics if they think the present political situation with a caretaker government will have a positive or negative effect upon universities’ internationalisation work.Professor Marc van Oostendorp at Radboud Universiteit Nijmegen toldUniversity World News: “I think it is reasonable to assume that not much will happen now until the elections (and then until a new government is installed). I also believe it is likely that almost regardless of what the new government will be, it will have at least a somewhat more moderate view on internationalisation.“The problem, however, is that it is not clear when a new government will be formed. It may take a long time after the elections to form a stable coalition. This means that universities may be insecure for another year (or if we are unlucky, even more), and until then, the present policy will be in place, and they will have to adapt to it to some degree.”Laurens van der Velde, spokesperson for the Executive Board at the University of Twente, agreed: “As we already had a caretaker government in place following the collapse earlier this year, the expectation was (and is) that further development of education policy may slow down. This expectation has not changed with the change of the responsible minister. We’ll have to wait and see if this has a positive or negative impact”.Ruben Puylaert, spokesperson for Universities of the Netherlands, toldUniversity World News: “It seems that there will besome new ministers from VVD and BBBand that Sophie Hermans will not be the new caretaker minister for Education, Culture and Science. However, it is unclear who this will be.“We assume that the wishes of the House of Representatives, which voted overwhelmingly in favour of the university plans, will be respected and that another minister will also implement this in the new legislation.“In addition, former minister Bruins has also confirmed on behalf of the Cabinet that he is scrapping the foreign language education assessment (TAO for existing programmes). In short, we do not expect any changes,” Puylaert said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSUDANNew post-war strategy wants to rebuild a damaged systemWagdy Sawahel27 August 2025TweetSudan’s Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research has unveiled a strategy including the reconstruction of universities and research centres that have been affected by the war that started in mid-April 2023, involving the paramilitary Rapid Support Forces (RSF) and the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF).The strategy was unveiled by Ahmed Madawi, the new minister of higher education and scientific research, on 19 August, according to an officialFacebook post.Madawi, who was appointed by Sudan’s Prime Minister, Kamil Idris, on 3 July, and is the former deputy vice-chancellor at the University of Khartoum’s faculty of medicine, is part of a civilian technocratically led transitional ‘Government of Hope’ under Abdel Fattah Al-Burhan, the chairman of Sudan’s Transitional Sovereign Council and the commander of the SAF.In Sudan, universities are slowly resuming classes after a two-year suspension due to the ongoing conflict. The prime minister has ordered thereopeningof universities in Khartoum with the ministry of higher education assessing damage and overseeing repairs.Academics said that, whereas the strategy focuses on the reconstruction of infrastructure, quality assurance, and digital transformation, it also needed to tackle social cohesion.Dr Rania Baleela of the University of Khartoum’s faculty of science toldUniversity World News: “The ministry should also prioritise addressing the mental health and psychosocial needs of displaced students and staff to ensure holistic recovery. Embedding peace education within higher education is essential to rebuild social cohesion, reduce class-based conflicts, and promote equal citizenship.”She added that the principles of social justice should also receive attention, such as fair wealth distribution, equal opportunities, the protection of fundamental rights, and a supportive social system, which will strengthen the foundations of a cohesive and equitable civil society.What the recovery strategy entailsThe ministry’s strategy includes three axes, including the reconstruction of universities to create an academic environment that supports creativity and innovation as well as ensures quality control and accreditation standards are in place at higher education institutions.In an effort to facilitate the gradual return of higher education institutions to their campuses, the strategy focuses on the reconstruction of universities as well as student accommodation and preparation of a suitable environment to receive students and resume studies, especially within universities in Khartoum State, along with addressing the problems and obstacles that hinder their progress.A 2025study, titled a ‘Preliminary investigation and analysis of the impact of the Sudan War on higher education and scientific research sectors’, found that the RSF-SAF Sudan war has caused widespread destruction in higher education infrastructure and services, including universities and research institutions, along with mass displacement and severe insecurity, forcing citizens, including Sudanese academics, researchers, administration officials and students, to flee.Besides encouraging universities to invest and diversify their resources to become productive, the new strategy provides support for the universities to enhance their capabilities to provide quality higher education that keeps pace with modern requirements.Under the strategy, reconstructing and developing digital technology infrastructure and implementing digital transformation projects will be carried out in the higher education and scientific research sector, including strengthening e-learning education, educational virtual platforms, and cloud computing, as well as enhancing cybersecurity and introducing artificial intelligence.The strategy called upon private, national and foreign higher education institutions to maintain and rehabilitate their institutions and improve the services they provide to students and the community.Besides improving the conditions of university faculty members and staff, the strategy also focuses on increasing opportunities for scholarships and training for faculty members at home and abroad for advanced training and capacity-building.In the field of scientific research, the strategy focuses on directing efforts towards solving the problems facing the country through promoting cooperation and forming partnerships between institutions to achieve a comprehensive renaissance for the country.Strengthening partnerships among public and private universities to contribute to improving the quality of educational and research outputs – and meeting the needs of the local, regional and international labour market – is another aim.The strategy called for enhancing the role of peace studies centres at universities in promoting a culture of peace, combating regionalism and racism, rejecting violence, and mending the social fabric.A joint participatory approachDr Mosab Nouraldein Mohammed Hamad, the director of the Excellence Research Centre at Elsheikh Abdallah Elbadri University in Sudan, toldUniversity World Newsthe strategy was a good plan if it would receive sustained support from the government and the community.“I think the first step to implement this plan is to maintain the stability of administrations of the higher education institutions and universities, open discussion with them to know their views and thoughts, along with their emergency policy during crisis time.“A joint participatory approach must be applied where all relevant higher education stakeholders actively contribute to a higher education plan such as decision-making, or problem-solving, to achieve a mutually agreed-upon outcome,” Hamad said.Sudan HE recovery fundProfessor Mohamed Hassan, the president of the Sudanese National Academy of Sciences, toldUniversity World Newsthe strategy offered a blueprint for rebuilding and revitalising the country’s universities.“However, its success hinges on addressing two critical gaps: a concrete implementation roadmap and a viable funding strategy.“The national commitment should be complemented by domestic investment through establishing a Sudan Higher Education Recovery Fund, managed by a consortium of multilateral partners, including the World Bank, the African and Islamic Development Banks, UNESCO, and UNDP [United Nations Development Programme],” he said.Twinning and partnerships with global universitiesAccording to Hassan, international twinning and partnerships with universities worldwide are strategically imperative for Sudan’s universities to overcome decades of isolation and underinvestment.“These collaborations, which can be facilitated by the African, Arab and international associations of universities, are crucial for building back better, creating high-quality institutions that are more resilient and digitally integrated than their predecessors,” Hassan said.Global Sudanese diasporaHassan also emphasised the importance of engagement with the global Sudanese academic diaspora.“This extensive network of Sudanese academics and professionals abroad, many in prestigious positions, serves as ideal bridges for fostering relevant partnerships. A structured programme must be implemented to leverage their capital, academic expertise, and technical skills.“This should encompass facilitating regular visits to Sudanese universities to provide direct assistance in teaching and cutting-edge research, particularly in priority fields like STEM, artificial intelligence, and digital technologies,” Hassan noted.HE decentralisationDr Husam Eldin Elhag of the faculty of science and technology at Shendi University in Sudan, toldUniversity World News: “I believe that reconstructing higher education should be based on decentralisation, ensuring that institutions are not concentrated in a single city.“Activating e-learning requires specialised training for ministry staff, covering not only e-learning, but also other communication methods such as administrative decisions, correspondence, and transactions,” Elhag said.“Implementing a key performance indicators (KPIs) system will help evaluate university performance and establish required standards for each institution and its staff.“Defining research pathways based on the country’s development needs in agriculture, veterinary medicine, and health should be prioritised,” Elhag added.He said new research centres could be established that operate through networks and can coordinate information flow and collaborative efforts.Peace and social justice educationBaleela, the director of the Toxic Organisms Research Centre at the University of Khartoum’s faculty of science, said the strategy will enable universities to reclaim their role as engines of sustainable development and innovation, while also restoring confidence in Sudan’s academic and research capacities.She stated that peace education, social cohesion and social justice should receive attention, and noted that the major challenges that might hamper the implementation of the strategy are resource limitations, political instability, the brain drain, and disparities in institutional capacity across regions, which may hinder the uniform application of the strategy.“To address these, the ministry needs to build strong partnerships with international donors, diaspora scholars, and the private sector, in addition to ensuring transparent governance and phased, context-sensitive implementation of reforms,” Baleela said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 蘇丹高等教育部近日公布了一項新策略，旨在重建受到2023年戰爭影響的高等教育體系。新任高等教育和科學研究部長艾哈邁德·馬達維表示，該策略將重建大學和研究中心，並強調質量保證及數字轉型的重要性。儘管重建基礎設施是重點，但專家指出，應同時關注學生的心理健康和社會凝聚力。這項計劃還包括促進國際合作和建立全球高等教育聯盟，以提升教育質量，並確保資源的公平分配。成功實施此策略需政府和社會的持續支持。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Ministry to boost key role of university environment centresGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALThe environmental emergency is real. HE is our best hopeGLOBALStudents say green skills are needed for all future jobsGLOBALHow student leadership drives sustainability activitiesGLOBALClimate change and education: Two sides of the same coinCAMEROONCalls on universities and academics to lead climate fightGLOBALSurvey shows growth in climate-related university coursesGLOBALHigher education and the emerging environmental revolutionUNITED KINGDOMUniversities should be drivers of local sustainabilityGLOBALUniversities are key to achieving sustainable developmentFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackINDONESIAMinistry to boost key role of university environment centresKafil Yamin26 August 2025TweetIndonesia’s Minister of Environment Hanif Faisol Nurofiq has stressed university involvement in the drafting of science-based environmental policies.“We must collaborate by strengthening academic studies. This is an important basis for the Ministry of Environment when we create policy instruments,” he said in mid-August, speaking at Brawijaya University, Malang, in East Java.Postgraduate researchers play a key role in shaping policy and enforcement instruments related to environmental protection in various regions, he was quoted by theAntaranews agency as saying.“In general, they can provide answers on whether environmental approval should be granted or denied for an activity or project.”In late July he told a forum on collaboration between the ministry and universities, attended by 41 university rectors, that the ministry would ‘revitalise’ environmental study centres at universities to strengthen science-based environmental management policies, underlining the function of university centres in providing support for policies, including by providing input into drafting Environmental Protection and Management Plans (RPPLH), conducting Strategic Environmental Assessments, and overseeing environmental approval processes.Nurofiq said that he would not base his policies on opinions but on scientific reasoning. “Every decision should be backed by science. Universities are the engines that drive and reinforce the nation’s scientific foundation,” he said at the forum.Almost all private and state-owned universities in Indonesia have centres of environmental studies, but despite the high number of research papers they produce, their impact has been limited so far, according to experts. Many of their recommendations remain as research documents and are not used to improve environment policies.Despite the criticism of the overall lack of impact of university centres, Fachruddin Mangunjaya, dean of the Biology Faculty at Indonesia’s National University in Jakarta, said several environmental study centres in universities contributed positively to government policymaking to some extent. “They collaborate with government institutions and give recommendations for policymaking. For example, in climate change-related projects.”“We can find such research collaborations in agricultural fields,” he toldUniversity World News.However, he added “together the centres do a lot of research without synergy and coordination among them, which often leads to overlap and repetition in topics or issues.”And, in general, they conduct research without devising a plan of what they should do with the research results. “They commonly receive short-term funding, so they focus only on the research they are working on, without thinking about what’s next,” he said.“If environmental study centres want their research to have an impact, they have to collaborate on government policies and make adjustments; they should also do regular monitoring and evaluations afterwards.”Little evidence of impact on key environmental issuesUniversitas Indonesia (UI) has its Research Centre on Human Resources and Environment; Gadjah Mada University has its UGM Environmental Study Centre. Bandung Institute of Technology and IPB University (formerly Bogor Agricultural University) both have their own Natural Environmental Study Centre, to name just a few.Combined, these university centres spend a huge amount of money from government budget allocations and some from foreign funding through joint research. Yet experts say these huge funding and research efforts have been unable to hold back deforestation or slow down pollution, for example.Data fromGlobal Forest Watchshows Indonesia lost 259,000 hectares of natural forest and produced 194 million tonnes of CO² emissions in 2024 alone.Slow policy implementation and inconsistent and non-binding international commitments have meant Indonesia’s transition to renewable energy is also slow, despite its target of 23% by 2025, analysts said.The country missed its 2024 renewable energy target, reaching only 14.68% of the intended 19.5% of energy sources, with implications for its overall emissions goals, the American Chamber of Commerce in Indonesia noted.Mohammad Fathi Royani, a senior researcher at Indonesia’s National Research and Innovation Body, BRIN, noted: “With their research, universities are more of a moral and intellectual power, not executive (power). We see an alarming rate of deforestation, but (knowledge of) this rate resulted from research by a number of environmental study centres in universities.”Pointing out decisions are often made before research is conducted into a particular environmental issue, he toldUniversity World News: “It’s not the environmental study centres that are ineffective, but rather the decision makers that are ‘exclusive’.”In the past, Royani was himself involved in research into land use changes in a forest area. The research intended to study the likely impacts if the authorities built on the land. “But the land had changed a lot,” he said, noting it was already used by the local community for farming and settlement. “The Environmental Ministry, it turns out, decided not to issue any licences on the land.”Paris Agreement targetsAs a signatory of the Paris Agreement, which Indonesia has ratified through Law No. 16 of 2016, the country is now developing its Nationally Determined Contribution (NDC), which includes targets for greenhouse gas emission reductions, peatland restoration, and sustainable forest management to help limit the rise in global temperatures.Indonesia is also developing science-based environmental policies through its Long-Term Strategy for Low Carbon and Climate Resilience (LTS-LCCR) framework, which guides its 2060 Net Zero Emissions goal. For this in particular, Nurofiq reaffirmed his commitment to involving universities in the drafting of science-based environmental policies.These include developing renewable energy, addressing food and land use systems, and protecting biodiversity, with the new administration in late 2024 announcing even more ambitious goals, such as retiring all coal plants within 15 years.While challenges remain, the policies are designed to integrate scientific understanding to manage environmental trade-offs and achieve sustainable development, according to the minister.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

``` 
印尼環境部推動大學環境中心角色增強

印尼環境部長哈尼夫·法伊索爾·努羅菲克近日強調大學在制定基於科學的環境政策中的重要性。他表示，應加強學術研究，以支持環境管理政策的制定。環境研究中心在提供環境保護計畫及進行環境影響評估中扮演關鍵角色。

雖然印尼大學的環境研究中心數量眾多，卻面臨研究成果未能有效影響政策的挑戰。專家指出，缺乏協調和整合的研究常導致重複性，並呼籲大學加強與政府合作，以提高研究的實際應用價值。新政策目標在於強化科學依據，促進可持續發展目標的實現。 
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Minister plays citizenship test hardball with universities
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Minister plays citizenship test hardball with universitiesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSWEDENBiggest threats to academic freedom are political – ReportSWEDENWhat will a shift to the right mean for higher education?SWEDENOngoing protest over migration law’s effect on PhD studentsSWEDENAcademics protest against ‘fatal’ changes to Aliens ActSWEDENCall for universities to push to decouple from the stateSWEDENBusiness criticises migration rules for foreign graduatesSWEDENNew migration law set to ‘drive foreign researchers away’SWEDENResidency restrictions eased for students and researchersSWEDENOmbudsman to examine expulsion of doctoral studentsSWEDENPopulism a threat to internationalisation, rectors saySWEDENMigration agency eases rule for international studentsSWEDENMinister in storm after overriding university autonomySWEDENInternational students face residency clampdownFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning -
