Open Rank FacultySWEDENMinister plays citizenship test hardball with universitiesJan Petter Myklebust26 August 2025TweetA new test for citizenship is to be introduced in Sweden in August 2026, and universities are being pressured into creating it, in a move that is seen as undermining university autonomy.In the Tidø Agreement, asreportedbyUniversity World News, the government, with the support of the right-wing Sweden Democrats party, decided that those applying for Swedish citizenships should have knowledge about Sweden and be able to speak Swedish, to be documented by tests. The government has asked the universities of Stockholm and Gothenburg to work on the tests in collaboration with UHR, the Swedish Council for Higher Education.Vice-chancellor of Stockholm University Hans Adolfsson is strongly critical of the request. He toldDagens Nyheter(DN) that this is a strictly political task that universities should not be involved in.However, he received a direct response from Minister for Migration and Asylum Policy Johan Forssell (Conservatives), who emphasised that “it is the government that governs the country, and our universities are state authorities that obey us”.Forsell’s remark led toan article inDNon 22 August, headlined: “The government is sending double-bind messages on academic freedom.”DNstated that the minister of education, Lotta Edholm from the Liberal Party, had confirmed by email that the government is standing by the decision that it is the universities that have to work on the citizenship test “because that is where the expertise is”.Jacob Adamowicz, an analyst at SULF, the Swedish Association of University Teachers and Researchers, said that he could not recall a time in recent years when a government has been so clear that it has the power to govern over higher education institutions.“This conflict illustrates the huge problem that 31 of the Swedish higher education institutions are under government control,” he said.Adamowicz noted that the government is sending double-bind messages, not least with regard to all the statements the Liberals party has given on the importance of academic freedom and that politicians should keep an arm’s length distance from universities.In their response letter to the government, Stockholm University wrote that it is sceptical about the assignment because it is not part of a higher education institution’s ‘core mission’,Universitatslararenreported.Furthermore, the letter stated that it is contrary to established practice, where arm’s length distance applies between the state and higher education institutions.“If you look at what the Higher Education Act says about the tasks of a university and what activities we are to carry out, it is very clear that these are about education, research and public engagement,” Adolfsson stated.For him, the decision to task higher education institutions with developing a citizenship test falls outside this framework.“It is a direct result of a political agreement, and that is why our reaction is so strong. It really feels completely inappropriate, and it is a task that should really be given to someone else,” Adolfsson toldUniversitatslararen.According to the government’s decision, UHR needs help from “bodies with subject expertise and experience in developing large-scale tests with a scientific basis” to design the citizenship test. The government writes that this assignment has been given to the two universities because they already design different types of national tests.The University of Gothenburg is responsible for national tests in social studies for the final year of secondary school, and Stockholm University produces the national tests for municipal adult education in Swedish for immigrants.Additionally, Stockholm University is responsible forTisus, which is a qualifying test in Swedish for university and college studies in Sweden.But unlike a citizenship test, these examinations are related to a university’s core mission, Adolfsson said. “If you look at the fact that we design the other tests, they have a direct bearing on our activities.”As things currently stand, the government has the power to assign any task to universities and colleges. AdolfssontoldUniversitetsläraren: “Well, as long as we are state agencies, they can obviously do that.”As the task has been assigned to them, it is now up to the universities to fulfil it, he pointed out. “Then you might question whether it is even possible to do it in the short time that has actually been stipulated.“It needs to be delivered back to UHR so that they in turn can have it ready by 16 August next year. Implementing or designing this type of test and questions with high quality and high validity requires extensive work.”Malin Broberg , vice-chancellor of the University of Gothenburg, explained the university’s position toUniversity World Newssaying the university was against the proposal to introduce a test, but “If the government, nevertheless, intends to introduce such a test, then the expertise available at the universities in this area should be used to ensure that the test is legally robust”.She stated: “The University of Gothenburg is committed to engaging with society at large and to contributing our expertise when other public authorities request research-based knowledge.“However, when we received a concrete request to assist the Swedish Council for Higher Education (UHR), we emphasised to the government that the current timeline is unreasonable and does not provide sufficient conditions to develop a legally sound test, since this type of test development takes time.”She added that the principal issue was “the government’s growing tendency to assign specific tasks to universities, as well as the approach and rhetoric used by certain politicians towards higher education institutions”.She noted: “This is reflected in the Minister for Migration’s response in the article, where he underlines that the government directs the authorities and that universities are expected to comply. I see a major risk in this type of statement, as it indicates increased political micromanagement of universities.“Legislation already provides an overall framework for our mission and stresses the importance of university autonomy and academic freedom. We have an important responsibility to safeguard the autonomy of higher education institutions, and there should be an arm’s length distance between politics and the universities.”Stricter rules for citizenshipWhen the present coalition government made the pact with the SD party, immigration was high on the agenda, and a special investigator was appointed in 2023 to investigate stricter rules for citizenship.The 560-page report,Stricter rules for citizenship, was delivered in January 2025, introducing tougher citizenship requirements to manage migration and enhance security, including increasing the residency period from five to eight years, and introducing new financial self-sufficiency requirements and civic and language tests.At the time, Stockholm University addressed the integration argument that tests should provide better integration: “One argument for introducing such requirements is that they improve integration. The assumption is that new citizens will more effectively become part of Swedish society if they can speak the Swedish language, have knowledge of Swedish society and can support themselves.“However, this argument ignores research in the field. It clearly shows that stricter citizenship requirements have integration-inhibiting effects.”Stockholm University explained further: “An illustrative example is a meta-study by Vasiliki Fouka in the Annual Review of Political Science from this year (2025) that reviews over 200 studies on the link between citizenship requirements and integration.“The conclusion is that language and civics requirements linked to citizenship are exclusionary when looking at the entire group of non-citizens in a country.“While such requirements can be an incentive for highly educated non-citizens, they are repulsive for low-educated non-citizens and others within the group with a weaker position in society.“The reason why the exclusionary effects are most prominent is that the majority of non-citizens are poorly educated and marginalised. This is also the case in Sweden.”The university also pointed to research that shows that easier requirements, or no requirements at all, for becoming a citizen work to promote integration. It stated: “Obtaining citizenship in itself seems to contribute to immigrants' economic and political integration and reduce their experience of exclusion. If the goal is to improve the integration of this group, access to citizenship should therefore be facilitated – not made more difficult.”Woke activismThe right-wing press has responded to concerns from universities regarding the citizenship tests.“Universities are making noise about the citizenship tests, when the government is introducing the citizenship tests to strengthen integration, the universities say no. The vice-chancellors are defending their academic prestige, but the question is if something else is behind this”,FrihetsNytt [FreedomNews], an anti-immigration news site, wrote on 22 August.“At the same time the universities are living on billions in tax incomes from the same people that are now demanding results. They often speak about ‘academic freedom’ but are often using this concept to escape responsibility. When the question is to develop tests that our Nordic neighbours already have, universities suddenly become powerless”,FrihetsNyttsaid.Victor Eriksson, who is the chief editor ofFrihetsNyttand has a long experience in the university world, identifies another pattern behind the protests.“Engaging the university for various issues is pretty much a given, since that is often where theoretical knowledge is found. The fact that you are standing in the way shows, in my eyes, something other than protecting your academic integrity: namely, the activism that permeates the university world and means that you oppose all restrictions on mass immigration,” he noted.Migration minister with a political agendaMeanwhile, in an opinion piece by Forssell published inDagens Industrion 26 June 2025,“Sweden can’t wait for talent”, it was stated: “For Sweden to be a leader of innovation and a hub of excellence in multiple sectors, we need to take concrete steps to actively attract and retain top international talent”. Forssell and the business council appointed by the minister list nine measures to strengthen Sweden as a top destination for talent.“For a long time, the debate has had a one-sided focus on asylum-related migration. We want to broaden that perspective and shift focus from low-skilled to high-skilled labour migration. Because immigration is also about Sweden’s competitiveness.“It is about people who want to work, invest and build a future in our country,” Forssell said, listing nine measures, some also addressing international doctorate students and researchers, that will be considered in order to make the shift away from asylum-related migration to high-skilled labour migration.Tobias Hübinette, who is a senior lecturer at Karlstad University, toldUniversity World News: “The current Swedish right-wing government, supported by the far-right Sweden Democrats, is bound to realise the latter party’s demands according to the so-called Tidö agreement, which was signed in October 2022, but during the course of this process the old right-wing parties of Sweden have been radicalised themselves.“The current example of yet another conflict between academia and the government is a good example of this when a proposal originating from the far-right party is now pushed through without any consideration about the feasibility of creating a citizenship test that is both fair and transparent and possible to test and revise properly.”Lena Adamson, associate professor of psychology and a guest researcher at Stockholm University toldUniversity World News: “Yet again this government shows its authoritarian style and lack of respect for universities and research.“Yes, it’s true that Swedish universities are government agencies and hence are ruled by the government. But does that mean that any minister can give any government agency any assignment?“My answer is ‘no’. Universities’ core mission is clearly stated in the Higher Education Act: education, research and public engagement. To develop a citizenship test is clearly a political mission. And, when in the reverse situation, universities voice political opinions; this is normally not taken well at all.“In my experience from working at three different government agencies, new missions are created in a respectful dialogue between individuals from the (right) ministry and the agency. Professional knowledge from the agency is considered and taken into account. In this case, this would be sophisticated psychometric knowledge.”She added: “To develop a reliable and valid citizenship test will take years. To knowingly not take this into account for a high-stakes test where people’s future lives will be decided is nothing but a total lack of empathy and shows a very callous attitude. To unknowingly not take this into account is nothing but stupidity.“If the government wants to go ahead with this mission (despite the lack of research showing clear links between citizenship requirements and integration), they can simply assign UHR to do a public procurement to companies specialising in test development. (This has been done before, for instance, at the former Higher Education Agency.) And then let universities do what they are supposed to do.”Professor Ole Petter Ottersen, professor emeritus at the University of Oslo and former leader of two of the region’s most internationalised universities – the University of Oslo in Norway and the Karolinska Institutet in Stockholm – toldUniversity World News: “This is a sad development in what should be a trust-based dialogue between universities and government. That universities are instructed to do something that is outside their traditional mission must be seen as an unfortunate example of political interference.“One must respect that the current government wants a citizenship test, but it should be possible to develop such a test in a more flexible way than by challenging what should be a fundamental principle in any democracy: institutional autonomy and academic freedom.”He explained further: “It is now a global megatrend that universities are put under undue political pressure – it happens in many countries, not least in Hungary and the USA – and it would be unfortunate if Sweden were added to this list of countries.“It must be incumbent on the Swedish government to find a solution that does not undermine what is the very basis for societal development: an arm’s length distance between politics and academia.“I have long argued that universities in Sweden and other Nordic countries should be given stronger legal protection to prevent political interference from escalating. This is especially important in Sweden, where universities are state authorities and – as such – easy prey for political intervention.”He concluded: “Perhaps it is time to remind ourselves of the report that served as the blueprint for the rise of the USA as the post-WWII world leader in research, innovation, and education:Science: the Endless Frontier. This report – authored by Vannevar Bush – was published 80 years ago, in July 1945.“In it, Bush highlighted the importance of institutional autonomy as a recipe for progress. It would be a paradox if the celebration of this 80-year anniversary should coincide with a neglect of this principle in one of the world’s top-ranked democracies.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Experts call to scrap the 2023 higher education reform
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Experts call to scrap the 2023 higher education reformGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksDENMARKHumanities applications fall, international students riseDENMARKMinister in crossfire over Danish masters degree reformsDENMARKPoliticians urged to renegotiate masters degree reformsDENMARKRectors call for more flexibility in masters degree reformsDENMARKScale of planned masters reforms unveiled in document leakDENMARKRectors propose ‘less risky’ path to shortened mastersDENMARKBusiness leaders oppose plan for one-year masters degreesDENMARKNew reforms include bid to attract international studentsDENMARKIs higher education set to become an economic handmaiden?DENMARKBusiness moots new plan to attract international studentsDENMARKMore international students needed to plug expertise gapDENMARKPact on international students may reduce numbers by 25%DENMARKUnions voice opposition to humanities cuts at CopenhagenDENMARKCopenhagen University set to cut humanities places by 24%DENMARKRising demand for HE places in large cities adds to problemDENMARKIndustry: ‘No need for more graduates in the humanities’DENMARKCopenhagen humanities jobs axed for third time in eight yearsFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Science - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackDENMARKExperts call to scrap the 2023 higher education reformJan Petter Myklebust25 August 2025TweetAs a poll from the Confederation of Danish Industry (DI) reveals 65% of Danes support the exemption of high-demand subjects from university reforms, higher education stakeholders in Denmark suggest that the 2023 reform has brought the ‘worst of both worlds’: fewer students in university programmes and a continuing decline in welfare-related fields.By excluding qualified applicants, the reform is also predicted to have long-term negative consequences for Denmark’s knowledge economy.“The major reform proves to be a massive failure,”Politikenwrote on July 31. “The young are flunking the politicians’ master plan,”Informationwrote on 1 August. “Fewer young people will be admitted to university. Those rejected shall be studying welfare education instead [teaching, nursing, social work]. According to a new analysis, this is not going to happen”,Weekendavisensaid.“Many young people, despite having high grades and increased grade pressure, have been rejected from their dream university programmes. This has not advanced the government’s educational policy goals of encouraging more young people to pursue studies in fields such as education, teaching or nursing,”Politikensaid.There was marked criticism from universities in 2023 when a majority in parliament backed the structural reform that meant that universities would have to reduce their intake of students by 10% towards 2030, asreportedbyUniversity World News.The vote included a condition that the reduction would be compensated by significantly expanded recruitment of students to so-called welfare education including nursing, teacher training, and social worker professions. But public support does not seem to be in favour.A recent poll for DIshows two thirds of the public believe that high-demand subjects from the reforms is a good idea, with only 11% disagreeing.Admissions fall, rejections increaseAccording to ministry reports toTV2 Denmark, the statistics on the admission to Danish higher education institutions for 2025 to 2026 are:• 59.957 students have been admitted, 1.400 fewer compared to 2024, a fall of 2%.• The five largest subject areas are education, diploma-engineering, nursing, civil-engineering and business economy.• Admission to the professional bachelor degrees for teacher training, education, social work and nursing has fallen by 4%.• Admission to bachelor degrees at universities has fallen by 5% as a consequence of the structural university reform in 2023.• The number of admitted students at the bachelor degree level in the humanities and in languages have fallen by 11 and 19% respectively.• The number of rejected qualified applicants has increased by 12%.The Danish TV station reported that in spite of a DKK2 billion (US$311 million) allocation in the parliament for the strengthening of welfare education in nursing, teacher training, pedagogy and social work, the number of accepted students for 2025 was 10,797, which is a reduction from 2023 on 7.9%.The 3,099 accepted to nursing education is the lowest number since 2009. “The government is throwing billions at higher education but fewer still want to study these subjects,”TV2 Denmarkreported.Meanwhile, the Danish Chamber of Commerce (DE)published a noteon the structural reform on 27 July which analyses the consequences the reduction in admission at universities will have.The note found:• The proportion of a youth cohort that is expected to complete a masters degree has fallen from 29.9% in 2013 to 23.7% in 2023.• The number of admitted students at masters degrees has fallen from 29,500 in 2013 to 25,600 in 2024.• A significant change between the fields of higher education has taken place while the humanities have fallen by 44.5% and the number of students in technological fields has increased by 19.4%. The reduction of the humanities will lead to greater demand.• The Ministry of Finance has estimated that by 2035, as a result of the reforms, there will be a surplus of graduates in welfare education, and the ministry has estimated that the reform will lead to a loss in GNP of DKK13 billion (US$2 billion).Negative consequences for the knowledge economyIn apress releaseon 27 July the Danish Chamber of Commerce stated that admissions at higher education institutions clearly demonstrate that the reform is limiting the intake of students and that many young people that are seeking to do, for instance, IT studies or business degrees are being met by closed doors, even if they are well qualified for admission.“The rejection of more students from IT education in this time of great global insecurities is irresponsible. We already today are lacking IT specialists on a grand scale, and with attacks from Russia and uncertainties about the USA we will have a significantly greater need for IT specialists in the future.“The sector dimension reform simply should be scrapped,” higher education and research head at the Chamber of Commerce Mads Eriksen Storm said.But the government hit back. “Big reforms take time, and I do believe that the combination of the different things we have done, is going to have an impact,” Minister of Higher Education and Research Christina Egelund said.However, experts disagree. Brian Arly Jacobsen, an associate professor in the Department of Cross-Cultural and Regional Studies at the University of Copenhagen and chair of DM University, a union for academic professionals, toldUniversity World News: “The structural reform of university admissions in Denmark was based on the assumption that reduced enrolment at universities would be compensated by increased interest in welfare-related professional educations.“That assumption has clearly failed. We now see the worst of both worlds: fewer students in university programmes and still declining numbers in, for example, nursing and teacher training. This reform does not solve any labour market challenges – it aggravates them.“In addition, turning away qualified applicants from higher education will have long-term negative consequences for Denmark’s knowledge economy.“Instead of top-down limitations, we need policies that respect students’ choices and focus on strengthening the quality and attractiveness of all programmes, including the welfare professions.“A successful knowledge society cannot be built by restricting access, but by investing in education that meets both individual aspirations and societal needs,” he said.Meanwhile, Christoffer Rosenkvist, chair of the National Union of Students in Denmark, toldUniversity World News: “I fully recognise that it is an important priority to ensure a continuous flow of students towards welfare-oriented programmes. However, the results of the 2025 uptake clearly show that the education reform, with the removal of places at universities, is an ineffective policy tool.“While the intention with the sector-dimensioning was to push more young people to take welfare-orientated bachelor programmes, this has shown itself not to be the case. The uptake of students this year underlines that it is not by making higher academic degrees more inaccessible that young peoples’ choices of education are shifted.”Rosenkvist noted: “We should aim to organise our higher educational system such that young applicants’ educational wishes, motivation and proficiencies are defined in the uptake of studies – not untested, ineffective political manoeuvres.“Not only does the ‘dimensioning’ not solve the political aim of the agreement, it also has adverse effects on a number of areas. It will result in further social inequality in the uptake of students at universities, as well as increasing pressure on students by raising the minimum grade point averages for admission by a notable margin for many academic degrees.”Rosenkvist added: “In a time where the need for higher education and qualified young candidates is more essential than ever, I find it difficult to understand why the government continues to support the dimensioning-policy, and I encourage them to repeal this aspect of the reform.“Instead of cutting places, the way forward is to prioritise broad investments across all higher educational sectors, to ensure that education of all forms, from welfare-oriented to academic bachelors, is attractive and motivating for young people.“This should be complemented by bettering the working conditions that newly-graduated young welfare professionals can expect to face once they have finished their degree.“It should come as no surprise that young people recognise and take into consideration the challenges that, for example, nurses and other welfare professionals face in terms of wage levels and working hours, when choosing their educational path.”A ‘better balance’ neededProfessor Hanne Leth Andersen, rector of Roskilde University and chair of the Universities Denmark education committee, toldUniversity World News: “While limiting the number of study places is a logical condition in an education system, we need a better balance between students’ freedom of choice and society’s needs.“Sometimes calculations from ministries with a focus on the economy draw politicians towards simplistic assumptions. This one is simplistic, as has been shown by theDEA(Danish think-thank analysing higher education issues and other analysts).“It is not the number of study places, but the attractivity of the studies and the ensuing possibilities and quality of life that attract students. Therefore, it will probably prove to be important that politicians have also given extra resources to so-called welfare education and that salaries have been raised in the corresponding job areas.“Further, I believe a general respect for care in our societies and an equivalent general understanding of the importance of our children's schooling would make a difference, rather than the number of places.“A supplementary obstacle is the prestige of high admissions demands (manifested by a high average of grades from high school to programmes with few places and many applicants) which make many young people apply for the same programmes.“This has been shown already in 2014 by the Quality Committee (Kvalitetsudvalg) with Jørgen Søndergaard and Nina Smith.“Good students are unlikely to‘waste’ their good average scores on a less high level programme, such as teaching or nursing. The Quality Committee in its report recommended that the admissions system be used more intelligently and actively to ensure a better match.“The individual programmes should set specific admission requirements that reflect the demands placed on the student during the programme, and the average of all grades should play a much less important role, if any,” Leth Andersen said.Calls to scrap the reformSusan Wright, professor of educational anthropology at the Danish School of Education at Aarhus University, toldUniversity World News: “It is a moment when think tanks like DEA, industrial interests like DE, along with university leaders, academics and students are all giving the same message.“The number of places in universities was already falling from 2013 to 2023, whereas the 2023 reform was based on the misplaced fear that they would rise exponentially.“The re-profiling to increase STEM subjects and decrease places in Humanities had already (since the 2003 reform) gone too far. Now the overall decline in Humanities places by 44.5% includes alarming threats to the viability of language courses that are crucial to Denmark’s ability to hold its own in Europe and the world.”Wright noted: “Students will not be bossed around, and just because it becomes harder to get a place in university does not mean they apply for professional bachelor places in the four welfare professions – and maybe the government’s projected labour market needs are wrong there too.“Overall, the students who miss out are those who come from lower incomes without academic parents, further exacerbating social and economic inequalities in Denmark.“The cry from all sides is to scrap the reform. To which I add, rethink the roles of universities that have also been undermined: move beyond a narrow (and often erroneous) labour market calculation, to include education, curiosity and critical thinking as values in themselves, as well as a source of social opportunities and a bastion of democracy.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Hispanic-serving institutions will not be defendedGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESJustice Department ramps up pressure against ‘unlawful’ DEIUNITED STATESTrump proposes 90% cut to funding of tribal collegesGLOBALEquity is under attack, but there are ways to resist – SalmiUNITED STATESUniversity can never be truly free, but it can be affordableFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATESHispanic-serving institutions will not be defendedNathan M Greenfield22 August 2025TweetThe decision of the United States Department of Justice (DoJ) not to mount a defence of Title V of the (Federal) Higher Education Act (HEA), which authorises federal programmes designed for Hispanic-Serving Institutions (HSIs), has made a motion by the Hispanic Association of Colleges and Universities (HACU) to seek intervenor status in the case filed against these programmes last June prescient.In an early morning text message exchange on Saturday 23 August, following the breaking of the story that the DoJ would not be defending by theWashington Free Beacon(WFB), a conservative website, the previous evening, Francisca D Fajana, a lawyer with New York City-based LatinoJustice PRLDEF, toldUniversity World Newsthat she was “unsurprised by the DoJ’s response”.“As we said in our motion to intervene, the DoE is dismantling the department. How can it possibly defend the programme? This confirms what we already knew,” says Fajana, who, along with a lawyer at the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) Foundation Tennessee, filed the case on HACU’s behalf.As of 5pm on Friday, the DoE had not filed any papers in court. Accordingly, Fajana explained, the case is still pending. If the court grants HACU’s motion, it would make the organisation that represents the more than 600 HSIs in 29 states plus Washington, DC and Puerto Rico a party to the case and, thus, would allow Fajana to defendTitle V, which was added to the HEA in 1992.Title V gives, as its raison d’etre, to “expand and enhance the academic offerings, programme quality, and institutional stability of colleges and universities that are educating the majority of Hispanic college students and helping large number of Hispanic students and other low-income individuals complete post-secondary degrees”.TheWFB’s report that the DoE was folding its cards was based ona letter dated 25 July from US Solicitor, General D John Sauer, to the Speaker of the House of Representatives, Louisiana Republican, Mike Johnson. In a part of the letter quoted byWSB, Sauer gives as the DoJ’s reason the determination that “those provisions violate the equal-protection component of the Fifth Amendment’s Due Process Clause”.This is one of the central arguments put forward by TN and SFFA presented to the US District Court for the Eastern District of Tennessee (Knoxville Division), and which was reiterated on 8 August when the National Association of Scholars (NAS) and Faculty, Alumni and Students Opposed to Racial Preferences (FASORP) filed a joint motion for intervenor status to oppose the HSI programme.NAS’ and FSAORP’s submission, which is supported by TN and SFFA, argues that “through these programmes, the federal government discriminates against faculty in their schools because of the race of their students [and]...incentivises schools (including state public schools) to violate federal civil rights statutes (and the public schools to violate the Fourteenth Amendment) [that is, the amendment’s “equal protection clause]. That’s wrong. It’s illegal. It must stop.”The motion brands the funds earmarked by Congress for HSIs a “bounties-for-race balancing programme”.Though Sauer’s letter uses more judicious languageto say much the same thing, it declared the HSIs programme’s “outright racial balancing” is “patently unconstitutional”.BackgroundThe HSIs programme was authorised in 1992 and began distributing funds three years later.“The purpose of this programme is to improve the quality of education; intuitional capacity building will benefit everyone who’s there, including the preponderance of Hispanic students enrolled [in a specific school],” says Deborah Santiago, co-founder and CEO of the Washington, DC-based Excelencia in Education, an NGO that works to improve educational opportunities for Latino Americans.In 2025, Congress allocated US$229 million for HSIs and another US$94 million for HSI-STEM articulation agreements (these last being links between community colleges and four-year, BA-granting, institutions), for new and multi-year grants. Unlike other minority-serving institutions, such as Tribal Colleges, of which there are 35 – each one being chartered by either the federal government or an Indian Tribe – the number of HSIs changes yearly.To be designated an HSI, the institution must meet three criteria; in 2024, 602 institutions did, and 39% were community colleges.They must have a high concentration of low-income students (that is, who are eligible to receive Pell Grants made to the nation’s poorest students) and a low E and G [attainment levels]; low educational expenditures, which, explains Santiago, “means that the institution does not have a lot of extraneous resources, endowments [or] big research grants, so the bulk of their resources are in education and general expenditures”.The third criterion is Hispanics make up 25% (or more) of the institution’s full-time-equivalent enrolment.Simply qualifying as an HSI does not result in an institution receiving funding under the HSIs federal programme. Grants are made on a competitive basis.According to Excelencia in Education, in fiscal year 2024, the last year for which complete data is available, US$28 million in new awards were made to 49 colleges and universities. The largest award was for US$600,000, while the smallest was for US$355,252; the average grant was US$573,184.What does the complaint state?The State of Tennessee’s complaint argues that it, its public institutions and its low-income Hispanic students are injured because none of its public institutions can access HSI funds because none of the schools meet the 25% Hispanic enrolment threshold.Forty-seven per cent of the students at the University of Memphis receive Pell Grants, and 62% of the students are racial or ethnic minorities. “But because the university lacks the ‘right’ ethnic make-up, it can’t receive an HSI grant,” says the complaint.Even if its colleges and universities met the 25% threshold, the programme would still break anti-discrimination laws, the complaint filed by the Republican-led State of Tennessee says.The complaint further argues that the “25% ethnic quota has nothing to do with the history of the institution, like whether it has historically served (or discriminated against) Hispanics”, and is, therefore, “arbitrary”.The HSI programme, TN alleges, puts its colleges and universities in an unconstitutional dilemma.“Either they continue to serve their Hispanic students lawfully, in which case they are ineligible for grants under the programme, or else they engage in affirmative action to satisfy the programme’s discriminatory criterion, which is illegal.”The Supreme Court of the United States’ (SCOTUS) outlawed affirmative action in 2023 in a case brought by SFFA against Harvard University and the University of North Carolina (Chapel Hill).Additionally, TN asserts, to receive grants, colleges would have to develop plans for assisting “Hispanic students”, which itself would be “dispositive evidence of a race- or ethnic-based intention for official government action”. [“Dispositive” means something that on its own can determine the outcome of a court case; in other words, here it means that plans, including tutoring designed solely for Hispanic students, would indicate that the HSI is unconstitutional.]Accordingly, the HSI programme “encourages Tennessee institutions and employees to violate the Constitution”, says the complaint.In its part of the complaint, SFFA alleges that “Member A” (a professor at an unnamed institution) has suffered harm because he cannot apply for a grant under the HSI programme because his institution is not recognised as an HSI.SFFA further argues that, in enacting the HSI programme, Congress exceeded its power under the Spending Clause of the Constitution and that the programme violates the Equal Protection clauses in the Fifth and Fourteenth Amendments. Quoting the SCOTUS decision that banned affirmative action, the complaint says governments cannot “remedy the effects of societal discrimination through explicitly race-based measures”.The defenceHACU’s motion to intervene is not a rebuttal to TN’s and SFFA’s complaint, still less is it a response to Sauer’s letter to Johnson. Yet, in seeking intervenor status, an outline of its defence of Title V of the HEA can be discerned, as can what a rebuttal to Sauer’s letter would sound like.The motionbegins by explaining to the court that HACU’s interest in the case is more than academic. HACU is the national organisation of the HSIs located in 30 states, Washington, DC, and Puerto Rico, and these institutions educate some 67% of Hispanic students – and, as such, if the HSIs programme were declared unconstitutional, HACU would be injured.In support of its argument that the HSI’s programme’s race awareness rule is constitutional, HACU then cites a 2024 SCOTUS decision in a case involving redistricting of Congressional districts (which the Constitution requires be done after every decennial census): “‘There is nothing nefarious about [Congress’] awareness of racial demographics or ethnic disparity.”This precedent is supported by a 2007 decision in which the court decided that “asking Congress – as Plaintiffs do – to turn a blind eye to ethnic disparity or forgo affording resources to those who need them ‘imposea counterproductive duty of ignorance on the part of public officials’”.Central to HACU’s motion to intervene is its claim that “The Defendants” (that is, the DoE and McMahon) will not adequately represent HAUC’s interests (as representative of the HSIs).HAUC uses McMahon’s own words to demonstrate why it fears the DoE’s lawyers will not forcefully defend the HEA. The Secretary of Education says she “is on a ‘final mission’ to eliminate the very agency that administers the HSIs programme and thereby end the programme’s benefits to Proposed Intervenor’s constituents, including 5.6 million students attending HSIs”.Accordingly, HACU’s intervention “is thus necessary to ensure a vigorous and full-throttled defence of the constitutionality of the HSIs programme and afford the Proposed Intervenor an opportunity to mount an appeal in the advent of an adverse ruling,” says the motion. (Appeals are accepted only if the petitioner can demonstrate either a mistake in fact or law.)Abolishing the DoE requires Congressional action. In the meantime, as has been covered byUniversity World News, at the same time that Elon Musk’s “Department of Government Efficiency” (DOGE) was leading departments to fire thousands of workers, McMahon oversaw draconian cuts at DoE. At the ceremony in which President Donald J Trump named McMahon as Secretary of Education, he said:“I hope you do a great job and put yourself out of a job” by dismantling the DoE.“The staff of the Department of Education has been reduced by half,” Fajana toldUniversity World Newsin an interview a few days before theWFBreported that the DoJ would not be defending the HSI programmes. “So, it is not a secret that it’s highly unlikely that this Department of Education will vigorously defend the constitutionality of the HSI programme."The DoJ’s decision not to defend the HSIs leaves open an interesting question about coordination within Trump’s administration. For as late as May 2, when Trump submitted the FY 2026 budget to Congress, funding of the HSIs was intact at the FY 2025 levels. As Santiago explained before word of the government’s decision not to defend the programme was known: “To me, that is an indicator that at the time this case was not on their radar or was something to eliminate.”The “Factual Background” part of HAUC’s motion explained that Congress established the HSI programme because it found that HSIs were “underfunded – including because they ‘receive significantly less State and local funding, per full-time equivalent student,’ than non-HSIs – which ‘limitthe ability of [HSIs] to expand and improve [their] academic programmes”.Among the reasons why HSIs are underfunded in comparison to peer institutions is that they do not receive the kind of research and other government grants that highly selective and wealthy institutions do, nor do they have the kind of endowment or alumni support that other institutions do, says Santiago.Another reason is that a disproportionate number of HSIs are community colleges (many in inner cities), which are traditionally the least well funded of America’s public colleges and universities.The motion to intervene disputes the part of the complaint that says benefits of HSIs designation accrue only to Hispanic students. Rather, it states, “grants simply allow eligible institutions that have been historically underfunded to strengthen their programmes for all students, including the large proportion of Hispanic students they serve”.As Santiago explained: “There are 15 allowable activities that an institution can do. These include academic and faculty development, student support services (such as tutoring), endowment improvement and library and lab improvement.“Our research has shown that approximately 2/3 of the money goes to faculty development or student support services. We see that many institutions invest in areas that are tied to student success.”It is a measure of how strong the credo is that, after learning that the government would not defend the HSIs, in her text toUniversity World News, Fajana’s words still echoed what is often called the American creed, symbolised by the Statue of Liberty in New York Harbour, nine miles south of her office on the west side of Manhattan Island.“America is meant to be a land of opportunity where fairness prevails. It seems unjust to entrench ethnic disparities which was the problem Congress sought to address by creating the HSIs programme. HSIs serve nearly two thirds of Latino students and are under resourced. The programme attempts to address funding gaps while simultaneously serving ALL students - a classic win win," she stated.An attack on race-related policiesEven before hearing that the DoJ would not defend Title V, Fajana was less than sanguine, partially because of what she sees as the overall strategy of organisations like the SFFA. The organisation is funded by right-wing and libertarian foundations with ties to the Federalist Society. In 2022,theYale Daily Newsreportedthat SFFA’s largest donors are part of “a network of primary libertarian and conservative lawyers” - every one of the six Republican members of the nine-member SCOTUS either was or is a member of the Federalist Society.According to Fajana: “Since the SFFA decision came down in 2023, organisations like the SFFA have filed almost, if not more than, 100 cases deploying the SFFA decision to attack ‘race conscious,’ ‘race neutral’ and other programmes like the HSIs that are just simply ‘race aware’.”Fajana pointed to the fact that the DoJ recently decided not to mount a defence inanother casefiled by the SFFA against race-conscious admissions programmes at the US Military Academy at West Point (West Point), New York and the Air Force Academy (AFA) in Colorado Springs.In this case, the government settled out of court with SFFA. The settlement between the DoJ and SFFA, which covers West Point and the AFA will see them discontinue these policies - even though the majority decision in the SFFA case that outlawed affirmative action containeda carve-outfor the nation’s military academies.For the SFFA and its allies, says Fajana, “it’s the naming of ‘race’ that is the real problem, not the fact that enduring racial and ethnic disparities remain in the United States”.(TheWFBall but invited the DoJ to “gut other congressionally established programmes that dole out grants based on an institution’s racial make-up” by saying that “Congress wouldn’t need to repeal those programmes in order for the Trump administration to shut them down. The Justice Department could simply take the plaintiff’s side each time a programme is sued, letting legal inertia run its course.”The programmesWFBhas in mind are the Predominately Black Institutions (PBIs), which are distinguished from the more famous Historically Black Colleges and Universities. HBCUs are eligible for federal funds if they, the PBIs, have at least 40% Black enrolment as well as the programmes that provides grants to colleges with at least a 10% enrolment of Asian American and American Pacific Islanders).According to Fajana: “It doesn’t matter if you look at graduation rates between Latinos and white Americans or white Americans and Asian Americans and African Americans, disparities endure.“Disparities endure in who’s attending college. Disparities endure in who’s graduating from college,” says Fajana.Her next words, “How do we redress these enduring racial and ethnic disparities if we cannot name ‘race’ and afford resources to institutions that are trying to level the playing field,” were said in a resigned tone. Her tone bespeaks the distance America has travelled from the early 1990s, when a Democratic-led Congress passed the amendments that would add the HSIs to the HEA – and a conservative Republican president, George HW Bush, signed the bill into law.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyETHIOPIAHE sector may benefit from World Trade Organization movesSamuel GetachewandKeith Nuthall21 August 2025TweetThe Ethiopian government’s bid to join the World Trade Organization (WTO), if successful, may help the Horn of Africa country reform and expand its higher education sector, experts claim.Addis Ababa has repeatedly stressed its intention to secure membership by the next WTO ministerial conference, the organisation’s ruling body, to be staged in Yaoundé, Cameroon, from 25-29 March 2026.This has been supported by WTO Director General Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, a Nigerian, who noted in April 2025 that Ethiopia is the most populous nation in its region (its population exceeds 128 million), “notably with a significant young population which will be entering the job market”.She said Ethiopia (once a communist country) had a “legacy of strong state presence which they are gradually reducing through market-based economic and legislative reforms”.She noted that Prime Minister Abiy Ahmed Ali’s ‘Home-Grown Economic Reform Programme’, aimed at boosting private-sector growth through foreign exchange reforms, liberalisation of key services and reduced restrictions on foreign direct investment, had resulted in “strong economic growth, averaging over 6% annually since 2022”. She did not mention the armed conflict in Ethiopia’s Tigray and Amhara regions in the past five years.While those struggles continue to simmer, with Human Rights Watch and others accusing the Ethiopian government ofhuman rights abusesand censorship, WTO membership could promote higher education in the country.WTO and higher educationEthiopia’s Minister of Trade and Regional Integration, Kassahun Gofe Balami, has been leading the country’s WTO negotiation efforts, noting how reforms would need to be fully compliant with WTO rules to “enhance economic resilience ... and create a conducive business environment ...”That would include Ethiopian access to the WTO General Agreement on Trade in Services, or (GATS).This is one of the WTO’s foundational agreements, where its 166 member jurisdictions (with the European Union representing its 27 countries) have to offer the same freedoms to access service markets, such as higher education, to all WTO members.Any carve-outs for specific countries can be negotiated during membership talks but cannot be imposed after accession, without a special waiver.All these restrictions are subject to WTO review and are supposed to last only 10 years and must be declared publicly. Member states are also not supposed to impose new restrictions on foreign service providers that exceed those mandated for domestic services, although Ethiopia can negotiate conditions and qualifications during membership talks.In higher education, WTO member states can impose quotas on the total number of higher education suppliers, limits on foreign capital participation and pay local institutions more subsidies than foreign players, said a WTO note oneducation commitmentsby members.It said: “Many countries have introduced important reforms in their education systems in recent years, allowing the entry of foreign suppliers of educational services,” because of population growth and developments in information technology, or IT.It added that issues of accreditation, quality assurance, recognition of foreign degrees and qualifications are also increasingly important for WTO members and reflected in their GATS liberalisation commitments.In ongoing WTO talks to upgrade GATS, member states have sought to better delineate ‘higher’ and ‘adult’ education services in agreements and add additional services to the agreement, such as training, testing and education community services.Alignment with international systemsJemal Mohammed, a senior researcher at the Policy Studies Institute in Addis Ababa, predicts that Ethiopia’s higher education sector will benefit: “WTO accession opens the door to foreign investment in education which can raise teaching standards, infrastructure quality and curriculum innovation through the introduction of global best practices,” he said.“Greater alignment with international systems may make it easier for Ethiopian students to study abroad, transfer credits and access globally recognised certifications and increased competition and efficiency.”Blene Aklilu Betemariam, an education leader and human capital development adviser based in the capital, Addis Ababa, believes that WTO membership can help Ethiopia improve its education sector, including boosting its graduation rates.According to a UNESCOreportreleased last January (2024) only 8% of Ethiopian males enrol in higher education and 13% of women.This is a problem, given the country’s fast economic expansion: “The rising demand for these skill sets will necessitate improved qualification for trainers, educators, educational leaders and institutional providers, ultimately leading to a high quality of education,” Blene toldUniversity World News.“If executed effectively, WTO membership could create a multiplier effect, resulting in a substantial improvement in quality, relevance and competitiveness in both local and global markets for local education,” she said.“It could create pathways to stimulate private-sector growth, opening up increased demand for globally competitive, specialised, mid- to high-level skill sets,” she said, noting that this could also boost education skills in Ethiopia.“It would enhance the relevance and responsiveness of the local education curriculum and skills development on the supply side,” she added.Can WTO membership support innovation?Solomon Assefa, an Ethiopian researcher and commentator based in Germany, stressed toUniversity World Newshow WTO membership obliges member states to comply with standardised governance and business practices that can help Ethiopia innovate: “China’s trade and innovation boomed after its membership and I believe aspiring and emerging African nations such as ours could also benefit from streamlined trade and promote innovation,” said Assefa.“Much of the development in poor countries in areas of innovation is negatively impacted by inefficiency and lack of transparency in cross-border trade,” he stressed. Ethiopia’s economy certainly has significant development needed – according to the World Bank, its annual gross national incomeper capita(with purchasing power parity considered) in 2024 was just US$3,270.The Ethiopian government, aware of the urgent need to reform its state-run economy has been liberalising much of its economy, such as its banking and telecommunications sector – in July (2025) allowing foreign banks and investors to enter its financial sector, for example.Blene said such change requires new skills, whose development should be promoted by WTO memberships, offering graduates well-paid local jobs in which to practise their new abilities.“With careful design of its regulatory frameworks and urgent implementation of policy reforms at meeting market demands while addressing skills gaps through targeted investment in education, WTO membership could create a multiplier effect, resulting in a substantial improvement in quality, relevance and competitiveness in both local and global market,” she argued.Mezemir Girma, a lecturer in literature and founder of a public library at Debre Berhan University, in the strife-torn region of Amhara, argued that WTO membership will open opportunities for Ethiopian academics: “Such a partnership offers us a chance to publish our work to an international audience, be able to compete in the global market and find employment opportunities to teach globally and ultimately earn an adequate living wage that we do not earn otherwise,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica 埃塞俄比亞高等教育部門或將受惠於世界貿易組織的動向。該國政府正積極爭取在2026年3月的WTO部長會議之前獲得會員資格，專家指出，這將促進高等教育改革與擴展。WTO總幹事表示，埃塞俄比亞擁有龐大的年輕人口，這對提升其教育體系至關重要。若成功加入WTO，將可吸引外資，提升教學標準和基礎設施，並促進課程創新。同時，這也有助於改善畢業率，滿足快速經濟增長所需的技能需求。

Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredSOUTH AFRICABlack international students are resilient, despite challengesWachira Kigotho21 August 2025TweetWhat are the challenges experienced by black international students in the 21st century at South African universities? What strategies do those students use to develop resilience skills in those institutions?Those are some of the key questions that Dr Peace Ginika Nwokedi, a lecturer and researcher in teacher education at the University of Johannesburg, and Fumane Portia Khanare, an associate professor of educational psychology at the University of the Western Cape in South Africa, pose in a contribution to a newbook,Internationalisation of Higher Education for Sustainable Development: A Southern African Perspective, by Brill, an international publisher of academic books.In a chapter titled,Black International Students in the 21st-Century South African University: Resiliencies Amid Adversities, the two academics focused on how South African public universities had been receiving students from Sub-Saharan Africa more than their counterparts in other countries in the region.The issue is that, since the end of apartheid in South Africa, international students from other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa have been attracted to South Africa, not necessarily to experience cultural diversity, but to take advantage of high-quality education in well-developed South African universities.In this regard, Nwokedi and Khanare pointed out that, since the dawn of the 21st century, South African universities have witnessed a significant influx and increase in the mobility of students across international borders, mostly from other African countries.“The diversity and quality of education in these universities enrich the learning environment,” stated the two scholars.Nevertheless, it had been a deliberate policy of the South African government to increase the footprint of international students, especially those from other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa.Currently, of the slightly over one million students enrolled in South African universities, about 6.5% are international students, mainly from Sub-Saharan Africa, and more so from the neighbouring countries which are members of the Southern African Development Community, or SADC.According to UNESCO’s Institute of Statistics, South Africa is the second-most attractive destination for Sub-Saharan African mobile students after France.Challenges for black international studentsHowever, according to Nwokedi and Khanare, most black international students who go to South African universities to pursue higher education encounter challenges that include language barriers, culture shock, financial pressures, cultural adjustments, institutional xenophobia, alienation, and low self-esteem.Citing astudyby Dr Bonginkosi Hardy Mutongoza, a lecturer of education and a social justice researcher at the University of Fort Hare, the co-authors of the chapter under review specifically noted that language barriers experienced by black international students caused alienation, loneliness and a lack of sense of belonging in South Africa.Similarly, in addition to family separation, some of those students also experience immigration difficulties, accommodation problems, and limited professional development opportunities.Besides, according to Mutongoza, learning in a foreign environment involves students encountering unfamiliar learning and teaching methods, cultural taboos, new lifestyles, and conflicting expectations, of which black international students joining South African universities are not an exception.Despite those challenges, Nwokedi and Khanare noted that most black international students still succeed in South African universities, excelling academically and contributing to university life through their engagement and participation in various activities and initiatives due to their resilience.Resilience-building skillsDefining resilience as the ability to develop a combination of mental, emotional and social skills to overcome adversity, Nwokedi and Khanare noted that many black international students in South Africa have managed to create a mindset and behaviour that allows them to complete their studies successfully.As culture shock and adjusting to a different environment have been significant challenges for black international students in South African universities, Nwokedi and Khanare noted that those students have developed cultural resilience through engaging in activities that promoted their cultural practices.“Association with the cultural or ethnic identity of their countries of origin has been one of the significant elements that has enabled black international students in South Africa to develop adaptive coping mechanisms to build their resilience in their new environment,” stated Nwokedi and Khanare.In this context, black international students, especially from Zimbabwe and Nigeria, have built strong relationships among themselves, practising self-care, developing social problem-solving skills and fostering a sense of purpose for having left their home countries and being in South Africa.In another contribution based on specific experiences of international postgraduate students at a South African university, Nwokedi and associates, which included Khanare and Samuel Nnadoziem Ndukuba, a lecturer in civil engineering at Cape Peninsula University of Technology, noted that foreign postgraduate students, especially those from other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa, have developed personal skills and academic skills that shaped their learning and enabled them to achieve their educational goals.According to those academics, black international students in South African universities, whether undergraduates or postgraduates, have also developed resilience through doing group assignments in their academic work as well as establishing a good rapport with their lecturers and supervisors.To strengthen their resilience, black international students have also been quick to seek the support of university services when they are available and build relationships with their South African peers.Contributions to higher educationExploring the role played by black international students towards the sustainable development of higher education in South Africa, Nwokedi and her associates noted that they bring with them diverse ideas, experiences and perspectives that lead to academic excellence and innovation in hosting universities.The issue is that, although black international students bring a financial stream into the South African economy, they also help to reduce regional social and educational inequalities by promoting intercultural interactions and partnerships in Sub-Saharan Africa.But, despite such positive trends, Nwokedi and her group noted that this has not been easy for many black international students who felt isolated and excluded by their local peers and community due to the language barrier. “I find it very difficult to communicate with the locals here, as they make fun of me, and this makes me develop a lack of sense of belonging in this community,” one student told Nwokedi.Several undergraduate and postgraduate students whom Nwokedi’s group interviewed expressed concerns over exclusionary practices that occur in lecture rooms and group work. “I found it difficult to interact or communicate with my classmates during lectures as they always sit according to their cultures or race. This makes me feel isolated and lonely,” said another student.The process of acquiring and renewing official documents to study in South African universities was also identified as another problem that black international students regularly encountered, as study visas are renewed annually. One student told the researchers: “The South African immigration policy is very rigid for the black international students. I have been waiting for two years after I applied for a visa renewal, and I have still not heard or got any information from them.”Subsequently, some students expressed frustration over neglect by the university administrators, whom they claimed were unwilling to assist. “Most of the staff in the international students’ office here are not willing to listen or help us if we have issues that urgently need to be resolved,” they said.There are also indicators that black international students, especially undergraduates, suffer financial constraints, as most of them pay for their education in South African universities, as they are not on scholarship, either from their countries of origin or from South Africa. One of the interview participants said: “Scholarships and funding are for South African students, and international students are excluded.”Black international students also complain of accommodation fees, whether at the universities or in private hostels. “Accommodation fees here are too unbearable. It makes me struggle to concentrate on my studies, as I am always thinking of how to pay my expenses,” one student told Nwokedi’s team.However, some of the participants said they have not only built confidence but have set personal goals, engaging in independent learning and participating in extracurricular programmes. The issue is that, beyond finding refuge and resilience in their cultural focus groups, most black international students are successfully engaging in academic tasks.For instance, one of them said: “I can analyse data very well now and develop my educational perspectives and philosophy.” Another told researchers that she has become a professional researcher and has acquired skills to enhance learning and research knowledge.Nwokedi and her team found that many black international students, primarily postgraduate students, are engaged in independent learning and research activities. Others are doing well in university sports.Promoting sustainable developmentNonetheless, despite various economic, cultural and social implications, Nwokedi and her associates noted that the presence of black international students in South African universities was promoting sustainable development through supporting many students from other countries in Sub-Saharan Africa.Nwokedi and her group, in their recommendations, called for further support of black international students through the enactment of policies that would promote increased internationalisation of university campuses, as well as encourage cultural diversity and inclusion in those institutions.In this regard, the study recommends that South African universities develop and implement inclusive policies that will support black international students and engage them in sustainability initiatives that would enhance their varied experiences.However, Nwokedi and her associates, in their current research, have established that, now and probably into the future, black international students will continue to be capable, active and resilient towards taking advantage of a sustainable learning environment in the South African universities.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & 南非大學的黑色國際學生展現出顯著的韌性，儘管面臨語言障礙、文化衝擊及經濟壓力等挑戰。根據約翰尼斯堡大學的Dr. Peace Ginika Nwokedi和西開普大學的Fumane Portia Khanare的研究，這些學生不僅為南非的高等教育帶來多樣化的觀點和經驗，還積極參與大學生活，促進學術卓越和創新。儘管存在排斥和孤立感，多數黑色國際學生依然成功適應並取得學業成就。研究者呼籲南非大學應加強對黑色國際學生的支持，制定包容性政策，促進文化多樣性和可持續發展。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: ‘Obscene’ executive order seen as ‘full-on assault on HE’
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: ‘Obscene’ executive order seen as ‘full-on assault on HE’GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsPresident Donald Trump, Image: The White House, Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESState legislators have cast a ‘web of control’ over HEUNITED STATESJustice Department ramps up pressure against ‘unlawful’ DEIUNITED STATESTrump proposes 90% cut to funding of tribal collegesUNITED STATESTrump tells Congress to cut basic science research by 34%UNITED STATESTrump’s ‘Big Bill’ to cost students, universities billionsUNITED STATESTrump administration asks commission to decertify HarvardUNITED STATESAll hopes turn to Congress to soften Trump’s assault on HEGLOBALTrump’s travel ban affects students from 19 countriesUNITED STATESTrump’s bill cuts support for low-income students in HEUNITED STATESTrump’s ongoing attacks on HE ‘reverberate across borders’UNITED STATESTrump administration cuts extra US$450 million to HarvardUNITED STATESHarvard stands firm against Trump’s ‘retaliatory’ threatsSOUTH AFRICA-GLOBALAcademy of Science warns of ‘extreme crisis’ under TrumpUNITED STATESTrump orders HE accreditation overhaul to stamp out DEIFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology -
