Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackUNITED KINGDOMSkills crisis without high-level university apprenticeshipsNic Mitchell24 July 2025TweetUniversities are in danger of becoming a footnote in a planned skills revolution in the United Kingdom if the government presses ahead with equating skills with the further education sector and axes Level 7 (masters-level) degree apprenticeships as it shifts funding to lower-level apprentice qualifications for 16- to 21-year-olds.That’s one of the key warnings in areporttitledSkills Sidelined: The importance of higher level skills provision to meet government ambitions, from the Social Market Foundation (SMF), a cross-party think tank. It claims that the Labour government’s approach to skills training is “muddled” – with mass skills shortages and a confusing skills training landscape holding back efforts to achieve economic growth.Despite pledging to reform the “confusing” skills landscape and launching a comprehensive review of post-16 education and a new body, Skills England, the SMF says government policy details remain light and that universities are being sidelined from plugging critical skills shortages, particularly among more mature and mid-career workers.Unlock universities’ potentialDani Payne, head of education and social mobility at SMF, said: “If the government is serious about tackling the skills crisis and delivering growth, it must unlock the full potential of our universities to provide high-quality, higher-level apprenticeships.“These courses are proven to deliver the kinds of skills we urgently need – in sectors like healthcare, construction, and digital.“But right now, universities face a funding system that doesn’t reward this kind of provision and layers of bureaucracy that actively make it harder to deliver.”She urged the government to use its upcoming Post-16 Education and Skills White Paper to “build a skills system that works” and says: “Universities must be part of that vision.”Dr Diana Beech, a former (Conservative) government higher education adviser, who was chief executive at London Higher before taking up a new role as director of the Finsbury Institute at the recently merged City St George’s University in London, welcomed the SMF findings.Level 7 degree apprenticeshipsSpeaking as someone who is just four months away from completing a Level 7 masters degree apprenticeship for a dual qualification in leadership and management, Beech said she enrolled in the apprenticeship programme, which is funded through an employers’ levy, because she was running a major higher education charitable-status organisation but without any training in handling big budgets or managing people.“Like many people today, I progressed in my career through a series of fixed-term appointments and precarious employment terms and didn’t get much of a chance to receive in-work training and development.“So, after becoming a first-time CEO at London Higher, I was humble enough to ask my board for support in gaining a strong grounding in the business and management skills to help me.“I learnt a lot from the whole process, which I’ve written about in aseries of blogson theLondon Higherwebsite,” she toldUniversity World News.Labour axing fundingThe incoming Labour government announced its intention to axe most of the funding for Level 7 degree apprenticeships last autumn after years of criticism that too much of the money raised by the apprenticeship levy – a tax on the payroll of employers – was being spent on higher-level courses to upskill existing employees.From January 2026, government funding for Level 7apprenticeshipswill only be available for learners aged under 21, although existing apprentices will continue to receive funding until they complete their training.This shift is part of a government strategy to redirect investment towards younger people to improve early career prospects and reduce youth unemployment by spending more on apprenticeships at the intermediate level.Disappointing for employersVivienne Stern, chief executive of Universities UK, said the government move to withdraw funding for Level 7 apprenticeships for anyone over 22 was “disappointing news for many universities and, more importantly, employers”.She said universities had been encouraged to grow the high-level apprenticeship provision by the last government and by employers and student demand.“These apprenticeships have become extremely valuable to many sectors and essential public services.“In 2023 and 2024 there were 2,040 Level 7 apprenticeship starts in Health, Public Services and Care apprenticeships – a figure that has significantly grown in the last five years.“The reality is that the National Health Service will need to continue to fund this training, but it will now have to be taken from existing budgets.“The exemption for those aged 21 and under will leave so few students on these courses eligible for funding that many will become unviable at a time when Skills England’s forecasts show we need more high-level skills in the economy,” she noted.Footnote in government plansBeech said she fears that “universities will be a footnote in the government’s plans for skills given its ambition to improve conditions for further education and its continued tendency to equate skills with the college sector”.She said higher education risks only being considered in the context of lifelong learning and increasing Level 4 and Level 5 provision and warns that not all universities offer programmes in that space, which is at the sub-degree level.“I think the decision to axe funding for Level 7 (masters) courses is very short-sighted and illustrates the lack of join-up in government – with the Health Secretary, for example, crying out for better leaders in the NHS and the Education Secretary cutting off the very routes that are used to upskill these leaders,” she noted.Universities only developed courses, such as theone she is takingat Bayes Business School, to respond to government calls to align provision better with workforce needs, said Beech.Pulling the plug“And when they finally get something working, the government goes and pulls the plug on funding, making it impossible for the public sector and small businesses to access the training they need.“A far better solution would have been to restrict public funding to those that need it the most (NHS, schools, councils, charities, SMEs) while the large corporations that can afford to pay for it can do so,” said Beech.The SMF report said higher level vocational training, like degree apprenticeships, was vital to tackle Britain’s chronic skills gaps and said their growth and popularity since 2015 had led to demand far outstripping supply as they helped to fill the 500,000 job vacancies due to a shortage of skilled applicants.The SMF paper said that universities are being held back from providing more placements on these degree apprenticeships, due to poor funding incentives, bureaucratic hurdles, and a lack of involvement in government discussions.Mindset holds back expansionIn addition, some older or higher-tariff universities are still too focused on traditional academic prestige or research and still see vocational training as outside their mission, despite growing demand for practical, work-based learning.They don’t see themselves as part of the skills and employment system – a mindset that may hold back expansion of vocational routes, the report claims.The report said the funding model prioritises universities’ research, traditional degrees, and widening access, and many institutions struggle to justify expanding degree apprenticeships without a more predictable and supportive funding model.Providers of apprenticeships also have to deal with two separate regulatory bodies: the Office for Students and Ofsted, which the report said creates duplication of effort, red tape, and confusion.Despite Level 7 (masters) provision likely to be almost entirely defunded, the SMF report recommends expanding Level 6 bachelor-level apprenticeships in key shortage and growth sectors, as well as widening opportunity in regions that have low levels of participation in higher education.Collaborate rather than competeTo overcome barriers holding back the success of higher-level skills provision, the report says institutions must collaborate with each other and with employers – despite the market encouraging competition.“Universities are (also) often left out of local skills planning,” claims the SMF report, which was sponsored byUniversity Alliance, a representative body for 16 of the UK’s leading professional and technical universities.Its chief executive, Vanessa Wilson, described theSkills sidelinedreport as “timely” in showing how vital universities are to delivering the high-level skills the economy needs through degree apprenticeships and vocationally focussed education, particularly in crucial sectors like healthcare and construction.“But they are being hamstrung by bureaucratic overload, financial disincentives, and a shifting policy and funding landscape.“The government must not have tunnel vision when it comes to skills: without a balanced approach across all levels and ages, it will struggle to build a skilled, adaptable workforce,” said Wilson.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsCHINANew plan to boost ‘elite’ engineering education in ChinaAmber Wang23 July 2025TweetAs part of its strong focus on developing a highly skilled science, technology and engineering workforce to support the country’s plans to transition to high-end manufacturing and technology, China’s Ministry of Education this month unveiled new plans to strengthen and accelerate the reform of engineering education and cultivate more ‘elite’ talent, particularly in shortage fields such as semiconductors and other strategic areas.The Ministry said it would establish specialist national graduate colleges for ‘elite’ engineers at more than half of all institutions offering engineering masters and doctoral degrees by 2030, piloting new accreditation standards and releasing a set of national standards later this year for ‘elite’ engineering training.The new national graduate colleges for elite engineers would be promoted overseas, while the Ministry will also seek international recognition for its new ‘elite’ training standards.The new benchmarks aim to support international cooperation and fill a gap in global engineering accreditation systems, the Ministry said.Professor Zhao Weisheng, vice-president of Beihang University and secretary general of the Chinese Union for Training Excellent Engineers, a representative body of the elite engineer programme, pointed to existing frameworks like the Washington Accord (focused on undergraduate equivalency) and the EUR-ACE system, covering bachelor and masters levels in Europe.“Engineering education certification that focuses on master's and doctoral training is still missing,” he said.The Ministry said it will continue to bring in leading international engineering experts to help develop its graduate engineering education system and promote more regular exchanges between Chinese and foreign faculty and students.Continuation of pilot elite engineering schemeThe expansion marks the next phase of reforms under the government’s ‘elite’ engineer education and training programme launched in 2022.Under a pilot scheme, 40 such colleges were established at leading universities, along with four innovation institutes in Beijing, Shanghai and Guangdong province’s Dongguan and Foshan, which are both manufacturing hubs.A hallmark of the programme is close partnerships between state-owned enterprises and academia, with joint recruitment and training of students, as well as shared projects.According to the Ministry, this will equip students to contribute to industrial innovation, particularly in areas where technological bottlenecks persist.The Ministry has also introduced a new evaluation system for graduate engineering degrees, broadening student assessment criteria to include patents, practical reports, and innovative accomplishments, instead of focusing exclusively on academic papers.This shift also aligns with China’s recently enacted Degree Act, which allows students pursuing professional degrees to fulfil graduation requirements through practical outcomes. Scholarsnotedthis could also open up new opportunities for international joint degree programmes and research collaboration.Three years into its initial rollout, the ‘elite’ engineer training programme is already showing results, according to Hao Tongliang, deputy head of the Ministry’s Department of Degree Management and Postgraduate Education, speaking at a press conference last week.As the first cohort of 2,100 masters students graduate this month, most are expected to take up roles in critical industries such as defence, military and high-end manufacturing – highlighting the programme’s alignment with national development priorities, Hao said.“Engineering education is the strategic high ground of higher education reform,” he noted.According to Hao, the next step is to scale the model across all 67 professional degree categories in engineering. “We are building a system that promotes the integration of education, technology, and talent,” he said, noting closer alignment with industry needs.The pilot graduate engineering programme would be expanded to the undergraduate level, he said.Cutbacks in traditional engineering programmesParticularly at the undergraduate level, many traditional engineering programmes are being cut as universities invest more resources in emerging disciplines such as artificial intelligence and robotics.Around a third of China’s five to six million strong engineering workforce is in mechanical engineering for lower-end industrial processes, while the demand for high-end engineers for AI, robotics, and chip manufacture with advanced degrees is high and increasing.According to data from the Ministry of Education, between 2018 and 2022 engineering accounted for 30.95%, or almost a third, of all programmes cancelled – an indication that many of these are not aligned to industry workforce needs.Student demand for some traditional engineering majors is also declining. Civil engineering, for example, has allegedly seen an uptick in student transfer applications to other majors amid weakening industry demand from China’s sluggish housing market.Universities are expected to have adjusted 20% of all majors and disciplines by the end of 2025 to be more in line with employment prospects in the country.Jiabin Zhu, an associate professor in the School of Education at Shanghai Jiao Tong University, called for a more balanced and holistic approach to the restructuring efforts.“An overemphasis on short-term employment outcomes in higher education should be avoided, as it can undermine the development of talent needed for long-term technological innovation,” she toldUniversity World News.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

新計劃推動中國精英工程教育改革  
中國教育部近日公布了一項計劃，旨在加強和加速工程教育改革，培養更多的精英人才，特別是在半導體等短缺領域。到2030年，將在一半以上的工科院校設立專業國立研究生學院，並推行新的認證標準。該計劃也將促進國際合作，填補全球工程認證體系的空白。此外，新的評估系統將考慮專利和實踐報告，而不僅僅依賴學術論文，以符合中國新頒布的學位法。這一改革預計將有效提升中國在高端製造和技術領域的人才培養能力。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: The Taliban bans books, escalates suppression of women
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: The Taliban bans books, escalates suppression of womenGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsThe doors open to Afghan women continue to shut as the Taliban order the removal of nearly 700 books. Image generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFGHANISTANTaliban steps up its ‘Islamisation’ of universities agendaAFGHANISTANNursing, midwifery training ban causes alarm and disruptionAFGHANISTANTaliban moves to entrench policy banning women from HEAFGHANISTANBan on women students to impact severely on higher educationAFGHANISTANUnder the Taliban, university access for girls is drying upFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFGHANISTANThe Taliban bans books, escalates suppression of womenManija Mirzaie25 September 2025TweetAcademics and women’s groups have condemned a Taliban decree ordering the removal of nearly 700 books – more than a fifth of which are authored by women – from Afghan university curricula.The decrees also contained instructions to stop teaching 18 academic subjects.According to the decrees, the banned subjects had been “reviewed by scholars and Sharia experts” and had been found to be contrary to Sharia, and removed from the curriculum. They include topics such as human rights and democracy, as well as women’s studies,Zan Timessaid.The moves have sparked outrage and deepened concerns over the regime’s escalating repression of academic freedom and women’s rights.Issued in late August, the directive mandated the removal of 679 textbooks deemed “anti-Sharia” and “anti-Taliban”, including 140 written by women. A large number were by Iranian authors or from Iranian publishers.A Kabul University lecturer, speaking anonymously toUniversity World News, described the order as “a very stern and direct” mandate from the Ministry of Higher Education, leaving staff with little choice but to comply.Among the banned titles are scientific works such asSafety in the Chemical Laboratory, as well as books on human rights, gender studies, and women's sociology. They also include textbooks on statistics, health communication, photography theory, and conflict resolution in business.Zan Times, a women-led investigative news organisation covering human rights in Afghanistan with a focus on women, said it had obtained copies of two directives issued by the higher education ministry to the heads of universities and higher education institutes across the country on 25 August prohibiting 700 textbooks and course materials at public universities.“While reviewing the list of 679 titles the Taliban have banned from Afghanistan’s university curricula, my eyes stopped at the name of Professor Laila Habib, whose bookLinear Programmingappears among the forbidden works.“My first thought: How couldLinear Programming– a specialised statistics book used in economics departments – possibly conflict with Taliban policy or their version of Sharia? Unless the author is a woman, of course,” wrote Khadija Haidary forZan Times.The list of banned books was compiled after the Taliban’s Ministry of Higher Education asked administrators of public universities to submit their syllabi and teaching resources,Zan Timesreported, noting that the number of banned books was expected to rise once these reviews are completed.Top-down directivesZiaur Rahman Aryubi, the deputy academic director of the Taliban government’s Ministry of Higher Education, also said that a letter to universities said that the decisions had been made by a panel of “religious scholars and experts”,BBC Afghanreported on 19 September.It followed a directive from Taliban supreme leader Mullah Hibatullah Akhunzada in July to identify “deviant” or “suspect” books from bookstores, universities and other public and private institutions. Akhunzada reportedly stated that books that “mislead” and “corrupt” society would be removed from libraries, educational institutions and public spaces.Akhunzada’s directive was followed by the setting up of a committee tasked with reviewing books, referring those deemed suspect to clerics for further scrutiny. The committee comprised representatives of four ministries, namely religious affairs, information and culture, education, and higher education.The ministries of education and higher education have already restricted access to certain books in schools and universities.In addition, a list issued by the Taliban’s Ministry of Information and Culture and sent to publishers and booksellers late last year comprised books considered to be “against national interests, anti-Islam, in opposition to the Islamic Emirate, containing false beliefs [and] promoting ideas contrary to Islam”. It included works on women’s rights and human rights.The Dari-language dailyEtilaat Roz, an Afghan newspaper now based in the US, highlighted the ban's implications, noting in an editorial that it raises “deep concern about the future of civic education, legal awareness and democracy” in Afghanistan for generations to come.The daily also pointed out that the Ministry of Higher Education recently carried out mass dismissals of female professors, further erasing women's contributions from the academic landscape.Zakia Adeli, the former deputy minister of justice prior to the Taliban's return and author ofPolitical Terminology and International Relations,toldBBC Afghanthe move was unsurprising given the Taliban’s track record over the past four years. She criticised the regime’s “misogynistic mindset and policies”, stating: “When women themselves are not allowed to study, their views, ideas and writings are also suppressed.”Dr Sayyeda Batool Haidari, an Afghan Shiite author and former university professor, responded toUniversity World Newsenquiries via a social media message, asserting that the Taliban’s prohibition of numerous books written by female authors is a “clear indication that the group is at war with enlightenment and knowledge”.“The last traces of Afghan women in academia [are] erased under ‘sharia law’. The future of the country is dystopia,” Sara Wahedi, an Afghan-Canadian tech entrepreneur and humanitarian, posted on theXplatform.Ahmad Azimi, a professor at a private university in Kabul, toldUniversity World News: “The removal of books written by female authors has a direct impact on the quality of teaching, research, and updating of course materials in universities.“This is an unbearable blow and amounts to completely erasing females from the academic ecosystem in Afghanistan. Although very little effort has been made to promote female academics during the republic [before the Taliban], this gap in the past three to four years has reversed all of that.”Herat-based women’s rights activist Hoorya Habibi toldUniversity World News: “Discrediting female authors and professors who studied up to doctoral levels and spent time and effort to reach the position to be able to write books is an utterly shameful act.“The female segment of society has been put under immense social and economic pressure. On behalf of all Afghan women, I call on the de-facto [Taliban] government to open the doors of universities and schools to everyone soon.”Escalation of suppressionThe book ban is part of a broader pattern of gender-based repression by the Taliban. Since their return to power in August 2021, women have been systematically excluded from secondary and higher education, barred from working in government offices and international organisations, prohibited from travelling without a male guardian, and even excluded from parks and gyms.The ban on women-authored books in Afghan universities – four years after the Taliban started to implement its ban on girls in schools – marks a troubling escalation in their campaign to further suppress women's voices, limit access to diverse sources of knowledge and information in the war-ravaged country and Islamise all reading materials.The Taliban Minister of Higher Education Neda Mohammad Nadeem said at an international quality assurance conference in Herat this week that his ministry had removed Charles Darwin’s theory of evolution from Afghanistan’s university curriculum,Amu TVreported.The TV channel alsoreportedlate last month that a “Poetry Regulation Law”, issued by the Taliban’s Ministry of Justice and published in the official gazette on 30 August, containing two chapters and 13 articles, prohibits the writing of poems that praise romantic love, encourage relationships between men and women, or express “unrestrained desires and misplaced emotions”.The law also explicitly forbids criticism of Akhunzada’s directives and decisions, requiring that poetry promote Islamic beliefs, self-purification, and unity among Muslims. It also urges poets to avoid referencing ideological schools of thought such as feminism, democracy, communism, and nationalism.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

塔利班禁止書籍，女性受到進一步壓制

塔利班最近發布一項命令，要求從阿富汗大學課程中移除近700本書籍，其中超過五分之一的書籍由女性作者撰寫。這項禁令還停止教授18門學科，包括人權和女性研究等，進一步加深了對學術自由和女性權利的擔憂。學者和女性團體譴責這一政策，認為它是對教育和知識的嚴重壓制，並可能對未來的公民教育和民主意識產生深遠影響。專家指出，這一禁令將直接影響教學質量和研究發展，致使女性在阿富汗的學術界被徹底邊緣化。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Top university official snared in ongoing anti-graft sweepGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINATwo universities slapped with military procurement banCHINAMinistry cracks down on misconduct in funding applicationsHONG KONGAnti-corruption agency to review universities’ admissionsGLOBALHow to tackle global academic corruptionCHINAThe politics of the drive against corruptionFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredCHINATop university official snared in ongoing anti-graft sweepAmber Wang25 September 2025TweetThe ousting of a senior official at China’s leading Peking University (PKU), Beijing, is being seen as the latest sign that the Chinese Communist Party government’s widespread anti-graft campaign has spread to higher education and may be intensifying.Last week, Beijing’s anti-corruption watchdogs announced that Ren Yuzhong, vice-president of PKU, who is also a member of the standing committee of the Communist Party of China branch at the university, is under investigation for suspected “serious violations of discipline and law”, a phrase commonly used for corruption offences.Peking University, now ranked No. 14 globally and No. 1 in mainland China in the QS World University Rankings 2026, is the most prominent Chinese institution whose senior leadership has been caught up in the anti-corruption drive this year.In its 17 September notice, the party’s Central Commission for Discipline Inspection (CCDI) and the National Commission of Supervision said Ren, who served as PKU’s vice-president since March 2024, “turned himself in” to the authorities. Sources said he had not been seen in public for over two months, while in August his profile was quietly removed from the university’s official website.While the details of his alleged misconduct are undisclosed, fuelling speculation, media reports suggested Ren had been under scrutiny for months.Ren’s downfall is the latest sign of political scrutiny of Chinese universities. In January, the CCDI for the first time singled out higher education for amassing “concentrated power, intensive capital, and rich resources”, and called for stronger accountability measures.It named the sector as one of 11 needing “systemic rectification” in their anti-corruption efforts, alongside finance, state-owned enterprises, energy, tobacco, sports and others. The CCDIannounced in Januaryit had disciplined 889,000 officials overall last year, up 46% from the year before.In July, Li Xi, party chief of the CCDI, told a meeting of the body’s standing committee that it needed to ensure the country’s scientific and technological development advanced “in the right direction” to create a good environment for innovation.He added that the anti-corruption body must “resolutely crack down on corruption that abuses project management authority to accept bribes or colludes to embezzle research funds”.50 university leaders snaredAccording to open-source records, at least 50 university leaders have been snared in the nationwide campaign in 2025 so far. Among these, 27 held top party and administrative positions.Among them, four were from higher-ranked “Project 985” and “Project 211” universities, including Peking University, China University of Mining and Technology based in Jiangsu, Shihezi University in Xinjiang, and Southwest University of Finance and Economics in Chengdu.An analysis by independent media outletCaixinfound recent cases involving officials “using their positions to seek improper benefits for others in exchange for money”. The abuses range from job promotions to project contracting, allocation, and settlement, and university leaders pocketed substantial sums of money, it said.Caixinalso reported that corruption in higher education is particularly prevalent in infrastructure projects. “Malpractice includes rigged bidding, excessive subcontracting, inflated designs, procurement kickbacks, and weak oversight,” it found.Academic research is seen as another breeding ground for wrongdoing, with some university officials accused of embezzling funds, grant fraud, false reporting, and diversion of resources for personal gain.Facing increasing criticism of inadequate oversight, the government stepped up itscrackdown on misconduct in research funding applications, which can include using personal connections to short-circuit the grants approval process.According to Wang Chengwen, a chief anti-graft inspector who oversees education under the Ministry of Education, cases in recent years reflect “unscientific and imprecise power structures and operational mechanisms” in some institutions.“There remains a soil for corruption to thrive,” he wrote in a commentary piece for party mouthpieceStudy Timesin June.In September 2024, the CCDI announced an investigation into the former Chinese vice-minister of education – the highest level of education official to be targeted. The CCDI said Du Yubo, who was also the Ministry of Education’s deputy party chief from 2010 to 2016, was accused of “serious violations of discipline and law”.In February then Technology Minister Jin Zhuanglong was removed in what was believed to be a graft-related probe, though this was never officially confirmed. He had not appeared in public since December 2024. He was replaced by Li Lecheng.Spotlight on young officialsAt 45, PKU’s Ren was the youngest vice-principal in the university’s 125-year history, holding a rank equivalent to deputy director-general in China’s bureaucratic system.A PKU-trained PhD in law, he steadily climbed up the university ladder, having served in numerous roles, including head of the publicity department and spokesperson, before taking up the vice-presidency, while also conducting research on higher education.On the day of the announcement about his investigation, PKU leaders convened a meeting on the decision to spotlight “young cadres” and strengthen their education, management and supervision to improve integrity in governance, according to a PKU press release.The latest cases come as a growing number of universities have rolled out anti-corruption majors, explicitly designed to train students for roles in China’s political apparatus.Graduates, who would receive law degrees, often go on to join discipline inspection and supervisory bodies, including the CCDI and the National Commission of Supervision.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Fears grow over US scrutiny of Chinese university outposts
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Fears grow over US scrutiny of Chinese university outpostsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINAChina is committed to global engagement. On its own termsCHINA-GLOBALChina expands tie-ups with global partners, including USCHINA-UNITED STATESCan Duke’s campus in China survive congressional pressure?CHINA-UNITED STATESDespite US-China tensions, joint universities are thrivingCHINA-UNITED STATESEnd of US-China institute raises fears of more ‘decoupling’UNITED STATES-CHINAStudy shows drastic decline in US-China scientific exchangeCHINA-UNITED STATESGeopolitical tensions dim prospects for US-China exchangesFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED STATES-CHINAFears grow over US scrutiny of Chinese university outpostsYojana Sharma25 September 2025TweetAcademics at United States universities with branch campuses in China that operate jointly with Chinese university partners are reporting nervousness among colleagues and in some cases “genuine fear” that the US administration will turn its sights on these flagship outposts amid heightened scrutiny of their activities by a US congressional committee.Congressional committees and US administration officials have said joint institutes, many of them with a focus on STEM degrees, pose a national security threat to the US by funding and advancing research that they claim boosts China’s military capabilities.The US House of Representatives Select Committee on the Chinese Communist Party (CCP) and the House Committee on Education and the Workforce released a report on 12 September titledJoint Institutes, Divided Loyalties, which presents “new findings on joint institutes and other problematic partnerships” between US and Chinese universities.“These partnerships must end,” said Congressman John Moolenaar, chair of the House CCP Committee.TheDivided Loyaltiesreport came hard on the heels of another House CCP Committee report –Fox in the Henhouse, released on 5 September, which revealed “a pervasive and deeply troubling pattern of US taxpayer-funded research being conducted in collaboration with Chinese entities”. This report said these are directly tied to China’s defence research and industrial base.Kevin Kinser, professor of education policy at Pennsylvania State University and co-founder of a database on international branch campuses,C-BERT, toldUniversity World Newsthe current US administration “certainly has the ability to encourage or even force the closure of partnerships with countries that are disfavoured”.He said: “We've seen when this government wants to put inordinate pressure on an institution that they can do so very effectively, and institutions will respond accordingly.”Kinser pointed out that if reports like that of the select committee get “political traction, then it is something that the executive branch [US president] could determine was a way of holding universities accountable from their political perspective”.Forcing joint institutes to closeIn itsDivided Loyaltiesreport, the committee named around 50 ongoing US-China partnerships. They include Duke Kunshan University (DKU), established by Duke University in partnership with China’s Wuhan University. The report said students at DKU “have participated in military-style drills and CCP-led ideological training.”The report also points to the joint institute shared by New York University (NYU) and East China Normal University in Shanghai, which it said hosts a laboratory that is a partner of the China Aerospace Science and Technology Corporation (CASC), according to the committee. NYU has its own free-standing campus in Shanghai.Joint Institutes “are not typical academic collaborations that benefit students from both countries. They are under the thumb of the CCP. They operate under PRC (People’s Republic of China) law, are run by Chinese-majority boards and have Party presence in leadership, and are aligned with the CCP’s national strategy, including its military buildup”, the committee report warned.“Chinese government funding dominates these joint institutes, and the use of funds is restricted by law to align with CCP goals. Curriculum and research prioritise CCP-defined goals – especially in military-relevant science and technology fields. Finally, US institutions agree to PRC-imposed limits on academic freedom, speech, and governance independence,” the report noted.A previous report released last year by the same committee, together with a concerted campaign of letters to US university heads, led to the closure of a number of joint institutes, including the Tsinghua-Berkeley Shenzhen Institute, the Georgia Tech Shenzhen Institute with Tianjin University, theUniversity of Michigan-Shanghai Jiao Tong University Joint Institute, and the Zhengzhou University of Light Industry-Oakland University Joint Institute, which the committee investigated and deemed “high-risk”.In February Moolenaar and Tim Walberg, chair of the House Committee on Education and the Workforce, sent letters to several US universities, urging them to terminate collaborations with Chinese universities on the grounds of national security risks.The committees had identified three joint institutes that “pair prestigious US universities with Chinese counterparts under the guise of academic cooperation”. In reality, though, the partnerships “serve as sophisticated conduits for transferring critical US technologies and expertise to the PRC.”Since then, all three of the joint institutes – Tsinghua-Berkeley Shenzhen Institute, the Georgia Tech Shenzhen Institute, and Sichuan University-Pittsburgh Institute (SCUPI) – announced their closure, along with eight others highlighted in the earlier report.The latest to shut down was SCUPI, reportedly shuttered in May.“The University of Pittsburgh’s relationship with SCUPI had run its course, and the decision was made to end its involvement with the programme,” a spokesperson for the University of Pittsburgh toldUniversity World News.Sources said the joint institute stopped enrolling new students for this academic year, but students currently enrolled in the programme will be able to complete their course of study and will receive degrees from both Sichuan University and Pittsburgh.Sudden closuresElsewhere, reports on Chinese social media indicate that closures of some courses previously held at joint institutes have been sudden, with students complaining that changes had not been communicated in advance.“Mostly, accreditation of the US institution requires them to have what are called teach-out policies. So if they're going to close, they have to have a procedure so that the students that are currently enrolled have the opportunity to finish their degree,” explained Kinser.“It’s not necessarily finishing their degree and earning the degree from the institution that's closing, but the opportunity to transfer somewhere else and earn an equivalent degree,” he said.Some, such as the partnership between the University of Michigan and Shanghai Jiao Tong University (UM-SHJT), continue in scaled-back form. The institution was officially renamed in June as Pujiang International College.“Students are feeling uncertain about the outlook of this new incarnation,” an alumna of the UM-SHJT programme toldUniversity World Newson condition of anonymity. She described the previous joint institution “as a resounding success”, pointing to quality teaching and an “ambitious cohort”.Graduates from her class of 2024 went on to top universities in the US, including Harvard, Yale, Stanford, and Berkeley. More than a dozen are also carrying on to do PhDs, she said.Focus on branch campusesWith the swift demise of several joint institutes, staff, faculty and students at the three main US branch campuses that operate jointly with Chinese universities – DKU, NYU Shanghai, and Wenzhou Kean University in Wenzhou – fear they may be the next target.Unlike the joint institutes, these are standalone campuses with thousands of students, both Chinese and international, earning joint degrees and taught in English by American and international staff. “These are much more significant investments by the US institution, not just in terms of resources but also in reputation and status,” Kinser said.“They’ve been around for a while. They're valuable, flagship investments for the universities, and it will be very hard for them to give them up, or give them up in a flash. It would take some wind-down time.”But he added: “The political and partisan circumstances that have emerged around higher education in general and around international students and activities at universities more specifically, [are] really important in the US right now, and [they are] going to be a drag on internationalisation for at least the next couple of years.“I’m optimistic for their survival,” he said, “but going through a phase of pessimism right now.”Speaking on condition of anonymity, a US academic who has taught at NYU Shanghai said many of her colleagues teaching at US-China joint campuses in China are nervous.“For some, there is genuine fear, that was not there even six months ago, that the US will not just target specific joint programmes with China or shut down joint institutes but also come after the major campuses and force them to scale back or even bail out, handing over to their Chinese partners,” she toldUniversity World News.“Previously there was some concern that the tightening situation in China, including compulsory military service for students on joint campuses and stricter rules on taboo subjects in the classroom, might in the long run mean the closure of one of these US joint campuses. But that always seemed further in the future. The current threat is more immediate, and it is coming from the US.”Chinese officials, including those in the Chinese embassy in Washington, are “having an increasingly difficult time separating out the substance from the noise,” said Denis Simon, a non-resident fellow at the Quincy Institute for Responsible Statecraft in Washington DC and a former executive vice-chancellor of DKU (2010 to 2016). “Increasingly, they understand that the political environment is so charged in the United States that these joint venture universities are an easy target to attack.”“I think they're hoping [it will] fade away, but so far it hasn't faded away, and this select committee keeps the pressure on with another report, and then another report.”Simon noted that no one is challenging the detail in the reports. “Where it comes from, what the sourcing is, all of that — nobody has done a thorough job of trying to push back against them.”The former NYU academic noted that the committee reports “link everything to national security and the Chinese military.” But, she added: “These are for the most part liberal arts institutions. This leads to more control by the Chinese side regarding sensitive topics, and that received attention in the US. But now we are seeing new pressures by the US on whether they should be allowed to exist at all.“They see these institutions as being under Chinese control and believe they can be used to undermine the US.”Under pressureSarah McLaughlin, senior scholar for global expression at the Philadelphia-basedFoundation for Individual Rights and Expression(FIRE), which campaigns for campus freedoms, toldUniversity World News: “Most people in the US were under the impression that these would be fully independent and self-moderating institutions within China. And I think they believed that in part because that's what NYU told them they would be. But over time, it's clear that that's not exactly the case.”While the joint institutes, but particularly the joint campuses, provide important opportunities for collaboration, McLaughlin suggested that in order to preserve the partnerships “these universities may well feel pressure to accommodate both the laws and sensitivities of the Chinese government in their operation of these institutes”, rather than protecting academic freedom and institutional values.Administrators may avoid topics deemed sensitive in China, like Hong Kong, Taiwan and Uyghur rights, in order to preserve the partnerships.Through these branch campuses, she argued, “American universities have not made China any freer … but universities have risked makingthemselvesless free institutions,” she points out in her book,Authoritarians in the Academy, published last month, which includes analysis of US branch campuses in China and elsewhere.Simon said he still talks to many people at DKU “on a regular basis”.“They’re always asking me, ‘Do you think we're going to close? People are nervous’,” he toldUniversity World News.Unlike other universities in China “they have unencumbered access at DKU and NYU [in Shanghai] to the Internet, so they're seeing everything [that’s going on in the US], and it is daunting, because the information that’s coming back to them is that they need to be worried, and it’s coming from their [academic] peers,” he noted.“Everyone is in a bit of trepidation about getting singled out, particularly the university boards, the board of advisors and [the] board of trustees … [they] are very cautious,” Simon said. However, he stressed, “If you would go to the three universities on the ground in China, you would not know that these problems exist back in the United States. They are operating almost trouble-free.“The leadership is making a concerted effort in both NYU Shanghai and DKU to keep things on a normal level, not to have disruptions … so that students don't feel any anxiety about coming to the campus, and that would include current students as well as future students.”Simon stressed that the Chinese side does not want to walk away. “The Chinese are very nervous about them [joint venture universities]. But they're not jumping out of the building because the building's on fire. Not yet.”McLaughlin said: “It’s hard to read the tea leaves, but I think universities have a lot of good reasons to perhaps be moving away from this style of collaboration with China.”However, she added: “Given their past willingness to stick it out, I would be surprised if they did not stick it out through further disruption … Universities see a lot of prestige in being global institutions – they think it’s a major feather in their cap.”Unfavourable political climateSimon pointed to possible action from US state legislatures on public universities or even private universities. Universities are in a “situation where they don't want to get on the wrong side of the federal government because that could have some ramifications in terms of curtailment of research funding and other kinds of things”.He said this could lead to the US administration making a link to their campuses abroad.Kinser said: “It would be a very simple thing for a state to say its public institutions are prohibited from doing this. But for private universities [such as Duke], it is a bit more complicated.”For example, Senator Rick Scott of Florida proposed a bill to restrict federal funding to US universities affiliated with certain Chinese institutions to prevent “espionage and theft of American research and IP”. The bill has passed in the House of Representatives.Moolenaar and Walberg wrote to Duke University President Vincent Price in May this year,urging the university to end its DKU partnership with Wuhan University. It was seen by some as a “shot across the bows”.New partnershipsDespite the closures, US universities are stilltying up new partnerships with China.But as Kinser pointed out, “A lot of these almost certainly have been in the works for probably at least a couple of years. So the legwork and money and investment in this predate a lot of the formal concern that was expressed in the committee and then the more recent policy objectives of the current [US] administration.“The part that is now currently sensitive, which was not previously so sensitive – this notion of partnership with institutions that have direct connections to military, or even indirect connections to military – that’s a relatively new thing,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: H-1B visa chaos sends international students into panic
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: H-1B visa chaos sends international students into panicGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIA-UNITED STATESIndian students reject US study over visa uncertaintyASIA-GLOBALGlobal talent chains in ‘tectonic’ shift towards AsiaCHINA-UNITED STATESStudents emerge as bargaining chip in China-US trade talksCHINA-UNITED STATESUS visa freeze upends study plans for Chinese studentsUNITED STATESTrump’s ongoing attacks on HE ‘reverberate across borders’UNITED STATESVisa trends: The US needs a new international education planFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATES-ASIAH-1B visa chaos sends international students into panicShuriah Niazi,Amber WangandYojana Sharma25 September 2025TweetThe decision by United States President Donald Trump to hike the fee for the H-1B skilled worker visa to US$100,000 – part of broader changes in US immigration policy – has sent shockwaves through the technology and higher education sectors in the US and created widespread anxiety among students planning future pathways.Indian and Chinese graduates currently make up the bulk of current H-1B visa holders. According to official statistics from the US Citizenship and Immigration Services (USCIS), 71% of current H-1B visas go to Indian nationals, while Chinese hold almost 12% of these visas.However, in addition to the stress caused to graduates, existing students and PhD holders also say their longer-term career plans have taken a knock as a result of the visa fee hike. The H-1B visa has long been the most widely available legal pathway for students to make the transition from study to skilled employment in the US, education agents noted.Nikhil Mudgal, founder and CEO ofLorien Finance, which helps Indian students secure loans for study abroad, said: “Trump’s visa fee hike has heightened concerns over the return on investment in a US education.Already burdened by high tuition and living expenses, students now face an additional fee, making the study-to-work-to-green card pathway increasingly uncertain. Without assured job sponsorship, many fear the substantial financial commitment may not yield the expected returns.“For many, studying in the US already means heavy loans or using family savings. The new H-1B visa fee adds uncertainty, making the US less affordable and less attractive. Families with limited financial backup may now choose cheaper, more stable countries over the prestige of a US degree,” Mudgal toldUniversity World News.Most Indian students pay for their US education through expensive loans, counting on working under the US Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme to pay these back.The OPT programme allows international students graduating from US universities to work in the country for up to 12 months in a job related to their course of study. Graduates with STEM degrees can get a 24-month extension, which adds up to 36 months of work.OPT attractiveness to ‘diminish’Education consultants are counselling international students to plan their careers carefully and look at alternate career choices. The OPT programme continues to permit temporary work in STEM fields for up to three years, but without a well-defined pathway to long-term residency, the attractiveness of OPT might diminish, they said.A masters-level Indian student currently studying in the US, requesting anonymity, said: “When I came to America the plan was simple: study, get some experience, pay back my loan, and fulfil my American dream. But if there is a US$100,000 fee, firms may decide against recruiting foreign students. Why spend so much money when they can recruit an American for a lesser amount?”However, some believe the US will remain an option for a select group of students.“The US will continue to be an aspirational destination, especially for STEM talent. But the recent H-1B petition fee hike means students aiming for long-term careers will be more conscious of the return on investment in a US degree,” Praneet Singh, associate vice-president for university partnerships-study abroad at upGrad, toldUniversity World News.“This will likely prompt a recalibration. Students are now evaluating which roles and industries are more likely to sponsor H-1B talent and are adjusting their study plans, programme choices, and university selection accordingly. The ambition to study in the US is not fading, but decisions are becoming more informed and future-proof.”Chinese students panicOpinions were sharply divided on the impact of the new rule among Chinese international students.On the popular Chinese social platformXiaohongshu, orRedNote, netizens described a whirlwind of emotions ranging from confusion to panic, frustration and anger, particularly as the original announcement on 19 September appeared to refer to all H-1B visa holders, and universities and companies in the US advised students and employees (with existing H-1B visas) who were out of the US to return to the country before the implementation date of 21 September.While the White House later clarified that the policy would apply only to new applications – “not renewals, and not current visa holders”, the reassurance came too late for some. Many netizens said they had rushed back to the US in a panic, only to learn of the clarification afterwards.“It struck me like lightning,” said one netizen, who cancelled her wedding in China to return urgently to Seattle.“I felt completely paralysed when I saw this news upon landing,” wrote another who had secured the visa just two months earlier and travelled home to visit family.Some Chinese netizens argued that heightened policy uncertainty would discourage companies from hiring foreign workers. “Do you think the company will still process H-1Bs in the future? Even if you are in the US, they will only directly hire Americans,” read one of the most upvoted comments onRedNote, which received hundreds of likes.Some visa holders responded with sarcasm, self-deprecatingly referring to themselves as “H-1B slaves” shackled by the visa scheme and the policy volatility of the Trump administration.“Many people began to clearly realise that the United States is making policies maliciously, purely for the purpose of torturing H-1B holders, and without justification,” wrote a US-based Chinese influencer who is also an H-1B visa holder in one of the platform’s most-liked posts.“[It’s] a compliance test, just to see how much people can endure,” another user remarked onRedNote.However, some netizens saw the new fee as reducing competition for Chinese applicants. “Those most affected will be those from offshore India who participate in the lottery and then come to the US after winning,” read a post. “This will reduce the number of participants in the lottery, benefiting international students in the US.”The US Department of Homeland Security typically awards a maximum of 85,000 H-1B visas each year to companies and other US employers who apply under a lottery system. Universities and non-profit research organisations are exempt from this cap.Intent to ‘create chaos’Sudhanshu Kaushik, founder and executive director of the North American Association of Indian students, toldIndia TodayTV: “At first I thought this is an ‘America first’ policy. They're trying to give more Americans jobs.“Now that I look back, now that I see the clarification, what is clear to me is that there was an intent to create chaos. There was an intent to create distrust and atmosphere among Indians that are H-1Bs, that [there is] a sentiment that you don't belong here, and that [with] a stroke of a pen from the executive office, from an executive order, you can disrupt people’s lives.“Now … they've clarified, but that distrust and that disbelief, to be honest, has already seeded in. So, I think, first of all, people are going to really reevaluate that. Is it worth it?“I think they're going to find other opportunities in Canada. They're going to try to find opportunities in the UK. They're going to try to find opportunities in the Gulf. They're going to try to make sure that they can stabilise, they can have a stable life and be able to work where they feel valued.”India's Ministry of External Affairs reacted with balanced alarm, saying it is studying the implications of the planned fee. The ministry pointed out that the movement of skilled talent has traditionally provided for technological development and economic growth in both countries. It also warned that this action would have “humanitarian consequences” by impacting families.Tech, HE sector in shockRyan Allen, associate professor of comparative and international education and leadership at Soka University of America, writing on theCollege Townswebsite, said the US$100,000 fee announcement had “sent shock through the tech sector and higher education”, noting that some of the panic stemmed from the proclamation’s “thin explanation or detail”.But Allen noted the discourse around the visa often revolves around the tech sector, which “gobbles the bulk of H-1Bs”, and which he pointed out was the main target of accusations around the hiring of cheap professionals from abroad.In the 2024 fiscal year, almost 64% of H-1B approved applications or renewals came from the tech sector according to a report from USCIS to Congress on “Characteristics of H-1B Speciality Occupation Workers”, which cited 255,000 approvals for the tech sector out of a total of 400,000.For the education sector, there were almost 24,000 H-1B approvals in 2024, while other areas like social sciences or mathematical and physical sciences could include university faculty for teaching and research in these subjects, Allen noted.For example, Stanford University received the most H-1B approvals for 2025, with 500 visas in the education category. “The rest of the list is full of other top research and flagship universities,” Allen pointed out, referencing the USCIS’s H-1B Employer Data Hub.These included the University of Michigan with 359 H-1B approvals, the University of Wisconsin, Madison, with 232 H-1B approvals, and the universities of Maryland, Pennsylvania, Pittsburgh, Columbia and the University of California, San Francisco, with over 200 H-1B approvals each so far in 2025.International collaboration ‘at risk’Kevin Kinser, professor of education policy studies at Pennsylvania State University, described proposed changes to H-1B visas as potentially “devastating” for international collaboration, while noting “the situation is still in flux from what I understand, and it's still not completely clear what's going to happen”.H-1B visas are used to hire international faculty, he said. If the changes come into place “that will be truly devastating for institutions trying to maintain global status”, as well as affecting pathways for international PhD students seeking academic and research jobs, Kinser toldUniversity World News.“US universities right now are exempt from the limits on H-1B visas that private companies are subject to,” Kinser explained. “There’s a lottery for private companies, but universities can hire outside of that.”“The question is: Are the changes to these H-1B visas only for the lottery versions, or are they for everyone? And right now, the best guess is that they would be for everyone. So universities, in general, will essentially be prohibited from hiring international faculty and staff because very few institutions are going to be able to afford the US$100,000 fee as it comes forward.“It is really difficult to imagine how many institutions would do that at the scale that we've been used to in terms of hiring international faculty,” Kinser said.“If a PhD student is coming to the United States, they get networked into the US ecosystem and have designs to then become employed in the United States, it would be much less likely for them to be able to fulfil those aspirations because the US$100,000 fee to sponsor them for an H-1B would be prohibitive for most companies and most universities too,” he pointed out.“Just think about the number of Indian faculty we have in our colleges of engineering, for example. Virtually all of those would have come in on H-1B visas, at least for a period of time, before getting a more permanent visa.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
