https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Amid insurgency, a Jesuit university stands up for peaceGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksLATIN AMERICAHow intercultural education is reshaping higher educationLATIN AMERICA-CARIBBEANNetwork of HE leaders takes aim at transformative changeGLOBALPeace education is now urgent, say university leadersCOLOMBIAPeace-building: Insights into a university’s ‘special role’COLOMBIAFrom insurgent to student: How education can build peaceGLOBALEducation as a path to peace, hope and prosperityFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredCOLOMBIAAmid insurgency, a Jesuit university stands up for peaceNathan M Greenfield26 September 2025TweetThe truck bomb believed to have been targeting the Marco Fidel Suárez Military Aviation School in the northern outskirts of Cali, Colombia, on 21 August that killed six civilians, including a pregnant woman and child, and wounded 70 others couldn’t be heard on the campus of Pontificia Universidad Javeriana, Cali, 20 km to the south, but news of the incident touched the lives of all staff, students and academics deeply.More so because the deadly attack came only hours after an explosives-equipped drone struck a Black Hawk UH-60 helicopter belonging to the anti-narcotics directorate of the National Police in Antioquia, 400 km to the north, killing all 13 police officers on board.In the early daysafter the attacks, the streets of Cali were filled with police and soldiers.Now, however, the streets are not filled with police or soldiers because, as explained by Javeriana, Cali’s rector Father Vicente Durán Casas SJ: “Military intelligence has more value than having many soldiers in the street”. But the security forces are operating in the mountains above the city, part of the Andes, in order to prevent the insurgents from entering.On the campus of Pontificia Universidad Javeriana, Cali – where a red brick tower bearing a cut-out cross soars above the palm trees – the military situation is calm, said Father Durán, but it is a “tranquillity that is filled with trepidation, filled with the knowledge that the armed insurgent groups will likely carry out more attacks on the city”.Father Durán indicated that despite and even because of the violence, the position of his university, which is Catholic and Jesuit, is to encourage and invite dialogue so that the opposing parties can get to know each other and recognise each other as legitimate actors.“The way to resolve differences is not through violent means, but through dialogue," he emphasised.Coordinated attacksThe latest attack on Cali is thefourth this year. On 10 June, Cali was among the 24 locations in its region where bombings and attacks by insurgents firing guns killed seven people and injured 24 others.“This [was] a particularly well-coordinated offensive. It really demonstrates the capacity that the [insurgent] group has built. I think very alarmingly it demonstrates their ability to conduct operations in the metropolitan area of Cali,” Elizabeth Dicksontold the French news networkFrance 24; Dickson is the senior analyst, Colombia, with the Brussels-basedInternational Crisis Group, an NGO based in Brussels that works “to prevent wars and shape policies that will build a more peaceful world.”France 24alsoreportedthat Agence France-Presse journalists in Cali saw “the tangled wreckage of vehicle bombs surrounded by scorched debris and damaged buildings.”These attacks were carried out by Estado Mayor Central (Central General Staff, EMC), three members of which were quickly arrested: Walter Esteban Yond Ipía, who is suspected of being a member of a special group within EMC that planned the attack, and Diomar Mancilla and Carlos Steven Obando, who now stand accused of transporting and then detonating the explosives in the truck.Two of these men, presumably Mancilla and Obando, werecaptured by residents nearbywhere the truck exploded.EMC was founded by dissident members of the Fuerzas Armadas Revolucionarias de Colombia (Revolutionary Armed Forces of Colombia, FARC) in 2016 when FARC and Colombia signed theJurisdicción Especial para la Paz.This peace agreement was designed to put an end to more than half a century of civil war in which, according to theColumbian Truth Commission, between 450,000 and 800,000 civilians, government troops and FARC fighters were killed.AsUniversity World Newshas reported,the peace agreement laid out a number of steps for the reintegration of former FARC fighters, into civil society,including pathways into higher education.In March of 2017, the EMC leadership issued amanifestoin which it accused the FARC Secretariat of having betrayed “the entire guerrilla force” by making a “pact with our class enemy”.The manifesto continued: “Let the world know that we remain in the struggle and that our objective is to achieve socialism through the only truly revolutionary alternative: arms in hand.”Irrespective of the revolutionary sentiments expressed in the manifesto, the EMC is a “criminal enterprise”,according toInSight Crime(InSight), a “think tank and media organisation that seeks to deepen and inform the debate about organised crime and citizen security in the Americas”, with offices in both Washington, DC, and Medellín, Colombia. Founded in 2010,InSighthas receivedsupport from the Swedish International Development Agencyand the Open Society Foundation Networks.In a2024 reporttitled, “Central General Staff – Ex-FARC Mafia”,InSight’s analysts wrote: “The EMC has significant armed power that allows it to maintain control of different criminal economies across the country. Depending on the region and the associated front, revenues vary, but its criminal portfolio is mainly based on drug trafficking revenues in the south of the country, illegal mining, and extortion.”According to Father Durán, EMC considers Cali and the surrounding areas important because Cali is only a two-hour drive from Buenaventura, the nation’s largest Pacific coast port.“This territory is very important for them because they can move many products from their illegal economies. At the beginning, we thought it was the illegal drug trade. But now we know that it’s also the illegal gold trade,” he said.Model of plunder imposedColombia’s illegal gold trade dwarfs the country’s most famous illegal export, coca leaf, from which cocaine is derived. In a 2022 press release, Gabriel Adolfo Jurado, the country’sdelegate comptroller for the environment, said that while “a kilo of coca can be on the Colombian market for around 5 million pesos [US$1,296], a kilo of illegal gold is in the order of 250 million pesos” and that 85% of the country’s US$9.2 billion gold trade was in the hands of four or five criminal organisations.In their explanations of the impact of the illicit gold trade, neither Adolfo Jurado nor Father Durán referred specifically to the United Nations Sustainability Development Goals, but their words referenced the issues at the heart of goals 6 (Decent work and economic growth), 14 (Life below the water) and 15 (Life on land).According to Adolfo Jurado, “66% of illegal mining in Colombia is done in reserve areas, in natural parks and in forest reserves,” and it is poisoning the country’s rivers.The “illicit exploitation of minerals, which fills the pockets of criminal organisations that are capturing this resource without caring about the contamination they produce to water resources with heavy minerals such as mercury, cyanide and others that are used in such activity”.Father Durán also highlighted the environmental impact the illicit gold trade has on Indigenous peoples and their communities and how the trade foments violence.“We know that illegal mining products are extracted in places and by techniques that can be harmful to the environment and the communities and will negatively affect the territory of the western mountains that divides the territory between Cali and the sea.”There is so much money to be made that the gold trade “brings a threat of violence to those regions, and it affects, especially, the people who work in the fields, the Indigenous people and other communities that live there. The illegal gold trade and processes bring more violence. That is the situation in the countryside now, Father Durán said.Further, explained Vanessa Rodríguez Navia, a researcher at Javeriana, Cali, and Major Alcibiades Escués, coordinator of research on economic systems, human rights, interculturality and sustainability at the university’s Institute of Intercultural Studies, commodification of both coca and gold disrupts Indigenous people’s world view.“The relationship between indigenous peoples and coca and gold reflects a deep tension between their sacred meaning and the commercial logic imposed by Western modernity. Coca,conceived as a sacred plant, has been used since ancient times for medicine, rituals and spiritual communication; and gold is understood as part of the backbone of Mother Earth, whose extraction threatens the vital balance of the territory.“Over time, both coca and gold have been the object of greed and processes of instrumentalisation which, instead of recognising their cultural and spiritual dimension, reduce them to commodities within extractivist economies.“In this way, a model of plunder is imposed on Indigenous communities that no longer comes exclusively from external actors but penetrates their own territories and practices, promoting the commercialisation of the sacred and fragmenting their spiritual, cultural and social fabric,” they wrote in a joint email toUniversity World News.Indigenous education for peaceThe university Father Durán directs is a leader in Indigenous education, with Indigenous themes woven through undergraduate programmes such as “Specialisation in culture of peace and international humanitarian law,” which includes courses such as Characterisation of the Colombian conflict, Human rights, Humanitarian crisis, and Humanitarian action and management for peace.The MA in Human Rights and Culture of Peace includes courses such as analysis of the Colombian conflict, urban violence and territories; Human rights and human rights protection systems; education for peace and interculturality; and peacebuilding and reconciliation.The Doctorate in Peace Studies includes lines of research such as epistemology, theories and methodologies for peace, Methods of peacebuilding and peaceful transformation of conflicts, governance for peace, and diversity, gender and peacebuilding.“Incorporating indigenous studies into the university, in the historical context of domination and eradication of the native peoples of the Americas,” Navia and Escués said, requires a root and branch reconceptualisation of the purpose of a university education.“It implies an exercise of recognition and reconciliation in the face of centuries of epistemic exclusion. It is about understanding biocultural memory as a horizon, understood as the wealth of knowledge, practices and values that inseparably link human life with nature, recognising that air, water, animals, stars and the entire cosmos possess life and agency.“In this sense, the university is not only called upon to make this knowledge visible and deepen it but also to review the role of academic and cultural marginalisation that has relegated Indigenous peoples.“This commitment is reflected in the commitment of the Pontificia Universidad Javeriana de Cali and the Institute of Intercultural Studies to generate processes of research, training and intercultural action that strengthen epistemic, environmental and social justice, contributing to the transformation of academia into a space capable of dialogue with the plurality of knowledge and open to a science that recognises life in all its forms,” they toldUniversity World Newsin an email.Actor in the peace processJaveriana, Cali, which was founded 55 years ago, is a branch of Pontificia Universidad Javeriana (Bogotá). The university is one of 30 pontifical universities worldwide. Unlike other Catholic universities, such as the famous Notre Dame University in Indiana, in addition to conferring secular degrees, pontifical universities grant ecclesiastical degrees in theology, philosophy, canon law, and sacred scripture.The roots of Pontificia Universidad Javeriana go back more than four centuries – to the founding in 1604 of Colegio Mayor de San Bartolomé (Bogotá), which is one of the oldest colleges on the continent. The Colegio was founded by the Society of Jesus, which had been founded almost 60 years earlier by St Ignatius of Loyola.From the mid-16th century, the Jesuits were dedicated to establishing colleges and universities in different territories of America. The educational mission of the Jesuits in these centres was to form both the children of Spanish settlers and the indigenous converts to Catholicism, integrating academic teaching with the spread of the Catholic faith.In 1767, the Spanish authorities expelled the Jesuits from their American colonies, which meant the closing of what was then called the University and Academy of Saint Francis Xavier. In 1930, President Enrique Olaya Herrera signed an Act of Restoration with the Vatican that allowed the Jesuits to return to Colombia.While the Jesuits continued to educate individuals for the priesthood, the re-founded Pontificia Universidad Javeriana taught a variety of secular subjects. In 1954, the university inaugurated programmes in bacteriology, art law, nursing, commerce, and philosophy.“Since the 17th century some members of the society raised their voices against the abuses committed against indigenous peoples. It was, however, in the 20th century and with special impetus after the Second Vatican Council that the Social Doctrine of the Church acquired a more explicit character in the defence of human dignity, social justice and the protection of the most vulnerable.“The austere discipline that had characterised the society in the past was then opened to a modern vision centred on solidarity, equity and respect for the life of native peoples, integrating these convictions into the academic and pastoral formation of institutions such as the Pontificia Universidad Javeriana,” said Father Durán.Following the Social Doctrine of the Catholic Church, Javeriana, Cali, has positioned itself as an actor in the regional peace process; theSocial Doctrineincludes teachings on the dignity of the human person, “the fight against the structural causes of poverty, inequality, lack of work, land and housing, the denial of social and labour rights … [the moral imperative] to confront the destructive effects of the empire of money: forced displacements, painful emigrations, the traffic of persons, drugs, war, violence and “a fit environment in which to develop its proper relationships”.An important messageNo one from the university was killed or wounded when the truck exploded in Cali. But, Father Durán toldUniversity World News, members of the academic community had family members or friends who were killed or wounded. Their city too, he stressed, was wounded, as was the country.Archbishop Louis Fernando Rodríguez Velásquez (Cali) said, “We are pained by the loss of human lives … We are pained by the feeling of uncertainty and fear … We are pained by the social and ethical breakdown.”In the masses held on campus in the immediate aftermath of the attack, a priest put a face to the dozens of victims by drawing attention to news reports of a primary school teacher who was killed in the blast.Her death “really gives insight into how this [the bombing] impacts and touches the deepest heart of the public,” said Father Durán in a Zoom call, “because this horror now gets a face, the victims get a face, like the teacher”.He added the words that bespeak the special role a pontifical university has among the faithful, “Estamos convencidos de que, como universidad, podemos comunicar un importante mensaje a la ciudad.” (We are confident that, as a university, we can communicate an important message to the city.)According to Father Durán, the violence altered lesson plans. “In social studies, social science and religious courses, professors incorporated the attacks into their pedagogical space, where students have been encouraged to speak about the bombings and what they mean for them. They have studied the region [and] the origins of the conflict so that they understand the roots of it.”Growing impatienceThe Catholic Church played a major role in the Colombian peace process, acting, for example, as a neutral party between the government and the FARC. The church also supports President Gustavo Petro’s “Total Peace Process”.In practical terms, said Father Durán, this means that the university seeks to further dialogue.“What we cannot accept is that violence is the way to resolve conflicts. Therefore, our great effort as a Catholic and Jesuit university is to encourage dialogue, to invite dialogue so that the opposing parties can get to know each other and recognise each other as legitimate actors who have the right to think as they think, as long as they do so. The way to resolve differences is not through violent means, but through dialogue.”Even such issues as the contamination of rivers by illegal gold mining and demands for fundamental social change in the country, Father Durán believes, are amenable to dialogue. But, in the present situation, the “terrorist groups don’t accept dialogue. Sometimes they accept, but they give conditions that are not sustainable or logical.“They are not interested in making peace because they are really interested in making their illegal trade grow,” said Father Durán.Though there is still public support for Petro’s peace policy, including among the university community, he finds that the general public is becoming impatient. “Sometimes you hear people saying: ‘If they [EMC] want to have a war, they will have war.’ Or that the solution could be a violent confrontation that eliminates these groups,” he said.However, Father Durán, who lived through the entirety of the Colombian civil war, hastened to add that “the experience of Colombia and its history of violence may cause more violence, because in every territory of the country, there is the potential for violence.”Against the violence Cali has seen and the mounting public pressure for a military solution that threatens the peace process, Father Durán’s faith is embodied in the university he leads.“Our university is Catholic and Jesuit, and based on that identity, we must affirm that all violent attacks, bombings, kidnappings, and recruitment of minors directed against the civilian population are direct attacks against human dignity and are unacceptable.“The paths to peace are difficult and sometimes obscure, but they are certainly paths that are opened through dialogue between opponents and by creating conditions of greater social equity and better opportunities for all,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT 耶穌會大學呼籲和平 在哥倫比亞，加利市的耶穌會大學——Pontificia Universidad Javeriana，面對持續的叛亂，強調對話與和平的重要性。校長杜蘭（Father Vicente Durán）指出，儘管最近的恐怖襲擊使社區感到恐懼，該校仍致力於促進不同方之間的對話，以尋求非暴力的解決方案。他強調，教育應該成為理解衝突根源的工具，並培養學生對於和平的認識。該校的課程涵蓋人權和文化和平，旨在加強社會正義與環境意識，並支持哥倫比亞的和平進程。 
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Is a ‘broken’ visa system fuelling anti-Indian sentiment?
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Is a ‘broken’ visa system fuelling anti-Indian sentiment?GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsProtesters at a recent anti-immigration rally in Australia in August 2025. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED KINGDOM-GLOBALImmigration curbs: Universities should have little to fearAUSTRALIALevy on foreign students could help migration woes – ExpertAUSTRALIAUniversities are economic growth engines, not cost centresAUSTRALIAWhat a levy on international student fees means for universitiesAUSTRALIAAustralia seeks reputation boost for international HEFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyAUSTRALIAIs a ‘broken’ visa system fuelling anti-Indian sentiment?Kalinga Seneviratne22 September 2025TweetA reported surge in this year’s enrolment of overseas students, particularly from India and Nepal, helped to fuel anti-Indian sentiment in large anti-immigration rallies across Australia at the end of August, with protesters claiming ‘Indians’ were flooding the country, exacerbating a housing crisis and taking away jobs.TheSBSnetwork reporting from Melbourne said Indian students stayed off the streets during the recent protests, fearing physical attacks. Parents back home, who largely fund their education, became agitated about their children’s safety after seeing the protests on television.“Our family back home [is] worried about our safety … and told us to stay indoors,” University of Melbourne masters student Varada Nair toldSBS. “There was someone who came and started throwing stones at us, calling on us to go home.”Shadow over the brandFederation of Ethnic Communities’ Council of Australia Chair Peter Doukas toldSBSinternational students have felt especially targeted in recent times.“These incidents occurring around the country will cast a shadow over the brand of Australia as a safe place for international students to study,” he said. “International students have been scapegoated for, among many things, the property prices and other economic issues, which they're not responsible for.”In the 2023 to 2024 financial year, temporary students were the largest group of migrant arrivals at 207,000 people, according to Australian Bureau of Statistics data.In the same period, international education contributed AU$51 billion (US$33.59 billion) to the Australian economy – AU$30.2 billion paid as goods and services and AU$20.6 billion paid as tuition fees. Education exports are Australia's fourth largest export behind iron ore, coal and natural gas, and the largest services export.India’sBusiness Standardnewspaper, in an article on 5 September, argued that “Indians power the economy, not flood it”.Jordan Tew, a Melbourne-based immigration specialist, told the newspaper that Indians make up 17% of international students, the second largest cohort. With a large migrant intake via the skilled migration programme, Indian migrants are “central” to the system and are not straining it, he is quoted as saying.“They are working as nurses, doctors, teachers and IT professionals and supporting universities and their communities across the country. The narrative of blame misses the bigger truth: Indian students and workers are not overwhelming Australia; they are helping it to thrive.”Earlier this year, in the lead-up to the new academic year, there were public allegations that educational and labour recruiting agents were manipulating the system to bring in illegal migrants.Minimal supportWriting in the international education news websitekoala.comearlier this year, Ewen Hollingsworth, co-founder of Spark Finance, an Australian fintech company that provides financial assistance for international students, claimed that the Australian government treats international students as ATMs and does little to include support systems for them.“In 2023, Australia increased the minimum savings required for a student visa by 17% … to AU$24,505. Then, in 2024, we doubled down with a 125% hike in student visa fees to US$1,600 – making them amongst the highest globally,” he said.Hollingsworth argued that international students keep regional universities open, support small businesses, and help sustain Australia’s higher education system “in the face of domestic enrolment decline”.There has been concern in the media here that foreign students, particularly from India and Nepal, are bringing their family members to work in Australia, undermining government (and opposition) policies aimed at bringing the migrant numbers down to stem a public housing crisis in the urban areas.Under a scheme known as “485 visas”, family members are allowed to work in Australia up to 48 hours a fortnight, and so are students.“Australian governments and universities actively promote education in India and Nepal along with permanent residency pathways. On the other hand, students are unfairly blamed for broader systemic issues like housing – issues that existed long before their arrival,” noted Dr Amit Sarwal, founding convenor of the Australia-India Interdisciplinary Research Network.He toldUniversity World Newsthat most Nepali students come to study and work in the hospitality industry. “For them, it’s a way to use their prior experience and get to work in their field quickly, thus sending money home to support their families.”South Asian community leader and social entrepreneur Ash Gholkar says that most Indian and Nepali students come to Australia to enjoy the safe and clean environment, as well as the opportunities available to work (due to Australia’s skills shortage).He added that for Nepalis there is the added issue of the economic instability following the major earthquake there in 2015, as well as the current political turmoil.Gholkar argues that since Australia has a declining population problem with a threat of many rural communities closing down due to a lack of people to work in farms and mines, “it is common sense to make sure that those who are potentially going to be future citizens and residents of this country come along with their families”.He toldUniversity World Newshe drove 3,000 kilometres from Cairns through the interior of Queensland to Sydney in January, and it was amazing to see Indians and Nepalis living in mining and farming communities, operating services and working in the farming, retail and cleaning sectors.“While they are studying, thanks to the government allowing remote study, online lectures and so on, they are working in these areas that otherwise will be closed.“They are working in areas that are underserviced, that Australians don't want to work in,” he said.Group of Eight (G8) CEO Vicki Thomson toldUniversity World Newsthe network’s universities are stringent in their enrolment processes, and thus such activities would not impact on the integrity of their institutions.“[We get] precisely the high-quality international students Australia needs to attract and retain to find solutions to the pressing challenges we face as a society and to build a sustainable workforce,” she said.A ‘broken’ systemBack in April, Australian authorities raised the alarm over a surge in “non-genuine” student applications, cases in which education was not the primary intent but a façade for residency ambitions or unauthorised employment.“If there is intention to complete your education legitimately and then work for the purpose of permanent residence, there isn’t anything wrong with that,” Aman Gupta, head of SAARI Collective, a South Asian community media network in Melbourne, toldUniversity World News. “Right now, it seems to be a bit broken, where the system can take advantage of disadvantaged students or the students find loopholes to take advantage of a ‘broken’ system.“The system needs to support the needs and requirements of an individual that comes here to study but also to live,” he argued.“However, once that education is complete, their pathway to permanent residency or back home needs to be clear and should not be exploited by either students or the system.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities harness AI to ‘modernise’ traditional medicineGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINAChinese universities told to step up moves to ‘go global’CHINA-ROMANIARomanian universities embrace Chinese traditional medicineCHINACHINA-US: Collaboration in traditional Chinese medicineFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsCHINA-HONG KONGUniversities harness AI to ‘modernise’ traditional medicineYojana Sharma21 September 2025TweetHong Kong is boosting research into traditional Chinese medicine as part of China’s bid to upgrade the scientific basis and promote the use of ancient herbal remedies, tapping into the power of artificial intelligence to analyse data on traditional treatments used informally across East and Southeast Asia.TheSchool of Chinese Medicineat Hong Kong Baptist University is currently a regional leader in research into old remedies.“We have a very active programme in drug discovery. A lot of these [Chinese medicines] are based on botanical drugs originally suggested by doctors a thousand years ago,” Martin Wong, provost of Hong Kong Baptist University (HKBU), toldUniversity World News.The “Hong Kong and the Chinese governments want Hong Kong to experiment and come up with a new model of how to have Western medicine and Chinese medicine combined,” he said.HKBU’s long-established School of Chinese Medicine was the first Hong Kong government-funded institution to offer undergraduate programmes in Chinese medicine and pharmacy. According to Wong, these combine elements of Western medicine as well.This month the Hong Kong government announced that the city’s first Chinese Medicine Hospital, currently under construction, will open this December. HKBU has been a key advisor for the government-funded hospital, which it will manage.Establishing Hong Kong as a centre for Chinese medicine and combining Chinese and Western medicine will also boost research into traditional medicines and could facilitate clinical trials, experts said.Collaboration agreements with HKBU and Hong Kong’s two medical schools at the University of Hong Kong (HKU) and Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK) – both with global reputations in biomedical research – were signed on 9 September. Experts at the three universities will support the new hospital’s clinical services, going beyond confining the use of Chinese medicines to primary healthcare. The universities will also conduct research there.Research on Chinese herbal remediesHKBU is using modern data science and AI to research herbal medicines with a view to developing new drugs aimed at the global market as well as modernising Chinese medicine, said Wong. AI is also used to analyse chemical compounds in plants.In 2015 Chinese scientist Tu Youyou was awarded the Nobel Prize for Medicine for her discovery of artemisinin, a groundbreaking antimalarial drug derived from a plant (Artemisia annua) mentioned in ancient Chinese texts.Tu isolated artemisinin from the Chinese herb known asQinghaosuin the 1970s and conducted clinical trials, which eventually led to the development of artemisinin-based combination drugs, now widely used around the world.Lyu Aiping, vice-president (Research and Development) at HKBU and a member of its Chinese medicine faculty, toldUniversity World News: “We envision a future where data science and AI illuminate what Chinese medicine research has long intimated, providing deeper insights into health classifications and compound interventions.”Wong explained: “At HKBU we start with a lot of clinical data based on historical [materials] about what doctors prescribed for patients with certain medical problems and what these plants are good for. Once we have that, we want to develop some drugs based on the clinical data.”“We do very sophisticated analysis using AI and figure out a lot of potential drug candidates. Once you have these, you go onto the next stage – modern biochemistry, pharmacology, toxicology, to see where these are viable and safe.”Wong said the aim was to file drug patents based on traditional Chinese medicines. Several drugs developed at HKBU have been given FDA (US Food and Drug Administration) approval for clinical trials, he pointed out.One of these has alreadyreceived “orphan drug” designationfrom the FDA to treat the rare disease myofibrillar myopathy, a neuromuscular disorder primarily caused by genetic mutations.A report titledEvolving Legacy: Decoding the scientific trajectory of Chinese medicinereleased in June by HKBU and Elsevier, the first bibliometric analysis of Chinese medicine covering the past decade, revealed that research papers on Chinese medicine nearly tripled between 2014 and 2023.The report found that Chinese medicine researchers, mainly in mainland China, are producing high-impact work and highlighted growing interdisciplinary collaboration and increasing international research partnerships.But it acknowledged that for Chinese medicine to become mainstream, global, transparent and evidence-based work was required.“To elevate its scientific profile, key strategies include developing internationally accepted clinical guidelines through expert consensus, enhancing trial reporting standards to improve research quality, and fostering interdisciplinary, cross-regional collaboration to drive innovation and systemic impact,” according to the report.Western vs Chinese approachesLyu explained that the approaches employed in Western drug development and Chinese medicine were different, with Chinese medicine emphasising combination therapies and a “whole body” approach to symptoms of disease, while also recognising social and environmental factors.“Data science and AI integration are shaping the future of Chinese medicine. We’re witnessing how traditional holistic principles can be enhanced,” he said, while also warning that an over-reliance on AI could harm the holistic and personalised nature of Chinese medicine.“We should use AI to enhance the precision, efficiency, and accessibility of Chinese medicine while preserving its core principles,” he added.Western medicines isolate medical diseases by diagnosis with no linkage between diseases, he noted, but with Chinese medicine “we can find some connection between two different diseases that would modify current interventions [treatments]”.He employs a “systems medicine” approach which focuses on complex interactions between diseases, not only single disease treatment, recognising that diseases like diabetes and hypertension interact dynamically in the human body.While the Western approach isolates single compounds and incorporates the active ingredient into drugs based on a specific diagnosis and without linkage between diseases, Chinese medicine combines herbs, which typically contain multiple compounds that work synergistically, according to Lyu.“The future of medicine lies in [understanding] these complex interactions and moving beyond the traditional single-compound drug discovery model,” he said.He believes treatment efficacy can be improved by combining two to three compounds. “Chinese medicine can actually show us how future medicine could be,” he said enthusiastically.Robust clinical evidence is crucial for acceptance, however, he said.“Clinical trials have become more and more important to prove efficacy,” Lyu said, noting: “Worldwide, more and more clinical trials on Chinese herbal products have been published.“In all the clinical trials I conduct, I collect more samples and try to analyse, with the help of AI, the difference in responsiveness and then try to find the reason for those non-responsive cases.“So rather than pure clinical trials on efficacy [this approach] would add a stage of clinical trial plus clinical pharmacology.”Dual fluencyHKBU’s Chinese medicine degrees are popular with mainland students, but only one in six applicants from outside Hong Kong are admitted to the HKBU programmes, according to Wong. The school also offers a Masters in Chinese Medicine Drug Discovery.Lyu believes the next generation of Chinese medicine professionals will require “deep, dual fluency – not only in traditional Chinese medicine, but also in contemporary biomedical sciences.”“Our graduates must master biomedical knowledge and clinical competence on par with their peers in Western medicine. This approach will raise a cohort of practitioners who are both guardians of tradition and agents of innovation. They will ensure that Chinese medicine continues to evolve and contribute meaningfully to global health,” he said.This article was amended on 23 September to correct the spelling of the name Lyu Aiping.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOMCuts bar access to humanities for swathes of studentsNic Mitchell18 September 2025TweetLarge swathes of the United Kingdom are becoming “cold spots” for social sciences, humanities, and the arts for people and the economy (SHAPE) degrees, warns the British Academy in a new report.Sweeping cuts to university funding caused by the failure of home tuition fees to keep pace with inflation over the last decade and rising costs, coupled with lower than anticipated numbers of high-fee-paying international students coming to the UK post-COVID, have led many universities to cut jobs and less popular degree courses.The newBritish Academy report,Cold spots: Mapping inequality in SHAPE provision in UK higher education, reveals that thousands of students across the country no longer have access to many humanities, social sciences or arts degrees (SHAPE) in their region.With over half of young UK students studying locally, the loss of SHAPE degrees hits those from disadvantaged backgrounds the hardest.The report says parts of the North of England, the Southwest and East of England, as well as large areas in Wales, Scotland and Northern Ireland, are particularly affected by these cold spots for humanities, social sciences and arts higher education.Modern languages feel the coldModern foreign languages have the largest number of cold spots in the UK, with the number of young UK undergraduates signing up for subjects like French, German and Spanish nearly halved since 2011.Linguistics, anthropology and classics show similar declines, and the British Academy warns that if current trends continue, English, history and drama could follow.To reverse the loss of student choice outside the big cities and major seats of learning in Southeast England, where Oxford and Cambridge are located, the British Academy calls on the government to use its forthcoming plans for post-16 education to:• Get higher education regulators to monitor “at-risk” subjects through a regional subject register and fund “at-risk” subjects.• Encourage universities in each UK nation and region to find opportunities for collaboration in teaching and research and other services.• Ask government to issue clear guidance on how far higher education providers can collaborate without breaching competition law.Symptom of financial distressProfessor Margot Finn, vice-president for research and higher education policy at the British Academy, said: “After more than 10 years of a market experiment in higher education, growing subject ‘cold spots’ are a symptom of universities in financial distress and prove that how we fund, regulate and run UK higher education isn’t working to protect students, our world-leading research, or our universities’ ability to serve their local communities.”Hetan Shah, chief executive of the British Academy, said: “Financial pressures in higher education have led to cuts, contractions and closures, and our subjects are acting as bellwethers for all.”Shah warned that the consequences are less choice and opportunity for students and the loss of critical knowledge, insight and ideas.“The humanities and social sciences combat misinformation, global disorder and vaccine hesitancy; support childhood learning, climate adaptation behaviours and good governance; and underpin cultural understanding, community cohesion and the social contract. We cannot afford to let them slip away,” said Shah.’Mickey Mouse’ degreesSarah Cowan, head of higher education and research policy at the British Academy, toldUniversity World Newsthat some politicians and parts of the popular press hadn't helped with their attacks on so-called ‘Mickey Mouse’ degrees.TheDaily Mail, for example, ran a storyheadlined: “Cash-strapped universities accused of offering ‘Mickey Mouse’ courses in rap music, esports and social media to stay afloat” in August last year.Among the courses coming under fire was the University of Bolton’s Bachelor of Arts degree in “digital content creation” to help “innovative and agile content creators” to “develop engaging digital content for a range of platforms”.Cowan hit back and toldUniversity World News: “Talk of ‘low value’ or ‘Mickey Mouse’ degrees” has little basis in fact.“Our evidence shows SHAPE subjects give young people the skills employers want: critical thinking and problem solving; curiosity and imagination; agility and adaptability.“The UK’s creative industries wouldn’t be a global success story without them either.”Critical thinkingHigher education experts agreed, with Professor Abigail Gregory, a former university senior leader of arts and humanities at both Salford and Manchester Metropolitan universities in the Northwest of England, tellingUniversity World News: “Many businesses and industries are struggling to recruit graduates with the right mix of critical thinking, problem-solving and communication skills to understand advances in technology, such as artificial intelligence, and translate and interpret them to support their use in different business settings.”Now, a higher education consultant and executive coach, Gregory urged higher education stakeholders to confront those who dismiss arts and humanities subjects as “Mickey Mouse” degrees.She pointed torecent researchshowing that 15.8% of SHAPE graduates were working in professional, scientific and technical services in 2023, with another 8.6% in finance and real estate and 4.5% working in arts and recreation.“We need to change the narrative and learn from the successful lobbying by the STEM (science, engineering, technology, and mathematics) subjects, which helped turn around student demand for engineering and mathematics in recent years. Now we must do the same for social science, the arts and humanities,” said Gregory.Lesson from IrelandTim Thompson, professor of anthropology and vice-president (students and learning) at Maynooth University in the Republic of Ireland, agreed and offered some advice from the near-abroad for UK higher education policymakers.Thompson worked previously as Dean of Health & Life Sciences at Middlesbrough’s Teesside University in the Northeast of England, identified by the British Academy as one of the SHAPE cold spots.He toldUniversity World News: “This isn’t just a UK problem, but the situation is exacerbated in the UK due to the way the university sector is funded.“I’m fortunate enough to be in the only Department of Anthropology in the Republic of Ireland, and we're growing, so there is still interest in the subject.“Part of our strength here is that we associate anthropology with other subjects, and so students in psychology, business, sociology and so on can also take some anthropology in their studies.”Thompson said universities needed to be “explicit about the benefits for their careers” in studying subjects like anthropology and that a national strategy was needed that recognises that the arts, humanities and social sciences are just as important for a successful economy as STEM.“When we speak to leading global companies engaging in AI, they want to hire arts and humanities and social science graduates because they are interested in how AI can be used, rather than the technical aspect of writing code. They are looking for creativity, not necessarily technical expertise,” said Thompson.Reaction to the new British Academy call to arms to fight to save SHAPE subjects across the country has been swift, with Dr Andrew McDonnell taking toLinkedInto tell how University Centre Peterborough, a postgraduate cold spot, plans to introduce a Master of Arts in Creative Writing degree to build on the success of its undergraduate programme in the same subject.“This will be the first postgraduate course in Peterborough, and Teesside (and some others) are also offering this provision. It shows that there is demand in these areas as detailed in this report,” he said.Dr Matt Watson, dean of the School of Social Sciences, Humanities and Law at Teesside University, toldUniversity World News: “We recognise the critical need to protect access to SHAPE subjects, especially for students who study locally and may face barriers to mobility.”Time to ‘own the narrative’Kyle Campbell, a higher education marketing consultant, welcomed the British Academy's new report and said it highlighted why it was wrong to leave academic provision to market forces and short-term gain.“There are clear, practical steps that can be taken to rethink the humanities.“English literature might have seen a 50% decline, but creative writing has seen a net increase of 28 providers since 2011, with student numbers having risen by 17% since 2021 and 2022.“Why? Because creative writing is practical. It is humanities applied,” said Campbell.‘Call it ‘practical humanities’ or whatever you like, but with the rise of AI and people increasingly outsourcing their thinking, I don't think there's been a better time for an institution to rethink the humanities.“It’s time to own the narrative,” he said.The increasing gaps in provision of social sciences, arts and humanities programmes in parts of the UK undermines higher education's contribution to two United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): SDG 4: Ensure inclusive and equitable quality education and promote lifelong learning opportunities for all; and SDG 10: Reduce inequality within and among countries.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs atwww.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

英國人文學科面臨重大削減報告指出，英國許多地區的人文、社會科學及藝術（SHAPE）學位正逐漸消失，導致學生無法獲得這些課程的機會。因為十年來學費未能跟上通脹，加上疫情後國際學生人數減少，許多大學不得不裁減工作崗位及不受歡迎的課程。英國學院呼籲政府採取措施，以確保這些學科的存續，並強調人文學科對於培養批判性思維及社會凝聚力的重要性。若不改變現狀，這些學科將無法支持社會所需的智慧和創新，對學生及社會造成長期影響。
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Indian students shift to lower-cost study destinations
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Indian students shift to lower-cost study destinationsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIA-UNITED STATESIndian students reject US study over visa uncertaintyINDIA-GLOBALIndian students abroad fall by 15%. And in Canada by 41%INDIAMore women are now seizing the chance to study abroadINDIARise in students abroad comes at a cost for India’s economyUNITED KINGDOMIndian students fight to protect post-study Graduate RouteINDIAEurope emerges as top choice for study abroad studentsFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIAIndian students shift to lower-cost study destinationsShuriah Niazi15 September 2025TweetThe increasing number of Indian students from smaller towns and cities now pursuing education abroad is transforming the profile of India’s outbound students, a profile previously dominated by aspirants from the country’s major metropolitan areas.But with overseas study more likely to be funded by commercial loans for this group, international education carries a higher risk, experts said, and this is influencing their choice of study destination.While two-thirds of all study-abroad aspirants still come from so-called ‘metro’ cities – prosperous economic hubs such as Delhi, Mumbai, Kolkata, Bengaluru (Bangalore) and Chennai – overseas enrolments increasingly reflect a rise in applications from tier 2 and tier 3 cities, according to theTransnational Education (TNE) Report 2024 to 2025, released this month by Indian education consultancyupGrad Study Abroad.The report indicates that 57% of enrolled students now originate from India’s tier 2 and 3 cities, often representing households with tighter financial constraints. This explains the growing reliance on education loans, with one-third of Indian students now borrowing, usually from banks, to fund their international studies.Cost sensitivity among these aspirants is also influencing destination choice for students who are looking for a greater return on investment, either in terms of lower fees or post-study visas and job prospects.“Indian students today are far more intentional about their education choices and are driven by geopolitical awareness, cost consciousness, and the realities of stricter visa regimes and uncertain post-study pathways,” said Praneet Singh, associate vice-president of university partnerships at upGrad Study Abroad.The report highlights a key transformation in the preferred destinations for Indian students, traditionally dominated by the United States, the United Kingdom and Canada.Now, countries such as Germany, the United Arab Emirates, Finland and France are increasingly popular among students who prioritise value, affordability and post-study opportunities.Reliance on loansThe report, based on a survey of over 100,000 study abroad aspirants, revealed that reliance on study loans has surpassed scholarships and family savings as the main funding source. While 28% of students rely on scholarships, many still turn to families for support or self-funding, which may be less available to those from tier 2 and 3 towns.“The shift towards loan-dependent overseas education represents both an opportunity and a risk for Indian families,” New Delhi-based Jessy Joseph, manager atLoyola Global Careers Ltd, toldUniversity World News.“While international degrees can open doors to global career prospects, students must carefully evaluate whether the potential salary premiums in their chosen field will support loan repayment timelines.”He added: “We're seeing families essentially betting their financial futures on education outcomes that aren’t guaranteed. The key is ensuring students have realistic employment projections and backup plans before taking on debt that could impact them for decades.”Budget considerations are also a key factor in this trend. Nearly three-quarters of students aim to remain within an overall budget of INR3 million (about US$33,000) for study abroad, with 40% budgeting INR1-2 million (about US$11,000-22,000) and 34% budgeting INR2-3 million (about US$22,000 to US$33,000) for study abroad.“The funding landscape is shifting, though family support remains substantial when you combine direct funding with parental assistance,” Maria Mathai, director of education consultancyMM Advisory Services, toldUniversity World News.“What’s concerning is that students borrowing for education face enormous pressure to succeed. When you’re borrowing for education, every semester becomes about proving ROI [return on investment]. Students can't afford to experiment; they need guaranteed outcomes,” Mathai stressed.Cost-sensitive choicesCost sensitivity is influencing destination choices, with countries like Germany, which does not charge tuition fees, becoming increasingly popular.Germany’s share of Indian students studying abroad has risen from about 13% in 2022 to 32.6% in 2024 to 2025, according to the report, surpassing both Canada and the US as a preferred destination and indicating a shift to high return-on-investment destinations.Factors such as low tuition fees, favourable visa policies, and strong STEM and management programmes contribute to its appeal. The number of Indian students in Germany is projected to grow from 49,500 in 2023 to nearly 60,000 in 2025, according to Germany’s Federal Statistical Office.Language barriers are less of a concern now, with more English-taught programmes becoming available in countries like France, Finland and Germany.Motivations for studying abroad are also evolving. Only 16.6% of students prioritise acquiring permanent residency overseas, while nearly half (48.2%) are focused on securing better job opportunities, particularly in management and STEM fields.Masters degrees remain the preferred choice, with 86.5% opting for postgraduate programmes, and there is a notable increase in demand for management courses, rising from 30% to 56% of chosen programmes in the past three years.“International education is being democratised. Digital access has enabled students from smaller cities, not just metros, to pursue global studies. Today’s first-generation learners are career-focused, bring fresh perspectives, and need tailored support from universities,” according to Mathai.Drop in traditional destinationsThe traditional appeal of the US, UK, and Canada is diminishing, particularly for students from smaller cities seeking more affordable, career-orientated programmes. Regions such asEurope, the Middle East, and Asia-Pacific are now viewed as viable alternatives, the report noted.Applications to US universitieshave droppedby 13% year-on-year,while Canada's share of Indian students has dwindledfrom 17.85% to 9.3%, a drop attributed to rising tuition costs, longer visa processing times, and policy changes.The UK remains relevant but saw an 11% decline in student visa approvals for Indian applicants in the past year.Countries like the United Arab Emirates and Qatar, which host international campuses of prestigious universities such as Georgetown, Carnegie Mellon, and Heriot-Watt, offer globally recognised degrees at a lower cost. Indian students now make up 42% of the total international student population in the UAE.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

印度學生轉向較低成本的留學目的地

根據upGrad Study Abroad最新報告，越來越多來自印度二線和三線城市的學生選擇出國留學，這一趨勢正在改變印度留學生的組成。報告指出，57%的海外留學生來自這些城市，許多學生因經濟壓力而依賴教育貸款來資助他們的國際學習。這使得他們在選擇留學目的地時更加注重性價比，德國、阿聯酋和芬蘭等國因低學費和良好的就業前景而受到青睞。與此同時，傳統的美國、英國和加拿大的吸引力正在減弱，特別是在面對高昂的學費和簽證困難的情況下。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Student-led Gen Z activists topple government, enter talks
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Student-led Gen Z activists topple government, enter talksGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNEPALAcademic leadership is a public trust, not a political prizeNEPALInternational universities eye Nepal’s high student mobilityNEPALHopes for university reforms as younger blood enters parliamentFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyNEPALStudent-led Gen Z activists topple government, enter talksBinod Ghimire12 September 2025TweetWhat started as a social media campaign by various groups in Nepal under the banner of ‘Gen Z’, but largely made up of college and university students frustrated by corruption and poor governance, turned into violent protests this week that have forced the resignation of the country’s prime minister.KP Sharma Oli, who has served as Nepal’s prime minister four times, stepped down on 9 September.“The government kneeled down in just two days,” Rakshya Bam, one of the Gen Z representatives and a masters student at a college affiliated with Kathmandu’s Tribhuvan University, toldUniversity World Newson Thursday 11 September. “We are still under negotiations. I have talked with the army leadership twice. We want a transitional government as soon as possible,” she said.With the resignation of the prime minister, the Nepal Army is now in control and is keeping President Ramchandra Paudel informed on talks being held with the protesters’ representatives to find a consensus candidate to lead a transitional government.While negotiations are ongoing, Paudel said he is working quickly to find a solution that follows the constitution and meets the protesters' demands. “I want to assure everyone that we are trying to solve the problem as soon as possible. I ask everyone to stay calm and help keep peace and order,” he said in a statement on Thursday evening.Meanwhile, with a curfew and prohibition orders in place, universities in the capital have been closed and students confined to their dormitories.Tribhuvan University in Kathmandu, with more than 480,000 students enrolled, announced a suspension of academic activities and postponed exams, according to a series of urgent notices issued by the office of the registrar, the office of the controller of examinations, and the Faculty of Management, saying exams will be rescheduled “in line with prevailing conditions”.Kathmandu University has said it is suspending all academic activities for one and a half months.Children of political elites under fireThe protests began online in early September on different social media platforms like TikTok and Reddit, with young people exposing the luxurious lifestyles of the so-called “nepo kids” – sons and daughters of the political class. They expressed rage, saying common people had been ripped off to fund their extravagance.Amid the ongoing online campaign, the government on 4 September shut down 26 unregistered social media platforms, adding fuel to the anger. The youth groups announced protests starting on 8 September, which they claimed were aimed at holding the country’s corrupt political leadership to account and removing them from power.The protest, largely peaceful in the beginning, turned violent when security forces used excessive force after protesters broke down a barricade and moved towards the federal parliament. As the situation spiralled out of control, and despite the ban on social media being lifted by the government after an emergency meeting on Monday night, the administration imposed a curfew, and security forces started firing at the protesters.Twenty youths were killed in the firing on Monday, many of them around parliament and government buildings, while approximately 350 were injured. The death toll had reached 34 as of Thursday evening.Outraged by the killings, the protesters began to target public properties, party offices and the houses of political leaders, intensifying the next day, 9 September, when government buildings were vandalised and set alight.Buildings housing ministries, including the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology, Parliament, the Supreme Court and numerous other structures, were burnt. Even big hotels, resorts, shopping malls and media houses were on fire, a situation that compelled Oli’s resignation by the end of that day.Although celebrating the prime minister’s resignation as their victory, the Gen Zs have claimed they had no hand in torching state properties. Issuing various statements, they claimed their protests had been infiltrated by “opportunists” that caused the damage.“Attacking properties is not in our plan. Our friends were not involved in it. There were infiltrations in our peaceful protest,” said non-profit organisation Hami Nepal, in a statement issued on 9 September.In a separate statement on the same day, Hami Nepal, which has been playing a mediating role between the authorities and protesters, said: “We have achieved our goal to topple the government. Now we will focus on forming a new government with our representatives.”Another statement issued by protesters said the movement “was and remains non-violent and rooted in the principles of peaceful civic engagement”.Interim leadership talksAfter discussions, a large section of the Gen Zs proposed former chief justice Sushila Karki, who has remained vocal against corruption, as prime minister of an interim government.Sudan Gurung, chair of Hami Nepal, said discussions are ongoing to reach consensus on the choice of Karki. “We are for Karki. She will lead the government while our Gen Z representatives will be in the Cabinet,” he said in a press meeting on Thursday. “We will show what people's government looks like.”However, some object to Karki’s candidacy, citing a constitutional provision which says the chief justice cannot take any political or other appointments except in the National Human Rights Commission.They are batting for Kulman Ghising, former managing director of the National Electricity Authority, who is credited for taking the country out of power shortage, for the position.The protests in Nepal mirror similar movements led recently by students and young people elsewhere in Asia, such as those inIndonesia,Bangladeshin August 2024 andSri Lankain July 2022.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Research and innovation hub to focus on intra-African trade
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Research and innovation hub to focus on intra-African tradeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsDr Yemi Kale, group chief economist at Afreximbank, Image: AfreximbankJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksALGERIAAcademia vital to intra-African trade, growth, economist saysAFRICAWanted: Skills, skills and more skills for Africa’s youngGLOBALBeyond greening in universities: A new social contractAFRICAAfrica’s economic growth depends on an overhaul in HE – ReportFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredAFRICAResearch and innovation hub to focus on intra-African tradeWagdy Sawahel10 September 2025TweetA new digital platform to promote research and innovation in intra-African trade and to support entrepreneurship development is set to advance collaboration between researchers, students, industry and policy-makers.The African Research and Innovation Hub (ARIH) was launched on the sidelines of the Intra-African Trade Fair 2025 (IATF2025) which took place in Algiers from 4-10 September. The theme was ‘Gateway to new opportunities’.The IATF2025 was co-hosted by the African Export-Import Bank, or Afreximbank, the African Union Commission and the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) secretariat.What is the hub about?The hub will enable university students, academics, researchers and national research institutions to exhibit prototypes of inventions to attract investors and to showcase published research papers on relevant topics.Priority areas include green and climate-smart solutions, digital transformation and health security, as well as food systems and agri-innovation.The establishment of the ARIH is in line with recommendations of several studies, including the 2025studytitled, ‘Beyond Policy and Grandiloquence: Africa Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) Protocol as a Vehicle to Accelerate Technology and Innovation’, which stated that “Africa should develop practical measures that encourage the adoption of digital technologies and innovation to enhance, synchronise and coordinate intra-Africa trade as the technologies could spur innovation, improve the creation of innovative business value, and alleviate the cost of trade.”Also, the 2025reporttitled,Science-Economic Diplomacy: Harnessing the African Continental Free Trade Area (AfCFTA) to Promote Indigenous Technological Capabilities, stated: “Properly harnessing the AfCFTA will facilitate technological capabilities, enable the development of innovative industries, and promote the production of high-value goods.”New development modelSpeaking during the official launch on 6 September,Dr Yemi Kale, the group chief economist and managing director for research at Afreximbank, said: “ARIH is more than an initiative, it is a long-term commitment to transform Africa’s greatest resource: the brilliance of its people.“By nurturing talent, financing ideas, and connecting research with markets, Africa can reposition itself as a knowledge-driven, innovation-powered powerhouse.“With ARIH, Africa signals that its future competitiveness depends on bridging knowledge with enterprise and aligning innovation with policy,” Kale said.ARIH focuses on connecting academia, industry, entrepreneurs, and policy-makers, showcasing African and diaspora ingenuity, turns research into market-ready products through commercialisation and venture capital and equip universities and innovators with data and tools to align with AfCFTA priorities, according to Kale.Why is a hub necessary?“I believe the timing of this launch is critical for three reasons,” Kale said. “First, unlocking AfCFTA’s promise – with 1.4 billion people and a US$3.4 trillion gross domestic product (GDP), AfCFTA can only thrive through innovation-led competitiveness.“But trade agreements, alone, do not generate competitiveness. Sustained competitiveness requires research, innovation, and the industrial capacity to transform ideas into products and services that can stand at the frontier of global markets,” Kale emphasised.Second, navigating global shifts – from artificial intelligence and digitalisation to green transitions and supply chain realignments, Africa must shape technologies, not just adopt them.“These shifts can either leave Africa behind or propel us forward, depending on how well we build and deploy our research and innovation capacity,” he said.Thirdly, Africa has to secure technological sovereignty. “To realise the ambitions of Agenda 2063, Africa must, therefore, reduce its reliance on external technologies and accelerate the development of homegrown innovations that reinforce autonomy, sustainability, and long-term security,” noted Kale.Challenges and opportunitiesAccording to Kale, Africa contributes less than 3% of the global research output and spends just 0.5% of GDP on research and development, which is far below the global average of 2.2%.“Doubling investment to 1% by 2030 could unlock US$60 billion to US$70 billion annually in agriculture, technology, and manufacturing. This would boost productivity, reduce import dependence, and expand Africa’s share of global trade.“With a median age of 19, Africa has both the demographic advantage and resources to leapfrog into the knowledge economy,” Kale said.He urged academics to align research with industry needs, industries to invest in partnerships with innovators, policy-makers to create enabling frameworks, and youth and diaspora to channel creativity and energy into Africa’s innovation journey.Becoming trailblazers in an economic renaissanceDr Mosab Nouraldein Mohammed Hamad, the director of the Excellence Research Centre at Elsheikh Abdallah Elbadri University in Sudan, toldUniversity World News: “The African Research and Innovation Hub must also put forward its education and training programmes for preparing African university graduates for dealing with real-world African trade issues and challenges, along with producing knowledge on African own terms and translate it into prosperity and well-being.“This will ensure that the graduates of today become the trailblazers of tomorrow’s African economic renaissance,” said Hamad.Hamad’s view is in line with the 2025studytitled, ‘From Inquiry to Impact: Strengthening Research Methodology for African International Trade Studies in Undergraduate Business Education’, which stated that Africa must produce “its own experts and solutions, rather than outsourcing its thinking “through preparing university graduates to conduct the feasibility studies for new trade logistics hubs, analyse data for evidence-based trade negotiations, and to innovate business models that succeed in African markets”.Homegrown, market-specific solutionsProfessor Ahmed Attia, the head of faculty affairs of the faculty of medical technology at the University of Tripoli, Libya, toldUniversity World Newsthat ARIH will act as a crucial engine for developing homegrown, market-specific solutions through linking academia and industry, along with supporting entrepreneurship, but it will face several challenges, including funding gaps, inadequate infrastructure, talent retention and insufficient policy support, along with a need to address the digital divide and lack of mentorship.“These challenges could be faced by strengthening public-private partnerships, and investing in skills development,” Attia said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal
