Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIAMedical school admissions scam probe prompts major reformsShuriah Niazi04 September 2025TweetIndia’s medical education sector has come under investigation following a large-scale admission scam involving special admission quotas for so-called non-resident Indians (NRIs) or international students of Indian origin residing abroad.The scandal has revealed deep-seated corruption and prompted nationwide reforms, including a re-evaluation of medical school admission guidelines.India’s Enforcement Directorate (ED), which investigates financial crimes and money laundering, in collaboration with the Ministry of External Affairs (MEA) and Indian embassies abroad, last month exposed an extensive operation involving 18,000 MBBS (undergraduate medical degree) and postgraduate medical seats fraudulently filled using counterfeit NRI documents.Agents partnered with private medical colleges to prepare forged embassy-issued NRI certificates and fake family trees to secure admissions for unqualified students.In some instances, legitimate NRIs were bribed to permit the misuse of their identities, according to the investigation that commenced in December 2024 with search and raid operations on several private medical colleges in West Bengal state. The findings were announced on 25 August 2025.For medical school admissions, NRIs can be Indian citizens living abroad but also children and grandchildren of Indian citizens who may hold foreign passports. These non-Indian citizens are issued with special documentation by Indian embassies abroad after verifying their descendant status.A multi-million rupee industry“The NRI quota in private medical colleges, originally intended to help children of overseas Indians pursue medical education in their home country, has instead turned into a multi-million rupee industry of corruption,” said Amulya Nidhi, national co-convenor of the People’s Health Movement of India, a network of civil society organisations and academic institutions.Nidhi toldUniversity World News: “This has sidelined genuine aspirants and pushed India’s medical education system into crisis, turning it from a public service into a profit-driven industry where commercial interests have far exceeded reason.”India has the world’s largest medical education system, with around 600 institutions, and produces around 70 to 80,000 doctors each year. The NRI quota varies between India’s states and institutions, but in private medical colleges it is typically around 15% to 25%, with students entering under the NRI quota paying higher fees.Last year some medical schools in Maharashtra state were found to be charging NRI quota students five times the normal fee.The NRI quota is much lower for publicly funded medical institutions. Rajasthan, Haryana, Punjab, and Himachal Pradesh are among the states allocating NRI quotas of between 7% and 15% in government medical colleges, charging US$75,000 to US$100,000 for the five-year programme.Cases uncoveredIn one case revealed by the ED, a student from Indore in central India obtained a seat at Aurobindo Medical College using a fake NRI certificate purportedly from the Indian Embassy in Belarus.During verification, however, the embassy declared the certificate fake within 33 minutes. The admission was immediately cancelled and legal action initiated. The parents claimed the certificate was genuine, but the investigation clearly showed irregularities, ED said.Raids conducted in states like West Bengal and Odisha uncovered large quantities of fabricated documents, including notarised seals falsely attributed to United States authorities.ED said the state authorities in West Bengal and Odisha failed to act promptly despite clear evidence of misconduct. As part of its crackdown, the ED confiscated assets worth INR123.3 million (about US$1.4 million) from a private college in West Bengal.Many certificates previously authenticated by Indian missions abroad were identified as counterfeit. ED also revealed that, contrary to regulations, fees were often paid by the students’ families rather than relatives holding NRI status, in violation of one of the requirements for NRI quota admissions.Revised regulationsIn the wake of the ED findings, the government has swiftly revised the regulations on NRI quota admissions, with stricter requirements for the upcoming 2025 to 2026 admissions cycle.The Medical Counselling Committee now stipulates only genuine NRIs or their children can qualify for admission. Sponsorship letters from extended relatives – such as NRI uncles, aunts and cousins, which have already been struck down by the courts as being eligible – are no longer accepted, and all NRI certificates must be verified by Indian embassies or missions.According to the new government guidelines, NRI certificates are valid for a period of one year and must demonstrate proof of continued residence abroad. Additionally, fees must be paid from NRI accounts to establish a financial relationship between the sponsoring NRI living abroad and the student being admitted under the NRI quota.According to Dr Aruna Kumar, director of the Medical Education Department of the state of Madhya Pradesh in central India, the aim is to increase transparency.After colleges examine documents at the preliminary level, they will be sent to the respective embassy for cross-checking. “Only if the NRI certificate is found to be correct at the embassy level will the student get admission. This will prevent wrongful admissions, and only genuine NRI students will be able to benefit. Nodal officers have also been appointed for all medical colleges for this purpose,” she toldUniversity World News.Punjab’s bid to expand NRI eligibilityIndia’s Supreme Court previously intervened to prevent some states from expanding NRI quota eligibility. In September last year, it rejected Punjab’s attempt to include distant relatives of NRIs in the state’s 15% NRI quota, labelling it a “fraud that must end”. Some other states had attempted to follow Punjab’s expanded NRI categories.The apex court upheld a prior decision by the Punjab and Haryana High Court, which struck down the state’s attempt to broaden the definition to include uncles, aunts, grandparents, and cousins.The Supreme Court criticised Punjab’s extended family list as “just a money-spinning device by the state” and emphasised that such expansions dilute meritocracy and enable manipulation of guardianship, ultimately undermining the policy’s intent to facilitate education for children of genuine NRIs and enhance foreign exchange inflow.Karnataka state rescinds higher quotaIn June 2024, Karnataka State Medical Education Minister Sharan Prakash Patil proposed a 15% NRI quota for government medical colleges, saying it was aligned with University Grants Commission guidelines and the National Education Policy 2020 to encourage international student admissions.However, by July 2025, the Karnataka Examinations Authority (KEA) proposed to reduce the NRI quota to 5% due to low uptake and widespread misuse. Data indicated that in the 2023-24 academic year, only 175 out of 767 seats were filled, and in 2024 to 2025, only 137 out of 883 seats were taken, leading to the conversion of remaining seats into management quota slots.KEA plans to propose its own measures to curb the misuse of the NRI ward category, where unrelated NRIs sponsor Indian students. While children of NRIs will retain quota rights, the proposal aims to eliminate sponsor-based admissions due to their high potential for misuse.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSOUTH KOREARecord research budget emphasises AI, talent recruitmentYumi Jeung03 September 2025TweetThe new South Korean government led by President Lee Jae-myung announced a significant increase in its first research and development (R&D) budget, prioritising science and technology as a key area for national competitiveness, ramping up investments in artificial intelligence and strategic technologies, and expanding funding for basic research and the attraction of researchers from abroad.The government announced on 22 August that the national R&D budget for 2026 will reach a record high of KRW35.3 trillion (US$25 billion), marking a 19.3% increase from the previous year. The government sees investment in R&D as an important driver of economic growth.During a meeting of the National Science and Technology Advisory Council, also on 22 August, President Lee said: “Historically, countries that respected science and technology thrived, while those that dismissed them failed. I believe this still holds true today.”The Ministry of Science and ICT (MSIT) announced its own 2026 budget this week, which is set to increase by 21.6% to KRW23.7 trillion (US$18.2 billion), with R&D expenditure alone rising to KRW11.8 trillion (US$9.7 billion). The ministry’s R&D budget accounts for approximately 33% of the total national R&D expenditure announced last month and confirmed for submission to the National Assembly on 1 September.Notably, MSIT announced a new funding track as part of the Sejong Science Fellowships for researchers to encourage the return of outstanding talent currently residing abroad.This is partly in response to a concerning brain drain of science talent from South Korea but is also seen by some experts as a lifeboat for some young scientists affected by US science budget cuts. “We expect to provide a research safety net for researchers,” MSIT said in its 1 September statement.Around KRW115 billion (US$82.56 million) will be allocated in 2026 to facilitate the return to Korea of around 130 postdoctoral researchers from overseas, it said.It has also expanded a programme to attract overseas researchers to Korea. “To expand Korea’s pool of science and technology talent and strengthen our world-class research capabilities, we will expand our programme to attract international talent,” the ministry said.“We will identify and attract leading international scholars and emerging researchers active around the world to Korea’s leading universities and research institutes and support their long-term settlement in Korea.” It announced funding of around KRW53.7 billion (US$38.55 million) for this.The ministry also said it would strengthen collaboration and support for joint research with leading overseas research institutions, allocating funding for a foundation for international R&D cooperation and a new collaboration hub with overseas research institutions.South Korea this year officially became anassociate member of Horizon Europe, the European Union’s flagship research and innovation programme. As a result, stronger partnerships and joint projects with European researchers are anticipated.In addition, several bilateral international cooperation initiatives are planned. These include a joint Korea-US project in advanced biotechnology (2025 to 2026), as well as Korea-Germany researcher workshops and training exchange programmes.At the city level, the Seoul Metropolitan Government announced in March it plans to launch AI-focused joint research with the US Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) within the year, along with efforts to establish a research centre in Seoul in partnership with MIT.Focus on AIAccording to the government’s announcement, the AI budget will increase by an impressive 106% compared to last year, with additional support also directed towards small and medium-sized enterprises and basic research to improve innovation.This follows the government’s National Vision Strategy, the government’s five-year plan released on 13 August, with a stated goal of elevating South Korea to one of the world’s top three AI powers, with the United States and China currently leading the field.MSIT stated in its own announcement on 1 September its primary focus would be strengthening the country’s science and technology capabilities with an “AI revolution”, as well as expanding strategic technologies such as energy and quantum research.Overall, the national budget specifically for AI will amount to KRW10.1 trillion (US$7.7 billion), tripling the overall budget for AI. MSIT will be responsible for KRW5.1 trillion (US$3.9 billion).The government has allocated KRW600 billion (US$46.15 million) specifically for innovation in AI, accelerating the development of an ecosystem that promotes technology integration and innovation.Its budget allocation includes training for around 11,000 postgraduate specialists in AI at some 24 universities.“To secure a competitive edge in the global AI talent pool, we plan to strengthen the development and acquisition of top-tier AI talent,” MSIT said. The plans include a new AI-focused university, which has been allocated KRW25.5 billion from the ministry’s budget.Record increase in basic researchMSIT said this week it will invest a “record-breaking” KRW2.74 trillion to “revitalise the basic research ecosystem”, with the number of basic research projects it supports expected to increase by 28.5% compared to 2025, including restoring around 2,000 fundamental research projects controversiallycancelled in 2024under the previous administration.MSIT First Vice-Minister Koo Hyuk-chae said “the 2026 basic research budget is the first starting point for a leap forward beyond the restoration of the basic research ecosystem”.He added: “We will effectively manage the allocated budget to create an environment where young researchers can conduct research without worry and in a stable manner and support excellent researchers so that they can continue to grow.”Researchers and experts generally welcomed the R&D budget increase, but many emphasised the need to improve the research environment, with more balanced support across various sectors like bioscience and robotics.Narrow focusThe strong focus on just a few sectors has been criticised by civic groups, including thePeople’s Solidarity for Participatory Democracy, which said the government’s approach risked reinforcing technology monopolies and economic concentration in certain sectors.A researcher at the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST), who is currently completing a postdoctoral programme in materials science, said: “While I welcome the tripling of the AI budget, creating a sustainable research environment and guaranteeing researcher autonomy are more important in the long term.”Another researcher with a PhD in engineering from Seoul National University, who now works at a government research institute, noted: “Increasing support for AI and related fields is positive. However, the focus should also be on improving short-term performance-based evaluations for researchers and reducing unnecessary administrative procedures and regulations.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackUNITED STATESUS withdraws from key human rights review processNathan M Greenfield01 September 2025TweetOn 28 August, PEN America and PEN International published its joint submission, “Rights to education, free expression, opinion, and non-discrimination” (Rights to Education), which was highly critical of the United States, to the United Nations Universal Periodic Review (UPR).But within hours President Donald J Trump’s State Department informed the UN Human Rights Council (UNHRC) that America was withdrawing from the audit of its human rights record.Following a resolution passed by the UN General Assembly in 2006, every four-and-a-half years, member states of the UN undergo such an audit and receive a report with recommendations on how they can improve human rights in their country – which includes the right to education.“Engagement in UPRs implies endorsement of the [Human Rights] Council’s mandate and activities and ignores its persistent failure to condemn the most egregious human rights violators,”a State Department official toldReuters.The official added that the US would not stand for being“lectured[to] about our human rights record by the likes of HRC members such as Venezuela, China or Sudan”.America’s unprecedented withdrawal from the processwas not unexpected. On 4 February Trump ordered that the US withdraw from the UNHCR as well as UNESCO and UNRWA (UN Relief and Works Agency for Palestine Refugees in the Near East), saying three UN agencies had drifted from their mission of “prevent[ing] future global conflicts and promot[ing] international peace and security” and of “act[ing] contrary to the interests of the United States while attacking our allies and propagating anti-Semitism”.Another reason America’s withdrawal was not unexpected was that it had failed to submit its National Report by the scheduled date of 4 August. Thisprompted the UPR Academic Network, a watchdog group based at Birmingham City University (UK), to mount a petition, calling on the US to participate in the process as it had in 2010, 2015 and 2020. The first countries called on to complete UPR reports submitted them in 2008.A ‘devastating precedent’PEN America responded to America’s withdrawal from the UPR process with a biting press release.“This action sets a devastating precedent for universal review of human rights norms and suggests a blatant disregard for the US’ international human rights obligations. It also signals to other countries like Russia, Iran and China that they do not need to be accountable for protecting the human rights of their people, including writers and journalists who seek to tell important stories in their society.“This is a misguided retreat from US leadership in the international community. Whereas the US has led participation and engagement in the past, it is now leading a potential retreat from this process.“PEN America condemns the decision to withdraw and strongly urges the administration to reconsider,” said Hadar Harris, managing director of the free expression and writers’ organisation’s Washington, DC office, in the statement.In an interview withUniversity World News, Harris added: “This withdrawal also signals that the United States is not open to examination of its own conduct – something which has been an important principle undergirding the United States’ promotion of rule of law, freedom of expression and human rights around the world.As the Trump administration reconsiders its multilateral engagement on human rights and other issues, we strongly urge the US to remain actively engaged and be a constructive force for freedom of expression and all other human rights at home and abroad,” said Harris.In a statement, Uzra Zeya, president and CEO of the New York-based NGO Human Rights First, alluded to the US’ decades-long claim to the leader of the Free World. “Showing up and explaining your record on human rights is the bare minimum for any government that purports to exercise international leadership and uphold democratic norms,” she said.“The United States isn’t being singled out – every UN member takes its turn having its human rights record assessed. Running away from scrutiny doesn’t just show weakness and a lack of confidence; it will give rights-abusing governments cover to do the same themselves,” said Zeya, who had been Under Secretary of State for Civilian Security, Democracy and Human Rights during the presidency of Joe Biden.Trump has boasted about being a strong president and that under him the US is respected as it never has been before, and Zeya’s statement turns that rhetoric on its head.Limits on what is taughtThe “Rights to Education” report details a number of US state- and federal-level laws and policies, as well as executive orders that “restrict access to education and infringe on the rights of free expression and non-discrimination” that “disproportionately impact marginalised communities”.Between 2021 and 2024, 372 “educational gag orders” were introduced into legislatures in 46 of America’s 50 states that targeted K-12 and-or college classrooms: 46 were passed into law in 23 states.A number of bills name what cannot be taught: critical race theory, that systemic racism is part and parcel of American history, that there is “structural racism”, and issues relating to gender identity and sexual orientation.For example, in Alabama, PEN America told the UPR about a bill that became law in March 2024 and which applies to both K-12 and universities prevents teachers from requiring students to “participate in coursework in which certain ideas related to race, colour, religion, sex, ethnicity or national origin are promoted”.PEN explained to the Geneva-based organisation that is about a short drive from the German border that the law, which also prevents the assigning of “any readings whose authors describe feelings of guilt for historical wrongs … would in effect ban a reading from a German citizen who expressed guilt over the country’s role in the Holocaust”.Referring to a similar law in Florida, PEN America’s analysis highlights how it “ [n]ot only… limits the educational rights of students enrolled in teacher training programmes, it ensures downstream consequences, embedding ignorance of history of racial and gender-based discrimination in the US into the curriculum for generations to come”.Students who attend high school in, for example, Florida,as these pages have detailed, do not learn that slavery is the cause of the American Civil War – despite the factthat Confederate politicians statedthat they seceded from the Union to defend their, so-called, “peculiar intuition” and the fact thatthe Confederate Constitutionitself defends slavery.Nor do these students learn about the brutality of slavery. Rather,their curriculum teachesthat enslaved people “developed skills” for “personal benefit”.“If students can’t be exposed to ideas about a slew of ‘divisive topics’ that are central to understanding our country and our uneven social fabric, it isn’t just about high school students coming [into college] with half-baked ideas about history – it’s about how a generation of students will have only a CliffsNotes version of things… with the controversial parts edited out,” Amy Reid, senior manager for PEN America’s Freedom to Learn Programme, wrote toUniversity World Newsfrom Geneva, where she was representing PEN America, one of the civil society groups meeting with the UNHCR in preparation for what was planned as the review of the United States.The ‘Balkanisation’ of US educationIn previous reports, PEN Americahas listed the books banned in school districts across the United States. Among these books are George Orwell’s1984andAnimal Farm, which are banned in half of Florida’s 67 school districts.Also banned in many districts in Florida are Margaret Atwood’sThe Handmaid’s Taleand Richard Wright’sBlack Boy, as well as Ralph Ellison’sThe Invisible Man, these last two being classic works of Black American literature.This intellectual Balkanisation of America’s students will create more than classroom management problems for professors in, for example, Illinois, California, New York or Massachusetts, where the history of slavery is taught and where these and other banned works are part of the high school curriculum and, thus, form the foundation upon which the university curriculum rests.“It’s a huge problem, because part of what is happening in trying to restrict certain books, concepts and ideas from public education,” Harris toldUniversity World News, “is to undermine public education as a whole.“And by doing that, this will create a situation where people don’t have common reference points for literature or history. They also don’t have civics education, and they won’t have the fundamental critical thinking skills, which are an essential part of education as a whole,” she said.In his email response toUniversity World News, Peter C Herman, a professor of English at San Diego State University who has taught1984many times and is editor ofTerrorism and Literature, began by reaching for Orwell.“Banning students from reading books that challenge authority puts into practice a key line from1984(one of the banned books): ‘Who controls the past controls the future: who controls the present controls the past.’ That’s what’s happening here: Republicans want to control the past so they can control the future, so they can hold power forever. This is how the kind of tyranny depicted in1984happens in reality,” he said.Herman then set the issue in the divide between America’s Red (Republican) and Blue (Democratic) states, albeit with an important twist.“The radical difference between what kids in California and Florida can read will only further deepen the polarisation between Red and Blue states. It’s no longer a matter of tax policy, but of two opposing views of reality.“The old saying, ‘you’re entitled to your opinion, but not your own facts’; doesn’t work anymore because nobody agrees on what’s a ‘fact’. If kids in one state are taught that slavery was basically okay, and kids in another state are taught that slavery was an abomination, how can they agree on anything? This will not end well.”America’s psych-historyShawn O Utsey, distinguished professor of counselling psychology and African American studies at Virginia Commonwealth University (Richmond, Virginia), discussed the censorship regimen that PEN America explicates in “Rights to Education” in terms of what can be called American’s psych-history.People, he began, work very hard to guard against, control or regulate their anxiety, including, he explained by way of example, regarding events in the political and historical world.“Think about how we regulate or modulate much of the very disturbing narrative of American history. We have already rewritten history with regard to the narrative of Columbus and the role of Native Americans, and the lack of involvement by the enslaved Africans in building this country,” said Utsey.Then, referring to Trump, both as a political figure and as what amounts to an avatar of a stream of American politics, Utsey toldUniversity World News, Trump “recognised that there was still room to help Americans with their anxiety about the past and the shame and guilt they were feeling” following what white Americans see as “the encroachment of various people from outside of America and, even in America, who are not considered to be American by virtue of their phenotypical traits”.He explained this term with a thought experiment: “When we think of Americans and close our eyes, most people, white, Black, Asian or Latino, will probably think of a white person.” This analysis, applies too, Utsey explained, to the backlash against diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) programmes in education, which PEN America anatomises for the UNHRC.The vague language in many of the bills banning DEI offices and programmes, PEN America told the UNHCR, “coupled with provisions that levy financial or other penalties on institutions that fail to comply, have resulted in egregious examples of institutional overcompliance that further chill the climate for free expression”.Iowa’s DEI ban is particularly broad, requiring the state’s public universities to “remain neutral on almost any topic related to social dynamics” including “unconscious or implicit bias, cultural appropriation, allyship, transgender ideology, microaggressions, group marginalisation, anti-racism, systemic oppression, social justice, intersectionality, neo-pronouns, gender theory, racial privilege, sexual privilege, or any related formulation of these concepts.”So what will be the psychological impact on students from, for example, Florida, who find themselves in history or English literature courses and realise that the standard story of history they learnt in high school was false and that they have huge (and politically engineered) gaps in their knowledge of literature?According to Utsey, their first reaction is likely to be to feel threatened by the unknown because it jeopardises their sense of identity.Utsey sketched a moment when students realise that the categorical structure that defines ‘Whiteness’, as opposed to ‘Blackness’ (of enslaved Africans or, later, the Irish or Italian immigrants who were at first considered ‘Black’), at the heart of America is akin to “a psychological Jedi mind trick”.The phrase comes fromStar Warsand denotes the ability of Jedi knights to implant a suggestion in a (weaker) person’s mind. InStar Wars, the Jedi’s use of the “mind trick” is bound by ethical limits that are, perforce, absent from the legislators and curriculum designers that formulated a curriculum divorced from the realities of American history but, nevertheless, was internally consistent, he said.The mind trick at the heart of the curriculum designed by those who would ban books and create a mythical American history is, Utsey averred, the psychodynamics of racism that in 1960 then Vice President Lyndon Johnson explained to his assistant (and later famed broadcaster) Bill Moyers.“I’ll tell you what’s at the bottom of it. If you can convince the lowest white man he’s better than the best coloured man, he won’t notice you’re picking his pocket. Hell, give him somebody to look down on, and he’ll empty his pockets for you,”said Johnson.In other words, the aim of the exercise is to make students defend the identity they established through their high school curricula because it works to elevate them above Blacks or others, such as LGBTQ+ people deemed inferior.The type of censorship regimen described by PEN America is an indication, Utsey explained, that “we’re in a battle for the narrative of truth and reality”.Borrowing from the African American philosopher Wade Nobles (emeritus professor of Africana Studies and Black Psychology at San Francisco State University), Utsey said that “power is the ability to define your own reality and to convince other people that your reality is their reality. That’s what’s happening now”, as a result of school censorship.“A new reality is being carved out. And the ultimate goal is to convince everyone that this [for instance, the benign view of slavery] is, in fact, the truth. That’s the scary part,” said Utsey.At this writing, whether the UNHCR can issue a report without the participation of the United States is unclear.On 28 August, the online Washington, DC-based news websiteThe Hill.comquoteda spokesperson for the UNHRC saying: “The Bureau of the Human Rights Council (its president and four vice-presidents) will discuss this matter at its earliest convenience. This is a matter for the 47 member states of the Human Rights Council to consider at a later stage.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

美國退出關鍵人權審查程序

美國於8月28日宣布退出聯合國人權理事會（UNHRC）的人權紀錄審查程序，這一消息引起了廣泛關注。該決定在PEN America和PEN International發表了一份批評美國的報告後不久發布，報告中強調了教育、言論自由和非歧視權利的問題。美國國務院表示，不願意接受來自如委內瑞拉和中國等國的批評，並認為參與審查會對美國的國際領導地位造成損害。此舉被認為對全球人權規範的普遍審查樹立了“災難性先例”，可能會使其他人權侵害國家不再受到問責。專家警告，這將影響美國在國際社會中的形象及對教育公平的承諾。

Associate/Full Professor TenuredGLOBALFears over global mismatch between research and health needsNic Mitchell29 August 2025TweetResearchers are warning that the gap between global research priorities and health needs will widen again if the United States presses ahead with slashing funding for international disease control, with Sub-Saharan Africa the most exposed geographical region.With the help of generative artificial intelligence (AI), an international team led by Dr Leo Schmallenbach and Professor Marc Lerchenmüller from Mannheim Business School in Germany mapped how closely research output aligns with the illnesses causing the greatest loss of life worldwide.The study linking 8.6 million disease-specific research publications to global disease burden data, from 1999 to 2021, found a narrowing of the gap over the two decades.However, this wasn’t down to changing research priorities. Instead, it was caused by a rise in non-communicable diseases, such as cancer, cardiovascular disease and diabetes, and the declining impact of communicable diseases, like tuberculosis, on global health.The researchers say the divergence between research and disease is now expected to widen by as much as a third over the next 25 years, as trends in disease burden continue to change around the world.US funding cuts will widen gapAnd they claim the risk will be significantly increased by cuts in US public funding for international research.Dr Schmallenbach, a habilitation candidate in innovation management at Mannheim University, toldUniversity World News: “The US has a long-standing commitment to funding research publicly, including international, collaborative research.“Cuts to this established system will inevitably have detrimental consequences.“Our data suggests that the disparity between research and disease burden will increase if US public funding is reduced, which would disadvantage research into both communicable diseases, such as HIV, and non-communicable diseases, such as neurological disorders.“Patients around the world are likely to be affected, with the Global South being particularly vulnerable.”China and UK could step inSome countries, including China and the UK, have the capacity to step in for certain disease areas.According to the findings of the Mannheim-led study, which were first published in the journalNature Medicinein August 2025, China has the largest share of non-US funding in both respiratory infections and tuberculosis (20%) and neurological disorders (16%), indicating potential capacity to fill emerging gaps in these domains.The United Kingdom leads in funding HIV/AIDS and sexually transmitted infections research (16%), while Canada (12%) and Australia (10%) are notable funders of research on substance use disorder.However, Schmallenbach toldUniversity World News: “Broader international coordination would be needed to compensate for retraction of public US funding in the short run and to create a more strategic research enterprise in the long run.”Sub-Saharan Africa most exposedTo identify which areas of global research are most vulnerable to potential cuts in US public funding, the study stratified publications receiving US public funding according to disease area and first-author region.It found Sub-Saharan Africa was the most exposed geographical region, with US public funding supporting over 20% of region-led publications in several disease categories.Among the most exposed diseases, 25% of research on respiratory infections and tuberculosis and 41% on HIV/AIDS and sexually transmitted infections received US financial support.Research on substance use disorders and neurological disorders also showed high dependency on US public funding.At the time of writing the report forNature Medicine, the US administration had proposeda 40% cut to the National Institutes of Health budget, from US$ 47 billion down to US$ 27 billion, while terminating existing grants midstream and doubling the rate of grant rejections.Two decades of progress at risk“Withdrawal of US public funding for non-US-led research will lead to a sharp increase in the divergence of global research and global disease burden, with the potential to reverse two decades of progress over the longer term,” say the researchers in their report.Looking at the disparity between global health research and disease burden, Professor Lerchenmüller toldUniversity World News: “There is no single disease that receives ‘too much’ research.“However, given finite resources, we must carefully consider how research attention is distributed. Disease burden is only one dimension of this allocation problem, but it is a critical one.”Lerchenmüller said research attention is strongly correlated with regionality, and the diseases that primarily affect people in Sub-Saharan Africa, such as respiratory infections, tuberculosis and maternal or neonatal disorders, are “consistently under-researched relative to their disease burden”.“In contrast, diseases like neoplasms (tumours), which cause substantial harm in Western societies, receive the highest share of research attention,” he said.Lerchenmüller stated that this was not so surprising, as regional research agendas are often funded by taxpayer money, but he added: “Nonetheless, our findings indicate a certain imperative for more pan-regional, international collaboration to better inform improvements for human health.”DALY metricThe Mannheim-led study built on previous research relating the distribution of research publications across diseases to the distribution of disease burden using the DALY (Disability-Adjusted Life Year) metric to quantify the total number of years of life lost because of illness, disability or premature death.Alignment was defined by the extent to which the proportion of global research devoted to a particular disease corresponded to the proportion of global DALYs attributed to that disease.Research production was relatively greater than the disease burden for disease areas such as neoplasms (abnormal growth of cells, or a tumour), neurological disorders and digestive diseases, while research production fell short of the relative disease burden for cardiovascular diseases, maternal and neonatal disorders, and respiratory infections.Only in a few disease areas, for example, diabetes and kidney diseases, did the research team find relatively close alignment.COVID proved world can act fastThe study said the world had shown that it could respond quickly to sudden global health challenges, as it did when DALYs caused by COVID-19 increased from about 8% (2019) to 13% (2020) and then to a 16% (2021) share of global DALYs.Research into COVID-19 quadrupled and showed the capacity of the global research system to align rapidly with emerging health needs, says the Mannheim research-led team.Schmallenbach toldUniversity World News: “We hope our findings will stimulate a broader discussion on how research priorities and funding agendas are set [and] that the new data will be used at global, regional and national levels by researchers, funders and policymakers, enabling them to assess how their own initiatives and investment priorities compare to societal health needs.The research team included colleagues based in Germany, the United States, South Africa and Switzerland.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
全球研究與健康需求間的鴻溝擴大之憂

研究人員警告，如果美國繼續削減國際疾病控制的資金，全球研究優先事項與健康需求之間的差距將再次擴大，尤其是撒哈拉以南非洲地區最為脆弱。由德國曼海姆商學院的Dr. Leo Schmallenbach及Professor Marc Lerchenmüller領導的國際團隊，利用生成式人工智慧分析了860萬篇疾病特定研究出版物與全球疾病負擔數據的關聯。

研究顯示，雖然過去20年間鴻溝有所縮小，但這主要是因為非傳染性疾病的增加與傳染性疾病影響的下降。若美國公共資金減少，將對研究造成負面影響，特別是在呼吸道感染、艾滋病等領域。專家呼籲國際合作，以彌補公共資金的撤回。

Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOMProgress in cancer survival rate stalled after 2010Nic Mitchell29 August 2025TweetAfter remarkable progress in cancer survival rates in England and Wales in the four decades leading up to 2010, the pace of improvement has stalled, with pressures on the National Health Service (NHS) cancer services leading to longer delays in patients receiving diagnosis and treatment, according to a study by researchers at the London School of Hygiene and Tropical Medicine (LSHTM).The 10-year survival levels for cancer patients more than doubled between the early 1970s and 2018, rising from 23.7% to 49.8%. However, most of the improvements came before the mid-2000s.Since then, the rate of progress has stalled as waiting lists for treatment lengthened, and delays to early diagnosis meant many patients didn’t receive care as quickly as needed.That’s one of the key conclusions from the study, published inThe Lancet Regional Health – Europe, which analysed anonymised records of more than 10 million adults diagnosed with cancer between 1971 and 2018 in England and Wales.Research comes at critical timeThe research, funded by the Cancer Research UK charity, comes at a critical time as the British Labour Government is preparing to publish a new National Cancer Plan for England later this year.The study, conducted by the Cancer Survival Group at LSHTM, strove to be non-political, but the year 2010 was an important date in the political calendar with the election of a Conservative-Liberal Democrat coalition government after 13 years of Labour governments which prioritised public health spending.Although the health service was spared from the most severe elements of the austerity programme introduced by the post-2010 Tory-led coalition government, the performance of the NHS declined in the years leading up to the Covid-19 pandemic when the Conservatives were solely in control.Dr Hugh Alderwick, Director of Policy at the UK’sHealth Foundationand a former fellow in Health Care Policy and Practice at the University of California, San Francisco, and Berkeley in the United States, is among researchers highlighting the deteriorating performance of the NHS after 2010.Alderwick, who previously worked at The King’s Fund, where he provided policy advice to the NHS and government,publisheda paper after Labour regained power in the 2024 General Election, titled ‘Conservative Party’s legacy on the NHS’.In it, Alderwick claimed that longer waits for planned hospital services, such as tests, scans, and surgery – and urgent cancer treatment – meant “totemic NHS targets were routinely missed, resulting in avoidable harm to patients” after 2010.Now he says it is up to Sir Keir Starmer’s Labour government to show that the NHS may be weakened, but it is not broken.National Cancer Plan revivedSo far details of how the year-old government intends to improve cancer survival rates are thin on the ground, but Health Secretary Wes Streeting is promising a 10-year health plan, which will include a new national cancer plan.As a first step, Labour has reversed the 2023 decision by the last government to “withdraw a new national cancer plan” after the Conservatives proposed subsuming cancer into a “major conditions strategy”, covering a wide range of other diseases, including cardiovascular disease, stroke, musculoskeletal disorders, diabetes, mental health and respiratory disease.According to the LSHTM study, this left England as one of the few high-income countries “in which a national cancer plan was not a central pillar of national health policy”.The Department of Health and Social Care (DHSC) has now recognised the need to “bring this country's cancer survival rates back up to the standards of the best in the world” and has issued acall for evidenceto assist the government in improving outcomes for cancer sufferers and reducing lives lost to the biggest killers.Their call, titledShaping the national cancer plan, accepts the disease in its many forms cannot be prevented but says early diagnosis is the best tool to beat it and hopes to improve access to diagnostics by shifting resources from hospital to community through community diagnostic centres and at-home testing.Cancer survival index (CSI)Should the government require the data to back up the need for a new plan, the LSHTM study provides excellent material on the cancer survival index (CSI) for all cancers.Their paper said the CSI has increased dramatically since the 1970s. In 2018, the overall likelihood of 10-year survival for all cancers combined stood at 49.8%, compared to only 23.7% in the early 1970s.It increased nearly three times faster in the early 2000s than in the early 2010s, and reached just under 50% of cancer patients surviving 10 years by 2018. However, the survival rate only increased by 1.9% in the eight years after 2010.Breast, bowel and cervical cancers have seen the largest improvements in survival patterns, partly due to effective screening programmes which help diagnose patients at an earlier stage, when the cancer is easier to treat, said the report.Improved awareness of symptoms and innovations in treatment, such as immunotherapy, have also contributed to better outcomes for many cancers.However, the LSHTM study showed: “The remarkable progress for some types of cancer has not been seen for several of the more lethal cancers.“Ten-year survival for cancers of the oesophagus, stomach, lung and brain has increased very little in the past 50 years and remains below 20%, whilst for pancreatic cancer, survival after 10 years is still less than 5%.“These forms of cancer are harder to detect and treat, which is why targeted research in this area is vital.”Targeted research is vitalThe difference in survival between cancer types in the UK is now bigger than it has ever been, with survival for 10 years or more ranging from 97% for testicular cancer to just 4.3% for pancreatic cancer.Professor Michel Coleman, lead researcher and Professor of Epidemiology and Vital Statistics in the Cancer Survival Group at the LSHTM, said: “The Cancer Survival Index (CSI) is a one-number summary of net survival for all cancers combined.“It is an important policy tool. Trends in the CSI provide a real-world baseline from which to develop and monitor targets for the national cancer plan.”Professor Claudia Allemani, co-author and Professor of Global Public Health at LSHTM, said: “For patients diagnosed in 2018, the CSI for all cancers combined at 10 years after diagnosis (49.8%) is now higher than the CSI at one year for those diagnosed during 1971–72 (46.5%).“That is a remarkable improvement, but allowing this trend to stall will have devastating consequences. Pressures on NHS cancer services result in longer delays for patients to receive diagnosis and treatment. The proportion of patients who are diagnosed at stage 1 or 2 is still well below the 2028 target of 75%. The national cancer plan should exploit our population-based research to improve NHS cancer pathways and accelerate trends in survival.”Crucial datasets invaluableDr Veronica Di Carlo, co-author and Research Fellow at LSHTM, said one of the study’s key messages for international audiences lies in the role of data.The analysis relied on decades of invaluable anonymised records curated by national cancer registries - datasets that are both scientific assets for researchers and an educational resource. This is something other countries could learn from the UK’s registry-based approach.Di Carlo toldUniversity World News: “It’s vital that Government provides the political and financial support to ensure that the national cancer registries can thrive. Without these crucial data, the Government will be flying blind on cancer control strategy to support all cancer patients.”For universities, the study reinforces the case for embedding data literacy, epidemiological methods and health informatics into curricula.Cancer Research UK’s chief executive Michelle Mitchell said: “Thanks to research, most patients today are far more likely to survive their cancer than at any point in the past.“But the reality is that this progress is slowing – and for some cancers, it never got going in the first place.“The upcoming national cancer plan for England must include commitments to spot more cancers earlier, as well as backing research into new treatments.”UK outpaced by other countriesWhether the UK can catch up again with the leading nations fighting cancer, which has been one of the driving forces of previous cancer strategies in England, remains to be seen.Survival rates in countries such as Sweden, Norway and Finland have long outpaced the UK, largely due to faster diagnosis, wider screening coverage and greater access to modern treatments.The US has also reported higher average survival, though with stark inequalities by race, and the authors of the new LSHTD study say that despite the CSI not being directly comparable across all countries because it is standardised differently, the trend is what matters – and England and Wales are falling behind the pace of improvement seen elsewhere.Visa fees put off international researchersSky-high visa fees are said to be putting off international researchers from accepting job offers to support research into curing and diagnosing cancers in the UK, according to a report inThe Observernewspaper on 24 August 2025.This said that Cancer Research UK had told them that several pieces of research had been affected by soaringimmigration costs, which have risen by 126% since 2019and are up to 17 times higher than the average of comparable countries, including France, Australia, the US and South Korea.“Analysis of Cancer Research UK’s annual spending on immigration costs shows that the amount the charity has paid the government in visa fees and other surcharges has nearly doubled since 2022 to 2023, rising from £477,244 (US$644,300) to £872,044 this year.“The money would be enough to train 40 PhD students, according to Dr Iain Foulkes, executive director of research and innovation at Cancer Research UK,” said the report.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. His blogs include‘Cancer-Talk’, recording his own experience of fighting bowel cancer after an early diagnosis in 2014.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Assistant Professor TenureTrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyINDIAPublishing partnership aims to promote better researchShuriah Niazi28 August 2025TweetThe government of India has teamed up with Springer Nature, a leading international research publisher, to enhance awareness of research integrity, open access and scholarly publishing practices in the country's Tier 2 and Tier 3 academic institutions.The Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser to the Government will collaborate with the publisher, leading to the launch of the RISE-UTTHAN initiative, a planned academic programme aimed at providing academia, researchers and scholars with globally recognised tools and knowledge to promote ethical, inclusive and impactful research practices.This comprehensive programme is designed to empower researchers and scholars with tools and knowledge, focusing on key areas of academic research.While there isn’t extensive specific research on the divide between researchers based on city tiers, a significant divide exists among Indian researchers based on city classification.According to some researchers, researchers based in large metropolitan areas enjoy superior access to funding, infrastructure and innovation opportunities, while those in Tier 2 and 3 cities struggle with resource constraints despite having substantial talent pools and being emerging economic hubs.Global knowledge sharingThe Government of India established the Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser (PSA) in November 1999. The PSA’s office aims to provide pragmatic and objective advice to the prime minister and the cabinet in matters of science and technology.According to the official statement, the initiative will promote research integrity in science and education, empowering scholars to conduct and communicate their research in alignment with the highest internationally benchmarked standards of ethics and transparency.“It will boost open access awareness and the future of science by supporting India's One Nation, One Subscription initiative by helping researchers steer the evolving global landscape of open science,” it said.It will also enhance researchers' capabilities to transition to changing publishing models, from traditional methods to digital and open formats, ensuring their work reaches broader audiences and contributes to global knowledge sharing.Principal Scientific Adviser Professor Ajay Kumar Sood has emphasised the importance of ethical and accessible research for India’s innovation-driven future.“The partnership with Springer Nature is set to empower researchers, in alignment with India's development goals, contributing positively to healthcare, education, and sustainable growth,” said Professor Sood in a statement.Building knowledge about open accessAcademics said the partnership aligns with India’s national priorities and reflects a shared commitment to fostering a culture of responsible, high-quality and accessible research.Sangeeta Verma, a researcher based in Sagar in central India, said: “Many of us in smaller institutions have limited knowledge about open access publishing and digital research tools. This initiative will help us transition from traditional publishing methods to modern, globally recognised formats, ensuring our research reaches international audiences.”“It will also enhance researchers' capabilities to transition to changing publishing models, from traditional methods to digital and open formats, ensuring their work reaches broader audiences and contributes to global knowledge sharing,” she toldUniversity World News.The initiative will utilise a combination of workshops, lectures and hands-on training to involve 25–30 research-active institutions and aims to reach around 5,000 researchers during the India Research Tour 2025. It is expected to expand in the following years based on feedback from institutions and alignment of resources.Springer Nature is one of the leading publishers of research in the world, publishing the largest number of journals and books.According to Venkatesh Sarvasiddhi, Managing Director of Springer Nature India: “For more than 180 years, the research publisher has been dedicated to advancing discovery by backing the global research community.”Springer’s affiliation with the Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser represents a significant advancement in fostering India’s research ecosystem. The aim is to ensure that researchers across the country, regardless of their location, have access to the knowledge, tools and support essential to publish with integrity, embrace open science and contribute effectively to both national and global issues. The association also aligns with India's national priorities, including the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), he noted.New inequalities?The initiative aims to engage 25 to 30 research-active institutions and reach around 5,000 researchers during the India Research Tour 2025, with plans for further expansion in subsequent years based on feedback from participating institutions and resource availability.Dr Sapna Poti, Director of Strategic Alliances at the Office of the Principal Scientific Adviser, stated that enhancing research integrity and open access is crucial for democratising knowledge and empowering institutions throughout India.The RISE-UTTHAN initiative aspires to create a strong ecosystem where researchers from tier-2 and tier-3 institutions can make meaningful contributions to global science and national development.However, a researcher requesting anonymity said: “I’m concerned about new academic inequalities. Selecting only 25 to 30 institutions creates a hierarchy within already marginalised Tier 2 and 3 systems – what about the hundreds of other deserving colleges?“The English-language emphasis troubles me, as someone researching traditional knowledge in the vernacular will be at a loss,” she said.“Instead of democratising research, we risk academic colonialism benefiting only the privileged within underprivileged institutions,” she added.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 印度政府與國際知名研究出版商Springer Nature建立合作夥伴關係，推出RISE-UTTHAN計劃，旨在提升二級和三級學術機構的研究誠信、開放存取及學術出版實踐。該計劃將通過工作坊和實踐訓練，幫助研究人員使用全球認可的工具，促進倫理和包容性研究，並提升他們轉型至數位和開放出版模式的能力。此舉希望能夠縮小城市間的研究資源差距，並支持印度的可持續發展目標。專家擔心此計劃可能會加劇學術不平等，因為僅選擇25至30所機構參與。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Former president arrested over alleged misuse of funds
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Former president arrested over alleged misuse of fundsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsSri Lanka’s former president and six-time prime minister Ranil Wickremesinghe. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSRI LANKAStudents mount renewed protests over their leaders’ arrestSRI LANKAStudents, young people play key role in ousting presidentSRI LANKAPM resigns amid violence, as student protest camp targetedFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSRI LANKAFormer president arrested over alleged misuse of fundsDinesh De Alwis28 August 2025TweetSri Lanka’s former president and six-time prime minister Ranil Wickremesinghe was arrested on 22 August 2025, on charges of misusing state funds during his presidency and in connection with a case involving the University of Wolverhampton in the United Kingdom, marking the first time a former Sri Lankan leader has been arrested and remanded.The allegations centre on a controversial trip to the University of Wolverhampton in the United Kingdom in September 2023, where his wife, Professor Maithree Wickramasinghe, was awarded an honorary professorship.The Criminal Investigation Department (CID) and the Attorney General’s Department allege that the visit, which cost over US$55,000 in public funds, was billed as an official state visit despite being a private trip that brought no tangible benefit to Sri Lanka, amounting to a waste of public money.Ranil Wickremesinghe was arrested on 22 August 2025. His deteriorating health led to his transfer to the Intensive Care Unit at Colombo National Hospital from prison, and he was granted bail on 26 August, considering his medical condition, while opposition parties staged rallies in Colombo demanding his immediate release and criticising the arrest as politically motivated.Charges tied to University of Wolverhampton visitThe allegations focus on a trip to the University of Wolverhampton, where Wickremesinghe and his wife, Professor Maithree Wickramasinghe, were invited for a special graduation luncheon celebrating her honorary professorship.The visit followed Wickremesinghe’s attendance at the G-77 summit in Cuba and the United Nations General Assembly in New York. A letter dated 1 September 2023, from the University of Wolverhampton’s vice-chancellor, Professor John Raftery, invited the couple to the event, stating it was on behalf of the university’s chancellor, the late Rt Hon Lord Paul of Marylebone, PC.Sri Lanka’s Criminal Investigation Department (CID) alleges that government funds were spent on his travel to the United Kingdom, which it has classified as a private trip, and that the state also bore the costs of his security detail.Investigators claim the tour incurred more than LKR16.6 million (approximately US$55,000) in expenses for transport, accommodation, and related arrangements for his entourage, including security personnel.The CID argues that charging these expenses to the state amounted to an abuse of presidential powers for personal benefit and a violation of Sri Lanka’s anti-corruption and public resource management laws.CID claims the letter was not submitted during months of investigation or mentioned by any of the 38 witnesses questioned, including Wickremesinghe’s private secretary.The CID alleges the document may have been fabricated to weaken the case, prompting the court to order an investigation into its authenticity. The Attorney General’s Department questioned the value of the visit for Sri Lanka, stressing that such an extravagant cost was unjustifiable given the nation’s ongoing economic difficulties.Wickremesinghe’s legal team argues the trip was official, not private, as it was undertaken in his capacity as president. Wickremesinghe has maintained that his wife covered her own travel costs and insisted that no state funds were used for the visit.The 76-year-old, widely regarded for his role in guiding Sri Lanka through its worst economic crisis and securing international support during a period of national collapse, is now at the centre of a landmark legal case over these allegations.His arrest has sparked widespread anger and sympathy, with political supporters, neutral voters, and ordinary citizens expressing outrage on social media and taking to the streets to show their displeasure with the government’s handling of the case.President Anura Kumara Dissanayake stressed his government’s commitment to upholding equality before the law, vowing to hold all individuals accountable for crimes, corruption, and abuse of power regardless of their status.The arrest has also divided public opinion, with student activist Wasantha Mudalige, a prominent leader of the 2022 ‘Aragalaya’ protests, welcoming the move and urging authorities to pursue further investigations into other allegations against Wickremesinghe, recalling his own 167-daydetentionunder the Prevention of Terrorism Act during Wickremesinghe’s tenure.Health concerns and public outcryFollowing his arrest, Wickremesinghe was taken to the prison hospital and later transferred to the Colombo National Hospital’s Intensive Care Unit due to severe dehydration, high blood pressure, and elevated blood sugar levels.Political leaders from Sri Lanka’s main opposition, Samagi Jana Balawegaya (SJB), Wickremesinghe’s own party, the United National Party (UNP), and other parties have condemned the arrest, with former President Mahinda Rajapaksa expressing regret over the imprisonment of leaders for “minor offences.”Opposition Leader Sajith Premadasa, addressing the media in Colombo on 25 August, stated the arrest of Ranil Wickremesinghe is not an isolated incident but a serious challenge to democratic politics, emphasising the need for all opposition parties to unite.Meanwhile, Wasantha Mudalige, former leader of the Inter-University Students’ Federation (IUSF), Sri Lanka’s largest and most influential student union, which played a leading role in the “Aragalaya” protest movement, described Wickremesinghe’s arrest as a positive step by the government and called for his continued remand over what he claimed were other unresolved allegations.“The arrest of Ranil Wickremesinghe is good and positive from the government. We urge authorities to pursue other reports against him, including the Batalanda Commission findings, the Central Bank bond scandal, and the Easter Sunday attacks, and ensure he remains in custody,” Mudalige said, addressing a rally in Kandy on 24 August, recalling that Wickremesinghe’s administration haddetainedhim for 167 days under the country’s controversial Prevention of Terrorism Act (PTA), accusing him of fuelling violence during the 2022 protest movement.International calls for releaseThe arrest of Ranil Wickremesinghe has drawn widespread international attention and commentary across social media platforms, includingX. Former Norwegian peace envoy Erik Solheim described the charges as “trivial” and urged Sri Lankan authorities to focus on “real issues”, noting that such allegations would not be considered criminal in Europe.Indian MP Shashi Tharoor also weighed in onX, calling for Wickremesinghe to be treated with “respect and dignity” in recognition of his decades of public service.Maldives’ former president Mohamed Nasheed used the platform to express hope for the former president’s swift release, highlighting Wickremesinghe’s role as a catalyst for democratic reform in the region.Wickremesinghe’s legacy and Sri Lanka’s political contextWickremesinghe is widely credited with steering Sri Lanka through its worsteconomic crisissince independence in 1948, securing a US$2.9 billion IMF bailout in 2023.The country faced severe economic turmoil with negative growth rates of -7.4% in 2022 and -3.8% in 2023, according to the Central Bank of Sri Lanka. His presidency followed a period of unrest marked by the resignation of President Gotabaya Rajapaksa in July 2022, driven by massive student-led protests.Wickremesinghe’s arrest now comes amid anotherhistoric political shift, with a Marxist-leaning party winning the presidency in September 2024, fuelled by young voters and academics demanding systemic reform.This is not the first time Wickremesinghe has navigated a crisis: in 2001, Sri Lanka’s economy experienced its first post-independence recession with a real GDP contraction of 1.4%, and after being sworn in as prime minister that same year, he introduced economic reforms and policies that restored growth in 2002, putting the economy back on track.Future and political motivesThe case has sparked a broader public debate over the ill-defined line between a president’s official duties and private life, raising questions over the use of state funds and resources. His arrest has fuelled discussions not only about the validity and potential political motivations behind the charges but also about emerging political realignments.Sri Lanka’s main opposition party, the Samagi Jana Balawegaya (SJB), and Wickremesinghe’s United National Party (UNP), once a single party before the SJB broke away, are reportedly moving closer, with supporters hoping that fragmented political forces could unite over shared grievances, potentially reshaping future coalition dynamics.Meanwhile, President Anura Kumara Dissanayake, addressing an event in Colombo on 26 August, has reiterated his administration’s commitment to strengthening accountability and ensuring equal treatment under the law.“If anyone in our country has engaged in crime, corruption, abuse of power, or misuse of public funds, every effort will be made to bring them to justice,” Dissanayake said, emphasising that the government’s actions were not politically motivated but aimed at restoring public trust in law and governance.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & 斯里蘭卡前總統被捕，涉嫌濫用國家資金 2025年8月22日，斯里蘭卡前總統及六屆總理拉尼爾·維克勒馬辛哈因涉嫌在擔任總統期間濫用公款而被捕。指控與他2023年9月前往英國沃爾弗漢普頓大學的私人行程有關，該行程花費超過55,000美元，並被視為浪費公帑。維克勒馬辛哈的辯護團隊則主張該行程為官方訪問，並否認使用國家資金。此事件引發了廣泛的社會反響，支持者和反對派均對案件的政治動機和法律正當性表達了不同看法。政府承諾將對所有腐敗行為追究責任，強調法律面前人人平等。 
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Supreme Court greenlights US$783m in health research cuts
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Supreme Court greenlights US$783m in health research cutsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsSupreme Court Associate Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATES‘Obscene’ executive order seen as ‘full-on assault on HE’UNITED STATESState legislators have cast a ‘web of control’ over HEUNITED STATESJustice Department ramps up pressure against ‘unlawful’ DEIUNITED STATESTrump proposes 90% cut to funding of tribal collegesUNITED STATESTrump tells Congress to cut basic science research by 34%UNITED STATESTrump’s ‘Big Bill’ to cost students, universities billionsUNITED STATESTrump administration asks commission to decertify HarvardUNITED STATESAll hopes turn to Congress to soften Trump’s assault on HEGLOBALTrump’s travel ban affects students from 19 countriesUNITED STATESTrump’s bill cuts support for low-income students in HEUNITED STATESTrump’s ongoing attacks on HE ‘reverberate across borders’UNITED STATESTrump administration cuts extra US$450 million to HarvardUNITED STATESHarvard stands firm against Trump’s ‘retaliatory’ threatsSOUTH AFRICA-GLOBALAcademy of Science warns of ‘extreme crisis’ under TrumpUNITED STATESTrump orders HE accreditation overhaul to stamp out DEIFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision -
