Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATESSupreme Court greenlights US$783m in health research cutsNathan M Greenfield26 August 2025TweetAssociate Justice Ketanji Brown Jackson’s swingeing dissent in the case in which the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) greenlit the termination of more than three-quarters of a billion US dollars in grants to medical research on 21 August had court watchers reaching for their computers or smartphones to determine the judicial meaning of ‘Calvinball’. The term comes from the comic strip Calvin and Hobbes, syndicated across the US between 1985 and 1995.“Calvinball has only one rule: there are no fixed rules,”wrote Jackson. It was invented by Calvin and his tiger Hobbes, who to everyone else is a stuffed animal but to Calvin is his living friend, to denote a game play ontology in which play cannot occur more than once because the rules and goals of a game are in constant flux.Jackson, the court’s newest justice, broke from the century-old tradition of deference to her senior colleagues, some of whom have been on the SCOTUS for decades, by writing that by agreeing that the National Institutes of Health (NIH) can terminate the grants for research which mostly takes place in America’s top research universities, the six Republican-appointed members of the nine-member SCOTUS have created “Calvinball jurisprudence with a twist”.“We seem to have two [rules]: that one (that is, ‘there are no fixed rules’), and [that] this administration always wins,” wrote Jackson, who was appointed to the SCOTUS by former president Joe Biden in 2022. Court watchers did not need Jackson to add that among the Republican majority are three judges appointed by President Donald J Trump during his first term.A grievous attack on science and medicine“We are very disappointed by the Supreme Court’s ruling that our challenge to the sweeping termination of hundreds of critical biomedical research grants likely belongs in the Court of Federal Claims (CFC),” said a joint statement by the American Civil Liberties Union (ACLU) of Massachusetts and the American Public Health Association (APHA), one of the four organisations that joined the ACLU after it successfully obtained an injunction last April stopping Secretary of Health, Robert F Kennedy Jr (RFK Jr) and the NIH from proceeding with the cuts.“This decision is a significant setback for public health. We are assessing our options but will work diligently to ensure that these unlawfully terminated grants continue to be restored,”added the statement.The Association of American Medical Colleges also useduncharacteristically strong languagein its response to the decision.“Make no mistake: This was a decision critical to the future of the nation, and the Supreme Court made the wrong choice. History will look upon these mass National Institutes of Health research grant terminations with shame. The Court has turned a blind eye to this grievous attack on science and medicine, and we call upon Congress to take action to restore the rule of law at NIH.”A country in limboThat by a 5-4 vote the SCOTUS also said that the Federal District Court in Massachusetts, where the case was originally filed, has the power to review the reasoning behind the NIH cuts is cold comfort – even the ACLU’s and APHA’s statement says that because of this the NIH does not have the power to terminate the grants.What Jackson called the court’s “bizarre claim-splitting regime” amounts to an order in which “plaintiffs like these – federal grantees who believe their grants were terminated pursuant to an unlawful policy” – are left with no response to the question of how they get complete relief. “The answer, it seems, is they cannot,” it states.Wesley Whistle, project director, student success and affordability with the Washington, DC-based progressive NGO New America, and an expert in America’s post-secondary system, said in an e-mail toUniversity World Newsthat “yesterday’s Supreme Court decision is deeply troubling”.“By allowing the NIH cuts to take effect for now, the court has opened the door to a policy that jeopardises life-saving medical research, slows down innovation, and undermines the fight against diseases from cancer to Alzheimer’s”.According to Whistle: “These cuts would harm patients and communities across the country while setting back America’s global leadership in science and public health.“In an odd twist, however, the court also allowed the case challenging those cuts to move forward.“That leaves the country in limbo: research funding hangs in the balance while scientists, patients, and communities face uncertainty about whether critical projects will continue.”The end of the road?A few days after the SCOTUS’ decisions were handed down, the situation is anomalous, explained Scott Delaney, a research scientist in environmental health at Harvard University’s TH Chan School of Public Health, who was a practising lawyer for 10 years.On the one hand, the SCOTUS said that the Federal District Court judge did not have the authority to stay the cuts because the case should have been brought in the CFC. The SCOTUS’ stay on the injunction did not order that the cuts be reinstated.On the other hand, the SCOTUS said that the District judge can go ahead and hear arguments on the real merits of the case: whether NIH’s procedures and internal directives are constitutional.As of early Monday 25 August, the grants are still active – and, according to Delaney, NIH “can’t use those policies to terminate the grants”, which means that NIH and the lawyers in its parent department, Health and Human Services (HHS), need different reasons to terminate the grants.However, Delaney said: “There may be a world where, in a week or two or three, the grants are still active, but I just don’t see it happening. There’s nothing about this administration, nothing about HHS, nothing about RFK Jr or NIH director Jay Bhattacharya that makes me think that they’re just going to change their mind all of a sudden and continue funding these grants. I just don’t see any reasonably likely scenario where these grants come back; I don’t see any viable way that the plaintiffs will succeed.”Speaking as a researcher, Delaney toldUniversity World Newsthat the plaintiffs (ACLU and APHA) could “refile in the Court of Federal Claims. But two years later, or a year later, whenever you finally win that claim, your research is gone. You can’t just pause it and then restart it. Two years later, you lose study participants, you lose staff, you lose cell cultures. You have to start the whole thing over again. Research just doesn’t work that way.”BackgroundFor the majority of the justices, the case, whichUniversity Wolrd Newshas previously covered, originally filed in February, turned largely on whether the plaintiffs were suing in the proper venue.Both the District and First Circuit Appeals Court (Massachusetts) agreed that the case was properly filed in the Federal District Court – and not the lesser-knownCourt of Federal Claims (CFC), which, according to its website, “has jurisdiction over claims seeking money judgements against the United States,” in, for example, tax or breach of contract cases.By contrast, the majority of the SCOTUS justices concurred with Associate Justice Neil Gorsuch, one of Trump’s appointees, who quoted from the SCOTUS decision intheDepartment of Education v. Californiahanded down last April.In that case the six Republican-appointed justices sided with the Trump administration by “stay[ing] a temporary restraining order issued by a federal district court, which enjoined the Government from terminating various education-related grants” ordered by Secretary of Education Linda McMahon as part ofher mission to dismantle the Department of Education.“California,”Gorsuch wrote, “explained that ‘suits based on ‘any express or implied contract with the United States’ do not belong in the district court under the Administrative Procedures Act (APA) but under the Court of Federal Claims under the Tucker Act (Tucker)”, which dates to 1887.“What the SCOTUS majority has said is that the scientists in national institutes and universities should have sued for breach of contract – and the place to do that is the CFC, as per the Tucker Act.As Associate Justice Amy Coney Barrett put it: “Suits against the United States are ‘available by grace and not by right,’ and the relief available is subject to the conditions Congress sets” and Congress did not intend for the APA to supersede the Tucker Act.”Jackson is unsparing in her critique of California, starting with the fact that it is a four-paragraph ruling made as part of the SCOTUS’s “Emergency Docket” and that it was “issued without full briefing or any oral argument”.Now, she writes, it appears to “govern all APA challenges to grant-funding determinations” and is put forward “to partially sustain the Government’s abrupt cancellation of hundreds of millions of dollars allocated to support life-saving biomedical research”.The facts on the groundJackson’s emphasis on biomedical research is important because none of the majority decisions even acknowledged the facts on the ground. Referring to the District Court’s decision to enjoin Kennedy’s termination of the grants, Jackson used the particulars of the case to distinguish it from California and, thus, provide a way out of the pigeonhole that case created.In California, there was a rough balance of equities between the DoE and the government of California, but in this case brought against the NIH cuts, the “plaintiffs stood to lose more and the government less: … These researchers lack the financial wherewithal to keep their programmes running on their own – meaning a stay would euthanise animal subjects, terminate life-saving trials and close community health clinics. And unlike in California, there is no fast-expiring temporary restraining order incentivising a rushed drawdown of granted funds”, she said.Since the researchers’ relationship with the NIH was not a classical contract, Jackson concluded, their case did not belong in the CFC but, rather, in Federal District Court.In the light of Jackson’s dissent, the majority opinions that refer to Trump’s executive orders banning diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) practices in the government or programmes to foster gender identity or study COVID-19 and vaccine hesitancy appear wilfully divorced from congressional intent.This is especially true given that the present Congress has not passed any legislation retracting previous legislation authorising these programmes, such as the one that, Jackson notes, requires the National Cancer Institute (which is part of the NIH) to fund programmes “designed to assist populations, low-income populations, or minority groups”.Barrett, for example, blithely listed the numbers of three executive orders that led the NIH to work to “align its funding with changing policy priorities” and the existence of new “internal guidance documents describing those priorities”.She then writes: “Going forward, the agency will not fund research related to DEI objectives, gender identity, or COVID–19. Nor will it continue the practice of awarding grants to researchers based on race”, notwithstanding the fact that a law, which required being passed by Congress and signed by the president, outweighs an executive order – and the law on the books requires that, for example, the NIH “ensurethat women and members of minority groups are represented in the clinical research it supports”.Associate Justice Brent Kavanagh, another Trump appointee, was dismissive of the lower court that found merit in the APHA’s argument that the NIH never defined the term DEI.“But, there is no APA rule that agencies define every term in every internal guidance document, particularly when that guidance steers highly discretionary decisions over how to allocate limited agency resources,” he stated.Kavanagh’s Olympian detachment, given the congressional direction that the NIH-based National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities “make awards . . . for the purpose of . . . supporting programmes of excellence in biomedical and behavioural research training for individuals who are members of minority health disparity populations.”Yet, as Jackson points out, the District Court didn’t just note that the NIH failed to define DEI; it found that the lack of a definition left “individual agency employees ‘to arrive at whatever conclusion [they] wished’,” a situation totally at odds with both “rational-legal government” as defined in Economy and Society by the sociologist Max Weber as well as the NIH’s own history: between 2012 and 2025, there were six midstream grant terminations, notes Jackson. (Using figures from 2013 and 2017 and2020 to 2025, when the NIH made a total of 784,000 grants, the rate of midstream terminations amounts to 0.00077% of the total number of grants made by the NIH.)“That definitional void left them applying ‘circular and nonsensical boilerplate language to cancel grants without explanation or reason and in a manner that had ‘absolutely nothing to do with the promotion of science or research’,” writes Jackson.Neither Barrett nor Kavanagh deigned to acknowledge the factual findings of the District Court – nor did they dispute them.“In place of science, meanwhile, came something more pernicious,” says Jackson. “The court found, as a factual matter, ‘an unmistakable pattern of discrimination against women’s health issues’ and ‘pervasive racial discrimination’ – indeed, ‘palpable’ racial discrimination of a sort the judge had ‘never seen’ in 40 years on the bench.”These facts led, the District Judge determined, and Jackson summarised, to “a policy of mass grant terminations that was ‘breathtakingly arbitrary and capricious’... that “the District Court declared unlawful and vacated the challenged directives” that undergirded the termination of the grants.No justice siding with the 5-4 majority disputed what either the district judge or Jackson said about the substantive question about the NIH’s directives or the findings about racism and other bias and, most importantly, Jackson’s references to the letter of the law.According to Delaney, the majority of the justices “didn’t engage or care to engage with congressional requirements and their interaction with executive policy. Or what the NIH ultimately did in terminating these grants and discriminating on the basis of race and sex”. And this might be the case “because they have a very expansive view of executive power”.In layman’s terms, the SCOTUS appears to believe that executive orders trump congressional mandates.Progress reversedJackson concludes her dissent by weighing the “harm” each party could suffer in different legal scenarios.Were the terminations to be stayed pending review by the lower court of the NIH’s procedures and were that court to then find that the procedures passed constitutional muster, she argues, the federal government would have lost a number of “incremental” payments.The SCOTUS, by contrast, “concludes that the Government faces irreparable harm simply because the plaintiffs do not pinky-promise to reimburse the Government if the Government ultimately prevails” in court.According to Jackson, the “harm that the plaintiffs and the public will suffer from [approving] a stay plainly dwarfs the purportedly irreparable injury to the Government if a stay is denied. For the government, the incremental expenditure of money is at stake.For the plaintiffs and the public, scientific progress itself hangs in the balance – along with the lives that progress saves,” writes Jackson, who went on to paint a dystopian picture of medical science under the SCOTUS decision inNational Institutes of Health, et al v American Public Health Association et al.“Make no mistake: Per the evidence in front of the District Court, the forward march of scientific discovery will not only be halted – it will be reversed. Because studies and researchers cannot be held in stasis, there is no way to recover the lost time, research continuity, or training value once disrupted. Thus, years-long studies will lose validity. Animal subjects will be euthanised.Life-saving medication trials will be abandoned. Countless researchers will lose their jobs. And community health clinics (providing, inter alia, preventative treatment for infectious diseases) will close.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

美國最高法院批准削減7.83億美元醫學研究資助 近日，美國最高法院（SCOTUS）以5-4的投票結果，批准了國立衛生研究院（NIH）終止醫學研究資助的決定，此舉將對生命科學領域造成重大影響。新任法官凱坦吉·布朗·傑克森（Ketanji Brown Jackson）在反對意見中指出，這一裁決是對科學和公共健康的重大打擊，可能會延誤生命拯救的研究並損害美國在全球科學領域的領導地位。美國公民自由聯盟（ACLU）和美國公共衛生協會（APHA）等組織對此表示失望，並將尋求法律途徑以恢復被終止的資助。此事件引發了對美國研究資助未來的廣泛擔憂，科學界面臨不確定性。  
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Spain’s political class faces a credentials crisis
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Spain’s political class faces a credentials crisisGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALThe academy in peril: The university at a moral crossroadsSPAINUniversity probes ‘irregular’ professorial appointmentSPAINSpanish universities to review ties with Israeli universitiesSPAINBologna and bust lead to a boom in doctoratesFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackSPAINSpain’s political class faces a credentials crisisPaul Rigg19 August 2025TweetWhat began as a single admission of academic misrepresentation has cascaded into a political reckoning in Spain.In recent weeks, a string of Spanish politicians from across the ideological spectrum have been forced to revise – or retract entirely – their academic qualifications. While the scandals have shocked the public, some scholars argue that the issue reflects deeper structural problems in Spain’s political culture, educational system, and labour market.At the heart of the current controversy is Noelia Núñez, a prominent figure in the conservative Partido Popular (PP) and a rising star in national politics. In July, Núñez admitted that she never completed her double degree in law and public administration, despite having claimed so in official biographies.Her resignation, swift and unusually contrite by Spanish political standards, triggered a domino effect. “I apologise to anyone who feels disappointed, but I also believe it’s not enough to say sorry,” she said.Since Núñez’s departure, at least half a dozen other politicians have followed suit, either correcting their official résumés or stepping down entirely.Among them:• Ana Millán, a senior official in Madrid’s PP-led regional government, downgraded her supposed degree in political science to a diploma in public administration.• Ignacio Higuera, a regional official from the far-right Vox party in Extremadura, resigned after it emerged that the university he claimed to attend did not even offer the marketing degree he listed.• José María Ángel, a Socialist Party official who oversaw disaster recovery in Valencia, stepped down amid allegations he had faked a university degree to secure a civil service post.• Juan Manuel Moreno, PP’s Andalusian regional president, has been asked to explain apparent changes in his business management qualification on his CV in 2000, 2004 and 2008.• In Valencia, Pilar Bernabé, the government’s delegate and Socialist Party representative, had to revise her biography to reflect that she never finished degrees in Spanish linguistics and audiovisual communication.• Pedro Rollán, Senate president and PP member, removed a diploma and master’s in marketing from his CV after investigative journalists revealed the institution in question never issued such credentials.A cultural and systemic phenomenonObservers have noted that this sudden wave of academic truth-telling is more than just a passing moment. It reveals a longstanding and widespread issue in Spain – one deeply rooted in the nation’s political system, labour market, and cultural attitudes toward formal education.‘Qualification inflation’ – the tendency to exaggerate academic achievements on résumés – is not uniquely Spanish. But Spain stands out in Europe for the extent and nature of the phenomenon.The country is in a curious situation regarding academic qualifications because while many inflate their credentials, a sizeable number are in posts for which they are overqualified.Data from Eurostat and Cedefop show that over 36% of university-educated Spaniards are employed in jobs that do not require a tertiary degree, compared to the EU average of around 22%. For foreign workers in Spain, the number rises to over 50%.‘La Picaresca’ and the political classRegarding qualification inflation, cultural factors also play a role. Many Spaniards recognise the concept of ‘la picaresca’, a term rooted in 16th-century Spanish literature that denotes cunning, rule-bending behaviour designed to survive an unfair system. Thepícaro, or trickster, is a beloved antihero in Spanish culture – not because they break the rules, but because they outsmart them.While ‘la picaresca’ is often invoked with irony or humour, scholars argue it influences real-world behaviour, particularly in contexts of bureaucracy, scarcity, or rigid institutions.“Fully agree,” says Ignacio Sánchez-Cuenca, professor of political science at Carlos III University in Madrid, when asked about the cultural influence of picaresque thinking.Sánchez-Cuenca goes on to suggest that the root of the problem lies not in the lack of formal education per se, but in the career path of many Spanish politicians, particularly younger or mid-career figures who enter party structures at an early age and never leave.“The issue is not whether politicians should have higher education – no one questions that someone from any background can win a seat in parliament. The real problem is politicians who have no profession at all, who have spent their whole lives within a party, and then feel compelled to claim strong academic credentials to compensate for their lack of professional experience.”He adds: “In Spain, this is becoming increasingly common. These are the people most likely to fake their CVs.”A broader Southern European patternSpain’s crisis of credentials is mirrored, though not perfectly replicated, in neighbouring southern European countries. Italy, Greece, and Portugal also struggle with youth unemployment, overqualification, and rigid public-sector hiring systems.In contrast, northern European countries such as Germany, Sweden, or Denmark put a stronger emphasis on vocational training and practical experience, making the social pressures around academic degrees less acute.In France, another country where academic pedigree holds immense weight, “prestige signalling” via elite institutions like the grandes écoles plays a similar role – though the inflation tends to focus more on where someone studied rather than what they claim to have studied.The UK and Ireland, by contrast, exhibit more skills-based CV embellishment, with candidates more likely to exaggerate experience or soft skills than to fabricate formal qualifications.Accountability?For some observers, the sudden spate of resignations and corrections in Spain suggests a welcome turn toward accountability.However, while many have welcomed the recent resignations as a sign of accountability, others pointed to a deeper concern: none of the politicians in question have faced legal consequences. And critics argue that the underlying incentives – weak enforcement, public tolerance, and the institutional fetishisation of credentials – remain intact.As Spain continues to grapple with high youth unemployment and labour market mismatch, it is unlikely that the pressure to overstate academic achievements will disappear.Until political structures value professional experience, vocational pathways, and transparency as much as formal titles, the culture of CV inflation may persist – however many resignations are forced.In the words of Sánchez-Cuenca: “Education becomes a way to compensate for the absence of a real profession. That’s the true scandal – the lack of careers.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Pakistani student mobility redefining campus diversity
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Pakistani student mobility redefining campus diversityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksPAKISTANSeparatist security threat shutters Balochistan universitiesPAKISTAN-PALESTINEPakistan offers places to 100 medical students from GazaKYRGYZSTANThousands flee as foreign students come under mob attackPAKISTANRise in number of scholarships for Afghans raises questionsPAKISTANPlans to attract foreign campuses in new ‘knowledge city’Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPAKISTANPakistani student mobility redefining campus diversityAmeen Amjad Khan18 August 2025TweetThe number of international students from some countries previously considered a major source of international students is no longer growing at the same pace in recent years. With the number of students from these countries either stabilising or declining, educational institutions are increasingly looking to new populations to diversify their campuses.According to a report by ApplyBoard, a Canadian education technology company providing a platform for international student mobility, Pakistan is emerging as a significant source of international students who have the potential to influence global student flows over the next decade.A significant reason for this surge is Pakistan’s youthful population, along with limited domestic opportunities and a stabilising economy.The report,How Pakistani Students Will Shape the Next Wave of Global Student Mobility, also notes that students from Pakistan are increasingly drawn to postgraduate and STEM programmes, with a focus on securing employment and career advancements.‘Big Four’ destinations increaseThe study finds that the United Kingdom is the preferred destination for Pakistani students, demonstrated by a significant rise in visa issuances. The United States is also experiencing a surge in F-1 visas, particularly for graduate-level STEM programmes. In contrast, new student numbers (from Pakistan) have dropped in Canada and Australia.The drop in the number of Pakistani students in Canada is attributed to government-imposed student caps, whereas a decrease in visa approvals caused a decline in the number of Pakistani students in Australia.However, Germany, at the same time, is gaining popularity as an alternative destination due to its affordable tuition and robust STEM programmes.For institutions aiming to diversify their enrolment, the study suggests that Pakistan represents a strategic priority. It recommends highlighting scholarships and financial support, showcasing top-tier STEM and postgraduate programmes, and engaging with local Pakistani communities to successfully attract these students.New student visas to the ‘Big Four’ destinations, which include the UK, US, Canada, and Australia, have increased fourfold since 2019. This surge in study abroad enrolments is mainly due to Pakistan’s youth population (under 24 years of age) having surged to 59% of its 241 million people.Other factors pushing more students to seek opportunities abroad are limited domestic opportunities, a growing economy and the perception of international education as a necessary competitive edge.Among all nations, the United Kingdom holds the leading position in attracting students from Pakistan. The UK issued more than 35,500 student visas to Pakistani citizens in 2024. This substantial increase, a 550% rise since 2019, has made Pakistan the third-largest contributor of new international students, up from 11th place.Postgraduate popularityPostgraduate studies are particularly popular, accounting for 83% of all new admissions. A growing number of these students are choosing computing and IT programmes, with enrolment nearly doubling from 2017 to 2018, a trend that mirrors the UK’s booming tech sector.Pakistani students have played a key role in supporting and stabilizing UK institutions at a time when student demand from India and China has softened.Across the Atlantic, the United States is also seeing a significant surge in interest. US F-1 visa issuances to Pakistani students are on the rise, with 4,500 visas issued in the year 2024.The current year of 2025 is having a similar trend to surpass this total by 30%, potentially reaching a new all-time high. Similar to the UK, there is a strong demand for graduate programmes, with nearly 5,000 Pakistani students pursuing master's or doctoral degrees in the US.The focus in the US is overwhelmingly on STEM (Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics) fields, with over 50% of students enrolling in engineering, life sciences, maths, or computer science programmes. This focus aligns with US workforce needs, as STEM job growth has outpaced all other sectors over the past 30 years.This growth story is not consistent across all fronts. In Canada, government-imposed limits on student numbers have slowed progress.After hitting a high of nearly 3,400 new study permits in 2023, approvals for Pakistani students fell by 33% in 2024, with further declines anticipated in 2025. Even so, Canada still represents a largely “untapped opportunity.”Pakistani students continue to show strong demand for both undergraduate and postgraduate studies, and the sizable Pakistani community of over 300,000 provides an established support network for newcomers. Institutions that engage with these communities and highlight the benefits of studying in Canada could help revive interest among this group.Australia faces an even tougher situation. After a sharp post-pandemic rise in 2023, the number of student visas issued to Pakistanis plunged by 65% in 2024 and is expected to decline further.This downturn isn’t mainly the result of government caps, since most Pakistani students choose university programmes rather than the more heavily affected VET (Vocational Education and Training) sector.Government restrictions or policy changes (such as student caps) have hit the VET sector harder than the university sector, and Pakistani students are mostly in universities, but the bigger issue for them is the visa approval rate.The visa approval rates dropped from 88% in 2019 to just 41% in 2024. Despite this, Pakistani students continue to show strong interest in pursuing quality education in Australia.Emerging playersBeyond the traditional English-speaking destinations, new players are also emerging. Germany, in particular, is becoming a preferred alternative for students from Pakistan. With low or no tuition fees at public universities and a strong reputation for STEM programmes, Germany is an attractive option.The number of Pakistani students in Germany has surpassed 10,000 in the 2023-24 winter semester, a 70% increase from 2018 to 2019. The support from the Pakistani expat community, which has doubled in the last decade, further strengthens this trend.The report projects that Pakistan is poised to become one of the top contributors to global student mobility. Its population is projected to reach 360 million by 2050, with citizens under 24 making up a significant portion. As the country’s economy continues to stabilise, more families will have the financial means to support overseas education.For institutions seeking to diversify their enrolments, Pakistan is not just an emerging market but a strategic priority. The report suggests that in order to successfully attract these students, institutions need to address key concerns, such as cost and career opportunities.Showcasing scholarships and financial support, highlighting high-quality STEM and postgraduate programmes, and demonstrating established career pathways will be crucial.The report recommends fostering stronger ties with local Pakistani communities and alumni, leveraging them as an influential word-of-mouth network to support student success.It notes that Pakistan’s young population, ongoing economic transformation, and strong educational ambitions are shaping a powerful new driver in international education. Institutions that recognise and respond to this trend will be well-placed to benefit in the years ahead.Vice-Chancellor of Lahore’s University of the Punjab, Professor Muhammad Ali, toldUniversity World News: “The findings of this report are both encouraging and insightful.”He said that when Pakistani students study at top institutions overseas, they return with fresh ideas, cross-cultural understanding, and entrepreneurial energy. These qualities (in foreign graduates who return to the country) can help shape a more dynamic and innovative Pakistan.Doctor Zafar Nawaz Jaspal, Dean of Social Sciences at Islamabad’s Quaid-e-Azam University, toldUniversity World News: “This is a timely and insightful study that captures the evolving aspirations of Pakistani students.”He said that as more of our young people access world-class universities abroad, they bring back not only advanced knowledge and skills but also global networks and perspectives that can fuel innovation, strengthen our institutions, and contribute meaningfully to Pakistan’s socio-economic development.He also opined that even if a portion of students from Pakistan prefer to work abroad, that will be a good contribution from our country towards international development.Data for this study (by Applyboard) comes from official national and international sources, including the UK Home Office, the United States Department of State, the Institute of International Education’s Open Doors Report (IIE), the German Academic Exchange Service (Deutscher Akademischer Austauschdienst – DAAD), the Federal Statistical Office of Germany (Statistisches Bundesamt), Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC), the Australian Department of Home Affairs (ADHA), the Higher Education Statistics Agency (HESA), Pakistan’s 7th Population and Housing Census, and the United Nations World Population Prospects (UN WPP). Figures reflect the latest available visa, enrolment, and demographic statistics for each country.Meti Basiri, co-Founder and CEO of ApplyBoard, toldUniversity World News: “For thisreport,we drew on a broad spectrum of data sources.“Our research combined both publicly available and privately sourced information, including official government statistics and data from partners working directly with institutions. Every data point was carefully gathered and then analysed manually to ensure accuracy and depth of insight.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: All academics should be scientific sleuths, says scholar
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: All academics should be scientific sleuths, says scholarGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyGLOBALAll academics should be scientific sleuths, says scholarEngela Duvenage03 October 2025TweetBringing academic fraud to light should be part and parcel of an academic’s job, not an after-hours hobby of sorts, and universities should give their staff the necessary scope and support to strengthen scientific integrity and transparency, according to Lonni Besançon, an assistant professor of data visualisation at Linköping University in Sweden.“Finding fraud in research papers is something I do on the side. Looking for such papers, criticising and reviewing them should be part of a scientist’ mandate in general,” he said at the 12th Heidelberg Laureate Forum (HLF) for young researchers in mathematics and computer science in Heidelberg, Germany.Besançon was part of a panel discussing the importance of upholding scientific integrity and transparency in research and academic publishing in an era of increased paper mills, falsified data and AI-produced journal articles and images.A panel discussion on integrity in the mathematical sciences closed off formal proceedings at the recent 12th Heidelberg Laureate Forum, which brought together 200 young researchers in mathematics and computer science from around the world. Credit: Bernhard Kreutzer/Heidelberg Laureate Forum“Scientific sleuthing is hard to do, with a lot of risk and close to zero credit,” acknowledged Besançon, who became interested in identifying questionable, unethical or downright fraudulent research practices during the COVID-19 pandemic and has since been called a “dogged critic” by the publicationScience.He said that most so-called scientific sleuthing is done by young researchers but noted that there is a growing and supportive community ready to help and guide efforts.Some culprits are caught out by the nonsensical ‘tortured phrases’ they or AI tools use when rewriting existing copy while attempting to cover up plagiarism.Examples include ‘machine learning’ being rewritten as ‘machine studying’ or ‘natural language processing’ becoming ‘herbal language processing’. Some people simply flip images around, while others paste ChatGPT’s full generated response into papers.“Honestly, the scary part is that cheaters are generally lazy or not very smart, or maybe both,” he noted.Real risksHis description of an illustration of a rat’s anatomy being “in all the wrong places” was met with laughter from the 200-strong audience of young researchers from across the world. Besançon, however, pointed to the risks associated with making real-life decisions based on fake scientific data.“The worst [are] papers with a massive impact, with completely fabricated data. It happens a lot in medical research. Recently, there was a massive scandal around Alzheimer’s research. It’s terrible, because people's lives will be impacted, all for the sake of someone's career or ego,” he noted.At HLF, Besançon’s fellow panellists were Dr Eunsang Lee of Nature Springer’s Research Integrity Group and Professor Yukari Ito of the Kalvi Institute for Physics and Mathematics of the University of Tokyo. Together, they highlighted initiatives such asPubPeer.com,Retraction Watch, theBullied into Bad Sciencecampaign andProblematic Paper Screeneras sources for sleuthing and support.Springer Nature(academic publishers) provides free online training material as “a first line of defence” on the best practices for authors and reviewers. According to Ito, researchers at her university must complete an e-learning course on research integrity before they may apply for grants.Professor Yukari Ito of the Kalvi Institute for the Physics and Mathematics of the University of Tokyo, during a panel discussion on integrity in the mathematical sciences at the recent Heidelberg Laureate Forum. Credit: Bernhard Kreutzer/Heidelberg Laureate ForumShe believes scientific ethics are under pressure because academics increasingly feel compelled to publish and be cited, and because they constantly measure their productivity against others on online platforms such as the pre-print repositoryarXiv.org.Academic fraud in mathematicsPanel moderator and science journalist Ben Skuse noted that the panel discussion was timeous, given apress releaseearlier in the week by the German Mathematical Society (DMV) and the International Mathematical Union (IMU) about the systematic rise in fraudulent practices within mathematics. It noted how “megajournals, which print anything as long as the authors pay for it, now publish more articles per year than all reputable mathematics journals (which do not require payment) combined”.British science journalist Ben Skuse (left) was moderator of a panel on integrity in the mathematical sciences at the recent Heidelberg Laureate Forum. Credit: Bernhard Kreutzer/Heidelberg Laureate ForumThe release follows a new internationalreportpublished onarXiv.organd inNotices of the American Mathematical Society.Skuse highlighted how cartels of scientists “band together to cite one another’s work or boost a university's ranking in a particular subject”. In one case, a Taiwanese university without a maths department was ranked first in the discipline by a global university ranking.“These days, to get a good sense of the quality of researchers’ work, you must seriously look at the content of all their papers, beyond the activity and number of papers produced or cited. But that is hard to do,” Ito admitted.Lee added that even in a so-called ‘slow science’ such as mathematics, the scale and rate of papers being produced make thorough data validation and in-depth analysis increasingly difficult. Reputable publishers “understand the full responsibility of keeping the research system and the peer review process safe”.The former computational chemist analyses data to detect fraud in manuscript submissions and helps develop screening tools. He admits that he “had no clue” about the widespread nature of scientific fraud until he joined academic publishing some two years ago. Since then, the research integrity group he is part of atSpringer Naturehas doubled to around 50 researchers and technical assistants.AI useWhile plagiarism and other forms of fraud are the ‘old, obvious risks’ in academia, new ones have emerged thanks to AI. Such tools are in turn used to pick up on tortured phrases, nonsensical sentences, AI-generated images, irrelevant reference decisions or to compare the relevance of abstracts against a paper’s contents.“According to the guidelines of the Committee on Publication Ethics, or COPE [a worldwide organisation on publication ethics], you may use AI to help you do your research or write a paper, but it cannot be named as an author. You must declare and be fully transparent about using AI in terms of how and which tools you used. If possible, share the prompts you used,” Lee noted.COPE also advises how authors can ensure full transparency, handle authorship changes and citations and declare conflicts of interest, among other things.“In terms of best practices, transparency is key. When in doubt, share or explain why you cannot [be transparent]. Be transparent about everything, as much as you can. It can't hurt you,” Besançon advised.Lee urged authors not to fear corrections and retractions that result from honest mistakes: “Responsible post-publication action by authors constructively helps to achieve higher research integrity standards.”To this, Besançon added: “We should correct mistakes in papers because we are creating a body of knowledge together that the world is supposed to use.”AddendumThe recent internationalreportonarXiVcites the following examples of academic activity happening in ‘the zone of fraudulent behaviour’:• Citation sales where a professional citation broker (with an anonymous email address) provides a list of papers needing citations, in return offering a so-called ‘thank you fee’.• Paper mills that buy papers from professional ghostwriters to publish under someone else’s name.• Authorship sales that increase the revenue of paper mills or reduce the cost to the person buying a paper by selling authorship on a ready-to-publish article.• Blackmailing by paper mills that threaten to reveal the scientific misconduct of authors to journals, employers, or grant agencies.• Abuse of power and bullying of ‘non-compliant’ researchers, most often by scientific seniors.• Identity fraud when predatory journals include researchers as editors without consent.• Plagiarising articles and publishing them with the wrong date to make them look like an original publication.• Using pseudonyms for publications to avoid being connected to previous scientific misconduct.• Non-disclosure of serious conflicts of interest.• Creating email accounts similar to those of established researchers and suggesting them as reviewers of an article, hence being able to review one’s own work.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Amid furore over admissions, Bangladesh students speak up

Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsDENMARKAmid furore over admissions, Bangladesh students speak upJan Petter Myklebust03 October 2025TweetThe Bangladeshi student community in Denmark has called for Danish authorities and universities to review the involvement of “intermediary agencies” in student admissions amid a deepening furore around the admission of Bangladeshi students accused of using the Danish study residence visa as a backdoor into the labour market.In astatementto the parliamentary committee for education, the Bangladesh students say: “Such a measure should aim not only to address existing concerns but also to establish clear guidelines for external recruitment partners and regular audits as proactive safeguards to prevent future misuse.”The comments from Bangladeshi students come amid a highly politicised furore that has seen two universities – Roskilde University (RUC) and the Southern University of Denmark – freeze their 2026 intake of third-country students and announce other measures to address what the government sees as an overly lax approach to applications from third-country students, particularly from Bangladesh.At Roskilde, where the board chair resigned after public criticism on the issue from Prime Minister Mette Fredericksen, there has been outrage from staff over comments by acting board chair, Dea Forchhammer, who said she agreed with Prime Minister Mette Fredriksen that the university had “lost control of the intake of foreign students” and went on to say: “Roskilde University should primarily be for young people from Zealand and Denmark, not for Bangladesh or elsewhere. There can be no two ways about that.”Call for unconditional apologyIn anopen letter, Roskilde staff have called for Forchhammer and the board of directors to “urgently retract their statement and issue an unconditional apology to the university’s staff and students – in particular Muslim, Black and Brown students”.“In expressing priority of one student group over another, the Board further amplifies an already unwelcoming and unsafe atmosphere for students at the university, and particularly Muslim students. This directly impedes the core purpose of our university: to allow students to learn and thrive as critical, academic thinkers,” the letter states.The letter, which was published on 17 September and signed by over 430 staff and students, including students from other universities between the period 17 to 22 September, goes on to say that Forchammer’s comments are in “direct contradiction” to the university’s purpose and vision.“Roskilde University admits excellent international students to our bachelor and masters programmes every year. These students enrich the learning experience for all students at the university, and there is a real risk that the acting-chair’s statement will discourage such applications in the future.“As a result, Roskilde University will become a more insular and myopic place, less able to equip students with the competences they need to act as critical thinkers in today’s world.”‘Political frenzy’The letter describes the acting-chair’s comments as “a response to an Islamophobic political frenzy over the uptake of Bangladeshi students at RUC’s masters programmes” and accuses several Danish media outlets of crafting “a sensationalist story about a high uptake of foreign students, which was cynically picked up by Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen, used by her as part of a larger, deeply Islamophobic smear campaign against ‘Muslims in Denmark’”.This is despite RUC having followed “every law and rule regarding its admission of non-EU, self-financed students”, the open letter states.“We are shocked at how readily our management is willing to jump on the bandwagon of populist, anti-immigrant rhetoric, rather than standing by its values, legitimate decisions, and beliefs,” it says.One of the letter’s authors, Kerstin Bree Carlson, associate professor in the Department of Social Sciences and Business at Roskilde University, toldUniversity World Newsthe letter’s concerns centre on “the impact of political race-baiting in regard to RUC students and faculty” and, secondly, on “the question of how RUC defines itself as an institution as articulated by its representatives (the board of directors).“We note that neither Roskilde University nor the relevant Danish authorities have so far responded adequately to the concerns addressed in our letter,” Bree Carlson said.Internal frictionsWhile Roskilde took the brunt of the prime minister’s public criticisms, the University of Southern Denmark (SDU) has also taken action to address its own steep increase in the number of students from Bangladesh (37 students in 2020, rising to 241 in 2024), according to aPolitikenreport), by halting its winter admission for about 700 students from non-EU countries expected to start their studies in February 2026.Steffen Saxil, a Danish academic and educator specialising in project management and international business education, currently an associate professor at Niels Brock Copenhagen Business College where he teaches programmes offered in collaboration with De Montfort University, UK, toldUniversity World Newsthe current issues at Roskilde University and University of Southern Denmark were, in his opinion, “mainly a political manifestation of the ruling Social Democratic Party [attempting] to set the tone for the upcoming local elections and try[ing] to gain traction among voters for the right-wing parties”.“The universities have done nothing wrong but follow the rules and have even informed the ministry of higher education of their actions and yet have been called out by the prime minister.“It should be said that the Minister of Higher Education Christina Egelund (from the party the Moderates) appears to be in favour of more international students, but internal friction in the three-party government means that these initiatives are being overruled by the Social Democrats. How strange it is to think of the social democrats as a right-wing party, but there it is,” Saxil said.Student union criticismRune Boelsmand Bak, vice-chair of academic matters at the Danish National Union of Students (DSF), toldUniversity World News: “What we see is an increase in the severity of the discourse on international students in Denmark and on their possibility of studying here in the future.”He said that the union was “appalled” by the decision taken by the SDU to close its upcoming winter admissions to all third-country students.“In the Danish Students Union, we are appalled by this decision. No higher education institution should ever blacklist an ethnicity or a geographical area from enrolment. This move tears the merit-based education system fully apart.“Furthermore, to act within Danish law, the university has had to bar all third-country applicants. This means Ukrainian, American and Chinese students, as well as the alleged ‘problem students’ from Bangladesh,” he noted.He added: “This decision was made without any student involvement, and as my colleague Sebastian MB Larsen [chair of the student union at SDU],said toTV2: ‘The case has run for a while. In that time, students could have been involved. After all, it is about us and those we study with ... Those of us who come from abroad find it highly provoking’.”Bangladesh students suggest a remedyIn its statement to the Danish parliamentary committee, the Bangladesh student committee said around 60,000 students from the country are currently pursuing higher education abroad, with a significant number receiving fully funded study opportunities, and the country ranks among the top 10 sources of international students, “highlighting the youth’s commitment to educational excellence and international engagement”.The students said it was “essential to safeguard the integrity of Danish higher education by maintaining an admissions process for international students that weighs academic merit, preparedness, and alignment with the values of the Danish welfare state”.They noted: “We respectfully call on the respective Danish authorities and universities to review intermediary agencies’ involvement in student admissions.“Such a measure should aim not only to address existing concerns but also to establish clear guidelines for external recruitment partners and regular audits as proactive safeguards to prevent future misuse.“Education must remain a pathway for genuine academic pursuit accessible to all internationals, not a vehicle for immigration nor a commercial enterprise for institutions, agencies, or employers.“As these measures are considered, we emphasise the importance of affirming that the actions of a few do not unfairly affect the broader community of Bangladeshi citizens in Denmark, who are committed to academic integrity and respectful of Danish values.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand 丹麥的孟加拉學生社群呼籲當局檢討中介機構在學生招生中的角色，因為目前針對孟加拉學生的入學問題引發了激烈的爭議。孟加拉學生在向議會教育委員會的聲明中指出，應制定清晰的指導方針和定期審計，以防止未來的濫用行為。近期，兩所大學已暫停接收第三國學生，並因總理的批評而面臨內部不滿。學生們強調，教育應保持對所有國際學生的公平性，而不應成為移民工具或商業行為。

Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackDENMARKState clamps down on third-country student applicationsJan Petter Myklebust03 October 2025TweetAmid elevated political temperatures in Denmark, particularly around the issue of immigration, the government has announced a series of measures aimed at preventing third country international students from using their entry into the country to study as a back door into the labour market.The measures are a response to the recently publicised steep increase in the number of students from Bangladesh and Nepal entering the country’s universities, apparently without the requisite qualifications.At the centre of the controversy are two Danish universities: Roskilde (RUC) and Southern University of Denmark – both of which have taken radical action recently to address accusations that they have admitted too many apparently unqualified Bangladeshi students.According to government data, students from Bangladesh made up the largest group of international students who began a masters degree programme in Denmark in 2024, followed by students from Germany and Italy.Ajoint press releaseon 18 September from the Ministry of Higher Education and Research and the Danish Agency for International Recruitment and Integration (SIRI), an agency under the Danish Ministry of Immigration and Integration, announced it will be “significantly more difficult for citizens from third countries, such as Bangladesh and Nepal, to be admitted to Danish universities if they do not have the right academic qualifications”.According to the Danish government, students from third countries are those with citizenship in all countries other than EU member states, Iceland, Liechtenstein, Norway, and Switzerland.Among the new initiatives is a tightening up of the requirements for admission of students from third countries, the removal of the possibility of bringing family members, and a shortening of the period – from three years to one – during which those who have completed their studies can seek employment within the country.In addition, there will be tighter verification of foreign educational documents and the requirement of a larger upfront payment of the first tuition fee instalment, according to apress releaseof the Ministry of Immigration and Integration.The ministry also states that the government will give universities the opportunity to tighten academic entry requirements for masters programmes, “for example, through an entrance exam or targeted language test. In addition, control of cheating will be strengthened, and cheating will be sanctioned more severely”.Party politicsThree consultation meetings of the parliamentary education committee were held on 12, 16 and 24 of September. At the meeting of 16 September then-minister of immigration and integration (now, Minister of Labour) Kaare Dybvad Bek was present.The right-wing parties in parliament want Minister for Higher Education and Science Christina Egelund to leave her position because of her failure to act before 2025 on the issue of student numbers from Bangladesh and Nepal.They also want her to commit to sending out of Denmark those Bangladeshi students found to have falsified their papers, a call made by members of the Danish People's Party in the consultation meeting on 24 September.Egelund referred to a planned National Identification (ID) Centre that would be established to assist universities in their control of foreigners' educational documents, but she did not say that she wanted to take any further actions now.The current sentiment in government could put the handbrake on any future moves to expand the number of new international students permitted to study in Denmark.Earlier this year, asreported byUniversity World Newsas part of extensive DKK2 billion (US$306.6 million) reforms to professional and vocational degrees in Denmark supported by a majority in parliament in March, the Ministry of Education and Research approved the provision of 800 English-taught STEM study places in institutions outside of the bigger cities, targeting international students seeking to study and work in Denmark.Data now emerging from the parliamentary committee meetings, which the government said served as a foundation for the most recent policy changes, indicated that the average percentage of international masters students from 2022 to 2024 who brought a family member was 15% of the 16,205 international students. That percentage was 74% for students from Nepal and 58% for those from Bangladesh.The figure for Indian students was 23%, for students from the US 1% and for students from Australia and Canada 0%.The 2,890 students from Bangladesh and Nepal brought with them 1,814 family members during the period 2022 to 2024.Data also showed that estimated total fees received by Danish universities over the three years were approximately US$162 million in total, or US$54 million per year, with students from China making up 16.7% of fee-paying students, Bangladesh (12.7%), the US (12.3%), Australia (6.8%), India (5.9%), Canada (5.7%) and Nepal (5.1%).A total of 65.7% of the 16,205, or 10,646, students come from these seven countries.Stricter oversight expectedIn addition to verification of educational documents, students on a study residence permit can expect to experience stricter oversight over their academic performance so that students who are not succeeding are sent home more quickly.The national ID centre will be established to assist universities in their control of foreigners' educational documents, making it more difficult for overseas students to obtain a study residence permit on the basis of false documents.The centre will also review already issued study residence permits for citizens from Bangladesh and Nepal in order to assess whether residence permits have been issued on the basis of false documents.Students from third countries are expected to pay for their education themselves and cannot receive government student funding in the form of loans and grants.International students have become a hot issue in the election campaign for the 2026 election, where immigration is going to be an important theme. Already the right-leaning Danish People’s Party and the Denmark Democrats are together polling at 17.7%, up from 11.66% in 2022.Accusations of ‘wokeism’The Copenhagen Poston 29 September reported that Stoklund, speaking specifically about those immigrants seeking to change Danish society, said: “I want us to do everything we can to get as many as possible to return home, so they go back to their homeland.” In a warning about radical Islam and “parallel societies”, he is quoted as saying: “We have to do everything to keep Islam out of the door.”In aninterview withBerlingske Tidendeon 29 September, the chairman of the Danish People’s Party, Morten Messerschmidt, said that Roskilde University – one of those accused of failing to properly manage its intake of Bangladeshi students – should be closed down due to “woke and identity political socialism”.“All my political life, which at least is over the last 20 years, there has been an everlasting problem of low academic quality and an extreme political governance towards woke and left-wing orientated political views and deviations from the ordinary academic work method, and I could go on listing more,” Messerschmidt said.Last month, RUC announced in response to the controversy that it was closing one of its masters programmes. The chair of the university board, Carsten Toft Boesen, also stepped down after Prime Minister Mette Frederiksen entered the fray, criticising the university over its recruitment of Bangladeshi students.Meanwhile, Southern University has taken a decision to halt the winter admissions of about 700 students from non-EU countries who have applied for admission to SDU in February 2026 in order to “thoroughly analyse” what was happening.Rector Jens Ringsmose toldPolitikenon 28 September: “We have a well-founded suspicion that a large part of the international students [from third countries] are here for other reasons than to study, and we do not want an admission to our courses to be a backdoor to the Danish work market.”Fair and transparent processesGoran Melin, a higher education expert in the Technopolis Group in Stockholm, toldUniversity World Newsthe Danish higher education authorities and universities seemed to have “rather strong evidence that the admission rules are being abused by certain student groups. It is understandable that under-qualification for a course, or cheating, is sufficient ground for rejection. It is a matter of fairness towards other international students and also of education quality”.However, he added that there are signs of other views in the debate.“Playing the ‘woke card’, as in the accusations against Roskilde University Centre, or having the ambition to ‘keep Islam out of the door’ signals a totally different agenda to one related to education quality, academic freedom and transparent but unconditional admission rules for all international students.“The Danish labour market is relatively hot, and there must be ways to admit qualified international students on fair and transparent grounds. And if they graduate and find a qualified job in Denmark, it must be a good deal for the Danish economy, regardless of their religion.”Associate Professor Brian Arly Jacobsen, based in the Department of Cross-Cultural and Regional Studies at the University of Copenhagen, said: “We share the government's concern that study stays in Denmark should not be misused, and we agree that there must be proper control of admission and documentation.”However, he said the current government initiatives risk impacting people beyond those allegedly trying to exploit the system.“International students contribute significantly to the research and education environments at Danish universities – economically, academically, and socially. Denmark needs talent from all over the world, and we must be careful not to send a signal that they are not welcome.“We are concerned that the general tightening of the rules may make Denmark less attractive for the international students we actually want to attract.“When new regulations are designed, it is important that they are proportional, transparent, and legally sound – and that they do not stigmatise students on the basis of nationality,” he noted.He said it is crucial that students who come to Denmark with the right qualifications and a genuine wish to study are met with openness and fair conditions.“If we want Denmark to remain an attractive country for education and research, we must distinguish clearly between misuse and the large majority of international students who are a genuine asset to our society,” he stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

丹麥收緊第三國學生申請新規定 近期，丹麥政府針對來自非歐盟國家的國際學生，特別是孟加拉和尼泊爾的學生，實施了一系列新的入學政策，以防止他們利用學習身份進入當地勞動市場。新規定包括提高入學學術要求、縮短畢業後尋找工作的時間、加強外國學歷文件的驗證及限制家庭成員的陪伴。這些措施旨在確保教育質量，並回應對部分學生學歷資格的質疑。專家指出，雖然需要加強監管，但應避免影響那些真正有意求學的國際學生，否則可能使丹麥在全球教育市場上的吸引力下降。  
```
