Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFGHANISTANInternet cut alarms students, sparks fears of full blackoutManija Mirzaie02 October 2025TweetAfghanistan appeared to be edging towards a total digital blackout earlier this week, as the Taliban cut off internet access for two days in a move that disrupted services and rattled the aspirations of a younger generation that depends on access to online platforms to learn, communicate and stay connected with the outside world.While mobile connectivity was restored in parts of the country on 1 October, for women and girls who have been shut out of education by the Taliban, the shock was particularly severe, as internet access represents their last hope for education via online learning. Many see the intensified restrictions on internet access as a calculated act of control by the Taliban.Smriti Singh, Amnesty International’s regional director for South Asia, said in astatementon 30 September: “Internet access is critical for their [women and girls] education. This shutdown disrupts their ability to learn and cannot be justified under any circumstances. A total shutdown of this nature is inherently disproportionate and would fly in the face of international human rights standards.”Beginning on 29 September, internet and mobile services were cut across much of the country, reducing connectivity to less than 1% of normal levels.The blackout followed earlier bans in mid-September on fibre-optic services in provinces such as Balkh, Kunduz, Baghlan, Takhar, and Badakhshan, ostensibly to “prevent immorality”, according to Taliban officials, who said at the time services would be suspended “till further notice”.“Prevent[ing] immorality” from online spaces, in this context, is seen by many as code for erasing women’s presence from public and intellectual life.The Taliban this week publiclydenied ordering a nationwide ban, attributing the blackout to failing fibre-optic cables in need of replacement.However, leaked statementsand monitoring by groups such as NetBlocks, a global internet monitoring watchdog, and UN observers suggested the shutdown was orchestrated and extended beyond routine maintenance.During the outage, aviation, banking, and humanitarian operations were disrupted: Kabul’s international airport cancelled flights; banks could not process electronic transactions; NGOs warned that aid delivery coordination was severely hampered, and citizens reported being unable to communicate with relatives.Online educationParwana M, a 22-year-old nursing student in Kabul province, had been studying remotely through an underground network of female-led legal education groups based in Australia. Every day, she and her classmates gather via the online platformGoogle Meetto exchange notes and study. “It kept our spirits alive,” she toldUniversity World Newson Thursday 2 October, by phone.“When the internet went, it felt like the roof had fallen on me,” she stated.Her story is not unique. Across the country, thousands of Afghan girls and women were cut off from virtual classrooms, study groups, exam preparation sessions, and international mentors. For many, online schooling is not simply an alternative; it is their only option following the Taliban’s three-year-old ban on girls and women in education, teachers said.Teachers themselves were also stranded. Obaid Ullah, a private university lecturer running private online economics seminars for over 100 students across four provinces in Afghanistan, said during the shutdown he could no longer reach students.“For many of these students in remote parts of the country, this wasn’t just about education; it was about survival,” he said.Qari Elaam Uddin, an Islamic studies teacher in northern Kunduz province, told the United Nations he was teaching the Qur’an and other religious subjects online to some Afghan families in the United States. “For the millions of Afghans living in Western countries where religious studies in Pashto and Dari languages are not easy to get, these online courses were their only options,” he said.“I hope they [the Taliban] do not ban it [the internet] permanently”, he said after internet services were revived on 1 October.Fears of future repressionMany educators fear the blackout was not a one-time act of censorship but a test-run for future digital repression.UK-based Afghan scholar Baryaalai Noorzai, an IT expert and data scientist, toldUniversity World Newsthat mass internet shutdowns violate rights to education, expression, and access to information enshrined in international law.“The Taliban’s recent shutdown of internet access in Afghanistan was not a technical upgrade – it was a calculated act of control. If we consider the definition of terrorism – the unlawful use of violence and intimidation, especially against civilians, to achieve political aims – the Taliban actions fit it clearly,” said Noorzai.“They continue to use fear, detention and restrictions to suppress Afghan people,” he said, adding that claims that the blackout was due to “fibre optic maintenance” were implausible.“Afghanistan’s fibre infrastructure was largely built during the US presence [in Afghanistan before 2021], and any major upgrade would take months or years, not a few days. Meanwhile, the Taliban also blocked satellite TV channels such asTolo News, detained IT directors overnight, and warned internet providers not to resume service without permission – clear intimidation, not technical maintenance,” he added.Right to learnExperts fromHuman Rights Watch, Amnesty International and the Malala Fund called the blackout a direct attack on Afghan women’s and girls’ right to learn.“The Taliban’s moves to cut internet access harm the livelihoods of millions of Afghans and deprive them of their basic rights to education, healthcare, and access to information,” said Fereshta Abbasi, Afghanistan researcher at Human Rights Watch. “The Taliban should drop its baseless rationales and end these shutdowns.”The Office of the UN High Commissioner for Human Rights has in the pastreportedon the dramatic effects of internet shutdowns, including the impact on freedom of expression, political participation, public safety, education, work, and health, as well as exacerbating pre-existing social, economic, andgender-basedinequalities.Shutdowns further restrict women’s and girls’ access to critical support and information, including emergency health services.“Afghans were already isolated from the world, but now they are [at risk of being] completely cut off,” Abbasi said. “The longer the Taliban internet shutdowns continue, the more harmful the consequences for both the people and the country.”For Afghan students, particularly women, the fear now is that the internet may disappear again, as the world watches in silence. “Before this, the world silently watched us lose our schools,” Parwana said. “Now they’re watching us lose the internet. What’s next?”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 阿富汗近期經歷了為期兩天的網路封鎖，這一措施令當地學生感到恐慌，並對未來的數位黑暗感到憂慮。雖然10月1日部分地區的行動通信恢復，但對於已被塔利班禁止接受教育的女性和女孩來說，網路連接是她們獲得教育的最後希望。國際特赦組織和人權觀察等機構指出，這一封鎖是對婦女教育權利的直接攻擊，並強調了網路對於教育、醫療和信息獲取的重要性。專家警告，這可能是未來數位壓制的前奏，呼籲塔利班結束這種行為，以避免進一步損害阿富汗人民的基本權利。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Court rules against Trump’s student deportation efforts
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Court rules against Trump’s student deportation effortsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALReport tracks US role in global squeeze on academic freedomUNITED STATESCould things get darker still for US higher education?UNITED STATESGuidance for international students on US’s hostile regimeUNITED STATESTrump’s policies are set to reshape global academic flowsUNITED STATESUniversities can expect a frontal attack if Trump returnsGLOBALUniversity governance: How to respond to the ‘Gaza effect’Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATESCourt rules against Trump’s student deportation effortsNathan M Greenfield02 October 2025TweetA judge has ruled that the United States government’s effort to deport international students who had protested in support of Palestine was unconstitutional because it violated their rights to free speech under the First Amendment.In his blistering decision, Judge William G Young compared masked Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officers to “cowardly desperados and the despised Ku Klux Klan”.Thedecisionin a case brought by the American Association of University Professors (AAUP) and several other groups, including the Middle East Studies Association (MESA), followed a 15-day bench trial.It is one of the first cases in which President Donald J Trump’s various executive and other administrative orders have been tested in trial and not just in an application for an emergency injunction. (In a bench trial, the judge conducts the case, decides the facts, renders a judgement and applies the law.)Young, the senior judge of the Massachusetts Federal District Court, took the unusual step of prefacing his decision with the First Amendment to the Constitution, which reads, in part: “Congress shall make no law … abridging the freedom of speech,” which he set in opposition to Trump’s view that “the First Amendment’s protection of freedom of speech applies to American citizens alone”.A ‘most important’ caseCalling the case brought by the AAUP and MESA “perhaps the most important ever to fall within the jurisdiction of this district court”, Young, who has sat on the court since 1985, having been appointed by Republican President Ronald Reagan in 1985, said it “squarely presents the issue of whether non-citizens lawfully present here in the United States actually have the same free speech rights as the rest of us”.“The Court answers this constitutional question unequivocally, ‘yes, they do’. ‘No law’ means ‘no law’. The First Amendment does not draw President Trump’s invidious distinction [between citizens and others lawfully in the country], and it is not to be found in our history or jurisprudence,” writes Young.Ramya Krishnan, a senior staff attorney at theKnight First Amendment Instituteat Columbia University, which was the lead advocate in the case, said: “No one should have to live in fear that they could be seized by ICE agents for their political expression. Today’s decision affirms that the First Amendment protects non-citizens just as it protects citizens. The administration’s deportations dishonour the First Amendment and democracy.”Lynn Pasquerella, president of the American Association of Colleges and Universities, wrote in an email toUniversity World News: “Judge Young’s ruling, which rebukes ideological deportations, is a compelling victory for academic freedom and democracy.“By rejecting the Trump administration’s attempt to silence dissenting voices, the decision makes clear that the pursuit of truth depends on protecting the right of all people to speak, question and challenge authority without fear of reprisal.”Dr Asli Bâli, who is the Howard M Holtzmann professor of law at Yale Law School and president of the Middle East Studies Association, which was one of the plaintiffs, responded to the landmark ruling bysaying in part: “We deeply appreciate the court’s principled ruling. The Trump administration’s actions have grievously harmed our colleagues by treating protected speech and freedom of association as deplorable offences.“Our ability to meet, as a scholarly association, and to discuss critical topics of our field [has] been directly diminished by the chilling effect of the government’s ideological deportations.”In an email toUniversity World News, Ronald J Krotoszynski Jr, the John S Stone chair, director of faculty research, and professor of law at the University of Alabama School of Law and the author ofFree Speech as Civic Structure: A Comparative Analysis of How Courts and Culture Shape the Freedom of Speech(Cambridge UP, 2024), wrote: “Judge Young’s opinion is important in two key respects. First, it makes clear that once a person is lawfully present in the United States, that person enjoys full, not watered-down, constitutional rights and liberties.“Second, the opinion also indicates that US citizens, no less than lawfully present non-citizens, enjoy the constitutional right to speak out about matters of public concern without the fear of legal punishment by the government.“Viewpoint discrimination – which is what this case is about – isn’t just a cardinal First Amendment sin; it’sTHEcardinal First Amendment sin. Judge Young’s opinion makes this point effectively and with crystal clarity.”Despite Young’s searing rebuke of the president, Secretary of State Marco Rubio, Secretary of Homeland Security Kristi Noem, and Principal Deputy Spokesperson for the State Department Tommy Piggot dismissed the ruling, saying in astatement toThe Independent: “The United States is under no obligation to allow foreign aliens to come to our country, commit acts of anti-American, pro-terrorist, and antisemitic hate, or incite violence”, and that the US “will continue to revoke the visas of those who put the safety of our citizens at risk”.For her part, Homeland Security Assistant Secretary Tricia McLaughlin went much further, accusing Young of “demonising federal law enforcement” and “stok[ing] the embers of hatred” against ICE by finding that ICE agents covered their faces to “terrorise Americans into quiescence”.Rights of non-citizensTo reach the verdict that Rubio, Noem and their subordinates “deliberately and with purposeful aforethought, did so concert their actions . . . to chill the rights to freedom of speech and peaceful assembly of the non-citizen plaintiff members of the plaintiff associations [such as MESA]”, Young had to first demonstrate that non-citizens enjoy First Amendment rights and, secondly, that the plaintiff’s actions fell well within this legal circle.Young begins this by showing that while in 1952, Congress gave the secretary of state the power to remove individuals who “would have potential serious adverse foreign policy consequences for the United States”, Congress later clarified that a non-citizen’s “past, current or expected beliefs, statements, or associations, if such beliefs, statements, or associations would be lawful within the United States”, are not grounds to prevent a non-citizen from entering the country “unless the Secretary of State personally determines that the [non-citizen’s] admission would compromise a compelling United States foreign policy interest”.In September 2021, then secretary of homeland security Alejandro Mayorkas issued a memorandum that stated, in part: “A non-citizen’s exercise of their First Amendment rights also should never be a factor in deciding to take enforcement action”, that is, deportation.Mayorkas’ memo has not been countermanded and, thus, remains in force. Accordingly, however laudable the Trump administration’s efforts to stamp out antisemitism on America’s campuses, the administration cannot use that goal as the reason for revoking hundreds of visas and incarcerating an unknown number of international students and green card holders, including Mahmoud Khalil, a graduate student at Columbia University in New York who is a permanent resident of the United States and is married to an American citizen.According to Krotoszynski: “Judge Young's opinion in the Khalil case provides a black letter analysis and application of core First Amendment principles; whatever vagaries or subtleties might exist about the application of the First Amendment to US citizens while abroad, or to non-US citizens while outside the United States, the First Amendment certainly applies – and must apply – with full force to anyone and everyone lawfully present in the US.”Further, Krotoszynski explains, in some cases, the Supreme Court “has held that the rights of free speech, assembly, and association apply with less force outside the territory of the United States.However, no good law exists for the proposition that persons lawfully present in the US do not enjoy the full protection of the Bill of Rights and the Fourteenth Amendment.Congress (not the President) may decide whom it will authorise to enter the country; the President has the authority and duty to enforce the laws on immigration and naturalisation that Congress writes. But neither Congress nor the President may condition lawful presences on the surrender of a person's constitutional rights and liberties”.Young shows that irrespective of the settled law vis-à-vis the rights of visa holders and permanent residents, Rubio, Trump and other members of the administration continued to make public statements that effectively denied the fact that non-citizens fall under the penumbra of the First Amendment, as they did in records of government meetings Young quotes from.On 13 March, Vice-President JD Vance asserted that “American citizens have different rights from people who have green cards, from people who have student visas. And so my attitude on this is: this is not fundamentally about free speech,” for example.Young established that the protestors’ actions fell within the ambit of the First Amendment by, among other arguments, showing that they committed no violent actions – in some cases, allegedly distributing pro-Palestinian leaflets – and that the government did not have a coherent definition of the “antisemitism” it was trying to stamp out.Young recounts an exchange with John Armstrong, senior bureau official in the Bureau of Consular Affairs, in which Armstrong could not give a definition of antisemitism other than what he believed it to be.This, despite the fact that, as Young writes: “Guidance exists within the Department of State … which has an entire webpage dedicated to the definition of antisemitism, in which it acknowledges, among other things, that “[m]anifestations [of antisemitism] might include the targeting of the state of Israel, conceived as a Jewish collective. However,criticism of Israel similar to that levelled against any other country cannot be regarded as antisemitic(emphasis in the original).”Nor was Young impressed with Rubio’s equation of the op-ed written by Tufts University PhD student Rumeysa Öztürk, criticising Israel for its war in Gaza, with vandalism. (Of her arrest by ICE agents on 25 March 2025, Young was scathing, reproducing a security photo that shows a woman being stopped by a masked man.)Targeting a fewHaving demonstrated the finding that the law did not circumscribe non-citizen’s speech and that their actions did not violate any laws, Young did not have to go any further. However, he took the next step and said that the government’s (illegal) crackdown on visa and green card holders threatened the First Amendment rights of citizens.“It was never the Secretaries’ immediate intention to deport all pro-Palestinian non-citizens for the obvious First Amendment violation that could have raised a major outcry.“Rather, the intent of the Secretaries was more invidious – to target a few for speaking out and then use the full rigour of the Immigration and Nationality Act (in ways it had never been used before) to have them publicly deported with the goal of tamping down pro-Palestinian student protests and terrorising similarly situated non-citizen (and other) pro-Palestinians into silence because their views were unwelcome.”As becomes clear a few paragraphs later, the phrase “and other” can have only one meaning: American citizens, after Young, referring to Öztürk, writes: “ICE goes masked for a single reason – to terrorise Americans into quiescence.”In the next paragraph, Young avers: “If the distinguished Homeland Security intelligence agency can be weaponised to squelch the free speech of a small, hapless group of non-citizens in our midst, so too can the Federal Home Loan Mortgage Corporation, and the audit divisions of the Internal Revenue Service and the Social Security Administration be unconstitutionally weaponised against the President’s ever-growing list of ‘enemies’ or opponents he ‘hates’, notwithstanding that political persecution is anathema to our Constitution and everything for which America stands.”As Krotoszynski put it: “If the federal government can suspend First Amendment rights for lawfully present non-citizens, by parity of logic, it may also deny them other constitutional rights, including freedom from arbitrary searches and seizures, a right to counsel, or even due process of law.“This simply cannot be correct as a matter of constitutional law; the government can admit or deny admission to anyone it wishes and, under the plenary power doctrine, has exceedingly broad discretion to decide who may lawfully enter the country. But, once a person is lawfully present, neither the federal nor a state government may condition their continued lawful presence on the surrender of their constitutional rights.”According to Krotoszynski, Young’s opinion “makes clear that once a person is lawfully present in the United States, that person enjoys full, not watered-down, constitutional rights and liberties”.It also “indicates that US citizens, no less than lawfully present non-citizens, enjoy the constitutional right to speak out about matters of public concern without the fear of legal punishment by the government”.This is why it was important for Young to say that MESA had established that the government’s campaign against non-citizen scholars “significantly harmed its core activity of facilitating academic discourse and scholarship about issues impacting the Middle East, including by reducing projected membership numbers and so membership dues” and “by pressuring members to self-censor and so not to contribute scholarship and perspectives that they would otherwise contribute, which scholarship and discourse are the lifeblood of the organisation”.Ignoring all lawsIn the middle of the decision, Young turns his fire on Trump himself.“The President may not have authorised this operation (or even known about it), but once it was in play, the President wholeheartedly supported it, making many individual case-specific comments (some quite cruel) that demonstrate he was fully briefed,” writes Young.“By ignoring the constitution’s command that the president “take care that the laws be faithfully executed”, the President, “violate[d] his sacred oath to ‘faithfully execute the office of the President of the United States, and . . . to the best of [his] ability preserve, protect and defend the Constitution of the United States” (ellipsis in the original).In the last fifteen pages Young returns to Trump, of whom he writes: “The Constitution, our civil laws, regulations, mores, customs, courtesies – all of it; the President simply ignores it when he takes it into his head to act.”Young continues: “Small wonder then that our bastions of independent unbiased free speech – those entities we thought unassailable – have proven all too often to have only Quaker guns [a term from the American Civil War for logs painted black to look like guns]. Behold President Trump’s successes in limiting free speech – law firms cower, institutional leaders in higher education meekly appease the President, media outlets from huge conglomerates to small niche magazines mind the bottom line rather than the ethics of journalism.”The final two paragraphs of the decision refer to a quote in which Reagan said that freedom is “never more than one generation from extinction. . . it must be fought for and defended constantly by each generation, for it comes only once to a people”.“I’ve come to believe that President Trump truly understands and appreciates the full import of President Reagan’s inspiring message – yet I fear he has drawn from it a darker, more cynical message. I fear that President Trump believes the American people are so divided that today they will not stand up, fight for and defend our most precious constitutional values so long as they are lulled into thinking their own personal interests are not affected.”Young’s final question: “Is he correct?” is not meant to be rhetorical.The ‘magnificent’ US constitutionYoung’s decision in this case is perhaps the only decision in the history of American courts to have a statement above the identifying title, which begins with the words: “United States District Court/District of Massachusetts”, and then on the lower left, the list of plaintiffs and defendants, the short form being “American Association of University Professors v MARCO RUBIO, in his official capacity as Secretary of State . . . .”Above this, the judge has placed a photocopy of a postcard dated 19 June 2025 disparaging his authority: “TRUMP HAS PARDONS AND TANKS . . . WHAT DO YOU HAVE?”To use the phrase by which attorneys refer to judges on appeal, the “Learned Justice” typed:Dear Mr or Ms Anonymous.Alone, I have nothing but my sense of duty.Together, We the people of theUnited States – you and me –have our magnificent Constitution.Here’s how that works out in a specific case –Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 美國法院裁定反對特朗普政府驅逐國際學生的努力。近日，馬薩諸塞州聯邦地區法院法官威廉·G·楊裁定，政府試圖驅逐因支持巴勒斯坦而抗議的國際學生違反了第一修正案的言論自由權利。這宗案件由美國大學教授協會及其他團體提起，標誌著特朗普行政命令在法庭上的首次重要挑戰。楊法官強調，合法入境的非公民擁有與公民相同的言論自由權利，並譴責政府的驅逐行為對學術自由的威脅。該裁決被視為學術界及民主自由的重要勝利。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: First Korean rankings reflect trend towards globalised HE
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: First Korean rankings reflect trend towards globalised HEGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH KOREAScapegoating international students misses the real problemsSOUTH KOREAUniversities turn to overseas students to boost financesASIAMixed results show need to strengthen internationalisationSOUTH KOREANew plan aims at 31% rise in international students by 2027SOUTH KOREAVisa changes part of plan to attract international studentsSOUTH KOREARecord influx of foreign students in post-pandemic reboundFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultySOUTH KOREAFirst Korean rankings reflect trend towards globalised HEYumi Jeung02 October 2025TweetA new university rankings system in South Korea – its first ever national rankings – is strongly weighted towards internationalisation to provide objective information to foreign students looking to study in the country.The Korea Times, the nation’s oldest English-language daily, released its first K-universities Global Excellence Rankings on 24 September. Unusually, indicators directly related to globalisation are given a 63.2% weighting in the new rankings, making it the most internationally focused national university rankings to date.The rankings assessed 54 four-year degree-awarding universities nationwide across four broad categories on a 190-point scale, comprising education (60 points), research (50 points), student support (40 points), and graduate outcomes and influence (40 points). However, the newspaper noted, “no single university dominates every category”.The rankings include 22 sub-indicators, with significant weight given to factors that matter most to international students, such as diversity of students’ nationality, scholarship provision, and retention rates, rather than a focus on research output, which dominates international rankings systems, the newspaper said.Based on the results,The Korea Times, together with six experts in university globalisation,selected the top 30 institutions. Korea University leads the field, followed by Seoul National University (SNU) in second place and Yonsei University in third.The three universities – collectively known as SKY – have for many years been the most sought-after universities for domestic students.Others in the top 10 include Sungkyunkwan, Hanyang, Sogang, the University of Seoul, Ewha Womans University, Kyunghee and Dongguk – all of them in the capital, Seoul.Kim Dong-one, president of top-ranked Korea University, said in a media statement on 26 September: “This achievement is thanks to the efforts of all members who used internationalisation as a driving force for development, even in the face of the challenges of a declining school-age population and the era of artificial intelligence.”He added: “Korea University will continue to pursue global cooperation and innovation to establish itself as Korea’s representative university, the most preferred by international students, and a global university that is indispensable to the world.”Foreign student experiencesThe newspaper described the rankings as a “student-centred evaluation that takes into account the experiences of international students”.“Existing university rankings have largely focused on domestic students, leaving few credible resources for foreigners seeking reliable information about where to study. This lack of accessible guidance highlighted the need for an evaluation tailored to the concerns of international students,” it noted.International students have expressed support for the new rankings. Fatma Ors, a student from Turkey at Yonsei University, welcomed the initiative, saying: “For foreign students trying to learn about Korean universities, this seems to provide information that isn’t easily available elsewhere.”She toldUniversity World Newsthat “details such as international research capacity, dormitory facilities, and graduate school admission rates are hard to find on our own, but they are crucial when choosing a university”.A professor from an international studies faculty at one of the highly ranked universities toldUniversity World Newsthis week: “In practice, there have often been gaps between the expectations set by universities’ promotional campaigns and the experiences of foreign students once they arrive.In their eagerness to recruit international students, some universities may have encouraged overly optimistic expectations regarding student support or research opportunities.”Pointing at the benchmarks set by the rankings, he added: “These new rankings, grounded in objective criteria, will not only help foreign students make better-informed decisions but also serve as a constructive challenge for universities themselves.”Academics endorsed the overall intent of the rankings. Shin Ha-gyun, a professor of international relations at Dongduk Women’s University, wrote in a 25 September commentary for theMaeilbusiness newspaper: “The goals of internationalisation are clear – to recruit outstanding international students, expand research collaborations with overseas universities, and raise the brand value of our institutions.“Through this, universities can secure global competitiveness in both education and research. Sustainable growth, however, cannot be guaranteed without strategic promotion, organisational innovation, and country-specific approaches. Universities must reset their internationalisation goals, restructure their organisations, and secure the right talent.”International student growthAccording toThe Korea Times, the evaluation was introduced in response to Korea’s shifting higher education environment, shaped by declining birth rates, shrinking school-age populations, and a surge in the number of foreign students. Korean universities are increasingly having torely on recruiting international students to survive.Official immigration data from the Ministry of Justice, in charge of immigration and visas, shows explosive growth in its international student population over the past three years. Foreign student numbers rose from 197,234 in 2022, 226,507 in 2023, and 263,775 in 2024 to approximately 275,580 as of May 2025.According to official figures, foreign student enrolment last month surpassed a government target to attract300,000 overseas students by 2027– regarded as ambitious at the time. Of the 300,000, the majority, or 220,000 (70%), were for degree programmes. Students from Vietnam and China together make up nearly half of the total.A July report titledTrends in the Recruitment of Foreign Students in Korean Higher Education Institutions, from theMigration Research and Training Centre(MRTC), a think tank, drew onKorean Education Development Institute(KEDI) data that showed as of 2024, 12 out of 389 universities (3.1%) had a foreign student ratio exceeding 20%.In 2014, not a single university crossed that threshold, highlighting the scale of the shift within just a decade. MRTC stressed universities should set recruitment targets with diversity in mind and develop diversity indices to manage the shift.Rankings dataThe K-universities rankings framework relied on publicly available data from the Ministry of Education andNaver Scholytics, which collates data mainly from Naver Academic, a comprehensive database of published academic content.Jung Da-hyun, theKorea Timesjournalist who led the project, toldUniversity World Newsthe K-university rankings were developed over the course of a year.“We listened to the views of multiple stakeholders, set indicators in consultation with researchers, and refined the methodology based on feedback from higher education experts.”She noted: “As an English-language newspaper based in Korea, our readership includes many foreign students interested in studying here. Korea is increasingly becoming a destination of choice for international students, and we wanted to provide them with reliable information about Korean universities.”She pointed out that “international rankings tend to feature only a handful of Korean institutions, while domestic rankings – such as those produced by the [local newspaper]JoongAng Ilbo– often focus on criteria that matter more to Korean students than to foreign ones.”Korea JoongAng Dailyhas published a Koreanuniversity competitiveness rankingssince 1994, covering a limited number of domestic universities.Key indicatorsThe education indicator in the K-universities rankings measures exchange student ratios, foreign student ratios, nationality diversity, and International Education Quality Assurance System (IEQAS) certification levels. Korea University placed first, followed by the University of Seoul, Yonsei, Sungkyunkwan, and Hanyang.These universities were recognised not only for attracting large numbers of international students but also for establishing stable “two-way exchange models” by sending their own students abroad.The evaluation team atThe Korea Timesnoted: “Universities should shift the focus of internationalisation from ‘how many students are recruited’ to ‘how well they are supported to graduate successfully’.”In the research category, which assessed international publications per faculty member, the proportion of international collaborative papers, international citation indices, the rate of publication in the top 10% of academic journals, and research funding, Sejong University took first place, followed by Yonsei University, Sungkyunkwan University, and SNU.“Sejong University’s surprising rise to the top showed how strategic partnerships can matter more than size, with the university rapidly lifting its research profile through joint projects with overseas institutions,” statedThe Korea Times.Yonsei and Sungkyunkwan demonstrated consistent performance in citation indices and high-ranking journal publications. SNU and Korea University distinguished themselves in securing global research funding and joint research projects.Student support and alumni networksThe student support category measures spending per student, scholarships as a proportion of tuition fees, and dormitory capacity for foreign students. SNU came first, followed by Korea University, Yonsei, the Catholic University of Korea, and Sogang.SNU led this category thanks to its strong financial capacity, substantial scholarships, high per-student investment, and fundraising strength. Korea and Yonsei also performed strongly in scholarships and dormitory provision, while Sogang was highlighted for robust accommodation and student support systems.Graduate outcomes cover employment rates, graduate school admission rates, and the social influence of alumni. This category saw SNU at the top, ahead of Korea, Yonsei, Sungkyunkwan, and Sogang. SNU was recognised for its “unrivalled” alumni network in major domestic institutions, corporations, and professional fields.Korea and Yonsei demonstrated strong influence in administration, law, and economics, while Sungkyunkwan and Sogang stood out in professional and administrative sectors.“This is the most long-term measure of internationalisation, reflecting not just the ability to attract students but to prepare them to become global leaders. For international students, strong alumni outcomes signal the value and credibility of Korean degrees in the global job market,”The Korea Timessaid.The Korea Timesevaluation team said it intends to expand the scope of the assessment in future editions and incorporate qualitative elements, such as surveys of foreign faculty and students, to provide a more holistic evaluation.“Many readers also asked us to reflect qualitative aspects that cannot be captured in data, and we plan to incorporate such indicators in future editions to better serve international students,” Jung said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 韓國首次國家大學排名強調國際化 新近發布的韓國K大學全球卓越排名，首次針對國內54所四年制大學進行評估，並賦予國際化指標高達63.2%的權重，成為最具國際化的國內排名。該排名考量教育、研究、學生支持及畢業成果等四大類別，旨在為國際學生提供可靠的信息。韓國大學在排名中表現優異，其中韓國大學、首爾國立大學及延世大學名列前三。這一排名不僅幫助國際學生做出明智選擇，也推動大學提升自身的國際化目標和支持系統，應對教育環境的變化。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Defence of free speech on campuses is anything but free
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Defence of free speech on campuses is anything but freeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESCould things get darker still for US higher education?UNITED STATESKirk assassination raises the political stakes for HEUNITED STATESSilencing international students corrodes the spirit of HEUNITED STATESA law to fight antisemitism. But what about free speech?UNITED STATESFreedom of speech according to the gospel of KochUNITED STATESUniversities, we have a problem we are afraid to speak ofUNITED STATESUniversities required to monitor ‘viewpoint diversity’Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED STATESDefence of free speech on campuses is anything but freeNathan M Greenfield02 October 2025TweetThe day after a gunman assassinated right-wing activist Charlie Kirk on the campus of Utah Valley University (UVU) in Orem, Utah, United States, the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE) published a survey of 68,000 college students that shows how far America’s campuses are from the bucolic places depicted by Hollywood.More than one-third (34%) ofsurveyed studentsagreed that “violence can be acceptable to prevent campus speech” – 10 percentage points higher than just four years earlier.Seven of 10 students on the 257 campuses surveyed (or 47,600) said that “it’s acceptable to shout down speakers”. More than half (54%) believed students could be blocked from entering an event.At about 42%,support for “using violence to stop a speaker” is highest among “Strong Democrats”, but in the last year it has risen faster among “Strong Republicans”, going from the high 20s to the mid-30s. Even among independents, support for using violence to stop a speaker sits at around 38%, up almost 15 percentage points in four years.“The implications for academic freedom are stark,” stated FIRE’s press release of 11 September.“Experts warn that the normalisation of violence or severe disruption as a legitimate response to unpopular speech risks creating a chilling effect, where both students and faculty may self-censor to avoid conflict. This environment threatens the core mission of universities as marketplaces of ideas,” it noted.“The trajectory of these attitudes is particularly alarming. Acceptance of violent disruption, once a fringe view, has grown across the ideological spectrum, including among traditionally more speech-tolerant conservative students,” the press release stated.FIREwarned: “If these trends persist, universities will likely confront mounting pressure to implement policies and enforcement measures to safeguard both physical safety and intellectual freedom, a balancing act with no easy solution.”The cost of free speechDefending free speech on the quad is costly, and, following the Supreme Court of the United States’ (SCOTUS) ruling in the 1992 case ofForsyth County v Nationalist Movement (Forsyth)and the 2023 case in New Mexico involving Turning Point USA (TPUSA), the conservative student organisation co-founded by Kirk, costs must be borne by colleges and universities.According to theDaily Californian, an independent newspaper published by students at the University of California, Berkeley, providing security for the American conservative commentator Ben Shapiro’s Free Speech Week in August 2017 was estimated at US$600,000 but eventually cost US$800,000 (US$1 million today, factoring in inflation).Two other political events that occurred in the month beginning 27 August 2017, including the appearance of ultra-conservativefirebrand Milo Yiannopoulos, who has usedantisemitic, anti-Muslim,anti-Black and anti-LGBTQ+language, pushed the cost of security for this period to US$4 million (or US$5.2 million), more than 5% of Berkeley’ssecurity budget of US$77 million(US$101.7 million today).The bulk of these funds went to paying for reinforcements from the City of Berkeley’s police department, the California Highway Patrol and the Alameda County Sheriff’s Office.Berkeley was not the only university shouldering hefty expenses when contentious speakers from outside the university came to campus. Providing security to Richard Spencer, a notorious white nationalist, when he visited the University of Florida (UF, Gainesville) a few months latercost UF US$500,000, according to media reports.Public universities like Berkeley, UF and UVU cannot close their campus gates to outside speakers because they are bound by the constitutional guarantee of free speech.“The University of California is a public institution, and therefore it’s governed and bound by the free speech clause” of the Constitution, Jesse Choper, a former Berkeley law dean and constitutional expert, toldCalifornia, a publication of the Cal Alumni Association.(The courts have not yet been asked to rule on whether private universities like Harvard or Duke are bound by the same constitutional requirements as public universities. However, in cases which turn on the fact that they accept federal funds, courts have ruled that private universities are bound by the Constitution and applicable federal law.)Standardised security feesFive days after Kirk’s assassination, 15 Republican state attorneys general co-signed a letter written byBrenna Bird, the Republican attorney general for Iowa, to the presidents and deans of her state’s universities and colleges.In it, she wrote: “Particularly at this moment, it is critical [that] our state institutions of higher learning serve as forums for robust debate from all perspectives. The campus should be the very place where the First Amendment rings loudest for all to hear. We urge you to say no to the ‘Assassin’s Veto’.”WhileForsythhad nothing to do with colleges and universities, it set a binding precedent.InForsyththe SCOTUS struck down a Forsyth County, Georgia, ordinance that authorised charging the white nationalist organisation known as the Nationalist Movement a higher fee for a demonstration permit (for a protest on Martin Luther King Jr Day): the higher fee being warranted because of perceived threats from “anticipated hostile crowds”.As Rebecca Roman sums upForsythand two other SCOTUS decisions in “When Speech Isn’t Free: The Rising Costs of Hosting Controversial Speakers at Public Universities”, published inThe University of Chicago Legal Forum(2024), “in traditional public forums, permits and fees must be assessed in an objective and content-neutral way and speakers cannot be excluded or surcharged based on the content of their speech”, a doctrine known as “viewpoint neutrality”.Bird and the other attorneys general reminded the presidents and deans that “any security policy that appears neutral on its face must not be applied in a discriminatory manner” and of the court sanction for this statement in the case ofLeadership Institute and Turning Point USA at the University of New Mexico v The University of New Mexico.In that case, “the US District Court for the District of New Mexico issued a preliminary injunction against the University of New Mexico after the university attempted to charge TPUSA over US$5,000 in security fees for an October 2023 event featuring conservative speaker Riley Gaines”, wrote Bird.Gaines has been outspoken in her opposition to transgender women in women’s division sports as well as in chess. “As the court found, that [fee] was unacceptable,” said Bird’s gloss of the case.She went on to state that since students “may be considered consumers under your state’s respective Consumer Fraud Acts or Unfair and Deceptive Acts and Practices laws”, to the “extent that your schools advertise your campuses as open for discussion and dialogue . . . you may violate your state’s laws if you invoke ‘security concerns’ to impose exorbitant fees on student groups hosting conservative speakers or refuse events altogether, while not applying the same scrutiny to liberal or neutral speakers”.Determining security levelsEspecially in the wake of the assassination of Kirk, campus security experts are understandably reticent about detailing security arrangements.Still, Rob Kilfoyle, president of the Bloomington, Indiana-based International Association of Campus Law Enforcement Administrators and director of public safety and emergency management at Humber Polytechnique in Toronto, Canada, was willing to explain the steps campus officials follow to determine how much security is needed for a particular speaker or event while protecting free speech.Although speakers like Kirk bring their own security teams (the size of Kirk’s team and where its members were positioned at UVU has not been made public), institutions, Kilfoyle said, undertake their own threat assessment, using open source and other data.“They look at who the speaker is, what their risk profile is and what threats are currently known to exist around them,” he noted. The next step involves developing a security plan with the campus police department.According to what is known about the Kirk shooting, the suspect, Tyler Robinson, climbed up to the roof of UVU’s Losee Center (which houses student support services) and fired one shot before jumping down from the back of the building and running to a copse.This highlights what Kilfoyle toldUniversity World Newsabout the importance of the peculiarities of the location of the event. “You would assess vulnerabilities and control of access and egress, as well as vantage points,” he explained.An essential part of this is determining where you are vulnerable and determining sight lines that a potential shooter could use.At the time of this writing, it is publicly assumed that Robinson fired a single shot at Kirk from about 250 metres away using a Mauser Model 98, .30-06 calibre (of the type used to hunt deer) with a mounted high-powered scope.Equally important, Kilfoyle said, is determining control points, such as berms and trees, which could impede security personnel pursuing someone.Kilfoyle described the security on the ground as being built out in concentric circles. “You have a tight box around the protected, and then you move out; you have different layers of security in place to reduce the risk,” he stated.Kilfoyle ended our discussion by underscoring the fine line that security personnel have to walk on campuses.“You’re always trying to provide enough security to create a safe environment where you are not intrusive. You’re not turning the campus into a jail or prison.Kirk’s assassination, Kilfoyle averred, was not on anyone’s radar. It is likely we are going to have to think more about certain risk profiles, particularly when it comes to political speech. “Tensions are high, and there’s so much polarisation; that’s kind of a new risk that we will have to take a hard look at.“But protection of free speech and the right to free speech is something we are always aware of,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

美國校園言論自由面臨挑戰 根據最近的調查，隨著政治極化加劇，美國大學校園的言論自由正遭遇前所未有的挑戰。調查顯示，34%的學生認為「為了阻止校園演講，使用暴力是可以接受的」，這一比例比四年前上升了10個百分點。調查中，近70%的學生認為「打斷演講者是可接受的」。專家警告，這種對暴力或嚴重干擾的接受度可能會導致學術自由受到威脅，學生和教職員可能因自我審查而不敢自由發言。這一現象對大學作為思想市場的核心使命構成了直接挑戰。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Just transition to sustainable future must put youth first
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Just transition to sustainable future must put youth firstGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksLATIN AMERICA-EUROPEFuture HE must prepare builders of society, not just workersAFRICAPassive? No, students want to be co-creators of solutionsSOUTH AFRICAFaith and sustainability: Mosques lead in green innovationFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSOUTH AFRICAJust transition to sustainable future must put youth firstEve Ruwoko02 October 2025TweetHigher education institutions in South Africa and across Africa must play a leading role in equipping young people with the skills, research capacity and opportunities necessary to drive a just and sustainable transition.This was one of the core messages at the inaugural International Conference on Climate Resilience, Smart and Sustainable Futures (ICCRSF), convened by the Vaal University of Technology (VUT) in Johannesburg, South Africa, recently.Academics and stakeholders from the public and private sectors deliberated on climate change and sustainability to map out a resilient future that is responsive to current global challenges. Sustainability and resilience in areas ranging from digital transformation to tourism and aviation were tackled under the umbrella theme, ‘Stocktaking and accelerating action towards the global compact under the 2030 agenda’.The conference emphasised the role of African universities in advancing climate action on the continent.The key objectives of the conference were to unite climate change and sustainable development practitioners to help identify critical challenges that have emanated from the increased impact of extreme weather events amid a global call to fast-track action towards achieving the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development, Professor Kaitano Dube, tourism geographer at VUT and an ICCRSF coordinator, toldUniversity World News.“At the heart of the conference was the need to address how we build a smart and resilient future that factors in issues of equitable justice within the frame of climate change and the green transition. Central to this was a discussion on how developed and developing countries can fast-track clean energy development,” he said.Universities as shapers of transitionDuring a youth symposium on ajust transitionon the first day of the conference, VUT’s deputy vice-chancellor of innovation, research, internationalisation and commercialisation, Dr Simphiwe Nelana, highlighted the urgency of rethinking education, policy, and skills development to ensure that the green economy does not leave communities and future generations behind.“For us as a university, the just transition is not an abstract concept. It is not something happening in government policy rooms alone. For our students, our researchers, and our communities, it is deeply personal. It will define the economy they inherit, the skills they require, and the society in which they live. This is why we cannot be mere observers,” Nelana said.“Universities must be active shapers of the transition. We must prepare our graduates, not just with technical knowledge, but with the capacity to innovate, to engage communities, and to lead in building a future that is equitable and sustainable.”The conference was hosted under a new memorandum of understanding between the South African Presidential Climate Commission (PCC) and VUT. The partnership was framed as an example of how national policy can align with institutional research to deliver tangible outcomes.Closing the gapAmong the joint objectives outlined were closing the gap between higher education provision and the demands of a new economy; developing pathways connecting education, innovation, and employment; ensuring youth perspectives are central to decision-making and producing actionable recommendations, publishable research; and networks of young leaders.“This is not symbolic; our goal is to deliver tangible outputs – whether that is publishable research that can influence government decisions, or pilot projects that can demonstrate new approaches to skills development. We want to build a strengthened network of young leaders who will carry this forward, not only in South Africa but across the continent,” Nelana said.South Africa’s demographics underscore the need for youth inclusion. “The democratic reality is that most of our population is young. If the transition is to be truly just, it must prioritise the needs, dreams, and opportunities of this generation. We cannot afford to design a future for our youth without youth. We must design it with them.”Developing a national skills agendaLindiwe Johnson, senior manager for Just Transition at the PCC, stressed that South Africa’s coal-based economy poses unique challenges, but also new opportunities in renewables, green manufacturing, and climate-smart agriculture. She highlighted that the success of a just transition depends on the people’s preparedness and their ability to participate meaningfully in the new economy. Skills development is, therefore, a critical component.“If we do not create pathways for training, reskilling, and upskilling, then the promise of new industries in hydrogen, in electric vehicles, in solar and wind, will remain only that, a promise. Higher education institutions are the engines that can turn these promises into reality by equipping people with the right capabilities,” Johnson said.She said that the PCC has had small initiatives across the country, including short-term training. However, what is lacking is a national skills agenda that could provide coherence.“What is commendable now is how higher education and other stakeholders have started working together to build that coherence. Through the Just Energy Transition Skills Desk, we are working to track emerging skills needs, connect government, industry, and academia, and ensure that no community is left behind in this process. This is where the partnership with universities like VUT becomes crucial,” she said.Challenging higher education institutionsDuring the closing remarks, the Minister of Higher Education and Training in South Africa, Buti Manamela, emphasised that the just transition is not merely a technological adjustment or a matter of shifting from coal to renewables. It is a “profound societal transformation” that calls for equity, inclusion, and fairness.He challenged universities to become catalysts in generating and disseminating knowledge that informs climate solutions, integrating climate change and sustainability into curricula, and building the critical skills and capacity needed for the green economy.Manamela unveiled plans to establish a climate change and policy research centre at VUT to bridge the gap between policy and climate change research.“Higher education matters in the global struggle against climate change; it is our universities and colleges where research is conducted, knowledge is generated, and where the next generation of leaders is shaped. Tackling climate change requires urgent, bold, and transformational changes across all sectors, which need innovation, infrastructure, and urban planning.“It needs a rapid shift to clean energy and sustainable use of land and water. It also needs the social capacity to adapt, ensuring that no worker, no community, and no student is left behind,” Manamela said.According to him, universities should not only analyse problems but also model solutions, committing to carbon neutrality, sustainable operations, and community engagement.The conference was organised in collaboration with the National Research Foundation of South Africa, Great Zimbabwe University, University of South Africa, University of Fort Hare, Cape Peninsula University of Technology, Botswana University of Agriculture and Natural Resources, and Maasai Mara University (Kenya).Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEGYPTNew university branches to target region’s skills gapsWagdy Sawahel02 October 2025TweetThe Egyptian higher education sector is expanding regionally with the establishment of several new branch campuses across Africa, adding to its existing three, while it also has plans to venture into the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Malaysia and Greece.These university branches must target the most in-demand majors on the African continent, such as medicine, pharmacy, agriculture, renewable energy, and artificial intelligence, according to a roadmap for the expansion.Egyptian international branch campuses in Africa will offer various undergraduate and postgraduate programmes to extend educational opportunities on the continent and will foster Egyptian diplomatic ties within the African continent, which will enhance its soft power impact, said Professor Morad Ahmed Morad from the faculty of medicine of Tanta University in Egypt, one of the institutions that is planning a new branch campus.On 20 September, Ayman Ashour, the minister of higher education and scientific research,announcedplans for the opening of a branch of Sadat City University in Tanzania.This will be in addition to the three branches that Egyptian universities have already established in Africa. They are two branches of Alexandria University – one inN’Djamena, the capital of Chad, and the other in Tonj, Warrap State,South Sudan. Cairo University also has a branch inKhartoum, the capital of Sudan.Another three branches are now under way, including a branch ofAin Shams Universityin Dar es Salaam, Tanzania, a branch ofTanta Universityin Djibouti City, the capital of Djibouti, and a branch of theUniversity of Mansourain the Comoros.In addition, as part of its ongoing efforts to internationalise its higher education, Egypt is planning to establishfour branchesof some of its universities in the Arab region, Asia and Europe including in the United Arab Emirates, Saudi Arabia, Malaysia and Greece.Roadmap towards branch campusesDr Mohamed Selim, the deputy chairman of the African Affairs Committee in the House of Representatives,presenteda roadmap for the opening branch campuses for Egyptian universities in Africa to act as a bridge for development and communication between peoples.He called upon the government in general, and the Egyptian Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research, in particular, to expedite the implementation of the plan.The roadmap includes preparing a comprehensive map of each African country’s needs for scientific disciplines and to establish specialised Egyptian university branches that match the needs in each of these local labour markets.As part of the roadmap, a dual education system will be established through linking Egyptian university branches in Africa with practical training programmes in factories, hospitals, and research centres within the host country.The roadmap also includes launching the ‘Africa Learns in Egypt’ initiative through offering joint scholarships to African students in new branches.Furthermore, it envisages the establishment of an African-Egyptian Universities Council as a coordinating entity under the auspices of the African Union for linking Egyptian universities with their branches across the continent and enhancing the exchange of expertise.Through a partnership with the private sector, the roadmap includes to attract Egyptian and African investments to finance the establishment of branches for reducing the burden on the general budget.Regionalisation of branch campusesMorad from Tanta University welcomed the roadmap for establishing branch campuses in Africa, hoping to get an ‘open ear’ from the ministry of higher education and scientific research.“This roadmap will boost the ministry’s ongoing efforts to regionalise its higher education through establishing new branch campuses for Egyptian universities in Africa as part of its 2030 vision for sustainable development,” Morad said.“Instead of operating as isolated extensions of a parent university, regionalisation of the Egyptian higher education sector through branch campuses, differs from pure internationalisation, which focuses on global partnerships. [It is] concentrating on intra-regional cooperation among Egyptian universities and its branch campuses along with African universities in the host countries,” Morad explained.“This roadmap also puts a framework for branch campuses to work in a regionalised model through establishing an African-Egyptian Universities Council for addressing the specific social, economic, and educational needs of the African region,” Morad pointed out.“Regionalisation of branch campuses fosters student, faculty, and staff mobility, which promotes knowledge sharing and cultural understanding within the region,” he said.Egypt’s soft powerAccording to Morad, the Egyptian international branch campuses in Africa will offer various undergraduate and postgraduate programmes to extend educational opportunities and foster Egyptian diplomatic ties within the African continent which will enhance its soft power impact.Morad’s view is in line with the Global Soft Power Index (GSPI), which evaluated states’ soft power strength based on higher education and science capabilities, among other indicators, and ranked Egypt in position 38 out of 193 countries worldwide in 2025.The index stated: “Universities that open campuses abroad … contribute to educating elites, future influential leaders, and personalities.”Egypt was ranked first among thetop 10most influential African countries, followed by South Africa, Morocco, Nigeria, Algeria, Tunisia, Ghana, Kenya, Tanzania and Côte d’Ivoire.Challenges and solutionsProfessor Magdi Tawfik Abdelhamid at Cairo’s National Research Centre in Egypt, toldUniversity World Newsthat Egypt’s regionalisation plans may face some challenges.“These include regulatory complexity related to legal requirements, quality assurance, and the recognition of degrees and credits along with political instability and cultural differences in the African region as well as securing adequate and sustained funding.“These challenges could be tackled by establishing functional frameworks that focus on aligning technical aspects of higher education, such as mutual recognition of qualifications and credits, as well as creating formal networks of African-Egyptian universities and associations to foster cooperation, along with promoting private-public partnership to facilitate funding,” Abdelhamid said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal
