https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Minister eyes five-fold growth in foreign student numbers
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Minister eyes five-fold growth in foreign student numbersGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksKAZAKHSTANInternational student interest in Kazakhstan is risingKAZAKHSTANA global scholarship with outsize impact at home and beyondKAZAKHSTANKazakhstan: A bridge for East-West academic collaborationKAZAKHSTANHow an international outlook stemmed Kazakhstan’s brain drainFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsKAZAKHSTANMinister eyes five-fold growth in foreign student numbersYojana Sharma10 October 2025TweetKazakhstan has moved at breakneck speed to increase the number of foreign students at its universities, reaching 31,500 last year – a record high. However, there are even more ambitious plans afoot which involve attracting 150,000 international students within three years, five times more than the current figure, according to Sayasat Nurbek, Kazakhstan’s minister of science and higher education.The target was revised upwards earlier this year, when it was 100,000 foreign students by 2028, after record numbers of foreign students were enrolled in just a few years.In a wide-ranging interview withUniversity World News, Nurbek said the government wanted to seize the opportunity presented by the tightening up of visa policies by traditional international student destination countries. “The reason we were in such a hurry is that the window of opportunity can close at any moment,” he said, describing the opportunity to attract international students as a “once-in-a-lifetime window”.“Canada, North America, the UK, Europe, and Australia – all of a sudden their governments decided to have stricter visa policies,” he said, noting those policies “backfired, with fewer international students going into these countries. But that might change again.”The latest example is the United States, where the government has imposed a US$100,000 fee on the H-1B visa for skilled professionals, he noted.But with other forces at play, such as the demographics of the Eurasian-Asian region, “there’s such a huge transnational education market forming as we speak. Think of over a billion young people living just in this [Eurasian] region – without China”.With “a lot of mouths to feed and a lot of minds to teach”, he pointed to “constant pressure on new academic infrastructure” in many countries, “which is only growing, so we think we can grasp this growing market”.At the same time purchasing power is going up, so the number of international students is also rising, he said, pointing to education hubs in countries such as the United Arab Emirates, where 75% of students are international students, or Qatar, where 40% of students are from overseas.Closer to home “there's a conflict in the region, and Russia is not the preferred academic mobility centre anymore,” he said, referring to the previous outflow from Central Asian states to Russia. “For the first time in our history we have over 3,000 Russian students coming to Kazakhstan, and [another] 2,000 are Chinese. We never had anyone coming to Kazakhstan for higher education from these countries before.”“But the [Russian] conflict might end; you never know,” he said, adding: “I’m rushing; I’m in a hurry. I push things as hard as I can because I know this window of opportunity might close at any moment.”Branch campusesHe pointed to five standalone foreign branch campuses that have been set up in Kazakhstan and 40 partnerships between Kazakh and overseas universities offering joint programmes in less than five years. “It’s a really good result. It’s getting a lot of momentum,” he said.Around two dozen of the 40 overseas branch universities operating together with Kazakh universities as ‘universities of excellence’ are already functioning, while a further 10 are opening this year, and six are projected for 2026.The five full-blown branch campuses include the United Kingdom’s Cardiff University, which opened last month; Coventry University in Astana; De Montfort University in Almaty; Woosong University from South Korea, which opened in September in the country’s Turkestan region; and Italy’s Marche Polytechnic University, with plans to open a branch in Taldykorgan.The branches were granted land and tax breaks by the government, and partnerships with private companies facilitated the construction of state-of-the-art campuses.Others, such as the Korea Advanced Institute of Science and Technology (KAIST) and the UK’s University of Glasgow, are based at partner institutions in Kazakhstan.The University of Arizona will have two campuses, the first – with 1,200 dormitory spaces based at M Kozybayev North Kazakhstan University constructed by its Kazakh partner – is already in operation. Arizona State University also plans two campuses in another part of Kazakhstan, while Colorado School of Mines has announced its first overseas campus in the heart of the mining region. The Camborne School of Mines at the UK’s University of Exeter is also planning a campus, according to the minister.Referring to other education hubs, he said: “A lot of these countries have good income, or one of their major sources of income is from foreign students.”According to Nurbek, attracting 31,500 students from overseas in 2024 contributed over 30 billion tenge (US$55.5 million) to the Kazakh economy and helped create 40,000 jobs – all part of the country’s strategy to become a “middle-tier power” in the region.“Just the sheer branding … brought us 31,500 students,” he said.With 700,000 local students currently in the Kazakhstan higher education system, foreign students make up just 5% of the student total. “So we still have room to grow to 150,000 students – that’ll be around 20-25% foreign students. But we are going to keep it to 30% so that we’re not very dependent [on foreign students],” he said.“The Middle East, Russia, Pakistan, India, Vietnam and Indonesia are our big markets,” he added, noting that the results of promotional tours were very promising. “We have some good traction, for example, with Pakistan,” he said, pointing to other efforts to attract students from China, Mongolia and Azerbaijan.The largest number of foreign students is from India, with over 12,000 students, particularly attracted to medical programmes. For the first time, students from Asia outnumbered those from the Commonwealth of Independent States, or CIS countries, he said.BottlenecksBut with such fast expansion, bottlenecks have emerged, including in the form of student accommodation.A report by Kazakh monitoring and analytical agency EnergyProm, released in August, identified a severe shortage of student dormitories “with many new facilities being built in locations that do not address the areas of greatest need”.On average, just under 40% of non-resident students were housed this year, but some regions fared worse.“Even in relatively well-served areas, many students are forced to rent costly and often substandard accommodation,” the report said. It predicted the problem would become “increasingly acute” with a larger influx of students.“Infrastructure is our largest limitation,” Nurbek acknowledged, but added: “We were able to tap into the resources of private companies” and combine this with public funding to build more. The ministry’s own figures showed a deficit of over 6,238 bed spaces in 2025.However, the rush to Kazakhstan may slow in the future. Previously, the ministry sought out university partners “very actively”, approaching universities such as Cardiff with concrete proposals at a time when that university was experiencing financial difficulties and proposing to lay off hundreds of faculty members, and offering them generous support to set up in Kazakhstan.Several branch campus schemes are being finalised or underway, and Kazakhstan is not reaching a “saturation point” due to the large youth population in the region. “But at this stage we won’t be so actively seeking new partnerships,” unless they are top-tier universities, among the world’s best, the minister said.Upgrading local universitiesWhile more than 20 regional universities in Kazakhstan receive extra funding under the Centres of Excellence Scheme to meet the demands of foreign partnerships, such as labs and other infrastructure, Nurbek acknowledged that a focus on foreign universities meant tensions with local universities were “inevitable”.But the goal is also “to transform our public universities into research-intensive universities. So we use these partnerships to make that transformation. We are encouraging our universities to create joint masters and PhD programmes with international partners.”Edinburgh’s Heriot-Watt University’s fourth global campus, and first in Kazakhstan, in partnership with Zhubanov University, is offering mainly engineering degrees.“They now have an obligation to transform the local partner university into an international research-intensive university, and we run what we call the faculty development programme. So, we create split PhD programmes and split masters programmes,” taught jointly by the two universities, to “share some of that financial burden”.Funds for Kazakhstan’sBolashak Scholarship Programme, which supports generous scholarships for Kazakh students to go abroad with funding for the entire programme, are being redirected from next year to make funds available for Kazakh students to study at partner universities abroad as part of the joint masters or PhD.“A certain number of Bolashak scholarships will be diverted to help these ‘faculty development programmes’ and do more with the same money.” Students will be able to spend a year with the partner university abroad with Bolashak funding, he said.“We can make sure our partners train enough PhDs under their supervision, controlling the quality, so that they can eventually become first junior faculty, then senior faculty in their branch campuses [in Kazakhstan].”“It is a very efficient tool to build this integration between local universities and the foreign partners we have,” he said.“It is most likely that partner universities use these split degree programmes for research collaborations and for faculty development.“We will use this funding tool to launch as many of these great PhD programmes as possible,” he added.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities from three countries to set up in Punjab
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities from three countries to set up in PunjabGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksPAKISTANPakistani student mobility redefining campus diversityPAKISTANPlans to attract foreign campuses in new ‘knowledge city’PAKISTANForeign universities drawn to first ‘knowledge park’Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPAKISTANUniversities from three countries to set up in PunjabAmeen Amjad Khan09 October 2025TweetIn a major development, Pakistan’s Punjab provincial government announced this month that foreign universities from the United Kingdom, Kazakhstan, and South Korea will establish branch campuses in the province.The announcement resulted from a major policy meeting on 24 July, chaired by Punjab Chief Minister Maryam Nawaz Sharif, who welcomed the expressions of interest from the foreign universities in establishing branch campuses in the province and directed relevant authorities to complete the required procedure.Four UK universities – University of London, Brunel University, University of Gloucestershire and University of Leicester – one university from Kazakhstan, and an as yet unnamed Korean university have agreed to open full branch campuses in the province. In addition, the South Korean government will establish a free language centre in Punjab to foster linguistic and cultural exchange.The campuses are to be located in Knowledge City, Lahore, where the government has reportedly allocated land and will soon issue standard operating procedures (SOPs) to facilitate their establishment and functioning.Kazakhstan’s Minister of Science and Higher Education Sayasat Nurbek toldUniversity World Newsthis week: “We are preparing to open the very first branch campus of a Kazakh university in Pakistan,” adding it would be the first Kazakh university to operate outside Central Asia.“When negotiating with the Higher Education Commission, they said they want a medical university or engineering. So, it is most likely to be Al-Farabi Kazakh National University, our largest public university,” he said.The branch campus would begin operating next year but was most likely to be in Islamabad, Nurbek noted.Kazakhstan currently hosts almost 1,200 students from Pakistan, the fourth largest group of foreign students there after students from India, Russia and China.The Kazakh ministry said Pakistan was a focus country for the Study in Kazakhstan Project, with bilateral meetings held this year with representatives of Pakistani higher education institutions, agencies and government bodies.“There is a lot of interest from Pakistani students to come to Kazakhstan, but some of them cannot come because of our visa regulations, and so they [Pakistan] said if you cannot let in so many students from Pakistan [why not] open your branch campus here,” Nurbek said.South Korea has around 1,600 students from Pakistan at its universities and is seeking to expand foreign student numbers due to a declining youth population.In June a delegation from South Korea’s Tongwon University announced plans to establish six Korean language learning centres in Pakistan in collaboration with the Ministry of Federal Education and Professional Training.These centres aim to enhance language proficiency and improve the overseas employment prospects of Pakistani students, particularly in technologically advanced sectors like automation, smart manufacturing, electronics, and renewable energy.Ambitious reform agenda“This initiative, if materialised, will help our students receive foreign-quality higher education without going abroad. This will save huge expenses associated with overseas study and slow down the brain drain, as many students who go abroad for foreign education prefer to work there,” Professor Muhammad Ali, vice-chancellor of Lahore’s University of the Punjab, toldUniversity World News.The entry of reputed foreign institutions is also expected to push local universities to raise their academic standards, improve faculty quality, and enhance research standards, he said.Senior education officials and policy analysts in Pakistan said foreign branch campuses act as a bridge between local and global academia, introducing international standards, innovative teaching approaches, and globally recognised degrees. They believe such opportunities at home would ease financial and logistical burdens for students who might otherwise have to pursue education overseas.It is part of an ambitious higher education reform agenda being rolled out by the Punjab government. The Punjab Higher Education Strategic Plan 2025–2029, a five-year roadmap produced by the Punjab Higher Education Commission (PHEC), also envisions turning a number of local public sector universities into globally competitive institutions through stronger governance, greater institutional independence, expanded research capacity, and academic modernisation.“The establishment of foreign university campuses is a major initiative to serve as [a] core component of [the] province’s higher education transformation strategy. Through this initiative, Punjab seeks to enhance its standing as a leading regional centre for high-quality tertiary education,” Provincial Minister for School Education and Higher Education Rana Sikandar Hayat toldUniversity World News.Any foreign university planning to operate in Pakistan must comply with the established higher education regulatory framework, primarily overseen by the federal Higher Education Commission (HEC) and the Provincial Higher Education Commission (PHEC) under the Transnational Education (TNE) Policy of 2024.According to this policy, any “Foreign Higher Education Institution (HEI) planning to operate in Pakistan (through their offshore campus) would be required to secure approval of the Ministries of Foreign Affairs and Interior, and respective Provincial Governments and apply to HEC for No objection Certificate (NOC)”.Past initiativesIn the past, several similar initiatives to bring foreign universities to Pakistan were announced with considerable enthusiasm but failed to fully materialise.Memoranda of understanding were often signed, and ambitious plans were shared publicly, yet many projects stalled at the implementation stage due to regulatory delays, funding constraints, shifting political priorities, or inadequate follow-through from partnering institutions.Iqrar Ahmad Khan, chairperson of the PHEC, toldUniversity World News: “Because many past projects didn’t move beyond announcements, the government is now being more careful and wants to make sure that this time all hurdles are removed so that these plans [for establishing foreign campuses] actually turn into realty.”A prominent example of foreign academic collaboration in Pakistan is the Pak-Austria Fachhochschule: Institute of Applied Sciences and Technology in Haripur (Khyber-Pakhtunkhwa province). Developed through partnerships with Austrian and Chinese institutions, this initiative is considered a successful example of how international alliances can help build specialised, industry-focused universities in the country.A similar vision was pursued in Lahore, where the Knowledge Park project sought to attract international institutions through formal agreements. In 2014, memoranda of understanding were signed with several foreign universities – including the UK’s Lancaster University.However, some academics are cautious and emphasise that the initiative needs to be based on strong foundations. Vice-chancellor of Islamabad’s Quaid-e-Azam University, Professor Niaz Ahmad Akhtar, toldUniversity World News, “Bringing foreign universities to Pakistan is a good step, but it must rest on a strong and clear foundation.”He said such institutions should be properly facilitated through smooth procedures and transparent policies so that the initiative can actually succeed.University World News Asia Editor Yojana Sharma contributed to this article.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Share of paper mill-style cancer research papers soars to 15%
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Share of paper mill-style cancer research papers soars to 15%GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsAI uncovers the industrial scale of fraudulent research in cancer science. Image generated with Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALAll academics should be scientific sleuths, says scholarGLOBALExplosion in big data-based research by bad actors using AIGLOBALArtificial intelligence: Ethical challenges for researchGLOBALWhy it’s important to know who did what in a research paperFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALShare of paper mill-style cancer research papers soars to 15%Wagdy Sawahel09 October 2025TweetA study using machine learning has revealed that around 10% of cancer research papers published in the past 25 years – including those in the top 10% of journals by impact factor – share title and abstract features with retracted paper mill papers. In recent years the proportion has soared to 15%.China is the biggest culprit, with 35% of the country’s cancer papers flagged – 177,907 – followed by Iran, Saudi Arabia, Egypt, Pakistan and Malaysia.“These results indicate that paper mills are a large and growing problem in the cancer literature and are not restricted to low-impact journals,” reports the paper published last month onbioRxiv, a preprint server for biology, titled “Revealing the Paper Mill Iceberg: AI-based screening of cancer research publications”.The authors said that to their knowledge, “detection methods based on text structure understanding have not been publicly developed”.The authors are France’s Baptiste Scancar and David Causeur of IRMAR – Rennes Mathematical Research Institute, supported by the University of Rennes and the Centre national de la recherche scientifique (CNRS) – and Australia’s Jennifer A Byrne of the University of Sydney and Adrian G Barnett of Queensland University of Technology.The study trained a machine learning model to distinguish between paper mill papers and genuine cancer research papers published between 1999 and 2024 and demonstrated that “using machine learning to identify papers resembling retracted paper mill papers is both feasible and effective”.Prevalence of paper mill cancer researchThe study found that after applying the machine learning model to 2.6 million cancer research papers published across 11,632 journals, there were 261,245 papers flagged as including characteristics of retracted paper mill papers – representing 9.87% of all the papers.There was a rapid increase in the number of flagged papers between 1999 and 2022, when the number peaked before showing a slight decline in 2023 and 2024.Flagged papers are largely concentrated in cancer biology and fundamental research, as well as in treatment development or evaluation and diagnosis and prognosis, where percentages exceed 10%, according to the study.Alarming figures of erroneous papersAnna Abalkina, a research fellow at the Institute for East European Studies at Freie Universität Berlin in Germany, toldUniversity World News: “This study is timely and very important. Paper mills are a growing problem, yet we still don’t know how many problematic papers exist across different disciplines.“This study examines cancer research and finds that 14% of papers published last year were likely associated with paper mills, and in gastric cancer research the number is over 20%,” said Abalkina, who is the lead author of a 2025articletitled “‘Stamp out paper mills’ – Science sleuths on how to fight fake research”.“These figures are alarming. Journals need to audit their portfolios to identify erroneous papers and take corrective action. The current challenge is that we don’t know about other sub-areas. But we have evidence that some other disciplines can also be contaminated.”An August 2025studyof paper mills focused on the journalBioengineered, led by Netherlands researcher Rene Aquarius, suggested that 25.7% of the 878 articles the team checked in the journal contained problematic images, said Abalkina.“We need more research to understand how scientific journals are contaminated by paper mill papers.”‘Smoking gun’ evidenceDr Elisabeth Bik, a science integrity consultant known for detecting image duplication in scientific publications and author of theScience Integrity Digest blog, toldUniversity World News: “This is a great paper, analysing 2.6 million cancer research papers using a machine learning model to search for papers that were similar to known paper-mill articles in terms of language.“This is a strong and well-executed study, showing that about 10% of cancer research papers use the same language structure as known paper mill articles. That number does not surprise me.“We have identifiedmany paper mill papers based on reused images across different publications from different authors,” she said.“But we have also seen many more papers written in a similar style where we could not find such clear evidence. These were always suspected to be paper mill products, based on similarities in experimental design, language, title structures and image style, but without a clear ‘smoking gun’.“This study provides convincing evidence of the scale of the problem. The real extent of paper mill infiltration might be even higher, because the model was trained only on known examples.“There may be clusters of paper mill articles that use different styles and have not yet been identified,” continued Bik. “It would be very interesting to see whether a machine-learning approach could uncover these additional clusters,” Bik said.Countries of flagged papersThe percentages of flagged papers per country show that papers from China were most frequently flagged, representing 35% of the country’s cancer papers (177,907 papers flagged), followed by Iran with 20% of its cancer papers flagged (6,801).Papers from four other countries were also frequently flagged: Saudi Arabia (16%), Egypt (15%), Pakistan (14%) and Malaysia (13%).In terms of the total numbers of papers flagged, the United States was second with 10,511 of its cancer papers flagged. This represented 2% of all US cancer research papers.Publishers and journals of flagged papersThe percentages of flagged papers in the top 10% of journals from the cancer research corpus by impact factor (Decile 1 or D1) show a clear increase over time. While the percentage remained low in the early 2000s, a sustained increase occurred in the following years, reaching around 10% of cancer papers in 2022.“The increase of paper mills in high-impact-factor journals highlights an important limitation of using impact factors as proxies for research quality,” the study reported.“This trend should also prompt high-impact journals to invest in implementing models such as this one presented here or human checks of submissions, thereby strengthening their capacity to detect paper mill papers.”The research shows that publisher Verduci Editore had the highest percentage of flagged papers, with approximately 65% in its cancer research journal,The European Review for Medical and Pharmacological Sciences, followed by International Scientific Information, with approximately 45% of papers flagged in one journal,Medical Science Monitor.The next five publishers were E-Century Publishing Corporation (42%), Spandidos Publications (37%), Ivyspring International Publisher (31%), and IOS Press (30%), according to the study.The largest publishers – such as Elsevier, Springer Nature, and John Wiley & Sons – have a relatively low percentage of flagged papers (around 8%). But they account for the highest absolute numbers of flagged papers across more than 500 journals, with flagged paper numbers of 39,738 (Elsevier), 39,626 (Springer Nature) and 28,200 (John Wiley & Sons).Screening cancer-related manuscriptsAccording to the study: “The rising percentage of flagged papers in high-impact factor journals indicates that paper mills have grown in ambition, and that all journals, reviewers, and researchers need to be alert to their presence.”The study model is currently integrated into the online submission systems of three journals from a major publisher and is being used to screen cancer-related manuscripts.“Authors are not informed if their paper is flagged, in order to prevent paper mills from adapting their templates,” the study reports. “This approach may serve as an example for other publishers and journals, fostering collective efforts to combat the proliferation of fake papers produced by paper mills.“We expect the paper mills to react and innovate, as detection methods like ours threaten their income.”The authors expressed concern that the rise of generative AI might further blur the boundaries between genuine and fabricated texts, making automated detection of fraudulent features even more challenging.Wake-up call to safeguard scientific integrityDr Mini Agrawal of Amity Business School at Amity University in Madhya Pradesh, India, toldUniversity World News: “The study is significant, as it exposes the alarming infiltration of paper mills into even high-impact cancer research journals, undermining trust in scientific evidence.“To tackle this, journals must adopt robust AI-driven screening tools, strengthen peer review, and promote research ethics training. A collaborative global response involving publishers, institutions and funding agencies is essential to safeguard scientific integrity.”Expanding further, Angel Calderon, director of strategic insights at RMIT University in Australia, toldUniversity World News: “The significance of this study is that it reaffirms the extent to which paper mills have an adverse impact on knowledge production.“It is also a reminder that paper mills hinder researchers’ ability to forge a career path in academia, are detrimental to fostering collaborative research practices, and place a question mark on research integrity across a range of actors who use these services.“I believe it also places a question mark on the research integrity of researchers’ home institutions,” Calderon said.“Universities need to take a more active role in advising researchers not to fall prey to such ‘contract-cheating organisations’. The extent to which other researchers bring into the open the practices of such organisations should improve awareness and restrict the spread of paper mill publications.”Editors and reviewers, too, should be cautious of paper mill practices.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

癌症研究中紙廠風格的論文比例上升至15% 一項使用機器學習的研究顯示，過去25年來約10%的癌症研究論文，包括在影響因子前10%的期刊中，與已撤回的紙廠論文具有相似的標題和摘要特徵，而近年來這一比例已上升至15%。中國的癌症論文中有35%被標記為有問題，隨後是伊朗、沙烏地阿拉伯、埃及、巴基斯坦和馬來西亞。專家指出，這一趨勢提示學術界須加強對研究質量的審查，並採用AI驅動的篩檢工具以維護科學信譽。此研究揭示了紙廠對癌症研究文獻的潛在影響，強調了需要更有效的檢測方法。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Rectors share ideas on how to ‘stay global’ in new eraGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNORWAY-NORDIC COUNTRIESRegion grapples with how to retain foreign PhD graduatesNORWAYReport puts academics at core of security-related decisionsNORDIC COUNTRIESUniversities urged to embrace global values in partnershipsNORDIC COUNTRIESResearch security rules: Is university autonomy at risk?NORDIC COUNTRIESUniversities gear up to help Ukrainian academic refugeesNORDIC COUNTRIESBoosting digital transition through lifelong learningNORDIC COUNTRIESLegislation on academic freedom set to be strengthenedFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyEUROPERectors share ideas on how to ‘stay global’ in new eraJan Petter Myklebust09 October 2025TweetHow should university leaders be preparing for the political and global changes currently underway in the world while maintaining global partnerships and safeguarding academic freedom and institutional autonomy at the same time?This was one of the complex questions discussed at the recent Nordic-Baltic Rectors’ Conference held in the Estonian capital of Tallinn under the theme “How to Stay Global: Global Academic Collaboration in Times of Counterforces”, which was arranged by the Nordic University Cooperation (NUS) and Universities Estonia on 29 and 30 September.The high-level conference gathered 72 heads of higher education institutions and 23 additional stakeholders in a dialogue on academic freedom, international networks and discussions around how universities also can contribute to better (civil) defence systems.Vice-chancellor of Lund University in Sweden, Professor Erik Renström, told the conference in apresentationtitled “Resilience and Preparedness” that geopolitical shifts, a weakened defence situation in Europe and political counterforces against universities were the new realities.Demonstrating how universities can contribute to a better [civil] defence of their countries, he set out examples of how Lund University is engaging in the new geopolitical situation.“I presented one national and one local initiative on how Swedish universities cooperate within the ‘Campus Total Defence’ project and how this is using the experiences of the Ukrainian academy during the ongoing war,” he wrote in ablogafter the event.The Campus Total Defence project involves 36 higher education institutions in an education programme offering courses and programmes relevant to total defence.A research programme also identifies critical knowledge areas in need of research and funding, each led by a coordinating institution in collaboration with multiple universities, he said. There is also external engagement between academia, government agencies, industry, and civil society.Renström also discussed Lund University’s investment in its recently launchedCentre for Preparedness and Resilience.“These presentations were met with some mixed reactions by my Nordic and Baltic colleagues; they were partly impressed by us having become so advanced in these questions, but there were also some reactions from people asking themselves if Swedish universities are becoming defence universities,” he wrote.Taking the lessons from Ukraine as a point of departure, Renström said that “keeping the universities up and running in a time of war is important to maintain hope for the future, and that teachers and students are typically exempted from military service”.He said that “clear leadership and resilient IT infrastructure for communication” are critical to maintaining operations and that universities have taken multiple secondary roles in the conflict, such as training for the medical and mental rehabilitation of returning soldiers, as well as in the “rapid innovation cycles for defence (drones), in the housing of internally displaced people, and as a hub for aid to the civilian population”.Renström said he was not worried by concerns that Lund University was becoming a kind of a “defence university”, arguing instead that it was important to keep alive a discussion on the role of universities.He referred to work on the long-term budget for FP10 (Framework Programme) in the European Union, where the goal is to strengthen Europe’s competitive edge through investments in strategically important technologies, and said that this might impact upon research funding.He said the EU Commission Chair Ursula von der Leyen has said that the EU “needs to build more bridges between civilian and military domains”.A challenging balanceOle Petter Ottersen, professor emeritus of Oslo University and a former rector of both Oslo University and the Karolinska Institutet in Stockholm, gave akeynote addresson “Maintaining international collaborative networks in an era of geopolitical tension: De-risk but not decouple”.Ottersen said achieving a balance between de-risking and decoupling from international collaboration in an increasingly tense geopolitical climate “had been a number one challenge in his academic life”.He outlined six determinants of academic freedom and institutional autonomy that impact international cooperation, and some of the political pressures universities face from populists.He quoted UK right-wing politician Nigel’s claim that “universities are poisoning students’ minds” and US President Donald Trump’s view that universities and law firms are “the enemies within” and highlighted talk about Harvard University’s decision to resist the Trump administration’s dictates.Ottersen returned to the changing situation for research in the US and the role of Vannevar Bush, a US engineer, inventor and science administrator who headed the US Office of Scientific Research and Development during World War II.Speaking toUniversity World News, Ottersen said: “Perhaps it is time to remind ourselves of the report that served as the blueprint for the rise of the USA as the post-WWII world leader in research, innovation, and education:Science: the Endless Frontier. This report – authored by Vannevar Bush – was published 80 years ago, in July 1945.“In it, Bush highlighted the importance of institutional autonomy as a recipe for progress. It would be a paradox if the celebration of this 80-year anniversary should coincide with the neglect of this principle in one of the world’s top-ranked democracies.”Heikki Holopainen, executive director of the Council of Rectors of Finnish Universities (UNIFI), who participated in the conference, toldUniversity World Newsit was important to “acknowledge the current geopolitical environment and not be naïve in collaboration”."It is important and useful to discuss these issues and exchange experiences together with colleagues from [the] region," he said of the significance of the conference, adding: "There are growing pressures on academic freedom associated with broader processes of erosion of democracy in many parts of the world and the Nordic and Baltic countries are not immune to these developments."Professor Hanne Leth Andersen, rector of Roskilde University and chair of the Danish Universities’ Education Policy Committee, whospoke at the conference on the goals and values of international relations, with a specific emphasis on Roskilde University, said the conference was “extremely valuable” given new mobility patterns, security issues, and political pressure facing the region.“Nordic and Baltic networks can build resilience and support academic freedom and values. Together, we must be able to identify academic freedom when it is under threat and educate ourselves and our researchers to deeply understand and defend it,” she said.“With new ideological and geopolitical determinants, universities can be seen as enemies from within, and at this moment [in] time, we are easily caught between security and collaboration,” she added.Leth Andersen said the conference participants had agreed that it ws essential to support international charters like theMagna Charta Universitatum, provide guidelines and build competences on responsible internationalisation, for example by putting up fair test systems and regulations that protect international students’ access to global education.“It became clear that resilience is also very much about being aware of culture and social backgrounds, and it was stressed how societal security is not only about technology but very much also about humanities and social sciences.“We see that in new centres such as the (SECURE – Centre for Societal Security and Resilience) at Roskilde University focusing on how democratic societies can maintain basic functions and ensure basic needs through governance, organisation, and management.”Conference representationStatisticsproduced by the conference organisers on higher education in the eight countries in the Nordic-Baltic region (made up of five Nordic countries – Denmark, Finland, Iceland, Norway, and Sweden – and three Baltic states – Estonia, Latvia, and Lithuania) showed that the total number of students in the eight countries increased from 1.62 million in 2014 to 1.66 million in 2023.The largest increase in student numbers was in Norway (+19%), followed by Sweden (+ 11%), Finland (+7%) and Iceland (+2%), while Lithuania experienced a 31% decrease. Estonia also fell by 27% and Latvia by 16%.The figures showed that the countries sending the most students to the region include Germany (9 199), India (8 096), China (8 044), Ukraine (4 746), Sweden (4 141), Russia (3 429), Norway (3 670) and Iran (3 650).Asked about the lack of representation from students at the conference, Hanna Kanep, secretary general at Universities Estonia, said the event was “intentionally designed to fill a specific niche: a space for university rectors to reflect directly and openly with their peers on the future of international academic cooperation in an increasingly complex world.”She noted: “We believe that this kind of focused, peer-to-peer exchange helps strengthen institutional leadership across the region, ultimately to the benefit of students and academic communities.“Just as students benefit from speaking with fellow students, rectors also need time and space to engage in direct dialogue with others in the same role – to share strategies, pressures, and long-term visions.”Johanna Kukk-Uuspalu, development and collaboration coordinator for Universities Estonia, said padlets were used to facilitate group discussions, with each group asked to identify three key points on global collaboration.“Common themes highlighted challenges such as safeguarding academic freedom, managing security risks, and adapting to political and technological changes while preserving institutional autonomy.“Looking ahead, the groups recommended that universities balance openness with caution, enhance resilience through proactive measures, and foster trust and solidarity – focusing on shared strategies for innovation, talent mobility, and regional alliances to position themselves as stable, visionary actors shaping an inclusive and sustainable future for higher education,” she noted.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: HE leaders brand Trump’s compact for HE as ‘extortion’
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: HE leaders brand Trump’s compact for HE as ‘extortion’GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsLinda McMahon’s plan for ‘academic excellence’ sparks elite backlash. Image: Gage Skidmore, Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESTexas university faculty resist ‘vague’ anti-DEI censorshipUNITED STATESIs DeSantis following Orbán’s playbook on universities?Featured EmployersFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackUNITED STATESHE leaders brand Trump’s compact for HE as ‘extortion’Nathan M Greenfield08 October 2025TweetUniversity leaders have reacted largely with derision and varying degrees of horror to the “Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education” that United States Secretary of Education Linda McMahon offered to nine elite universities last week.Announced on 2 October, thecompactis a list of 10 pledges McMahon is seeking from universities.Among them is the commitment that the admissions processors eschew proxies for race, nationality, sexual orientation or gender identity, that universities use commercially available standardised tests in making admissions decisions and that the share of international students is limited to 15% of undergraduates.University leaders have warned that the conditions heavily incentivise universities to abandon university autonomy and academic freedom – and punish those who don’t.Erwin Chemerinsky, who occupies the normally staid position of dean of the University of California, Berkeley’s law school, referred to it as “extortion, plain and simple” in an essay inThe New York Times.Michael Ignatieff, rector between 2016 and 2021 at Central European University (CEU) – located in Budapest, Hungary, where it was targeted with legislative curbs imposed by the government of Viktor Orbán before relocating to Vienna in 2019, an act of resistance – reached back into German history as he characterised the compact as being part of a “Kulturekamph, or cultural struggle”, before switching to more colloquial terms.“The guys who said that culture is upstream from politics understand something deeply about politics. What’s happening is that they’ve decided that the way to secure power as a group is to take charge of the commanding institutions of intellectual life,” said Ignatieff, who taught at Harvard and was the leader of Canada’s (Liberal) Official Opposition from 2008 to 2011 and is now a professor of history at CEU.“They believe that the elites were educated, [and] are educated in the great universities. And, if they change the education and the great universities, they will change the elites and solidify a permanent kind of domination of American politics. They have a big strategic vision of what they’re trying to do, and the compact is part of it,” he stated.10 pledges to adhere to Trump’s agendaUnder the compact, universities would agree to “maintain … a vibrant marketplace of ideas” by, among other acts, “revising governance structures . . . [and] abolishing institutional units [that is, departments or offices] that purposely punish, belittle, and even spark violence against conservative ideas,” as well as by conducting rigorous, “broad spectrum” assessments of “viewpoints among faculty, students and staff at all levels” and making these findings public.They would also commit to defining ‘male’, ‘female’, ‘woman’, and ‘man’ “according to reproductive and biological processes” (that is, erasing transgendered individuals) and sign on to the belief that “Women’s equality requires single-sex spaces, such as bathrooms and locker rooms, and fair competition, such as in sports”.Concomitantly, the compact requires that other “immutable characteristics, particularly race” do not lead to “unequal treatment” in grading, university spaces (for example, providing space for affinity groups), scholarships or other university resources.Among the stipulations in the compact that McMahon praised in aletterthat accompanied the release of the text as being part of the administration’s “forward-looking vision of higher education that serves our nation” are several that affect international education.“Universities that rely on foreign students to fund their institutions risk . . . potentially reducing spots available to deserving American students, and if [such students are] not properly vetted, saturating the campus with noxious values such as anti-Semitism and other anti-American values, creating national security risks,” it reads.Accordingly, universities are to take upon themselves the task of “screen[ing] out students who demonstrate hostility to the United States, its allies; and to provide [mandatory] instruction in American civics to all foreign students.“The pay-off is that universities which sign the compact will have privileged access to federal research funds and contracts, and their students will be “free to develop models and values other than those” specified in the compact and, by so doing, will “elect … to forego federal benefits”.Agenda ‘stinks of bribery’At the time of writing, of the nine universities McMahon asked to sign the compact – including the University of Pennsylvania, and the University of California in Los Angeles (UCLA), the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT) and Dartmouth College – only one, the University of Texas, Austin (UTA), has publicly responded with enthusiasm.Kevin Eltife, chair of the University of Texas System Board of Regents, which governs UTA, said that the university was “honoured” to have been invited to sign it.“Higher education has been at a crossroads in recent years, and we have worked closely with Governor [Gregg] Abbott and Lt Gov [Dan] Patrick and [State House of Representatives] Speaker [Duston] Burrows to implement sweeping changes for the benefit of our students to strengthen institutions to best serve the state,”Eltife said.“As these pages have shown, among these changes are banning diversity, equity and inclusion (DEI) offices and programmes, ending tenure protections, etcetera. Today, we welcome the new opportunity presented to us, and we look forward to working with the Trump Administration on it,” he noted.According to apress releaseissued by the American Association of University Professors (AAUP), White House Policy Strategist May Mailman reportedly hoped that "a lot of schools see that” government control of their institutions is “highly reasonable”.Within hours of the compact becoming public, California Governor Gavin Newsom issued a strong counterdemand onX, posting: “California universities that bend to the will of Donald Trump and sign this insane ‘compact’ will lose billions in state funding—IMMEDIATELY. California will not bankroll schools that sign away academic freedom.”Frederick Lawrence, secretary and CEO of Phi Beta Kappa, America’s oldest academic honour society (founded 1776), a distinguished lecturer at Georgetown Law School and former president of Brandeis University, said the compact “is an attempt to take academic decisions made by academics on academic grounds and turn them into political decisions made by politicians on political grounds – that’s exactly what the theory of academic freedom was designed to prevent”.The federal government, he continued, could decide to stop funding higher education entirely. “What they can’t do is say we will fund but only on these following conditions, which include what you say, what you don’t say, what you teach and what you don’t teach,” Lawrence toldUniversity World News.The AAUP, which for more than a century has been instrumental in defining academic freedom and America’s all-but-unique system of shared academic governance, held little back. Trump’s “offer to give preferential treatment to colleges and universities in exchange for allegiance to a partisan ideological agenda stinks of favouritism, patronage, and bribery,” it said in a press release.The compact, AAUP said, “would steer taxpayer money in ways that violate core principles of US higher education … They would reward campuses that toe the party line … In doing so, it would commit the very viewpoint discrimination it [the compact] claims to redress.”Withconsiderable understatement, the American Association of Colleges and Universities (AAC&U) said the compact “is not constructive engagement” and said as “stewards of America’s system of higher education, college and university presidents cannot bargain with the essential freedom of colleges and universities to determine, on academic grounds, whom to admit, and what is taught, how and by whom” – a gloss on a number of Supreme Court decisions that have defined academic freedom dating back more than half a century.The stakes, the AAC&U declares, “could not be higher”, while Ted Mitchell, president of theAmerican Council on Education, called it a “Faustian bargain”, which casts Trump as Mephistopheles, not far from arecent postingon Truth Social showing him as a minion of the Grim Reaper.Compact dubbed ‘flatly unconstitutional’The Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE), a conservative academic freedom watchdog group (partially funded by libertarian and conservativedonors, including the Charles G Koch Foundation), has also criticised the compact, including the part that says university employees must refrain from “actions or speech related to politics”.“If the language merely barred high-ranking employees from engaging in partisan political activity on behalf of the university, it would reflect existing and generally permissible IRS restrictions,” said FIRE’s press release.“But the compact’s reported wording goes further by suggesting a blanket prohibition on all staff engaging in political speech,” FIRE said.“For public institutions, that is deeply problematic. Public university faculty have the First Amendment right to speak about politics in their teaching and scholarship. Outside of their official duties, faculty and non-faculty university employees retain full First Amendment rights to speak off-the-clock as private citizens on matters of public concern. Banning them from doing so would be flatly unconstitutional.”Connor Murnane, FIRE’s campus advocacy chief of staff, toldUniversity World Newsthe requirement that standardised tests be used for admissions “that probably should not be mandated by the government.”Concerning also is the section titled “Marketplace of Ideas & Civil Discourse” and, specifically, the sentence: “Signatories commit themselves to revise governance structures as necessary to create an environment, including but not limited to transforming or abolishing institutional units that purposely punish, belittle, and even spark violence against conservative ideas.”The locution is of concern for a number of reasons. One, mentioned by Ignatieff, is that there is simply no evidence that these words “purport to describe something occurring, which, to my knowledge, is not occurring anywhere in American universities. It’s pure invention, a malicious invention, whose purpose is to kind of create imaginary facts that can then justify this kind of stuff”, meaning the apparatus of the compact.Further, while the sentence does not cite the agreements that Harvard, Columbia and other universities reached under duress with the government that required the disbanding of their DEI offices and programmes, and the restructuring of their Middle East departments, the compact makes clear that that is what the administration expects.According to Murnane, the issue boils down to “targeting departments based on their viewpoint” and, hence, offends the Supreme Court’s definition of academic freedom. Further, he said, placing “belittle” between “punish” and “spark[ing] violence” conflates constitutionally protected speech.“Criticising political views isn’t violence,” Murnane said, before asking rhetorically, “How can you have a political science class? How can you have a real history class?”This sentence also drew the attention of Liviu Matei, head of the School of Education, Communication and Society at King’s College (London), who was provost of Central European University (CEU) when Prime Minister Viktor Orbán’s attacks on it forced the university to move from Budapest to Vienna. Matei told University World News the sentence was one example of many in the compact in which language is “corrupted”.The compact proclaims its support for academic freedom and the marketplace of ideas; this last being a metaphor used in a number of theSupreme Courtrulings that increased the bounds of academic freedom. But, Matei said, despite this rhetoric, the compact calls for the establishment of regulations that would undercut the marketplace of ideas by “protect[ing] the freedom of speech of particular categories [that is, conservatives]”.“It’s not about the universities [being] a space for [the] creation of knowledge, not even the free market of ideas,” said Matei. “It’s simply saying, you are going to police particular positions,” which, thanks to the viewpoint monitoring reports, administrators and government officials will be able to track and, presumably, act on, to correct injustices they perceive.US politicians ‘copying Orbán’s tactics’That part of the compact echo Orbán’s bringing Hungary’s universities to heel is hardly surprising given that, as Ignatieff puts it, “Orbán taught a whole generation of American politicians [including Florida’s Republican Governor Ron DeSantis] how profitable it was to go after a university. He’s the founding father of this kind of politics,” said Ignatieff.While still being considered for the position of Trump’s 2024 running mate, (Republican) senator from Ohio, now Vice-President JD VancetoldCBS’s “Face the Nation”: “We could learn from” Orbán.“On the university principle, the idea that taxpayers should have some influence in how their money is spent at these universities, it's a totally reasonable thing, and I do think that he's made some smart decisions there that we could learn from in the United States,” said Vance, referring to Orbán.“Universities are part of a social contract in this country. They educate our children. They produce important intellectual property. They get a lot of money because of it, but if they're not educating our children well, and they're layering the next generation down in mountains of student debt, then they're not meeting their end of the bargain.“I think it's totally reasonable to say there needs to be a political solution to that problem,” said Vance.Ignatieff focused in on the financial point Vance raised and pointed to the compact’s statement that tells universities they are “free to develop models and values other than those below” but at the cost of federal monies, to underscore the point that defenders of academic freedom have not talked about a major issue, “Does he who pays the piper call the tune?”“They’ve not dealt with the dependence of American higher education on federal funding,” which opened the door to the government saying, “We are going to use federal expenditure across the board in universities to bring you into line as part of theKulturkamphto change the composition of the American elite,” said Ignatieff.Attack on scienceThere are specific parts of the compact, Matei noted, that are ripped straight from Orbán’s playbook.“In Hungary, there was a blatant disregard for the value of science, primarily also the value of advanced education,” he said, which distinguishes it from China: “Xi Jinping doesn’t care about democracy, but he cares about science.“The United States is, by contrast, closer to Hungary. Trump doesn’t care about science, and what this shows is he doesn’t care about the country. He doesn’t care about America,” said Matei, who added that America’s military and economic power was “based to a large extent on science, on research and advanced education”.The attack on science, Matei explained, is evident in the compact’s new regulation that international students make up no more than 15% of a university’s undergraduate student population and that no more than 5% shall be from any one country.Since students from China and India – who pay much higher tuition than do domestic students – make up most students in engineering faculties and STEM programmes across the US, this regulation would make it extremely difficult, if not impossible, for many of these programmes to survive.Further, this regulation would cut the ‘pipeline’ through which many of the best of these students go on to masters and PhD programmes (in both providing the backbone of America’s research laboratory labour) and then on to becoming research professors themselves.Accordingly, Matei said the compact’s very title inverts the normal meaning of “academic excellence”.Rather than promoting excellence, in addition to infringing on the university’s right to determine who can study on their campuses, these restrictions will, Matei said, “backfire” on America because it “will lose the capacity to recruit excellent people from wherever they are”.Jonathan Becker, vice-chancellor for the Global Higher Education Alliance for the 21st Century; executive vice-president and vice-president for academic affairs and director of the Center for Civic Engagement at Bard College (Annandale-on-Hudson, NY), cast part of his response to the compact in terms of dollars and cents: “The reduction of international students to 15% is really corrosive. The United States has an amazingly valuable export, and that's education.“That is people coming to this country and paying for it, right? And especially on the graduate level, you have many programmes which would not exist. You know, it's a nearly US$50 billion a year industry, and why the government would want to assault a US$50 billion a year industry is not clear to me.”Another example of the corruption of language is the requirement that universities “provide instruction in American civics to all foreign students”, despite the compact’s purported claims to support academic freedom; Ignatieff described the compact as “weaponising” the term “academic freedom” and turning it against itself.For, as Lawrence explained, universities are free to require a civics class as a core requirement for a degree. “But that’s not a government decision. When the government decides what shall be taught at universities, that is a one-way ticket to undoing the public good that universities can do for training enquiring and free-thinking minds.”While the compact is silent on the details of such courses, it provides a broad outline when it says that international students, “if not properly vetted, saturate … the campus with noxious values such as anti-Semitism and other anti-American values” and when it says that, presumably in the admissions process, universities are “to screen out students who demonstrate hostility to the United States”.Committee to foster ‘patriotic education’A few weeks before publishing the compact, McMahon announced the formation of a committee, made up of well-known conservatives and evangelical educators, to foster “patriotic education” as part of the 250th anniversary celebrations of the United States next July.“Patriotic education presents American history in a way that is accurate, honest, and inspiring,” said McMahon in apress release.“It emphasises a unifying and uplifting portrayal of the nation's founding ideals [and] highlights the progress the United States has made in living up to those principles.”The committee has not reported yet, but the trajectory of the administration’s thoughts on history, national identity and academic freedom was laid out in Trump’sexecutive ordertitled “Restoring Truth and Sanity to American History”.After listing a number of exhibits to be removed by presidential fiat – including one that shows how “sculpture has been a powerful tool in promoting scientific racism” and claims that the United States has “used race to establish and maintain systems of power, privilege, and disenfranchisement” – the executive order ends with a section titled “Celebrating American Greatness” that will “build … a sense of national pride.”As part of instilling what Trump calls “pride”, a number of plaques and exhibits dealing with slavery have been removed or changed over the past two months. At Mount Vernon, George Washington’s estate, information showing that when he died the nation’s first presidentowned almost 400 enslaved persons, has been removed from public display.Such tendentious historical narratives under the rubric of “academic freedom” are an American version of what Orbán accomplished in 2010, Matie explained, by using his super-majority in the parliament to “modify the constitution of the country to say that “academic freedom and autonomy are subordinated to the principles of national pride and government control”.A few moments later, Matei went further and said that international students studying in the Soviet Union were also required to take mandatory civics/history classes.What is to be done?When asked “What is to be done?” to oppose the wholesale takeover of American higher education McMahon envisages, the individuals interviewed for this article made the same point many of the organisations that criticised the compact did.Lawrence toldUniversity World Newsthat universities and colleges not only have to do a better job explaining to the public and business why academic freedom is important for society, how it benefits, via, for example, inventions, but also America’s academic leaders must do something out of character and stand together.“This is hard, but I think it is very important at this moment for universities to stand together. It is very important for universities to take a deep breath and not succumb to unconstitutional conditions being forced upon them because I do believe there will be an end to this. But if some universities collaborate and some don’t, then the resistance will break down.”Matei fears that the substance of the compact is likely to embolden others like Argentina’s president and Trump acolyte Javier Milei, who, asdiscussed inUniversity World Newsthis week, is eroding the foundations of universities and research.But he also thinks it signals that “Trump and his allies don't have all the power they thought they had to impose their models and rules on universities” – and that is why he believes concerted opposition could stymie Trump.Unlike Orbán in Hungary, who changed the constitution “every time he wanted to do something new about higher education”, Trump cannot do that. Nor does Trump fully control the judiciary.Instead, “to go ahead with this programme, the compact shows, he is trying to get some kind of voluntary submission. In this way, indirectly, it acknowledges the limited power his administration has. And also the fact that universities have more leverage than they think,” said Matei.“I think we have learnt from experience that you cannot compromise as a higher education sector or as a university with guys like Hitler, Orbán or Trump. You cannot win. So you better resist.“You may not win. And there will be a price. There will be a cost to resisting. But you cannot win if you try to compromise,” Matei said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
