PositionsAFRICAScience granting councils poised to advance African researchClemence Manyukwe08 October 2025TweetAfrican Science Granting Councils (SGCs) have committed themselves to collaborating across borders, advancing science and technology for Africa’s development and pushing for increased government funding while supporting the implementation of the African Union’s (AU) Science, Technology and Innovation Strategy for Africa (STISA-2034).Theresolutionswere reached at the conclusion of the 2025 Science Granting Councils Initiative in Sub-Saharan Africa (SGCI)All Partners Forumand STISA 2034 Advocacy Meeting which ended on 19 September and was attended by representatives of 20 African Science Granting Councils, the African Union Commission, and funders, among others.The science forum took place 10 years after the launch of the SGCI, which aims to strengthen the capacities of SGCs in Sub-Saharan Africa to improve research management, strengthen regional collaboration, and advance gender equality and inclusion.In their communique, the science councils said they were committed to developing flagship multi-country research calls under the SGCI that align with STISA 2034 priorities, including agriculture, health, environment and information and communications technology (ICTs).Financing mechanismsThey said they would encourage African governments, development banks, private-sector partners and donors to mobilise additional resources, including innovative financing mechanisms such as public-private partnerships and blended finance to achieve Africa’s development agenda.Furthermore, they would advocate for the creation of dedicated national and regional STI funds that can leverage external resources and co-finance large-scale AU-STISA 2034-aligned continental projects, uphold and promote equity and inclusion in research funding to advance gender equity, youth engagement, linguistic diversity and participation of marginalised groups in STI systems.AU-STISA 2034 objectives include strengthening research and development capacity, establishing robust science, technology and innovation infrastructure, building technical competencies, promoting innovation and entrepreneurship, and creating an enabling environment for STI-led initiatives.In their communique, the science councils said their declaration reaffirms the central role of African SGCs in driving the implementation of the AU-STISA 2034 and their continued key role in transforming the STI landscape on the continent.“[We] acknowledge that achieving the vision of STISA-2034 requires significantly increased and sustained investment in research and innovation. We commit to working with African governments to progressively raise domestic funding towards 1% of GDP for R&D [research and development] by 2034 and commit ourselves to demonstrating the impact of research and innovation to justify increased public investment,” reads part of the communique.“[We] recognise that most STI funding currently comes from outside the continent and is often directed towards thematic areas that do not fully align with the AU-STISA 2034 priorities. For example, external funding to engineering and manufacturing, which are critical pillars for industrialisation, is disproportionately low. We reaffirm the need to advocate for better alignment of external resources with Africa’s strategic priorities, aiming to double investment in engineering/manufacturing research by 2034,” it said.Council allianceThe councils said the SGCI was preparing to launch its third phase (2026-30), which establishes the SGCI Alliance, a councils-led coordination mechanism to respond to the increasing opportunities of Africa’s STI landscape, including a youthful population eager to innovate, a vibrant start-up ecosystem, growing digital infrastructure, and increased political recognition of the role of STI in driving development.In an interview withUniversity World News, Professor of Science and Innovation policy at the Graduate School of Technology Management at the University of Pretoria, John Mugabe, who attended the science forum, said it is important to note that the communique from the Accra event had two bold decisions: the first one being the decision to strengthen the SGCI by transforming it into an alliance, which is a more recognisable legal person. He said the alliance would have more members from across the continent.“The second bold move is the SGCI’s commitment to engage in the implementation of STISA 2034. It is the biggest African constituency funding STI, and its support will enable the AU Commission and African Union Development Agency-NEPAD [New Partnership for Africa’s Development] to fast-track the implementation of STISA 2034,” said Mugabe.He said that, following this meeting, science granting councils have an opportunity to influence the development of the STISA 2034 implementation plan and are likely to have greater ownership of the strategy than before.“Another aspect is the emerging link between science financing and STI policy (largely in STISA 2034). Previously, STISA was largely discussed and implemented with STI policy bodies with little input and-or engagement of the funding bodies or mechanisms. Having the SGCI annual meeting together with STISA 2034 may strengthen the nexus between STI policy and STI financing,” Mugabe added.Another expert who attended the science forum, Dr Cephas Mensah, head of research in Ghana’s Science Granting Council, toldUniversity World Newsin an interview that the initiative started with 17 SGCs, with the number increasing to the current 20. The discussion now is how the councils can extend to all African countries so that states can be helped to strengthen their national science systems to achieve a vision where science is the enabler or pillar for economic transformation in African countries.He said the SGCI has made a number of impacts in Ghana.These include the ability to manage science grants and entrench good financial grant practices, with the later initiative being initiated by the African Academy of Sciences.“The other aspect is that each and every country has its own national priorities. We developed a research and innovation management framework. The research management framework looks at how our research needs are tailored to our national science priorities in terms of issues of gender equality and inclusivity, in terms of issues of looking at investment in R&D itself, and in terms of our public private partnership,” said Mensah.He said there has been collaboration between science councils in Africa and they have carried out joint research projects. There have also been reciprocal visits to learn best practices from each other. Through the SGCI, he added, Ghana has now taken steps to operationalise its National Research Fund to provide funds to support research in all fields of national endeavour.Mensah said the All Partners Forum and STISA 2034 Advocacy Meeting reflected on progress made in the past 10 years, covering phase one and phase two.During that period, science councils received support to digitalise grant management practices, develop monitoring, evaluation and learning (MEL) frameworks, implement gender equality and inclusivity initiatives, and gain access to other global networks like the Global Research Council, among others, he added.“The next phase of the SGCI, 2026-30 is Phase III, focusing on grant administration and management, policy alignment and integration and capacity-building targeted at the councils,” said Mensah.Influence within governmentAhead of the science forum in Ghana,Dr Nicholas Odongo, who leads the implementation of SGCI projects to support science granting councils on STI policy, impact assessments research and innovation management as well as work on STISA-2034, wrote onsocial mediathat SGCI’s support for SGCs has turned them from mere administrators into strategic drivers of national STI agendas, empowering them to lead policy and grant processes.“Partnerships between SGCs and technical advisers have proven vital, creating solutions that are both expert-led and contextually relevant, a model for addressing Africa’s fragmented STI systems,” wrote Odongo.“By helping SGCs build robust data systems, the SGCI has enabled evidence-based policy-making, strengthening their influence within governments.”Odongo said the initiative has highlighted the need for stronger connections between research, policy, and society to ensure that scientific findings are put into practice.He said joint funding schemes and research partnerships have amplified Africa’s impact on global challenges, demonstrating the power of continental collaboration.He also said the future success of the initiatives depends on domestic buy-in and diversified funding, moving away from donor dependency towards self-reliance.“Key Takeaway: The SGCI’s journey shows that building strong, connected, and inclusive STI systems is possible in Africa. This requires patient development, smart partnerships, and a commitment to creating a sustainable, locally owned future for science and innovation,” wrote Odongo.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 非洲科學贈款委員會推進科研合作 近日，非洲科學贈款委員會（SGC）在2025年科學贈款委員會倡議的全體會議上重申了加強跨國合作的決心，旨在推動非洲的科學與技術發展。與會的20個非洲科學贈款委員會的代表達成共識，支持非洲聯盟的科學、技術與創新戰略（AU-STISA 2034），並呼籲各國政府增加科研資金，尤其是農業、健康、環境和信息通信技術等重點領域。該倡議將轉型為SGCI聯盟，強化科學資助與政策之間的聯繫，促進非洲科學體系的可持續發展。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: University of Hong Kong to set up branch campus in EuropeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINAChinese universities told to step up moves to ‘go global’CHINA-HUNGARYRow over plan for huge Fudan University campus in BudapestSWEDENConfucius institutions close as China relations deteriorateFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHONG KONG-EUROPEUniversity of Hong Kong to set up branch campus in EuropeYojana Sharma08 October 2025TweetOne of Asia’s top-ranked institutions, the University of Hong Kong (HKU), is expanding into Europe with a new branch campus in Barcelona, according to recentnews reportsfrom the Spanish city, which identified the university’s acquisition of a large building in the city’s technology district.“Information is really scarce, at least on the Spanish side, because this news didn’t come out until three, four weeks ago, and it was very hush-hush business,” Shiany Pérez-Cheng, China analyst and former lecturer at the University of Salamanca, Spain, toldUniversity World News.“For now, the only news is that it is happening and that Hong Kong University will be open soon in Barcelona, but there hasn’t been any comment or political opinion. Nobody is talking about this,” Pérez-Cheng noted.In response to enquiries, HKU said only that it would share more information in “future announcements”.What seems clear thus far is that HKU’s Faculty of Economics and Business – HKU’s business school – recently finalised the purchase of a newly constructed building in Barcelona for HK$313 million (US$40.2 million) and has had strong backing from the Barcelona city council’s investment office to set up a campus. The campus is billed by property companies in Barcelona as HKU’s “first European campus”.Political approvalWhile HKU already has a joint exchange programme with Spain, the new HKU campus goes “beyond having joint programmes in Spain between a Spanish university and a Chinese one. We are directly getting to the point where we have a Chinese university that’s going to be implanted here in Spain. So this is also a first for Spain”, Pérez-Cheng said.Others noted that the decision to set up a branch campus in Europe could not be made solely by the Hong Kong institution.“We know that the political oversight of Hong Kong University is Beijing,” Pérez-Cheng added, noting the political nature of the decision to host a branch campus.“The Spanish government is in a honeymoon with Beijing and is in dealings with [Chinese technology company] Huawei on critical infrastructures. But there haven’t been any articles or op-eds criticising this [HKU] deal,” Pérez-Cheng stated.Hernán Alberro, a member of the China in the World Network and author of a report on China-Spain academic relations for the Central European Institute of Asian Studies (CEIAS), which also maintains a database of interactions between European academic institutions and Chinese entities, noted: “I don’t think they [Catalunya authorities] would take the risk of inviting HKU without previous [mainland Chinese] approval, so they can be confident there won’t be a conflict.“My guess is that they have already discussed this possibility with the central government in China,” he toldUniversity World News.Alberro added: “The government of the Catalunya community is from the same party as the ruling government in Spain; hence, they are pretty close, and they are very active in trying to strengthen their trade, commercial investment, [and] also political links with the PRC [People’s Republic of China].”Spain is considered friendly towards China compared with many other Western European countries and is regarded as being a low-cost option for setting up a branch campus.“Barcelona is also a good place because a lot of technological companies are based there,” Alberro said, adding: “China is currently popular among Spanish business people, who praise Chinese developments in green energy, solar panels, and electric vehicles.”Alberro believed there would be minimal public outcry in Spain over the HKU issue, such as was seen some years ago whenChina’s Fudan University, Shanghai, proposed to set up a branch in Hungary. This acceptance is partly because Hong Kong is seen as a ‘cleaner’ brand than mainland Chinese institutions.Business school expansionHKU’s business school has campuses in China’s capital, Beijing, and in China’s technology hub, Shenzhen. The HKU business school is also in the midst of a major expansion plan, constructing a large new complex in Hong Kong itself to position itself as a ‘global business school’ in the city.There have already been contacts and exchange programmes between HKU and Barcelona University as well as between Hong Kong’s Lingnan University and the Autonomous University of Barcelona, with a joint masters degree in economic analysis involving one year in Hong Kong and one year in Barcelona operating since 2017.“I think they're trying to get MBA students from Spain, even Latin America, who are interested in the future to develop trade or economic relations with China,” said Pérez-Cheng.“There is a growing interest in Spain around the opportunity to go to study in China. And if there is this possibility in Spanish universities with joint programmes or even [to study] directly for Spanish students and also Latin American students, then I think that there's a market for it,” she said.“The Chinese are also really interested in Spain as a nexus for North Africa, for [the rest of] Europe and for Latin America,” she added.Risky policiesIn a recent article for CEIAStitledSpain: Bridges and blind spots in the relationship between Spanish universities and China, Alberro pointed out that Spain now hosts a dozen Confucius Institutes, with around a quarter of Spanish universities having one.Pérez-Cheng also noted that engagement between Spanish universities and Chinese universities has grown and has begun to extend to Hong Kong, suggesting China could be “using the Hong Kong brand so as to not spark any red lights”.She noted that elsewhere in Europe several countries have been progressivelyclosing Confucius Institutes. “As the agreements ran out, they didn't renew them. But in the past two years, two Spanish universities opened Confucius Institutes: at Seville University last year and [at] my hometown university, Salamanca, this year.“While the rest of Europe is considering derisking or derisking policies – though not to the point of decoupling – Spain is doing the opposite,” she pointed out.Alberro noted from his research that most Spanish universities do not have risk-mitigation policies in place, even when they say they do. He contrasted this with other EU countries, noting that Spain has not implemented the EU’s ‘blacklist’ of Chinese technology companies.According to incomplete statistics,22 Chinese universities have set up campuses overseas, the majority located in Asia.In 2018, the Shenzhen-based Peking University HSBC Business School opened a branch in the English city of Oxford. However, it does not have a permanent student body in Oxford but rather hosts visiting students – around 20-30 a year – from the main campus in Shenzhen.Several Hong Kong public universities operate branch campuses on the Chinese mainland, but none elsewhere.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

香港大學在歐洲設立分校的計劃近日獲得確認，將在巴塞隆納的科技區建立校區。該校已以約3.13億港元（約4020萬美元）購得一棟新建築，並獲得巴塞隆納市政府的支持。這將是香港大學在歐洲的首個校區，旨在促進中西學術交流。

專家指出，此項目不僅是香港大學的擴展，也是中西關係的體現，因其背後涉及北京的政治考量。巴塞隆納的科技環境和對中國的友好政策，為這一計劃提供了良好的背景。香港大學的商學院希望吸引來自西班牙及拉丁美洲的MBA學生，促進貿易和經濟關係。

Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMOROCCO-MAURITANIAMauritania set to host its first Moroccan branch campusWagdy Sawahel07 October 2025TweetThe Moroccan School of Engineering Sciences (L’École Marocaine des Sciences de l’Ingénieur – EMSI) is to open a branch in the capital of Mauritania, Nouakchott, marking a milestone in Mauritania-Morocco bilateral academic cooperation and offering a model for intra-Africa higher education cooperation.Founded in 1986, EMSI is Morocco’s first private engineering school recognised by the state, offering accredited degrees equivalent to public Moroccan diplomas.The agreement to build anEMSIbranch campus was announced on 30 September, according to an officialpostby Mauritania’s Ministry of Higher Education, Scientific Research and Innovation (MHESRI).On occasion of the announcement, Mohamed El Amine Ould Hamady, MHESRI director of higher education,saidthe “pioneering initiative” marked the beginning of a new model of South-South cooperation.Hassan Filali, CEO of the university network Honoris, of which EMSI is a member,saidthe Mauritanian decision to host a Moroccan institution specialising in engineering training will provide a valuable contribution to the future of Mauritanian youth, while positioning Nouakchott as a hub of academic excellence in Africa.TheEMSIbranch campus will be the first Moroccan branch campus in Mauritania and the fourth Moroccan branch campus abroad after the three international branches of Mohammed VI Polytechnic University (UM6P) –UM6P United States,UM6P France, and UM6P Canada– were established to act as a bridge to link Morocco with Africa, Europe and North America.National plansThe establishment of Morocco’s EMSI branch aligns with the Mauritanian government’s five-year implementation plan for research and innovation and its 10-year strategy for higher education, which focuses on making research and innovation a lever for socio-economic transformation and producing the scientific workforce needed to develop a knowledge economy.The Moroccan branch campus of EMSI will offer high-quality engineering educational, research and training programmes adapted to local market needs in various specialities, including computer and network engineering, automation and industrial computing, civil engineering, construction and public works, electrical engineering, and intelligent systems.Mohamed Yeslem Elbagher, a former Mauritanian researcher at the University of Nouakchott in Mauritania, toldUniversity World Newsthe EMSI branch campus will increase the supply of university places and allow more domestic students to study at home. It would also help in “strengthening scientific capacity and producing industry-ready graduates and spread knowledge about innovation and its impact on sustainable development”, he said.The EMSI branch campus would also help to save students the trouble and expense of studying abroad and tackle the problem of academic brain drain, he said.“Having an EMSI branch campus on home soil will help in getting home-grown future engineers and scientists who are capable of producing local knowledge for the development of an innovation-based economy as well as the achievement of the sustainable development goals,” he said.Al-Tohamy Sayed Mohamed, secretary general of the General Union of Mauritanian Students, or UGEM, toldUniversity World Newsvia WhatsApp that the opening of a new branch of EMSI in Mauritania was a step in the right direction, contributing to the support of higher education and scientific research in the country and strengthening the academic partnership between Mauritania and Morocco.“It is essential for the ministry of higher education and scientific research to focus on leveraging Moroccan academic and technical experience to ensure the success of this initiative and achieve the greatest possible benefit for students and teaching staff as well as opening up broader scientific horizons in the future that will positively impact the nation,” Mohamed said.Student supportMohamed said there was a need for “careful planning and good coordination” to ensure the success of the new EMSI branch, particularly in relation to securing the services of qualified teaching and administrative staff.“In the absence of clarity and good supervision, students may feel uneasy, which could lead to future batches refraining from enrolling at the institute, posing a threat to its continuity,” he said.“We, therefore, call for careful planning and good coordination to ensure the success of this important educational initiative.”Sheikh Ibrahim Ould Al-Din, the former secretary general of the General Union of Mauritanian Students, cautiously welcomed the news of the campus launch.“While the EMSI branch campus will respond to our national developmental priorities and will help in supporting higher education development, there are fears that it could contribute to the widening of the social and class gap and transform university education into a method of class and community separation instead of being a tool for social development,” Ould Al-Din toldUniversity World Newsvia WhatsApp.“To deal with the possible negative impacts of the EMSI branch campus [in terms of] exacerbating inequality and class division, scholarships and financial support must be provided for outstanding students from poor families,” Ould Al-Din said.“The EMSI branch campus must also collaborate with local universities, higher education institutions and industry, open avenues and opportunities for funding, exchange and research activity, and carry out services for local communities,” he recommended.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to 毛里塔尼亞將設立首個摩洛哥分校 毛里塔尼亞首都努瓦克肖特將迎來摩洛哥工程科學學校（EMSI）的分校，這標誌著毛里塔尼亞與摩洛哥之間學術合作的新里程碑。此項目於9月30日由毛里塔尼亞高等教育、科學研究與創新部宣布，旨在支援本地青年，並將努瓦克肖特打造成非洲學術卓越的樞紐。EMSI分校將提供高質量的工程教育、研究和培訓，特別針對市場需求，幫助學生在本國獲得學位，減少留學負擔。專家指出，此舉不僅能增強科學能力，還能促進可持續發展目標的實現。

毛里塔尼亞將設立首個摩洛哥分校 毛里塔尼亞首都努瓦克肖特將迎來摩洛哥工程科學學校（EMSI）的分校，這標誌著毛里塔尼亞與摩洛哥之間學術合作的新里程碑。此項目於9月30日由毛里塔尼亞高等教育、科學研究與創新部宣布，旨在支援本地青年，並將努瓦克肖特打造成非洲學術卓越的樞紐。EMSI分校將提供高質量的工程教育、研究和培訓，特別針對市場需求，幫助學生在本國獲得學位，減少留學負擔。專家指出，此舉不僅能增強科學能力，還能促進可持續發展目標的實現。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Seychelles HE taps into the power of regional partnershipsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFRICASmall island states lead the way in tertiary enrolmentsSEYCHELLESCOVID-19: Educational institutions ordered to closeFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredSEYCHELLES-AFRICASeychelles HE taps into the power of regional partnershipsAndreia Nogueira07 October 2025TweetThe higher education system of Africa’s least populous country – the Indian Ocean archipelago of Seychelles – is increasingly reaching out to universities in other African countries in order to expand its educational offerings, strengthen its competitiveness and attract more students.Despite the fact that the 49-year-old country, with 115 islands and only121,354 people,has the best education system in Africa, according to theWorld Population Review, it has only one university: the public University of Seychelles (UniSey).That said, it also has 11 non-university tertiary education and training institutions, such as the Seychelles Institute of Technology and the National Institute of Health and Social Studies.Partnership with UK universityUniSey, currently with 358 students, was launched in 2009, aided by the University of London, which partnered with the institution to develop curricula.“The [University of] London’s courses really helped us to establish ourselves as a university and [helped] our lecturers to learn to develop the curriculum at the level of degree courses. After we stopped [our relationship] with London, we developed our own programmes,” Joëlle Perreau, vice-chancellor of UniSey, toldUniversity World News.Initially, in 2009, the University of London provided course texts, set and marked examinations, while UniSey taught the courses and managed student admissions, with London making final admissions rulings. Later, UniSey developed its own programmes, for example, in law. In 2022 those courses evolved into UniSey’s own Bachelor of Laws (LLB), aided by the Seychelles judicial system.“This was a wonderful project because everybody was involved, and now we have our own local LLB that better suits the judicial system that we have in Seychelles ... which is more similar to the Mauritian law system,” said the vice-chancellor.Today, the university offers 13 of its own bachelor degrees, four masters degrees and two PhDs in business administration and business leadership.“We are also working on a PhD by research on the environment, which is a niche area. And, hopefully, after having this framework validated by the Seychelles Qualifications Authority, we will then continue with other areas – for example, languages, education, even health and social care,” said Perreau.Such PhDs will help workers advance their studies, sometimes upon the request of the organisations they work for, without the need to leave their jobs and the country, she explained, adding that there are many in-service students in Seychelles adding theory to their work experience.Open to opportunitiesHowever, given Seychelles' small population, cooperation is still important. Since the initial collaboration with the English university, the whole objective of UniSey has been to boost cooperation agreements, said Perreau.“The partnerships are mainly to give us more opportunity for training, for teaching and learning through universities that have more possibilities and more experience than we have.”Furthermore, she said, such partnerships offer students international exposure, and allow them to study abroad for one year, instead of taking a whole degree in another country. In that way, the country avoids more brain drain.Since 2010, UniSey has been cooperating with the public University of Réunion, located in the French overseas region of Réunion, also in the Indian Ocean, helping the Seychellois institution offer its students a Bachelor of Arts in French.At present, UniSey counts 16 active memoranda of understanding with other higher education entities, and a few more are approaching finalisation.UniSey offers two bachelor degrees in information technology and computer science linked to Malaysia’s SEGi University, and a bachelor of science degree in food safety and environmental health as well as a masters degree in educational leadership linked to the University of Mauritius (UoM).The agreement signed with UoM in 2012 has enabled UniSey to train 28 Seychellois (since 2021), according to the University of Seychelles.Perreau stressed that this collaboration boosts local development because the Seychelles Public Health Authority worked with academics to review the modules of the programmes. “And working with Mauritius, the authorities here could ask for specific things they would like to be incorporated in the training,” she said, adding that UniSey is discussing further deals with the Mauritians, including around IT programmes, since their education systems are similar, and English is an official language in both nations.UniSey is also trying to take advantage of local strengths. For instance, it is trying to leverage its reputation as a peaceful country in the region and its capacity for proactive diplomacy in order to attract international students for peace studies courses.In 2011, the Seychelles government helped to mediate between parties in a three-year political crisis in Madagascar, sparking a peace deal in September of that year. As a result of that experience and after receiving help from South Africa’s Durban University of Technology, UniSey launched its own masters in peace and conflict resolution during 2022.Africa partnershipsMeanwhile, UniSey, whose chancellor is Seychelles’ President Wavel Ramkalawan, is also trying to extend partnerships with Ghanaian universities, following long-term political ties between the two countries.In March (2025), a delegation from UniSey visited four institutions in Ghana – theKwame Nkrumah University of Science and Technology (KNUST); theCentral University(CU); theUniversity of Education, Winneba; and theUniversity of Cape Coast.Perreau said that a memorandum of understanding, or MoU, with KNUST signed in January 2025 aims to promote staff and student exchanges, collaborative research and training for Seychellois in both countries, probably in IT, since UniSey has just opened a Centre for Innovation and a Centre for Open Lifelong Learning.“The universities we visited there [in Ghana] were very helpful ... with innovation, with artificial intelligence, and so on. They are very much advanced,” argued Perreau, noting that both African and European universities have proved helpful in developing UniSey.Dr Kingsford Kissi Mireku, the head of Ghana’s CU international programmes office, toldUniversity World Newsthe collaboration, based on mutual capacity-building and knowledge exchange, is part of CU’s broader internationalisation strategy to deepen academic ties across Africa.He added that the partnership, while still in its preliminary stages, is expected to focus on joint research projects, student and faculty mobility, including short-term exchanges and collaborative teaching, and curriculum development and institutional strengthening, particularly in administrative and financial systems.CU anticipates the initial phase will involve 10 to 15 students participating in exchange or internship programmes and five to eight faculty and administrative staff engaging in joint activities or training visits, said Mireku.“Cooperation is important to Central University, not only for its academic value, but also for its symbolic significance, as it strengthens South-South collaboration and showcases African-led innovation in higher education,” he said.Perreau also hopes UniSey will sign agreements with Kenyan universities, including Egerton University, in 2026, following contacts developed by Seychelles Minister of Agriculture, Climate Change and Environment Flavien P Joubert.“We can’t just go and sign [agreements] with anybody. We need to make sure the ministry, the companies … also have a say in the university we choose because we are very small and we have limited resources here at the university, but also, we have limited numbers of potential students,” she said, while also stressing that UniSey hopes to attract foreign students by expanding its study options.Since 2014, Seychelles has been sending students and teachers to theUniversity of Botswanato be trained and who then return to assume leadership roles in education services and in the government.Peter Kwasi Kodjie, secretary-general of the Accra, Ghana-based All-Africa Students Union, which represents more than 170 million students from over 80 national student unions in Africa and in the diaspora, toldUniversity World Newsthat the increasing cooperation between Seychellois and other African universities is strengthening competitiveness, both nationally and continent-wide.“These partnerships help Seychelles integrate more fully into the African knowledge economy. They allow Seychelles to contribute its unique strengths in fields such as marine science and climate change adaptation, while simultaneously drawing from the scale and research depth of larger African institutions,” he said.Kodjie said such collaboration can also boost research. For instance, UniSey’s James Michel Blue Economy Research Institute, established in 2015, opened pathways for Seychellois academics to engage in research linked to the national priorities of sustainable ocean governance, biodiversity and climate resilience.Challenges of a small island stateIndeed, Kodjie said: “Seychelles presents a fascinating case of a small island state that has worked hard to build a domestic higher education system while navigating the structural limitations of size and labour market scope. The country has made notable progress through partnerships and investments, but challenges of brain drain, programme breadth and graduate employment remain.”He warned that such cooperation agreements could bring financial risks and make a small higher education system over-reliant on external partners: “The University of Seychelles could risk being seen as a satellite institution of larger partners, rather than a hub. This could undermine its ability to set an independent academic agenda,” he warned.Brain drain remains an issue in the country, even with more higher education on offer, and international agreements might encourage students and academics to remain abroad, said Kodjie.According to the government’s 2024-29 diaspora policy, Seychelles’diaspora is estimated at between 29,000 and 37,000 people– between a quarter and a third of the resident population.“For those graduating in specialised fields such as medicine, engineering, ICT, or advanced scientific research, the local market cannot easily absorb their skills. Government statistics reveal that youth unemployment remains consistently higher than the national average, ranging between 8% and 12% [2024 data],” said Kodjie.This is despite Seychelles having “one of the highest tertiary enrolment ratios in Africa, exceeding 65% in recent years”, said Kodjie, who added that the economy was heavily concentrated in tourism, fisheries and public administration. Kodjie estimated that the total higher education student population is between 2,000 and 2,500.“Government scholarship figures indicate that, in 2022, the Agency for National Human Resource Development supported 376 Seychellois students abroad and 265 locally,” which, according to Kodjie, despite being vital to create opportunities, also facilitates local brain drain, given that the country’s higher education portfolio remains narrow, with limited postgraduate opportunities and modest research output.Still, Kodjie noted, local students view higher education with pride and ambition. “Students have consistently praised the personalised teaching approach that comes with small class sizes and the responsiveness of the curriculum to national development priorities, particularly in the fields of the blue economy, environmental science, and ICT,” he added.The sector welcomed the creation of a Seychelles Qualifications Authority in 2021 to ensure all local programmes are subject to external quality assurance and aligned with international standards.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESColleges feel the pinch as overseas student numbers fallUNITED STATES-CHINAWhy admitting 600,000 Chinese students to the US is a good ideaUNITED STATESUniversities pay the price of transnational paranoiaUNITED STATESNAFSA predicts 30% to 40% fall in international studentsGLOBALThree of ‘Big Four’ see steep drop in postgrad enrolmentUNITED STATESInternational students who stay in US land top jobs – StudyUNITED STATESInterest in US for postgrad study has plummeted, data showsUNITED STATESInterest in studying in US dropped 42% in JanuaryGLOBALDecline in interest in ‘Big Four’ study destinationsFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATESInternational interest in US masters degrees falls by 61%Nathan M Greenfield06 October 2025TweetInternational interest in studying for a masters degree in the United States has fallen by 61% since 6 January, according to data provided by Studyportals, the online global repository of academic programmes and educational institutions.The decline in international interest continued in September, with Studyportals logging 82,885 page views from non-US perspective masters students seeking information about studying in the US, a decline of 119,708 page views as compared to September 2024.In the year ending 28 September 2025, Studyportals logged 565,612 fewer page views by students outside the US seeking information about US masters programme.“This is the lowest level of student interest for the United States we’ve seen in over five years,” said Cara Skikne, head of communication and thought leadership at Studyportals, which is based in the Netherlands.“The last time page views for degrees in the US were so low was in August 2020, at the height of the COVID pandemic.“What makes this moment especially concerning is that the decline is not driven by a global crisis or public health emergency, but by policy choices. This suggests it could become a more entrenched, long-term shift rather than a temporary shock,” wrote Skikne in an email.Policy changesAccording to Studyportals, this most recent decline appears to be a cumulative effect of the drivers that pushed down interest in studying in the US earlier in the year, plus changes the Trump administration is making to visa duration and two graduate pathways: the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme and, especially, H-1B visa work authorisation rules.AsUniversity World Newsreported in March, within weeks of Donald Trump’s return to power on 20 January, Studyportals saw a 42% decline in interest in studying for a masters or PhD in the US.These were the weeks during which Trump issued a myriad of executive orders and other administrative orders, some targeting visa students who had taken part in pro-Palestinian protests the year before; others starting the process of cutting billions of dollars in medical and scientific research; still others directed at universities like Harvard and Columbia for, allegedly, allowing antisemitism to flourish; and others ordering universities to close their diversity, equity and inclusion offices and suspend all such programmes.Speaking only to the cutting of research funds, Edwin van Rest, co-founder and CEO of Studyportals, toldUniversity World Newsat the time: “Students, especially international students who are investing a great deal of money and are going to be far from home, need to know that their future at the schools they apply to will be stable. Hundreds of millions of dollars in US government cuts splashed across the world’s newspapers do not suggest stability.”In May, theGlobal Enrolment Benchmark Survey, for which Studyportals provided the analytics, showed that by the end of March, interest by prospective international students in taking their masters in the United Stateshad fallen by 54%since the beginning of the year.At the end of July, the Washington, DC-based NAFSA (Association of International Educators) published theFall 2025 International Student Outlook and Economic Impact, which predicted between a 30% and 40% drop in total international enrolment in the United States.NASA identified as thereasons for this declinethe more than month-long delay in setting up the bureaucratic systems to screen applicants’ social media accounts; reductions in the number of consular interview appointments that resulted in long wait times (in the Indian city of Ahmedabad, waits stretched up to 60 days); the decreasing number of visas being issued (the figure for May 2025 being 22% lower than a year earlier); and visa bans for 19 countries.Trump’s surprisevolte-facein late August regarding the doubling of the number of Chinese students he wants American colleges and universities to enrol to 600,000 came too late to impact this year’s enrolment and, as Studyportals data is showing, is not buoying the numbers of international students looking to take their masters in the US.Trump’s reason – that without the international tuition fees Chinese students pay, “Our college system would go to hell very quickly. And it wouldn't be the top colleges, so it'd be colleges that struggle on the bottom” – may have been crudely put, but from a financial point of view, he was accurate.It’s also worth noting that neitherhis words nor those of Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick, who said toFox Newshost Laura Ingram that if the US did not take in 600,000 Chinese students destined for the nation’s top schools, American students who would have gone to second-tier schools “would go up to better schools, and the bottom 15% of universities and colleges would go out of business in America”, convinced Ingram or Republican Representative Marjorie Taylor Green.Greenposted onX: “If refusing to allow these Chinese students to attend our schools causes 15% of them to fail, then these schools should fail anyways because they are being propped up by the CCP.”Chilling effectOPT and H-1B visas were pathways for (mainly) STEM graduates to remain in the United States after graduation by working in their chosen field. The proclamation Trump signed on 19 September, “Restriction on Entry of Certain Nonimmigrant Workers”, raised the cost of theH-1B visasfor employers from between US$5,000 and US$7,000 to US$100,000, starting on 21 September 2025, that is, two days after the proclamation was announced, which effectively priced international visa students out of the market.Also on 19 September, Jim Banks, a Republican senator from Indiana,tabled a billthat would do away with the OPT programme entirely.Banks posted on his website that under the programme, which allows almost half a million Indian graduates to remain in the United States, “corporations rigged the system to flood the country with cheap foreign labour and drive down wages.” His bill, he said, “puts American workers first”.According to Van Rest, these changes combined to have a chilling effect on student ambitions and reshaped global talent flows.“The policies we adopt today will echo for years in global talent flow,” said Van Rest. Prospective international students and their families weigh not only academic reputation but also the regulatory stability and post-graduation prospects. Right now, those factors are working against US institutions.“Prospective students are making go/no-go enrolment decisions, while current students are making stay/leave decisions. Policy changes ripple through both ends of the pipeline, reducing new inflow and pushing out existing talent already contributing to US research, innovation and competitiveness,” he said.Studyportals data shows that every type of American higher education institution is suffering declines in interest from overseas students. According to Skikne: “The chilling effect of policy uncertainty cuts across the entire spectrum of institutions.”Likewise, the decline in interest is impacting every part of the US. “Out of the top 10 states by student interest,” Skikne said in an email, “the biggest declines are in Texas, Florida, Illinois and Ohio. Out of the top 10 states by student interest, only one state has not dropped between 40% and 60%” – and that state is Pennsylvania, for which interest declined by 26%.In 2023 and 2024, 502,000+ international students in the US were taking graduate degrees, compared to nearly 343,000 taking undergraduate degrees and 242,000 entering on the OPT programme, according to Open Doorsdatapublished by the Institute of International Education.America’s loss of interest at masters level is other countries’ gain. Both the UK and Ireland have posted year-to-date increases of 16% in interest by prospective international masters students. Australia, Austria, Sweden and Spain have all seen a 12% increase in interest.“The message is clear: students still want to study abroad,” said Skikne, “but they’re voting with their feet and choosing destinations where the policy environment is more welcoming.”By contrast, when they look at the United States, “many prospective students see a country that increasingly views them as a threat to domestic students and workers, even though the data shows the opposite is the case”, she wrote.An unattractive cycleSurveying the numbers, I asked Skikne, at what point would a decline in interest and the number of international students begin to look like the beginning of a death spiral for US international education.For American supporters of international education and university administrators who have built their research programmes on the assumption the best and the brightest from around the world would be flocking to America, Skikne’s answer gives little comfort, especially because the collapsing market is due to regulatory and policy changes beyond the control of university presidents, provosts and chancellors.“I don’t think we’re there yet,” she responded. “But fewer international students means less investment into innovation, reduced programme diversity and diminished global visibility, all of which [would] make US institutions less attractive to the next cohort.“Once that cycle starts, reversing it becomes exponentially harder,” she wrote.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: South Africa-Sweden forum extended with pan-African goalsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFRICAAfrica needs to mobilise its own resources for researchRWANDA-SWEDENHuman capital flourishes in Rwanda and Sweden programmeRWANDASweden may extend partnership with flagship universityFeatured EmployersFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSOUTH AFRICA-SWEDENSouth Africa-Sweden forum extended with pan-African goalsDesmond Thompson03 October 2025TweetA three-year extension of the South Africa-Sweden University Forum (SASUF) was launched on 22 September, marking the start of a new phase that aims to deepen academic partnerships, expand intra-African collaboration and strengthen student mobility – even as international funding pressures raise questions about the sustainability of such initiatives.SASUF+, coordinated byUppsala Universityin Sweden, brings together 25 South African and 12 Swedish universities. The network has secured the equivalent of US$1.4 million in new external funding – consisting of SEK10 million (about US$1,062,642) from the Swedish Foundation for International Cooperation in Research and Higher Education (STINT) and ZAR6.4 million (about US$370,332) from South Africa’s National Research Foundation (NRF).In addition, all participating universities contribute support of their own, both in the form of monetary contributions to SASUF centrally, and in-kind through salary costs for local project management and the participation of researchers, coordinators and student representatives.The online launch featured presentations on upcoming events, funding calls, and six new work packages designed to shift SASUF into a more decentralised, impact-driven and pan-African endeavour.AchievementsSASUF was established in 2017 as a three-year pilot. A second phase, known as SASUF 2030, ran from 2022 to 2024 and laid the groundwork for the new phase.To date, the partnership has engaged more than 4,000 researchers, students and university leaders in joint events and funded 109 international projects, each involving at least two Swedish and two South African universities.About 75% of these projects included historically disadvantaged institutions (HDIs) in South Africa, reflecting a key priority of the partnership, Helin Bäckman Kartal, SASUF project manager, toldUniversity World News.Previous projects addressed themes ranging from climate change to health and digital education, produced joint publications and teaching innovations, and embedded student involvement more firmly into the network. A research-driven evaluation will now map the long-term outcomes and spin-offs of all projects funded so far.‘Glocal’ approachIn a mediarelease, Uppsala Vice-Chancellor Anders Hagfeldt said the new funding allows the consortium to “deepen our cooperation, while taking concrete steps towards decentralising the project to increase shared ownership and reach further into the community”. With what he called a “glocal” mindset – linking global challenges with local solutions – SASUF+ would serve as “a common platform for learning, action and long-term benefit”.Themes and work packagesFour interdisciplinary themes will shape the collaboration: sustainable health; green transition; migration and urbanisation; and democracy, social justice and indigenous knowledge systems.These are embedded into six work packages:Academia, Industry, Society – Dialogues for Impact:Multisectoral partnerships with business, government and civil society, including virtual Academic Industry Meeting Days (AIMdays). Co-leads: University ofPretoriaandLuleåUniversity of Technology.SASUF+ Beyond Borders:Expansion to African universities beyond South Africa. Co-leads:StellenboschUniversity and University ofGothenburg.Students as Gamechangers:Scholarships for fieldwork abroad, mobility for PhDs and postdocs, interdisciplinary hackathons and a sustainable leadership model. Co-leads:TshwaneUniversity of Technology andLinköpingUniversity.Science and Education Policy – From Policy to Action:Aligning priorities with the AU-EUInnovation Agenda, the UNSDGs, and the African Union’sAgenda 2063. Co-leads: University ofJohannesburgandStockholmUniversity.Communication and Dissemination:New channels to share outcomes and increase visibility. Co-leads: Mangosuthu University of Technology (MUT) and the University of theWitwatersrand.Coordination:Led by Uppsala, which manages administration and coordination.A steering committee of Swedish and South African universities, including vice-chancellors, coordinators and student representatives, will provide strategic direction.Pan-African visionWhile SASUF began as a bilateral partnership between Sweden and South Africa, the new phase clearly aims to make it a pan-African platform. One of the six work packages, SASUF+ Beyond Borders, has an explicit intra-Africa angle through cooperation with the Science Granting Councils Initiative (SGCI) – a multilateral initiative that strengthens the capacities of science-funding agencies in Sub-Saharan Africa to support research and evidence-informed policies.Established in 2015, SGCI has become an important mechanism for advancing science in Africa. It is backed by funding agencies, including the UK’sFCDO, Canada’sIDRC, South Africa’s NRF, Sweden’sSida, Germany’sDFGand Norway’sNorad.SGCI currently brings together 18 African countries: Botswana, Burkina Faso, Côte d’Ivoire, Ethiopia, Ghana, Kenya, Malawi, Mozambique, Namibia, Nigeria, Rwanda, Senegal, Sierra Leone, South Africa (as founding member), Tanzania, Uganda, Zambia and Zimbabwe. South Africa is already part of SASUF, and the remaining 17 SGCI members will now be invited to engage with SASUF+, with the expectation that selected universities from their respective countries will become involved too.The intention seems to be to leverage and synchronise existing funding mechanisms in order to multiply their effect and increase the impact of research and higher education investment across the continent.“Earlier surveys have shown that levels of collaboration within Africa remain limited. This was highlighted as a problem that SASUF could potentially – and hopefully – help to change,” Bäckman Kartal said.The hope is that the SASUF model, which has already proven effective in building collaborations between South Africa and Sweden, could serve as a platform to strengthen research and education links within Africa.An external review by STINT praised the “pan-African vision” of SASUF+, noting that it “broadens impact while also strengthening Sweden’s role in global academic diplomacy. The commitment to ‘glocal’ thinking … confirms the relevance and timeliness of the project.”South African perspectivesMUT described its involvement as an important step in the university’s development as a research-driven, globally engaged institution. “This isn’t just a communication role – it’s a leadership role in shaping how South Africa and Sweden tell their shared story of academic impact to the world,” Mbali Mkhize, the senior director of marketing and communications at MUT, said in a statement.Cape Peninsula University of Technology (CPUT) highlighted SASUF’s role in positioning students and researchers as drivers of change.“CPUT is honoured to be part of this new SASUF+ journey, which, not only deepens South Africa-Sweden cooperation but also unlocks opportunities for our students, staff, and researchers to lead in solving global challenges with local impact,” said Dr Tasmeera Singh, the director of strategic initiatives and partnerships.Funding headwindsThe renewal of SASUF comes at a time of heightened uncertainty for international academic collaboration. In 2025, the Trump administration’s actions in the United States triggered a wave of funding cuts that disrupted global science partnerships. The UK and several EU nations have also pulled back support for international research and mobility programmes.The STINT review commended a match-funding structure and institutional contributions from both Swedish and South African universities, while encouraging project leaders to consider how the initiative might be sustained beyond the current funding cycle – a concern echoed more widely across African higher education.Across its three phases to date, SASUF has mobilised about US$3.3 million in agency funding, excluding additional monetary and in-kind contributions from participating universities.The pilot drew US$1.3 million, with South Africa’s NRF andDepartmentof Higher Education and Training (which only funded that phase) contributing slightly more than Sweden’s STINT. In the second phase, Sweden covered 60% of the US$582,000 raised, with the NRF providing the balance. In the current phase, STINT has increased its share to three-quarters of the US$1.4 million committed, with the NRF contributing the remaining quarter.Looking aheadRunning until August 2028, the new phase will include upcoming calls for funding and mobility. With its combination of research themes, structured work packages, student opportunities, and alignment with continental and global agendas, SASUF+ seeks to build long-term resilience for university cooperation.As universities worldwide confront financial and geopolitical pressures, SASUF+ will, no doubt, be watched closely to see whether joint investment from the Global North and South can reimagine internationalisation and sustain higher education partnerships and research collaboration across borders.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

SASUF自2017年成立以來，已經吸引超過4000名研究人員、學生和大學領導參加聯合活動，並資助了109個國際項目。新階段將聚焦可持續健康、綠色轉型、移民和城市化，以及民主和社會正義等主題，並計劃將合作擴展至更多非洲國家。這一計劃有望增強非洲高等教育的研究和合作能力。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: International enrolment plummets 36% in Atlantic Canada
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: International enrolment plummets 36% in Atlantic CanadaGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsA sharp drop in student visas has sent international enrolment in Atlantic Canada into freefall. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCANADASteep drop in overseas students leaves universities reelingCANADA2024 international study permit rates set to fall by 45%CANADASurge in student asylum claims and fraudulent applicationsCANADAUniversities brace for more fallout after immigration U-turnCANADAData shows 35% drop in interest in studying in CanadaInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsCANADAInternational enrolment plummets 36% in Atlantic CanadaNathan M Greenfield18 October 2025TweetThe impact of Canada’s decision to drastically slash the number of new student visas – issuing90,000 fewer international student visasbetween January and July of this year than a year earlier, for example – has had a disproportionate impact on Canada’s poorest region, the four Atlantic provinces.The average percentage of international students in Canadian universities is 21%, according to Statistics Canada. But Gordon MacInnis, vice president of finance and operations at Cape Breton University (CBU), Sydney, Nova Scotia,told CBCon Thursday: “Nova Scotia has almost double the percentage of international students as a percentage of total enrolment than the Canadian average, and Cape Breton University was well above the Nova Scotia average.”MacInnis was on CBC discussing the finding of the Association of Atlantic Universities (AAU) report,2025-2026 Preliminary Summary of Enrolments, thatshoweda 36% decline in international students enrolled by the region’s 16 universities but a decline of 41.5% (4,200) in the number of undergraduate international students coming to Nova Scotia’s 10 universities.The decline in international students, whose tuition fees have buoyed up the finances of universities in the region, has caused several universities to restructure themselves, negatively impacted the communities in which the universities are located and threatens to undermine efforts to increase the diversity of the region’s universities. It also reflects the tarnishing of Canada’s reputation as a welcoming destination for international students.Two interconnected causes of declineThe drastic decline in the number of international students choosing to study in Atlantic Canada has two interconnected causes, says Peter Halpin, executive director of the AAU.The first, whichUniversity World Newshas covered, is the regulatory changes brought in over the past two years by the Canadian government to decrease the number of international students from one million in 2023, a number that was viewed as contributing to the nation’s housing affordability and availability crisis.The regulatory changes included reducing the number of international students by first reducing the number of international student visas by 35% in 2024, and an additional 10% fewer this academic year, and still another 10% reduction next year.Additionally, in an attempt to ensure that individuals were not using the student visa system as a back door entrance into the country, the government of Canada instituted a requirement that international students have CA$20,000 (US$14,300) in the bank that they could access for living expenses. The second cause stems from these new policies, explains Halpin.“We have a very serious concern, as do other universities across the country, that Canada’s brand has been badly tarnished by virtue of these policy changes. We know that Canada is no longer seen as a welcoming country among prospective international students,” he toldUniversity World News.Additionally, asUniversity World Newshas reported, there have been long delays in processing visas, especially in India, traditionally the country from which the largest number of international students originate.“Despite what Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada [the ministry that issues student visas] tells us, there still seem to be real challenges around the student visa processing around the world,” says Halpin.“All that being said, international students are pretty discouraged about applying to university in Canada, and again, that’s really being felt by our universities in Atlantic Canada,” he says.Universities plan for declineOver the past year, universities in the region began dealing with and-or planning for the decline in international students.In September 2024, Dalhousie University (DU), Halifax, Nova Scotia, the region’s largest and most prestigious university, forecast a decline of 20%, which was less than 1% off the actual decline of 20.9%.To close the resulting CA$18 millionforecast deficitopened in large measure by the loss of international students who pay tuition fees higher than Canadian students’ tuition, the university instituted a hiring freeze.However, theprovince prevented DU from increasing tuition feesfor in-province students by more than 2%, which was less than the rate of inflation – nor did the province increase its grant to the university.As a result, in July DUincreased the forecasted deficitby CA$2.6 million to CA$20.6 million.Newfoundland and Labrador’s only university, Memorial University of Newfoundland in St John’s, enrolled 21% fewer international undergraduates (415) this year than it did last year, which pushed its enrolment decline to 5.2%, slightly higher than the university predicted last October.In July, the university laid off 20 people as part of its plan to deal with the projected CA$6 million shortfall, though Jennifer Lokash, Memorial’s president,told CBCthat both programmes and other university functions were being looked at in an effort to fill the financial hole.“We had a significant shortfall last year as well, so we are really focusing on our financial sustainability. And enrolment is part of that,” Lokashtold CBCradio.The University of Prince Edward Island (UPEI), Canada’s smallest province, with apopulation of 170,000, saw a decline of 14% (263) in its number of international students.While the decline in international students is very concerning, Greg Naterer, vice president, academic and research, says that because enrolment of domestic students increased by 2.7%, the UPEI’s decline in total enrolment of 2.3% compares favourably with the average decline in the Atlantic region of 4.8%, and that at this point the university is stable.As did other university leaders interviewed for this article, Naterer stressed that universities are not looking at a one-year blip. There is no indication that the federal government is going to change its immigration policies, so universities must plan for several years of restricted international student enrolment.Further, as it moves through the four years of university, the class of 2029 is going to be significantly smaller, he stressed.“As we move ahead into the future, this problem will persist. The smaller first-year incoming class is going to imply a continued problem going forward. It is impacting programme offerings and course offerings,” he said.Structure of courses reviewedOne of the ways UPEI is planning to deal with the ongoing impact of smaller cohorts is by what’s called the Curricular Coherence Initiative. “It’s a broad assessment of the curriculum and structure of courses that are being offered in different programmes in a way that facilitates better student success and progression to graduation,” says Naterer.“We are looking at things like number of prerequisites, chains of courses required before one gets into the next course. We’re looking at courses with low enrolment and other aspects to make sure that the curriculum is coherent, well organised and structured, and that the courses students need and want to take are available,” says Naterer.Greatest decline at Cape Breton UniversityIn both percentage terms (60%) and absolute terms (3,226 students) Cape Breton University (CBU) has suffered the greatest declines, meaning that today the university’s enrolment stands at 4,226 compared with last year’s enrolment of 7,452, a decline of 43%.To plug its budget shortfall, since last spring, CBU has cut about 112 positions, half of which were full-time professors who were given access to a voluntary retirement incentive programme.The loss of 3,000 students would be felt by Canada’s largest university, the University of Toronto, which has about 94,000 students, but at CBU, which has about 7,500 students, the loss threatens the university’s financial viability and its ability to provide a full suite of programmes to students in Cape Breton, one of Canada’s poorest areas.As these pages have discussed, CBU is one of a number of Canadian colleges and universities that – partially because provincial grants were low and have not kept up with inflation – turned to international students as a way of buoying their bottom line.As is the case across the country, CBU can set tuition rates for international students at whatever level it believes the market will bear.This academic year at CBUinternational student tuition fees for the four-year BA for international students are listed as CA$90,832 (for four years), while out-of-province students will pay CA$43,136 and Nova Scotians will pay CA$36,900.Breadth of programming threatenedSpeaking directly to the question of what international students and their tuition dollars mean to CBU, Gordon MacInnis told CBC: “When I started at CBU many years ago, about 99% of our students came from Cape Breton. With the demographic decline in Cape Breton, you can imagine a day when the institutions would have in the range of 2,000 students, which would not allow us to maintain a breadth of programme offerings for our Cape Breton students.”As it stands, because the federal government eliminated the post-graduate work permits, CBU has closed the supply chain management, healthcare management and occupational health and safety programmes because international students were no longer eligible to work as required for these baccalaureate business programmes.Canada’s brand is ‘tarnished’Both Halpin and MacInnis made the further point that the federal government’s cuts to visa students, and its other policies that have tarnished Canada’s brand, work at cross purposes with another federal policy, the Atlantic Immigration Program, which is designed to attract immigrants to the region to counter its declining demographics.According to Halpin, 60% of the international students who study in Atlantic Canada choose to stay in the region following their graduation.“They represent the region’s future, its business leaders, community leaders, professionals of all kinds. They are a very significant impact for our region.“So, we have one federal programme that is literally undermining the objective of another federal programme. Those in government and political representatives, and business and industry leaders recognise the importance of international students to Atlantic Canada. But, regrettably, at the present time, in Canada, there is an anti-immigrant bias among the general population,” says Halpin.For his part, Naterer also pointed to this issue, tellingUniversity World Newsthat even though UPEI filled most of the places previously occupied by international students, the decline of 2.3% of the university’s enrolment in the small city of Charlottetown, with a population of 41,127, was visible.Impact on community prosperity“Definitely it [the decline in the numbers of students] has a negative impact in many different ways, from the number of rooms filled in residences to various places on campus like the bookstore, like the coffee shop. International students are very important to the economic prosperity, the development, job growth and social well-being of the university and the community,” Naterer said.And then there are the missed benefits for domestic students. “International students enrich the learning environment on campus with the diversity they bring. They help domestic students become more globally aware of new languages, viewpoints and different traditions. They also strengthen our international ties with collaborations that we have, alumni and research partnerships,” Naterer said.Outlier has French advantageThe big outlier in Atlantic Canada is l’Université de Moncton (U de M), a French-language university in L’Acadie, the French part of New Brunswick. Its enrolment of international students fell by 17% or 302 international students (mainly from French-speaking African countries).However, as Paul Ward, communications director at the university, explained, many of these students were in two-year programmes on the university’s campuses in the smaller centres of Shippagan and Edmundston and not on the main campus in Moncton.“Our Moncton campus recorded the second largest cohort, just 10 students short of the record. So, while we are feeling the impact of the declines, we are filling the spaces with new Canadian students and with new permanent residents,” Ward toldUniversity World News.“Accordingly, the shortfall [a total of 1%] was less hard on us than anticipated,” he said.The cohort on the Moncton campus includes 200 international students from Madagascar.One of the reasons U de M is able to attract new students from across Canada is because it is one of the few French universities outside Quebec; the language of instruction in its law school, for instance, is French but, unlike Quebec’s French-language law schools, where civil law is taught, U de M’s law school teaches common law, the law used across Canada with the exception of Quebec.One of the university’s draws, says Ward, is its strong immersion programme for students who want to upgrade their language skills to study in French.Read more about the overall drop in international students in Canadahere.Read more about the Canadian government’s decision to cut student visas by 35%here.To read more stories by Nathan M Greenfield, click on hisbyline.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
