FacultyUNITED KINGDOMUK expands global talent route but curbs graduate stay-onBrendan O’Malley17 October 2025TweetThe United Kingdom government has announced new rules that allow expansion of its highly skilled talent route but restrict the time allowed for international students to find a graduate-level job and raise the English language requirements for migrants to A-level equivalent.The measures are aimed at attracting the best global researchers and talent while tightening up on immigration in general, including by international students.In new rules set out in Parliament on Tuesday, the government announced an expansion of the High Potential Individual (HPI) route to graduates from the top 100 international universities – doubling the previous scope of eligible institutions – with a cap of 8,000 applications per year.This measure could increase the ability of leading universities to attract top talent but make it harder for those outside of the top 100 in leading global rankings.The number of people coming to the UK through the HPI route is expected to double from 2,000 to 4,000, giving graduates from the world’s best universities the chance to base their careers in the UK.In a press release theHome Office said: “The government aims to double the number of highly skilled people coming to the UK on our high-skilled routes, including the best researchers, designers, and creatives working in film and TV to ensure continued competitiveness in growth sectors.”The world’s most talented entrepreneurs studying in the UK will also be able to “seamlessly establish innovative business ventures” in the UK after concluding their studies while transitioning from a student visa to the Innovator Founder route.Home Office Minister Mike Tapp told Parliament that the white paper published in June sets out changes to “further support entrepreneurial talents currently studying at UK universities so that they can build their business and career in the UK after completing their course. We are therefore amending the conditions given to students to enable them to start work on their business while they are transitioning to the innovator founder route”.Changes to the Global Talent route for high achievers in technology, arts and academia include an expanded list of prestigious prizes, and changes to expand some of the evidential requirements of achievement and contribution for architects are also due to be made.Further changes to the Global Talent route will be made in 2026.Graduate stay-on rule tightenedMeanwhile, the time allowed for international students to find a graduate-level job after completing their studies will be cut to 18 months from the current two years for most graduates. PhD graduates will continue to be eligible for three years’ permission.The new rule is being written in November and will come into effect on 1 January 2027. Tapp told MPs that the change came after data clearly showed that “too many graduates are not progressing into graduate-level employment, which is what the graduate route was created to facilitate access to”.“It is intended to ensure that those who remain in the UK transition into graduate-level jobs and properly contribute to the UK economy,” Tapp noted.Finance requirements for student visas will also be increased for the 2025 to 2026 academic year, raised to £1,171 (US$1572,54) per month outside London (from £1,136) for up to nine months, with foreign students having to demonstrate they have such funds to support themselves.In addition, migrants will be required to pass tough new English language requirements under a law introduced in Parliament on Tuesday, as the government continues to tighten up the immigration system, seeking to ensure it is “controlled, selective and fair”.From 8 January, immigrants applying through certain legal routes must meet an A-level equivalent standard in speaking, listening, reading and writing.This higher standard will require some applicants to resit tests such as IELTS or the Secure English Language Test (SELT).The tests must be conducted with a Home Office-approved provider, and the results will then be verified as part of the visa application process.Application of the new language requirement to dependants in work and study routes will be included in future changes to immigration rules.Home Secretary, Shabana Mahmood, said: “This country has always welcomed those who come to this country and contribute. But it is unacceptable for migrants to come here without learning our language, unable to contribute to our national life.“If you come to this country, you must learn our language and play your part.”The net level of migration for the UK fell to 413,000 by the end of December, less than half the record level of 960,000 the year before.However, the government says this is still well above historic levels, and immigration continues to be a top issue of concern among the British public and high on the agenda of political parties.Shara Pledger, an immigration expert at law firm Pinsent Masons,saidthe requirement for international students to meet higher financial requirements from 2025 and 2026 appears designed to reduce the number of international students applying to study at less prestigious universities.“The government’s white paper earlier this year partly attributed the UK’s high net migration to the ‘rapid increase in sponsored study visas at lower-ranked education institutions’.“Home Office data suggests that UK visas for universities globally ranked between 601 and 1,200 increased by 49% between 2021 and 2023, whereas visas for top 100 universities fell by 7% over the same period.Shepreviously saidshe believes the rules for attracting global talent are geared to making it easier for businesses in the UK’s ‘strategic industries’ to recruit foreign research interns in AI as well as design and scientific talent.Louise Nicol, founder and CEO of Asia Careers Group, said although President Donald Trump’s Executive Order imposing a US$100,000 threshold for H-1B visa applications for highly skilled professionals will make the HPI visa in the UK more appealing, the situation otherwise looks “very difficult, particularly for lower-tariff institutions”.She said a hike in fees for employers sponsoring skilled or specialist workers from £1,000 to £1,320 – although it does not apply to many scientists and researchers – will make employers even less likely to hire international graduates.She pointed out that reducing the time allowed to study post work before finding a graduate-level job to 18 months compares unfavourably with schemes in other countries, which generally run for two years, further denting the competitiveness of the UK’s offering to international students.“Universities will have little choice other than to support international students in securing great jobs back home. To this end, they need access to non-EU graduate outcomes data, now available to all UK and Australian universities,” she toldUniversity World News.She said it increasingly looks as if the government considers a number of lower-tariff institutions “going to the wall” to be a price worth paying, simply to appear “tough” on immigration.“The sector must act now and focus on supporting international students to transition into successful careers back home, as the government slams shut once-open doors for international students hoping to establish careers in the UK after graduation,” she noted.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities at a turning point in an era of AI insecurity
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities at a turning point in an era of AI insecurityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsUniversity leaders must navigate the growing storm of AI and global insecurity without losing sight of their academic mission. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALRethinking student assessment in the age of AIGLOBALUniversities need AI sovereignty to protect free thoughtGLOBALThe end of AI and the future of higher educationGLOBALAI won’t save higher education. It will further divide itGLOBALAI use in accreditation can help, but we still need humansGLOBALAssessment and AI: What can we learn from other cultures?GLOBALThe future of knowledge and who should control itGLOBALAI literacy as the new academic literacy for studentsGLOBALAI in academic writing – Game-changer or short-changer?SOUTH KOREARecord research budget emphasises AI, talent recruitmentGLOBALUniversities’ focus on policing is fighting the wrong AI battleInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackGLOBALUniversities at a turning point in an era of AI insecurityAmber Wang17 October 2025TweetAs artificial intelligence and global insecurity challenge university leaders worldwide, it is more important than ever for universities to remain true to their fundamental principles, according to the President of the American Council on Education (ACE), Ted Mitchell.“Independence, intellectual inquiry and academic excellence, inclusion, [and] the expectation that education is a good to be consumed by all of our citizens – these are things that we can’t compromise [on],” he said via video link to a conference of global university leaders of the International Association of University Presidents (IAUP) this week in Seoul, South Korea.As universities face what one keynote speaker described as an “existential crisis”, university leaders not only need to reimagine higher education, but also need to do so with urgency in order to tackle emerging political and technological issues, the conference heard. It was part of the conference theme: ‘Higher Education Reimagined: Innovation, Inclusion, and Global Collaboration in the AI Era.’The 13 to 15 October conference brought together about 180 university leaders and participants from 28 countries to take stock of IAUP’s legacy – this year it marks its 60th anniversary – combined with a forward-looking dialogue on the future of higher education.IAUP Secretary-General Tatsuro Tanioka, who is vice-chancellor of Tamioka Gakuen Education Foundation, Japan, told the conference the organisation’s 60th anniversary represented more than a milestone.In many East Asian cultures, 60 years marks the completion of a life cycle. For IAUP, it is a moment to be ‘reborn’ to brace the challenges ahead, he said.Korea’s Education Minister Choi Kyo-jin told the conference “in this era of AI and digital innovation, universities must go beyond the simple transmission of knowledge to create new value and nurture global talent capable of addressing humanity’s shared challenges”.IAUP Vice-President for Asia and the Middle East Eunjoo Lee, who is president of Seoul Cyber University (SCU), which hosted the conference, toldUniversity World News: “What we need today is not only reflection but a fundamental reimagining of the purpose and direction of higher education – even to envision something that has not yet existed.”The conference theme “reflects a critical turning point for universities worldwide. While higher education has experienced continuous change, the core structure of what and how we teach has remained largely the same for centuries.However, with the full emergence of the AI era, the traditional model of knowledge transmission has reached its limits”, Lee explained.She added: “We now face an unprecedented crisis: the rapid decline of stable jobs for graduates, the erosion of the social value of university degrees, and diminishing public and private support for higher education. Universities can no longer rely on past assumptions.“Yet no single institution can confront these challenges alone. This is why global dialogue and collaboration among higher education leaders are indispensable.”Embracing AIShe added that in this context, embracing AI-driven innovation is not optional but inevitable.SCU is South Korea’s first fully online university and represents one of the emerging trends shaping the future of learning, Lee said, pointing to the conference theme of “reimagining” higher education.Her institution aims to be an “AI-leading” university, integrating artificial intelligence across disciplines, curricula, and teaching methods, while requiring all students to complete a specialised AI application track and explore alternative career paths, she said.“These efforts are not recent initiatives but the result of 25 years of continuous commitment to innovation. And they will continue, as SCU remains dedicated to redefining higher education in the age of AI,” she noted.Arturo Cherbowski, the general director of Universia México, in his address, titled “Crisis and disruptions in higher education: the need for reinvention and going back to basics”, told the conference “what is missing from the conception of reimagining education? It's a sense of urgency that universities worldwide are facing”.Cherbowski cautioned that the conference’s ‘reimagining’ theme risked underplaying “a crisis of existential dimensions”.Cherbowski, who also serves as executive director of Santander Universities, the bank’s philanthropic arm providing funding and opportunities for education, employment and entrepreneurship, said many institutions missed the transformation opportunities offered by the pandemic, reverting to pre-pandemic operations with little sense of urgency.Now, AI-driven disruption threatens to make institutions obsolete if they fail to reinvent their curriculum, teaching, and operating models.“It’s very rare, even in countries riddled with poverty and exclusion, that they don’t have one of these [a smartphone] in their pockets,” he said. “They have access to anything as a transmission of knowledge that one of their professors can give them.”Yet, according to Cherbowski, universities continue to fund bloated faculties whose skill sets no longer align with current realities. Drawing parallels with the private sector, he noted: “Most large transnational corporations in every segment now have plans in place to cut about 30% of their labour force in the next three to four years.”“If we didn’t get the warning signs before,” Cherbowski said, “we now need to have the warning signs in front of us and take them with a sense of urgency – like the newborn baby crying in anger from being born.”Pressure of political influenceBut AI disruption is only part of the challenge, as universities also face mounting financial pressures and political interference.“For the last 20 years – despite little blips and exceptions – public money for universities has consistently gone down. And this is not a syndrome of one particular country,” Cherbowski pointed out.Where public funding exists, it is also increasingly influenced by what he termed “short-term political interests” and “electoral rentability”.Meanwhile, populist governments continue to criticise institutions as elitist or detached from societal needs. For private universities, raising tuition to offset declining public support has limits, with no “infinite elasticity”, he warned.The rising influence of politics on higher education was described by past IAUP President Fernando León García, president of CETYS University, Mexico, as the ‘three Ps’ – ‘polarisation’, ‘populism’, and ‘politicisation’ – which make it increasingly difficult to persuade governments to increase funding for universities.Last year, García toldUniversity World Newsthis prompted an IAUP seminar focused on “fundraising and establishing partnerships with industry and philanthropists to fund R&D projects without depending on government support”.Emerging opportunitiesIn Seoul this week discussions also turned from challenges to opportunities as university leaders heard from Minister of Science and Higher Education Sayasat Nurbek, of Kazakhstan, who described how Central Asia is capitalising on global shifts.“Traditional academic mobility centres are now in turmoil,” Nurbek said. “There are ‘Make America Great Again’-type stories, visa and migration issues, and changing policies. For example, Canada lost 55% of its international students in the last two years,” Nurbek noted.At the same time, Central Asia benefits from a strong demographic advantage – half of its population of two billion is under the age of 25, bucking the ageing trends in countries such as China, Japan, and Korea. These shifts are positioning Kazakhstan as an emerging “alternative” academic hub, combining innovation with international collaboration, he said.A key part of Kazakhstan’s vision is embedding AI education across disciplines. Last month, the government declared AI a mandatory subject at every university. Starting this academic year, all 650,000 undergraduates must take AI courses – many developed in partnership with Nvidia, Huawei, and Google – with seed funding to support AI-based entrepreneurship for students.“Maybe, just maybe, these long-lived dreams of any academician to change the millennia-old model of education, face-to-face, frontal, with the power of his new great tool – maybe it’s coming,” he said adding universities needed to be bold and push ahead.“We're using our national curricula as a testing ground. We're bold to test new ideas, and the rationale is very simple – you can wait and see what happens, or you can run and be in the process, fail fast, make a lot of mistakes, but learn and gain that valuable experience.”He asked rhetorically: “Should we go and be bold in this brave new world, try new things, try to do very unconventional things, or wait and see?”Data from Kazakhstan’s Ministry of Science and Higher Education shows that 93 universities have integrated AI into their programmes, while 20 institutions launched 25 new AI-related educational tracks.Integrating AI – a key taskAI integration is a central priority for the IAUP’s work under the leadership of its incumbent president, Shawn Chen, chairperson of Sias University, China.In his welcome remarks to the conference, Chen highlighted that the IAUP, which “remains the world’s only international organisation led exclusively by university presidents”, is committed to helping institutions navigate change.“IAUP is uniquely positioned to set standards, share best practices, and ensure that artificial intelligence serves civility, inclusivity, equity, and humanity,” Chen told university leaders.He outlined the action required, including redesigning curricula for AI integration, empowering faculty to view AI “not as a threat but as a tool to enhance teaching,” and building alliances for ethical AI use.Speaking toUniversity World News, Chen described the conference as a “launchpad” for new initiatives to support members across all regions, including those affected by conflict or natural disasters.A key initiative is advancing women’s leadership via a mentorship programme connecting emerging female leaders and individuals from under-represented regions with experienced university presidents and rectors.“Ultimately, I aim to ensure that IAUP does not only serve as a network for discussion but also as a catalyst for measurable change,” Chen said.Entering his second year as IAUP President, he said his priorities include deepening IAUP’s global reach through regional meetings in 2026 – Kuala Lumpur in January, Vilnius in April, Almaty in June, Adelaide in October, and Dhaka in November – while addressing region-specific challenges and creating pathways for more institutions to join the network.Founded in 1964, IAUP comprises university presidents, rectors, and vice presidents from higher education institutions around the world. Today IAUP membership spans across more than 70 countries.On the conference’s opening day, the IAUP welcomed nine new members from Kenya, Bangladesh, Gambia, Zimbabwe, the Philippines, Malaysia, and China, expanding the organisation’s global reach.This article is published with the support of the International Association of University Presidents.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyMIDDLE EAST-TURKIYEArab-Turkish partnership for dual degrees, research hubsWagdy Sawahel17 October 2025TweetArab and Turkish universities are to establish a dual-degree system, a joint digital platform for the exchange of expertise and knowledge, and bi-regional hubs for research, innovation and entrepreneurship.The underlying aim is to strengthen academic collaboration and boost scientific research to increase universities’ contributions to achieving sustainable development.These Arab–Turkish partnership initiatives were outlined in the “Tunis Declaration” adopted at the fifthCongressof Arab and Turkish University Presidents held in the Tunisian capital, Tunis, on 10 and 11 October.Thecongressrun on the theme of “Transforming Higher Education through Leadership, Innovation, and Regional Integration”, was organised by the University of Tunis El Manar, in collaboration with the Association of Arab Universities and the Eurasian Universities Union.Professor Anas Ratib Alsoud, executive director of the Association of Arab Universities, toldUniversity World News: “The congress represents a strategic milestone in building a sustainable Arab–Turkish higher education ecosystem”.Professor Erol Özvar, who is head of YÖK, the Council of Higher Education in Türkiye, representing all Turkish universities, wrote: “The Turk-Arab University Rectors Congress holds great importance in terms of deepening the unity of strength in the field of higher education in our geography, which possesses a shared cultural and intellectual heritage.“Today, cooperation between universities is not just a preference but a necessity.”Özvar added: “Turkish and Arab universities, by increasing our capacity to produce solutions to the problems of our region through joint research, degree programmes, and academic exchanges, will contribute to scientific progress, cultural understanding, regional stability, and sustainable development.“I sincerely believe that this gathering will be the beginning of new and lasting collaborations.”At the congress, Dr Amr Ezzat Salama, secretary general of the Association of Arab Universities, described academic partnership with Türkiye as a“strategic priority”for shaping the future of higher education in light of technological, economic and political transformations.Professor Mustafa Aydn, president of the Eurasian Universities Union, which is based in Istanbul, stressed the need to transcend national boundaries in academic collaboration.Also, the Tunisian Minister of Higher Education and Scientific Research, Mondher Belaid, underlined the importance of “knowledge alliances” as “a foundation for intellectual and developmental sovereignty”.Abdelmajid Ben Ammara, head of the Federation of Arab Scientific Research Councils, and president of the Arab Council for Scientific Research, added that Arab–Turkish cooperation has become an “urgent necessity”, noting that the two sides have produced more than 45,000 joint research papers over the past decade.Türkiye’s broader internationalisation strategyDr Mosab Nouraldein Hamad, director of the Excellence Research Center at Elsheikh Abdallah Elbadri University in Sudan, toldUniversity World Newsbuilding cooperation between Turkish universities and universities across the 22 Arab states is part of a “broader Turkish strategy for the internationalisation of Turkish higher education”.He noted that Türkiye has more than 350,000 international students already enrolled from more than 200 countries and is seeking to raise that number to one million “in the near future”.About 36% of the total number of international students studying at Turkish universities come from Middle Eastern countries including Syria (28.7%), Iran (5.1%) and Egypt (2.3%), according to a 2023reportby Skyline Education Services.Hamad said through increased cooperation between universities, Türkiye is attempting to rebuild its soft power in the Middle East arena at a time when Arab-Turkish relations have been improving in recent years.“These partnerships will be fruitful for the development of higher education in both Arab countries as well as Türkiye,” Hamad said.The Global Soft Power Index 2025 (GSPI), which evaluated soft power based on higher education and science capabilities, among other indicators, ranked Türkiye 26th out of 193 countries worldwide. That places it fourth among Middle Eastern and majority Muslim states, behind the United Arab Emirates (10th), Saudi Arabia (22nd) and Qatar (23rd), and ahead of Malaysia (36th).“Besides expanding its joint university model abroad, focusing on Turkic states like Azerbaijan, and Uzbekistan to share its higher education expertise, Türkiye has started to set up joint universities in the Arab world, including Egypt and Syria,” Hamad said.For example, the International Turkic States University, established in Tashkent, the capital of Uzbekistan and the Türkiye-Syria Friendship University planned to be established in Damascus, the capital of Syria.Hamad’s views are in line with the June 2025studytitled “The internationalisation of Turkish higher education: A critical analysis of policy” which indicated that the Turkish agenda for internationalisation of higher education (IHE) is used strategically to position the country as a regional power and to strengthen ties with regions such as Central Asia, the Balkans, the Middle East, and Africa.“Tools such as scholarship programmes and overseas educational institutions support geopolitical objectives and soft power narratives,” the study said.Joint declaration adoptedThe conference concluded with the adoption of a document titled the “Joint Declaration of Tunis” under the theme “Transforming Higher Education through Leadership, Innovation, and Regional Integration”.The declaration outlined five main pillars, including leadership and governance, innovation and scientific research, regional integration, and sustainable development.Besides strengthening university leadership capabilities and developing institutional governance systems and establishing joint research and innovation centres in the fields of digital transformation, artificial intelligence, health, and sustainability, the declaration called for supporting academic exchange programmes, scholarships, and inter-university summer schools and aligning academic cooperation with the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs).To achieve that, the Tunis declaration called for the establishment of bi-regional hubs for research, innovation, and entrepreneurship and the creation of a joint Arab-Turkish digital platform to facilitate the exchange of expertise, share experiences, and coordinate research projects between universities.It also called for the establishment of a dual-degree system between Arab and Turkish universities, under which students will be allowed to complete part of their studies in each country and obtain either two separate degrees or a joint one recognised by both partner institutions.This will in turn enhance academic mobility and mutual credential recognition of qualifications within the Arab-Turkish education space.‘Boost for both regions’Alsoud, of the Association of Arab Universities, said the congress’ initiatives, particularly the dual-degree system, joint digital platform, and regional research hubs, create an “integrated academic space that enhances innovtion, talent mobility, and shared research impact across both regions”.Professor Hamed Ead, based in the Faculty of Science, Cairo University, who is a former cultural counsellor at the Egyptian Embassy in Morocco, toldUniversity World News: “These initiatives are significant because they create a structured, integrated academic alliance, moving beyond sporadic cooperation to boost the global competitiveness of both regions, combat ‘brain drain’ by creating attractive local pathways, and foster deep cultural and intellectual ties that strengthen geopolitical and civilisational alignment”.Walid Gashout, president of the Higher Education Students Foundation in Libya, toldUniversity World Newsthat the Arab–Turkish partnership initiatives represent a “strategic step toward building a shared academic space capable of transforming scientific cooperation into tangible developmental value”.Implementation challenges and solutionsEad said key challenges for the initiative include bureaucratic incompatibility between different national education systems, significant quality disparities among universities, linguistic barriers, the high cost of sustainable funding, and potential disruption from regional political volatility.“Success requires establishing a joint authority to standardise academic rules, launching small-scale pilot programmes before a full rollout, investing in language preparation, and creating a diverse, multi-source funding model to ensure long-term stability beyond political cycles,” Ead said.Gashout believes harmonising legal and administrative frameworks among countries and ensuring sustainable funding and project continuity are the main challenges.Alsoud said key challenges might include alignment of accreditation frameworks, recognition of qualifications, and ensuring sustained institutional commitment amid diverse regulatory and funding contexts.He said that to address these challenges it was vital to establish a permanent Arab–Turkish coordination mechanism under the umbrella of both the Association of Arab Universities and the Eurasian Universities Union.“Harmonising standards, launching pilot projects, and mobilising joint funding can turn the declarations into actionable, measurable programmes,” he noted.“This partnership should evolve into a model for interregional academic integration, linking knowledge production to development priorities and positioning universities as engines of innovation and diplomacy between the Arab world and Türkiye,” Alsoud said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
阿土耳其高校建立雙學位及研究中心合作計劃

阿拉伯和土耳其的高等教育機構將建立雙學位制度、數位平台和雙區域研究創新中心，以加強學術合作和科學研究。這些合作措施在10月10日至11日的「突尼斯宣言」中被確定，旨在促進可持續發展。會議主題為「透過領導、創新和區域整合轉型高等教育」，並強調建立聯合研究及創新中心的必要性。專家指出，這將有助於提升兩地的學術流動性和合作，並解決區域內的問題，促進科學進步和文化理解。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: AI is already transforming Indian higher education – Report
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: AI is already transforming Indian higher education – ReportGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIABlocking Sci-Hub spotlights unequal access to knowledgeINDIANVIDIA’s AI university part of new industry-academia driveGLOBALThe end of AI and the future of higher educationGLOBALAssessment and AI: What can we learn from other cultures?GLOBALCan AI design a better university for the ‘Age of Complexity’?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIAAI is already transforming Indian higher education – ReportShuriah Niazi16 October 2025TweetArtificial intelligence is no longer an experimental, futuristic tool for India’s universities but is transforming how institutions teach, manage and innovate. Once a buzzword, AI now drives academic planning, administration and student support, according to a new report.The reportFuture-Ready Campuses: Unlocking the power of AI in higher educationby EY-Parthenon and the Federation of Indian Chambers of Commerce and Industry (FICCI) revealed that over 60% of Indian higher education institutions permit students to use AI tools.“AI is not just another digital tool for campuses; it’s an accelerator for achieving the long-standing goals of access, quality and employability,” explained Dr Vidya Yeravdekar, pro-chancellor of Symbiosis International University and chair of the FICCI Committee on Higher Education.“It allows us to personalise learning, simplify administrative work, and open up new frontiers for research and innovation,” she toldUniversity World News.The report’s findings from a survey of 30 leading institutions showed AI is used beyond computer science. Over half of these institutions use generative AI to create learning materials, while others use AI chatbots, adaptive learning platforms, and automated grading systems.In addition, AI is transforming how Indian universities engage globally. Many are adopting AI-driven systems for virtual exchanges, multilingual support, and personalised admissions, enhancing their appeal to international students and strengthening India’s academic reach.The overarching challenge is not whether institutions should adopt AI, but how to do so responsibly, inclusively and strategically, aligning with India’s National AI Mission and the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs), according to the report.It suggests a three-stage adoption plan: start with small, low-risk pilots, expand successful tools, and gradually integrate AI across operations to build confidence and address ethical concerns.Bridging inequalitiesAI adoption in Indian higher education is uneven – elite institutions advance rapidly, while many state-funded and smaller colleges lag due to poor connectivity, outdated infrastructure, and limited teacher training.“There is a clear digital divide,” Yeravdekar warned. “We need regional centres of excellence that can share resources such as computing infrastructure, datasets and faculty exchange programmes. That’s the only way to ensure equitable access.”She added that partnerships with tech companies and cloud service providers could make AI tools affordable through subsidised credits and shared platforms.The report identified a blend of public and private financing as the best way forward. Public investment is essential, with India’s union budget 2025 allocating funds for a Centre of Excellence in AI for Education. State governments and the ministries of education and science and technology are expected to contribute as well.“Private universities are reallocating budgets, while CSR [corporate social responsibility] programmes, tech start-ups, and philanthropic foundations are supporting AI research labs and innovation hubs. International donors are also supporting under-resourced campuses, particularly those in rural areas,” Yeravdekar pointed out.In the future, smaller colleges, aided by federated learning and shared infrastructure, will join high-quality research for the first time.Experts cited by the report predicted that in the next decade, AI will make Indian higher education more flexible, inclusive and distributed. Micro-credentials and modular degrees will promote lifelong learning, while AI tutors will complement human mentors.AI’s role in the curriculumCurriculum design is also evolving – about half of Indian students feel underprepared for AI-related jobs, according to the Digital Education Council. To bridge this gap, universities are embedding AI modules across disciplines, including business, law, health sciences and the arts.A 2024Digital Education Councilsurvey found that 86% of students already use AI for studies, with nearly one in four using it daily.Symbiosis International University has even launched the Symbiosis Artificial Intelligence Institute (SAII) to develop AI-based programmes and promote interdisciplinary research. “AI should be taught across subjects, not just in engineering,” Yeravdekar said. “In the coming years, every graduate – whether in journalism, design, or law – will need to understand AI.”To make this possible, several institutions are introducing short-term micro-credentials and stackable courses that allow students and working professionals to continually upgrade their skills. Others are adding AI-focused degrees or integrating AI components into existing courses to prepare ‘future-ready’ graduates.Still, faculty readiness remains low. A 2025 Digital Education Council survey found only 17% of educators are advanced AI users, with just 6% satisfied with the institutional support they receive.Yeravdekar recommended layered training through AI literacy courses, practical workshops, and industry-linked exchanges to build faculty confidence: “Once teachers see AI as an ally that saves time and improves engagement, fear turns into collaboration,” she said.Research and industry partnershipsIndia ranks fourth in global research output, the report noted, but falls short on impact and citations. AI can bridge this gap, with nearly half of the institutions surveyed in the report establishing dedicated AI research centres.The centres explore various topics from computer vision and machine learning to applications in energy, agriculture, and disaster management, aligned with the SDGs.However, collaboration remains key. “Universities working hand-in-hand with industry can create more relevant courses and applied research opportunities,” Yeravdekar said. “These partnerships not only strengthen innovation but also produce graduates who are truly job-ready.”Examples of such cooperation are emerging across several sectors, especially healthcare and fintech, where AI is being used for predictive analytics, diagnostic support and fraud detection.Industry partnerships are also shaping curricula through co-created courses and AI clinics, which provide students with applied, practice-orientated learning experiences. Scaling such initiatives will require investments in labs, computing infrastructure, and flexible college curricula supported by lifelong learning pathways, the report said.As AI becomes more entrenched in academia, ethical and human challenges persist. Key concerns include algorithmic bias, data privacy lapses, and opaque evaluation systems.The report urges universities to follow frameworks like UNESCO’s Ethics of AI (2021) and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act (2023) to safeguard transparency and accountability.Yeravdekar noted: “Policy, funding and innovation are converging. With equal focus on ethics and faculty growth, AI can make education not just smarter – but more human.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

印度高等教育正受到人工智慧的深刻變革。根據EY-Parthenon和印度工商聯合會的報告，超過60%的高等教育機構允許學生使用AI工具，以促進學術規劃、管理和學生支持。報告指出，AI不僅限於計算機科學，還用於生成學習材料、聊天機器人和自適應學習平台。

然而，AI的採用在不同機構之間不均衡，精英院校發展迅速，而許多州立及小型學院則因基礎設施不足而落後。為確保公平接入，專家呼籲加強公私合營的資金支持並建立區域卓越中心。報告還建議逐步整合AI，以應對倫理問題並提升教育質量。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Leaders against proposed tax on licensed patent royalties
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Leaders against proposed tax on licensed patent royaltiesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsUnited States Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESHE leaders brand Trump’s compact for HE as ‘extortion’UNITED STATES-GLOBALTrump is ‘catalyst, not cause’ of HE decline – PasquerellaUNITED STATESToday it is UCLA, but who is the next target for Trump?UNITED STATESTrump tells Congress to cut basic science research by 34%UNITED STATESTrump’s ‘Big Bill’ to cost students, universities billionsUNITED STATESAll hopes turn to Congress to soften Trump’s assault on HEUNITED STATESTrump administration cuts extra US$450 million to HarvardUNITED STATESNew bills threaten tax breaks for top New York universitiesUNITED STATESElite universities face tax-exempt status investigationUNITED STATESNew York universities’ tax breaks come under more scrutinyUNITED STATESHarvard submits public comments to endowment tax rulesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyUNITED STATESLeaders against proposed tax on licensed patent royaltiesNathan M Greenfield16 October 2025TweetUniversity and entrepreneurial leaders have reacted strongly against United States Commerce Secretary Howard Lutnick’s proposal to impose a 50% tax on royalties that federally funded basic scientific research at universities receives from companies that license patents (intellectual property) developed at these universities and that are brought to market.Lutnick sees the tax as a way to recoup some of the US$55 billion the federal government spends in basic university research grants per year.According to a June 2025 report from theInformation Technology and Innovation Foundation (ITIF)under the Bayh-Dole Act or Patent and Trademark Law Amendments Act (Pub. L. 96-517, 12 December 1980), which exempted these royalties from taxation, the pipeline from basic university research licensed to the private sector resulted in an additional US$1.9 trillion in US gross industrial output, the creation of 18,000 start-ups and 554,000 inventions.In a survey of its members that showed unanimous opposition to Lutnick’s proposal published last week, the Bayh-Dole Coalition (Coalition) quoted one member as saying: “A 50% tax on royalties would be the death knell for many such investments.”At a 9 October podcast titled “Does the Government Receive ROI [Return on Investment] for Academic Inventions”, sponsored by the Coalition, the Coalition’s executive director Joseph P Allen pointed out that not only does Lutnick’s50% proposed taxon royalties introduce a new level of uncertainty that will scare off venture capitalists and, hence, undermine the creation of start-ups, but it also creates a precedent dangerous to the companies that bring products based on the research to market because it goes beyond the normal taxation rate profitable companies pay.“If the precedent is that when the government funds something, it can take [royalties] if you’re successful, why can’t they do that to small companies? Why is it just universities? Why wouldn’t the same apply to a small company making a product that they developed out of a federal contract [for basic research]? Why couldn’t the government come in and say, ‘I want 25% of your profits?’ [before taxes]?” asked Allen.“We have public and private sector members, and predictably, [they] say this would really hurt innovation. The industry people said this would shake confidence in the whole system because it would be the first time that Bayh-Dole has been arbitrarily changed. And once you start doing that, you’re like China, where you have rules, but anytime the rulers want to make a new rule, they do so,” said Allen.Failure to ‘comply with preferences for US industry’Lutnick’s surprise proposal was made in an interview on the online news serviceAxiosin September – and he goes further in aletterhe sent to Harvard University and posted onXon 8 August.In that letter, Lutnick charged that Harvard was in violation of the Bayh-Dole Act (B-DA), which since its passage 45 years ago has governed the relationship between government-funded research at universities and their trust relationship to enable fundamental research to be brought to market either by private companies, which include start-ups dedicated to bringing a particular product to market.Among Lutnick’s claims is that Harvard failed to “comply with preferences for United States industry” for commercial development of patents. Pursuant to the B-DA, Lutnick wrote that the “US Government intends to grant third-party licensees to Harvard’s patents or take title where Harvard has failed timely to disclose or elect title to inventions.”When issued, Lutnick’s letter to Harvard was seen as only the latest move in the battle that the administration of President Donald J Trump had been waging for months because of what the administration considered Harvard’s tepid response to antisemitism on campus and its diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) policies.The proposal mooted onAxioshad no such reasoning.Rather, it appears to be modelled on the US government’s 10% stake inIntel, which ensured the US chipmaking giant would remain under American control, and the 15% tariff placed on chipmakerNvidia’sexports to China, since Lutnick’s answer followedAxios’ founder Mike Allen’s question: “If that’s the model, who’s next?”Lutnick, who had been president and CEO of the multi-billion-dollar finance company Cantor Fitzgerald since 1990, began by calmly answering: “I think universities, who are getting all this money. The scientists get the patents.”His next words, “And the funder of fifty billion dollars, the US government, you know what we get?” are almost shouted. After a pause, he said, in what amounted to a stage whisper, “zero”, which for added emphasis was accompanied by raising his right hand and putting his thumb and forefinger together to form a ‘0’.Lutnick continued in words and a tone redolent of the streets of a Hollywood film noir version of theStreets of New York: “It’s time that we should get [our share]” before giving a potted lesson from Business 101.“In business, if I gave a hundred per cent of the money, I would get half the profits for the science. So, I think, if we fund it, and they [university researchers] invent a patent, the United States of America taxpayer should get half the benefit.” That is, 50% of the licensee’s profit.Trump’s Commerce Secretary completely ignored two important facts. First, is that while the government does not receive revenue on the royalties, it receives income tax revenue from companies that turn a profit on products that are successful in the market, as well as revenue from personal income taxes paid by individuals working for those companies and those who work in universities.Additionally, in most states, state governments receive revenue from sales taxes and state income taxes.Lutnick also ignored the fact that his proposal would require Congress to amend the B-DA, which at present does not allow the government to tax royalties.Were Congress to amend the B-DA, Allen explained, it would be going against the evidence presented to Congress when the senators Bayh and Dole were writing the act. As Allen, who was one of Bayh’s legislative aides, told the podcast: “I don’t know how you're going to do it. You're not going to do it under B-DA because Congress rejected that idea,” when the legislation was passed in subsequent amendments.Fundamental structure of scientific researchCo-sponsored by Democratic senator Birch Bayh (Indiana) and Republican senator Robert Dole (Kansas), the B-DA established the structure under which basic scientific research – funded by the US federal government – the intellectual property could be patented by the researcher and-or university and then licensed to either existing companies or, in most cases, start-ups.Many of these start-ups are founded by “graduate students, post-doctoral fellows and/or research professors involved in the research”, Walter G Copan, vice-president for Research and Technology Transfer at the Colorado School of Mines and who, while Under Secretary of Commerce for Standards and Technology between 2017 and 2021, was responsible for overseeing the implementation and modernisation of the B-DA, toldUniversity World News.A partial list provided by the Coalition of Innovations that came from research brought to market under the B-DA includes Google, high-definition television, organ preservation solutions, Epivir (AIDS therapy), firefighting drones, and the nicotine patch – to which at the panel Copain added the GPS and the mRNA technology that allowed for the rapid development of the COVID vaccine.According to the Coalition, since 1996, under the B-DA, university research has led to more than 580,000 inventions, and some 6.5 million jobs have been supported bythe transferof academic-produced technology into the economy. The transfer has contributed almost US$2 trillion to the US gross industrial output, and more than 19,000 start-ups have been created.As Allen explained in an interview, while the B-DA provided the legal structure for this success, its philosophical roots went back to the end of World War Two and the publication ofScience, The Endless Frontier, a report written by Vannevar Bush, President Franklin D Roosevelt’s science advisor.Published in June 1945, Bush’s report looked to science to keep the nation’s economic system running as it had during the war and, thus, prevent the US from falling back into the Great Depression.Building on the wartime example of the US government funding basic research at universities in, for example, the Manhattan Project, the development of the atomic bomb, Bush argued for the “federal government’s role in funding basic research at universities”, stated Allen.Bush, however, drew an important distinction between war- and peace-time research.In the section on government-funded medical research, for example, he writes that funding agencies should “encourage individual initiative and freedom of research, and with a careful avoidance of coercion and regimentation, which might lead not only to mediocre work but to disastrous impairment of the spirit of cooperation and of research itself. Individual scientific curiosity, community of interest and regard for the common weal must in peace replace as a cohesive force the patriotism of war”.Allen, who was on Bayh’s senatorial staff when the B-DA was written and became law, explained that Bush’s report called for the government to fund basic research; bringing products to market was the responsibility of the private sector (President Harry Truman, FDR’s successor, accepted).The process is often not straightforwardAs speakers at the webinar noted, research universities and colleges have technology transfer offices which have the job of bringing together venture capitalists with researchers who hold the patents.According to Kevin Walters, public affairs associate in the office of Strategic Communications and Public Affairs at the Wisconsin Alumni Research Foundation (WARF), which he describes as being “adjacent to” but not “part of” the University of Wisconsin (UW, Madison), foundations such as WARF have the “job of figuring out what research findings can be applied to the market.”The process is often not straightforward, Walters explained, even while staying out of the weeds of venture capitalist and investment logic.“It’s a difficult process because we’re dealing with basic research. It’s almost inherent, therefore, that you’re talking about solutions that are looking for problems. We have to go to industry and say, ‘Okay, what are your needs? Is there something we’re doing at the university that can solve this particular problem?’“The scientist isn’t necessarily thinking about the next app on an iPhone or the next drug against cancer. They’re thinking about their discipline in the world in which they’re working,” he stated.WARF, Walters added, gave its name to Warfarin, the world’s most used blood thinner, which came out of research by a biochemist seeking to find out why a farmer’s cows were bleeding to death 80 years ago. In WARF’s development pipeline are drugs that could treat multiple types of cancer, vision diseases and cystic fibrosis.Under the B-DA, after paying legal expenses for technology transfer, lab expenses, remunerating the patent holder, etcetera, any profits realised by an invention are given to the university; at UW, Walters said, the figure is US$150 million a year. “That’s nothing to sneeze at, but UW Madison had a research budget of about US$1.6 billion per year,” he noted.Not all technology transfer offices are as successful as WARF, as Copan told the B-DC webinar, titled “Does the Government Receive ROI [Return on Investment] for Academic Inventions.” The majority of technology transfer offices and start-ups lose money because the vast majority of discoveries do not lead to marketable products – and those that do can take a decade or more to produce a profit.“They’re not money makers, per se; they are set up in the public trust to provide pathways of impact for society and for the economy arising from basic research. And so these are long journeys.“They’re risky journeys, and we know that there’s a relatively high percentage of venture-backed startup companies coming out of universities that just never make it. The risks are too substantial, and sometimes the technology is not able to penetrate the marketplace in that journey of navigating those risks.”‘Chilling effects on the broader economy’Unlike the B-DA, which did not create government bureaucracy, Lutnick’s proposal to tax 50% of the royalties would both create more bureaucracy and would “chill the technology transfer industry and have chilling effects on the broader economy”,reported the Coalition.The reason why Lutnick’s proposal would add to the bureaucracy in Washington is because there is no ready definition of intellectual property and because the vast majority of the start-ups lose money.“The consequences of what may appear to be a good idea on the surface,” the 50% tax on royalties proposed by Lutnick, stated that Copan would require the creation “of new taxation regimes” and of “a whole new administrative function that would be required to track and report.”Even more importantly, “since net income from [most] technology licensing is negative, if this is set up as a new tax regime, then the federal government, instead of receiving revenue, will actually be issuing tax credits,” in other words, it would cost the government money, Copan said in an interview.Teri Willey, Managing Director and Officer of Pathway to Cures, a venture philanthropy fund established by the Philadelphia-based National Bleeding Orders Foundation, bristled at Lutnick’s insinuation that universities were somehow getting a “free ride”.“It’s really easy to zero in on the dollars and look at this royalty income and feel like maybe it’s some sort of windfall. Well, it’s not.“I think it’s really important to look at where the money comes from and where it goes.“It comes from hundreds of individual licence agreements that got put together over time, from identifying intellectual property, from determining whether you could file meaningful claims that would create an incentive for private investment so that this idea could have an impact on society by being delivered to commercial channels, and by sorting out the relation, multiple relationships, so that you could file and have clear title.“Every bit of funded research at an academic institution has multiple sources, not only the federal government, but also disease-specific foundations, other types of grants, donors, and each of those funding sources comes with strings attached.“You’ve got to sort out all those obligations before you can move forward and support a bit of intellectual property that takes people, that takes time. It takes dedication, and it takes people,” she said.‘Incentives for innovation have now been disrupted’A constant theme in the podcast and in what interviewees said is that, in addition to being one of the prime engines of American scientific innovation, the structures developed under the B-DA and tens of billions of dollars of federal funding for basic science are vital to universities – though not necessarily because any particular university researcher will develop the next equivalent of the Covid vaccine or make discoveries in physics without which there would be no GPSs. The reason is rather more quotidian.The system supports the feedback loop whereby whatever monies a patent might produce, after expenses and licensing charges, are reinvested back into the university are, Willey said “reinvest[ed] in research at those institutions, and that includes postdocs and graduate students who are working in these laboratories”.Willey added: “You can never guarantee you are going to have a big winner in the tech transfer programme, but you can do your best to be poised to manage it when it comes along, and when it does, that can make all the difference to a Midwestern university that has its first big revenue stream, and maybe that means it can move up its neuroscience programme expansion by five years, and maybe it means that it can support more postdocs in studying Parkinson’s disease.“It makes a world of difference, and it changes people’s lives.”Copain also underlined how the world of finance and business, which for the most part are undiscovered countries, is directly tied to the university scientific enterprise in ways that Lutnick does not appear to appreciate.His “proposal does not take into account the breadth of investment, the risk capital that is brought together to enable a fledgling enterprise to move forward, and the fact that the founders of these companies, [who] may be graduate students and postdoctoral fellows as well as supported by academic faculty, are putting a lot on the line”, she noted.“And so, if the government steps in, in such a way as to disrupt this whole process, this virtuous cycle that’s been created in the United States by the Bayh-Dole Act, then so much of US innovation will ultimately be put at risk, because the incentives for innovation have now been disrupted,” she concluded.Walters toldUniversity World Newsthat he was as concerned with the disruption of the academic pipeline as he is with the product production pipeline.The loss of basic research funding will lead to the loss of scientists working to understand those diseases, he said.“But, hand in glove with that, is the fact that it is correct to say that if you don’t have basic research, then you don’t have graduate students who are helping you or who are going to, after the biomarkers are found, for example, to be working on producing a drug in pill form.“The PhDs in your organisation or the MAs, their training has to occur also in that pipeline,” stated Walters.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMADAGASCARStudent-led anti-government movement helps topple presidentVillen Anganan16 October 2025TweetProtests by students at Madagascar’s largest university have helped bring down the embattled former president of the island country, replaced on 14 October by an elite group of army troops.Colonel Michael Randrianirina, head of the CAPSAT, which is the Personnel Administration and Technical and Administrative Services Corps unit, declared on national radio: “We have taken the power,” saying the military had dissolved all national representative institutions except the National Assembly, which subsequently impeached former president Andry Rajoelina, who has fled the country.What began as an anti-government outcry on 25 September over water and electricity cuts sparked a wave of protests, led largely by a new generation of activists. Mobilising through social media, they had shut down the University of Antananarivo, based in the capital city, Antananarivo.These daily demonstrations were spearheaded by ‘Generation Z Madagascar’, a social media youth group framing grievances in language resonating far beyond lecture halls. They initially complained of persistent water shortages in student residences and erratic power supplies, with protests morphing into a broader denunciation of corruption, unemployment and rising inequality under Rajoelina’s administration.Francophone Madagascar is, indeed, one of the poorest countries in the world, with 75% of its 30 million people living below the poverty line, according to the World Bank.‘We are tired …’“We are tired of constantly having to complain about water and electricity cuts. Tired of begging for what should be our most basic rights. But that’s not all. Beyond these daily deprivations, we also endure the poor state of our campuses and university residences, which stand as symbols of a youth left behind,” Hendry Randriantsizafy, the chairman of theAssociation des Étudiants en Droit de Madagascar(the association for law students) at the University of Antananarivo, toldUniversity World News.The University of Antananarivo, home to nearly 30,000 students, became ground zero for the protests. However, Randriantsizafy said the university has now announced a partial resumption of activities.According to the university presidency and its scientific council (its ruling body), the central campus is reopening during mornings for essential administrative services and specific academic tasks.This includes masters degree theses defences, submission and generation of PhD dissertations and presentations for post-doctoral qualifications, while all other classes, examinations and entrance tests remain suspended until further notice.However, the students’ association chairman argued that the disruption to the course schedule has been limited, as the university is currently on recess for bachelor degree students in their first to fourth years, with only fifth-year masters students attending classes.Uncertainty about the futureDespite the dovetailing of protests with the course downtime, other students toldUniversity World Newsthat the closure has thrown thousands of students into uncertainty.Many come from rural areas and rely on university dormitories for accommodation and subsidised meals. With dorms sealed and classes halted, many have returned to their villages, unsure when or if they will be able to resume their studies.“This is not just about politics. It is about the future of education in Madagascar,” said a second-year student. “Our dreams are on hold. But, if the system does not change, what use is our diploma?”Professors speaking toUniversity World Newslamented the disruption but acknowledged the depth of student frustration: “It’s the students who would inevitably bear the consequences,” said historian and Professor Richard Ranarivony, lecturing at the same university: “But what is unfolding is bigger than academia. The university has become the cradle of a new civic consciousness, a privileged ground for protests ...”Observers noted that the student-led movement has been unprecedented in both organisation and reach. Unlike past uprisings in Madagascar’s unstable politics that have been dominated by opposition parties, this protest wave was largely spontaneous, coordinated through encrypted WhatsApp groups and viral TikTok clips.“Gen Z is rewriting the rules of dissent in Madagascar,” said a political analyst who requested anonymity. “They have no patience for the old political class and are skilled in using digital tools to rally support. That made it much harder for the government to control the narrative.”More than half of the students at the University of Antananarivo joined the protests, frustrated with the overall economy and society of Madagascar, claimed Randriantsizafy. “After five years of study, there is little hope of finding a job in the administration, which [they regard as] reserved for a privileged few, close to ministers. In the end, many of us end up working in call centres,” he added.What will the toll be on higher education?The movement gained momentum as other groups – civil servants angered by unpaid wages, informal traders squeezed by inflation and disillusioned civil society groups – joined in. The government’s attempts to quell the unrest with tear gas, arrests, and temporary internet shutdowns only fuelled the anger.Former president Andry Rajoelina appeared increasingly out of touch. He had, ironically, risen to prominence himself during student protests in 2009, being a former mayor of Antananarivo catapulted into leading a provisional government, again backed by the military.During these protests, as president, his calls for dialogue were dismissed as too little, too late. The image of young protesters chanting “Rajoelina dégage” (Rajoelina, step down) outside the National Assembly was a stark reversal for a leader once cast as a champion of youth.The breaking point came when segments of the military, long seen as Rajoelina’s power base, began signalling their reluctance to suppress student protests. The army announced on Tuesday (14 October) it was “withdrawing its confidence” from Rajoelina, effectively forcing him from power.Randrianirina, who has been asked to exercise the functions of the head of state by Madagascar’s High Constitutional Court, told reporters in the capital that a military-led committee would rule the country for a period of up to two years alongside a transitional government before organising new elections.Soldiers are patrolling the streets of Antananarivo as crowds celebrate outside the university. Yet, amid the euphoria, questions linger about the toll on tertiary education.“There is no doubt this will set back higher education in Madagascar,” admitted a faculty of sciences student, who requested anonymity. But another student argued that the sacrifice has been justified: “If we had stayed silent, nothing would have changed.”Meanwhile, the political future of Madagascar remains uncertain, with some student leaders calling for a seat at the transitional government in shaping reforms.“This generation has shown it cannot be ignored,” said Ranarivony. “The challenge will be to translate street energy into sustainable change – without sacrificing education and development.”For now, the campus of the University of Antananarivo remains silent. But, in the hearts of many students, the university has already fulfilled a different role: not just as a place of learning, but as the birthplace of a movement that has helped topple a president.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

馬達加斯加學生主導的反政府運動促使總統下台 2025年10月14日，馬達加斯加前總統安德里·拉喬利納被迫下台，這一變革主要源於大學學生的抗議活動。抗議起初因水電供應問題而爆發，隨後擴大為對腐敗和不平等的廣泛譴責。這場由“Z世代”學生主導的運動，利用社交媒體組織抗議，吸引了大學的廣泛參與。雖然抗議活動影響了大學的運作，但學生們堅信，這場運動為馬達加斯加的未來帶來了希望和變革的可能性。學者認為，這一運動標誌著新的公民意識的誕生，並可能在未來影響高等教育的發展。
