https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Another academic strike, but union says it’s ready for talksGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNIGERIAAcademics fear mental breakdown as work keeps piling upNIGERIAAcademic unions reject Tinubu’s payment offer after strikeNIGERIAStrike called off, but ASUU’s labour issues unresolvedInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNIGERIAAnother academic strike, but union says it’s ready for talksAfeez Bolaji16 October 2025TweetStakeholders in the higher education sector have blamed the Nigerian government for not properly managing the longstanding issues that have now culminated in a new round of industrial action by the Academic Staff Union of Universities (ASUU), which has disrupted academic activities across the country.The government has accused the ASUU, which has about 50,000 members and 89 branches, of ignoring its efforts to resolve issues, but Professor Chris Piwuna, the ASUU president, indicated that the union is willing to resume negotiations after receiving signals from government officials.“I received a call from Alhaji Yayale Ahmed, the chairman of the federal government team, and he’s eager to resume talks with us. I also received another call from the Minister of State for Labour [Muhammadu Maigari Dingyadi], stating that he’d been directed to intervene and get this matter resolved. ASUU is willing. We are ready and available to discuss this matter once and for all,” Piwuna said.ASUU had declared a “total and comprehensive” two-week warning strike across all the federal-run universities from Monday, 13 October, accusing the government of failing to honour most parts of renegotiated agreements, dating back to 2009.The strike that has just begun comes two years since the union called off its previous industrial action, which lasted eight months, in 2022. Academics have gone on strike 17 times since Nigeria’s return to democracy in 1999, shutting down universities for an accumulated period of about five years.Among other matters, the academics demand improved staff welfare funds and autonomy for universities, the payment of outstanding allowances, the settlement of 25%-35% of salary arrears, the payment of three-and-a-half months’ salaries withheld during the last strike, and payment of at least four years’ promotion arrears, according to theDaily Trust.The government had recently paid NGN50 billion (about US$34 million) to ASUU members and other university staff as part of settling unpaid earned allowances, but implementation of the remaining demands has stalled.Stakeholders blame the governmentPublic affairs analysts, education rights activists and academics who spoke toUniversity World News, said, although the union’s incessant strikes are painful, the government is to blame for paying lip service to higher education by not honouring the agreements it reached with the academics.Dr Olasunkanmi Arowolo, a scholar and founder of Research Africa, said the recurring strikes reflect a deeper problem of misplaced priorities in governance, noting that, for a nation touted as the giant of Africa, the authorities’ consistent failure to invest meaningfully in education signals a neglect of the very foundation of national development.He said: “The repeated breaches of these agreements have made strikes inevitable. ASUU is not asking for luxury; it is asking for fairness – adequate welfare, improved infrastructure, and conditions that enable quality teaching and research. If educators are poorly treated, demoralised, and underpaid, we cannot expect excellence from them or their students.“The way forward is sincerity, not politics. The government must engage in genuine consultation with stakeholders and demonstrate the political will to bring lasting reform. If the agreements contain 20 items, and it is not feasible to fulfil all at once, start with the most urgent and achievable ones, then move systematically to the rest.”Hassan Soweto, the national coordinator of the Education Rights campaign, deplored the “nonchalance” of successive governments to ASUU’s demands, urging the current administration to implement the agreements without further delay.“These are issues that concern the future of the entire country; the future of Nigeria’s public education system. It’s sad that this strike is taking place, but the era of blaming ASUU for incessant strikes is over because the government is nonchalant towards the demands of the union. The government should ensure that the strike ends as soon as possible by meeting the demands,” Soweto added.Threats of no work, no payMeanwhile, Dr Tunji Alausa, the minister of education, in a memo dated 13 October, directed vice-chancellors to enforce the no-work-no-pay policy against any lecturer “who fails to discharge his or her official duties during the period of strike action”, accusing ASUU of not being cooperative, despite efforts by the government to resolve the issues.Also, in aninterviewwithChannels TV, a local television station, Alausa said the Tinubu administration has been prioritising the welfare of university staff, insisting that the union’s demands had been met.“I do not see reasons why they have gone on this strike. I can tell you that all the demands of ASUU have been met. For the arrears of their earned academic allowance, we have paid NGN50 billion. We have addressed the issue of postgraduate supervision allowances, which are to be paid by the institutions, and I have been told that they are being paid.“We have told them the promotion arrears will be paid with the 2026 appropriation budget. I have had multiple meetings with them on the 25%-35% wage award, where I told them that all public servants have not been paid, but this will be paid in 2026,” he said.The minister further explained that NGN50 billion had been released from its assessment fund and the remaining would be disbursed in tranches.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

尼日利亞學術工會再度罷工，準備重啟談判 學術工會（ASUU）於10月13日展開為期兩週的全面罷工，指責政府未能履行自2009年以來的多項協議，導致全國大學的學術活動受到干擾。ASUU主席皮武納表示，工會願意與政府重新談判，並已收到政府官員的邀請。此次罷工是自2022年長達八個月的罷工後再次發生，學者和教育權益活動家批評政府未能妥善管理高等教育問題。教育部長阿勞薩則強調政府已滿足部分要求，並對罷工期間的教職員實施「無工資無付款」政策。這場罷工反映出尼日利亞高等教育面臨的深層問題，迫切需要政府採取真誠的行動來解決。

尼日利亞學術工會再度罷工，準備重啟談判 學術工會（ASUU）於10月13日展開為期兩週的全面罷工，指責政府未能履行自2009年以來的多項協議，導致全國大學的學術活動受到干擾。ASUU主席皮武納表示，工會願意與政府重新談判，並已收到政府官員的邀請。此次罷工是自2022年長達八個月的罷工後再次發生，學者和教育權益活動家批評政府未能妥善管理高等教育問題。教育部長阿勞薩則強調政府已滿足部分要求，並對罷工期間的教職員實施「無工資無付款」政策。這場罷工反映出尼日利亞高等教育面臨的深層問題，迫切需要政府採取真誠的行動來解決。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Mounting admissions fraud cases are threat to Hong Kong HEGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksHONG KONG-EUROPEUniversity of Hong Kong to set up branch campus in EuropeHONG KONGCap on non-local undergrads raised to 50% of student bodyHONG KONGNon-local student influx ramps up accommodation pressureHONG KONG‘Accountability agreements’ raise autonomy concernsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHONG KONGMounting admissions fraud cases are threat to Hong Kong HEYojana Sharma15 October 2025TweetClaims of admissions fraud are spreading more widely in Hong Kong, as the city’s police this week revealed that it had received 126 reports of allegedly fraudulent academic qualifications from the city’s universities in the first seven months of this year.But these numbers are just the tip of the iceberg, as universities are not reporting many cases to police, causing concern that the acceleration in cases could cause damage to Hong Kong’s reputation as a higher education hub.A spokesperson for the University of Hong Kong (HKU) said on 13 October, “HKU has received a few hundred cases this year involving applicants suspected of submitting false academic credentials for admission,” adding “none of these cases has been enrolled”.The cases related mainly to applications to the city’s universities from mainland Chinese students, which have expanded in the past 2 to 3 years.Last year over 30 students from mainland China were caught trying to use fake documents to enroll in HKU’s business school. While initially played down by the university, dozens of cases have since emerged.In May, a mainland student, Li Sixuan, was jailed in Hong Kong for eight months after admitting to using fake Ivy League credentials to gain admission to HKU for a masters’ programme in applied linguistics. She falsely claimed to have graduated from Columbia University.Her defence lawyer told the Hong Kong court his client was “very determined” to study at HKU.The Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK) also said it had rejected “several hundred” applications with suspected fake credentials during this year’s application period, mostly from mainland Chinese applicants.Andy Wong, director of admissions and financial aid at CUHK, said in remarks on 11 October that the university did not lodge police reports “since the applicants were outside of the city and there is no evidence that the applicants had accomplices in Hong Kong – there is not much they (the Hong Kong police) can do”.Nonetheless, it was a sharp increase from about 10 cases at CUHK the previous year, academics noted, pointing to an escalating problem with exaggerated admissions statements, falsified references and fake credentials.Hong Kong Polytechnic University (HKPolyU) said it had identified about 10 cases of falsified credentials and had cancelled the applications and admissions offers.Police said following the reports of fraudulent academic qualifications, 55 people had been arrested between January 2022 and July this year, with so far nine charged and six convicted. Some are still under investigation.Hong Kong police also said 21 suspected “unlawful intermediaries”, or agents, in mainland China, had been referred to mainland authorities.More than half of those arrested were detained in the first half of this year.Fear of reputational impact“Hong Kong is seriously worried about the impact of such fraud on the reputation of its highly regarded universities. It has a lot at stake, as it has promoted Hong Kong internationally as a higher education hub,” a CUHK academic toldUniversity World Newson condition of anonymity.“There has been a big rush of applications from the mainland due to Hong Kong universities' good results in global university rankings. But the downside is that many want to get a Hong Kong degree by hook or by crook. And some are assisted by unscrupulous agents in China.”Hong Kong’s independent Commission Against Corruption (ICAC) heldanti-fraud workshopsin January and earlier this month for 40 admissions officers and other university staff. Director of the ICAC’s Corruption Prevention Department, Cheuk Chi-yan, said in early October the participants were all managers of admissions procedures.“They are not only implementers of the system but also guardians of educational values,” he said, addressing the workshop.Hong Kong undersecretary for education Shi Junhui said at the same workshop that with the plan for Hong Kong to become a higher education hub “the authorities attach great importance to safeguarding the integrity, quality, and reputation of Hong Kong's post-secondary education sector”.He urged institutions “to work together to safeguard the reputation of Hong Kong's higher education, establish a robust anti-corruption mechanism, uphold the principles of fairness and justice, and work together to create clean campuses”.A Hong Kong Education Bureau spokesman said on 9 October on the release of the World University Rankings 2026 announced by Times Higher Education (THE) that publicly funded universities “have continued to hold top spots, rendering Hong Kong the only city in the world with five universities ranked among the global top 100, with all its ranked institutions gaining higher positions”.According to the latest THE World University Rankings released this month, HKU rose two places to 33rd globally; the CUHK moved up three places to 41st; the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology (HKUST) jumped eight places to 58th;“Mainland families are highly rankings-orientated when choosing a university, and these results make an impact. Many more families areturning to Hong Kong, especially with visa problems in Australia and Canada and uncertainty over visas for Chinese students wanting to study in the United States,” the CUHK academic said.Hardline stanceHKU is among those taking a hardline stance. An HKU spokesperson said in a statement this week: “HKU consistently treats cases involving fraudulent documents with the utmost seriousness, adopting rigorous measures during the admissions process.“Applications suspected of containing fabricated qualifications will not be considered. Should an applicant be found to have falsified academic credentials, the University will take appropriate action.”The spokesperson added: “Where concrete evidence of fraudulent misrepresentation is established, we will invariably report the matter to the police for further investigation and prosecution.”The Hong Kong government last yearincreased the cap on non-local students studying at the undergraduate levelin publicly funded universities. More than 90% of non-local places are taken up by mainland Chinese students.“Following the Government’s doubling of the enrolment ceilings for non-local students of UGC-funded universities from 20% to 40% from the 2024 to 2025 academic year, the number of non-local students enrolled in the UGC-funded programmes has recorded double-digit year-on-year growth,” the education bureau spokesperson said.Starting from the 2026 and 2027 academic years, the Hong Kong government will raise the enrolment ceiling for self-financing non-local students for taught programmes from around 40% of local student placesto 50%the city’s Chief Executive John Lee said in his 17 September policy address.With the number of non-local students set to rise even further, universities fear even more fraud as students from mainland China flock to Hong Kong’s highly regarded universities.AI-assisted verificationAt the ICAC workshop earlier this month, a representative of HKPolyU said the university followed a rigorous process to verify the qualifications and submitted materials of applicants, including interviews and credential checks.The university said it plans to integrate artificial intelligence (AI) technology into its admissions application system to further enhance academic verification.HKU’s Assistant Director of Admissions, Raymond Wong, told the workshop that HKU’s AI-assisted interview system checks student identities and anomalies during interviews, preventing impersonation and cheating.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Research excellence needs redefinition for gender equity
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Research excellence needs redefinition for gender equityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksJAPANHow can Japanese universities attract more students?JAPANQuotas for women in universities: One step towards equityJAPANMinistry ramps up bid to close gender gap among academicsJAPANRecord numbers of female students, but is it enough?JAPANCritics say gender bias survey does not go far enoughJAPANBreakthrough highlights lack of women in scienceInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsJAPANResearch excellence needs redefinition for gender equitySuvendrini Kakuchi15 October 2025TweetWomen leaders in science face deep-rooted gender-based societal barriers. To boost the low number of female scientists and researchers in Japan, research excellence, which relies heavily on publications and citations, needs to be redefined, a new report by the Elsevier Foundation and RIKEN states.“Excellence should be defined beyond traditional bibliometrics. Institutions should formally recognise mentorship, interdisciplinary collaboration and societal impact,” according to the report by the Elsevier Foundation and RIKEN, Japan’s leading national science research institute, titledEnvisioning Futures report: Amplifying women’s voices and leadership in Japanese Science, launched this month.“The report raises awareness on a holistic view of career. There are many different modes of excellence as the concept of a linear career changes in modern society,” Ylann Schemm, executive director of the Elsevier Foundation, pointed out, adding that academic bodies and funding agencies needed to broaden their definitions of excellence when making decisions about promotions and funding.“This means moving beyond the traditional metrics of just research output and citations to recognising interdisciplinary work, societal and policy impact, mentorship and inclusive leadership styles,” Schemm toldUniversity World News.Careers that recognise such aspects as resilience and collaborative action are critical for research progress.“By recognising these important norms as success, women scientists will be able to strengthen their careers, such as [by] applying for grants and promoting flexibility and inclusion,” noted Schemm.“These are areas that will help us make progress towards our big global challenges outlined by the United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and that women often truly excel in.”Women academics comprise more than 44% of researchers in studies related to the SDGs, according to the report.Japan’s research ecosystem still lags behind the West in attracting and retaining women in STEM, particularly in leadership roles. Work-life balance is a major challenge given the demands of long hours linked to career promotion.Going beyond dataElsevier’s gender reports have tracked data across 18 countries for a decade, providing evidence-based information and analysis for policymakers. But the Japan report goes beyond data, highlighting the lived experiences of leading female scientists and the journeys, achievements and challenges of the less traditional success stories in Japanese science.“By combining the lived experience of successful Japanese women research leaders with robust data, we’re providing evidence-based inspiration – or a roadmap for policymakers, heads of institutions and the next generation of early-career women researchers who are trying to navigate an often confusing research landscape,” Schemm explained.The interviews in the report show there is no ‘right’ path to success. Many women science leaders described nonlinear career journeys, shaped by unexpected opportunities, family responsibilities and career interruptions.Instead of viewing these as setbacks, they “reframed them as sources of resilience and perspective”. Flexibility, adaptability and the ability to seize opportunities emerged as essential traits, the report found.The report was launched in partnership with Dr Yuko Harayama, professor of Management of Technology and former RIKEN executive director in charge of international affairs, as well as having spent several years at the Cabinet Office as an executive member of theCouncil for Science, Technology and Innovationand continues to advise universities and industry on research strategy. She is also a former Elsevier Foundation Board member.Harayama is a potent example of a woman who has built a successful career in science in Japan. While she put her career on hold to raise a family, she earned a doctorate later, which she described as a launchpad for “what you are hoping to do next.”She also highlighted the need to build resilience in order to achieve a career in science. Harayama said in the report: “Filling your fuel tank of experiences is so important. Not only with positive ones, but hard ones too: they will help you when facing similar difficulties down the road.”She added: “Opportunities come along in many shapes and forms, and it all comes down to whether or not you seize them.”Other voices cited in the report’s ‘Meet the Leaders’ section showcased successful careers that do not always follow a linear trajectory.Dr Makiko Naka, a leading psychologist exploring memory, worked in many Japanese institutions and is an independent researcher. She described her commitment to an inclusive and flexible environment in her lab. She has also balanced her research career to follow her career goals, for example, often transforming her home into her lab.Importance of mentoringMentoring younger female scientists is another signal of career success, promoting research progress by encouraging women to accept unexpected opportunities, career interruptions and academic detours.Mentorship does not just focus on professional issues but also personal development.Dr Yukiko Goda, a neuroscientist who is currently a senior professor at the Okinawa Institute of Science and Technology (OIST), said she introduced a ‘flat hierarchy’ culture in her lab. “Informal meetings are conducted to encourage collaboration and problem-solving,” she said.The international experience of top featured Japanese scientists also highlighted the call in Japan for expanding students’ global experience. For example, Harayama resumed her education in Geneva, Switzerland, while Dr Reiko Masuka, a brain scientist and leading authority on child language acquisition, held senior research positions while at Duke University in the United States.New emphasis on women in STEMThe report comes amid a new emphasis in Japanese universities, supported by the government, to encourage more female students to graduate in Science, Technology, Engineering and Mathematics (STEM) subjects.Japan’s Basic Plan for Gender Equality, adopted in December 2020, lists 89 specific goals to be achieved in 2025 as part of a vision “Towards a Reiwa Society where all women and girls can thrive and achieve their full potential.” Reiwa refers to the current era in the Japanese calendar, based on the reigning emperor’s ascent to the throne.Schemm said: “National Japanese legislation has made strides in supporting better work-life balance in recent years, but it is essential that these frameworks are implemented across all publicly funded research organisations to enable both women and men in achieving better work-life integration and ensuring a stronger pipeline for women’s academic success.”According to Japan’s Ministry of Internal Affairs and Communications data for 2024, women account for 18.5% of Japan’s total research workforce, or 182,800 individuals – a modest increase in recent years but still lagging behind international benchmarks.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of 日本研究卓越性需重新定義以促進性別平等

根據Elsevier基金會和RIKEN的最新報告，日本女性科學家面臨根深蒂固的性別障礙，亟需重新定義研究卓越性。報告指出，卓越性不應僅依賴於傳統的出版和引用指標，而應包括導師指導、跨學科合作和社會影響等方面。這一變革有助於提升女性科研人員的職業發展，並促進更靈活和包容的研究環境。報告還強調，女性在SDG相關研究中佔比超過44%，呼籲學術機構和資助機構擴大對卓越性的定義，以支持女性在科學領域的進步。此舉對改善日本的性別不平等和提高女性在STEM領域的參與尤為重要。
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Gen Z protests are set to continue, despite fatalities
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Gen Z protests are set to continue, despite fatalitiesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksMOROCCOStudents arrested as Gen Z protests take to the streetsDEMOCRATIC REPUBLIC OF CONGOStudents injured in violent protests over tuition feesMOROCCOProtests are ongoing against Morocco-Israel HE partnershipsSOUTH AFRICAFault line: Student protests and the burden of leadershipInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMOROCCOGen Z protests are set to continue, despite fatalitiesWagdy Sawahel14 October 2025TweetMorocco’s Generation Z movement nationwide protest for ‘dignity, justice and freedom’ is set to continue on 18 October, despite the alleged police killing of three people, including a Moroccan film student, during the uprising that started at the end of September.The protests, focused on reforms in social sectors such as education and health, are largely coordinated online by the Generation Z movement, or Gen Z 212. Morocco’s Generation Z – those aged 15 to 29 – total about 8.2 million people, nearly a quarter of the population – and the 212 refers to Morocco’s phone code.During the weekend of 11-12 October, the movement temporarily suspended its activities in a bid to reorganise, according to astatement. The movement explained that the pause was strategic to strengthen its organisation and coordination, so that the next phase of its actions can be more impactful.The statement also declared that the next protest would “target the government and all those blocking the aspirations of the Moroccan people”.On 10 October, King Mohammed VI, Morocco’s highest authority, delivered aspeechin the country’s parliament, mirroring some of the grievances raised by Gen Z protesters about regional inequalities and uneven development, but he did not address the movement directly.Calls for an investigationTheauthoritiessaid the police killed three people “in legitimate defence” at the beginning of October, after they allegedly tried to storm a police station in the village of Leqliaa, in the south of Morocco, and attacked the security forces “to steal ammunition and weapons”.One of the dead was identified asAbdessamad Oubella, a 25-year-oldstudentat the Specialized Institute for Cinema Professions at Ibn Zohr University.The student’s familysaidhe waskilledby a bullet to the head while documenting theprotestsand called for a fair and transparent investigation.In response, Gen Z issued astatementemphasising that the protests have been peacefulFailure of government and political partiesThe movement called on the authorities to listen to the voice from the streets and initiate urgent reforms that could restore trust between citizens and the state and transform social demands into concrete policies.It also emphasised that there would be no dialogue with the current government, considering that it was nearing the end of its term and has lost its political and moral legitimacy after failing to fulfil its electoral promises.The statement also held political parties responsible for the conditions facing the Moroccan people, noting that the lack of effective oversight and accountability has made political action “disconnected from the concerns of the citizen”.A comprehensive national vision for reformRegarding its objectives, the movement emphasised that it was not proposing a limited set of demands, but rather a comprehensive national vision for reform, based on key axes, including educational reform, improvement in the health system, the creation of job opportunities for youth, strengthening the independence of the judiciary, combating corruption, and involving young talent in public decision-making.The Gen Z movement emphasised that its voice was “not a fleeting cry of anger”, but rather a sincere national call aimed at building a Morocco governed by justice, dignity, and fairness – one in which young people can regain their confidence in their homeland and their future.Student associations support the movementSeveral students’ bodies have issued statements in support of the peaceful protest, condemning the police violence against protesters and calling for a release of all detainees.These student bodies include the National Union of Moroccan Students (Union Nationale des Etudiants du Maroc, orUNEM), which is affiliated to Morocco’s largest Islamic movement,Al Adl Wal Ihsane(Justice and Charity); theProgressive Left Student Factionof the National Union of Moroccan Students, which is affiliated to the Democratic Path Party of Morocco; the Morocco-basedOrganisation of the Student Renaissance, a national association open to all university students in Morocco; and theNational Association of Unemployed Graduates in Morocco.Social and economic conditionsMorocco, a modest performer in terms of its knowledge infrastructure, ranks 98th out of 141 countries in the Global Knowledge Index (GKI2024), which measures knowledge performance worldwide using seven main sectoral indices, including higher education, alongside research, development and innovation. This marks a six-place decline from its position in 2023, with the country ranking 92nd last year.Morocco’s performance, with a score of 42.8 points below the global average of 47.8, reveals ongoing challenges in key areas, including higher education, gender equity and economic competitiveness, affecting its ability to advance towards a stronger knowledge-driven economy.A quarter of university graduates in Morocco were jobless in 2023 – the highest unemployment rate for those who hold degrees in about two decades, according to a 2024reportpublished by theHaut Commissariat au Plan, or the High Commission for Planning.The number of medical and paramedical staff providing direct patient care in Morocco has barely exceeded 1.65 per 1,000 people, which is well below the critical threshold of 4.45 care staff per 1,000 people required according to suggested ratios, according to the April 2020reportentitled,The truth about health in Morocco: No health without workforce development.The report estimated the deficit of medical staff in Morocco to be 32,387 and the paramedical shortage at 97,161.“This deficit will worsen over the next 10 years with the retirement of 24% of acting paramedical staff: more than 8,500 people,” according to the report.At least half of Moroccans under 35 say they have considered emigrating, according to a June 2025surveyfrom AfroBarometer which also found that unemployment tops the list of important problems that youth say their government must address.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related Image generated by SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIAIndia rankings penalise retractions for the first timeINDIA‘Game-changing’ journal access scheme to launch in JanuaryINDIAIndia’s ranking framework is at odds with national policyINDIAIITs lead in national rankings but continue global boycottINDIAWhat India’s performance in the Impact Rankings tells usInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackINDIANational rankings study reflects 10 years of HE progressShuriah Niazi23 October 2025TweetThe Indian government’s university rankings, known as the National Institutional Ranking Framework (NIRF), have become the defining benchmark for excellence in Indian higher education a decade after they were launched, according to a recent report.Introduced by the Ministry of Education in 2015, NIRF has transformed how universities and colleges are evaluated, shifting focus from mere reputation to measurable performance.Over the same period, Indian higher education has become more globally competitive, as seen in the rise of institutions in global rankings, and its research ecosystem has shown substantial progress, notes the analysis by business consultancy firm KPMG based on 10 years of NIRF data.“We’ve begun realising our potential in research and innovation,” Anil Sahasrabudhe, chair of the National Assessment and Accreditation Council, toldUniversity World News. “The next step is to move from quantity to quality so India can soon rank among the world’s top 10 in research,” he noted.Sahasrabudhe said the NIRF had transformed institutional policies and faculty development.“To stay competitive, institutions are hiring quality faculty, offering seed research grants, and building partnerships with top universities in India and abroad. This has created a stronger research culture that inspires both teachers and students,” he said.KPMG’s analysis, published just over a month after this year’s NIRF ranking was released on 4 September, pointed to a noteworthy increase in the number of university lecturers with PhDs — an international benchmark of quality — with nearly 60% of faculty in India’s top 100 institutions having doctoral qualifications.Over 90% of faculty at top management schools have PhDs, while at top engineering colleges the figure is over 80%.The study also showed that from 2019 to 2025, the number of students enrolling in PhD programmes went up by 21%, and the number of students finishing their PhDs increased by 49%, which enhances research productivity, the report said.Research publications have grown significantly, with universities and engineering schools seeing a 150% rise, while pharmacy and management schools saw a three-fold increase. India’s share of research publications worldwide grew from 3.5% to 5.2% between 2017 and 2024.“These improving trends suggest that institutions may be strategically focusing on research excellence,” the report noted.Spirit of competitionSahasrabudhe said growth in research output and faculty qualifications in India’s higher education sector reflected the deep impact of competition and quality benchmarking introduced through the NIRF.“This improvement has been driven largely by the spirit of competition created through the National Institutional Ranking Framework,” he said.“When we started NIRF in 2016, there were only four categories; today there are 17, covering areas such as medicine, law, engineering, and pharmacy, alongside universities and colleges.“Participation has grown from about 3,500 institutions to nearly 9,000, creating healthy competition and motivating institutions to improve across teaching, research, innovation, and outreach,” Sahasrabudhe pointed out.The NIRF assesses institutions’ performance based on five core areas: teaching, learning and resources; research and professional practice; graduation outcomes; outreach and inclusivity; and perception.As higher education in India evolved, NIRF also changed to focus on new priorities, helping to improve the quality of teachers, research, student success, and inclusion at higher education institutes.It now includes new parameters such as commitment to the Sustainable Development Goals and several innovation indicators. This year an academic integrity indicator was introduced, underlining the commitment to high-quality research, in the form ofnegative marking for retracted publications.Strengthening researchSahasrabudhe said: “With 30% of the (NIRF) rankings weightage assigned to research and professional practice, institutions are now strengthening their research culture, building collaborations, and investing in innovation – leading directly to more publications and patents.”The KPMG analysis said India was doing well in research and innovation, making it one of the top six countries in the world for patents. Top-ranked higher education institutions have excelled in converting published patents into granted patents, attracting more sponsored research funding than their lower-ranked counterparts.In fact, India’s top 25 universities in the NIRF rankings receive nine times more funding than those ranked 76 to 100, highlighting the importance of collaboration with industries, the report said.Public institutions like the Indian Institute of Science in Bengaluru (Bangalore), Jawaharlal Nehru University in New Delhi, and the Indian Institute of Technology Madras in Chennai are still at the top, while private universities like Manipal Academy of Higher Education (MAHE) and Birla Institute of Technology and Science Pilani (BITS) are becoming strong competitors, the report said.Even small score increases led to big jumps in rankings, indicating that competition in India’s higher education is getting tougher.Improved global rankingThe advances in research have translated into improved global rankings for Indian institutions, which have seen afivefold increase in number since 2015in the QS World University Rankings 2026, with 54 Indian institutions ranked among the top 1,500.Only 11 Indian universities appeared in the 2015 QS ranking. India is now the fourth most represented country in the QS rankings after the United States, the United Kingdom and China.Sahasrabudhe said India’s innovation ecosystem now supports research growth at every level.“From Atal Tinkering Labs in schools to AICTE’s (All India Council of Technical Education’s) IDEA Labs and Innovation Councils in universities, the entire pipeline encourages creativity and innovation. Many ideas are evolving into startups or technology transfers,” he added.He cautioned, however, that while research output has increased, quality remains uneven. “Many papers still appear in lower-tier journals. To improve quality, we need more funding and better infrastructure.”He noted that the government’sOne Nation, One Subscription initiativehas widened access to global academic journals for over 6,000 institutions.However, the study also pointed to resource disparities: well-funded IITs and central universities tend to dominate the rankings, while state universities, colleges and rural institutions often face constraints in faculty strength, infrastructure and reporting capacity.The NIRF’s 2025 rankings also showed a big improvement in employability over the decade. Between 2021 and 2024, the average employability score for colleges went up by 11%, indicating improvements in meeting employers’ needs.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

印度高等教育十年進步的國家排名研究報告顯示，自2015年推出的國家高等教育機構排名框架（NIRF）已成為評估印度高等教育卓越的主要標準。報告指出，印度的研究生和教師質量顯著提高，近60%的頂尖學府教師擁有博士學位。此外，近年來博士生入學人數增長21%，畢業生人數增長49%。隨著研究成果的增加，印度在全球學術發表中所佔比例也上升至5.2%。NIRF的推動激發了學術界的競爭，促使高校改善教學與研究，提升整體教育質量。

Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyINDONESIAPrivate enrolment plunges as students pay for state placesKafil Yamin23 October 2025TweetEnrolments in Indonesia’s private universities have fallen by 28% this year, according to data from Indonesia’s Ministry of Higher Education, Science and Technology.This comes on top of declining applications over the past three years, in part due to new routes into coveted state university places and the emergence of digital platforms offering skills training over short periods and at lower cost.The downturn in private university enrolment is having a serious impact on higher education in the country.Approximately 80% of Indonesian students study at private universities, according to the Association of Indonesian Private Higher Education Institutions (APTISI). Data from Indonesia’s Statistics Bureau shows there are 2,982 private universities and 125 state universities in Indonesia.In 2023 and 2024, 23 private universities closed down. This year, 80 private universities are threatened with closure for failing to meet accreditation requirements, including lack of students and permanent lecturers, as well as poor facilities, according to APTISI and the ministry.Willingness to payThe growth in state university enrolment of new students through the so-calledJalur Mandiri, or independent channel, has contributed to the decrease in applicants to private universities, according to Desi Sommaliagustina, senior lecturer at Andalas University, Padang, West Sumatra.The ‘independent’ routes allow admission to non-subsidised places at state universities which demand fees as high as their private university counterparts.“This [independent] channel enables university applicants to be admitted to state universities without sitting the national exam, on one condition: willingness to pay the required amount for admission,” Desi was quoted as saying on 13 October by local media outletKompas.com.Achmad Nurmandi, rector of Yogyakarta Muhammadiyah University, said diminishing interest in private universities is causing these universities to collapse.“In Yogyakarta, 12 private universities closed down this year,” he told the localJogja Insighton 18 October. “Thejalur mandiriroute opened by state universities really affects us,” he added.While private universities enrol a much larger proportion of Indonesia’s higher education students, they receive only 5% of the state budget allocated to education, Dr Ricky Agusiady of the Association of Indonesian Private University Organizing Bodies (ABPPTSI) toldUniversity World News: “Things will get worse because there is no restriction for state universities in opening up new classes and recruiting new students throughjalur mandiri.”“We are told to be competitive universities. But without a fair budget allocation and a restriction of state university expansion, it turns out to be a one-sided competition,” he said.Economic conditionsSommaliagustina said the willingness to pay for education at state universities is fuelled by students’ pursuit of institutional reputation and brighter career prospects, as job opportunities for state university graduates are better than those for graduates of private universities.The economic impact on families of the 2019 to 2022 COVID-19 pandemic continues to be felt in Indonesia. Parents want their children to get a job immediately after graduating from senior high schools or to pursue vocational courses that give them job skills within a shorter time, she pointed out.The cost of education to families has become the main reason for the dwindling number of applicants to private universities, agreed Muhammad Arif, an education analyst at Syarif Hidayatullah Islamic University, a public institution in Jakarta. This has followed a new workplace trend for skills that do not require a university certificate, he said.A characteristic of the current generation of students, often labelled ‘Gen Z’, is that they seek out study programmes that offer immediate skills, Arif noted. “They are not interested in theoretical studies like physics or philosophy,” he said.“If they acquire a skill, he or she will get a job because more and more companies no longer require degree certificates,” he added.Arif pointed out that digital learning institutions have become an alternative, lower-cost option. The emergence of digital learning institutions such as bootcamps, online courses, and certified training has made private universities less attractive, he toldUniversity World News.“A big shift is happening now in the fields of work and education – from conventional to digital work and learning. The government is being challenged to adjust the higher education curriculum to the current trends of this generation.”Financial impact on private universitiesThis leaves private universities struggling with conventional and costly ways of teaching. At the same time the government wants them to be financially independent. Government-sanctioned operational assistance for higher education mostly goes to state universities that are already financed by the state budget, Arif said.A private university needs at least 5,000 students to carry on; with less than that, the university would go through hard times, according to Agusiady.“Fewer students mean less revenue, while lecturers in private universities carry the same [teaching] burden as their counterparts in state universities. In many cases, it is even heavier. They teach, do research and write papers for academic journals. Many of them leave their university,” he said.Without immediate measures by the government, things may get worse. There will be no academic regeneration in private universities, Agusiady said. “Then the nation will lack intellectuals and thinkers.”Sommaliagustina also explained that a number of local private universities attempt to survive by lowering tuition fees, but they fall into a downward spiral as they still have to pay lecturers’ salaries, utility bills and other expenses that never decrease. “If it’s not the universities that are closed, classes are,” she added.Private university lecturers are particularly vulnerable, she toldUniversity World News.“They don’t have pensions and the other allowances that their counterparts in state universities have. Those who still teach in private universities do not do so mainly for an income, but because it is a moral calling,” she noted.House of Representatives member Lita Machfud Arifin called on the higher education ministry to set a limit on the number of students admitted through thejalur mandirichannel.“There should be a maximum number of students to be admitted throughjalur mandirito keep private universities playing their significant role in the country’s higher education,” she told the media in Jakarta on 20 October.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Network advocates for continental internationalisation plan
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Network advocates for continental internationalisation planGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFRICAAfrica needs a coordinated approach to internationalisationNETHERLANDSWill the reshuffle affect internationalisation work?GLOBALInternationalisation: Under assault but worth fighting forGLOBALHow the SDGs are redefining internationalisation in HESOUTH AFRICAInternationalisation in HE is not a simple proxy for qualityInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackAFRICANetwork advocates for continental internationalisation planClemence Manyukwe23 October 2025TweetThe African Network for Internationalisation of Education (ANIE) is proposing a continental internationalisation strategy and a fund for African higher education institutions to support their efforts as they set out to build cross-border collaborations.These were part of a package of resolutions or recommendations accepted at an ANIE conference from 8-10 October in Kampala, Uganda, themed, ‘Internationalisation in Challenging Times: Sustaining Impact, Driving Change; Towards New Futures for Africa’.In May, experts participating in a webinar of the Association of African Universities called for a coherent and coordinated continental internationalisation plan. The ANIE conference appeared to have moved stakeholders further in this direction.ANIE’s next step is to submit a comprehensive policy brief capturing these resolutions to the Human Resource, Education and Science Directorate of the African Union Commission (AUC), for consideration and alignment with its continental frameworks, including the Continental Education Strategy for Africa (2026-35) and the African Union’s Agenda 2063.Professor Goski Alabi, the chair of the ANIE board, said Africa had to rethink internationalisation practices in the light of geopolitical and technological shifts. As borders tighten and global inequalities persist, for instance, the task is not merely to internationalise in the traditional sense, but to Africanise internationalisation through embedding African values, priorities and realities into global engagement.“Our new future must be one where African universities are co-creators of global solutions, not peripheral participants,” she added.Against this background, the conference also resolved that African higher education institutions must integrate sustainability frameworks into their internationalisation strategies financially, institutionally and environmentally, so that partnerships endure beyond project cycles and donor funding.In addition, ANIE decided to focus on data-driven policy and research, as well as to develop common tools for monitoring, evaluation and impact assessment of internationalisation efforts, while its member institutions must commit to expanding and deepening intra-African academic collaborations, including joint research, dual degrees and staff-student exchanges, to strengthen continental cooperation and reduce dependency on extra-regional partners.Member institutions should also adopt digital, hybrid, and blended models for virtual exchanges and virtual internships to sustain engagement, enhance access, and reduce costs in teaching, learning, research, and partnership activities. This would require an investment in digital infrastructure and capacity-building, according to part of the resolutions which were shared withUniversity World News.A further resolution is that a continental data repository on internationalisation be established under ANIE’s coordination.Network for early-career researchersDuring the conference, an African network of early-career researchers known as the Network of Early-Career Researchers on Digitalisation and Internationalisation (NECREDI) was also established.The network was established as part of the European Commission’s funded Internationalisation and Digitisation of Graduate Education and Research in Africa (DigiGrad)programme, which is meant to enhance the training of African graduate students and young researchers for the attainment of global development goals.ANIE is responsible for the coordination and sustainability of the DigiGrad programme, a three-year initiativelaunchedin 2023 to help strengthen African research capacity focused on the sustainable development of the continent.In its work, ANIE is supported byOBREAL Global, a non-profit association that promotes interregional cooperation and dialogue in the higher education sector.Jaume Fortuny, a senior project manager at OBREAL who attended the ANIE conference, toldUniversity World Newsthat NECREDI is aimed at masters and PhD students, postdoctoral researchers and academics who obtained their PhD less than seven years ago with special attention to women and young people with disabilities affiliated with African institutions.“The Network of Early-Career Researchers on Digitalisation and Internationalisation is an initiative created within the framework of the European DigiGrad project (2023-26) with the aim of strengthening Africa’s role in global research by creating a community of young researchers committed to internationalisation and digital innovation,” he said.“DigiGrad has a series of activities being funded by the European Commission. These include training resources, researcher networks such as NECREDI and web portals with useful and updatable materials. All these project results will be maintained in the future, thanks to ANIE’s support for their sustainability. Not only that, but the resources, materials and networks resulting from the project have been designed to be expanded during and after the project’s funding period, as ANIE wants to establish itself as a true centre of excellence in education in Africa.”Fortuny said DigiGrad’s main achievements to date include the consolidation of a pan-African university cooperation network, integrating eight African and several European universities into a stable framework for exchange and joint training; institutional and human capacity-building, with multiple workshops on internationalisation management, digital tools and participation in global research networks; and production of digital resources and reference frameworks for integrating technology into postgraduate education, including online guides and training materials.He said the creation of the NECREDI network to bring together young African researchers in the fields of digitisation and internationalisation, offering mentoring, workshops and mobility opportunities, is another achievement.“The ANIE conference placed emphasis on creating resilient partnerships that bring long-term benefits to African higher-education institutions, highlighting the need to develop inclusive and equitable policy frameworks for international education. To improve Africa’s position in international education, leadership strategies for the global participation of African universities were discussed,” said Fortuny.“The influence of geopolitics on the financing of mobility and research has become increasingly evident. Shifting global power relations are reshaping cooperation priorities, often leaving African higher education systems to navigate competing interests and conditionalities. These dynamics expose the fragility of current funding models for research, innovation and cooperation which, despite European support, remain insufficient to ensure genuine autonomy and long-term sustainability for African institutions.”The conference also explored the issue of new funding models and, as part of its resolutions, said institutions and ANIE must collectively develop diversified funding strategies, engaging governments, the private sector, alumni, and development partners to support internationalisation.Internationalisation in AfricaANIE chair Alabi, providing historical context, talked of how internationalisation has evolved on the continent. She said that, when the organisation held its first conference 15 years ago, internationalisation of education in Africa was still an emerging conversation, but that it has become central to how institutions imagine their futures.Going forward, she said: “Internationalisation should not only ensure that our students and scholars are mobile, but also empowered, that our partnerships are equitable, and that our knowledge systems are recognised as contributors – not just consumers of global knowledge.“Internationalisation has a key role to play in transforming Africa’s higher education and research if it is focused on Africa’s priority needs," she said.According to Alabi Africa has been internationalising in all areas of higher education, ranging from transnational education and digital credentialing to epistemic plurality and grooming global citizens who shape Africa’s dwarfed voice in the scholarship of the world.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackCAMEROONMinistry seeks alternative scholarship funds after US cutsElias Ngalame23 October 2025TweetThe freeze on foreign aid in the United States has impacted the higher education sector in Africa, including in Cameroon where the government is seeking alternative funding strategies with a shift towards scholarship offers from other countries and donor institutions.The Cameroon Ministry of Higher Education has said the government had to look at alternatives, asking for increased scholarship openings from Commonwealth scholarship programmes and other partner countries such China, Turkiye, Russia and India to bridge the gap in funding for international studies and to bolster funding shortfalls created by this year’s United States Agency for International Development (USAID) cuts.“Following cuts in USAID funding, we had to solicit an increase in scholarship opportunities from other friendly countries. We are happy this is yielding fruits and our students are adapting to the changes,” Jacques Fame Ndongo, the minister of higher education, said at a media briefing ahead of the new academic year that started earlier in October.Ndongo said the USAID funding had been instrumental in promoting higher education in Cameroon, as in other countries in Africa, through collaborations with universities to advance research, community engagement, and capacity-building. He expressed regrets about the setbacks triggered by the cuts.“The USAID cuts for Cameroon have affected, not only the higher education sector, but also health and other local development programmes,” Ndongo said.According to anews report, of 43 projects, mainly in the health and education sectors, initially planned for Cameroon in 2025, 32 have been cancelled outright by the new US administration. This represents a drastic reduction of 74%, equivalent to a budget cut of US$51.13 million (approximately FCFA28.8 billion) from an initial budget of US$82 million.Overhaul of international aidThe shift in US policy aligns with a broader strategic recalibration. On 21 January this year, Washington announced an overhaul of its international aid approach, spearheaded by USAID. The immediate fallout included a 90-day suspension of funding, directly impacting Cameroon. The health sector, including training and research, the primary recipient of US assistance, is experiencing the most significant consequences, the news report said.According to officials of the Cameroon Ministry of Higher Education, the cuts have reduced educational opportunities, especially for marginalised groups such as girls and women.“The funding supported initiatives that helped us to improve school infrastructure, especially laboratories for research, provide learning materials, and train lecturers, thus impacting the quality of university education. Programmes specifically targeting girls’ and women’s education, which are crucial for promoting gender equality and broader socio-economic development, were also supported,” Professor Wilfred Nyongbet Gabsa, the secretary general in the ministry, toldUniversity World News.He said, apart from turning to other countries for scholarship opportunities, the government has also been soliciting private foundations for research grants and capacity-building support as well as garnering partnership support within Africa to help Cameroon’s universities become more self-reliant in research and funding.ScholarshipsMost of the 12 state universities have been encouraged to offer more scholarships and research grants to top students.According to Professor Horace Ngomo, the vice-chancellor of the University of Buea, the number of student beneficiaries of scholarships and grantsoffered via the universityhas increased as a result of the foreign aid squeeze.“Research grants are crucial to foster innovation and so we have stepped up the number of students benefiting from funding opportunities,” the vice-chancellor toldUniversity World News.“The University of Buea offers a variety of scholarships and fellowships designed to help students and their families pay for university. Scholarships and grants are types of gift aid that do not have to be repaid. They may take the form of university scholarships, or research state grants,” the university states on its webpage.Students have also been encouraged to look to other countries like the UK, Canada and China for higher education scholarship opportunities and to visit the ministry’swebsitefor announcements about university scholarship opportunities.A student site dubbedCampusJeunesalso offers to help students seeking scholarships abroad to access available opportunities.“There are many scholarship opportunities up for grabs. Students should regularly visit the website of the ministry of higher education for information. Meanwhile, we encourage students to also make individual efforts and those who merit it will certainly find openings elsewhere,” Ndongo said at the briefing.Meanwhile, private university officials say the cuts have also affected the private sector, especially in health research, climate change and agriculture projects that were receiving support.“Private universities have been doing a lot of partnership work in health research in areas like malaria and emerging diseases, thanks to funding and essential health interventions,” Dr Nick Ngwanyam, a surgeon and founder of the St Louis Clinic and University Institute, toldUniversity World News.He said that, through the Higher Education Solutions Network of USAID, funding in health research areas was previously available.Ban on non-governmental organisationsThe funding cuts have not only resulted in a reduction in international scholarship opportunities, but also funding for research and non-governmental organisations (NGOs) that support higher education training in Cameroon.Civil society officials say the situation in Cameroon has also been aggravated by the Cameroonian government’s restrictions on foreign aid to non-governmental organisations.“Universities have been getting support for research work from NGOs. But these opportunities have been considerably cut following recent restrictions on foreign funding by the government,” Dr Augustine Njamnshi, executive director of the African Coalition for Sustainable Energy and Access, or ACSEA, toldUniversity World News.Njamnshi was referring to theban announced last yearby Cameroon’s Minister of Territorial Administration, Paul Atanga Nji, of five NGOs accused of funding terrorist activities in the country.According tomedia reports, the announcement sparked outrage among civil society organisations, which called for a reversal of the suspension, which they described as “incomprehensible”.Some NGO experts say such funding restrictions also limit research funding opportunities from NGOs to university students. In Cameroon, many NGOs, such as the Cameroon Initiative for Research and Innovation, orCIRI, and the Center for International Forestry Research and World Agroforestry orCIFOR-ICRAF, inter alia, provide research scholarship opportunities for university students.A recentreport byUniversity World Newshighlighted the opportunities for researchers and students in the region provided by the work of CIFOR and ICRAF in partnership with a European Union research project known as RESSAC (French for Research in Ecology and Social Sciences) which supports the sustainable management of Central Africa’s forest ecosystems.However, the funding restrictions to NGOs by the government are limiting these opportunities, experts say.“Our CIFOR-ICRAF RESSAC programme, this year provided opportunities for 27 postdoctoral fellows from different universities in the Central African Congo Basin forest region. These researchers, through their work, improve the living conditions of the population, protecting and restoring forest resources.“However, restrictions on funding for NGOs will only go to limit the number of student beneficiaries of these research opportunities,” Dr Abdon Awono, a senior forest scientist at CIFOR-ICRAF, toldUniversity World News.These restrictions, academics say, have negatively affected higher education institutions that depended on support from NGOs in areas of research and infrastructure development.“These restrictions and the control of NGO funding have reduced support for university work that has been promoting educational quality, sustainable development and innovation, especially in research. Research students from the University of Yaounde II increasingly find it difficult to get funding for their research work,” Dr Christopher Nsoh Ndikum, of the University of Yaounde II, toldUniversity World News.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 喀麥隆政府尋求替代獎學金資金以應對美國援助減少的影響。由於美國國際開發署（USAID）削減了對喀麥隆的資助，教育部不得不向其他國家和捐助機構尋求更多獎學金機會，包括英聯邦、土耳其、中國、俄羅斯和印度。教育部長雅克·法梅·恩東戈指出，這些資金的減少不僅影響高等教育，還影響了健康和當地發展計劃，特別是對於邊緣化群體如女孩和女性的教育機會。政府也在尋求私營基金會的研究資助和內部合作，以提高喀麥隆大學的自我依賴能力。
