https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities make own decisions about foreign student fees
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities make own decisions about foreign student feesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNORWAYTuition fees take a toll on international student numbersNORWAYStakeholders feel sting of tuition fees for non-EU studentsSWEDENMarket forces at play: Tuition fees are a ‘game changer’NORWAYUniversities uncertain in the face of impending fee regimeNORWAYOpposition mounts over government’s non-EU student fee planNORWAYUniversities see sharp rise in applicants from abroadNORWAYHuge increase in funding for students from the Global SouthGLOBALNorway’s new academic partnerships with global SouthInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNORWAYUniversities make own decisions about foreign student feesJan Petter Myklebust22 October 2025TweetOngoing uncertainty about the university tuition fees to be charged to third country students embarking on studies in 2026 and 2027 means Norwegian universities are making their own decisions about fees – a situation attributed, in part, by higher education experts to the absence of a coherent national internationalisation strategy.In a surprise turnaroundreportedbyUniversity World Newsin June this year, the government announced a proposal to remove the full-cost funding requirement for students from outside the European Union and European Economic Area (EU/EEA) and allow higher education institutions to set the level of tuition fees they charge foreign students.The proposal arose out of concerns about a loss of international talent and the drop in international student numbers following the introduction of the full-cost fees policy introduced in 2023 by the former minister of research and higher education Ola Borten Moe.The number of third country students studying for a degree was 3,700 in 2022 and approximately 2,000 in the autumn of 2023 and in 2024.According to the Norwegian Directorate for Higher Education and Skills, by mid-2025, more than 800 third-country students had paid for education in Norway for one or more semesters, most of them for degree studies.In the autumn semester of 2024, 660 students from third countries paid tuition fees to take a degree study. Compared to the fall semester of 2023, there was a decrease in new paying students in degree programmes – from 427 students in fall 2023 to 324 students in fall 2024.However, the shift in policy signalled by new Minister of Higher Education and Research Sigrun Aasland in June was too late to benefit students who had already applied for a study place for 2025 and 2026 at full-cost fees.Tight timeframeThe current situation leaves higher education institutions, which usually announce the allocation of study places and tuition fee levels for 2026 and 2027 in November, with an application deadline for February or March the following year, with a challenge.The timeframe is now too tight for reduced tuition fees to be officially implemented, as such a measure requires a legislative change. It means that institutions will technically have to apply the full-cost fees model for the study years 2026 and 2027 – unless they think the political signals are so strong that action to reduce the fees could be taken immediately.Higher education institutions are also waiting for a formal decision on whether the budgetary cuts for 2023, 2024 and 2025, which amounted to NOK322.6 million (US$321.83 million), will be reversed by the new government.Whenasked in June by Liberal Party chair Abid Raja, Aasland said the budgetary cuts would not be reversed.She said: “Setting the tuition fees lower than today does not need to mean that the total income will be reduced. On the contrary, the institutions can recruit more tuition fee-paying students and thereby increase the total income.”Free tuition principleHowever, Henriette Johnstone, president of the Norwegian Students’ and Academics’ International Assistance (SAIH) organisation,told theKhrononews outlet that when the government announced that universities and the university colleges could decide fees themselves, this was understood to be something that would come into effect immediately or, at least, quickly.She said Norwegian higher education institutions were suffering severe losses of international students and were feeling “cheated” by the government’s actions. The SAIH has appealed to the government to reintroduce the principle of free tuition for everyone.This positionwas also articulatedby chair and vice-chair of the Young Academy of Norway Guro Nore Fløgstad and Bjørn Hallstein Holte, who took issue with an editorial inAftenpostenpublished on 6 August which recommended that the government retain the full-cost model for tuition fees.“Instead of reintroducing full-cost tuition fees for international students on dubious grounds, the government should, after the Autumn election, remove the tuition fees for all students and reverse the cut in budgets to the institutions,” they argued.“Then the institutions can recruit students based on qualifications rather than nationality and economic capacity. This will give a greater supply of potential research talents to Norwegian higher institutions, which also will benefit the students,” the explained.Head of the national student union (NSO) Sigve Næss Røtvold agreed, tellingUniversity World News: “We are still thinking that removing the tuition fees for international students is the only way to lift the future competence deficit in Norway.“Even if NSO is positive about the government allowing the institutions themselves to decide the level of the tuition fees, we are critical of the fact that students from outside the EU/EEA now are coming in a state of limbo, and it is not crystal clear when the legislation proposal will come and when the institutions can start the changes.”Breach of valuesOle Petter Ottersen, professor emeritus of Oslo University and a former rector of both Oslo University and the Karolinska Institutet in Stockholm who has been a spokesperson on research and higher education collaboration with the Global South, toldUniversity World Newsthe introduction of tuition fees for students from outside the EU/EEA was not just a “statistical issue” but represented “a serious breach of fundamental academic values”.Ottersen said: “Higher education is built on principles of openness, knowledge sharing, and international mobility. When education becomes a privilege for the few, we undermine universities’ role as global knowledge hubs.“Knowledge is a global public good, and Norway has both the resources and the moral responsibility to contribute to global justice. Many of the major societal challenges – climate change, pandemics, social inequality – require solutions grounded in diverse perspectives.“By imposing hurdles for students from the Global South, we lose insights and competencies that are crucial for sustainable development. Norwegian academia becomes more homogeneous, something SAIH and the Young Academy of Norway have strongly warned against,” he said.Ottersen argued that from a global justice perspective, tuition fees deepened existing inequalities. “Students from low-income countries already face structural barriers to accessing higher education. Charging full-cost fees in one of the world’s wealthiest nations reinforces a system where privilege determines access to knowledge.”He said this contradicts Norway’s commitments to the UN Sustainable Development Goals, particularly Goal 4 on inclusive and equitable quality education.“Education should not be treated as a commodity but as a shared responsibility for global progress,” Ottersen said.Institutional internationalisation strategyAsked what advice she could offer institutions grappling with the prospect of setting their own tuition fees for third country students, Associate Professor Dian Liu at the Department of Media and Social Sciences at the University of Stavanger, who has publishedresearchon international student tuition fees in Norway, said the recent proposals from government represent an important opportunity for Norwegian higher education institutions to rethink their internationalisation strategies.She said institutions should focus on their value proposition and consider tuition as part of a broader positioning strategy.“Tuition fees are not only about covering costs. They are also a signal of value, reflecting both academic quality and perceived international reputation.“This goes beyond the degree itself and includes aspects such as strong teaching and research qualifications, close ties to industry and high graduate employability, comprehensive student support (financial aid, scholarships, housing assistance, career guidance, integration programmes), and unique Norwegian experiences that contribute to students’ overall learning and wellbeing,” she said.Liu said it was also important for institutions to differentiate tuition fees by programme and institution.“Norwegian higher education institutions differ in their strategic priorities for international student recruitment (orientated towards volume and diversity or orientated towards prestige and global talent attraction). Likewise, not all programmes share the same cost structure or market appeal.“To reflect this diversity, tuition strategies should move beyond a uniform approach and adopt a tiered pricing model at programme level,” she toldUniversity World News, adding that premium programmes, or flagship offerings such as lab-based sciences and engineering, characterised by high international demand, significant operational costs, and strong graduate outcomes, should be priced at the higher end.“For market-rate programmes operating in fields with strong international competition (such as business, economics, and social sciences), tuition should be benchmarked against comparable universities in other European countries to remain competitive and attractive.“Finally, strategic programmes that are central to an institution’s identity or regional mission but have lower external demand may justify a subsidised, lower tuition fee to support long-term development,” she noted.Liu said institutions should collaborate nationally with transparency and data.“Norway’s higher education sector benefits from coordination rather than competition. The national task established by peak universities body Universities Norway (UHR) is a positive step towards developing a standardised methodology for calculating costs.“Institutions should adopt transparent, data-driven cost models that justify tuition levels and enable fair benchmarking across programmes. Sharing cost methodologies, market data, and good practices through national platforms will strengthen trust and ensure that Norway presents a coherent and competitive profile as a study destination.“Ultimately, setting tuition fees should be viewed as part of a long-term internationalisation strategy, which balances institutional autonomy, social responsibility, and Norway’s appeal as a destination for high-quality, inclusive, and research-informed education,” Liu stated.Institutional responses to feesUniversity World Newsreached out to Nord University, the University of Southern Norway, the Norwegian University of Science and Technology (NTNU) and the University of Stavangar, and asked them how they intended to set their fees for the 2026 and 2027 academic years.Pro-rector for education at NTNU Geir Egil Dahle Øien said: “In the government budget proposal for next year that was presented on 15 October, it is proposed that the ministry will work out a legalisation proposition to remove the demand for full cost fees. Since the law proposal is sent out for comments now during the autumn term we adhere to the current regulations when determining tuition fees.”Similar responses were given by the Nord University and the University of Southern Norway.At the Norwegian University of Life Sciences (NMBU), a meeting of the education committee on 14 October also proposed to keep the full cost tuition fees from the previous year in six categories and adjust the fees for inflation. This would set the lowest fee at NOK87,799 (US$8,700) per year for the international bachelor’s degrees and the highest fee at NOK284,057 per year in veterinary sciences.In a note to the committee seen byUniversity World News, the director of studies said NMBU compares their tuition fees “with comparable institutions in the Nordic countries and with partner universities in Europe such as Wageningen University and Research (WUR) in the Netherlands and The Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences”.The note also said that of the NOK8.5 million taken in in tuition fees in 2023 and 2024, NMBU has allocated NOK3.3 million to a project focused on recruitment of international students.Taking a different path, Director of Education at the University of Stavanger Bjarte Hoem toldUniversity World Newsthe university hasannouncedit will lower tuition fees for 2026 and 2027 when the admission application opens on 16 November 2025.The new pricing model includes two rates per year: NOK60,000 for humanities, social sciences, and economics/administrative subjects; and NOK 85,000 for natural sciences, technology, health, social work and sports, teacher education, and performing arts and media studies.The university website notes that while previously, universities were required to charge fees equivalent to full cost coverage, including all direct and indirect costs associated with the study programme over time, it became clear that “very few students from the affected countries” paid those amounts.“In 2025, only 13 out of 79 students from countries outside the EEA paid tuition fees at UiS. The others had scholarships or met the criteria for exemption,” it stated.National internationalisation strategyProfessor Bjørn Stensaker at the Department of Education at the University of Oslo, who was vice-rector for education from 2021 to 2025, toldUniversity World Newsthe current challenge was the lack of a coherent national strategy for internationalisation.“The government introduced tuition fees a couple of years ago as part of a generic cost-cutting in the sector. However, internationalisation is far more than an expenditure on the national budget.“For a small country such as Norway, internationalisation is crucial for quality, for diversity, for our national labour market, and for having the opportunity to create a dynamic learning environment for the domestic students.“At the moment, the economic dimensions of internationalisation are so dominant that the academic dimensions are not discussed at all. Given the current geopolitical situation, we need to rethink our national ambitions regarding internationalisation. This is job number one,” he stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: International student cap to cut GDP by €4bn, report warns
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: International student cap to cut GDP by €4bn, report warnsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNETHERLANDSCoalition parties back plan to keep international studentsNETHERLANDSProvince warns of dire impact of foreign student numbers capNETHERLANDSIndustry adds to criticism of HE cuts and language mandateNETHERLANDSDiscord over planned cuts to international student numbersNETHERLANDSHE groupings, unions join forces to fight austerity cutsNETHERLANDSForeign students and research funding face ‘shocking’ cutsGLOBALInternational students: Are countries throwing away the gold?DENMARKNew report documents net value of international studentsNORWAYNon-EU student tuition fee debate enters another roundSWEDENImpact of tuition fees for international students assessedGLOBAL: The real economic returns from foreign studentsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNETHERLANDSInternational student cap to cut GDP by €4bn, report warnsJan Petter Myklebust22 October 2025TweetAhead of a general election on 29 October to replace the existing caretaker government in the Netherlands, a new report provides evidence that an existing government proposal to cap international students will translate into huge financial losses for Dutch universities and the nation.SEO Amsterdam Economics found that restricting international student numbers would lead to an annual gross domestic product (GDP) loss of €4 billion (US$4.6 billion) to €5 billion (US$5.8 billion), far outweighing any short-term budget savings. Regions, businesses and society as a whole will feel the substantial economic consequences, it argues.It is estimated that the Randstad region, which is home to five major universities – Leiden University, Utrecht University, Erasmus University Rotterdam, University of Amsterdam, and Vrije Universiteit Amsterdam – and accounts for half of the Dutch GDP, will experience the worst effects of the caps – 82% of the loss will occur here, the report notes. The most severely affected sectors will be business services (39%), financial institutions (20%), and the public sector (10%).The report,Out of Balance without Internationalisation?, was commissioned by the five Randstad universities and was released in October this year.It speaks directly to the Dutch government’s controversial “Internationalisation in Balance” bill, which gives the government strong powers to intervene to reduce the inflow of international students – a move that will result in budget cuts to all universities.While an earlier proposal to introduce aToets Anderstalig Onderwijs(a test to examine whether a programme is allowed to be taught in a language that isn't Dutch) has been dropped from the bill, it will still require universities to report annually on their internationalisation strategies and gives power to the government to set conditions on English-taught programmes where necessary.The bill, proposed by former Minister of Education, Culture and Science Robbert Dijkgraaf, is expected to be discussed in the House of Representatives early next year, with possible consideration in the Senate in mid-2026.National talent strategyThe position of the country’s universities, which have pushed to retain some control over the number of international students they admit, has been bolstered by the latest SEO report.“International students are vital to the Netherlands’ earning capacity and long-term prosperity. They contribute directly to the quality and diversity of higher education, help fill persistent labour market shortages in key sectors such as business services, finance, healthcare, government, and education, and strengthen the international position of the Dutch knowledge economy,” said Femke van Zijst, information officer at Universities Netherlands (UNL), a trade group of government-funded research universities.“That is why Dutch universities urge policymakers to take a long-term, evidence-based view and develop a National Talent Strategy that recognises international students as essential contributors to the country’s economic and societal vitality both for the Randstad and the country.”Van Zijst toldUniversity World Newsin an email that Dutch universities had developed a “self-regulation proposal” published in April 2025 that gives institutions “the tools to responsibly manage international student intake within national frameworks.“The proposal allows universities to adjust recruitment and admissions at the programme level, taking into account regional context, labour market needs, and teaching capacity,” she said.“This differentiated, data-informed approach prevents one-size-fits-all national caps and ensures that the Netherlands remains both attractive and sustainable as a study destination.“Several universities have decided to switch specific programmes to Dutch, mainly larger programmes like psychology or business.“Universities have already begun implementing measures such as improved intake planning, selective English-taught provision and offering language programmes for their staff to learn the Dutch language,” said Van Zijst.Asreported earlier byUniversity World News, the proposal by UNL that Randstad universities scrap a number of English-taught programmes – a proposal that heavily affects bachelor psychology programmes at five major universities – was met with strong criticism from academics and students.Contributions of international studentsAccording to the SEO Amsterdam Economics report, the share of international graduates who stay in the Netherlands and contribute to the economy during the first year after graduation has risen from 40% (cohort 2017 to 2018) to 57% (cohort 2022 to 2023).“After five years, 25% still live in the Netherlands, of whom 80% have paid employment,” the report states.It argues that in addition to the impact on the labour market and associated GDP effects, fewer available highly educated international personnel are expected to have a negative impact on the business climate in the country.It notes that other research shows that approximately 30% of companies with a relatively large number of theoretically trained foreign employees have indicated that they will relocate growth plans and/or business activities abroad if they cannot attract sufficient talent.While no research was conducted into the consequences for the housing market for students, the report states it is possible that fewer international students would “reduce the pressure on the housing market for students, resulting in shorter waiting lists and potentially lower rents”.It also pointed to a “net saving for the central government” of €80 to €132 million, although it points out that such a saving – calculated by examining lost taxes and premiums and savings on social benefits, education funding and student loans was “small compared to the GDP loss”.The report notes that consequences of fewer international students that were “less economic in nature, such as the impact on the quality of education and accessibility of university education” were not examined.Those benefits were outlined by Margrethe Jonkman, chair of the executive board of Vrije Universiteit (VU) Amsterdam, whotoldVU News: “International students enrich our education. The world is becoming increasingly interconnected. As a university, we believe it is important that students gain experience with different perspectives during their studies.“This experience is not only crucial for personal development but also essential for future careers and valuable from the perspective of companies and organisations. That is why we want to work together to ensure the necessary intake of international students, who are of great importance to both the region and the Randstad.”Annelien Bredenoord, chair of the executive board of Erasmus University Rotterdam and a key stakeholder in research, also voiced strong opposition to any move that restricts international student numbers.“Companies need well-qualified employees across various sectors – not just engineers, but economists, business administrators, and psychologists alike,” she said.Bredenoord also advocates for a comprehensive national talent strategy that values diverse educational backgrounds as much as technical skills. She warns that with a demographic decline leading to fewer Dutch students, other countries are ramping up efforts to lure international talent.“The Netherlands must do everything possible to maintain its appeal to global talent,” she toldFranetic.Investment in science and higher educationReacting to the SEO report, Associate Professor Eddie Brummelman from the University of Amsterdam, who is chair of the Dutch Young Academy, toldUniversity World Newsthe academy had published several statements on the devastating consequences of budget cuts in science and higher education and the reduction in the number of international students.“We will have elections soon. In thisstatement, we urge political parties (and the to-be-formed coalition) to invest in science and higher education.”Brummelman said it is only with science and scholarship that the Netherlands can tackle the major challenges of tomorrow.“After all, science not only boosts the economy, it also strengthens the resilience of Dutch society. Therefore, we urge the government to invest at least 3% of GDP in research and development (like our neighbouring countries do), to invest in education, and to protect and invest in international talent,” he noted.Brummelman also referred to astatementin which the academy called for the government to scrap the Internationalisation in Balance bill and preserve internationalisation to strengthen Dutch society.“In public discourse, internationalisation has been mentioned in relation to several problems facing universities and the cities in which they operate.“Crucially, however, the extent to which internationalisation causes or contributes to these problems is often exaggerated, conflated with other issues, or unsupported by facts.“This has led, particularly in political discourse, to an atmosphere in which the [bill], as well as the recent self-regulation measures, no longer appear to genuinely address the actual problems but merely serve as an instrument to keep international students out. We call on academics to join together in resisting this harmful atmosphere and policy,” he added.Göran Melin, a higher education expert at the Technopolis Group in Stockholm, said all evidence points to "significant economic and social gain from international students, as long as admittance and integration are careful and follow sound principles.“This is true for the Netherlands just as well as for other Western countries with declining populations in parallel to the business sector’s loudly expressed demand for a highly educated and qualified workforce,” Melin said.“Cutting the numbers of international students just for the sake of receiving fewer must be seen as the most counterproductive politics. As the leading Dutch universities furthermore point out, education quality will certainly suffer too, besides the obvious measurable negative effects on the national economy.”A ‘less alarming’ context?Hans de Wit, professor emeritus and distinguished fellow at the Boston College Center for International Higher Education in the United States, toldUniversity World Newsthe upcoming elections were important in respect of the Internationalisation in Balance Bill and the higher education budget.“As the three parties which were most in favour of the law and budget cuts will almost certainly not be in the next government (the PVV [Party for Freedom] of Wilders, although in the polls still the biggest, excluded by the others, and BBB [Farmer Citizen Movement] and NCS [New Social Contract] encountering extremely big losses), there might be a less alarming context than is currently the case, although one cannot be too optimistic either,” he said.Jo Ritzen, a professorial fellow of International Economics of Science, Technology and Higher Education at the UNU Maastricht Economic and Social Research Institute on Innovation and Technology (UNU-MERIT) and Maastricht Graduate School of Governance, a previous minister for education and science and former president of Maastricht University, toldUniversity World Newsthere could be no better way for the Dutch government “to shoot itself in the foot than by limiting the number of foreign students” in the country.He said the long-run impact of the move would be “devastating”.“Europe needs more social cohesion to maintain its democracy and its competitiveness in the arena where the US and China are increasingly dominant. Increasing cross-border studies is an effective means. Limiting foreign students means loss of cohesion and competitiveness,” he noted.Ritzen said the “proper answer” to “too many foreign students” was: “Manage by diplomacy and compromise to have more European funding for foreign European students” and “encourage Dutch students to study abroad”.Student housingIndependent higher education strategist Peter van der Hijden said while the report from SEO represented “compelling arguments” and that it was clear that international students bring cross-sector benefits “outweighing costs by far”, the housing shortage was the “killer argument” – a “big unease that is played out in the asylum seeker debate as well, especially now in the run-up to the 29 October elections”.Van der Hijden said the issue of student housing was also on the radar of EU Housing Commissioner Dan Jørgensen, who will present his European Affordable Housing Plan in early 2026.“It is high time to consider unorthodox measures, accelerated procedures and public-private partnerships.The Class Foundationbased in Amsterdam has made a number of suggestions for an ‘EU Action Plan to Student Housing’,” he said.“Next to that, my mantra: Universities for their part should open their degree-locked learning offer so that certified citizen-learners become allies and feel part of the local academic community,” he concluded.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Time’s up: Seven of nine universities reject Trump’s deal
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Time’s up: Seven of nine universities reject Trump’s dealGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsUS Education Secretary Linda McMahon. Image: The White House, Wikimedia Commons.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESHE leaders brand Trump’s compact for HE as ‘extortion’UNITED STATESBrown University is second to reject Trump’s HE compactUNITED STATESMIT rejects White House funding terms, other schools mullUNITED STATESCould things get darker still for US higher education?UNITED STATESKirk assassination raises the political stakes for HEUNITED STATESToday it is UCLA, but who is the next target for Trump?UNITED STATESTrump proposes 90% cut to funding of tribal collegesUNITED STATESTrump’s ‘Big Bill’ to cost students, universities billionsUNITED STATESUS universities face a precipice under a Trump presidencyInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackUNITED STATESTime’s up: Seven of nine universities reject Trump’s dealNathan M Greenfield22 October 2025TweetMonday’s deadline set by United States Education Secretary Linda McMahon for responses to the ‘Compact for Academic Excellence in Higher Education’ (Compact) passed, with seven of the nine universities having categorically rejected McMahon’s signature policy for restructuring American higher education.The Compact, whichMcMahon touted as a “proactive improvement of higher education for the betterment of the country”, has been roundly criticised by university and political leaders as well as by dozens of national organisations as being a threat to university autonomy and academic freedom.Of the other two, one – Vanderbilt University – has taken a wait-and-see attitude, while the other – the University of Texas, Austin (UTA), which had been expected to sign onto the Compact – has not made a public statement.UTA’s silence is curious because just hours after the Compact was unveiled, Kevin Eltife, chair of the University of Texas System board of regents, which governs UTA,said the university was “honoured” to have been invited to sign it.The Compact would see universities and colleges commit to objective admissions processes (for example, standardised tests) and reject the use of proxies for race, nationality, sexual orientation or gender identity.It would also require the adoption of institutional neutrality policies that require faculty and staff to “abstain from actions or speech relating to societal and political events” on issues that do not directly affect the institution.In the name of preserving “a vibrant marketplace of ideas”, theCompactwould require “revising governance structures . . . [and] abolishing institutional units [that is, departments or offices] that purposely punish, belittle, and even spark violence against conservative ideas”, as well as conducting rigorous, “broad spectrum” assessments of “viewpoints among faculty, students and staff at all levels” and making these findings public.Under the Compact, international undergraduate enrolment would be cut to 15%, with no more than 5% from any one country, and universities will need to “screen out students who demonstrate hostility to the United States, its allies, or its values; and to provide instruction in American civics to all foreign students”.Higher education sector pushes backAsUniversity World Newsreported on 8 October, the Compact has been denounced by university leaders, leaders of national organisations like the American Association of University Professors (AAUP) and the American Association of American Colleges and Universities, as well as politicians like Californian Governor Gavin Newsom and international scholars of education like Michael Ignatieff, who had been rector between 2016 and 2021 at the Central European University when it was forced to move from Budapest, Hungary, to Vienna, Austria, because of the policies of Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán.The AAUP said, for example, that the “offer to give preferential treatment to colleges and universities in exchange for allegiance to a partisan ideological agenda stinks of favouritism, patronage, and bribery”.For his part, Ignatieff said: “The guys who said that culture is upstream from politics understand something deeply about politics. What’s happening is that they’ve decided that the way to secure power as a group is to take charge of the commanding institutions of intellectual life.“They believe that the elites were educated [and] are educated in the great universities. And, if they change the education and the great universities, they will change the elites and solidify a permanent kind of domination of American politics. They have a big strategic vision of what they’re trying to do, and the compact is part of it.”On 17 October, the American Council on Education issued a statement co-signed by 35 other national organisations, including the Council of Catholic Colleges and Universities, the Council of Christian Colleges & Universities (which represents conservative evangelical colleges), the President’s Alliance on Higher Education and Immigration, and the Association of American Law Schools.After noting that signatories span the “breadth of the American higher education community”, thestatementdeclares in part: “The compact offers nothing less than government control of a university’s basic and necessary freedoms – the freedoms to decide who we teach, what we teach, and who teaches – as outlined by [Supreme Court] Justice Frankfurter seventy-five years ago.“The compact is just the kind of excessive federal overreach and regulation, to the detriment of state and local input and control, [that] this administration says it is against.”Institutions explain rejectionCiting threats to theiracademic freedom, seven universities – the University of California, Los Angeles (UCLA), the Massachusetts Institute of Technology (MIT), Brown University, Dartmouth University, the University of Virginia (UVA), the University of Pennsylvania and the University of Arizona – turned down the Compact that the administration promised would ensure their access to federal funds.In a statement made last week, after noting that the college’s motto is“Vox clamantis in deserto”– “A voice crying in the wilderness” (Isaiah 50:35) – Dartmouth’s president, Sian Leah Beilock,informed the campus communitythat she had told McMahon in a phone call that Dartmouth “does not believe that a compact – with any administration – is the right approach to achieve academic excellence, as it would compromise our academic freedom, our ability to govern ourselves, and the principle that federal research funds should be awarded to the best, most promising ideas.”Aware that his university, UVA, is the subject of a number of civil rights investigations by McMahon’s department (dealing primarily with the university’s now defunct diversity, equity and inclusion [DEI] practices) and that the former president, Jim Ryan, wasforced from officebecause of his support for DEI, Paul Mahoney, UVA’s interim president, kicked off his refusal with what amounts to a précis of McMahon’s letter.“We also agree with many of the principles outlined in the Compact, including a fair and unbiased admissions process, an affordable and academically rigorous education, a thriving marketplace of ideas, institutional neutrality, and equal treatment of students, faculty, and staff in all aspects of university operations”.Yet, he too turned down McMahon’s offer. “We seek no special treatment in exchange for our pursuit of those foundational goals. The integrity of science and other academic work requires merit-based assessment of research and scholarship.“A contractual arrangement predicating assessment on anything other than merit will undermine the integrity of vital, sometimes lifesaving, research and further erode confidence in American higher education.”Also rejecting the Compact, MIT’s president, Sally Kornbluth, reminded McMahon of MIT’s “record of service to the nation” in both the health and security fields and that it provides free tuition to students who come from families that earn less than US$200,000 a year.The Compact,Kornbluth wrote: “also includes principles with which we disagree, including those that would restrict freedom of expression and our independence as an institution. And fundamentally, the premise of the document is inconsistent with our core belief that scientific funding should be based on scientific merit alone.“In our view, America’s leadership in science and innovation depends on independent thinking and open competition for excellence. In that free marketplace of ideas, the people of MIT gladly compete with the very best, without preferences. Therefore, with respect, we cannot support the proposed approach to addressing the issues facing higher education”.Vanderbilt University, by contrast, took alegalistic approach, seizing on McMahon’s words in her letter accompanying the Compact: “we welcome limited, targeted feedback to ensure mutual alignment” (of administrations in Washington and campuses).On Monday 20 October, Daniel Diermeier, Vanderbilt’s chancellor, wrote in a public letter to his university: “Despite reporting to the contrary, we have not been asked to accept or reject the draft compact. Rather, we have been asked to provide feedback and comments as part of an ongoing dialogue, and that is our intention.”Still, he wrote: “Since the compact could directly affect our teaching, research and other operations, our policy of institutional neutrality not only permits comment by the university’s leadership, it encourages it … Vanderbiltwill continue to share our point of view with the administration.”Federal benefitsIn none-too-subtle language,McMahon said that “multiple positive benefits”of signing the Compact include an “allowance for increased overhead payments where feasible, substantial and meaningful federal grants, and other federal partnerships”.Universities that do not agree to the Compact, McMahon wrote, are “free to develop models and values other than those”, but by doing so, they “elect … to forgo federal benefits”.In addition to making the same arguments about academic freedom and university autonomy as the other six presidents who gave the Compact a thumbs down, Suresh Garimella, president of the University of Arizona, took aim at McMahon’s statement that not agreeing to the compact would cut the university off from federal funds.“A federal research funding system based on anything other than merit would weaken the world’s preeminent engine for innovation, advancement of technology, and solutions to many of our nation’s most profound challenges. We seek no special treatment and believe in our ability to compete for federally funded research strictly on merit,” wrote Garimella.In itsanalysis of the Compact, the Knight First Amendment Institute at Columbia University said: “Requiring universities to give up their First Amendment rights [which the speech provision and government intrusion into discipline and curriculum matters do] – and those of their community members – in order to receive benefits from the government” is unconstitutional.Further, the institute said, the Constitution “prohibits the government from conditioning benefits – including discretionary benefits to which a recipient has no right – on relinquishing or waiving their constitutional rights, First Amendment or otherwise.”The institute argued that the Compact would give the government powers it presently does not have under legislation to withdraw benefits if it deems that a university violated the Compact.“The compact would allow the government to withdraw benefits or exact penalties on institutions without the regular processes required under governing statutes – and indeed, the compact gives the Department of Justice complete discretion to review and find an institution in noncompliance without any required process, standards, oversight, or other check.”To read more details about the terms of the Compact, clickhere.Receive email updates from 美國七所大學拒絕特朗普高等教育協議

根據美國教育部長琳達·麥克馬洪的要求，九所大學中有七所已於截止日期前拒絕了特朗普政府提出的「高等教育卓越協議」。該協議旨在重構美國高等教育，但遭到大學及政治領袖的廣泛批評，被指控威脅學術自由和大學自主權。拒絕協議的大學包括加州大學洛杉磯分校、麻省理工學院和布朗大學等。大學領導人強調，協議條款將限制自由表達和獨立運作，並要求以學術成就為基礎的資金分配。這一拒絕行為可能對未來的聯邦資金分配及高等教育政策產生深遠影響。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Tuition fees to rise in Labour bid to fix higher education
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Tuition fees to rise in Labour bid to fix higher educationGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED KINGDOMEnglish universities lose international student levy fightUNITED KINGDOMSkills crisis without high-level university apprenticeshipsUNITED KINGDOMWarnings on ‘unsustainable’ international tuition fee hikesUNITED KINGDOMUniversity chiefs partly to blame for funding woes – ExpertsUNITED KINGDOMUniversity head calls for university tuition fee shake-upUNITED KINGDOMIs the UK on the verge of a new age of apprenticeships?UNITED KINGDOMParties divided on best way out of the HE funding impasseInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOMTuition fees to rise in Labour bid to fix higher educationNic Mitchell22 October 2025TweetAfter a lengthy wait, the Labour government finally unveiled its plans to fix the higher education system in England with a landmark Post-16 Education and Skills White Paper.It aims to put vocational education on an equal footing with academic study and offers universities some financial relief by increasing home tuition fees in line with inflation – but only if they meet certain quality thresholds.With nearly half of UK higher education institutions heading for deficits without action to shore up their finances, university leaders gave a broad welcome to the rise in tuition fees for the next two academic years – with the government pledging legislation so that they automatically increase with inflation after those two years.However, not everyone is happy with indexing tuition fees to inflation, with one expert calling it a “short-term fix” that fails to address fundamental problems with funding higher education and research on the backs of home and international students paying higher tuition fees.The inflation-proof increases will take home tuition fees for students attending universities in England up to nearly £10,000 (US$13,380) from 2026 to 2027. They are currently £9,535, having gone up this year for the first time since 2017.ConditionsBut there will be restrictions imposed on tuition-fee caps when legislation is brought before parliament, and only institutions that meet tougher new quality thresholds set by the Office for Students (OfS) in England will be able to charge the maximum amount.In a statement to MPs, Secretary of State for Education Bridget Phillipson said: “Charging full fees will be conditional on high-quality teaching, balancing stability for universities with fairness for students and taxpayers.”A government press release issued on behalf of Phillipson elaborated on this point, adding that where standards fall short, the OfS will act quickly to stop the expansion of “low-quality courses” and hold providers to account.“Universities that underperform could face financial and regulatory consequences, ensuring public money is spent only on courses that deliver for students and the economy,” thepress releasestated.The white paper said that maintenance loans will also rise with inflation, and the government has previously announced that means-tested maintenance grants will be reintroduced for disadvantaged students, to be funded by a 6% levy on international student fees, asUniversity World Newshas alreadyreported.The government framed the white paper as an attempt to boost opportunity for disadvantaged students, hold universities to account and put the sector on a firm financial footing.But University and College Union (UCU) General Secretary Jo Grady accused the government of doubling down on “the disastrous tuition-fees funding model, which created the crisis the sector is currently facing”.New vocational qualificationsThewhite paperwas unveiled in two halves on Monday 20 October, with details first emerging about the new V-levels – new vocational qualifications to bridge the gap between the more technical T-levels and the more academic A-levels for 16 to 18 year-old students.According to the Department for Education (DfE), the new vocational qualifications will provide “a clear option for young people who want to get on in the world of work, university or apprenticeships” but wish to explore different key sectors, such as engineering, agriculture, digital or creative, before choosing where to specialise.The V-levels are also a key part of a drive by current Labour Prime Minister Sir Keir Starmer to get two-thirds of young people participating in higher-level learning, whether that be academic, technical or an apprenticeship.This replaces a previous Labour goal of getting 50% of young people into higher education and aims to put vocational education and training on an equal footing with degree-level academic education.GuildHE, which champions diversity in UK higher education and represents 70 institutions – from universities and colleges to specialist providers – welcomed what it called “the clear and consistent focus within the white paper on the need to protect and preserve diversity in the sector”.Dr Brooke Storer-Church, GuildHE chief executive, toldUniversity World Newsthat linking tuition fees to inflation was “a positive step towards recognising the increased investment needed in higher education”.However, she said it needed to be complemented by improved public funding for institutions “since the benefits of higher education reach far beyond the individuals undertaking study”.“This is particularly true of those undertaking vocational and technical programmes, many of which are provided through higher education institutions.“Our members focus almost exclusively on vocational and technical programmes and we welcome the Government’s commitment to increase the number of young people undertaking higher-level training and will work with officials to understand the implications within the overall figure,” she noted.Storer-Church has agreed to sit on the newly formed Higher Education Access and Participation Task and Finish Group, which will help the government push through its reforms to post-16 education and skills and help to drive up access and drive out low-quality provision and improve collaboration between higher education and further education.“A more joined-up system requires reforms to regulation so that it actively incentivises meaningful collaboration and reduces differential burdens,” said Storer-Church.International student levyThe Russell Group of 24 research-intensive UK universities described the white paper as “an important milestone” and said it was ready to work with the government to “support more collaboration with further education, maximising the benefits of our collective teaching expertise and local partnerships in a joined-up skills ecosystem”.The Russell Group’s outgoing Chief Executive, Dr Tim Bradshaw, toldUniversity World News: “To do this, there are urgent financial challenges to tackle.“That's why we’re pleased to see tuition fees linked to inflation moving forward, as a first step in putting the sector on a more financially sustainable footing.”However, Bradshaw urged the government to rethink the proposedinternational tuition fee levy, saying it will seriously hamper universities' ability to “invest in students and communities".Short-term fixDr Diana Beech, a former government advisor on higher education and now director of the Finsbury Institute at City St George’s, University of London, toldUniversity World News: “The proposal to increase fees was the most obvious lever the government could pull, albeit risky because it means it will be heading into the next general election as the government that put fees up to over £10k.”Beech said what is missing in the white paper is “outside the box” thinking about how to fund universities.She toldUniversity World Newsthat the government has acknowledged the financial pressures facing higher education institutions, but it stops short of offering a long-term funding settlement.“Indexing tuition fees to inflation is a short-term fix that risks deepening inequalities unless accompanied by broader reform.“I mean, we can’t just keep putting fees up and up until they go over £20k!“It's kicking the can down the road for a future government to deal with. So, I would have urged ministers to consider a sustainable funding model that supports both teaching and research and protects the civic and social missions of universities – potentially bringing in employers more to pay for it, given this whole strategy seems to be about delivering for their skills needs,” she stated.Beech also said regional authorities should get more of a look-in if the government is serious about devolution and questioned the wisdom of increasing the regulatory and fee-setting powers of the Office for Students, which has come in for stinging criticism in recent years, asUniversity World Newshasreported.Greater collaborationProfessor Andre Spicer, executive dean of the Bayes Business School at City St George’s, University of London, summarised what he described as “no dramatic changes, but lots of smaller interventions” in a post about the white paper onLinkedIn.Apart from the modest increase in tuition fees, which will still leave a gap in university finances, Spicer predicted greater focus on collaboration between universities in course provision and research, which “probably means research grants will go to cross-university collaborations and teaching may be delivered in partnership”.He also said the call to move away from generalist models, where all universities appear the same, “to a focus on specialising” could lead to clear divisions between research-intensive teaching and applied research institutions, and specialist and teaching-only institutions.The renewed focus on the Lifelong Learning Entitlement (LLE) will increase the focus on increasing access to higher education “from those outside the system, mainly through new routes from lower-level qualifications”.There is also “more scepticism towards franchising arrangements” (which have come under the microscope recently).“My guess [is] the upshot could [be] in 20 years’ time [that] the UK uni sector will have fewer institutions, they will have more differentiated business models, there will be regional lead institutions, research will involve big science across multiple institutions (versus individuals or small teams competing), unis will educate a wider age range, [and] there will be clearer links to government priorities, external audit and oversight,” said Spicer.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs atwww.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticleHow the LLM evolution is driving AI applicationIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: IAUP at 60: Presidents seek common ground on HE in AI era
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: IAUP at 60: Presidents seek common ground on HE in AI eraGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALUniversities at a turning point in an era of AI insecurityGLOBALLeaders mull how to balance power of AI with real learningGLOBALIAUP report examines the challenges women face as HE leadersGLOBALNew IAUP president faces some new, some older challengesGLOBALUnderstanding local needs can give universities the edgeGLOBALHE leaders must be able to succeed amid uncertaintyInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALIAUP at 60: Presidents seek common ground on HE in AI eraNathan M Greenfield21 October 2025TweetSix months before the turn of the millennium, Donald Gerth, then president of the International Association of University Presidents (IAUP), speaking at the 12th triennial meeting of the organisation founded in 1965, drew a distinction between ‘internationalisation’ and ‘globalisation’, both of which were buzzwords at the time.Universities in Central and Western Europe had been doing “internationalisation” since the mediaeval period, said Gerth, who was also president of California’s Sacramento State University (1984-2003).Internationalisation included “faculty and university exchanges, internationalisation of the curriculum, [and] inter-institutional arrangements”, as well as “research and creative activities related to other countries [and] cultures” – practices that the career of the Dutchman, Desiderius Erasmus, namesake of the European Union’s student exchange programme, who was educated at the University of Paris and taught and worked at Oxford University between 1498 and 1500, exemplifies.Gerth, a political scientist who specialised in the history of education, knew that his definition of globalisation – which was distinct from internationalisation – constituted a challenge to institutions that for more than a millennium had used the term ‘extra-mural’ to mean the world beyond their walls that was to be kept from impinging on the ‘intra-mural’ life of the mind. But this definition could stand as the IAUP’s raison d’être.Knowing one or two foreign languages didn’t cut it. “Globalisation has to do with a simply stated fact: universities, our faculties, work in a global marketplace, as well as national or regional ones,” Gerth said.He did not use ‘marketplace’ in its economic sense but, rather, “as a word encompassing culture, politics, economics, science, and work and the leisure place”, he said in 1999.Gerth reminded the university leaders meeting in Brussels of several of the markers that IAUP had laid down over the years, including working with the United Nations Commission on Disarmament Education, Peace and Conflict Resolution.Though peace “escaped our grasp” as the Cold War ended and regional and civil wars broke out, he urged the IAUP’s members “to teach about peace, peace which is more than the absence of war, peace as an extension of human decency with healthy economic, cultural, social and environmental conditions as a foundation”.Establishing common groundAccording to the IAUP president-elect, Dr Tomás Morales, president of California State University, San Bernardino, and a former president of the College of Staten Island (New York) between 2007 and 2012: “For the past 60 years the IAUP has been a place where presidents have been able to meet, establish common ground and seek to learn from and benefit from each other.”Morales toldUniversity World News: “In today’s world, where higher education in the United States and internationally is being questioned for its relevance and its costs, presidents at these meetings have the opportunity to learn what is happening in other countries and continue to position higher education to meet the needs of students, countries and societies.”Since the IAUP’s inaugural meeting in London, which brought together 120 university presidents, provosts and chancellors from 21 countries, its membership has fluctuated, falling in 2021 to 60 members.During the 2021-2024 IAUP presidency headed by Fernando León García, president of CETYS University, the organisation began to bounce back from the impact of COVID-19 as membership more than doubled and grew to more than 150 institutions, in particular with a wide array of leadership-related initiatives.Shawn Chen, IAUP president for 2024 to 2027 and president of SIAS University in Xinzheng, China, said his goal is to double the membership to at least 300.“But this is very ambitious, especially given the very different realities of our membership.”Pornchai Mongkhonvanit, who is the president of Siam University (Bangkok) and was president of the IAUP from 2005 to 2008, explained that the economic situation in countries neighbouring his own – Laos, Cambodia and Vietnam – requires that the IAUP have a flexible dues policy.“If we charge the same rate, then there might be problems with them becoming members. So, we have scholarships or lower membership [fees] for some developing economies,” Mongkhonvanit said.Global reachThe IAUP’s membership numbers tell only part of the story of its reach. From the mid-1990s, it has participated in events such as the UNESCO World Higher Education Conference and the Conference on Human Resource Development in the Pacific Basin.For decades it has run in-person training sessions and, in recent years, online sessions. Even before the COVID pandemic began ebbing, in July 2021, the IAUP mounted a two-day virtual conference titled “Innovation and Inclusion: Key Priorities for Higher Education in a Post-Pandemic World”, in which 80 experts worked with 430 senior university leaders from 20 countries.Sessions included “Leadership and governance during the pandemic and post-pandemic” and “Fundraising and institutional development during the pandemic and post-pandemic”, “Trends in internationalisation during the pandemic and post-pandemic” and “Inclusion/social responsibility during the pandemic and post-pandemic”.In 2022, the IAUP cohosted a webinar series with the Chicago School of Professional Psychology, which explored the impact of the pandemic on students’ mental health and what universities could do to increase the size of the community that mental health providers can support and increase the number of those providers.In May 2023, IAUP hosted a two-day in-person semi-annual meeting in Egypt under the theme of “Transforming global higher education through quality, innovation, and inclusion” that was attended by participants from 18 countries from the Americas, Africa, Europe, and Asia.Although, as Mongkhonvanit put it, “much of what universities do is uniform”, the diversity among them and among regions led the IAUP to establish regional meetings and regional chairs who report to the general meeting.When Mongkhonvanit was regional chair of Southeast Asia, for example, the IAUP worked with a cluster of universities to modernise the business curriculum in the context of traditional regional practices.“We had new ways of doing business. But, we also had the traditional Southeast Asia model of recognising the local wisdom, of trading among ourselves and trading with India and trading with China.“We worked to develop a common curriculum for the MBA [master of business administration] that included both old ways of doing things and new ways,” Mongkhonvanit toldUniversity World News.Upon completion, the project was presented to the general meeting so it could serve as a model for other regional projects.Regional realities also account for the way the IAUP approaches academic freedom. As a signatory to theCharter of the United Nations and the Challenges of the International Association of University Presidents, the IAUP pledged “commitment to human rights, among them freedom of inquiry, opinion, and speech” – constituents of “academic freedom.”On the ground, the IAUP recognises that academic freedom has different meanings in different countries and regions.“Frankly speaking,” Mongkhonvanit said, “in my neighbouring country Myanmar, there are problems with academic freedom (as well as with the freedom of the people themselves); while formally, Myanmar’sconstitution protects freedom of speech, for example, there are a number of laws and practicesthat restrict it and criminalise criticism of the government.On the other side of the world, Hungary has redefined academic freedom in such a way that any research that does not accord with the narrative approved by Prime Minister Viktor Orbán isillegitmate, if not actually a danger to the state.”During his time as president, Mongkhonvanit said he was aware of a number of professors who had to flee their countries.The IAUP cannot use the same standard for academic freedom across the board, he said. “But at least we can try to make sure that things are not getting worse.”The IAUP and member institutions may lack the “force” to extend academic freedom, but they can defend academic freedom through their academic and intellectual work,” he toldUniversity World News.Significant impact on women’s leadershipWhile some of the IAUP’s initiatives have not been as successful as hoped – for example, opposition from the United States stymied the disarmament initiative the IAUP was part of under the auspices of the United Nations starting in 2014, Dr Ichiro Tanioka, senior administrator of Osaka University (Japan), explained – others, like the programme to train women university leaders, have had a significant impact.“I recall that early in this century, the environmental problem and women's issues kept coming up in our executive committee meetings. We could not deal with environmental issues because IAUP includes many developing countries and developed countries that had different concepts about the environment,” Tanioka stated.Women’s leadership issues, by contrast, Tanioka said in an interview withUniversity World News, “were easier to deal with” in part because there were strong women leaders like Devorah Lieberman, president of the University of La Verne (California) from 2011 to 2023.In June 2022, the IAUP mounted a Leadership Development Programme (LDP) in Mexico City.One of the participants, Rosa Alejandra García Bado, then the incoming rector ofUniversidad Marista de Guadalajara, credited the programme with inspiring her to set aside her first 100 days in office to stabilise the struggling university and “to listen, to watch and see how things were done at the university and what its character was” before setting out to build a strategic plan, she toldUniversity World Newsin anearlier interview.Each of the presidents of the IAUP interviewed for this article pointed with pride toSpotlighting Female Leadership: Strategies, Stories, Perspectives, edited by Lieberman and published by the IAUP’s Women’s Leadership Initiative (WLI), which contains thestories of 10 women in university leadership positionsin Spain, Lithuania, Mexico, Türkiye, Bangladesh and South Korea.Several authors discussed ‘imposter syndrome’, which Fernando Léon García, the IAUP’s immediate past president and president of CETYS Universidad (Centro de Enseñanza Técnica y Superior) in Mexicali (Baja California, Mexico), brought up in our interview. (Lieberman’s study was spearheaded by Léon García.)“The imposter syndrome is more frequent in women than in men. So what do you do about it?” Léon García asked rhetorically, before emphasising that female presidents have to learn to “believe in themselves and become more assertive”.In her chapter, Sue Cunningham, president and CEO of the Council for the Advancement and Support of Education (Washington, DC), encouraged women presidents “to both recognise their imposter syndrome (methinks we all have one!) and put it in its place”.“Self-doubt is undoubtedly the better end of the spectrum than arrogance. That said, it can be undermining.“If you have reached a leadership role, there is significant evidence of your effectiveness and success. I encourage women leaders, when our imposter syndrome pokes his or her head above the surface, to balance the self-criticism with actual evidence,” she noted.She also wrote: “As CEOs, whilst showing vulnerability with our teams and our boards is a healthy thing to do, there are times that one needs someone disconnected from the institution with whom one can unpack challenges and determine how to be our best selves in different and sometimes challenging contexts.”One way of dealing with these challenges, Cunningham averred, was to seek out mentors, which the IAUP’s WLI provided, as Bado discovered.Lieberman, whose years of work in developing women’s leadership were praised by Tanioka, also stressed the importance of women presidents seeking outside support.“As you move through your journey to the presidency, with eyes wide open, finding a mentor/coach/confidante who is not a family member or a university colleague is a critical element. Years ago, hiring/having a coach was perceived as a weakness.“As I look back, having a coach during my early professional years would have been an asset to me. I strongly encourage women (and others) to hire or identify a coach that will be an honest partner and sounding board as one encounters the high points and low points and is always authentically available throughout the journey toward the presidency,” she wrote.Artificial intelligence’s impact on HEAs befitting the place in which it was hosted, Seoul Cyber University (South Korea), the theme of this year’s semi-annual meeting and 60th anniversary held between 13 and 15 October was “Higher Education Reimagined: Innovation, Inclusion, and Global Collaboration in the AI Era”.The impact of technology was not a new issue for IAUP presidents; two decades ago, they received an official IAUP report on information communication technologies and higher education, and there were discussions among members long before that.What is new, however, is the way in which artificial intelligence, dimly visible 20 years ago, has quickly reshaped university administration and, of course, teaching.As much as they are academic leaders, university presidents lead large administrative bureaucracies and are concerned with physical plans. Accordingly, there are appealing benefits to AI.Morales, for example, toldUniversity World Newsthat he had just read an article that said AI would “monitor every single classroom and every single facility on a campus and would generate data on maintenance, deferred maintenance and student density”.For his part, Chen noted that in Latin America, AI is “improving admission fairness and resource allocation”.While noting the digital divide between the Global North and Global South, Chen said it was a mistake to think that universities in the North are leading the way in applying AI to teaching.“Some African universities are using AI to expand access for distance learners. Indian institutions are using AI to personalise tutoring on a massive scale,” he noted. AI can do this, he added, because it can categorise learners and pitch the subject matter to their individual levels.Léon García, who is concerned that “there are very few regions where the governments have the mindset and are committed to putting in the resources to move forward AI”, was, nevertheless, anxious to discuss its promise – and how it will mean that faculty and students will have to learn to do things differently.“First of all, the university will not be defined by four walls. It’s not going to be only defined by someone dictating for 50 minutes or whatever the length of a class happens to be,” he toldUniversity World News.Instead of a course being designed by one or a group of experts who create an armature of the information they want you to learn, the professor/s are going to develop prompts that will guide the learning experience. This, he said, will lead to “courses that are much more tailored and probably more robust”.Students’ work will also change, with more teamwork assignments, noted Léon García, echoing what Chen had said about how assignments will have to be reoriented towards real-world applications in which human judgement and creativity matter most. (Responding to a question about cheating via the use of large language models like ChatGPT, Chen made an important point: “If our assignments can easily be done by ChatGPT, maybe there’s a problem with the assignment.)Soon, AI-structured courses will adjust themselves to individual learning styles – something they will learn, Léon García said, because the prompts offered and those that the student types in will inform the algorithm of this student’s learning style. (Léon García also believes that because the algorithm will ‘learn’ over time, it should become less biased.)“It is getting to the point where, through assistant chat boxes that are linked to each student, the student might be given recommendations on the best approach to improve their learning.“It’s not imposed,” Léon García stressed. “Rather, the AI system gives you the option. Right now, you don’t have an option unless the faculty or teaching assistant manages to identify that you might be lost, because the type of approach used in a classroom now is to teach towards the mean.”Coping with politicsSeveral of the presidents interviewed for this article expressed grave concern about what had been happening to higher education in the United States since the return of Donald J Trump to the White House in January. Léon García’s university and others in Baja California, which is adjacent to California, have close ties and partnerships with US universities like San Diego State University.So that Mexican students who had hitherto routinely crossed the border are not endangered by American border patrol, Léon García’s university is hosting online courses from American institutions.Tanioka said that he did not agree with political figures or congressmen involving themselves in educational matters because they do not know what education is needed and what research is important for the future, before saying, “So, I do worry about the situation in the United States, about higher education now.”Tanioka’s explanation of why he does not think Trumpian attacks on universities would spread to other countries glosses his view of the American government: “Leaders in many other countries clearly know the importance of education; [they know] that what may look useless is important for the future.”Pointing to how the influx of Jewish scientists from Germany and the rest of Europe in the 1930s fuelled American science and helped win the Second World War, Morales lamented the drop in the number of international students in American science programmes.In words that share none of the hope present in Gerth’s address to the IAUP three decades ago, Morales said he feared that under the Trump presidency: “The United States is heading for being, some would argue, is already, an autocratic, illiberal society.”To read more about IAUP’s conference on the theme ‘Higher Education Reimagined: Innovation, Inclusion, and Global Collaboration in the AI Era,’ held in Seoul, South Korea, from 11 to 13 October, clickhere.This article is published with the support of the International Association IAUP六十周年：校長們在AI時代尋求高等教育的共同立場  
國際大學校長協會（IAUP）於2023年10月在韓國首爾舉辦六十周年會議，主題為「重新想像的高等教育：創新、包容與全球合作」。會議中，校長們探討了人工智慧對高等教育的影響，強調AI能提升教學及行政效率，但也需面對全球南北之間的數位鴻溝。與會者指出，AI可以個性化學習體驗，並鼓勵學生團隊合作。IAUP會長肖恩·陳表示，未來的課程將需更靈活、富有應用性，以滿足學生需求。此會議為校長提供了分享經驗及學習的機會，旨在提升高等教育的相關性與可及性。
