‘Big 4’ Anglophone nations falls – StudyNathan M Greenfield28 October 2025TweetOver the past two years, interest by international students from Asia, Latin America, and the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) seeking to study in English in the Big Four Anglophone countries (the United States, Britain, Canada and Australia) and other well-known destinations with English-taught programmes (ETP), such as Italy, Germany and France, is largely stagnant or continues to decline.At the same time, says a study conducted by Studyportals with the British Council, interest in ETPs in Asia and MENA is growing.According to the study, interest in studying in Britain by Asian students for a bachelor degree rose 0.3% and for a masters by 0.7%, while among those in MENA countries, interest in going to Britain for a bachelor degree remains unchanged and has declined by 0.3% for prospective masters students.Latin American students evince significantly less interest in studying in Britain with the figure for prospective bachelor students falling 1.7% and for prospective masters students falling 0.5%.By contrast, the percentage of Japanese students looking to do their bachelor degree in an ETP who wish to remain in Asia rose by 6.6% and among prospective masters students the percentage rose by 4.9% over the last two years.“The main idea of this study is to give a sense of alternative study destinations that international students could be considering in the short and, potentially, in the long term,” Karl Baldacchino, a co-author ofAsia, Latin America and MENA in global education: Emerging trends as destination and source regions (Emerging Trends)published today, toldUniversity World News.The team studied Asia, Latin America and the MENA region “because they are three very distinct, very different regions in terms of their higher education systems. Asia seems the most developed, at least in terms of trying to meet the demand that they have from their domestic markets, despite the fact that India, Pakistan and Bangladesh are still struggling to keep up with and retain their own domestic students”, said Baldacchino.“The MENA region is certainly up and coming, with Saudi Arabia and the United Arab Emirates (UAE) emerging as probably two of the most competitive countries – Qatar is another. Latin America, the report makes very clear, still lags behind the other regions,” he noted.The report is based on Studyportals’ proprietary data, drawn from a database that adds 51 million users every year from more than 220 countries and territories; Chinese students’ interest in studying in ETPs outside China is likely under-represented in Studyportals’ dataset because of the ‘Great Firewall’ that restricts Chinese users of the internet, the report notes as a caveat vis-à-vis its Chinese numbers.“These visitors generate 60 million sessions and more than 45 million programme page views annually,” write Baldacchino and the report’s principle author, Cara Skikne, head of communications and thought leadership at the Netherlands-based global study platform.Canada sees sharpest fallCanada has seen the sharpest decline in interest. The study covers the period when, because of a housing affordability crisis blamed in large measure on the 1 million international students in the country, the government of Canada drastically reduced the number of student visas it was prepared to issue and imposed the requirement that each international student have CA$20,000 (US$14,287) in the bank (in order to cut down on fraudulent claims of being a student). During this period there were also slowdowns in the processing of visas.All of these factors, as well as diplomatic cooling between Canada and India and Canada and China, the countries whose students make up the lion’s share of international students in Canada, have been well-covered by the international press.The numbers of prospective bachelor students from Asia and MENA countries were -1.9% and -1.6%, respectively, while the number from Latin America (by far the smallest of the three) rose by 1.2%. At the masters level, Canada lost ground in each region: -4.8% among Asian students, -3.7% among Latin American students and -4.1% among students from the MENA countries.Though the US has registered declines in five of the six categories of students, the declines do not appear to be what might be expected given worldwide news reports about arrests of international students, the closing of the border to a number of countries, massive cuts to university research budgets and the anti-immigrant rhetoric of the administration of President Donald J Trump.The US experienced a decline in interest by prospective bachelor students from Asia of -1.2% and of -1.3% of prospective masters students. The figures, respectively, for Latin American students are -0.2 and +0.4. Among MENA students, the figures are -0.2 for prospective bachelor students and -0.4 for prospective masters students.However, Skikne said: “These figures are for the total time period from July 2023 [when Joe Biden was president and would remain so until 20 January 2025] to June 2025. So that’s quite a long time period. I think when we look at the shorter time period, then you can start to see the effect over 2025 [since Trump returned to office].”As wasreported in these pages on 6 October, referring to the 61% decline in interest in studying for a masters degree in the US that Studyportals had tracked since January, Skikne toldUniversity World News: “What makes this moment especially concerning is that the decline is not driven by a global crisis or public health emergency, but by policy choices. This suggests it could become a more entrenched, long-term shift rather than a temporary shock.”Asian students’ interest in studying in Australia for their bachelor degree has declined by 0.5% and for their masters by more than double that, while among Latin American students there has been no change in interest in studying for their bachelor degree and a decline of -0.2% for their masters.Germany, France and Italy have the most number of ETPs in Europe. Both German bachelor and masters programmes have seen declines, -0.4% and -0.2%, respectively, among Asian students, while MENA students looking to study for a bachelor degree in Germany have decreased by 0.8% and masters degree interest has grown by 0.1%.Interest in studying in Germany is down sharply among Latin Americans: -2.3% among prospective bachelor students and -1.4% among prospective masters students. This last number is particularly important since German universities are of interest to the largest percentage, 16.2% (after the decline), of students who are thinking of leaving Latin America to study in ETPs.Over the two years of the study, interest in French bachelor ETPs by Asians rose by 0.8% and by 1.1 for masters programmes.Interest for studying in German ETPs by Asian bachelor students declined by 0.4% and by 0.2% among masters students. Interest among MENA students looking to study in ETPs in French rose by 0.2% among prospective bachelor students and by 0.4% among prospective masters students.Interest in Italian universities by MENA masters students declined by 0.1% but increased by 0.5% among prospective bachelor students. The percentage of Latin American masters students seeking information about studying in ETPS in Italy rose by 0.5% and by prospective bachelor students by a healthy 1.2%.Interest in remaining in AsiaInterest in remaining in Asia and enrolling in one of the more than 30 Asian universities with ETPs varies greatly by country. The percentage of Japanese students looking to remain in Asia for their bachelor degree has increased by 6.6% and their masters by 4.9%.The percentage increase by Chinese students is even higher: +32% at the bachelor level and +6.9% at the masters level. Both prospective bachelor and masters Indian students too are showing greater interest in studying in Asia, +3.2% and +15.5% respectively. (The increase in Chinese and Indian students looking to remain in Asia is, at least, partially due to Canada’s new restrictive visa regimen, as students from these two countries made up the bulk of the 1 million international students in Canada in 2023.)South Korean students and those from Singapore were the two largest groups to show less interest in remaining in their region for their bachelor or masters. Among South Koreans, the decline was 12.4% and 12.2%, while among Singaporean students it was 17% and 15.4%, respectively.Among smaller nation Studyportals groups in Asia, students from Vietnam and Brunei showed differing interests in remaining in Asia.The growth in the number of prospective Vietnamese bachelor students who sought information about remaining in Asia was just over 100%, albeit from a small baseline of about 2,290; prospective masters students’ interest in remaining in Asia declined by 2.4%. For Brunei the figures flipped, with prospective bachelor students’ interest in Asian ETPs falling by 9.5% and the interest by masters students increasing by more than 175% to 3,781.Over the past two years, the mix of ETPs in Asia that the students who wish to remain in Asia have investigated has changed. Japan’s 167 bachelor programmes represent a decline of more than 15%, while its 418 bachelor programmes are 3.4% fewer than two years ago.China too has seen a decline in the number of ETPs: -5.8% at the bachelor level and -11.4% at the masters level. By contrast, both India and Singapore have seen large increases. There are +54.7% new bachelor ETPs and + 53% new masters programmes in India, while the figures for Singapore are +63.2% and +28%.City variationOne of the most interesting metrics presented inEmerging Trendsconcerns cities. At the bachelor level, universities in the Chinese cities of Suzhou, Beijing and Shanghai saw increased interest of 60%, 41% and 22.3%, while at the masters level, the figures are considerably lower: +2.0%, +3.2% and -6.7%, respectively.Tokyo, Toyama and Kyoto’s universities saw increased interest in their bachelor ETPs of 13.8%, 12.3% and 45.2%, while at the masters level the numbers are between +4.0% and +7.1%.In South Korea, interest in studying in a bachelor ETP in Anyang-si fell by 25.5%, while it rose by 14.7% for Seoul and 17.4% for Ulsan; the figures for students interested in masters programmes are between -11% and -13.4%.In Latin America, Rio de Janeiro is the city that is benefiting the most from interest in ETPs: +43.1% at the masters level.Two of Mexico’s cities have seen drastic declines in interest. Interest in studying in an ETP in Monterrey has declined by 71.2% and by 41.1% for a masters in Azcapotzalco.According to the report, at the bachelor’s level in the MENA countries, Istanbul, Ankara, and ili in Türkiye remain the three most in-demand cities in the region, together attracting over 126,000 page views. However, all three saw sharp declines, with demand towards ili plummeting by 55.5%.The trend is mirrored in the United Arab Emirates, where Dubai saw demand fall by 26.3% and Sharjah by 21.4%, despite Abu Dhabi growing by 28.4%, hinting at a possible realignment of student preferences within the federation.Interestingly, cities in the Kingdom of Saudi Arabia (KSA) saw the strongest gains, with Jeddah experiencing a remarkable 560.5% growth, while demand for Dhahran and Riyadh also grew substantially. This spike aligns with KSA’s ambitious Vision 2030 reforms, the growing number of ETPs and regional outreach.“At the masters level, Istanbul, Ankara, and ili again lead in Türkiye, but with a more dramatic drop in demand for ili (-63.1%), reinforcing the downward trend at both levels. By contrast, Abu Dhabi and Dubai in the UAE remain appealing, whilst demand for Dubai is up 22.2% and Abu Dhabi is up 32.8%.“In KSA, demand for Thuwal, home to King Abdullah University of Science and Technology, fell by -65.8%, a notable shift that may be linked to programme- or visa-related developments,” write Skikne and Baldacchino.“Elsewhere, Egypt’s Giza and Awsim saw exceptional growth (+203.3% and +25.9%, respectively), while demand for Cairo fell by -11.0%, echoing this emerging pattern of demand shifting from traditional megacities toward specialised locations. In Jordan, Ar Ramtha recorded an impressive +47.6% increase, while demand for Amman fell by -18.4%.”Academic fieldsThe granular analysis of disciplines and sub-disciplines points to significant differences among Asian, Latin American and MENA students’ academic interests and what they see as their career trajectories.Among students in Asia, interest in ETPs in business and management (B&M) grew strongly over the past two years: 6.4% at the bachelor level and 1.6% at the masters level; within this programme area, prospective bachelor students have driven increases of 10.3% and 10.6% in business administration and management studies, while international business has grown by the significantly smaller figure of 2.6%.Interest in B&M by Latin American bachelor students has, by contrast, declined by 1.8% and by 3.6% among prospective masters students. The subfields of business administration and management studies have, nevertheless, grown by 3.1% and 5.4%, respectively, among prospective bachelor students – but have declined by over 10% among prospective masters students.Bachelor students from the MENA countries are 1.2% more interested in B&M than two years ago, while masters students are -1.2%. At the bachelor level, interest in business administration grew by 2.5% and management studies by 0.6%; international business declined by 2.3%. At the masters level, interest in management studies declined by 5.2% and in business administration by 1.5%.Among prospective Asian bachelor students seeking to study in ETPs, computer science and IT (CS&IT) declined by 4.7% and among masters students it grew by 1.3%.Latin American bachelor students drove a 1.5% decline in interest in CS&IT, while the region’s masters students showed an increased interest of +2.0%. Prospective bachelor students in the MENA viewed 2.3% fewer pages on CS&IT than they did two years ago, while masters students saw 3.9% more pages.Perceptions of valueTowards the end of our interview, Baldacchino, pointing to the flows away from the Big Four and traditional European destinations, put himself in the position of a prospective international student searching for information.These students are navigating “quite a turbulent international situation in terms of where they can realistically decide to study abroad.“The burden of the choices that these students have to make has increased, because they not only have to look at the visa rules, the standard of living and even tuition fees, but they also have to weave in all of the return on investment costs with the potential risks that they might face when they’re studying in, say, the US or the UK”, said Baldacchino.“Especially given the whole importance of the student experience, and as transnational education becomes more available in regions like the Middle East and North Africa and in Asia, and potentially also in the future in Latin America, you would likely see a gradual shift of students deciding to study closer to home as the relative perception of the value, the safety, [and] the difference in culture and languages becomes narrowed,” Baldacchino noted.Skikne added: “Previously, you saw that the percentage of market share between the Big Four was quite stable. When one market lost out, they lost out to another Big Four destination, whreas now, that's not the case as much. Now, there's a lot more interest going outside of the Big Four, where the destinations they're competing with for students have diversified massively.”Baldacchino, a statistical analyst, was appropriately circumspect when asked whether the rise of ETPs in other regions and, especially, the interest students in those regions are evincing in staying closer to home indicate that the longstanding model of international students flowing to the Big Four and other traditional European countries may be reaching the end of its shelf life.“It is possible. I wouldn’t say anything is out of the realm of the possible, but it is too hard to say because, at the same time, these regions remain attractive from an academic point of view.“But, I think that beyond what is academically provided by the study destination, the student experience and the ability to work in the place that you decided to study in once you graduate is becoming more important,” he stated. (Visa changes in the US and Canada make this very difficult now.)“For those destinations that provide a healthy and positive environment for that experience for an international student, there is more likelihood they’ll have a successful student that will want to contribute to the local economy, to the national economy, to any research if they're in STEM-related fields, whether the shelf life is there or not,” he concluded.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredGLOBALImpact awards recognise value of gender-sensitive researchScovian Lillian07 November 2025TweetThree women researchers, recognised for combining cutting-edge science with social impact, are this evening presenting their work at the annual Falling Walls Science Summit in Berlin, a high-profile gathering of leading scientists, innovators and policy thinkers from across the world.Their award, the Female Science Talent Women’s Impact Award, is a flagship initiative of the Falling Walls Foundation, founded in 2009 on the 20th anniversary of the fall of the Berlin Wall. The Female Science Talents programme was launched to address a persistent blind spot: the under-representation of women in science leadership and the slow adoption of gender-sensitive research approaches.The award, which attracted 97 applicants from around the world and has 10 finalists, has become one of the summit’s most anticipated events, drawing attention to projects that push beyond the laboratory to tackle entrenched inequities in health, technology, and governance.Among this year’s award winners in the Women’s Impact Award, announced earlier this year, are Ângela Gonçalves of the German Cancer Research Center for her research on healthy ageing in women through the use of AI-driven, non-invasive tools to detect endometriosis, ovarian cancer, and menopause-related risks; Colette Wabnitz of Stanford University and the University of British Columbia, who is working to co-create data and policy tools to elevate women’s leadership in ocean governance; and Omneya Attallah of the Arab Academy for Science, Technology, and Maritime Transport for her work on AI diagnostics for breast cancer in low-resource settings.Institutional supportBy elevating the work of scientists like Gonçalves, Wabnitz, and Attallah, the programme aims to inspire institutions to provide the resources, mentorship, and recognition women need to translate discoveries into societal benefit.Although their disciplines differ, the three winners share common themes, with each project addressing a form of invisibility of female biology, of women’s labour in fisheries, and of patients excluded from high-tech diagnostics.Each blends scientific innovation with a commitment to equity, seeking not only to produce knowledge but to change systems. Each research project highlights the role of universities and research institutes as platforms for global collaboration, where interdisciplinary teams can develop solutions to urgent challenges that cross national borders.“Winning this award is a powerful recognition of my journey as a researcher, mother, and mentor. It affirms that every challenge has led to impact and strengthens my commitment to using AI to transform healthcare and advocate for gender equity in science,” Attallah toldUniversity World News.“I wanted to see technology directly improve people’s lives. Early in my career, I was fascinated by how AI could help detect diseases earlier and guide better medical decisions. Its potential to transform healthcare for women and children in underserved areas made it a personal mission.“We need systems that value diversity, inclusion, and work-life balance. Equal access to funding, leadership, and gender-responsive research opportunities is key. Mentorship, visibility, and global collaboration can help women lead with confidence and shape research priorities,” she added.Gender bias in medicineConditions such as endometriosis, ovarian cancer, and menopause-related complications remain under-researched and often misdiagnosed, resulting in delayed care and unnecessary suffering; hence, Gonçalves’ project is rooted in a simple but transformative question: what does healthy ageing look like in women, and how can medicine detect problems earlier and less invasively?By combining artificial intelligence, molecular biology, and clinical insight, her team is developing tools that could generate essential biological references for the female reproductive system, enabling clinicians to distinguish normal ageing from early signs of disease.Her approach directly challenges gender bias in medicine, which for decades has relied on male-centric data sets and overlooked the nuances of female health.“Historically, biomedical science has focused on male biology, leaving conditions that affect millions of women – such as endometriosis, ovarian cancer, and menopause – comparatively understudied and poorly treated.“This has led to delays in diagnosis, with invasive procedures like hysterectomy remaining the standard of care for over a century, and significant gaps in understanding basic processes like menstrual biology and reproductive ageing.“By using single-cell genomics, machine learning, and spatial transcriptomics, our project addresses the lack of biological references for female organs across the lifespan,” she said.Goncalves and her team are developing detailed, high-resolution atlases of the reproductive system and creating non-invasive methods like menstrual fluid sampling to make diagnostic tools more accessible.“These advances aim to improve health outcomes by enabling early detection and disease monitoring. Our work also questions long-held scientific assumptions, such as uncovering the hidden costs of the menstrual cycle and reframing menopause as a broader mammalian phenomenon,” Goncalves said in an interview.Women in ocean governanceWabnitz’s work highlights another overlooked reality, where millions of women support global fisheries but remain invisible in official statistics and decision-making.As a researcher at the Stanford Center for Ocean Solutions and the University of British Columbia’s Institute for the Oceans and Fisheries, she collaborates with coastal communities to develop gender-inclusive fact sheets, policy analyses, and regional workshops.These tools aim to amplify women’s voices, support their leadership, and promote fair, inclusive, and sustainable ocean governance.By bringing women’s contributions into focus, Wabnitz’s project also enhances the evidence base for policies that can improve livelihoods, safeguard ecosystems, and increase fisheries’ resilience to climate change.“By systematically analysing national fisheries governance documents, we’re identifying both the gaps where commitments to gender equity fall short and inspiring policy examples that actively support women’s empowerment and can spark wider change. This creates an evidence base to inspire policy innovation and strengthen accountability.“We’re building networks between gender and fisheries institutions through regional workshops that foster dialogue and amplify women's voices in policy conversations.“These gatherings create spaces for shared learning and collective action. Importantly, the process ensures that women are not only sources of data but active participants shaping the solutions that affect their lives, fostering ownership and recognition,” Wabnitz said in an interview.Accessible healthcareAttallah’s research, on the other hand, tackles one of the most feared and costly diseases worldwide, breast cancer, but from a perspective shaped by the constraints of low-resource settings.Traditional mammography can be expensive, invasive, and inaccessible for many women, leading to late diagnoses and poorer outcomes, but her non-invasive, affordable AI tool aims to offer early detection in communities where radiologists and advanced equipment are scarce.Attallah designs systems that augment clinical expertise, allowing more women to access timely care and potentially saving countless lives, challenging the assumption that cutting-edge technology must be costly or exclusive to high-income countries.“My work uses artificial intelligence to make healthcare more accessible, accurate, and equitable, particularly for underserved communities. Through AI-driven imaging, wearable biosensing, and remote monitoring, I aim to improve early disease detection and resource use globally,” Attallah toldUniversity World News.Gonçalves urged young women in science, research, and innovation to pursue the questions that truly matter, especially those that challenge accepted ideas or shine light on overlooked areas of science.“Many transformative discoveries begin where few have thought to look. In women’s health, for example, there’s enormous potential to make findings that directly improve lives. Embrace interdisciplinarity; the most exciting advances often emerge at the crossroads of biology, computer science, and clinical medicine,” she toldUniversity World News.“Don’t hesitate to learn new tools, whether in AI, genomics, or imaging, and use them to rethink longstanding problems in fresh ways. Expect setbacks; science is a long journey where persistence and resilience are just as vital as talent. Be patient and let your purpose keep you motivated and bold enough to push boundaries.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

影響獎項表彰性別敏感研究價值 在柏林舉行的年度Falling Walls科學峰會上，三位女性研究者因其結合尖端科學與社會影響的工作而獲得Female Science Talent Women’s Impact Award。該獎項旨在解決科學領導中女性的代表性不足及性別敏感研究方法的採用緩慢。獲獎者包括研究女性健康的Ângela Gonçalves，提倡女性在海洋治理中領導的Colette Wabnitz，以及專注於低資源環境中的乳腺癌AI診斷的Omneya Attallah。這些研究不僅產生知識，還致力於改變不平等的系統，提升女性在科學界的地位。 
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New plan to woo diaspora scientists amid US policy shift
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New plan to woo diaspora scientists amid US policy shiftGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsAs US policies tighten, India seeks to draw its brightest minds home – turning outward migration into a return flow of ideas and innovation. Image generated by SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIAIndia eyes top G20 talent with a special visa for scholarsINDIABudget allocation brings new apex research body to lifeINDIAMany PhD scholars go abroad due to poor payment at homeINDIAMost top 100 IIT achievers have left the country – StudyINDIA-UNITED STATESUS education mission taps high demand from Indian studentsINDIABig budget lift for research eco-system, top universitiesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIANew plan to woo diaspora scientists amid US policy shiftShuriah Niazi07 November 2025TweetThe Indian government is working on a new plan to attract top Indian-origin scientists and researchers who are settled abroad to work in Indian institutions.The proposal comes amid restrictive higher education policies in the United States under the Trump administration, which many critics believe threaten university autonomy and academic freedom.Currently, the Ministry of Science and Technology operates the Visiting Advanced Joint Research Faculty Scheme (VAJRA). This programme aims to attract scientists and academics from abroad, including non-resident Indians and overseas citizens of India, to work in publicly funded institutions and universities for a specific period of time.Now, in light of recent developments in the US, a new scheme is being developed in consultation with the ministries of education and science and technology to encourage these researchers to spend time in India for teaching or research.Brain circulationArpan Tulsyan, senior fellow at the Centre for New Economic Diplomacy, Observer Research Foundation, said this is a much-needed step.“For years, we’ve been witnessing large numbers of Indian students and skilled professionals moving abroad, settling down, and building successful careers. We’ve spoken for at least two decades about the problem of brain drain. It was high time we started transforming that into brain circulation,” she said.According to Tulsyan, the goal should be to create structured opportunities for them to give back, not just through remittances but through knowledge-sharing and collaboration.“Even if these professionals don’t permanently return, it’s important that they maintain ties with India – so we can leverage their skills, networks, and expertise,” she toldUniversity World News.“This is the right time to engage more systematically with the Indian diasporas. There are both push and pull factors at play. On the one hand, we see uncertainty around the US H-1B visa regime and a growing anti-immigration sentiment in several countries. On the other, India is positioning itself for innovation-driven growth,” she stated.According to sources, meetings have been held between the principal scientific adviser’s office and the Ministry of Education, the Ministry of Science and Technology, and the Department of Biotechnology to design the scheme.The plan aims to bring back reputed Indian-origin academics with proven research credentials to teach or conduct research in India for a specified period. They will be offered substantial ‘set-up grants’ to establish laboratories and research teams.Moving towards competitive salariesTulsyan said: “Our research infrastructure and institutional culture have improved, and salaries are more competitive, though still not on par with the US, but we're moving steadily in that direction. Given this context, it's a real opportunity for India.”Experts emphasise that beyond financial incentives, the success of the plan will depend on creating a welcoming environment – including smooth housing, hospitality, and administrative support – to ensure returning scholars have a seamless experience.While Tulsyan agreed that money does matter for returning academics, as they expect a certain lifestyle, she said financial incentives alone won't be enough.“What’s essential isholistic support. That includes help with spouses’ careers, children’s education, and assistance with documentation or relocation. For those who’ve been away for years, easing the process of reintegration is crucial.“The government has also been working on mutual recognition of qualifications with several countries, which helps returning professionals transition more smoothly,” she said.Boost for research ecosystemThe Indian Institutes of Technology (IITs) are supporting the initiative, with several directors participating in discussions. Initially, 12 to 14 priority STEM fields of national importance will be targeted. The government hopes the scheme will strengthen India's research ecosystem and curb the longstanding outflow of talent.According to Tulsyan, the initiative enhances India’s global image. Bringing back Indian-origin researchers and scientists helps to reposition India as an emerging innovation hub and boosts our international credibility, she said.Returnees bring not just their expertise but also their global networks and relationships.This can greatly strengthen India's academic and research ecosystem. It supports international collaborations, joint research, co-supervised PhDs, and exchange programmes – all of which enhance India's research output and global partnerships, she said.“Such returnees also serve as cultural and scientific ambassadors. Their global exposure and entrepreneurial orientation can help bridge knowledge gaps in emerging areas like green technology, AI, biotechnology, and clean energy,” Tulsyan added.“They can help accelerate the transition from research to product development, making India more globally competitive.”Procedural bottlenecks continueWhile existing programmes like the VAJRA and Ramanujan Fellowship already encourage overseas collaboration, participation has remained limited due to procedural bottlenecks, modest funding, and short-term tenures. The new scheme aims to address these gaps by offering longer-term roles and greater research autonomy.But India may still face challenges in implementing such a scheme. Key challenges include research infrastructure, funding continuity, and institutional autonomy.Tulsyan said many of the traditional challenges are gradually being addressed. Research infrastructure has improved significantly, and with the establishment of the Anusandhan National Research Foundation (NRF), funding and access to opportunities are becoming more democratised – extending beyond just the IITs and IIMs to Tier-2 and Tier-3 institutions as well.However, thereal challengesare institutional rigidity and bureaucratic barriers. Infrastructure can be built quickly, but changing mindsets and administrative cultures takes time, she said.Tulsyan said funding remains a concern, though less than before. While funding as a percentage of GDP has stagnated, in absolute terms it has grown. “We’re also seeing new funding models, such as public-private partnerships, emerging. The real issue is not the availability of funds but the disbursement – delays in approvals, paperwork, and procedural bottlenecks.“So, if I had to prioritise, administrative delays would be the top challenge, followed by issues around talent retention and institutional flexibility,” Tulsyan said.Officials are finalising details regarding intellectual property rights, tenure structures, and funding flexibility. Once completed, the proposal will be submitted to the union cabinet for approval. The move aligns with India's broader goal to position its universities competitively in the global academic scenario and nurture world-class research capacity at home.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMALAYSIANew Monash Malaysia campus: A signal of global ambitionsKalinga Seneviratne07 November 2025TweetThe decision by Monash University, Australia’s leading research-based university, to invest US$1 million in a new campus in Malaysia’s capital, Kuala Lumpur, in association with a Malaysian government-linked company, is a “powerful expression” of the university’s global ambitions.In a statement onLinkedIn, Craig Jeffrey, deputy vice-chancellor (international) and senior vice-president of Monash University in Australia, said: “The announcement of a new Monash campus in Kuala Lumpur is a defining moment in our international journey … The ASEAN region is central to Monash’s global ambitions, and this new campus, located in the heart of Malaysia’s financial district, is a powerful expression of that commitment”.Monash University’s (MU) Vice-Chancellor and President Sharon Pickering made the announcement about the new campus in Kuala Lumpur in October alongside Australia’s Prime Minister Anthony Albanese (who was in Malaysia to attend the East Asia Summit) and Malaysia’s Minister of Higher Education Zambry Abd Kadir.Albanese said that this is about “showcasing Australia’s best in the world-class higher education sector overseas”, and he added that “it’s also an important opportunity to extend deep connections in our region”.MU’s partner in the project is TRX City Sdn Bhd, a fully owned company of Malaysia’s Ministry of Finance, which is developing the capital’s new financial centre on a 70-acre block.The Edge, a Malaysian newspaper, said that TRX will build the new campus on a 15-acre block there and lease it to MU on a 15- to 20-year basis with provisions to extend it for further periods.The new campus is set to open in 2032, with eventual capacity for 22,500 students and 1,700 staff.A statement from the university said more than 35 extra courses will be offered at the new campus in areas such as digitisation, AI and cyber security, climate change and sustainability, healthcare and digital health, semiconductor and advanced manufacturing, banking and finance. These are areas vital for the next stage of BRI’s development of regional economic connectivity.A ‘global experience in the heart of Asia’Adeeba Kamarulzaman, president and pro vice-chancellor of Monash University Malaysia (MUM), toldUniversity World NewsMonash Malaysia offers “a truly global education experience in the heart of Asia”, and she reiterated that it will offer students “a dynamic, multicultural environment that is both accessible and globally connected”.“What makes us distinctive is our strong industry partnerships and our ‘One Monash’ network that allows students to move seamlessly between our campuses in Malaysia, Australia and Indonesia,” she noted. “This combination of academic excellence, affordability, and access to real-world opportunities across ASEAN continues to attract students who want a degree that’s both internationally recognised and regionally relevant.”MUM, in Subang Jaya, was the first foreign campus to be opened in Malaysia in 1998. Asian Studies Professor James Chin of the University of Tasmania was the foundation head of the School of Arts and Social Sciences of the MUM campus.In an interview withUniversity World News, he said right from the beginning, MUM had been profitable and has a good track record with Malaysian students and increasingly with students from India. “People are confident that Monash can deliver quality undergraduate education”.Chin said he believes MU got a good deal in linking with TRX, “a strong Malaysian government-linked company”. He also believes MUM will become a major feeder of Asian students for higher studies to Australian universities.“In Southeast Asia, as the middle classes grow, there is a yearning for higher education, so the market is big for MUM to tap into,” he noted.Chinese expansionIt is not only MU that is tapping into this market; Chinese universities have been opening campuses in the region, while their students are venturing there as well.In 2016, China’s Xiamen University (XU) opened a branch campus in Malaysia, and, unlike its Chinese campus, teaching on the Malaysian campus is in English, except for Chinese Studies and Traditional Chinese Medicine.Professor Wang Ruifang, president of Xiamen University Malaysia (XUM), told the Singapore Chinese language newspaperLianhe Zaobaorecently that it was the 2013 launch of the Belt and Road Initiative by China that inspired XU’s expansion to Malaysia.“China needs talent with global perspectives, [and we] aim to cultivate [such talent] with an understanding of diverse cultures,” he stated.While XUM has a cohort of 9,000 students currently, with 2,500 from China, according toLianhe Zaobao, the Malaysian government has invited China’s number one-ranked Tsinghua University to set up a campus in the country, while Malaysia’s top institution, University Malaya, is planning to become the first Malaysian university to set up a branch campus in China, in Shandong.As Chinese capital expands across the region, more students are recognising the benefits of developing cross-cultural communication skills crucial for employment and entrepreneurship in Asia.Yanfang Liu from China saw the benefits early on. She chose to go to Bangkok University in Thailand to do a masters in mass communication. Recalling the decision she made almost a decade ago, she toldUniversity World Newsshe was impressed with the creativity in the advertising industry there, and by Thailand’s cultural and geographical proximity to China.“I believed that studying in an Asian context with global influences would offer a fresh perspective compared to Western-centric media education,” she said.Now working in the digital communication field in Beijing, she said the “telling big stories through small perspectives” approach of the Thais is inspiring. “The emphasis on storytelling and emotional connection in Thai advertising has influenced how I develop content and campaigns in the fast-paced digital landscape,” she said.“Education is central to regional growth, and Monash is uniquely positioned to connect Australia, China and ASEAN through research and innovation. Our programmes in engineering, IT, business, finance and the social sciences align closely with the opportunities created by the Belt and Road Initiative,” Kamarulzaman toldUniversity World News.“Through Monash Malaysia, we’re preparing graduates with the skills and mindset to drive sustainable growth and collaboration across the region”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New recommendations aim to boost outward student mobility
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New recommendations aim to boost outward student mobilityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksFINLANDEnglish taught degrees see two-fold increase in demandFINLANDUniversities dismayed by plans to cut research fundingFINLANDInternational masters programmes turn slump aroundFINLANDVision for 2030 commits to investment in HE and R&DFINLANDUniversities join forces to attract foreign studentsFINLANDTuition fees and cuts hit internationalisation of HEInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsFINLANDNew recommendations aim to boost outward student mobilityJan Petter Myklebust06 November 2025TweetUniversities and key higher education stakeholders have jointly drawn up eight new recommendations aimed at strengthening outward student mobility, which is currently 24% lower than inbound mobility, and at making international experiences a core element of higher education studies.According todatafrom the Finnish National Agency for Education (EDUFI), last year, the number of students from higher education institutions going into exchange periods longer than three months was 6,582. At the same time 8,160 foreign students came to Finland for an exchange period longer than three months. A record high number of students – 10,443 – went into exchange in 2016 for more than three months.Jonna Korhonen, director of the Department for Higher Education and Science Policy at the Ministry of Education and Culture, who chaired theworking groupthat devised the recommendations, toldUniversity World Newsthe work of the group, which included representatives from universities, universities of applied sciences, student organisations, the quality assurance agency and EDUFI, was “very interactive, enthusiastic and solution orientated”.A joint statement by Korhonen and Maija Airas, head of unit for International Higher Education Cooperation at EDUFI, said a recent Council of the European Union recommendation puts the number of higher education graduates that should have a learning mobility experience abroad as part of their studies at 23%.Currently, the proportion of students who went on mobility periods abroad accounts for 19% of new students at universities and 11% at universities of applied sciences (UASs). The decrease in mobility rates has been particularly significant at universities of applied sciences. In 2019, these rates were 23% in universities and 22% in UASs, according toEDUFI data.“International competence is an essential part of working life skills and should be included in every degree and qualification. While international student mobility has recovered since the COVID-19 pandemic, participation, especially in long student exchanges continues to decrease. However, there have also been some positive developments in individual higher education institutions,” the statement said.Therecommendationsemphasise the importance of setting targets and monitoring progress and specify which governmental organisations are responsible for which tasks.The Ministry of Education and Culture, for example, will record a performance indicator on internationalisation as part of the overall governance of higher education institutions and commission a follow-up report in 2025 and 2026.Specifically, the recommendations call for a “widespread awareness of the means to increase mobility”, and they call on academic staff to commit to promoting internationalisation to their students and communicating the importance of international competence in society and in working life.The recommendations also call for the development of “common digital solutions for supporting the management and mobility of Erasmus+ and other funding programmes to make them more user-friendly and functional” and to “ensure funding for student mobility through EU programmes, other exchange programmes and higher education institutions’ own funding”.There is also a call to “increase information and understanding of the effectiveness and benefits of short and/or blended mobilities in developing international competence”.Student aid cutsSaku Pesonen, the adviser for international affairs and EU advocacy at the National Union of Students (SYL), who was a member of the working group, told University World Newsthere was a “clear link” between the drop in numbers of long-term mobility periods in 2016 and government cuts to student financial aid and a reduction in the maximum period for which students could rely on financial aid for their higher education studies, among other measures.“A clear link can be seen between the cuts to students’ income and the decrease in the number of student mobility periods," Pesonen said.Pesonen said from the union’s perspective, the main reasons for the downward trend were: added financial costs for students; pressure on students to graduate within a tight deadline; difficulties in including a mobility period as part of a degree; and separation from family.However, Pesonen said the new national goals were "definitely welcomed" and "indeed ambitious".“We’re happy that there is some emphasis put on funding measures and also on the supervision of universities to have a growing level of exchange periods in different degree programmes.“What is also positive in the goals is that there is emphasis on making the recognition of studies done during exchange periods more seamless and also on providing more guidance and information for students on mobility periods.”Pesonen said the goals address some of the challenges but not all.“Now, of course, it’s up to various actors to put the recommendations into use, and also it’s important to note that these are only recommendations and not mandated. We’re now looking forward to seeing how they're taken into practice in the near future.”Financial signals neededProfessor Michael Jeltsch of the IndiviDrug Research Programme, which is based at the Wihuri Research Institute of the University of Helsinki, toldUniversity World Newsthat going abroad during bachelor of science studies will “inevitably prolong the studies, resulting in direct financial losses for the university”.“If the Ministry of Education prolonged the ‘default graduation time’ in accordance with the time spent abroad (for the purpose of money distribution among universities and for the student benefits), that would go a long way in encouraging students to go abroad,” he said.He said without such clear financial signals, the government campaign would not have any significant effect. “Due to recent cuts in government support for students (for example, housing subsidies), the incentives to finish as fast as possible continue to rise (this applies to both BSc and MSc programmes),” Jeltsch said.“For the increasing numbers of international MSc students, going from Finland abroad is not very attractive, because they are already abroad. And if they are fee-liable (coming from outside EU/EEA), they cannot risk not graduating in time because this would require them to pay one additional semester of tuition fees (€9,000 [US$10,365] for our students).“I have experienced recently such a case in my own lab, where a fee-liable student received extra funding for a student exchange, but decided not to accept it because he did not want to endanger his timely graduation.”Jeltsch said while Erasmus, for example, had helped with recognising studies abroad, there were still study programmes (notably those that involve state examinations such as pharmacy) where universities do not recognise studies abroad because they are not done in the correct language.“Furthermore semester/teaching periods are not synchronised throughout Europe, making seamless switches sometimes difficult even if credits are transferable.”Jeltsch said in his understanding, the number of short exchanges had not declined as much as full-semester or full-year exchanges, because shorter periods were easier to accommodate. However, it was sometimes difficult to earn credits within short exchanges or to do meaningful research (for example, do the MSc project work abroad).“It’s a complicated mix; students respond rationally to the integrated plusses and minuses, and just telling them to change that behaviour is pointless.”He said when he asked a group of PhD students about study abroad, many of them saw it as a simple financial calculation. “They said: ‘It’s not doable without accruing debt and we do not want to follow the US example, starting an insecure precarious professional future with a huge amount of debt’.”Progress in doctoral pilotMeanwhile, the Rectors’ Conference of Finnish Universities (UNIFI) has emphasised the importance of doctoral training, arguing in a recent statement that a doctorate is a strong asset in the labour market.“Unemployment among doctorate holders is 3.8%, and they are increasingly employed in society and business. The proportion of doctors working as entrepreneurs and in the private sector is growing relatively fast,” said UNIFI chair Ilkka Niemelä and UNIFI executive director Heikki Holopainen in ajoint statementon 2 November.The statement follows the release of a report on progress made in the national pilot project launched last year which saw the government undertake to fund 1,000 new doctorates, asreported byUniversity World News.“Doctoral education at universities provides excellent skills for working in different sectors of working life. Sixty seven percent of doctoral graduates work outside universities. The share of PhDs working in R&D tasks in companies has almost doubled in 10 years,” said the UNIFI statement.It noted that the parliamentary Research, Development and Innovation (RDI) group estimated that Finland will need more than 2,000 new doctorate candidates annually for R&D work alone in the period 2024 to 2030.In addition, the press release said: “Doctoral holders currently account for about 20% of R&D employees in all sectors. The aim is to increase the share, and companies in particular need more researcher-trained R&D professionals to carry out demanding research and development work.“The share of PhDs among R&D personnel in the corporate sector has risen by 48% in 10 years but is still only 8%. In Finnish companies, the level of education of R&D personnel is lower than in Finland's reference countries, and productivity development is weaker.”Overseas talentAccording to the statement, the doctoral pilot is a “significant national investment for Finland”, which has attracted the best talent potential from around the world. Foreign doctoral researchers account for a significant proportion of the researchers recruited, about half.“The research results highlight the critical importance of foreign R&D experts for the renewal of the Finnish economy and the full utilisation of R&D investments,” it said.Miia Ijäs-Idrobo, senior adviser at the Union of Research Professionals (previously FUURT), toldUniversity World NewsFinland was seeing “growing internationalisation” within its doctoral education.“Not only within the doctoral education pilot programme but more generally as well. In our union too, we emphasise the importance of successful integration of the international early career researchers into Finland and the Finnish job market. This will be one of the determining factors of whether the pilot can be seen as a success or not.“While it is important to educate new doctorates for the need of the private sector, in our union we emphasise that we should not increase the number of doctorates significantly unless we can also make sure that there are enough places and options to continue on an academic career too, since an academic career is usually the first option for many early-career researchers.“Doctoral education needs to sustain its quality too, so that it gives the necessary skills to continue either on an academic or non-academic career.”Samuli Autti, chair of the Young Academy of Finland, said international academic mobility was "a key pillar of academic research and education".“Many of us have personal experience in the benefits,” Autti said. “I am working in the UK currently, and this has been absolutely crucial for my development as a scientist in many unforeseeable ways, not just because mobility is often explicitly required by universities and funders. In this sense, mobility is a prime example of the benefits of ignoring ‘efficiency’.“The Young Academy of Finland supports increased funding for higher education, but I cannot avoid asking how much space for creative inefficiency there can be in the new three-year doctoral degrees?” Autti stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

芬蘭新推薦促進學生國際流動性 近日，芬蘭各大學及高等教育相關機構共同提出八項新建議，旨在增強學生的國際流動性。目前，出境學生流動性比入境學生低24%。根據數據，去年進行三個月以上交流的芬蘭學生僅有6,582人，而外國學生則達到8,160人。建議強調國際經驗在高等教育中的重要性，並呼籲政府機構監控進展。教育部將記錄國際化的績效指標，並計劃在2025和2026年進行後續報告。專家指出，資金支持和學校的認可程序需進一步改善，以提高學生參與國際交流的意願。

Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMIDDLE EAST-NORTH AFRICAArab observatory is needed to manage climate risks – StudyWagdy Sawahel06 November 2025TweetAn Arab observatory for climate and environmental risks should be established to help manage current and future risks and uncertainties in a rapidly changing climate, according to a new study.It should be tasked with developing educational and scientific research programmes, promoting public awareness and nature-based solutions and integrating risk indicators into urban and economic planning.This is the conclusion of the study,Towards a Strategic Vision for Managing Climate Change Risks in the Arab Region, published by the Arab Scientific Community Organization (ARSCO) in Qatar on 20 October and authored by Adil Salhi, head of the Geography and Sustainable Development Research Laboratory at Abdelmalek Essaadi University in Morocco.The study examines the accelerating climatic transformations across the Arab region and the resulting compound risks manifested in sudden rainstorms, flash floods, and landslides.Through the analysis of satellite imagery and big data from 2001 to 2023, the study reveals that more than 15% of high-rainfall-prone lands have entered the medium-to-high flood-risk category as a result of vegetation loss, soil degradation, and unsustainable urban expansion.Towards proactive risk managementThe study indicated that the present and future climate change dangers necessitate the need to move from describing risks to reformulating managerial thinking in the light of recent evidence derived from remote sensing and the analysis of massive datasets.To achieve that, the study pointed out that Arab states must “shift from disaster management to proactive risk governance, based on three interconnected pillars: scientific precision, good governance and societal awareness”.The study noted: “Arab countries must move from a post-disaster response model to a proactive system based on scientific monitoring and open data.”The availability of relevant data redefines ‘knowledge’ to become a collective product based on spatial and numerical evidence, not on assumptions and impressions, allowing governmental, academic, and community institutions to speak a common language, the study explained.“Hence, the need arises to establish a culture of open science in the Arab world and facilitate access to reliable environmental information, as it is the cornerstone of any effective policy for adapting to climate change and confronting its disasters,” the study indicated.The study also stressed the significance of the human element, saying: “the science of predictive models is useless if the human element is ignored, as there is no point in focusing on the technical element if the decision-making in the risk management process is not quick and efficient”.It stated: “Addressing climate change transcends technical dimensions to become a civilisational endeavour aimed at redefining the human–environment relationship within a framework of sustainability, resilience, and environmental justice.”The study is in line with a July 2025reporttitled “The Arab world at a crossroads: assessing future risks under changing climate”, which highlighted “the necessity for high-risk Arab countries to implement immediate policy interventions and sustainable strategies to enhance their resilience to climate change stressors”.Practical field training needed“Despite the scientific progress made by the Arab region in understanding climatic and environmental phenomena, the application of this knowledge remains limited,” the study indicated.“The main problem is not a lack of data or weak monitoring, but rather the institutional failure to translate scientific evidence into effective executive decisions,” the study pointed out.“Besides the weak integration of education and training in risk management, Arab universities rarely include practical and field training, leading to a lack of a qualified generation for implementation,” the study noted.“Scientific knowledge remains largely theoretical within the framework of prediction, early warning, and rapid response, which makes it difficult for institutions to keep pace with the rapidly evolving risks, especially since the Arab region suffers from a significant gap in climate governance,” the study added.“The absence of a scientifically and practically qualified generation in the field of climate governance exposes the Arab region to a greater future gap, as the risks are evolving at a faster pace than the ability of institutions to keep up with them,” the study explained.The study, therefore, indicated that the ‘real start’ of dealing with technical, educational, training and research challenges is the establishment of an Arab Observatory for Climate and Environmental Risks.Speaking toUniversity World News, Salhi, the author of the study, said: “establishing an Arab Observatory for Climate and Environmental Risks would mark a turning point in how the Arab world understands and manages the growing threats of climate change”.Salhi, who is also the chair of the Mediterranean Universities Union’s Sub-network on Climate and Environmental Change, stated: “Such an observatory could become a regional hub for collecting, analysing, and sharing data on floods, droughts, and other environmental hazards, turning science into practical guidance for policy and everyday life.“It should train young researchers and policymakers in the use of modern tools such as satellite monitoring, data analysis, and nature-based solutions.”He concluded: “Through education and public outreach, it could also help build a culture of environmental responsibility and preparedness.“Above all, the observatory would symbolise a shared Arab commitment to transform awareness into action, linking knowledge with resilience and sustainability for generations to come.”Climate-responsive curriculaMagdi Tawfik Abdelhamid, professor of agricultural biotechnology at Cairo’s National Research Centre, toldUniversity World News: “Despite the Arab region being considered one of the most vulnerable regions of the world to the negative impacts of climate change, no specific regional observatory exists to monitor, analyse, and report on climate change impacts, [and] risks”.Yet this was needed to support educational and scientific research policy-making and decision-taking, raise awareness, and inform stakeholders about climate change.Abdelhamid said: “We are already witnessing the dire impacts on water scarcity, food security and human health, but these impacts are only a fraction of what future generations will have to confront if climate commitments are not turned into tangible actions.“The proposed observatory, therefore, is the need of the hour for supporting Arab countries in transforming higher learning environments and curricula to be more climate-responsive and action-oriented through consolidating a shared vision for advancing Education for Sustainable Development and greening university education systems across the Arab region.”Integrating monitoring systemsDr Athari Abdulaziz Almutawa, a researcher at the Department of Desert Rehabilitation and Restoration at the Public Authority of Agriculture and Fisheries in Kuwait, toldUniversity World News: “Establishing an Arab Observatory for Climate and Environmental Risks is both timely and essential”.Almutawa, who is the lead author of the 2024studyClimate Change Impacts on Food Affordability and Access of Various Socioeconomic Groups in Kuwaitnoted: “The region’s growing exposure to extreme heat, dust storms, and flash floods demands a unified, data-driven framework to translate environmental monitoring into actionable policy.“Such an observatory should integrate satellite, ground-based, and bio-monitoring systems to generate harmonised, open-access datasets that can inform early warning systems and guide evidence-based urban and agricultural planning.“Beyond data, it should invest in capacity building by training young scientists and policymakers across Arab countries to apply geospatial tools, remote sensing, and risk modelling towards and in decision-making.”Almutawa said: “Ultimately, this initiative would not only strengthen regional resilience but also foster collaboration and innovation across academia, government, and civil society, thus transforming fragmented responses into a coordinated Arab climate strategy.”Evidence-based policies for facing climate changeTadeusz Patzek, emeritus professor of Earth Systems Science and Engineering at King Abdullah University of Science and Technology in Saudi Arabia, toldUniversity World News: “The Middle East and North Africa are experiencing climate instability and warming at roughly 1.5 to 2 times the global average rate.”Patzek, who is the advisor for a 2024 PhDthesistitled “Quantification of climate change in Saudi Arabia and its impacts on power supply, water resources and health”, noted: “Intensifying heat, chronic water scarcity, and the depletion of fertile soils together make the establishment of the Arab Climate Observatory not only necessary [but] of critical and immediate importance.“Establishing an Arab climate observatory will help in formulating evidence-based policies for facing climate change.”Andrew Mackenzie, head of the Institute of Sustainable Development at Middlesex University Dubai in the United Arab Emirates, toldUniversity World News: “As an expert in climate governance and education for sustainable development (ESG), I strongly support the call for an Arab Observatory for Climate and Environmental Risks.”“It’s not just about managing regional climate challenges; it’s about strategically positioning the Arab world to navigate the rapidly evolving global landscape of ESG regulations and investor demands for verifiable sustainability,” Mackenzie said.“This Observatory will be instrumental in providing the data, analysis, and policy guidance needed for businesses to build sustainability-integrated, regulatory-ready, and ultimately, more attractive operational models in the face of growing scrutiny and mandatory reporting,” Mackenzie added.Amal Rhema, a lecturer at the Al-Jabal Al-Gharbi University in Libya, toldUniversity World News: “The proposed observatory must also foster higher education institutions as powerful actors for aligning Arab countries with the Sustainable Development Goals to mitigate climate change impacts.”“This could be done by helping Arab universities address climate change through research projects along with educational, training and outreach public awareness programmes,” Rhema said.“The proposed observatory should also support sustainability initiatives to combat climate change and organise student clubs around environmental and sustainability issues, along with monitoring and preparing lists of environment-related courses and study programmes to help students grasp the impact of climate,” Rhema pointed out.Sustainable development in the Arab worldDr Marwan Al-Raeei, assistant professor and researcher at Damascus University in Syria, toldUniversity World News: “I strongly agree and support the establishment of an Arab observatory for climate and environmental risks, as it can effectively address the region’s unique challenges.”Al-Raeei, who is the author of a 2025studytitled “The smart future for sustainable development: Artificial intelligence solutions for sustainable urbanisation”, noted: “This initiative would enhance data collection and monitoring, allowing for a better understanding of climate trends and vulnerabilities along with facilitating risk assessments to identify areas most affected by climate change, enabling targeted interventions, especially in the Arab world.“Additionally, the observatory can support policymakers with tailored recommendations, promote regional collaboration, and raise public awareness about environmental issues,” Al-Raeei indicated.“By integrating traditional knowledge and attracting funding, it can significantly contribute to sustainable development and resilience in the Arab world.”Challenges and solutionsDr Mosab Nouraldein Hamad, director of the Excellence Research Center at Elsheikh Abdallah Elbadri University in Sudan, toldUniversity World News: “The proposed Arab observatory could face challenges including data quality and accessibility, the need for consistent, long-term records, and insufficient funding.“Challenges also involve data collection across a vast and diverse area, monitoring changing ecosystems, and adapting observatory efforts to address the specific local impacts of climate change.”Hamad explained: “These challenges could be tackled by leveraging technology like artificial intelligence (AI) and satellite data, improving data management systems to ensure quality control and access, strengthening global collaboration, and increasing investment in observation networks.“The observatory must also link to other observatories across the world to benefit from their best practices in designing educational and research programmes along with boosting knowledge and technology transfer.”These observatories could include: theSpace for Climate Observatory, which aims to adopt solutions based on space technologies to help limit the climate challenges the world is facing; the Global Observing System for ClimateGCOS), which observes networks that provide comprehensive climate data on the atmosphere, oceans, and land; theObservatory of Climate Action in Africa, which analyses African trends and initiatives for climate change mitigation and adaptation; and theEuropean Climate and Health Observatory, which supports Europe in preparing for and adapting to the impacts of climate change on human health by providing access to relevant knowledge, according to Hamad.“What matters more is ensuring that the observatory's activities and findings are translated into effective higher education programmes, efficient scientific research projects, actionable climate change policies and public awareness campaigns for accelerating sustainable development goals,” Hamad concluded.Receive email
