https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Engaged research: A most credible model for excellence
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Engaged research: A most credible model for excellenceGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFRICAHow engaged are Africa’s universities in their communities?SOUTHERN AFRICA‘Engaged University’ initiative aims to respond to SDGsSOUTH AFRICAEngagement: Universities should ‘get their hands dirty’GLOBALScholars who know the real meaning of engaged researchEUROPEMeasuring community engagement: The limitation of metricsSOUTH AFRICA-AFRICAReflections on engaged, more responsive universitiesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackSOUTH AFRICA-AFRICAEngaged research: A most credible model for excellenceEve Ruwoko05 November 2025TweetOver three days, delegates to the 2025 Engaged Research Conference hosted by the Human Sciences Research Council (HSRC) in South Africa explored how African scholarship can move from producing knowledge about society to producing knowledge with society – a vision that demands co-creation, equity and tangible public impact.During opening remarks, Dr Fulufhelo Nelwamondo, CEO of South Africa’s National Research Foundation (NRF), described engaged research as “the most credible answer we have to the twin crises of inequality and irrelevance in science” and challenged universities and councils to rethink their models of excellence.The conference theme was: ‘Engaged research as a pathway to bridging knowledge and society’, and was held from 21-24 October near Pretoria, South Africa.Addressing policymakers and innovators from 32 countries across Africa and the Global South, including representatives of the Southern African Development Community and the African Union’s Science, Technology and Innovation Strategy for Africa (STISA 2034), Nelwamondo said: “In our context, which is a context of deep talent and deep inequality, how we do research matters as much as what research we do. In the Global South, the question is no longer whether science matters, but how it matters, to whom, and with what change. We must move from research on people, to research with the people, and ultimately, research for the people, by the people.”Nelwamondo outlined three “non-negotiable shifts”: from extraction to co-creation, where communities shape questions, methods, and outcomes; from outputs to outcomes, where excellence extends beyond journal articles to policy influence and community capability; and from open by default to open and ethical by design, where privacy and sovereignty data is respected.He noted that the NRF’s five-year partnership with the HSRC, a ZAR15 million (over US$850,000) initiative launched in 2022 is putting those ideals into practice by supporting mobility programmes, peer-learning exchanges, and community-based pilots designed to embed engagement across the national system of innovation.“No engagement plan without an engagement budget. No data collection without data stewardship. No authorship without authorship justice. Communities are not end-users of research; they are its co-architects,” he emphasised.Justice as the centre for researchHSRC CEO Professor Sarah Mosoetsa reminded participants that research cannot claim relevance without epistemic justice which ensures that African voices, languages and lived experiences shape both the agenda and the evidence base.“Engaged research is not about speaking to ourselves; it is about listening and learning in partnership. Justice must sit at the centre of our research – that is justice for the marginalised, for women, for those excluded from the circuits of knowledge because they happen not to live in urban centres,” she underscored.Mosoetsa pointed to the HSRC HIV & AIDS household survey, which reaches families in informal settlements and rural areas to hear what she described as “stories of resilience in the fight against HIV”.She also highlighted the Equitable Education and Economies division’s work with small-scale farmers and land reform beneficiaries in the Eastern Cape and KwaZulu-Natal provinces in South Africa. According to Mosoetsa, engaged research also means the humanities disciplines reclaiming their place in public life.“We do not extract data; we co-produce solutions. When farmers tell us how drought has changed their planting cycles, that insight becomes the starting point for policy briefs, not the footnote.“Those days of humanities being outside communities are long gone. Research must not only describe society; it must help transform it. Our task is to turn evidence into empathy, and empathy into action,” she said.Sovereign research agendaDuring his keynote address, South African Minister of Science, Technology and Innovation Professor Blade Nzimande situated the conversation in the moral and political realities of the 21st century, arguing that Africa must build a sovereign research agenda to break donor dependence and align inquiry with local priorities.“We live in a world of extraordinary scientific promise and yet extraordinary inequality. While new technologies emerge every day, billions remain trapped in poverty. Science and technology must serve justice; they must help us design a more humane world.“Although Africa accounts for 14% of the world’s population, less than 1% of global research output is credited to African scholars. Who determines the focus areas of our research? Who funds it and for whose benefit?” he asked.The minister linked research to continental strategy, calling for stronger pan-African research infrastructures and youth-led innovation ecosystems, emphasising the inequities of the COVID-19 pandemic.“We were the last in the queue for vaccines. We should never find ourselves in that position again. Africa’s research sovereignty must include health innovation and pandemic preparedness.“If we can empower Africa’s young scientists to innovate and collaborate across borders, they will not only transform our continent, but they will transform the world,” Nzimande said.Research that listens firstThe conference showcased examples of engaged scholarship in action, for example, the HSRC’s Centre for Community-Based Research in KwaZulu-Natal that has become a hub for partnership and trust-building, where researchers and local residents jointly design projects on housing, food security and environmental resilience.Through HSRC Connect, the council’s digital platform, findings are shared with journalists, policymakers and communities in accessible formats that encourage feedback and debate.Dr Konosoang Sobane, chief research specialist and acting manager for Strategic Partnerships and Internationalisation at the HSRC, discussed the importance of the engaged research approach.“For more than five years, we’ve been deliberate about ensuring that the research we do is co-creative and innovative. We don’t want to do research for people but we want to do research with the people whose lives we seek to change. Engaged research is more than a method; it is a promise that research will listen first, act with, and deliver change that matters,” she said.From evidence to engagementSessions throughout the conference explored practical modalities such as establishing strategic advisory boards, embedding fellows in municipalities, and investing in translation roles that turn evidence into action.In his closing remarks, Nelwamondo emphasised that the HSRC’s engaged-research framework, rooted in co-production and social justice, stands as a regional model which bridges the gap between knowledge and society, evidence and empathy, and research and real-world change.“If we change practice, we must change incentives. Funders, universities and science councils should reward engagement and impact in promotions, grants and awards. We must pay for participation, translation, facilitation and community data stewardship.“When we do this, engaged research will no longer be a side programme. It will be how we do science in a credible, inclusive and transformative way,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

參與式研究：卓越的可信模式
在2025年南非人文科學研究委員會舉辦的參與式研究會議上，與會者探討如何將非洲學術從“關於社會的知識”轉向“與社會共創的知識”。南非國家研究基金會的首席執行官德拉·內爾瓦蒙多指出，參與式研究是解決不平等和科學無關聯雙重危機的最佳途徑，呼籲大學重思卓越模型。他強調，研究必須尊重社區，並以共創的方式進行，以促進政策影響及社區能力的提升。與會者一致認為，研究不僅要描述社會，還要助力轉型，並且必須包容各種聲音，特別是邊緣群體的經驗。

Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNETHERLANDSWill Dutch post-election deal boost the knowledge economy?Jan Petter Myklebust05 November 2025TweetHigher education sector stakeholders, including the Knowledge Coalition, have urged the political winners of the 29 October general election in the Netherlands to redress the current austerity measures affecting higher education, research and innovation during the forthcoming government negotiations among various political parties.The centrist-left party D66, led by Rob Jetten, garnered 16.93% of the vote, a fraction more than the 16.66% achieved by the right-wing PVV party, both holding 26 parliamentary seats. D66 is therefore expected to be invited to establish a new coalition government with two to three other parties. Compared with previous elections, this might take several months.“Like many others, we followed the election results with great interest. We are confident that, together with politicians, we will be able to take steps towards a stronger knowledge and innovation country,” commented Professor Marcel Levi, chair of the Knowledge Coalition, a partnership between scientists and entrepreneurs advocating for investment in education, research and innovation, in response to the election results in apress releaseon 30 October.“In the run-up to the elections, we heard indications that the importance of knowledge and innovation is recognised. The Knowledge Coalition is keen to enter into discussions with the new cabinet to work on strengthening and stabilising the Netherlands’ position as a knowledge economy,” Levi noted.Creating optimal R&I conditionsThe Knowledge Coalition is a collaborative network comprising university and applied sciences associations, vocational education institutions, university hospitals, the Royal Netherlands Academy of Arts and Sciences (KNAW), the Netherlands Organization for Scientific Research (NWO), and the employers’ organisations VNO-NCW and MKB Nederland that represents several hundred thousands of scientists, businesses and entrepreneurs.The Coalition works together to create optimal conditions for research and innovation to grow in the Netherlands and functions as a discussion partner for the government.“Future prosperity and economic growth are under pressure. In addition, social challenges relating to security, (economic) resilience, ageing and climate change, for example, are greater than ever. High-quality knowledge, the innovative capacity of Dutch companies, the growth of R&D-intensive start-ups and scale-ups, and a commitment to sufficient talent are therefore crucial,” the Coalition stated.The Coalition also argued that the Netherlands remains among the world leaders in terms of innovative capacity, having the talent, the brainpower and the entrepreneurial spirit. In order to remain at this level, a solid foundation is needed, topped by strategic choices in research and innovation policy.The pledge for the next Cabinet, the Coalition said, must therefore fully commit to the target of spending 3% of GDP on R&D by 2030, a target to which the Netherlands has committed itself.Achieving this goal will require additional public investment of €2 billion (US$2.304 billion) per year in 2026, rising to €3.5 billion in 2029.“On behalf of the organisations for entrepreneurs and researchers in the Netherlands, we hope that the new government will take action. Without further efforts, spending on knowledge and innovation will continue to decline, causing our country to fall behind neighbouring countries. Moreover, investments in knowledge and innovation pay for themselves many times over and deliver value for the Netherlands,” Levi said.Stimulating private fundingAccording to the Knowledge Coalition, the additional public investment should focus primarily on attracting and stimulating private funding. An increase in public R&D investment acts as a flywheel for private investment in research and development, resulting in a substantial and structural increase in private R&D expenditure in the Netherlands.According to the Knowledge Coalition, additional public investment could be used for:• A new instrument for strategic innovation programmes;• An instrument for co-funding Dutch participation in strategic EU programmes;• Modernising research infrastructure via OCW [Ministry of Education, Culture and Science] and NWO [Dutch Research Council] and experimental and new valorisation infrastructure at public knowledge institutions, such as TO2 institutions, universities of applied sciences (UAS), MBO institutions, university medical centres, research institutes and universities;• The establishment of a national investment institution for further growth of start-ups and scale-ups (for example, through the use of pension funds);• Stable funding for scientific, applied research.Wider stakeholders cautiously optimisticTheNL Timesrecently reported on talking with student stakeholders after the election. Sarah Evink, chair of the National Student Organization (Interstedelijk Studenten Overleg, ISO), said that a D66 victory presents “new opportunities for students”. She highlighted D66 proposals for a higher basic student grant, mandatory internship compensation, and the reversal of higher education cuts implemented by the outgoing cabinet.The National Student Union (Landelijke Studentenvakbond, LSVb) warned that it will closely monitor the formation of a new cabinet. “With the past years of stagnation and harsh cuts in mind, now is the moment to truly reverse the tide for students,” the organisation said.The union stressed that several parties, including D66, have promised to raise the basic student grant and abolish the binding study advice. LSVb chair Maaike Krom added: “We have not forgotten the long-study penalties and budget cuts, so if necessary, we will certainly protest again.”Coba van der Veer, chair of the General Education Union (Algemene Onderwijsbond, AOb), said the union will carefully assess the education policies of D66 leader Rob Jetten if he becomes prime minister.“He has repeatedly stated during election debates that his cabinet will not cut spending on education. We are following him critically and will hold him to that promise. The cuts must be completely off the table,” Van der Veersaid.Before the election,Dutch Newsreportedon Rob Jetten and his views on higher education and research and D66’s plans to increase investment in higher education and ‘groundbreaking research’.“Jetten supports English-language teaching at universities, arguing it attracts international talent in terms of students and professors. He wants to scrap the rule that no more than 30% of bachelor degrees should be taught solely in English,”Dutch Newsstated.In aneditorialpublished on 30 October,Cursor, the University of Eindhoven TU/e newsletter, welcomed what the election results could mean for students, higher education, and science.“For students, universities of applied sciences, and universities, a government led by D66 could be good news regardless. While it remains to be seen what the coalition partners will agree on, some outcomes are already foreseeable,” the editorial stated, cautiously welcoming the possibility of increased student finance.“D66 wants to increase the basic student grant by €164 per month, a measure that would cost roughly €600 million. The two parties GroenLinks–PvdA support this proposal, but other potential coalition partners do not consider it necessary.“Compromise seems likely. The coalition might decide to raise only the supplementary grant or to increase the grant for students living on their own. It seems unlikely that student finance will remain untouched,” TU/e stated.Professor Dr Robbert Dijkgraaf, President-Elect of the ISC and Distinguished University Professor at the University of Amsterdam & Minister of Education, Culture and Science from 2022 to 2024, toldUniversity World News: “I am happy to see that some of the likely coalition partners in a new government support increased investments in education and science.“It is absolutely necessary that this agenda moves to the top of the agenda of the next cabinet, given the current social, economic and security challenges and the leading role the Netherlands plays in research and innovation in Europe.”Professor Dr RR Ganzevoort, rector of the International Institute of Social Studies in Den Haag, and chair of the SAIL network for higher education programmes dedicated to the Global South, toldUniversity World Newsthat it is crucial to overcome the short-sighted focus on only Dutch students and the Dutch labour market.“Research and education are embedded in global conversations. The grand challenges we focus on are global in nature. And investing in students from the Global South pays off manifold in terms of international relations, business opportunities, supporting democratic tendencies, and, in short, global citizenship.“I see that some – but not all – of the potential coalition partners intend to invest in science and innovation, also globally. I truly hope that the choice of coalition partners and the ensuing coalition agreement will reflect the need to make these investments!” he stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 荷蘭選舉後新政府能否促進知識經濟？在10月29日的選舉中，荷蘭中左派政黨D66獲得微弱勝利，教育界持份者呼籲新政府重視高等教育、研究及創新。知識聯盟主席馬塞爾·萊維表示，未來的繁榮依賴於對知識和創新的投資，並強調需將國內生產總值的3%用於研發，以支持荷蘭的創新能力。學生組織也對可能的學生資助增加表示樂觀，但警告若未能實現承諾，將繼續抗議。選舉結果為荷蘭高等教育帶來希望，未來的政策走向仍需密切關注。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Completion, not just entry, is measure of HE progress
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Completion, not just entry, is measure of HE progressGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsThe forest of higher learning continues to expand in South Africa, but unequal roots remind us that access alone is not achievement. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH AFRICATime to move from ‘integrated thinking’ to ‘integrated doing’SOUTH AFRICANew policy boosts access; more reform needed to level fieldSOUTH AFRICAExperts explain gains in an ‘inefficient’ HE systemAFRICAAfrica unlikely to achieve some SDGs by 2030 – UN reportGLOBALUnlocking tomorrow’s scientific solutions today for SDGsGLOBALGlobal higher education meets to shape post-2030 SDGs agendaAFRICAMeeting SDGs requires degree quality, but can Africa do this?SOUTH AFRICAJourney from access to success at South African universitiesSOUTH AFRICASiyaphumelela student success initiative to expand in 2024GLOBAL‘Rethink, reinvent and adapt’, WAHED tells higher educationGLOBALImproving access and equity in internationalisationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredSOUTH AFRICACompletion, not just entry, is measure of HE progressDesmond Thompson04 November 2025TweetWhen it comes to widening participation in higher education, South Africa tells a story of both extraordinary progress and persistent inequality. Three decades after democracy, the country has one of the most diversified university systems on the continent – yet one still grappling with who gets in, who stays, and who ultimately succeeds.That paradox was on full display at the World Access to Higher Education Day (WAHED) 2025 conference, hosted at the University of the Western Cape (UWC) on 28 October. The event coincided with the release ofDrawing the Global Access Map 2, the World Access to Higher Education Network’s (WAHEN) new report showing that, worldwide, massification has increased participation but not equity.Across more than 200 countries and territories, low-income and first-generation students remain far less likely to complete their studies. South Africa’s experience – a nation that has expanded access faster than most of its peers – reveals why throughput (student completion), not just entry, must now be the measure of progress.Massification’s promise – and limitsAccording to the new WAHENreport, South Africa’s gross tertiary enrolment rate has roughly doubled – from about 12% in 2000 to 25% in 2020. Yet, as the report cautions, massification has widened access without closing attainment gaps, with low-income and rural students still least likely to complete their studies.Speaking at theconference, Professor Pamela Dube, vice-chancellor of the Central University of Technology (CUT), said that, according to the Council on Higher Education, only about 30% of students graduate in regulation time – with Black African students most affected.“These figures tell us we have improved access but not yet achieved equity,” she said, describing how the country’s rapid expansion has outpaced the ability of institutions to support students once they enrol.In an interview withUniversity World Newsat the conference, Bill Moses of theKresge Foundation’seducation programme drew a similar lesson from their experience in both the United States and South Africa.“Many universities, unfortunately, still have a sink-or-swim attitude – ‘if they can’t cut it, that’s on them.’ But actually, institutions themselves have to change to serve students more effectively,” he stated.In his opening address, Professor Robert Balfour, vice-chancellor of the University of the Western Cape (UWC), located the roots of inequality in finance rather than ability.“The carriers of student debt are mostly young Black women – the largest group within the so-called ‘missing middle’ who earn too much for full bursary support but too little to pay their way,” he said. The shift, he argued, has turned human rights into a market commodity: class now shapes who finishes university as much as race once did.Resilience amid inequityYet the people most affected by this economic burden are often those redefining what success means.UWC masters student Zoe Bok closed one session with a poem that drew loud applause. “I am not a victim of my circumstances, but the architect of my own future,” she declared – a line that captured both her personal journey and the purpose of the student-success movement she represents.Bok is set to travel to the ‘Achieving the Dream’conferencein Portland, Oregon, in March 2026, as part of a Kresge-sponsored delegation of South African student-success practitioners. Her voice speaks to what can happen when financial aid and emotional support meet institutional intent.So does that of Dr Lungile Hobe-Nxumalo, who grew up nearMseleni Hospitalin rural KwaZulu-Natal, where her mother worked as a cleaner and dreamed of seeing her daughter become a doctor. Supported by theUmthombo Youth Development Foundation, she studied medicine and today is a family medicine specialist and medical manager at that very same hospital.The foundation, which has supported students since 1999, now counts 624 graduates across 18 health-science disciplines, including 250 doctors. It selects rural students for health-science studies, provides academic and psychosocial mentoring, and requires graduates to serve in district hospitals that face chronic staff shortages.“These graduates prove the extraordinary potential that exists in rural youth when they are supported to succeed,” Umthombo Director Dr Gavin MacGregor said. “Money alone is not the issue – social factors affect success. Our role is to hold students accountable, but they are the ones doing the work,” he added.His remark underscored his belief that the most transformative interventions are those that empower students to shape their own destiny. The challenge, however, is translating these inspiring small-scale successes into systemic, national reform.Designing equity into the systemThat question – how to translate access into attainment – has driven one of South Africa’s most ambitious higher education collaborations: theSiyaphumelela(“We succeed”) network.Launched in 2014 with Kresge support and implemented by education support organisationSaide, the network has trained more than 1,000 university staff in evidence-based student-success strategies, predictive analytics and early-warning systems.“South Africa may be the most sophisticated country in the world in terms of a national, data-driven student-success movement,” Moses observed. “Eighty per cent of institutions and almost ninety per cent of students are part of it,” he noted.Dr David Naidoo, Saide’s senior programme lead, told delegates that the project’s strength lies in integrating academic, psychosocial and financial support. “Access without success reproduces inequality; success without equity erodes justice,” he said.Ashton Maherry, who leads Saide’s student-success programme, distilled a decade of lessons. “We know what works,” he said:• Executive buy-in;• Integrated student-success committees;• Institution-wide collaboration; and• Holistic support that looks at the whole student, not just their marks.Leadership as a catalystSeveral speakers emphasised the importance of leadership buy-in. At CUT, Dube has made student success the university’s top strategic goal. Early-intervention systems and cross-departmental student success committees have already helped reduce the institution’s dropout rate from 15.1% to 11.3%. “Transformation requires coordinated action, not isolated projects,” she said.The message echoed across sessions: reform takes evidence, but transformation takes leadership. “Any meaningful change must come from the top,” WAHEN Chair and University of West London (UWL) Vice-Chancellor Professor Peter John said.An ecosystem of supportFor many students, financial aid remains the make-or-break factor. Russell Davies, Chair of the National Bursary Support ProvidersForum, described how his network of corporate and non-profit bursary organisations works to combine funding with mentorship and life-skills training.Demand, he noted, still far outstrips supply. The country’s 26 public universities serve more than 63 million people; private institutions are growing fast, but affordability divides the system. “The wealthy can afford private education, NSFAS supports the poorest, and the middle class is squeezed,” Davies said.Beyond accessSpeakers warned that if equity is to be sustained, universities must value teaching and community engagement as much as research output. Several called for funding incentives that recognise student success, not only publications.Professor Rasigan Maharajh of the Tshwane University of Technology (TUT) argued that transformation must be systemic, connecting higher education to social and economic renewal rather than technocratic performance metrics.Universities, he said, must choose whether to “preserve a destructive disorder” or commit to “advancing human and social flourishing over profit.”A model for othersTaken together, South Africa’s initiatives suggest a blueprint for other systems wrestling with the same dilemma: how to ensure that opening the gates does not simply widen the gap between those who graduate and those who fall through.The evidence is striking:• Data analytics and early-warning systems are helping universities intervene before students fail.• Collaborative leadership has made student success “everybody’s business”.• Partnerships among universities, government and funders are proving that small, data-driven improvements can aggregate into national impact.And the stories of young graduates succeeding against the odds, like Zoe Bok and Zinhle Sibiya, remind us why that impact matters – not as statistics, but as lives transformed.South Africa’s experience shows that access and success are not separate goals but two sides of the same coin. When institutions, funders and communities work together, the result is not just higher graduation rates but a generation of graduates able to imagine – and build – a more equitable society.As Balfour reminded delegates, higher education is both a moral imperative and an economic necessity – an investment that strengthens the nation’s productivity and its shared humanity.Widening access and success in higher education relates to United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 4 (Quality education and lifelong learning for all), SDG 5 (Achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls) and SDG 10 (Reduce inequality within and between countries).Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
```  
南非高等教育的進步：重視完成率而非入學率 南非的高等教育系統雖然在入學機會上取得顯著進展，但依然面臨不平等的挑戰。根據最新報告，儘管高等教育的入學率已從2000年的約12%增至2020年的25%，但低收入和農村學生的畢業率仍然偏低，僅約30%的學生能按時畢業。專家指出，教育機構需要改變對學生的支持方式，將重心從入學轉向學生的成功。同時，南非的學生成功倡議“ Siyaphumelela”正致力於提供全面的學術、心理和財務支持，以促進學生的整體成功。該倡議的成功或許能成為其他國家教育改革的藍本。  
```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Equity in access and success must be a global HE priority
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Equity in access and success must be a global HE priorityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsAs higher education expands globally, equity depends on keeping growth balanced with the cultivation of knowledge and opportunity. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH AFRICATime to move from ‘integrated thinking’ to ‘integrated doing’AFRICAAfrica unlikely to achieve some SDGs by 2030 – UN reportGLOBALUnlocking tomorrow’s scientific solutions today for SDGsGLOBALGlobal higher education meets to shape post-2030 SDGs agendaAFRICAMeeting SDGs requires degree quality, but can Africa do this?SOUTH AFRICASiyaphumelela student success initiative to expand in 2024GLOBAL‘Rethink, reinvent and adapt’, WAHED tells higher educationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALEquity in access and success must be a global HE priorityDesmond Thompson04 November 2025TweetInequality in access to and success in higher education persists in every country – regardless of wealth, geography or system design. That is the stark conclusion ofDrawing the Global Access Map 2: Understanding higher education inequality across the world– the most comprehensive study yet to detail disparities in participation and attainment globally.Produced by the World Access to Higher Education Network (WAHEN) with support from theLumina Foundation, thereportwas launched on 28 October 2025 at the World Access to Higher Education Day (WAHED) Conference, hosted in hybrid format at the University of the Western Cape (UWC) in South Africa and online.Drawing on data from more than 200 countries and territories, the study finds that although tertiary enrolment continues to expand worldwide, students from low-income, first-generation and marginalised backgrounds remain persistently under-represented.Gender equality also remains elusive. In most countries, more women than men enrol, yet completion rates for women still lag. Students from higher-income families, meanwhile, are more than twice as likely to enter university.The study calls for stronger global cooperation and better data sharing to reduce these inequalities. It urges governments to set national targets for access and attainment by socioeconomic status (SES) and recommends that the post-2030 Sustainable Development Goals explicitly include both gender and socioeconomic equity in higher education.The lead author, Professor Graeme Atherton, Director of WAHEN and founder of WAHED, spoke at the launch. He also authored the 2016 report, which laid the groundwork for the new study.“Bringing this report back now is crucial,” he said. “We live in an era when truth is increasingly questioned. Without evidence-based knowledge, we’re left with conjecture and opinion, which undermines the very principle of equitable access.”A worldwide conversationWorld Access to Higher Education Day (WAHED) began in 2018 as an initiative of the United Kingdom’s National Education Opportunities Network (NEON) – a global day of action to address inequalities in higher education access and success.Between 2018 and 2022, it supported nearly 200 partner events across more than 100 countries, engaging over 1,000 organisations and working with the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), theWorld Bankand theInternational Association of Universities.A three-year pause followed, not simply because of COVID-19, but to allow for the creation of a more sustainable global framework. During that period, NEON and its partners establishedWAHEN, formally launched in 2024, to extend WAHED’s mission year-round through capacity-building, collaboration, research and advocacy. This year’s WAHED marks the initiative’s return as a key annual activity under the WAHEN umbrella.A global movementWAHEN is governed by a board that includes representatives from the World Bank, UNESCO’s International Institute for Higher Education in Latin America and the Caribbean (IESALC), theAsia-Europe Foundation, the Saville Foundation, the Kresge Foundation, theUniversity of Oxford, and the University of California,Berkeley. It operates out ofRuskin College, which is part of the University of West London (UWL).“In these straitened times, I think we are needed more than ever. I’m sure today will give us an even stronger collective voice in this somewhat disordered world,” Professor Peter John, vice-chancellor of UWL, said at the start of proceedings.Its 2025conferenceconvened vice-chancellors, policymakers, researchers and students from more than 20 countries. Over 35 research papers were on the agenda, while 21 partner events took place across the world – from Australia and Ireland to India, East Timor and Colombia.UWCprovided a fitting setting for the conference. Opening the event, Rector Professor Robert Balfour said: “Access and equity are central to our identity. Founded in 1960 within a divided, racially segregated system, UWC quickly transformed into an institution committed to the struggle against apartheid – and, ever since, to widening participation and advancing knowledge for the public good.“We view access as the beginning of a journey supported by innovation, engagement and, above all, care.”The conference session, ‘Pathways to Equity – Policy, Practice and Personal Journeys’, highlighted how institutions and organisations across the world are translating commitments to access and equity into concrete outcomes.Reforming systemsDelegates heard examples of how system-level reforms are achieving measurable change in participation and attainment.•Mauritius:Commissioner of the Higher Education Commission (HEC), Professor Romeela Mohee, outlined how the country is using micro-credentials to build flexible learning pathways and promote lifelong learning across Southern Africa. This exemplifies systemic reform in small and developing states, showing how flexible learning can enhance both access and employability.With support from UNESCO and theCommonwealth of Learning, the Commission has developed a national credit value transfer system, trained academic staff to create 88 open online courses, and piloted micro-credentials in fields such as information and communication technology, finance and agriculture.HEC also coordinatesPoMiSA(Potential of Micro-credentials in Southern Africa), a regional collaboration involving 18 institutions across seven countries, supported by the European Union (EU). The initiative aims to establish a regional framework for quality assurance, recognition and regulation of micro-credentials linked to industry needs.“Flexibility enhances both access and resilience, enabling students from varied backgrounds to enter, re-enter, or exit higher education at multiple points,” Mohee said.•Brazil:Affirmative action here has been policy-driven, using legislation to tackle racial and socioeconomic inequality.Erich Dietrich, professor of higher education and international education atNew York Universityand vice-provost for undergraduate education atNYU Abu Dhabi, traced how two decades of reform have reshaped who gains access to Brazil’s most selective universities – the federal institutions that are tuition-free, publicly funded and historically dominated by wealthier white students.The Federal Law of Quotas (Law No. 12,711/2012) reserves half of all federal university places for students who completed secondary education in public schools, with priority for those from low-income families and sub-quotas for Black, Brown and Indigenous students.“In Brazil, the word ‘quota’ – it’s not taboo as it is in the United States (US),” Dietrich said. “The system is transparent and quantifiable.”Research shows that students admitted under the quota programmes perform as well as, or slightly better than, their peers – challenging assumptions that inclusion comes at the expense of quality.The impact has been transformative – in 1992, only 18% of students in Brazilian universities were Black, Brown or Indigenous; by 2019, that figure had risen to 42%.•Australia:Dr Mary Teague of the University of New South Wales (UNSW) described how her institution’sGatewayprogramme is transforming access for students from under-represented communities.Operating across more than 400 schools, it combines outreach, mentoring, financial support and early conditional offers for students from low-income, regional and Indigenous backgrounds.Since its launch in 2020, Gateway has more than doubled enrolments from disadvantaged schools. Retention and success rates for these students now exceed national averages – 96% and 90%, respectively, among low-SES students, with similarly strong results for regional and Indigenous cohorts.Teague said UNSW’s model shows how locally tailored, evidence-based interventions can deliver both access and success. “Widening participation is not only about access. It’s about success, belonging and transformation.”Building a culture of careProfessor June Pym, education advisor to theSaville Foundation, emphasised the importance of collaboration, self-reflection and a “pedagogy of care” in advancing student success.Presenting examples from the University of Cape Town (UCT), she argued that real equity work depends as much on transforming institutional culture as on supporting individual students.She said sustainable transformation means “weaving multiple ways of being into daily operations – admin, communications, classrooms and ceremonies – rather than relying on specialists to fix systemic issues.” The aim, she added, is to build a genuine culture of care and culture of learning within universities, not just access at the point of entry.Investing in student successTheKresge Foundation, a private grantmaking and social investment organisation based in Detroit, focuses on expanding equity and opportunity across American cities. Its Education Programme works to increase post-secondary access and success for low-income, first-generation and under-represented students.“We want institutions to adapt to the students they admit, not force students to adapt to the institution. If you do that, you can make a real difference in student success,” Bill Moses, managing director of Kresge’s education programme, told delegates.The share of US adults holding a post-secondary credential has risen from 38% in 2008 to nearly 55% in 2023 – progress driven by coordinated interventions in affordability, advising, developmental mathematics and student support.South Africa is Kresge’s only international focus. Here, the foundation supports theSiyaphumelela(“We succeed”) network, an initiative to build data capacity and strengthen student success strategies across the system. Launched in 2014 with five institutions, it now includes 20 of the country’s 26 public universities.“We listen to students – through surveys, focus groups and participation on panels – to understand barriers and the conditions for success,” Moses said.From exile to opportunitySome of the conference’s most powerful moments came from students whose lives were transformed by access to higher education.Muleka Ilunga, originally from the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Ivan Hirwa, from Rwanda, fled to South Africa with their families as young children to escape conflict in their home countries. They are now postgraduate students at UCT and UWC, respectively.They got assistance from theDAFI scholarship programme, administered globally by the UN Refugee Agency (UNHCR), which has supported more than 24,000 refugee students across 54 countries since 1992. It provides scholarships for tuition, accommodation and living costs, as well as academic and psychosocial support.“Behind every refugee is a human being whose only misfortune was being born in the wrong place at the wrong time. Refugees are not defined by weakness or despair; they are defined by potential,” Ilunga said.“I stand here as proof of what happens when potential meets opportunity. Education changed my life, and through it I’ve seen how knowledge can restore hope where it was once lost,” Hirwa said.Frankie Randle of theUNHCRsaid: “It’s not just about degrees; it’s about enabling people to live with dignity, rebuild societies and contribute wherever they are – whether in exile, in their host country or when they return home.”The way forwardTogether, these stories – from systemic reforms to personal triumphs – reflected the conference’s theme of “Opportunity, Innovation and Lifelong Learning.”Across continents, the message from WAHED 2025 was clear – progress has been made, but the work of widening participation is far from complete.As the world looks beyond 2030, WAHEN is positioning equity as a defining measure of success – calling on governments, universities and partners to act collectively so that access and achievement become a right, not a privilege.As Balfour put it: “In a world shifting politically, economically and technologically, one truth remains: knowledge must serve all people, not only those enabled by it. Higher education institutions must be accountable not only for employability outcomes but for their public-good role.”Widening access and success in higher education relates to United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 4 (Quality education and lifelong learning for all), SDG 5 (Achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls) and SDG 10 (Reduce inequality within and between countries).Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: President launches no-fee plan for students with disability
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: President launches no-fee plan for students with disabilityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGHANA‘No Fees Stress Policy’ reimburses over 120,000 first yearsGHANAGovernment ends tertiary fees for students with disabilitiesGHANAUniversities asked not to exceed proposed 15% fee increaseGHANAAccess to education still depends on who can payGHANAPresident set on an increase in tertiary enrolmentsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyGHANAPresident launches no-fee plan for students with disabilityFrancis Kokutse04 November 2025TweetPeople with disabilities who gain admission to tertiary education institutions in Ghana will no longer pay fees under a new initiative that is intended to activate the national conscience and a sense of equity and justice, Ghana’s President John Mahama has said.“This initiative envisions a Ghana where a visually impaired student becomes a software engineer. It envisages a Ghana where a young woman in a wheelchair earns her law degree and comes out to defend the voiceless,” Mahama said.Speaking at a ceremony to launch the Free Tertiary Education for Persons with Disabilities initiative on 24 October, he said it “envisions a Ghana where a child with hearing impairments grows up to be a teacher, to heal, or even lead” the nation.“This is the inclusive Ghana we dream of. A nation where opportunity, not disability, defines our destiny,” he said, adding that the programme was a “reminder that the soul of any nation is measured by how it treats its most vulnerable citizens”.Under the programme, persons with disabilities who gain admission into accredited public tertiary institutions will have their fees fully covered by the state. The implementation of this initiative will be led by the Student Loan Trust Fund, which has been modernised and digitised to ensure transparency, efficiency, and sustainability.Mahama said data from the 2021 Population and Housing Census show that there are more than two million Ghanaians living with a disability. “Behind this statistic, behind this figure, are real human stories. Stories of perseverance, of talent waiting to be nurtured, and of dreams that deserve to be fulfilled.”National lossHe said people with disabilities faced barriers of poverty, stigma, and lack of access to education and livelihood opportunities.“These are not just individual challenges. They represent a national loss. When we exclude, we waste talent. But when we include and empower, we multiply national progress.“Our collective task, therefore, is to ensure that no Ghanaian is denied the chance to learn, to work, and to serve because of disability,” Mahama said.Arguing that the country has not been idle in its efforts to support people with disabilities, Mahama said that, in 2006, under the administration of former president John Kufuor, the country’s parliament passed Act 715, the Persons with Disability Act. In 2012, during his previous term, Mahama’s government ratified the UN Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities, aligning the country’s laws with global standards of equality and dignity.“In 2015, we introduced the Inclusive Education Policy, which opened mainstream schools to learners with disability, and began the process of transforming our educational culture. Today, we are advancing that vision one bold step further,” he said, adding that the Free Tertiary Education for Persons with Disabilities initiative fulfils a pledge he made to the people of Ghana to make higher education accessible to all, regardless of fiscal condition or circumstances.He said the government intends to distribute rechargeable motorised wheelchairs to people with disabilities, adding that all ministers of state have been directed to review their obligations under the disability act and present action plans for full compliance within the shortest possible time.Mahama said the government will establish a disability health and resource centre in every district to provide specialised health assessments and build a credible national health database for people with disabilities for informed action.In addition, the government is also to enforce a minimum 5% employment quota for people with disabilities. And this will be across public and private sectors. Private-sector organisations that meet the quota will be given tax incentives.To ensure that this is properly implemented, he said, public departments, through their ministers, will report annually on their progress in meeting the quota. There are also plans to launch dedicated programmes for women with disabilities providing those eligible with entrepreneurship grants and leadership training.Fee reimbursement for first-year studentsMahama said the government, in collaboration with the Ghana Catholic Secretariat, would use a US$30 million Chinese grant to establish a university in the Savannah Region which will be devoted to science and technology, robotics, artificial intelligence, digital science, as well as advanced medicine training. The university is expected to be aligned with the country’s policies on disability and inclusion.Saajida Shiraz, acting CEO of the Students Loan Trust Fund, or SLTF, said the launch of the initiative is a testament to Mahama’s unwavering commitment to inclusivity and equity in education and marks a significant milestone in the government’s efforts to empower persons with disabilities through education.Shiraz said the SLTF has facilitated academic fee reimbursement – an initiative introduced for the 2025-26 academic year to refund monies to students who have paid their academic fees in public universities – to 142,440 first-year students enrolled in public tertiary institutions as part of the national ‘No Fees Stress’ policy, which provides free tuition to first-year students in public tertiary institutions.Shiraz added that, as part of the initiative, validated first-year students with disabilities have received reimbursement, not just for academic fees, but for their full fees, as promised.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & 加納總統推動殘疾學生免學費計劃

加納總統約翰·馬哈馬於10月24日啟動了一項新的「免費高等教育計劃」，旨在為所有入讀公立高等教育機構的殘疾學生免除學費。這項政策的推出旨在促進公平與正義，並期待創造一個所有學生都能充分發揮潛力的包容性社會。該計劃由學生貸款信託基金負責實施，並將確保透明和可持續性。此外，政府計劃在每個地區建立殘疾健康與資源中心，以提供專業健康評估，並計劃在公共和私營部門推行至少5%的就業配額，以支持殘疾人士的就業機會。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: African states unite behind ADEA node for research strengthGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksTANZANIAHE project is redefining role of universities in modern eraAFRICA‘Time to rethink the future role of African universities’AFRICAStudy stresses need for TVET reforms to plug skills gapInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackGHANAAfrican states unite behind ADEA node for research strengthFrancis Kokutse03 November 2025TweetThe Association for the Development of Education in Africa (ADEA) will, in future, promote the development of education on the continent through its Inter-Country Quality Node on Higher Education and Scientific Research (ICQN-HESR), rather than relying on the activities of the former Working Group on Higher Education (WGHE).This follows a decision by the education ministers of the ADEA member states. The ICQN-HESR, an ADEA initiative, is an intergovernmental unit for policy dialogue and collaborative action. The WGHE was a key platform for advocacy, knowledge-sharing, and capacity-building to improve education on the continent, directed by the organisation’s partners, Albert Nsengiyumva, ADEA’s executive secretary, toldUniversity World News.In an interview on the sidelines of the 2025 Triennale of ADEA, which ended on 31 October 2025 in Accra, Nsengiyumva said that “the nodes are hosted by ministries of education in countries that have been designated or expressed interest. There is one on higher education hosted by the ministries of higher education and scientific research in Senegal.”The ICQN-HESR is focused on making higher education systems in Africa diverse, dynamic, responsive, and relevant to socio-economic development. “It is a community of practice that allows countries to share some of those practices, understand what their needs are, see how those needs can be addressed, and create a community that can continue engaging through peer learning and knowledge action,” he said.Learning poverty undermines goalsEarlier, in her keynote address during the opening ceremony, Ghana’s Vice President, Jane Naana Opoku-Agyemang, said Africa must identify gaps in what has worked in the continent’s education systems, explore what can be done differently to improve quality and relevance, and consider how emerging global trends integrate with the continent’s cultures.Opoku-Agyemang said: “Across our continent, governments continue to grapple with learning poverty through problems like [low levels of] basic literacy skills.” Despite improvements in school enrolment, learning poverty continues to undermine development goals.She said the continent must prepare education systems to cope with rapid technological change and societal transformation but cautioned that, “We need to go beyond coping. We must also create, contribute to that technology, and we need the skills that make it possible.”Opoku-Agyemang said Africa needs more, and this demands that academic knowledge must be matched with avenues for creativity, adaptability, and resilience. “[The] 2025 Triennale will strengthen collaboration among African countries so that our youth will develop the skills and cultural orientation necessary to thrive in today’s world,” she said.She called for the mobilisation of the continent’s collective expertise and innovation to create sustainable African-funded mechanisms, “because Africa is being challenged by circumstances to explore indigenous financing strategies that respond to national needs”.New hub facilities to comeEyerusalem Fasika, the African Development Bank Group’s country manager in Ghana, said the bank has approved US$96 million to support the second phase of the Pan-African University after having assisted 1,896 graduates (1,591 graduates at masters level and 305 graduates at PhD level). The first phase started in 2013.“One of the components of the African Development Bank’s support to the Pan-African University is the establishment of innovation and incubation hub facilities in each of the Pan-African University institutes to strengthen the innovation and employability of graduates as well as partnerships between universities and the private sector,” Fasika said.She believes that the resilience of Africa’s education systems will depend, not only on increased investment, but on how innovatively and efficiently that investment is deployed. “We must reimagine education finance, ensuring that every dollar spent delivers measurable improvements in quality, relevance, and impact for our children.”Fasika said education must be demand-driven, aligned with labour market needs, and designed to foster innovation, competitiveness and lifelong learning. There is a need “to commit to good action, investing in education, scaling innovation, and empowering our youth. Africa’s future depends on it,” she added.Future-ready education systemProfessor Gaspard Banyankimbona, the commissioner for education, science, technology, and innovation at the African Union Commission, said a new continental strategy on education covering 2026-35 will be launched with “a framework to define our continental ambition to build a serious, innovative, inclusive and future-ready education system”.Banyankimbona said: “These new guiding policy frameworks demand that we reclassify to labour markets the excellent green and digital competencies and ensure that children with disabilities, or those within conflict zones and climate-vulnerable regions, are not left behind.He said the African Union, together with the African Development Bank, has been advancing the African Education, Science, and Technology Innovation Fund, an African-initiated and financing mechanism designed to mobilise domestic resources, catalyse private investment, and foster innovation in TVET [technical and vocational education and training], STEM [science, technology, engineering and mathematics], and research.Banyankimbona said the African Union will continue to engage member states with a view to formalising their commitments, recognising that sustained financial and political will is essential to launch the African Education, Science, and Technology Innovation Fund and to implement its policy frameworks successfully.He said: “Education is Africa’s most strategic investment, the foundation of sovereignty, prosperity and peace. If we educate our children well, our place in the world will no longer be negotiated but earned through the excellence of our human capital. ADEA, as a trusted continental institution, must be and is, indeed, at the forefront of the implementation, driving the evidence-based policy and accountability across member states.”Critical systemic imbalancesIn a speech read on his behalf, Ghana’s Minister for Finance, Dr Cassiel Ato Forson, said Africa’s youthful population, estimated at 420 million under the age of 25, requires that the best and most significant resources in education be put to use. “We are just five years away from the SDGs deadline, and we have Agenda 2063 in focus. Thus, Africa is in a race for its economic fate and future.”Forson said: “It is a race against time, and a race we must win. Even before Africa increases its speed and race, the continent faces a dual and present challenge: insufficient domestic funding, and a sharp decline in external assistance.”He said the United Nations’2026 Global Education Monitoring Reporthas revealed that African nations spend on average 4.7% of GDP on public education. “Although this is many times within the global threshold of 4%-6% of GDP, the truth is that it is insufficient to unlock the full economic potential of the continent. More worryingly, the breakdown of the educational spending exposes critical systemic imbalances.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

非洲國家聯合推動教育研究強度

非洲教育發展協會（ADEA）最近決定將重點轉向其高等教育與科學研究的跨國質量節點（ICQN-HESR），以促進非洲教育的發展。教育部長們表示，ICQN-HESR將取代之前的高等教育工作組，成為政策對話與合作行動的平台。該節點旨在使非洲的高等教育系統更加多樣化、動態和回應社會經濟發展的需求。

此外，非洲開發銀行已批准9600萬美元支持泛非大學的第二階段，並將設立創新和孵化中心，提高畢業生的就業能力。專家指出，教育的未來需要更大程度地與勞動市場需求對接，以促進創新和終身學習。

comes to job readiness?GLOBALThese two strategies will help universities stay relevantUNITED KINGDOMReport calls for end to ‘world-class university’ modelUNITED KINGDOMLifelong learning revolution going on under the radar in EnglandUNITED KINGDOM‘Levelling up’ needs a truly flexible skills system – CommissionInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open PositionsUNITED KINGDOMGovernment urged to prioritise flexible over full-time studyNic Mitchell03 November 2025TweetToo many young people are going straight from school into full-time undergraduate study because higher education resists the radical changes needed to make it fit for purpose for a sustainable economy, according to the former boss of the United Kingdom’s Open University, Professor Tim Blackman.With the world facing technological upheaval, ecological disruption from climate change, and democratic erosion caused by disinformation, more higher education is needed – but not all in one go for 18- to 22-year-old school leavers.Instead of concentrating on full-time honours degrees, which were created when universities were small and elite institutions, Blackman argues that higher education should be spread over a working lifetime for the majority of learners – with the focus on shorter, more efficiently designed and delivered qualifications, supported by a mix of individual, employer and state funding.Blackman was vice-chancellor of the UK’s Open University from 2019 to 2024, and he spells out his recipe for change in a newdebate paperfrom the Higher Education Policy Institute (HEPI) titledA Call for Radical Reform: Higher Education for a Sustainable Economy.The paper states that the old elite system rolled into the modern mass system of higher education with little thought about the appropriateness and affordability of providing such a large volume of learning straight after school, with the educational content expected to last a lifetime.The result leaves students with mountains of debts and universities running out of cash and is unsuitable for a world faced with rapid technological change, ecological disruption and democratic erosion from disinformation and economic insecurity.Government interventionHowever, Blackman warns the necessary changes won’t happen without direct government intervention in higher education to prioritise part-time flexible learning over full-time study.Blackman also wants to drive greater standardisation of courses between different universities to enable lifelong learning to succeed and make it easier for learners to switch from one university to another as they move jobs and to different parts of the country later in life and want to top up their qualifications.Leaner regulation of higher education is also required, says Blackman, who recommends scrapping the complex and expensive “apparatus” of England's Office for Students and replacing it with less costly commissioners and a return to the old Higher Education Funding Council for England model to regulate quality in universities.In an exclusive interview withUniversity World News, Blackman said: “Despite past attempts by governments and some individual universities to encourage uptake of alternatives to the three- or four-year full-time honours degree, this qualification has continued to dominate undergraduate provision.“The reason is more due to its status as a rite of passage for young people and a selection tool for employers than due to the qualification being the best way for people to benefit from higher education, or for the economy or society to benefit.”He said shorter courses provided over time would make higher education more affordable and enable people to upskill or reskill over their working lifetime.“This is especially important as economies and societies become more complex as technology advances and disinformation erodes trust in knowledge,” he noted.More, not less, higher educationBlackman toldUniversity World Newsthat far from arguing that fewer people should go to universities, the UK needs more higher education “if we are to rise to the challenge of debt, overconsumption and under-investment that threaten the sustainability of our economy”.And he welcomed the UK Labour government’s recentPost-16 Education and Skills White Paper, which set a new target to get two-thirds of young people participating in higher-level learning and training, saying: “Higher education needs to be universal, like secondary education. There is no biological basis for believing that less than about 90% of the population would not succeed in higher education.”However, Blackman said universities must change how they do things, and “that won't happen without strong government intervention in what and how they teach and research”.He accepts that many governments are reluctant to be seen as infringing institutional autonomy and “prefer to use complicated and costly arrangements to incentivise and regulate”. However, “this will not be enough to tackle the scale of risks we face,” he said.Blackman described the UK'sLifelong Learning Entitlement (LLE)– due to come into effect next year – as “a step in the right direction, but not far-reaching enough”.Standardised curriculumAmong Blackman’s most controversial proposals is that the higher education curriculum should be standardised to put an end to what he describes as “a cottage industry” of universities designing different courses for the same subject and the same qualification, such as a degree in geography.He points out this doesn’t happen for professional qualifications such as nursing, “where the core content is consistent across institutions because there are national standards for someone to be recognised as a nurse”.He asks: “Why should it be any different for a geographer or other non-accredited subjects?”Blackman said: “Consistency of core content would mean consistent assessment standards and, very importantly, would enable people to complete a diploma in, say, geography, and then top up to a degree later or at another institution without the time-consuming bespoke curriculum mapping that is currently often required – with some institutions not accepting credit from another institution at all.”The idea of a common curriculum is likely to find opposition in some academic circles, with Rachel Brooks, professor of higher education and fellow of Linacre College, University of Oxford, tellingUniversity World News: “We already have subject benchmark statements, published by the Quality Assurance Agency, which inform the curriculum across the country.“However, to go further than this and argue for a common curriculum seems highly problematic.“Different universities have different areas of specialism – which feed into the undergraduate curriculum, commonly through optional modules.“Not only are such courses appreciated by students – but they also consolidate the important links between education and research in many of our higher education institutions.”Brooks agreed there are “many good reasons for wanting to promote more part-time, flexible learning and catering for those who want to learn on a part-time basis”.However, she said this shouldn’t mean stopping further growth in full-time undergraduate degrees.“This belies a narrow view of higher education in which it is seen as exclusively about preparation for the labour market. There is still huge demand for higher education in the UK – and we should not restrict the many benefits that flow from it to a specific proportion of the population,” said Brooks, who is president of the British Sociological Association.Diversity and innovationAnother higher education expert questioning some of Blackman’s arguments, particularly on a common curriculum for higher education, is Dr Diana Beech, director of the Finsbury Institute at City St George’s, University of London.She toldUniversity World News: "While standardisation could bring consistency – especially around key themes like sustainability and digital literacy – it risks undermining the diversity and innovation that make UK higher education globally distinctive.“A careful balance is needed between coherence and institutional freedom to innovate, particularly if universities are to remain responsive to local and global challenges.”However, Beech supported Blackman’s arguments about the LLE, tellingUniversity World News: “The LLE is promising in theory, but risks falling short in practice unless it is accompanied by a shift in how we value and support future learning.”She agreed that if full-time honours degrees continue to dominate the student support system, the LLE could become a “bolt-on” rather than a genuine enabler of flexible, lifelong learning.She also said it was crucial that any higher education reforms should not deter people from pursuing full degrees, adding: “The LLE should allow those unable to commit to three years of full-time study to progress at their own pace – without diminishing the aspiration of others.“Encouraging shorter, stackable qualifications is, of course, welcome, but this must not come at the expense of aspiration. The goal should be to create a system that values both modular progression and full-time study, offering genuine student choice rather than forcing people into a binary decision.“Reforming professional and occupational standards to support modular progression will be a good first step towards progress.”MicrocredentialsOn the question of microcredentials, Blackman said that while they fitted into his future model for higher education, he considered short qualifications to be more important, as learners were “qualifying” in something.“In that sense, microcredentials, modules and other accredited short courses are complementary but not substitutes,” he explained (noting that they may often not lead to qualifications).Blackman toldUniversity World News: “You might be interested to know that in my discussions with the Department for Education I tried to persuade them not to include modules in the LLE but only qualifications, with strong incentives to have shorter qualifications.“I felt modules should be funded from the apprenticeships levy and that students shouldn't take on debt for a course that isn't a qualification.“In the end, modules are to be funded by the LLE but only ‘vocational’ modules, while the apprenticeships levy – now the ‘growth and skills levy’ – is being opened up to modules.”Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs atwww.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
