Learning - Open Rank FacultyUNITED STATESTrump poses ‘existential threat’ to HE and democracyBrendan O’Malley16 November 2025TweetThe Trump administration’s push to control universities is posing an “existential threat” to higher education and democracy and is transforming universities into breeding grounds of compliance, a panel of university leaders and academic freedom experts warned the Anniversary of the Magna Charta Universitatum MCU2020, hosted at King’s College London from 11 to 13 November 2025.But universities have to do better at standing together and demonstrating and communicating their contribution to society in general and to their local community in particular, the panellists added.Lynn Pasquerella, president of the American Association of Colleges and Universities, said: “That we gather around at this convening, academic freedom, institutional autonomy, shared governance, the right to teach and learn and global collaboration are more urgent than ever.“Yet in the United States, every one of these core values is under attack in ways that pose an existential threat to both higher education and our democracy.“For more than half a century, the productive partnership between America's colleges and universities and the government has served as an engine of innovation, research and scholarship, a magnet for international talent, a catalyst for social and economic mobility and a mechanism for fostering global security.”She said the unravelling of this partnership through the weaponisation of research funds, tax-exempt status and international students alongside fines for institutions that fail the president’s ideological litmus test has precipitated a “moment of enormity” for American higher education.The American court system, which once championed the distinctive role of colleges and universities, no longer seems to recognise the critical importance of freedom from political interference as foundational to the strength of colleges and universities and their ability to fulfil higher education’s public purpose, she argued.“Today’s courts have demonstrated a troubling willingness to allow for the infringement of academic freedom, the erosion of the marketplace of ideas and unwarranted governmental intrusion into every aspect of college and university operations, from who gets admitted, to what is taught, how it is taught, and by whom.”She said these are not abstract concerns.“When the federal government conditions research grants, accreditation, financial aid funds and the ability to admit certain students on ideological adherence, it threatens academic freedom and practice.“When institutions and individual faculty, students and staff fear punishment, including being fired or expelled for dissenting views, the university is transformed from a site of inquiry into a breeding ground for enforced compliance.”Global academic enterprise threatenedThis crisis matters not only for American institutions of higher education but for the academic enterprise globally, Pasquerella said.“If the US model of the autonomous university is weakened, the ability of colleges and universities to partner internationally in ways that model freedom and independence is reduced.”At the same time, it sends a signal that integrity is up for grabs, and it emboldens those who seek to dismantle higher education precisely because it poses a challenge to their authoritarian, autocratic playbook, she said.Moreover, the grand challenges and wicked problems of the future necessitate global collaboration, yet the current policies in the US are making US colleges and universities less global in orientation, she said.This is despite one of the most significant strengths of American universities being their diversity, she said, with higher education institutions of all types long having welcomed international students and faculty, enriching their communities and strengthening US research programmes.Increased scrutiny of international scholars and visa revocations imposed by the Trump administration linked to protests or political views and stricter enforcement of foreign funding conditions undermine the capacity to attract global talent and engage in global collaboration, she said.“When US institutions scale back global partnerships, the entire ecosystem of international collaboration shrinks. This undercuts the global mission of higher education and weakens the US's role as a global hub of research and innovation.“Under the circumstances, American higher education is incapable of serving its previous role as a benchmark for mobility of talent, for models of institutional autonomy, for international cooperation.“When pressure builds on that system, it limits our collective capacity to sustain an open global network of universities and foments mistrust in international research, teaching and exchange in a globally interdependent world where challenges are transnational and the health of one major system impacts all.”Ripple effects far beyond the USPasquerella said the weakening of US higher education’s global orientation or institutional independence has ripple effects that reach far beyond the US.“Engagement with the Magna Charta offers an opportunity to reaffirm the principles that sustain the global university, and we need to use this moment to recommit ourselves to defending those principles and strengthening global higher education in its ecosystem, in a world that needs us now more than ever.“We need to start by emphasising the public mission of higher education, not merely as a national good, but as a global common good,” she stated.“Universities do not simply serve their home country. They serve humanity. We must insist that the global dimension remains front and centre.“Second, we must build networks of solidarity among institutions globally through organisations like the Magna Charta, Scholars at Risk and Open Society University Networks.“The attacks on academic freedom and institutional autonomy are not isolated. When any one institution is attacked around the world, each university must stand up and support the others,” she added.“Third, we need to work together to ensure that across countries and across institutions, research remains free, global and orientated toward shared human challenges. This will require advocating for sustained and stable funding for flexible models of collaboration that are insulated from politicisation,” she noted.Pasquerella said in addition, universities and colleges need to use “whatever vectors are available to us, including virtual exchange and collaborative online international learning, to encourage research and teaching projects that span borders and promote inclusive global outlooks”.‘All universities must push back’“Finally, we need to make clear that institutional autonomy and academic freedom are non-negotiable. All universities must push back against any form of coercion that seeks to condition academic inquiry on ideological conformity,” she concluded.Robert Quinn, executive director of the Scholars at Risk Network, based at New York University, said the forms of pressure on US higher education are not new.“They have happened in Turkey and Hungary and are happening in India today. They are not even new in the United States, with the current agenda of the federal administration, because individual states have been doing some of these things for, certainly, the last several years, if not longer.”He said Scholars at Risk would not be involved if the Trump administration had chosen normal legislative processes to try to exercise policy changes. But much of the pressure on higher education and research was being exerted through extra-legal measures that “clearly resonate with authoritarian and anti-democratic processes”, and what was needed was a discussion about what to do about it.“When the state declares certain terms are off limits, when the state declares that certain questions and areas of research are off limits, when the state specifically names individuals to be targeted for sanctioning, all of that is outside the norm,” he said.“All of that is a violation not only of the core principles of the university but of any democratically legitimate society.”The situation in the US has become so serious that Scholars at Risk now puts out a separate weekly media review with clips of articles about attacks on academic freedom in the US, in addition to its normal global review.Isolation used as a tool of oppressionQuinn believes the Trump administration is seeking specifically to cut off the university from the public and prevent broad-based social legitimacy of the university, removing it as a source of power and critique.Scholars at Risk has extensive experience of tracking these types of violations around the world, and one lesson learnt is that “violence is not the primary tool of repression”.“Isolation is the primary tool, separating us from each other, making everyone feel isolated, making everyone think they’re under surveillance, because it’s a lot easier to do and a lot more efficient than to take power.“So when isolation is the primary vector, then the response has to be ‘How do we unite? How do we come together?’”Like Pasquerella, Quinn advocates cultivating counter narratives. “We have to continually assert the legitimacy of the values and functions that we insist the university space has in society. So that means getting out coverage and stories and examples about how the university does serve society.“This is your job,” he told university leaders present. “It gives people courage and the space for resistance. And there’s a ton of experience in American higher education with various projects aimed at civil dialogue and discourse and its role in democracy and so forth, and all of those we need at this particular moment.”But fundamentally, creative resistance in the teaching space and creative resistance in the public education spaces are needed, he said. And to do that, the sector needs to be “intellectually and emotionally honest” and not only defend itself but also look at what reforms it can take to be fully in step with the general public.‘We need more leaders to assume risk’“When I look at it from our point of view, at Scholars at Risk, there are two ways to engage the broader public, and that's by figuring out how to be of service to the public and how to assume risk for the public.”He said those leaders who have stood up and spoken have taken on some risk for the sector, “and we need more people to do that, because this is about the societies which we serve”.Scholars at Risk has begun working with a group of 600 to 700 academics called the Emergency Defense of Universities Coalition (EDU), sharing information on what’s happening on different campuses from public universities in red (Republican-leaning) states to private institutions in blue (Democrat-leaning) states.“If all it does is pierce the isolation, that’s valuable,” Quinn said, “and if it gives a forum for people to form creative resistance of different kinds, that's even more valuable, and we're beginning to see that. In 2026 we're going to be rolling out new US-focused activities that are based on our global activities.“That means adding staff to the Scholars at Risk protection team that works with individual scholars in the US who are feeling vulnerable at this moment – we spend a lot of time talking to scholars who are worried their phone is tapped – and organising litigation and working groups aimed at trying to counter the narratives that the administration and others are using to try to separate higher education from the public.Confusion over neutralityQuinn said during and since the campus Gaza protests there has been a lot of confusion over academic freedom and freedom of expression and protest and the legitimacy of those inside the higher education space.This is common globally – in Scholars at Risk’s annualFree to Thinkreport, 60% of the cited cases involve some form of violent incident that results from student expression or the crackdown on student expression.Another area of confusion in the US is the call for university neutrality. Quinn said: “Of course these institutions should be neutral. They shouldn't be taking sides, except when you see that they really mean the university shouldn't speak to the public, it shouldn't engage with the public, and even within our institution, some say this is a way where we can protect ourselves.”But pulling up the drawbridge and taking refuge in “our tower” is no guarantee of being safe, as SAR’s global experience shows.“We quite well know that that is not true, and that when power wants to come forward into the university space, it will, whether we did something to trigger it or not.”Many cases to learn fromHe concluded by asserting that there has never been a better time to defend academic freedom because “we have never had more connection”.“There are more networks that work on academic freedom today than there had ever been in the history of human freedom. We have a bigger body of experience dealing with these threats than we have ever had before.“So it's our fault if we're not learning from the cases.“We also have more tools. There is a globalAcademic Freedom Indexto measure academic freedom at the country level. There are efforts to develop measurement tools for the national levels, and these should be encouraged.“We have the attention of more interstate bodies today on issues of academic freedom and autonomy, whether it’s UNESCO or the European Union or the Organisation of American States or others.Rights being stripped awayJonathan Becker, vice-president for academic affairs, director of the Center for Civic Engagement at Bard College, US, and vice-chancellor of the Global Higher Education Alliance for the 21st Century (GHEA21), said the US was going through a “second redemption”.A professor of political studies, he explained that after the American Civil War, after Reconstruction, there was the Redemption, when the rights given to African Americans were systematically stripped away, and the era of the Jim Crow laws – entrenching unequal rights for Blacks and Whites – began.This was countered roughly from the 1950s when theBrown versus the Board of Educationdecision spoke out against separate but equal and ushered in an era of desegregation and civil rights, culminating with the empowerment of African Americans, but also the 26th amendment, which lowered the voting age from 21 to 18.“That was called the Second Reconstruction, and now we’re in this period of Second Redemption, where many of the rights of things that we hold dear are being stripped away.“And first and foremost [under attack] are universities, because they’re seen to represent two things that our ‘Redeemer in Chief’ really objects to.“One of them is that young people tend not to vote that way, and [the other is the] independent thinking which goes on on college campuses.“So we face this political and authoritarian moment. And the greatest threat in an authoritarian moment is not specific actions; it’s self-censorship. It's the actions that people take without being seen.“But he said there was also a second challenge which has not been discussed enough, which is the “neoliberal challenge” because of the emphasis on career outcomes in universities and the “dismantling of social science and humanities departments, done in part by institutions themselves.”Universities ‘need to be self-critical’However, he agreed that universities need to be more self-critical. He cited his own experience of being rebuffed by an elite university when he asked if they could ask their students to join a Bard project teaching young people in the inner cities who needed support during the COVID pandemic.He said it is really important, not just for US institutions but also institutions globally, to remember the role universities play in “shaping future citizens, not just employees”.But he said it was also about cultivating citizens of other countries. He cited examples of the work Bard does, running ‘early colleges’ in six major US cities and running the largest degree-granting prison education programme in the US, but also supporting universities abroad, including in Palestine, and universities in exile such as the American University in Afghanistan.“Two things that I think the Magna Charta Universitatum talks about that are worth underlying are, one, the civic role and responsibility of institutions.“American institutions historically have emphasised this civic role, going back to Benjamin Franklin before the revolution, Thomas Jefferson, and the Truman Commission after the Second World War, and they all talk about the link between higher education and democracy, and almost every American mission statement – read Princeton’s, read Stanford’s – talks about that.“He said it is really important not just for US institutions but all institutions globally to remember that.“Our slogan in the Global Higher Education Alliance is, ‘What can we do together that we cannot do alone?’ And I would say, when universities are under assault, there is much that we can do together, but what we do together is much easier if we've been cooperating earlier.”As an example, he cited the fact that Bard academics, when the Taliban retook control over Afghanistan, were able to work with partners from the American University of Central Asia, from the Central European University, from the American University of Beirut, and from the American University of Afghanistan to get many students, including many young women, out of the country, overland, so they could continue their education.“We did this because we had been working earlier as a network who believed in academic freedom. I literally had a student who said, ‘I can't get my homework in because the Taliban is invading my city’.”That same student now is at Yale University, running a programme which educates young Afghan women in English abroad using WhatsApp.At the opening of the session on Insights from the US,University World Newspresented an assessment of the impact of the Trump administration’s proliferating attacks on US academic freedom, university autonomy, and its weaponisation of funding for higher education and science on higher education, research, and student and scholar recruitment worldwide. 特朗普對高等教育和民主構成“生存威脅”

近期，數位大學領導人和學術自由專家在倫敦舉行的馬格納憲章周年慶典上警告，特朗普政府對大學的控制行為正對高等教育和民主構成“生存威脅”。美國大學協會主席林恩·帕斯克雷拉指出，政府對研究資金和國際學生的武器化，危及學術自由，並將大學轉變為服從的溫床。她強調，這一危機不僅影響美國的教育體系，也對全球學術合作造成威脅，呼籲大學共同捍衛學術自由和機構自治，強調高等教育的公共使命。```

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Magna Charta signatories top 1,000 amid growing threats
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Magna Charta signatories top 1,000 amid growing threatsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsDalarna University from Sweden became the 1000th institution to sign up to the Magna Charta Universitatum. Image: Wikimedia Commons.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALMagna Charta signatories top 1,000 amid growing threatsBrendan O’Malley16 November 2025TweetDalarna University, Sweden, became the 1,000th higher education institution to sign up to the Magna Charta Universitatum on 13 November when its vice-chancellor, Jörgen Elbe, inked his name on the document.It was an act charged with symbolic importance, sparking loud applause among the 250 university leaders in attendance at a ceremony in the chapel at King’s College London, given the unprecedented context of relentless attacks on academic freedom and university autonomy by the Trump administration in the United States and the questioning of universities’ role in society in many countries across the world.In akeynote speechat the signing ceremony, Sjur Bergan – former head of the Council of Europe’s Education Department and one of the main authors of the Lisbon Recognition Convention, the only international treaty in the European Higher Education Area – said: “Without academic freedom and institutional autonomy, we cannot have high-quality learning, teaching and research. Without academic freedom and institutional autonomy, we also cannot have a fully functional democracy.“There are few governments and institutions that do not aspire to quality. There should be no fewer governments and institutions that do not aim for democracy.“No rector will admit to aiming for second best. Some governments are, alas, unapologetic about aiming for the worst.”Including Dalarna, the 36 universities that signed theMagna Charta Universitatumon 13 November joined a growing global family of institutions concerned with and committed to academic freedom and institutional autonomy.Originally adopted and signed by 388 universities in 1988 as a commitment to academic freedom and autonomy, the document was revised in 2020 to include a focus on universities’ responsibility to address society’s needs. By this year it had accumulated 975 signatories. The further 36 who signed at the London ceremony brought the total to 1011.A key section of the 2020 text states: “Universities acknowledge that they have a responsibility to engage with and respond to the aspirations and challenges of the world and to the communities they serve to benefit humanity and contribute to sustainability.”Shitij Kapur, vice-chancellor and president of King's College London, said the university was a fitting place to host the ceremony because King’s was founded on “an ethos of knowledge with purpose”, at a time when some people said that “knowledge is for knowledge’s sake, let knowledge lead where it does”.But, he said, purpose can be “a dangerous thing, whose purpose, who gets to decide?”.“I think gathering here allows us to actually reflect on the purpose, the values, the beliefs that the Magna Charta tries to serve. I think it is a compass and it is to reinforce our commitment to that compass that we gather here.”Expression of shared strengthFor Dalarna University, Elbe toldUniversity World News: “Becoming the 1,000th institution to sign was unexpected, special, and a great honour.“The applause this gave rise to was an expression of the shared strength that all of us institutions have achieved together in defending the fundamental and important values that bind us together and that are essential for a free society.”Dalarna, established in 1977, is located in a rural area in the middle of Sweden where the population generally has a low level of education and there are many regional challenges, Elbe said.“We are currently engaged in work aimed at strengthening the academic values within the university. This effort is prompted not least by the fact that academic freedom and the institutional autonomy of universities are under threat in many parts of the world.“Having the opportunity to sign the Magna Charta was therefore very important to us.. By signing, we join all the higher education institutions around the world that have collectively taken a stand to safeguard these values.”The signing ceremony was the final event in a three-day conference celebrating the Anniversary of the Magna Charta Universitatum MCU2020, on the theme, “What are universities for? Higher education principles, values and responsibilities in a fragmented world”, held from 11-13 November.The sessions focused on how to defend higher education from threats to academic freedom, university autonomy and funding for higher education and research and many of the discussions focused on improving and communicating universities’ contribution to society.Leaders suffering moral distressHighlighting the tense context of Trump’s “fire hose” of executive orders, court cases and funding threats against colleges in the US, Lynn Pasquerella, president of the American Association of Colleges and Universities, which has more than 1,000 member institutions, warned of universities facing an “existential crisis” that was taking a heavy personal toll on the country’s university leaders.“Over the past several months, I have never seen such a rise in moral distress among campus leaders, where they feel like they're being coerced into making decisions they believe are unethical, but they think they have no choice, and in many instances, that moral distress has morphed into a kind of moral injury that results from the continual erosion of one's moral compass,” she said.“And the question they need to keep asking themselves is, ‘How much individual injustice am I willing to countenance for the sake of long-term reform?’“So it's for this reason that participation in the Magna Charta is not symbolic. It's strategic and moral. It connects American higher education to a global coalition that insists on the independence of knowledge, on the integrity of research and on the role of universities as accelerators of human progress.“It reminds us that we're part of a centuries-old tradition of inquiry that belongs to the world. In this moment when the politicisation of higher education seeks to narrow the nature and scope of what we do, the Magna Charta confirms that we cannot be compromised, that universities must be free, inclusive, globally engaged and accountable to the truth, above all of us.”Challenging the lieRobert Quinn, executive director of Scholars at Risk, the New York University-based scholar rescue organisation, said there has never been a more important time to defend academic freedom.“One side is trying to tell the public this university space is not good for you. It has nothing to offer you, and that's a lie. So if we can show that it's a lie, and we can make it true that we are in service to the communities in which we're in, then I think we have a very good chance of winning that area.”“So I applaud Magna Charta for looking in this space, and we look forward to being in partnership however we can.”Conference speakers discussed public responsibilityofhigher education vs public responsibilityforhigher education in our fragmented world and urged universities to think critically about whether they should be doing more to engage with society, co-create research agendas to find solutions to local challenges, for instance – and how they can regain control of the narrative and communicate their value to society more effectively.In a panel discussion on fulfilling rights and responsibilities within the sector, Monika Steinel, deputy secretary general of the European University Association (EUA) said: “What is important for us to really suggest ways for universities to protect and promote values themselves and thereby strengthen their own internal and external resilience.”To this end EUA in February published principles of how universities themselves can protect academic freedom.Steiner led the adoption of EUA’s Autonomy Scorecard, a key monitoring tool, and the development of a conceptual model for university autonomy which panel moderator Liviu Matei, said has been influential across Europe and elsewhere.But, Steinel stressed, that it is always important to always highlight universities’ responsibilities as well as their rights.“We are very careful not to present academic freedom as a professional privilege, because during times when universities are sadly losing public trust and solidarity that is a problematic avenue to take.“Instead I think we need to demonstrate and explain to the public why healthy and strong universities benefit them too. So maybe we need a new concerted public case for academic freedom and for fundamental values and why the entire society benefits from it.”Read about whether universities have delegitimised their own expertisehere.For a deeper exploration of the existential threat to higher education and research, readhere.To read more about the worldwide impact of the Trump administration’s assault on higher education and science, clickhere.Watch a video of the proceedings of the Anniversary of the Magna Charta Universitatum MCU2020 on YouTubehere.This article is published in partnership with the Magna Charta Observatory.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Global|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Have universities delegitimised their own expertise?
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Have universities delegitimised their own expertise?GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALMagna Charta Universitatum: A call to engage, not retreatGLOBALIAU conference to grapple with loss of public trust in HEGLOBALUpdated Magna Charta marks shift in universities’ thinkingGLOBALA meeting with a premise: Universities as change agentsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALHave universities delegitimised their own expertise?Nathan M Greenfield14 November 2025TweetAt a time when universities around the world are working to regain the trust of their publics, two senior leaders in the higher education landscape on two continents took the opportunity presented by the recent Magna Charta Universitatum’s anniversary conference to highlight some of universities’ self-inflicted wounds.Rather than speak on the issue of “Public responsibility of higher education vs public responsibility for higher education in our fragmented world”, Michael S Roth, president of Wesleyan University (Connecticut), devoted most of his keynote address on 12 November to explaining why “colleges and universities in the United States have lost the respect of a broad swath of the electorate”.His explanation did double duty, offering an explanation as to why President Donald J Trump’s relentless attacks on universities and efforts to punish them and seek control of them have not led to mass protests.As would David Willets, president of the Resolution Foundation and a former UK Minister of State for Universities and Science, when he spoke next from the perspective of British universities, Roth pointed to a number of self-inflicted wounds.One is the lack of what has become termed “viewpoint diversity”.A few decades ago, Roth said he bought the argument that faculties were skewed towards “the left side of the political spectrum” because graduates interested in making more money than professors took lucrative positions in think tanks.Now, he said, department hiring committees (knowingly or unknowingly) filter the professoriate, and the skew towards liberal professors is much more “extreme”. Meanwhile, the economic conditions haven't changed nearly as much as the filtering would lead one to believe – if indeed there ever was an “excellent economic explanation.This “narrowing”, he told the session, has been defended “with the condescension that only people on the left seem to be able to muster”. He said the argument that it’s “because smart people are on the left” was “as pathetic” as “the kind of explanation that white people gave about why they didn’t hire people of colour 50 years ago”.From an institutional political point of view, with ‘political’ here meaning the relation between the academy and the funder (the American taxpayer) that cannot be ignored, this opened the door to Trump taking up the argument that universities and colleges were out of touch, or, as Roth put it: “We’re [now] seen as parochial in the worst sense of the word.”“Our narrowness is not justified by expertise,” he said. Rather, and devastatingly for the stature of American higher education: “Our expertise is delegitimised because of our narrowness.”“And expertise”, Roth continued, “across a wide range of fields in our culture and society has been delegitimised. We have accelerated the delegitimisation of expertise in the universities by a kind of meta-commentary on expertise that we give ourselves in the humanities and social sciences and, secondly, by the flagrant appearance of bias across colleges and universities.”Roth did not elaborate on the “meta-commentary” in the humanities and social sciences he was referring to, and there were no questions about it. The phrase itself and Roth’s PhD work on post-war French Hegelian philosophers, presumably thinkers like Alexandre Kojève (died 1968), who are seen as setting the stage for structuralists like Michel Foucault and later, deconstructionists like Jacques Derrida, suggest that Roth was alluding to critical theory.‘Dangerous arguments’Willetts said something similar: “Some of these forces that are now doing such damage to universities themselves originated in universities: exhibit ‘A’ being critical theory, the catch-all phrase for structuralism, deconstruction, post-structuralism, anti-racism, queer theory, post-colonial theory, post-modern feminist theory and the Marxist-inspired work of the Frankfurt School.”Willetts was careful to say that the “critical theory movement, which is absolutely legitimate” is a “set of ideas that should be and can be expressed in the university environment”. “But they bring with them some quite dangerous arguments.”“They unleash identity politics,” he said, describing it as “a doctrine that very much appeals to a certain group of academics”.“It says words matter, and words themselves are instruments of oppression,” which he followed by saying, “It’s very hard to maintain the principles of free speech in the way that Michael [Roth] expounded them” [earlier in the programme] if you believe that words can oppress.A richer, more open agendaWilletts’ address contained an encomium to university education through a curious criticism of several decades of neuroscience that, he said, mistakenly held the view that neuroplasticity ends with the end of childhood, what he calls “early years’ determinism”.This belief, he said, which was “endorsed within the social science community”, had led educationists to claim the priority for education budgets should be on early years in order to make up for deprivations poorer children suffer, such as less access to books.Willetts attributed this argument to “a great economist, James Heckman”, though he left out that Heckman was awarded the Nobel Prize in 2000. Though Willetts admitted he was “not an academic economist, he declared (without attribution) that Heckman’s was “actually quite defective research”, because it was too focused on American instances.His reason for bringing up neuroplasticity was to argue that the belief that the brain is only plastic in the very early years was “mistaken”. Characterising the view as “Calvinist” was a clever way of saying it pushes towards a view of universities as being only for the intellectually elite.“What we now know about neuroplasticity [is that it is] carrying on for much longer.” His argument was for a richer and more open agenda for the university. “Being at a university changes you,” he said.Independence under pressureIn different ways, Roth and Willetts averred that universities on either side of the North Atlantic are wrestling with the dilemma of institutions that are both dependent on government funding and academic freedom. In Roth’s words: “You want money from the government, and the government wants things from you, from us.”During the Second World War, both in Britain and the United States, the interests of the professoriate, universities and the governments intent on winning the war were “conveniently joined”, said Roth.But, “when the government decided that we should not educate certain kinds of people because of their race, when they claim to have a gender the government doesn’t recognise, or that we’re teaching things that the government doesn’t find useful or, worse, is corrupting the youth, then the principle of independence comes under enormous pressure.”For his part, Willetts presented a convincing distinction between freedom of speech and academic freedom, which was all the more interesting because it did not, as most American efforts to draw this distinction do, make reference to the First Amendment of the US Constitution.“Academic freedom is the right to determine the shape of one’s discipline and the boundaries to it, and it is hugely different from freedom of speech. In some ways it is the power to exclude. It is the power to say what you are researching or believe is not part of the corpus of knowledge or disputed issues in my discipline.”For example, he told of a physicist in a university in the Middle East who “lost his job because he would not accept that an Islamic account of creation should be taught as part of the physics course at his university”. This refusal to teach the Islamic account of creation was an exercise in academic freedom because, the physicist told him, “A part of academic freedom is the power to shape the discipline.”According to Willetts, the physicist “was correct in standing up for what constituted physics, and eventually, I believe, he got his job back. The university agreed and satisfied the authorities that this doctrinal account of the origins of the world could be taught as part of theology … but it was not part of the physics course.“That is academic freedom, and that is a power, partly, to exclude as illegitimate views which are so divergent from the core of the scientific doctrine”. Willetts ended this argument by making the same point about the efforts of Americans to have schools teach the Christian account of creation.)Free speechHow then to rebuild trust in universities? Roth was adamant that government surveys of how many Democrats or Republicans a department has were both useless and dangerous. Instead, he pointed to examples of how, as president of Wesleyan, he has modelled both free speech and academic freedom.In one case during the Black Lives Matter (BLM) protests following the murder of George Floyd in May 2020, after a student wrote an op-ed saying that the BLM movement was corrupt, Roth wrote a piece saying that while Black lives matter, so does free speech, “which did not go over well with many of the activists on campus”, he told the session.Around the same time, Jelani Cobb, who is now dean of the Columbia Journalism School, wrote a piece in theNew Yorkersaying that “free speech is often a cover for racism”. Roth asked Cobb, who accepted, to come to Wesleyan to make his argument. Cobb told the activists enraged at Roth that he and Roth had just had dinner together. The point was to show that “people with strong disagreements can argue them out”.On another occasion, Roth, who has been very active in the anti-boycott Israel movement, invited Judith Butler, who is strongly in favour of the movement, after her talks were cancelled in New York, to come to Wesleyan and speak, “much to the chagrin of pro-Israel people on campus and to the puzzlement of the pro-Palestinian people”.Liberal educational idealsIn the session that followed the keynote addresses, a number of other examples emerged of what universities and supranational organisations can do to embody the ideals of liberal education that require the university to, as Roth put it, avoid any effort to cloister itself and, as Willetts said, to make “the wider case for independent institutions in a rich civil society”.Jonathan Becker, vice president for Academic Affairs at Bard College (Annandale-on-Hudson, NY), director of its Center for Civic Engagement, and vice-chancellor for the Global Higher Education Alliance for the 21st Century (GHEA21), emphasised the need for universities to lead by “deeds, not words”, agreeing that the push for neutrality can be problematic, particularly when it comes to standing up for democracy. Universities should speak up about access to education, and not doing so was “harmful to universities’ role in society”, he said.He argued that universities should embrace constructive engagement with society. He cited an example of Bard’s large prison initiative and GHEA programmes for displaced students and faculty, numbering thousands, around the world, and support for a network of institutions in exile from Myanmar, Russia, Afghanistan, Hungary and Belarus.Becker said another core part of the institution’s work was support for civic engagement.“Building up students’ involvement is core to the institution’s work, empowering students, and tapping into their idealism and energy to achieve important things in education.”The prison initiative was a good example. It has become the largest degree-granting programme in the US for incarcerated students, and it gained national recognition when its debate team beat Harvard, changing the narrative on education and incarcerated students.“That was started by a student, who pitched the president and said there is a group of people who don’t have education, and that is wrong.”Similarly, Bard set up a programme for Florida students affected by Hurricane Katrina and is now working with the Kyiv School of Economics to support education for high school students in Ukraine.Global realitiesAmong the speakers representing supranational organisations was David Akrami Flores, head of the Erasmus+ Policy Support Section at the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD). He told the session about the development ofWissenschaftfor the 21st century. Whereas the word has traditionally meant “systematic knowledge”, as in the sciences, political, social and geopolitical realities mean that today it requires “a real political kind of science diplomacy that is realistic but still value-driven.“We often remind ourselves that the European education area is built on shared values, and the same goes for the European research areas” as well as “Europe’s core values, academic freedom, institutional autonomy and participation.”The COVID pandemic and rise of populism, as well as wars, were challenges to these values, but such values should nonetheless “be defended and lived in policy, in institutions, and of course, also in daily academic practice. And for us, that means academic freedom, autonomy and social responsibility are not optional ideals,” Flores said.After the Russian invasion, DAAD suspended institutional cooperation with Russian state organisations. But it continued to support individual Russian students and researchers who were committed to academic freedom and international dialogue and who wanted to come to Germany to study or do research.“That’s a form of science diplomacy that protects principles while remaining open to people. It means acting realistically, but never cynically, supporting individuals today who may become the bridge builders of tomorrow.“Value orientation and pragmatism can go hand in hand, because science diplomacy must defend fundamental principles while staying responsive to global realities,” said Flores.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Peak university body suspends Al-Falah after Red Fort blast
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Peak university body suspends Al-Falah after Red Fort blastGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIAStudents hope for return to routine as border tensions abateINDIA-PAKISTANKashmiri students in India targeted after tourist attackINDIA-PAKISTANIndia will no longer recognise degrees from PakistanINDIAMilitary tensions lead to rise in campus sedition chargesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIAPeak university body suspends Al-Falah after Red Fort blastShuriah Niazi14 November 2025TweetThe Association of Indian Universities (AIU) has suspended Al-Falah University, a private university in India’s northern Haryana state, after it emerged that some of its faculty members and doctors are under police investigation in connection with the 10 November blast at New Delhi’s historic Red Fort.The explosions, officially designated by the Indian government as a terror attack, claimed 13 lives.In a notification issued on Thursday 13 November, the AIU informed the university located in Faridabad, Haryana, that its suspension had been prompted by “serious concerns” about its “good standing in light of recent developments”.The New Delhi-based AIU is a consortium of central and state-funded universities, institutes of national importance, and private deemed universities. It is responsible for evaluating foreign university courses, academic standards and credit structures to determine their equivalence with those offered by Indian institutions.In their announcement, AIU said: “According to the by-laws of the Association of Indian Universities, all universities shall be treated as members as long as they remain in good standing. However, media reports indicate that Al-Falah University, located in Faridabad, Haryana, which is close to Delhi, does not seem to maintain that good standing.“Hence, the AIU membership granted to Al-Falah University is suspended with immediate effect. Furthermore, the university is not allowed to use the AIU name or logo in any of its activities, and the AIU logo must be removed from the university’s official website without delay.”AIU Secretary General Dr Pankaj Mittal toldUniversity World News: “The suspension implies that the university will not be an AIU member until the inquiry concludes. If they clear themselves of any wrongdoing, their membership could be reinstated, but if not, it might be revoked entirely.”She added: “During the suspension, they can't take part in any AIU events, use the AIU logo, or let their students engage in AIU's sports, cultural, or other activities.”‘White-collar’ terror ecosystemThe decision comes in the wake of ongoing investigations into a suspected network of professionals, including several medical practitioners and an assistant professor associated with the university, who may have been involved in what the authorities have described as a “white-collar terror ecosystem” involving radicalised professionals and students.Arrests this week included three medical doctors linked to Al-Falah University. According to reports, a joint operation by Haryana police together with Jammu and Kashmir police uncovered a large cache of explosives in accommodation used by medical doctors at the university.Faridabad Commissioner of Police Satender Kumar Gupta said a medical doctor, originally from Jammu and Kashmir, who used to teach at Al-Falah University, had rented the room.“The group was using encrypted channels for indoctrination, coordination, fund movement, and logistics. Funds were raised through professional and academic networks under the guise of social and charitable causes.“The accused were found [to be] involved in identifying individuals for radicalisation, initiating and recruiting them into militant ranks, raising funds, arranging logistics, and procuring arms, ammunition, and materials for preparing IEDs [improvised explosive devices],” a spokesperson for Jammu and Kashmir police was quoted by theIndian Expressnewspaper as saying.There is considerable media speculation that the attack is linked to Pakistani militant groups in Kashmir, including Jaish-e-Mohammed and other groups, for example, Hizb-ul-Mujahideen. Police said posters showing support for Jaish-e-Mohammed led to the initial arrests of the doctors, which triggered police raids in Faridabad, where the doctors worked.The case has prompted a wider discussion about the responsibilities of educational institutions – particularly private universities – to bolster internal oversight and ensure that professional credentials are not misused for covert or illegal activities.Scrutiny of university staffThe incident involved a vehicle registered in Haryana which exploded near the Red Fort on 10 November, shortly after authorities dismantled an alleged terror network. Officials named a deceased suspect, Dr Umar Nabi, an assistant professor at Al-Falah Medical College and Hospital, saying he was behind the wheel at the time of the blast.Investigators have said they view this as an important lead that connects individuals holding academic posts to covert operations.Three other medical doctors from the same university are being held in police custody for questioning. Their suspected involvement seems to surpass ordinary medical duties, as their names came up during investigations connected to the “white-collar module”, according to police.Consequently, their financial dealings, professional relations, and associations have come under thorough scrutiny, according to officials.Official sources indicate that the doctors may have had roles ranging from logistical support to establishing key contacts within the network.Following the arrests, forensic experts and bomb disposal teams carried out extensive searches across the university campus, indicating that investigators were looking for physical evidence such as explosive residues or communication devices to reconstruct the events leading up to the blast.The intense scrutiny has unsettled many students and faculty members, worried the investigation may cast a shadow over the entire institution. One student privately expressed concern that such enquiries can create a stigma for those at the university.Meanwhile, the university has affirmed its full cooperation with authorities and voiced support for all measures aimed at ensuring a fair and transparent investigation.Financial Investigation under waySources close to the investigation revealed the government has also ordered a forensic audit of Al-Falah University’s financial and administrative records.The Enforcement Directorate under the Ministry of Finance, which investigates money laundering and violations of foreign exchange laws, along with other financial oversight agencies, is tasked with scrutinising transactions, funding sources, and the university’s accounts.The decision to conduct a financial probe was made at a high-level review meeting chaired by Minister of Home Affairs Amit Shah after senior officials briefed him on the progress of the probe. The meeting lasted over 90 minutes, reflecting the gravity of the situation and the need to thoroughly establish any institutional links, the sources said.Al Falah University was set up by the Al-Falah charitable trust in 1995. In 2014, the Haryana government granted it university status. The Al-Falah Medical College, affiliated with the university, established undergraduate medical degrees in 2019, followed by postgraduate classes in 2023. Al-Falah operates a small hospital with 650 beds.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

印度高等教育協會近日宣布暫停阿爾法拉大學的會員資格，原因是該校部分教職員涉嫌與11月10日新德里紅堡爆炸案有關。該爆炸事件被官方定性為恐怖襲擊，造成13人喪生。

協會指出，阿爾法拉大學未能維持良好的聲譽，因此立即停止其AIU會員資格，並禁止其在任何活動中使用AIU名稱或標誌。此事件引發了對教育機構的責任和內部監督的廣泛討論，尤其是在私立大學中。大學表示將全力配合當局的調查，並支持確保調查公正透明的所有措施。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Worldwide impact of TrumpŌĆÖs assault on HE and science
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Worldwide impact of TrumpŌĆÖs assault on HE and scienceGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsUnited States President Donald Trump, Image: The White House, Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALMagna Charta Universitatum: A call to engage, not retreatSOUTH AFRICA-UNITED STATESThe triumph over apartheid offers hope to US universitiesUNITED STATESTimeŌĆÖs up: Seven of nine universities reject TrumpŌĆÖs dealGLOBALUniversities are at risk of becoming factories of consentSOUTH AFRICA-GLOBALAcademy of Science warns of ŌĆśextreme crisisŌĆÖ under TrumpUNITED STATESTrump orders HE accreditation overhaul to stamp out DEIUNITED STATESHE leaders unite over TrumpŌĆÖs ŌĆśoverreach and interferenceŌĆÖUNITED STATESTrump cuts US$2.2 billion and threatens rebellious HarvardGLOBALScientists self-censor as Trump axes research fundsGLOBALTrumpŌĆÖs USAID shutdown impacts universities worldwideInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED STATES-GLOBALWorldwide impact of TrumpŌĆÖs assault on HE and scienceBrendan OŌĆÖMalley14 November 2025TweetAt the Anniversary of the Magna Charta Universitatum MCU2020, I was asked to speak about the impact of the Trump administrationŌĆÖs policies on science, students and systems of higher education around the world, setting the scene for a panel discussion on insights from the US on the conferenceŌĆÖs theme of ŌĆ£What are universities for? Higher education principles, values and responsibilities in a fragmented worldŌĆØ.We have been reporting extensively on these developments inUniversity World Newsthrough our worldwide network of correspondents from India to Africa, from North America to Australasia ŌĆō and in particular by North America correspondent Nathan M Greenfield, who has provided relentless in-depth coverage of these issues.Many of our articles on this issue are presented in ourTrump tracker hubso that you can scroll down and see the impacts over the months of the Trump administration, going all the way back to inauguration day in January.What emerges is not just a story of proliferating attacks on academic freedom and university autonomy and very significant cuts in funding for higher education and research in the US, but also a serious knock-on impact on international science collaborations, a reorientation of international student flows and a raft of policy changes across other countries to capitalise on the flight of scholars from the US.The turmoil this has caused for universities in the US and in other countries highlights universitiesŌĆÖ vulnerability to political pressure due to resource dependence and demonstrates a downside of international interdependence, which is that decisions made in one country ŌĆō especially if it is a world leader in science ŌĆō can have implications elsewhere.The Trump administration began its attacks on higher education and research with his executive orders dismantling diversity, equity and inclusion programmes; the revoking of visas, and the arrest and detention of international students or former students who were active in the Gaza campus protests; and the slashing of research budgets for key universities and science institutes.Mass cuts of the budgets of the National Institutes of Health and other agencies that fund research via US universities, and other cuts implemented via the shuttering of USAID, left researchers across the world afraid to speak out for fear of jeopardising other projects.The first direct threat to university research funds, the letters from Education Secretary Linda McMahon to Harvard and Columbia universities in early March, justified slashing US$2.6 billion as part of the effort to fight antisemitism on these Ivy League campuses.The disconnect between McMahonŌĆÖs chosen means and the declared end was hard to miss.As Julio Frenk, chancellor of the University of California, Los Angeles,toldUniversity World News: ŌĆ£The grants that are being cancelled by the government . . . have nothing to do with antisemitism, wokeness [or] not allowing viewpoint diversity. These are grants in basic science, in astronomy, and in physics, which have been the basis for American prosperity and security. We need to address the real problem, but not weaponise it.ŌĆØIn June,Forbesreported that the NIH had cut US$3.8 billion in research grants. Court cases ensued.In August, the Supreme Court of the United Statesgreenlighted cuttingUS$738 million of health research funds from the NIH that had been ordered by the Secretary of Health and Human Services, a decision that led to thousands of research projects being cancelled in both the United States and in universities around the world where researchers worked in tandem with the Americans.To understand how this plays out on the ground, let us consider thestory of Dr Julie Bruneau, a researcher atCentre de recherche du Centre hospitalier de lŌĆÖUniversit├® de Montr├®al(CHUM) in Canada. Her lab was working with Columbia University, studying the efficacy of two models of medical care for people who inject drugs but are disengaged from the health system.ŌĆ£We received a very, very short email saying that in the midst of the cuts at Columbia, our project was terminated.ŌĆØ Brueau did not have to destroy her data; many others have not been so lucky.The ban on diversity, equity and inclusion programmes in the federal government and in federally funded institutions, and other declarations that there are only two genders, and the banning of critical race theory provoked shivers andself-censorshipamong researchers worldwide afraid of losing US funding.Also in March, the Trump administration, ignoring the sovereignty of at least Australia, Britain and Canada, moved to try to control researchers outside the US working on projects funded by the US Treasury.Researchers weresent a questionnairethat asked:ŌĆó ŌĆ£Does this project reinforce US sovereignty by limiting reliance on international organisations or global governance structures (for example, the UN, the WHO)?ŌĆØŌĆó ŌĆ£Can you confirm that this is [sic] no DEI project, or DEI elements of the project?ŌĆØ;ŌĆó ŌĆ£Can you confirm that this is not a climate or ŌĆśenvironmental justiceŌĆÖ project or includes such elements?ŌĆØPutting USAID through the chipper, as billionaire Elon Musk gleefully described it, impacted universitiesacross Africa(in addition, of course, to health and food programmes that served the general population).The cuts negatively impacted Africa-focused capacity-building programmes and research into HIV/AIDS, malaria and other diseases. Climate research was curtailed. Projects to create robust crops and environmentally friendly farming methods were made insecure.USAID did not only fund food and medical aid and university students and research. In Colombia, it helped fund the peace process that began several years ago when guerrilla group FARC agreed to lay down its arms.According toAstrid Liliana S├Īnchez Mej├Ła, vice rector of research at thePontificia Universidad Javeriana(Bogot├Ī), cuts to NGOs working to further the peace process in Colombia threaten the universityŌĆÖs contribution to the reconstruction of ColombiaŌĆÖs civil society.Another key impact area is international student flows.The number of international studentsaffectedby the travel ban against 19 countries announced in June was fewer than 20,000, less than 2% of the total number of international students in the country.But the ban is just one of a number of the Trump administrationŌĆÖs policies that have created a hostile environment for immigrants in general and international students in particular.An untold number of international students have been among those targeted by Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE). As of August 2025, 6,000 student visas had been revoked, according toThe Washington PostandTimemagazine. They include Tufts University student Rumeysa ├¢zt├╝rk, whose visa was revoked for writing an op-ed in support of Palestine in the student newspaper.In April the records of thousands of international students were erased, many due to minor or dismissed legal infractions, leaving them without legal status until the decision was reversed, asTimereported.The Trump administration has also changed visa duration and two graduate pathways: the Optional Practical Training (OPT) programme and, especially, H-1B visa work authorisation rules which allowed international students in STEM programmes to work at universities ŌĆō vital to the supply of the nationŌĆÖs teaching assistants and research assistants ŌĆō and stay on for three years after graduation. The cost of company-sponsored H1-B visas wasraised twentyfoldfrom US$5,000 to US$100,000.All these factors are part of a wider narrative against immigrants, not just international students, that by the end of August had seen230,000 people arrested, 234,000 deportedand at that point there were 60,000 people in detention.It was, therefore, hardly surprising when at the end of September, Studyportals data showed that interest in studying for a masters degree in the US haddeclinedby a staggering 61% since Trump returned to the Oval Office in January.Deepening the instability for higher education in the US, on 1 October, the so-called Trump Compact, the offer to nine elite universities in exchange for preferred access to Federal grants,set outcommitments universities had to make, including:ŌĆó ŌĆ£Revising governance structures that purposely punish, belittle, and even spark violence against conservative ideasŌĆØ;ŌĆó Committing to defining maleŌĆÖ, ŌĆśfemaleŌĆÖ, ŌĆśwomanŌĆÖ, and ŌĆśmanŌĆÖ ŌĆ£according to reproductive and biological processesŌĆØ (that is, erasing transgendered individuals)ŌĆó Conducting rigorous, ŌĆ£broad spectrumŌĆØ assessments of ŌĆ£viewpoints among faculty, students and staff at all levelsŌĆØ and making these findings public;ŌĆó Keeping international undergraduate enrolment to no more than 15% and enrolment from any one country to no more than 5%.ŌĆó Requiring all ŌĆ£foreign studentsŌĆØ (the phrase itself is revealing) to take a mandatory course on American civics.The cuts to research, the visa restrictions and the increasingly hostile environment for international students have both provided cover for some countries to bring in their own restrictions and caused others to seize an opportunity to attract more top talent deterred or seeking escape from the current US context.In June, for example, JapanŌĆÖs then Prime Minister Shigeru Ishibaorderedrelevant ministries to recruit researchers seeking to leave the US.Last month the United Kingdom governmentannounced new rulesthat allow expansion of its highly skilled talent route while tightening up on immigration in general.Only last Tuesday the 2025 Canadian budgetcontainedalmost CA$1.7 billion (US$1.2 billion) to recruit one thousand scholars of note but at the same time cut student visas by 49%.This is a reminder that while the Trump administration is pursuing an agenda right out of an authoritarian playbook, it is by no means the first country to seek to tighten control over higher education and will not be the last. You only have to look at earlier examples from Hungary and Turkey. The Trump administration is copying many measures straight from Viktor Orb├ĪnŌĆÖs playbook.It has been interesting to see the thoughts of academic experts on how universities should respond to the political pressures exemplified by the Trump administrationŌĆÖs demand for compliance as a condition of federal funding.One is by Chilean education Professor Julio Labra├▒a, whostated: ŌĆ£Autonomy is not merely the absence of interference; it is the capacity to establish and pursue oneŌĆÖs own priorities without coercion.ŌĆ£It demands structural conditions that enable a degree of organisational latitude: stable and predictable funding, legal safeguards and broad-based social legitimacy.ŌĆØHe said that given the financial pressures, the task ahead is ŌĆ£not to romanticise universities but to reimagine their autonomy in a world where dependence is increasingly inevitableŌĆØ.Another view is our lead commentary this week by Sioux McKenna, who has seen this playbook before in her own country, South Africa, during apartheid. Shearguedthat both contexts have involved systematic attempts to subordinate higher education to authoritarian political projects.One lesson from the eventual triumph over apartheid was that individual courage mattered. So did international solidarity. Contemporary threats, McKenna wrote, require similar collective responses.This means professional associations defending member institutions. It means universities standing together rather than competing for scraps of funding. And it means the international academic community refusing to abandon US colleagues to their fate.To explore our coverage of the impact of Trump administration policies on higher education and research in the US and worldwide, scroll down ourTrump tracker hub.Brendan OŌĆÖMalley is editor-in-chief ofUniversity World News.This is an edited version of a speech he made at a panel on insights from the US at the Anniversary of the Magna Charta Universitatum MCU2020, which focused on the theme of ŌĆ£What are universities for? Higher education principles, values and responsibilities in a fragmented worldŌĆØ, held at KingŌĆÖs College London, from 11 to 13 November 2025.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinetŌĆÖs tagline is: ŌĆśImmensely Powerful and Insanely FastŌĆÖIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI ŌĆō Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
