Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPALESTINEMinister appeals for help to safeguard higher educationTarek Abd Elgalil14 November 2025TweetAfter two years of a war that has destroyed higher education infrastructure and killed hundreds of university workers and well over one thousand students in Gaza, Palestine’s Minister for Education Amjad Barham says his ministry is doing everything possible to ensure the continuity of university education for Palestinians and is appealing to the international community for help.In an exclusive interview withUniversity World News, Barham said Israel’s war on Hamas in Gaza has destroyed more than 80% of university buildings in the territory, including workshops and laboratories.Citingministry statistics, he said nearly 400 schools had been either destroyed or damaged, and more than 60 university buildings have been completely destroyed during the recent aggression on the Gaza Strip.Yet Palestinians remain steadfast in defending their right to learn, he said.While education has endured a “fierce assault” in Palestine, it remains a fundamental pillar of building an independent Palestinian state. “We hold an absolute belief that through education, we can build state institutions, safeguard their resources, and ultimately achieve liberation from the occupation,” he toldUniversity World News.The scale of the destruction in Gaza supports claims byUN experts and scholarsof “scholasticide” in Gaza, a deliberate campaign by Israel to destroy Palestinian educational and cultural institutions and the intellectual and social fabric of Palestinians.“Today, we cannot speak of having a single hall ready to receive students for face-to-face learning,” Barham said. “The remaining university buildings – which are very few – are being used as shelters and accommodation for our people who have been displaced from their homes after their destruction.”Ceasefire uncertaintyA US-brokered ceasefire to end the war has been in place since 10 October 2025, giving the territory some relief from relentless Israeli airstrikes, but it’s too early to talk about rebuilding, Barham said. The ceasefire agreement has repeatedly come under pressure, with flare-ups of violence on both sides.“First and foremost, we desire security and safety for our students”, and an end to all aggression, he said. “Subsequently, we will work to provide temporary buildings and facilitate the return of in-person education in these structures until, God willing, the reconstruction process is complete.”According toavailable reports, 241 university personnel have been killed since the war began, including three university presidents and at least 96 academics, with the remainder being administrative and technical staff.The figure is based on special documentation efforts, while a joint report by the ministry and UNESCO confirms that around 1,351 university students have also been killed. Thousands of others – both staff and students – have been injured, Barham said.Laboratories and libraries can be restored, but scientists, scholars, and intellectual leaders are irreplaceable. In this context, the saying “a loss beyond price” is tragically fitting, he said.Beyond Gaza, higher education in the Israeli-occupied West Bank has also suffered under settler violence, Israeli policies that withhold funds the Palestinian Authority needs to run schools and pay salaries, and roadblocks that restrict the movement of professors and students.Universities in these territories have been forced to shift to e-learning because of the difficulty students and teachers face in trying to reach the campuses. Arab students and teachers in occupied East Jerusalem have suffered as well, said Barham. “At times, entering and leaving was almost impossible.”Within war-torn Gaza, the impact of war on the economy has directly impacted the educational process. “Students’ inability to pay tuition fees has negatively affected universities and their capacity to meet their obligations, especially salaries for staff,” he said.The situation raises fears in the ministry of an exodus of scientific expertise and teachers. “We do not want the exodus of scientific expertise and teachers within the universities because of this aggression. Therefore, we must work to provide their salaries,” he said.Resilience amid ruinBarham said Palestinian students have largely maintained their enrolment despite the harsh circumstances – relying on virtual instruction and distance learning.“Teachers conduct classes for students in the West Bank and the Northern Governorates in the morning, and then, through virtual schools and distance learning, they teach students from Gaza in the evening session,” he said.Barham said that this approach has ensured regularity in the educational process across Palestine.“There has been consistent attendance for the majority of students in the educational process, and we have been able to complete the two academic years through the virtual schooling system.”He said the ministry had been able to hold the Tawjihi high school exit exams this fall. Exams for Gaza students born in 2006 were conducted online in September, a year later than normal, and those born in 2007 took their exams in October, Barham said.Passing the Tawjihi is an important occasion for Palestinian high school students, as their scores may determine whether they get into university, which subjects they may study, and what careers are open to them.Barham said education is considered a priority for Palestinian families, who “greatly support and sponsor the educational process. Everyone is united to protect education and our right to education.”“This [Tawjihi exam] represents a major challenge, but one that has restored hope and illuminated the future, despite the hunger, thirst, displacement, home demolitions, and killing. The curriculum has brought back hope to our families inside the Gaza Strip and to our male and female students within the strip,” he said.Barham said the ministry is in contact with the heads of Palestinian universities to facilitate the enrolment of Tawjihi students (classes of 2006 and 2007). Ninety per cent of the 60,000 students who sat the exam (both cohorts) are expected to enter higher education.Priorities in Gaza and the West BankWith a ceasefire agreement in place and hopes that the war will end soon, Barham said his ministry’s foremost priority is the restoration of regular education in the Gaza Strip, alongside strengthening academic institutions in the West Bank and East Jerusalem, which are also facing challenges.In the Gaza Strip, Barham said: “the very first step would be to restore the in-person educational process”, initially in temporary learning centres while campuses are repaired or rebuilt.However, before launching any large-scale reconstruction of Gaza’s higher education institutions, Barham said, certain political, security, and logistical conditions must be met.Guarantees are needed that universities and colleges will not be targeted again in the future, he said. Reconstruction efforts must also address environmental damage. Debris removal should follow scientific protocols to avoid toxic hazards that could threaten the educational environment if not properly handled.The scale of destruction at Gaza’s universities is immense, and restoration will require significant time, expertise, and funding. The cost of restoring universities will be in the hundreds of millions of dollars, he said, and rebuilding schools could cost several billion dollars more, Barham said.Cooperation with international communityBarham said his ministry was working in full cooperation with ministers of education in other Arab countries. “Some students who were displaced from the Gaza Strip are currently registered in friendly and neighbouring Arab countries as visiting students,” he said, enabling them to continue their studies until they can return to their original universities.‘There are also some countries where students have been registered and their academic records transferred to complete their studies, especially students in their final years,” he said. “This allows them to finalise their education and graduate, thereby qualifying them to join the job market and assist their families during these difficult circumstances.”The ministry was also “trying to provide whatever assistance is possible to support students in completing their postgraduate studies through all available means – whether through friendly nations that offer full scholarships or countries that support such programmes”.According to Barham, regular consultations are held with university presidents to shape a recovery plan in coordination with international and Arab partners.“These include initiatives with the Education Above All Foundation, the Welfare Association (Taawon), and the PAMA Foundation, as well as ongoing discussions with UNESCO to expand its operational support. It is time for concrete, on-the-ground interventions,” he said.The ministry has also received expressions of solidarity from numerous universities and academics around the world and offers of teaching assistance.“There is already a group of professors from several countries contributing to distance learning for students in the Gaza Strip,” he said.However, still more support from the international community is needed to pay staff salaries, to reduce the flight of academic talent, to help students to pay their tuition fees, and to generally keep universities afloat.In the Gaza Strip, where universities are non-operational, the “only solution” has become reliance on external aid and student tuition fees, he said.Coordination is underway with international partners to help secure tuition payments for students. “If tuition fees are secured, the university gains a budget and can then fulfil its responsibilities and provide salaries for its employees during this very significant challenge,” he said.Universities in the West Bank face their own financial difficulties: the checkpoints, gates, closures, and the economic siege have affected attendance at Palestinian universities. “We are also trying to provide support to subsidise student tuition fees so that the universities can meet their obligations despite this major challenge,” he said.Governance of aidHe appealed to the international community to help safeguard education in Palestine.“The international community is aware of what is happening in the Gaza Strip,” he said, “and the ministry hopes that international institutions will assume their responsibility to support the educational process and provide support to Palestinian students and pupils and support the safety we seek to preserve the lives of our children, whether in the Gaza Strip, Jerusalem, or the West Bank.”The ministry has well-defined and transparent procedures for managing all types of aid, coordinated through the General Directorate of Grants and the Student Loan Fund, both trusted by universities, students, and donors alike, Barham said.His ministry has introduced new mechanisms of financial governance to ensure that international aid reaches students and universities directly, “away from any risks or suspicions of corruption”.“Trust is the foundation,” he said. “As long as we are talking about regulated mechanisms and full transparency, there is no room for concern. Student fees and aid classified as direct assistance are credited to students’ accounts under strict oversight, with reports based on clear standards and effective monitoring tools that enhance governance and credibility.”He said that no corruption allegations have ever been raised in this context.“We believe the issue is well within the scope of accountability and scrutiny. Our credibility is proven by the well-governed operation of the Student Loan Fund, which enjoys a high degree of trust and enables us to adopt mechanisms that further reinforce transparency,” Barham said.Referring to the impact of the war on research, Barham noted: “Scientific research requires budgets, and today, under the economic siege, we cannot even pay salaries … Therefore, we can say that scientific research is progressing, but not with the momentum we wish for, due to the siege, the scarcity of funds, and the lack of necessary laboratories and workshops.”Despite these constraints, Barham said that the ministry continues to support international collaboration: “We always focus on providing opportunities for researchers to establish scientific partnerships and work in universities abroad and then return home to utilise the results of their research in training and teaching students within Palestine.”Perseverance despite lossBarham said he felt deep admiration for Palestinian educators and students, commending their perseverance in the face of immense loss and hardship.“I extend my utmost respect and appreciation to the Palestinian educator, whether in the university or the school. This educator has carried the trust amidst very significant challenges,” he noted.He praised professors as central figures in shaping both education and national identity.“University professors are like parents to our students, treating them as their own children, working to intellectually and systematically prepare their minds,” he said. “They are shaping competencies capable of shouldering responsibility and building the nation’s institutions,” Barham said.Barham dismissed any doubts about universities’ ability to remain independent, stressing that Palestinian universities are already autonomous institutions.“Universities have their own institutional identity,” he said. “Through the Higher Education Council and the boards of trustees, we are confident that universities are capable of maintaining their autonomy. I say ‘maintaining’ because this is a long-established tradition built over decades,” Barham added.“Our universities have always been committed to neutrality and professionalism. They provide academic services with objectivity, free from political influence,” he said.He said that the ministry’s work is guided by a comprehensive assessment conducted by the Accreditation and Quality Assurance Commission to define priorities and design evidence-based interventions.Barham concluded by emphasising his ministry’s ongoing commitment: “Ensuring the continuity of higher education in Gaza – supporting its students, faculty, facilities, and academic programmes – has been our core mission for the past two years, and we remain determined to do everything possible within our means,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Scholars appeal to regional body over professor’s axing
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Scholars appeal to regional body over professor’s axingGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksASIA-INDIA-SRI LANKARegional university: A good idea that failed, says former VPINDIA500 scholars write letters after four member suspensionsINDIAAnger over suspension of faculty following student protestsINDIAStudent unrest escalates at flagship regional universityINDIAStudents, professors seek investigation of violenceINDIAVC of university stormed by police demands inquiryInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyINDIA-ASIAScholars appeal to regional body over professor’s axingShuriah Niazi14 November 2025TweetOver 300 lecturers, professors and researchers from universities around the world have written to the South Asian Association for Regional Cooperation (SAARC), asking it to step in regarding what they called the “unjust and punitive dismissal” of Dr Snehashish Bhattacharya, an associate professor of economics at the South Asian University (SAU) in New Delhi, India.In their letter to SAARC Secretary-General Md Golam Sarwar, the scholars urged the regional organisation, members of which include Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan, and Sri Lanka, to quash the dismissal. They called on the New Delhi-based university to fully endorse its founding principles of dialogue, intellectual freedom, and democratic governance.The signatories include prominent figures such as Ha-Joon Chang, SOAS University of London; Jayati Ghosh at the University of Massachusetts Amherst; Prabhat Patnaik and Amit Bhaduri at Jawaharlal Nehru University in New Delhi; Partha Chatterjee at Columbia University in the United States; and Rathin Roy, former member of the Indian Prime Minister’s Economic Advisory Council.In their letter dated 5 November, the scholars said sacking Bhattacharya threatened the university's reputation as a leading educational centre in the region. The university’s degrees are recognised in all the SAARC member countries.The signatories said SAU had seen “an exodus of international faculty members and repeated attempts to censor students and staff”, which are contrary to its vision of a world-class institution emerging out of a collaborative effort in South Asia.Opposition to police involvementBhattacharya was sacked on 11 September 2025, with retrospective effect fromJune 2023, when he was suspended along with three other faculty members. The confirmation of his dismissal followed his opposition to the university administration’s decision to involve policeduring peaceful student protests in 2022.These protests sought fair representation in gender sensitisation and anti-harassment committees and demanded a rollback of stipend cuts.However, instead of addressing students’ concerns, the university allegedly resorted to suspensions, expulsions and police action, creating an atmosphere of intimidation, according to students and academics.The scholars’ letter noted Bhattacharya and three other faculty members were suspended in June 2023, and their salaries cut to 25%, actions that were not “legally supported”, they said.Bhattacharya’s final dismissal happened after he refused to write a “letter of regret” that the administration had reportedly demanded as a prerequisite for reinstatement.The signatories describe this act as “punitive and coercive”, aimed at silencing a faculty member who stood up for democratic dialogue and fairness on campus.Decline in academic freedomThe scholars also pointed to a decline in academic freedom at SAU in recent years, which went against the university’s mission to promote a liberal and humane education and weakened its role in fostering intellectual cooperation among SAARC countries.Symbol of regional cooperationFounded in 2010 as a collaborative effort among the eight SAARC member countries, SAU was intended to serve as a symbol of regional cooperation in education, science, and social sciences. Its founding charter highlights the importance of liberal education, critical thinking, and collaboration among South Asian scholars.Amid growing concern about the state of academic freedom at the university, one SAU faculty member, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the university’s founding ideals of regional cooperation and intellectual exchange had been hollowed out by “internal compromises and bureaucratic control”.“Three hundred-plus signatories is no small number. But I’m not optimistic about the outcome. You can get [Nobel Laureate] Amartya Sen or anyone else to write, but if the people in power don’t value intellectual voices, it won’t matter. But still such gestures of solidarity are necessary,” he toldUniversity World News, acknowledging that the SAU administration has no idea what “regional cooperation” entailed.Pointing to one example, he said: “If they did, they would have acted long ago to help Afghan students. For years, students from Afghanistan haven’t been able to come here. When you’re dealing with a non-democratic regime, that’s exactly when you should educate students from there – not shut them out. But since the Indian government doesn’t recognise Taliban-issued passports, we refuse to admit them.”He added: “Regional cooperation, as seen by the [Indian] Ministry of External Affairs [which oversees the university], is about diplomacy. For universities, it should mean intellectual and cultural exchange. Universities can’t function like ministries. If they start doing that, they lose their mandate.”No resistanceIf SAU had truly succeeded in its vision for cooperation, it could have been a model for the world, he noted. “Unfortunately, that vision has withered away. What remains now is only the shell of that dream.”The academic said: “Universities still have some autonomy if they choose to exercise it.” However, he noted current SAU President Professor KK Aggarwal is closely aligned with a right-wing political organisation.After he became president of the university, “if all faculties had stood united, they couldn’t have acted against everyone. Instead, one or two spoke up – and they were thrown out. The rest stayed silent.“We must also look inwards. We didn’t resist enough. We failed to check authoritarian tendencies within our own institution.”The academic also agreed that some departments at SAU had become passive or compromised. For example, SAU’s Sociology Department is “practically a graveyard, the International Affairs Department is the most compromised, and the Economics Department has gone cold”.University World Newswas unable to reach the SAU administration for comment.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: EU welcomes Swiss researchers back into the Horizon fold
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: EU welcomes Swiss researchers back into the Horizon foldGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsSwiss researchers are welcomed back into Horizon Europe, marking a renewed era of collaboration, funding access and scientific partnership. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksEUROPEHorizon Europe and Erasmus+ win in EU budget proposalsEUROPE-UNITED KINGDOMUK rejoins EU’s Horizon Europe programme: Terms agreedEUROPE-SWITZERLANDSwiss universities urge action after Horizon Europe blowEUROPE-UNITED KINGDOMBrexit and universities – The last pieces of the puzzleInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultySWITZERLAND-EUROPEEU welcomes Swiss researchers back into the Horizon foldNic Mitchell14 November 2025TweetAfter four years out in the cold, Swiss researchers and innovators were welcomed back into the Horizon Europe fold at a signing ceremony in Bern on 10 November 2025.The deal enables Switzerland to rejoin the European Union’s flagship €93.5 billion (US$108.9 billion) 2021-27 research and innovation programme retrospectively, from 1 January 2025, and puts Swiss researchers and organisations on the same footing as those in EU member states.Swiss researchers can now lead consortia, receive direct EU funding, and access all “thematic pillars and instruments” of the programmes, said anannouncementon the deal issued by the European Commission.The agreement, signed between Guy Parmelin, Swiss federal councillor and head of the Federal Department of Economic Affairs, Education and Research, and Ekaterina Zaharieva, European commissioner for startups, research and innovation, ends four years out in the cold after a breakdown in negotiations over Switzerland’s bid to achieve full association status in Horizon Europe in 2021, asUniversity World Newsreported at the time.The United Kingdom also found itself outside the EU’s flagship scientific research scheme when it was excluded from the scheme for three years due to a post-Brexit fallout with the EU over the Northern Ireland Protocol. That period of isolation ended when the UK’s then (Conservative) prime minister Rishi Sunak concluded abespoke dealwith the EU in September 2023.Warmly welcomedSwiss universities and researchers are clearly delighted that pen has finally been put to paper, ending their isolation from Horizon Europe.Professor (Dr) Michael O Hengartner, president of the ETH Board, toldUniversity World News: “We warmly welcome Switzerland’s association in the EU programmes, which will strengthen European and international scientific cooperation.”The ETH domain is Switzerland’s leading higher education and research system with two universities (ETH Zurich and the Swiss Federal Institute of Technology in Lausanne) and four research institutes.Hengartner said: “Participation in the EU’s programmes for research, innovation and education is a key factor in ensuring the competitiveness of our institutions.”However, as with the UK’s deal with the EU over rejoining Horizon Europe, the agreement between the Swiss and the EU only runs until the end of the current Horizon 2021 to 2027 programme – although the Swiss, like the British, will be able to influence the direction and framework of future EU programmes coming into effect from 2028.Swiss-EU bilateral relationsWhile the ETH Board welcomed Switzerland's (re)association with the EU programmes, they pointed out that association with future EU programmes from 2028 onwards will only be possible if the Bilaterals III package is supported by the Swiss parliament and the Swiss people.TheBilaterals III packageis a set of agreements aimed at modernising and strengthening Switzerland's relationship with the EU, including issues around migration and labour mobility and allowing Switzerland access to the EU’s single market without full membership of the EU.However, despite some uncertainty about the long-term future of Swiss-EU bilateral relations, the Swiss Federal Department of Economic Affairs, Education and Research (EAER) said in astatementthat the agreement to rejoin the current Horizon Europe is very welcome, as it enables Switzerland to be associated with Horizon Europe, the Euratom and the Digital Europe programmes with retroactive effect from 1 January 2025.The federal government added that Switzerland’s association with the ITER (International Thermonuclear Experimental Reactor, a global collaboration to build the world's largest fusion machine in France) research infrastructure is planned from 2026.Erasmus+ deal on the cardsAnd the deal opens up the prospect of Switzerland rejoining the Erasmus+ mobility programme from 2027. “The Federal Council will request the funds necessary to finance Erasmus+ from Parliament as part of the Switzerland-EU package,” said the federal government statement.Professor Kurt Deketelaere, secretary-general of the League of European Research Universities (LERU), toldUniversity World Newsthe agreement between the Swiss and EU means that “two research and innovation superpowers are joining forces once again”.He said part of the reason it took so long to reach agreement is that EU law is complicated as it relates to non-EU member countries and because “Swiss people take matters like this seriously.“But finally we are where we need to be,” he added.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs atwww.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Swedish researchers are positive about China ties – Survey
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Swedish researchers are positive about China ties – SurveyGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSWEDENUniversity bosses call for more collaboration with ChinaSWEDENAcademics wary over centralised approach to security risksCHINA-UNITED STATESGeopolitical tensions dim prospects for US-China exchangesCHINAHow China is bidding for global intellectual dominanceCHINA-UNITED STATESIs the world splitting into opposing science ‘blocs’?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredSWEDENSwedish researchers are positive about China ties – SurveyJan Petter Myklebust13 November 2025TweetDespite increasing geopolitical tension between China and some Western democracies, Swedish researchers have a generally positive view on collaboration with China, which is now Sweden’s fourth largest research partner as measured by co-publications, according to the findings of a study to be published this week by two analysts at the Swedish National China Centre, a part of the Swedish Institute of International Affairs (UI).Analysts Erik Mo Welin and Gustav Sundqvist argue that considering China’s increasing importance as a science nation combined with its authoritarian system and politicised research environment, more China-specific knowledge initiatives in higher education institutions are necessary. Topics could include more in-depth knowledge of the relationship between China’s universities and the Communist Party, and China’s use of transnational repression, they suggest.They say that their study reveals that many researchers are “deeply frustrated” with the way that security measures with regard to China are implemented at the higher education institutions. This indicates a significant gap between security and academic discourses at institutions, which new administrative roles with broader competence in security, geopolitics and internationalisation of research could help to bridge.Growth in co-publicationsAccording to thepaper, based on a study conducted by Welin and Sundqvist, data from the scientific database Web of Science (which covers mostly research in STEM fields) shows that the total number of co-publications involving both a researcher with an affiliation in Sweden and a researcher with an affiliation in China has increased from 194 publications in 2001 to around 5,088 in 2024, and bibliometric data shows that co-publications have continued to increase during the last five years.The paper notes that although the increase in Sino-Swedish research collaboration “is showing signs of slowing down, the data does not suggest a similar trajectory to that of the United States, whose research collaboration with China has decreased in recent years”.It goes on to state: “While formal collaborations such as agreements with the China Scholarship Council (CSC) have been reduced or cancelled, the publication data indicates that many collaborations are still proceeding as usual.”The study indicates that researchers that collaborate with China regard collaboration with China as important in their respective fields. An online survey with Swedish researchers, mostly from STEM fields, conducted for the study showed that almost all (89%) respondents felt that their collaboration with Chinese partners had worked well. Eighty-eight per cent of respondents also either fully agreed or partly agreed that collaboration between the two countries was valuable in their field.The paper, titled “Swedish research collaborations with China still increasing, but politically more difficult”, was produced with funding from the Swedish Psychological Defence Agency (MPF) and was a collaboration between the Swedish National China Centre and Mälardalen University.It is part of a larger research project exploring political challenges in Sino-Swedish research collaborations in light of rising geopolitical tensions and investigates the recent development in Sino-Swedish co-publication, how Swedish scholars perceive the current trajectory of Sino-Swedish research, as well as how political challenges have affected such collaborations.China’s authoritarian influence on Sino-Swedish research relations will be discussed in a forthcoming report, Wellin and Sundqvist said.China’s rise in research strengthIn their latest paper, the analysts take as their point of departure China’s rise as a global scientific superpower, a rise that has been unprecedented in both speed and scale. By 2017, they say, China had surpassed the United States in the annual number of published research articles, and in recent years, various indicators suggest that China is closing the gap – or even surpassing the United States – in research quality across multiple fields.This growth was confirmed in a recent article inUniversitetsavisaconcerning an Australian Strategic Policy Institute report which showed that Chinese research leads in 57 out of 64 key technologies for the future, while the United States leads in the remaining seven.According toUniversiteitsavisa, in 2003, China was the world leader in only four technological research areas and the US led in over 50.“In the space of two decades, this picture has been turned upside down,” it notes before quoting ASPI project manager Bethany Allen saying: “We are moving towards a situation where Chinese universities are responsible for leading research. In many areas, we are already there.”According to Welin and Sundqvist, as China’s scientific capacity has expanded, so has its significance as a partner in international research. In 2022, China was the United States’ largest research collaborator and the second most important partner (after the United States) for the United Kingdom between 2018 and 2023.But the geopolitical tension between an authoritarian China and Western democracies now causes constant problems for developing sound research collaborations, they argue.And the escalating trade disputes between the United States and China – beginning during the first Trump administration – alongside Russia’s full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, have made research collaboration between China and Western countries increasingly complex.While China remains the US’ largest research partner, collaboration between the United States and China has significantly declined since 2018, they say.Swedish collaborationTurning to Sweden, a small but innovation-driven liberal democracy with a strong and internationalised research sector – 71% of all Swedish papers in 2019 were international co-publications – the analysts seek to examine the growth in research collaboration between Sweden and China and the experience of Swedish researchers in spite of strained geopolitics, particularly in the 2020s.Welin and Sundqvist’s research shows that when limiting the scope of analysis to bilateral collaborations (collaborations involving only researchers with affiliations in Sweden and China), which gives a better view of the extent of collaborations where researchers from both countries play a central part, one can discern a sharp increase in co-publications: from 119 in 2001 to 1,663 in 2024.Swedish-Chinese cooperation mainly takes place in the natural sciences, medicine and technology. Social sciences and humanities (a total of 72 categories in the Web of Science) accounted for only about 5% of all collaborations in 2024.“It is worth noting that co-publications between Sweden and China more than tripled during the period 2012 to 2024. Even if one looks only at bilateral cooperation, an almost threefold increase in cooperation can be perceived.“As mentioned earlier, there has been a decrease in cooperation between China and the United States in recent years. In contrast, although one can see indications of the increase of cooperation slowing down, no signs of a decrease in cooperation between Sweden and China can be discerned from the available data,” they state.Frustration at politicisationThe study also reveals a frustration among scholars that research collaboration with China is becoming increasingly politicised. More than half (57%) of participants believed that political obstacles had led to downsizing of research collaboration with China, despite the fact that no decrease in the number of co-publications could be detected.The analysts note in a summary of their findings that the survey’s free text answers “revealed a frustration with how security policies were implemented at their universities, which by one respondent was described as a ‘witch hunt’ aimed at collaborations with China”.Examples of poor implementation of security policies include “potentially discriminatory recruitment practices and the indiscriminate cancellation of research collaborations with universities known for close military ties – commonly referred to as the ‘Seven Sons of National Defense’ on security grounds, without consideration of the specific research area”, they note in the summary.Several respondents also expressed regret that Swedish universities were no longer able to accept students from the China Scholarship Council (CSC).Welin and Sundqvist note that although most concerns identified were related to the perceived over-politicisation of collaboration with China in Sweden, the study also uncovered smaller but not insignificant concerns about political problems stemming from the Chinese side.This is reflected in the fact that 34% either partly or fully agreed that scholars cooperating between Sweden and China face the risk of unjust surveillance by Chinese authorities, and 16% said their safety might be negatively affected when they travel to China. Thirty-nine per cent also thought that expressing their political views may harm cooperation with their Chinese partners.Access to research infrastructureAsked byUniversity World Newswhether survey participants showed signs of being caught in a dilemma over whether to collaborate with Chinese colleagues or be left outside the arena of scientific advances, Welin said: “Our study did not reveal any such specific dilemmas.It is, however, likely that such dilemmas are common considering China’s increased importance as a research partner. Almost 9 out of 10 of the scientists that participated in the survey thought that cooperating with China was valuable in their field of research, and we know from previous studies that Swedish scientists value access to research infrastructure in China.“In the survey there were also concerns that not cooperating with China would undermine the research leadership of Sweden. Several of the scientists we talked to in our project also thought that concerns of knowledge transfer to China were misguided in many cases because China is now ahead of Sweden in multiple research areas.”Acknowledging China’s place in global research, Professor Dan Banik at the Centre for Global Sustainability, University of Oslo, toldUniversity World News: “China today hosts some of the most dynamic academic environments tackling issues of global development and sustainability. We cannot solve global problems in isolation. Indeed, we need open, responsible, and reciprocal collaboration that builds bridges between the best minds, wherever they are.”Shift to individual networksMats Benner, a professor in science policy studies at Lund University’s School of Economics and Management and past member of the Swedish government’s Research Advisory Board (2009 to 2010, 2015 to 2016), and author of “China: A global academic hegemony in the making”, toldUniversity World Newsthe Welin and Sundqvist paper indicated “some interesting tendencies”, for example, a reduction in formalised collaboration, whereas collaboration based on individual partnerships and networks continues to be of importance.“The frustration with the heavy-handed management of collaboration on both sides (but not least the Swedish one) was also an important finding,” he said.Erik Forsberg, the Swedish Foundation for International Cooperation in Research and Higher Education (STINT) representative in China and ASEAN, said the report by Welin and Sundqvist added “much-needed nuance” to the debate on collaboration between researchers in Sweden and China.“Their findings show that such collaborations are overwhelmingly positive and, crucially, demonstrate that partnerships with Chinese researchers strengthen Swedish science. This stands in contrast to a common public narrative suggesting that cooperation with researchers in China undermines Swedish innovation and competitiveness – a claim that sits uneasily with the well-documented reality that China has become a world leader in research and technology.”Forsberg said while most respondents in the study reported positive and productive collaborations, the survey also highlighted challenges that underscored the importance of responsible internationalisation as a framework for engagement.“Notably, many of the obstacles cited by researchers stem not from research partners or authorities in China but from Swedish institutional or governmental policies – including risk-averse or poorly implemented security measures.“This finding is highly relevant for Nordic universities more broadly: it reminds us that ensuring integrity and openness in international research is not the task of individual researchers alone, but a shared responsibility that must also involve university leadership, policymakers and the security community,” he stated.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 瑞典研究人員對中國的合作持正面態度。根據瑞典國家中國中心的最新研究，儘管地緣政治緊張加劇，瑞典與中國的研究合作仍在增長，2024年的共同出版數量達到5,088篇，顯示出持續的學術交流。研究顯示，89%的瑞典學者認為與中國的合作對其研究領域至關重要。然而，超過一半的受訪者對於安全措施的實施表示不滿，並擔心政治因素影響合作。此研究強調了在當前政治背景下，加強對中國的了解和合作的重要性。

Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackUGANDANew research chair at Makerere signals a funding shiftClemence Manyukwe13 November 2025TweetA new research chair at Uganda’s Makerere University, which is part of the Africa-wide OR Tambo Africa Research Chairs Initiative (ORTARChI), is funded by home-grown funds as part of comprehensive efforts by the country’s science community to address gaps that opened up after a shift in and the loss of donor income.The Uganda chair, seeking to address critical challenges such as climate change, public health and food security on the continent, waslaunchedduring an annual gathering of ORTARChI’s representatives at the end of October at Makerere University.ORTARChI, a collaborative research funding initiative led by South Africa’s National Research Foundation (NRF) in partnership with the country’s Department of Science, Technology and Innovation (DSTI), the Oliver and Adelaide Tambo Foundation and Canada’s International Development Research Centre (IDRC) started in December 2018 to enhance research and innovation capacity by establishing 10 research chairs across Africa.Chair for infectious and neurological diseasesProfessor David Bisagaya Meya holds the newly established Uganda research chair that will focus on infectious and neurological diseases.Meyais an associate professor of medicine at Makerere University’s College of Health Sciences and an adjunct associate professor at the University of Minnesota in the United States, who has been involved in clinical research of central nervous system infections and complications, with a focus on HIV Immune Reconstitution Inflammatory Syndrome.The chair held by Meya, who has also led epidemiological and transnational research studies as well as randomised clinical trials in the prevention and treatment ofcryptococcal meningitis, is the 10th in Africa following the others in Botswana, Burkina Faso, Ghana, Mozambique, Tanzania, Zambia and Uganda.Finding alternative funding sourcesDuring the Uganda chair launch, Dr Maxwell Otim, the country’s National Council for Science and Technology (UNCST) deputy executive secretary, told the annual gathering that Uganda was experimenting with domestic financing solutions to support its research chair.“The funding landscape has really changed significantly and now we are looking towards ensuring that, domestically, we strengthen the funding mechanism and I would say that the contribution from UNCST towards the ORTARChI chair is actually being funded by Ugandan scientists. This money comes from the research registration and clearance fees [which scientists have to pay if they conduct research] and part of it we re-circulate back to strengthening our research ecosystem.“But, of course, we shall have to engage more intensively with our policymakers and also ensure that we raise more revenue to strengthen this – also from Makerere University’s side.“There is the national research and innovation fund, [and some of the] funds can be portioned towards the Oliver Tambo Africa Research Chairs Initiative. It’s still an experiment, but an experiment that I’m confident will be scaled up and will contribute significantly to our growth and innovation,” Otim said.‘Every partner brings something to the table’Dorothy Ngila, the director of knowledge and institutional networks at South Africa’s NRF, said ORTARChI was never meant to be a charity project, but a strategic architecture that asks every partner to bring something to the table.“The IDRC, NRF, DSTI, anchor the investment, the Oliver and Adelaide Tambo Foundation keep the memory of Oliver Tambo alive. The sister granting councils of the NRF across the seven countries [with chairs] commit co-funding and oversight in this programme.“The universities guarantee the salaries of shareholders and the infrastructure to support the shareholders, and the shareholders, themselves, leverage resources and networks to multiply what we begin with,” she explained.“It is, in essence, a programme that says Africa has what it takes to build excellence, but only if we do it in community and, in doing, so it has shown that, even with limited means, we can produce world-class research and researchers, from postdoctoral fellows to professors that are rooted in African realities, yet globally competitive.”Ngila said that, before this programme, the NRF primarily funded programmes within South Africa’s borders by dispersing government allocations, but the research chair initiative required it to reimagine its identity, not only as a South African agency, but a continental partner.“Colleagues, the path before us is not without difficulty. Many of our countries this year are feeling the sting of funding withdrawals from traditional partners. Uganda, like several others, [including] our council in South Africa, has seen first-hand how fragile externally driven systems can be,” she added.Science and policymakingDr Monica Musenero, the Uganda science, technology and innovation minister, said there was a need to take African science to the policy table as it was taking a back seat when it comes to policy discussions.Musenero said science was currently keeping a respectful distance [from policy discussions] where professors and researchers were very highly respected in their small enclaves. Researchers must engage policymakers, simplifying their results for the latter to appreciate the return on investment from research.The minister said working in silos will not take Africa far, hence collaboration on the continent is necessary to produce useful materials.“Africa has excellent labs, publications, presentations at international conferences without protecting intellectual property, which translates to professors retiring on pension as opposed to royalties from … the Western world through registration and sale of patents,” she added.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Study shows role for robust governance in research impact
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Study shows role for robust governance in research impactGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsEffective governance acts as the conduit through which research can drive economic and social progress. Image generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksMIDDLE EAST-TURKIYEArab-Turkish partnership for dual degrees, research hubsEGYPTEgypt’s satellite launch to advance climate researchMIDDLE EAST-GERMANYNew DAAD programme supports HE reform in the MENA regionMIDDLE EAST-NORTH AFRICAIt is imperative to build open science capabilities in MENAMIDDLE EAST-NORTH AFRICAIran leads the way to revive MENA commitment to knowledgeMIDDLE EAST-NORTH AFRICAMobility in MENA – Two steps forward, one step backInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMIDDLE EAST-NORTH AFRICAStudy shows role for robust governance in research impactWagdy Sawahel11 November 2025TweetPolicy-makers in the Middle East and North Africa (MENA) region should prioritise a dual strategy of enhancing the quality of research in specific scientific fields and simultaneously strengthening institutional frameworks – particularly government effectiveness, transparent governance and the rule of law – to transform knowledge into sustainable economic growth and social progress.This was the main message of astudytitled “Research output and economic growth: Is there any role for institutions?” published inScientometricson 25 October.The study indicated that examining the real effect of scientific output on economic development is highly important, especially for developing countries. As the study noted: “Channelling funds to finance research may have an important opportunity cost, since it could have been easily channelled towards the course of poverty reduction intervention.”Additionally, the majority of empirical research on the connection between research production and economic growth was primarily focused on developed countries, according to the study.Given the recent rise in government spending on research and development in developing countries, the study pointed out that examining the impact of academic research output on economic growth will enable policy-makers to evaluate the return of capital invested in research activities and the extent to which research endeavours succeed in advancing society.“To the best of the author’s knowledge, no other study has examined the impact of research output (quantity and quality) on economic growth separately for each of the 27 research areas in the MENA region, which consists of a group of developing countries,” the study stated.“This is important, since it may give more insights to decision makers in these countries, to choose where to invest their scarce resources to realise higher levels of economic growth,” the study indicated.Therefore, the study examined the economic impact of quantity (number of publications per year) and quality (the number of citations of research output) in all 27 research areas presented in the Scopus database for 15 MENA countries for the period from 2000 to 2017.While the quantity of research output was measured by the number of publications per year, and the quality was measured by the number of citations, one of the worldwide governance indicators which seems most relevant to research production, namely: voice and accountability; government effectiveness; and rule of law, was used to measure institutional quality as a key determinant of economic growth.Key findingsSpeaking toUniversity World News, the author of the study, Professor Chadi Azmeh at the International University for Science and Technology in Syria, said: “The study aims to deepen the understanding of the mechanisms through which research activity influences economic development, with a particular focus on institutional factors.”Azmeh added: “The study uncovers a nuanced relationship between research output and economic growth within the MENA region, revealing that both the quantity and quality of research can exert negative direct effects on growth when considered in isolation.”But crucially, the report also shows that institutional quality – especially government efficacy and the rule of law – acts as a crucial moderator, turning these connections into positive, mutually reinforcing dynamics.In essence, strong institutions and excellent research work together to promote long-term economic growth rather than just being correlated.“These results underscore that investments in research must be complemented by robust institutional reforms to realise their full potential,” Azmeh stated.“They challenge simplistic notions that merely increasing research output guarantees growth, highlighting instead that the societal and political context – governance, legal frameworks, and institutional integrity – are indispensable for translating knowledge into tangible economic benefits,” Azmeh pointed out.“This insight is especially critical for policy design in regions where institutional weaknesses often hinder development efforts,” Azmeh stressed.“This research underscores the vital importance of viewing research and institutional development as interdependent pillars of sustainable growth,” Azmeh said.He noted that policy-makers in the MENA region should prioritise a dual strategy: simultaneously strengthening institutional frameworks and improving the quality of research.Azmeh suggested that specifically, promoting transparent governance, the rule of law, and government effectiveness would amplify the positive impacts of research endeavours.“Building a conducive environment for innovation, backed by effective institutions, is fundamental for transforming scientific output into sustainable economic growth and social progress. For the MENA region, embracing a holistic, long-term vision – where science, governance, and socio-economic policies evolve hand-in-hand – is the way forward,” Azmeh noted.“Only through concerted, context-sensitive efforts can the region unlock its full innovation potential and achieve equitable, resilient development,” he added.Besides opting to concentrate efforts to improve the quality of research, especially in biochemistry, genetics and molecular biology, engineering, mathematics, physics and astronomy, and psychology, to realise the positive impact on economic growth, policy-makers should allocate resources to build new laboratories in universities and research centres and encourage researchers to publish in prestigious international journals, according to Azmeh.Implementation challenges and solutionsAccording to Azmeh, entrenched governance deficiencies, political instability, corruption, limited research capacity, and resistance to institutional reforms are major hurdles.Azmeh noted that “competing priorities, resource constraints, and societal scepticism towards reforms may impede progress.”According to Azmeh, disjointed efforts and lack of coordination could further weaken policy impact.“Addressing these obstacles requires a phased, inclusive approach – combining incremental institutional reforms with strategic investments in research capacity building,” Azmeh emphasised.“Promoting transparency, accountability, and civil society engagement will be essential to build trust and sustain reforms,” Azmeh said.Additionally, Azmeh found that “aligning research and innovation policies with broader socio-economic development goals will help embed reforms into the fabric of national agendas”.Transforming knowledge into economic benefitsProfessor Hamed Ead, who is based in the Faculty of Science, Cairo University, and is the former cultural counsellor at the Egyptian Embassy in Morocco, toldUniversity World News: “The study’s core message offers a crucial framework for policy-makers.“Scientific research alone cannot drive economic growth in MENA countries without strong institutional foundations.”The study presents a comprehensive strategy for converting knowledge generation into concrete economic advantages by conducting high-quality research in targeted areas and implementing institutional reforms.“This necessitates strategic investments in modern laboratories and technology, alongside incentives for researchers to publish in top-tier international journals,” he noted.Research excellenceProfessor Lokman Meho, based at the American University of Beirut in Lebanon, toldUniversity World News: “This is a timely study, which makes an important point: more research output doesn't automatically lead to development. Strong, transparent institutions are what turn knowledge into progress.“My Research Integrity Risk Indexfindingsecho this.”Meho is the developer of RI² – the world’s first empirically grounded, composite metric designed to identify and profile institutional-level risks to research integrity.“In several fast-rising universities across MENA, research output grew by hundreds of per cent. Yet, first and corresponding authorship declined, and retractions rose, showing how weak governance can reward quantity over quality,” said Meho, who is the author of a 2025studytitled “Gaming the Metrics? Bibliometric Anomalies and the Integrity Crisis in Global University Rankings”.“Scientific productivity divorced from institutional quality and integrity produces fragile gains,” Meho said.“Sustainable economic and social returns emerge only when research excellence is embedded in high-quality systems that reward accountability, transparency, innovation, and responsible knowledge production,” Meho said.Adopting clear long-term objectivesThe study highlights the importance of having a set of policy interventions designed to enhance the research and innovation ecosystem in line with national priorities and long-term planning, instead of responding to noisy or external factors, according to Angel Calderon, director of strategic insights at RMIT University (Melbourne), Australia’s largest university, who spoke withUniversity World News.“While it is important to focus on the dual purpose of enhancing the quality of research endeavours and strengthening institutional frameworks, it also requires ongoing attention, maintaining clarity of the long-term objectives and clear lines of accountability,” Calderon said.“There is often a tendency to switch strategies midcourse instead of staying the course. It pays to take a long-term view … To put it simply, research quality is built over time and requires having a clear understanding of direction and leadership. Research impact and reach are also built over time,” Calderon said.“I also believe it is important to focus on building the research workforce by supporting a greater number of doctoral students and fostering collaboration between peers and across the globe.”Combined approach for socio-economic developmentDr Marwan Al-Raeei, assistant professor and researcher at Damascus University in Syria, toldUniversity World News: “By utilising advanced statistical methods and focusing on the MENA region, the study highlights that effective institutions – characterised by government effectiveness and the rule of law – are crucial for maximising the benefits of research on economic development.”Al-Raeei, who is the lead author of an October 2024comparative studyon the influence of open access on research output in MENA countries, said the study suggests that “merely increasing research output is insufficient; it must be complemented by robust institutional frameworks to foster sustained economic growth”.“Policy-makers in MENA countries are, thus, encouraged to prioritise improvements in both research quality and institutional effectiveness, reinforcing the idea that research plays a pivotal role in driving economic and social development,” Al-Raeei pointed out.“MySageopen study andAzmeh’s studysuggest a synergistic relationship: improving institutional quality can enhance the benefits of research output, while promoting open access publishing can increase the visibility and impact of that research.”“This combined approach can lead to a more robust research ecosystem in MENA countries, ultimately fostering greater economic and social development,” he said. all for measuring how research is used for improving people's livesHow to measure impactHayfa Jafar, director of institutional effectiveness at American University in Iraq-Baghdad, toldUniversity World Newsthe Azmeh study raises an important point about the link between research and economic development, but she did not fully agree with how the relationship was measured.“Counting publications or citations doesn't always show how research is actually improving people's lives,” Jafar said.“What matters more, I think, is how research is used – for example, in shaping public policies, supporting industries, or addressing issues like poverty, education, and the environment.”She said in MENA many universities focus on publishing in indexed journals, but it is not always clear whether such studies have a real impact on society.“I believe we need to look more at the use of research, not just the number of studies. This may require more qualitative work and stronger links between universities, government, and industry,” Jafar said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSOUTH KOREAEnglish-track degrees fuel rise in foreign student numbersYumi Jeung11 November 2025TweetA burgeoning number of degrees taught in English at universities in South Korea has fuelled strong growth in foreign student numbers in recent years, part of a deliberate government strategy that includes the streamlining of admissions and visa procedures for international students.English-track programmes at South Korean universities are now offered in 1,024 departments across 73 universities, according to the Education Ministry’s latest data released on 25 September. This compares to 882 English-track departments recorded in April 2022 by the Korean Educational Development Institute (KEDI), which represents a 16% increase in just three years.As of April 2025, the number of foreign students enrolled in Korean universities surpassed 250,000 for the first time, reaching 253,400 – a 21.3% increase from 209,000 the previous year, according to the ministry. The government’s Study Korea 300K initiativeaims to attract 300,000 international studentsand position South Korea among the world’s top 10 study destinations by 2027.A ministry official toldUniversity World Newsthat reaching the 300,000 mark by 2027 “should not be difficult at the current pace”.The expansion of all-English degree tracks reflects the influence of the Study Korea 300K Project, launched in 2022 as part of a national strategy to boost global education competitiveness and attract top international talent.Information on English-track degreesAs part of the biennial review of its “Plan to Enhance the Educational Competitiveness of International Students”, initiated in August 2023, the ministry in September announced an overhaul of the nationalStudy in Koreaportal, increasing the amount and accuracy of information on English-taught degree programmes.An increasing number of universities have created degree programmes exclusively for international students. Under the Study Korea 300K Project, many aimed to teach more than half of their courses in English by 2030. Some have offered English-taught courses for some time, while others are now scaling up pilot departments in response to rising demand.Korea’s prestigious Yonsei University stands out as a long-running success story.As of 2025, it offers over 960 English-taught courses, including graduate-level programmes such as its Global Master of Business Administration (MBA).According to the 2024 Korean university rankings, published by Korean-language mediaJoongAng Ilbo, Yonsei’s undergraduate international student body includes 99 nationalities — the most diverse international student body in Korea — followed by Korea University with 93 nationalities represented, Hankuk University of Foreign Studies with 74, and Hanyang University with 73. All of these universities have longstanding international studies departments.Tapping Korean cultural exportsMore recently, Korea University launched a new Global Entertainment Department exclusively for international students, aiming to tap the huge regional and global popularity among young people of Korean films, television series, music and other cultural exports.The programme offers courses on Korea’s entertainment industries – K-pop, webtoons, film and e-sports – combining business and production perspectives.“It’s a major that drew strong interest from the start,” a university official toldUniversity World News. “Applications were high, and we’re committed to building its professional capacity.” This could enhance their graduates’ employment in some of these fields.The university’s Office of International Affairs held around 30 recruitment fairs in countries such as China, Japan, Canada and Thailand, working with local schools, education agencies and Korean language institutes.At Gachon University, a private university in Seongnam, two existing departments – International Liberal Arts and Korean Studies – already cater exclusively to international students.At the beginning of the 2025 and 2026 semester, the university will formally launch a new College of International Studies, integrating the two departments and adding six new international-only majors: business administration, media communication, tourism management, psychology, Korean language education, and computer engineering.“We’ve systematised international student management to improve educational efficiency,” a Gachon representative said, pointing to a range of support services including scholarships, academic support and assistance with immigration and residence issues. “All eight majors are popular among our international cohort.”Sookmyung Women’s University, a private university in Seoul, opened its Hallyu International College solely for international students in March 2024. Hallyu refers to modern Korean culture popular abroad.First-year students undergo intensive Korean language study before specialising in one of three tracks: global Korea, which focuses on Korean politics, society and culture; digital communication, which covers public relations, communication and business analytics; or international business, which includes marketing and AI-driven business practices.Seoul Women’s University launched its Global Commerce Department at the beginning of the 2025 academic year in March. It is also open to international students, with a focus on international trade, business and the entertainment sector.Students specialise from their second year, with two options: global business covering human resources, accounting and marketing, or K-content media which covers storytelling, video editing and digital advertising.“Though it’s currently a single department, it has strong potential to evolve into a college-level division as new majors are added,” a university official said.Despite eye-catching new programmes related to the Korean entertainment Industry, 60% of foreign students in the country are enrolled in science and engineering programmes, according to the ministry’s September statistics.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

韓國英語授課學位推動外國學生人數激增 根據韓國教育部最新數據，韓國大學的英語授課學位數量已增至1,024個，涵蓋73所大學，與2022年相比增長了16%。截至2025年4月，在韓國的大學外國學生人數首次突破25萬，達到253,400人，比前一年增長21.3%。這一增長是政府“Study Korea 300K”計劃的直接結果，該計劃旨在到2027年吸引30萬國際學生，並提升韓國在全球教育的競爭力。隨著越來越多大學推出針對國際學生的專業，韓國正努力成為世界前十大留學目的地之一。
