https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: German researchers study avian flu impact in Antarctica
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: German researchers study avian flu impact in AntarcticaGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsPenguins in Antarctica at serious risk of H5N1 bird flu. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksDENMARKNew strategy to strengthen research, HE in the ArcticAUSTRALIAAustralian sky scanner for US Antarctic baseSOUTH AFRICAAntarctic Legacy Project takes shapeBELGIUMResearchers harness 40,000 computers to fight bird fluInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALGerman researchers study avian flu impact in AntarcticaMichael Gardner11 November 2025TweetA team of researchers from the University of Jena, in eastern Germany, is revisiting a region in the Antarctic this month to study the impact avian flu is having there. Signs of the region being hit by the disease were already detected earlier this year.Friedrich Schiller University Jena has been monitoring birdlife in the Antarctic since 1983, when the institution’s Polar and Bird Ecology Group was set up to perform long-term observations of various bird species in the Fildes Peninsula of King George Island in Antarctica. The project is supported by Germany’s “Umweltbundesamt” (UBA – German Environment Agency).For some years now, the research teams involved in the project have included students from the University of Jena Biology Department’s masters programme Evolution, Ecology and Systematics. They support activities such as recording stocks of species but also gathering waste at local level for two to three months, during which they cover up to 15 kilometres a day on foot.The two masters programme students taking part in the expedition earlier this year conducted monitoring at the Uruguayan research stationBase Científica Antártica Artigas(General Artigas Station).Volumes of waste such as burnt plastic, packaging or waste from fisheries proved to be high for a region that is virtually uninhabited. Also, an unusually large number of animals were found which had succumbed within days or, at most, weeks before they were discovered.‘Highly contagious’The highly contagious H5N1 variant of avian influenza had already been detected on the sub-Antarctic island of South Georgia in October 2023 and, in February 2024, in the Antarctic itself, on James Ross Island.Samples taken by the Jena research team and examined by researchers from Germany’s Alfred Wegener Institute and Chilean scientists confirmed the team’s suspicion that the disease had indeed reached the region they were working in.“The area we are monitoring is very small and rich in birdlife. Furthermore, the Arctic birds typically breed in colonies, so there is a very high risk of infection.“And if a population has a very high mortality rate, it may completely collapse,” explains Markus Bernhardt-Römermann of Jena’s Institute of Biodiversity, Ecology and Evolution (IBEE).Bernhardt-Römermann pointed out that penguin populations are under particular threat, because as a rule, they huddle close together to protect themselves from the cold. H5N1 is then spread via saliva, like in human flu.H5N1 already caused huge numbers of deaths among mammals such as seals, sea lions and sea elephants in coastal areas in Chile, Peru and Argentina in 2023, and since the virus can also infect humans, special protective clothing was essential for the research team.“Cases of humans being infected following direct contact with infected birds have also been recorded,” noted Christina Braun, a professor of ecology in IBEE’s Polar and Bird Ecology group. “So the researchers have to be very careful and see to it that they are protected.”Braun, who has already taken part in 15 research expeditions to the Antarctic, noted that Antarctic birds are also strongly affected by climate change and that there have been marked shifts in species composition.For instance, while hundreds of Cape petrels were breeding in the region only a few years ago, the research team didn’t find a single breeding pair this season. On the other hand, giant petrels formerly preferring sub-Antarctic regions are now taking advantage of the new conditions in the Arctic.“We don’t know whether the birds are frequenting other areas to breed or whether they are actually disappearing – comparable long-term survey data from other research teams isn’t available,” Braun noted. “That’s precisely why continuous projects like ours are so important.”Julia Engelhardt is one of the two students from the Biology Department’s masters programme Evolution, Ecology and Systematics, who supported the research team in the Antarctic last winter.“Activities here on King George Island give us students the opportunity to carefully gather data and samples from animals which have had hardly any contact with humans,” Engelhardt explains. “And through international cooperation, we learn more about effective communication and coordination in research teams.”This time, a total of four students are taking part in the research activities in the Antarctic. The team will be working there from November to February.Michael Gardner can be contacted via: michael.gardner@uw-news.comReceive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 德國研究人員在南極研究禽流感影響 本月，一支來自德國耶拿大學的研究團隊前往南極，調查禽流感對當地鳥類的影響。這一地區早在2023年就已發現禽流感跡象。該項目自1983年以來持續監測南極的鳥類，並得到德國環境局的支持。參與的碩士生不僅記錄物種數量，還進行環境清理工作。研究人員發現，禽流感H5N1變種可能威脅到聚集在一起的企鵝等鳥類，且這一地區的環境變化也影響了鳥類的繁殖。持續的研究對於理解和應對這些變化至關重要。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Visa reductions threaten the Canadian research ecosphere
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Visa reductions threaten the Canadian research ecosphereGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCANADAInternational enrolment plummets 36% in Atlantic CanadaCANADASteep drop in overseas students leaves universities reelingCANADA2024 international study permit rates set to fall by 45%CANADAData shows 35% drop in interest in studying in CanadaCANADAInternational student visa targets look likely to be missedCANADAStudent visa cuts: Ontario’s colleges come out swingingCANADANew visa rules tackle international student housingInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyCANADAVisa reductions threaten the Canadian research ecosphereNathan M Greenfield07 November 2025TweetThe federal budget tabled on 4 November in Canada’s parliamentallocatedCA$1.7 billion (US$1.2 billion) to help universities to recruit more than 1,000 international scholars of note and CA$400 million to help improve research infrastructure.At the same time, the budget drastically reduced the number of new international student visas by 49% for 2026 to 155,000 and reduces it still further to 150,000 for 2026 and 2027, respectively.Since 63% of international students are enrolled in the sciences or STEM fields – many of whom staff university labs or are research assistants – cuts of this magnitude could threaten the government’s plan to grow Canada’s research ecosphere.“One of the things that stands out for higher education in the budget is that the Canadian government has signalled very clearly that the days of international students making up for public funding are over, with another 50% cut in international students, [and] international student visas coming our way.“Earlier in the decade, Canada issued 700,000 study permits in a year. Beginning last year, those permits were capped, and the target next year was about 306,000.“The budget further reduced that target to 150,000. That’s bad news for those institutions that are heavily reliant on international student revenues,” said David Robinson, executive director of the Canadian Association of University Teachers.AsreportedbyUniversity World News, starting in early 2024, the federal government began putting the brakes on international student visas – partially in response to the realisation that thousands of students were using Canada’s lax rules as a backdoor means of immigration.Additionally, international students, who numbered close to a million (out of Canada’s population of 41 million), were considered as contributing to the country’s housing affordability crisis; because universities did not have enough on-campus housing for them, hundreds of thousands of these students were renting apartments, distorting local housing markets across the country.‘The best and brightest’The CA$1.7 billion International Talent Attraction Strategy and Action Plan (ITASAP) to help universities recruit leading professors is the largest such investment in Canadian history.Finance Minister François-Philippe Champagne announced the plan, saying in the House of Commons: “Mr Speaker, Canada is the best place to live, and top talent from around the world wants to come here because they see opportunities and possibilities to contribute to cutting-edge research.“With our flagship International Talent Attraction Strategy of CA$1.7 billion, we will make sure that the best and brightest continue to choose Canada to innovate, invent and grow our industry.”The lion’s share of the ITAPfundingis CA$1 billion over 13 years for the National Sciences and Engineering Research Council, the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council and the Canadian Institutes of Health Research (known collectively as the Tri-Councils) for a new Accelerated Research Chairs Initiative.Four hundred million dollars will be spent over the next seven years to improve research infrastructure in Canadian universities. As well, the government is investing CA$133.6 million over three years to help international PhD students and postdoctoral students relocate to Canada and CA$120 million over 12 years to assist universities in hiring international assistant professors.In the leadup to Champagne’s budget, Ottawa was abuzz with rumours of major cuts, and there have been many, most notably the plan to reduce the size of thefederal workforceby 40,000 jobs by March 2029, with 16,000 of these job cuts being this fiscal year, and CA$2.3 billion beingcutfrom programmes to secure First Nations’ access to clean water and CA$1 billion for Arctic infrastructure.Accordingly, Canada’s universities can be excused for feeling they dodged a bullet; a 2% cut to the TriCouncils’ budget was deemed good news, explained Vincent Poitout, theUniversité de Montréal’s (UdeM) vice-rector of Research and Innovation toldUniversity World News.“The call from the universities and various scientific leaders in Canada that we’ve been trying to push since last spring for the attracting of international talents seems to have been heard,” he said, referring to the new money to attract new professors and the money for the Research Chair initiative.Poitout also drew attention to the new accelerated pathway for holders of American H1-B visas to move to Canada, noting that because of it, “we will be able to strengthen Canada’s research capacity, taking advantage, I hate to say, that way, of the unfortunate geopolitical situation [in the United States] is overall good news.”(In September, US President Donald J Trump changed the rules for theH1-B visa, the type held by MA and PhD students working primarily in the STEM fields; included in the change was the imposition of a $100,000 fee for companies wishing to obtain an H1-B visa for a foreign student worker.)While details on how the new monies will be doled out are not yet available, Poitout felt secure in saying that the new infrastructure money “will allow us to complement the start-up packages that will be offered to international scientists with infrastructure funds”.Additionally, he said that there is some research money and development funds embedded in the Bureau of Research Engineering and Advanced Leadership in Innovation and Science and the Department of National Defense.“There will be some money for research in dual-use [military/civilian] technologies and some research areas prioritised by the government in the context of defence spending, including aerospace, cyber security, AI, biodefence and quantum computing,” said Poitout.Consequences of cutsThe additional cuts to international students will be extremely hard on a number of universities and colleges that rely on international students’ tuition and fees.“I think a lot of smaller, regional institutions that are heavily reliant on international students are going to be nervous about what lies ahead,” said Robinson.“Institutions [such as] Cape Breton University (CBU, Sydney, Nova Scotia) or St. Mary’s University in Halifax have already taken a big hit [in loss of international student tuition fees] and have already gone through programme closures and various enrolment caps, hiring freezes and even layoffs,” Robinson noted.AsUniversity World Newsreportedrecently, many of these institutions are in Atlantic Canada.The Association of Atlantic Universities’2025-2026 Preliminary Summary of Enrolmentsreport a 36% decline in international student numbers enrolled by the region’s 16 universities but a decline of 41.5% (4,200) in the number of undergraduate international students coming to Nova Scotia’s 10 universities. (This decline is partially due to the fact that for this academic year, Canada issued90,000 fewer visasthan had been planned for.)CBU is the most affected institution in the region by the drastic decline of international students that has already occurred. In both percentage terms (60%) and absolute terms (3,226 students), CBU has suffered the greatest declines, meaning that today the university’s enrolment stands at 4,226 compared with last year’s enrolment of 7,452, a decline of 43%.To plug its budget shortfall, since last spring, CBU has cut about 112 positions, half of which were full-time professors who were given access to a voluntary retirement incentive programme.Both Poitout and Robinson raised concerns about the impact of the loss of graduate students who serve as teaching assistants and, even more importantly given the money the budget allocates to improving Canada’s research capabilities, are the backbone of research: research assistants.Robinson also noted that the plan to limit international studentsdoes not sit logicallybeside the new accelerated pathway for H1-B visa holders to immigrate to Canada “to strengthen Canada's innovation ecosystem, address labour shortages and attract top talent in healthcare, research, advanced industries and other key sectors.”“This is not a fully thought-through policy,” said Robinson. “We need to look at how the different things interact. I don't think that was actually thought through very well in terms of the budget. To the government's defence, I suppose they could say that post-secondary education is not really our bailiwick. It's not within our constitutional ambit. [Education is, as Canadians put it, “a provincial responsibility”.]“But at the same time, I think these kinds of programmes can create distortions in the system,” he says, noting that the budget did not contain much about funding homegrown Canadian talent and ensuring that Canada’s domestic research assistants are not pushed out by students recruited from the United States.International scholar opportunitiesAccording to Poitout, UdeM is one of many universities that is ready to make firm offers to international scholars, mainly, but not only, working in American universities, that the university has been speaking with for a number of months.“We’re sitting on a bunch of dossiers that have already been vetted internally, some of them, and we had more excellent applications. So we're ready to go as soon as we know the parameters of this new talent initiative.“We’ve been waiting for the announcement for months now. There was very good momentum for this at the end of the beginning of summer. Then it was sort of silent during the summer, because I think that was when I heard the decision was made not to make any announcements for spending money before the budget was tabled.“We are already in the starting blocks. From what I’ve heard, the programme is going to unfold very quickly, so we better be ready,” said Poitout.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Equity in access and success must be a global HE priority
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Equity in access and success must be a global HE priorityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsAs higher education expands globally, equity depends on keeping growth balanced with the cultivation of knowledge and opportunity. Image generated by SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH AFRICATime to move from ‘integrated thinking’ to ‘integrated doing’AFRICAAfrica unlikely to achieve some SDGs by 2030 – UN reportGLOBALUnlocking tomorrow’s scientific solutions today for SDGsGLOBALGlobal higher education meets to shape post-2030 SDGs agendaAFRICAMeeting SDGs requires degree quality, but can Africa do this?SOUTH AFRICASiyaphumelela student success initiative to expand in 2024GLOBAL‘Rethink, reinvent and adapt’, WAHED tells higher educationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALEquity in access and success must be a global HE priorityDesmond Thompson07 November 2025TweetInequality in access to and success in higher education persists in every country – regardless of wealth, geography or system design. That is the stark conclusion ofDrawing the Global Access Map 2: Understanding higher education inequality across the world– the most comprehensive study yet to detail disparities in participation and attainment globally.Produced by the World Access to Higher Education Network (WAHEN) with support from theLumina Foundation, thereportwas launched on 28 October 2025 at the World Access to Higher Education Day (WAHED) Conference, hosted in hybrid format at the University of the Western Cape (UWC) in South Africa and online.Drawing on data from more than 200 countries and territories, the study finds that although tertiary enrolment continues to expand worldwide, students from low-income, first-generation and marginalised backgrounds remain persistently under-represented.Gender equality also remains elusive. In most countries, more women than men enrol, yet completion rates for women still lag. Students from higher-income families, meanwhile, are more than twice as likely to enter university.The study calls for stronger global cooperation and better data sharing to reduce these inequalities. It urges governments to set national targets for access and attainment by socioeconomic status (SES) and recommends that the post-2030 Sustainable Development Goals explicitly include both gender and socioeconomic equity in higher education.The lead author, Professor Graeme Atherton, Director of WAHEN and founder of WAHED, spoke at the launch. He also authored the 2016 report, which laid the groundwork for the new study.“Bringing this report back now is crucial,” he said. “We live in an era when truth is increasingly questioned. Without evidence-based knowledge, we’re left with conjecture and opinion, which undermines the very principle of equitable access.”A worldwide conversationWorld Access to Higher Education Day (WAHED) began in 2018 as an initiative of the United Kingdom’s National Education Opportunities Network (NEON) – a global day of action to address inequalities in higher education access and success.Between 2018 and 2022, it supported nearly 200 partner events across more than 100 countries, engaging over 1,000 organisations and working with the United Nations Educational, Scientific and Cultural Organization (UNESCO), the Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD), theWorld Bankand theInternational Association of Universities.A three-year pause followed, not simply because of COVID-19, but to allow for the creation of a more sustainable global framework. During that period, NEON and its partners establishedWAHEN, formally launched in 2024, to extend WAHED’s mission year-round through capacity-building, collaboration, research and advocacy. This year’s WAHED marks the initiative’s return as a key annual activity under the WAHEN umbrella.A global movementWAHEN is governed by a board that includes representatives from the World Bank, UNESCO’s International Institute for Higher Education in Latin America and the Caribbean (IESALC), theAsia-Europe Foundation, the Saville Foundation, the Kresge Foundation, theUniversity of Oxford, and the University of California,Berkeley. It operates out ofRuskin College, which is part of the University of West London (UWL).“In these straitened times, I think we are needed more than ever. I’m sure today will give us an even stronger collective voice in this somewhat disordered world,” Professor Peter John, vice-chancellor of UWL, said at the start of proceedings.Its 2025conferenceconvened vice-chancellors, policymakers, researchers and students from more than 20 countries. Over 35 research papers were on the agenda, while 21 partner events took place across the world – from Australia and Ireland to India, East Timor and Colombia.UWCprovided a fitting setting for the conference. Opening the event, Rector Professor Robert Balfour said: “Access and equity are central to our identity. Founded in 1960 within a divided, racially segregated system, UWC quickly transformed into an institution committed to the struggle against apartheid – and, ever since, to widening participation and advancing knowledge for the public good.“We view access as the beginning of a journey supported by innovation, engagement and, above all, care.”The conference session, ‘Pathways to Equity – Policy, Practice and Personal Journeys’, highlighted how institutions and organisations across the world are translating commitments to access and equity into concrete outcomes.Reforming systemsDelegates heard examples of how system-level reforms are achieving measurable change in participation and attainment.•Mauritius:Commissioner of the Higher Education Commission (HEC), Professor Romeela Mohee, outlined how the country is using micro-credentials to build flexible learning pathways and promote lifelong learning across Southern Africa. This exemplifies systemic reform in small and developing states, showing how flexible learning can enhance both access and employability.With support from UNESCO and theCommonwealth of Learning, the Commission has developed a national credit value transfer system, trained academic staff to create 88 open online courses, and piloted micro-credentials in fields such as information and communication technology, finance and agriculture.HEC also coordinatesPoMiSA(Potential of Micro-credentials in Southern Africa), a regional collaboration involving 18 institutions across seven countries, supported by the European Union (EU). The initiative aims to establish a regional framework for quality assurance, recognition and regulation of micro-credentials linked to industry needs.“Flexibility enhances both access and resilience, enabling students from varied backgrounds to enter, re-enter, or exit higher education at multiple points,” Mohee said.•Brazil:Affirmative action here has been policy-driven, using legislation to tackle racial and socioeconomic inequality.Erich Dietrich, professor of higher education and international education atNew York Universityand vice-provost for undergraduate education atNYU Abu Dhabi, traced how two decades of reform have reshaped who gains access to Brazil’s most selective universities – the federal institutions that are tuition-free, publicly funded and historically dominated by wealthier white students.The Federal Law of Quotas (Law No. 12,711/2012) reserves half of all federal university places for students who completed secondary education in public schools, with priority for those from low-income families and sub-quotas for Black, Brown and Indigenous students.“In Brazil, the word ‘quota’ – it’s not taboo as it is in the United States (US),” Dietrich said. “The system is transparent and quantifiable.”Research shows that students admitted under the quota programmes perform as well as, or slightly better than, their peers – challenging assumptions that inclusion comes at the expense of quality.The impact has been transformative – in 1992, only 18% of students in Brazilian universities were Black, Brown or Indigenous; by 2019, that figure had risen to 42%.•Australia:Dr Mary Teague of the University of New South Wales (UNSW) described how her institution’sGatewayprogramme is transforming access for students from under-represented communities.Operating across more than 400 schools, it combines outreach, mentoring, financial support and early conditional offers for students from low-income, regional and Indigenous backgrounds.Since its launch in 2020, Gateway has more than doubled enrolments from disadvantaged schools. Retention and success rates for these students now exceed national averages – 96% and 90%, respectively, among low-SES students, with similarly strong results for regional and Indigenous cohorts.Teague said UNSW’s model shows how locally tailored, evidence-based interventions can deliver both access and success. “Widening participation is not only about access. It’s about success, belonging and transformation.”Building a culture of careProfessor June Pym, education advisor to theSaville Foundation, emphasised the importance of collaboration, self-reflection and a “pedagogy of care” in advancing student success.Presenting examples from the University of Cape Town (UCT), she argued that real equity work depends as much on transforming institutional culture as on supporting individual students.She said sustainable transformation means “weaving multiple ways of being into daily operations – admin, communications, classrooms and ceremonies – rather than relying on specialists to fix systemic issues.” The aim, she added, is to build a genuine culture of care and culture of learning within universities, not just access at the point of entry.Investing in student successTheKresge Foundation, a private grantmaking and social investment organisation based in Detroit, focuses on expanding equity and opportunity across American cities. Its Education Programme works to increase post-secondary access and success for low-income, first-generation and under-represented students.“We want institutions to adapt to the students they admit, not force students to adapt to the institution. If you do that, you can make a real difference in student success,” Bill Moses, managing director of Kresge’s education programme, told delegates.The share of US adults holding a post-secondary credential has risen from 38% in 2008 to nearly 55% in 2023 – progress driven by coordinated interventions in affordability, advising, developmental mathematics and student support.South Africa is Kresge’s only international focus. Here, the foundation supports theSiyaphumelela(“We succeed”) network, an initiative to build data capacity and strengthen student success strategies across the system. Launched in 2014 with five institutions, it now includes 20 of the country’s 26 public universities.“We listen to students – through surveys, focus groups and participation on panels – to understand barriers and the conditions for success,” Moses said.From exile to opportunitySome of the conference’s most powerful moments came from students whose lives were transformed by access to higher education.Muleka Ilunga, originally from the Democratic Republic of Congo, and Ivan Hirwa, from Rwanda, fled to South Africa with their families as young children to escape conflict in their home countries. They are now postgraduate students at UCT and UWC, respectively.They got assistance from theDAFI scholarship programme, administered globally by the UN Refugee Agency (UNHCR), which has supported more than 24,000 refugee students across 54 countries since 1992. It provides scholarships for tuition, accommodation and living costs, as well as academic and psychosocial support.“Behind every refugee is a human being whose only misfortune was being born in the wrong place at the wrong time. Refugees are not defined by weakness or despair; they are defined by potential,” Ilunga said.“I stand here as proof of what happens when potential meets opportunity. Education changed my life, and through it I’ve seen how knowledge can restore hope where it was once lost,” Hirwa said.Frankie Randle of theUNHCRsaid: “It’s not just about degrees; it’s about enabling people to live with dignity, rebuild societies and contribute wherever they are – whether in exile, in their host country or when they return home.”The way forwardTogether, these stories – from systemic reforms to personal triumphs – reflected the conference’s theme of “Opportunity, Innovation and Lifelong Learning.”Across continents, the message from WAHED 2025 was clear – progress has been made, but the work of widening participation is far from complete.As the world looks beyond 2030, WAHEN is positioning equity as a defining measure of success – calling on governments, universities and partners to act collectively so that access and achievement become a right, not a privilege.As Balfour put it: “In a world shifting politically, economically and technologically, one truth remains: knowledge must serve all people, not only those enabled by it. Higher education institutions must be accountable not only for employability outcomes but for their public-good role.”Widening access and success in higher education relates to United Nations’ Sustainable Development Goal (SDG) 4 (Quality education and lifelong learning for all), SDG 5 (Achieve gender equality and empower all women and girls) and SDG 10 (Reduce inequality within and between countries).Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Students say university falls short of innovation promises
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Students say university falls short of innovation promisesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH KOREASouth Korea’s answer to Minerva University: The Taejae experimentUNITED STATESRevolutionary delivery meets traditional standardsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsSOUTH KOREAStudents say university falls short of innovation promisesYumi Jeung23 November 2025TweetFounded in 2023 with ambitions to become “Korea’s Minerva University”, Taejae University now finds itself in crisis. Students are leaving.According to three students who have left the university, of the 32 students who entered as the first cohort in September 2023, nine – nearly 30% – have withdrawn. Among the second intake, only 25 of the 42 admitted students actually enrolled in September 2024, and four of those have since dropped out. Some departing students say the university’s teaching methods fall far short of its innovation promises.In response to questions fromUniversity World Newsabout the withdrawals, a Taejae University representative acknowledged some students had left mid-programme, but argued that the reasons were varied.“As with many universities, some students struggled to adapt to an innovative educational model, while others left for military service, returned to their previous universities, orfor other personal reasons. It would be inaccurate to conclude that a single problem explains the decline in student numbers.”An ‘active learning’ approachWhen the institution was established, it championed an “active learning” approach – students would read materials in advance, engage in discussion, and work independently to find solutions. Taejae University promoted itself as rejecting the “traditional method of memorising predetermined problems and answers”, instead seeking to equip students not only with practical knowledge but with the capacity to solve unstructured, real-world problems.Positioning itself as a “21st-century university for the future”, Taejae adopted a September academic calendar – unusual in Korea, where most universities begin in March – and marketed itself as a global learning hub combining flexibility, interdisciplinary knowledge, and international experience.Under Korean law, Taejae University is registered as a cyber university, allowing students to enrol in other institutions simultaneously.Interest in enrolling was intense: Taejae’s inaugural class in 2023 drew a 13:1 acceptance ratio, and several successful applicants left top-tier institutions such as Seoul National University, Yonsei University, and Korea University (collectively known as “SKY”) to enrol.One of them was Kim Jeong-beom, the first student council president of Taejae University. Kim previously studied at Yonsei University and was in a pre-medical programme at the University of Melbourne at the time he enrolled at Taejae.Kim spoke toUniversity World Newson 29 September, saying: “We receive a huge amount of pre-reading material before classes. The volume and difficulty are considerable, but you can still participate fully in class – and even receive top grades – without reading them. Some lectures are just one-way information delivery, much like at other universities.”During his time at Taejae, Kim achieved a GPA of 4.3 out of 4.5 and twice ranked second in his cohort.Another student, who did not want to be named, said: “They promised to cultivate creative and problem-solving skills, yet the exam questions were four-choice multiple-choice taken directly from the textbook. I came from Yonsei University, but I kept questioning the quality of the teaching and the lectures. The pre-class materials often had no connection to what was taught, and the learning atmosphere suffered as a result.”Having spent a year at Taejae, this student has since returned to Yonsei. “Because I had experience in a conventional university, I could easily compare the quality of instruction,” they added.Another currently enrolled first-cohort student, who also asked not be named, toldUniversity World News,: “I agree with the philosophy of active learning, but I don’t yet know whether I am genuinely experiencing its benefits. I often feel that the connection between the pre-class learning materials and the actual lesson is weak. The classes are enjoyable, but there are clearly areas that still need improvement.”Active learning modelThe university’s active learning model was developed with input from Stephen M Kosslyn, a Harvard emeritus professor and former dean ofMinerva University, the groundbreaking private universityheadquartered in California. Kosslyn is also founder and CEO of Active Learning Sciences, a company that designs and implements active learning strategies in higher education and hybrid learning environments.At a Taejae education forum in Seoul on 15 October 2024, Kosslyn stated: “Active learning goes beyond rote memorisation. It enables students to internalise information and apply it to their lives and careers, fostering adaptability and problem-solving skills essential for navigating future uncertainty.”Elizabeth Callaghan, vice-president of Active Learning Sciences, also served as dean of Taejae’s School of Innovation Foundation from May 2022 to August 2024.A faculty member currently teaching at Taejae, speaking on condition of anonymity, toldUniversity World News: “The university is serious about upholding active learning. But given the diversity of nationalities and backgrounds among students, it’s difficult to maintain consistent quality across every class. I believe the university’s efforts will show results once things stabilise further.”Another staff member, who also asked not to be named, toldUniversity World News: “Because we are pioneering a new instructional model, it may have felt unfamiliar — or even unsettling — for some students. However, every member of staff is working to ensure that students can learn what they need. Those who have adapted well report very high satisfaction. Once Taejae graduates its first cohort, that will be the moment its true value becomes clear.”Quality assuranceTaejae University President Yom Jae-ho, a former president of Korea University, discussed the institution’s quality assurance system in an interview with Korean news platformUniversity News Networkon 15 September.“Our university does not evaluate faculty based on research output but on their teaching. Newly hired professors receive a full semester of active learning training. To teach a course, they must prepare a detailed class scenario at least three months in advance,” he said.“All lectures are recorded, and the Education Innovation Team – soon to be reorganised into a dedicated centre – analyses them. Using the online platform Engageli, we assess engagement levels and participation rates. Each student’s speaking time is visualised as a graph, and those with low participation receive individual guidance. Professors also conduct mid-term evaluations to ensure their classes are well-prepared, logically structured, and effectively received.”Despite these assurances, student sentiment remains cool. A current student who enrolled in September, speaking on condition of anonymity, toldUniversity World News: “I haven’t yet felt the innovation or effectiveness I expected from ‘active learning’. There are many discussions and a lot of preparatory material, but it still feels too early to say I’m learning meaningfully.”Speaking toUniversity World News, a staff member familiar with the matter, who also did not wish to be named, stressed that Taejae’s active learning model is still undergoing refinement and that Taejae President Yeom Jae-ho continues to meet with Kosslyn every two weeks to discuss improvements.“We are constantly gathering data on how long students speak in class, the level and quality of their participation, and how professors facilitate engagement, and we are continually adjusting based on that evidence.“In fact, students at Taejae have already achieved notable success internationally, including winning the CHI 2025 Student Design Competition and securing first place in a debate competition jointly hosted by Oxford and Stanford under the Oxford-Stanford-Taejae (OST) Fellowship programme in March 2025”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Government acts on bogus UAE colleges after complaints
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Government acts on bogus UAE colleges after complaintsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNEPALInternational universities eye Nepal’s high student mobilityNEPALCrackdown on foreign-affiliated colleges flouting rulesNEPALGovernment increases ‘study abroad levy’ on outbound studentsNEPALMinistry restores skills study abroad permit – with provisoInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNEPAL-UAEGovernment acts on bogus UAE colleges after complaintsBinod Ghimire22 November 2025TweetThe Nepal government has stopped issuing ‘no objection certificates’ (NOCs) to students departing for unaccredited academic institutions in the United Arab Emirates following complaints by Nepali students about colleges and universities in the UAE that appear to be accepting enrolments for degrees they are not qualified to deliver.“NOCs are being issued only for selected colleges and universities after thorough screening,” Birendra Jung Thapa, chief of the NOC department under the Ministry of Education, Science and Technology in Kathmandu, toldUniversity World News.The NOC is a requirement enabling Nepali students to send money overseas to foreign universities to pay their admission costs and tuition fees, along with the cost of food and lodging.The UAE, which was relatively unknown as an academic destination for Nepali students until a few years ago, has recently seen a large influx of Nepali youth. As many as 5,152 students from Nepal have received the NOC to study in the UAE since the beginning of fiscal year 2023 to 2024, according to government figures.Exploitation of loopholeAccording to the affected students, the UAE institutions in question are located in special economic areas, also called free zones, designated to attract foreign investment, in the cities of Dubai, Ajman and Sharjah.Institutions operating in such zones generally follow a different set of rules from those on the ‘mainland’.This ‘loophole’ allows such institutions to operate without proper oversight from the UAE’s education authorities, the students allege.Students have lodged complaints with the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs over the matter and have also filed fraud charges with police against the consultancies who recruited the students for the UAE institutions.A letter to the Nepal foreign ministry from the Nepali Consulate General in Dubai (written in Nepali), seen byUniversity World News, says it has reviewed the details of students studying in the UAE in November 2025.“The details and information presented by the students participating in the programme have shown the serious situation of educational fraud committed against Nepali students by educational institutions run by some entities in the UAE, especially in the free zones.“The consulate general received information that Nepali students have been cheated by 10 to 12 institutions in various free zones,” the letter states, according to an unofficial translation of the letter.Affected students say some of the UAE institutions showcase massive infrastructure, technology-laden classrooms, and reputed teaching staff on their websites and social media pages to attract students, but, in reality, are little more than a set of rooms.Some institutions, the students said, brand themselves as affiliates of universities in France, the United Kingdom and other European countries. They also make false promises to students that credits can be transferred to different universities in Europe after studying for one year in the UAE.Nepali student Manish Tiwari (23) said he enrolled at a Sharjah-based university college in late 2023 for a Bachelor in Hospitality Management (BHM). Days after arriving in the city, the college administration refused to allow him onto the college premises. Instead, he was asked to take online classes. After insisting repeatedly, he was called in for physical classes.Tiwari said early suspicions were confirmed after visiting the college. “It was depressing at the college where I was enrolled with very high expectations. It was housed in four rooms,” Tiwari toldUniversity World News. The institution has subsequently appeared on a list of institutions that are unaccredited by the UAE Commission for Academic Accreditation.Bishwajeet Kumar Yadav, a student from Nepal who was sent to pursue his PhD at the so-called British University College International in Ajman, said: “The training institutes have named themselves as universities and colleges, but they are not authorised to grant academic degrees.”Yadav returned to Nepal after finding the institution had no authority to grant a degree. A document issued by the Ajman Free Zone seen byUniversity World Newsstates that the college was given a training licence. However, it is not an academic institution with degree-granting authority.Cases against agentsAfter registering complaints at both the Ministry of Education and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs, the students also filed fraud cases with the police against the consultants who act as agents for the UAE institutions in Nepal. Students said they wanted them to be investigated for “misrepresentation [and] fraudulent advertising and [for] their financial ties with the UAE unaccredited institutions”.Students who gathered at a police station in Kathmandu to lay the charges toldUniversity World Newsthey were assured by the agents of getting part-time jobs and told their earnings would be enough to cover their living expenses and partially cover education costs. It was only after entering the academic institutions that the students realised they had been cheated.The Nepali students who spoke directly toUniversity World Newssay students typically pay between Rs750,000 (US$5,260) and Rs1.5 million (US$10,520) in consultancy fees to education agents, in addition to the living and travel costs.“Nepali consultancies, driven by greed, are exploiting hundreds of youths, taking away both their future and their money,” said Yadav, who blames both his consultant and the university for cheating him.After he challenged the consultant, Yadav’s fee was refunded, but he is among few known to have succeeded.While welcoming the Nepal government’s move to tighten up on the issuance of NOCs, a group of students, led by Yadav, is campaigning against the consultancies under the banner “Scan the Scammers Campaign”.Since last week, they have been regularly pressuring the authorities to take prompt action while alerting prospective students to the risks of being duped through statements and social media posts.Pushback from students in UAEStudents reported that in the UAE itself, dozens of students have intensified efforts, mainly at the personal level, to get their money back. A BHM student enrolled at the Central Academy of Management Studies in Sharjah, speaking on condition of anonymity, said the institution’s administration threatened to cancel his visa and send him back home if he raised questions about the authenticity of the course.The Central Academy of Management Studies is also named in the document from the Consulate General of Nepal in Dubai as one of those not accredited by the UAE’s Commission for Academic Accreditation.“Our morale to seek justice has increased after those who have returned to Nepal have [raised] their voices, compelling some action from the government side,” said a student from the academy, speaking anonymously. “I have asked the [college] administration either to guarantee the academic degree it had promised, which is not possible because it is registered as a training institute in a free zone, or to pay my money back.”Sagar Dhakal, advisor to Nepal Education Minister Mahabir Pun, said beyond suspending NOCs in certain cases, the ministry is investigating the matter more widely.The ministry is committed to acting in accordance with victims’ demands, he toldUniversity World News. Referring to the suspension of NOCs for some institutions on 14 November, he said: “NOCs have been tightened [up] after [the] finding that students were cheated.The ministry is now investigating the consultancies. It is also mobilising its diplomatic channels to see if the UAE-based institutions can come under some action,” Dhakal said, adding the ministry would “come out with some actions in a few weeks”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

尼泊爾政府對假冒阿聯酋大學採取行動 尼泊爾政府近日停止對前往阿聯酋未經認證學校的學生發放「無異議證明書」（NOC），此舉是因應學生對該國多所學校的投訴。這些機構在經濟特區內運營，避開了阿聯酋教育當局的監管，導致許多尼泊爾學生被誤導而註冊。據報導，自2023財政年度以來，已有5152名學生獲得NOC前往阿聯酋。尼泊爾政府正在加強對NOC發放的審查，同時調查相關的教育顧問，以保障學生的權益。學生們已經展開「掃描詐騙者運動」，希望提高對此問題的認識並爭取正義。

尼泊爾政府對假冒阿聯酋大學採取行動 尼泊爾政府近日停止對前往阿聯酋未經認證學校的學生發放「無異議證明書」（NOC），此舉是因應學生對該國多所學校的投訴。這些機構在經濟特區內運營，避開了阿聯酋教育當局的監管，導致許多尼泊爾學生被誤導而註冊。據報導，自2023財政年度以來，已有5152名學生獲得NOC前往阿聯酋。尼泊爾政府正在加強對NOC發放的審查，同時調查相關的教育顧問，以保障學生的權益。學生們已經展開「掃描詐騙者運動」，希望提高對此問題的認識並爭取正義。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Lund University crowned world’s best for sustainability
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Lund University crowned world’s best for sustainabilityGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsLund University in Sweden has topped the 2026 Quacquarelli Symonds World University Rankings: Sustainability. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGLOBALLund University crowned world’s best for sustainabilityUWN Reporter21 November 2025TweetLund University in Sweden has taken the top spot for the first time in theQuacquarelli Symonds World University Rankings: Sustainabilitysince it was established in 2023. The University of Toronto in Canada drops to second place after being the world’s best in 2024 and 2025, and University College London (UCL) in the United Kingdom climbs two positions to third overall.This year, some 2,000 universities are featured across 106 higher education systems, up from around 1,750 in the previous edition. Overall, the United States is most represented, with 240 universities, followed by China with 163, the UK with 109, India with 103 and France with 76.Three universities join the top 10, including the London School of Economics and Political Science (LSE), up from 39th to sixth, the University of New South Wales (UNSW Sydney), up from 12th to seventh – two below its 2023 position when it ranked fifth – and McGill University, up from 15th to ninth.The University of California, Berkeley (UCB), which was number one in the first iteration in 2023 and third last year, drops out of the top 10 for the first time. Meanwhile, the University of Melbourne drops from ninth to 16th and ETH Zurich slips from second to 11th.Leigh Kamolins, QS director of analytics and evaluation, and co-chair for the United Nations Higher Education Sustainability Initiative (HESI) Action Group on Rankings, Ratings and Assessments, said: “The publication of the QS World University Rankings: Sustainability 2026 arrives at a pivotal moment. Withonly 17% of the 169 global Sustainable Development Goals on track for delivery in 2030, the need for accelerated action on environmental and social challenges has never been clearer.”Sustainability is ‘no longer optional’He said for the 20th-anniversary edition of theQS World University Rankings, released in June, performance in the sustainability rankings was introduced as a new indicator influencing each institution’s overall position.“This addition was designed to send a clear message: sustainability excellence – across research, governance, education, operations, skills development, and social impact – is no longer optional but a core dimension of institutional strength,” Kamolins said.“The integration also directly reflects the priorities of the more than half a million prospective international students surveyed by QS. Some 76% of students we have surveyed indicate that an institution’s commitment to reducing their environmental impact is either extremely or very important to them.”Duncan Ross, also co-chair of HESI’s Action Group on Rankings, Ratings and Assessments, and former chief data officer forTimes Higher Education(THE), writing on LinkedIn, said it was impressive to see how the QS Sustainability ranking had grown. “It is clear that sustainability has become a key way of evaluating university impact and performance.”He pointed out the difference with theTHEImpact Rankings is that the QS ranking is more heavily biased towards research and reputation than theTHEranking, “so we would expect that it would lean more towards the results that we expect to see in a research-focused ranking”.But the biggest difference, he said, is the inclusion criteria. “THEadopt a mechanism that lets any (paying)…university participate if they provide data, but for QS you will be included if you are in one of their research-focused rankings (more or less). One result is that QS has a big US bias that you don’t see inTHE, with 236 US participants to 46.”Erik Renström, vice-chancellor of top-ranked Lund University, said: "Sustainability has long been a clear priority for the university, and the way we work is shaped by a Scandinavian tradition of trust, collaboration, and openness. I am incredibly proud of our employees, students, alumni, and the many organisations and companies we work with – this result is theirs as much as the university’s."At the same time, we remain humble, because we know that major challenges remain. Being number one raises the bar for us, and those challenges inspire us to keep pushing forward in an interdisciplinary and innovative way so that the knowledge we generate leads to real impact and lasting change in society."A total of eight countries see at least 10 institutions debut in the ranking this year, with Mainland China adding 49 debutants, followed by India with 26, France with 19 and Türkiye with 18. Germany (16 debutants), Spain (15), the UK (13) and the US (11) all have more than 10 entries.The UK leads the rankings, with more top 10 universities than any other country and nearly twice as many in the top 50 and top 100 as its closest competitor, the US. Canada, meanwhile, has the highest concentration of top 10 universities, with 7% in this tier.Belgium and Ireland each have two institutions in the top 50 – 20% of their entries, the highest concentration of any country. Singapore has the largest share of top-100 entries, with two of its four ranked universities in this tier.QS’methodologyfor the World University Rankings: Sustainability 2025 is based on university performance across three assessment categories: Environmental Impact (45% – aggregating three lenses: Environmental Sustainability, Environmental Education and Environmental Research); Social Impact (45% – aggregating five lenses: Equality, Knowledge Exchange, Impact of Education, Employability & Outcomes and Health & Wellbeing); and Governance (10% – Considering factors related to good governance: ethics, hiring practices, transparency, decision-making, etcetera.)EuropeThe 500+ European universities are, on average, the best performing across the nine lenses that the rankings measure.Lund University is ranked top for Equality and places second for Health and Wellbeing and Environmental Sustainability. The University of Vienna, which ranks 396th overall, places second in the Impact of Education lens, worth 7% of the overall score. The Netherlands has the best average score in the Social Impact lens, and Belgium has the second-best average result in the Environmental Impact lens.France has the most represented higher education system in continental Europe, with 77 ranked entries. Of these, 19 rank for the first time. Université Paris-Saclay is the only French university among the top 50, placing 45th, down six places, while Sorbonne University is 61st, up seven.German institutions feature 65 times in the ranking this year, with 16 universities debuting in 2026. A total of 12 improved overall this year. Three German universities are among the top 100, including Universität Hamburg, Rheinische Friedrich-Wilhelms-Universität Bonn and the University of Cologne, placing 46th, 57th and 88th, respectively.A total of 57 universities from Italy feature in the ranking, five of which are new. In all, 16 improved their ranking this year, and six retained their position from last year.Spain is the third most represented higher education system in continental Europe, with 15 new entries, bringing its total entries to 58. Among these, 16% (nine universities) improved their ranking this year, and two remain in the same position as last year.Among the Nordic countries specifically, Sweden and Finland perform exceptionally, with five and two universities in the global top 100, respectively. The University of Helsinki is the region’s second-best university (after Lund University), placing 22nd. Chalmers University of Technology climbs into the top 100, up 73 positions to 64th. Denmark’s highest-ranked university, the University of Southern Denmark (SDU), climbed from 110th to 107th.However, despite their success in the rankings, both Sweden and Norway record more drops than gains. Finland and Denmark both see as many universities rise as drop, while Iceland’s only ranked university, the University of Iceland, dropped from 749th to 890th.Health and Wellbeing marks an exceptional strength for Nordic universities, with four placing in the world’s top five in this indicator, including first- and second-placed University of Helsinki and Lund University. This suggests excellent on-campus health provisions and reflects strong research in related fields.UK & IrelandIreland sees two universities in the global top 50, with Trinity College Dublin rising to 29th and University College Dublin cementing its position among top performers, appearing at 47th.The UK is the most successful higher education system in the Sustainability Rankings, with more elite universities than any other country or territory. It has the highest number of institutions in the top 10, top 50, and top 200.It is particularly exceptional in Governance, with eight universities among the top 10, and Health and Wellbeing with nine, and Knowledge Exchange with five. It sees room for improvement in Environmental Research, which is the only lens in which no UK university is among the top 10.LSE and the University of Oxford enter the top 20 for the first time, climbing to sixth and 14th, respectively.After UCL, the University of Edinburgh is second in the UK in 2026 for a second consecutive year. It has also improved its overall standing, climbing to fourth from seventh last year.The third institution in the UK is LSE, which has risen to sixth place from 39th in 2025. Of the 52 UK universities that have improved this year, Keele University, UWE Bristol and Birkbeck, University of London are the most improved, climbing to 482nd, 232nd, and 451st, respectively.Overall, 17 UK universities feature in the top 50 – the same number as last year. However, two of these institutions are new to the top 50: the University of Oxford and the University of Leeds.Three UK universities enter the top 100, including the University of Bath, the University of Strathclyde and the University of Warwick. This means 30 UK universities feature in the top 100, up from 26 in the 2025 iteration.North AmericaOnly the UK has more entries in the top 10 than Canada’s three, while the UK is also the only country that has more universities in the top 50 than the United States, which has nine.The United States remains the most represented higher education system in the QS World University Rankings: Sustainability 2026. However, 38 universities improve their overall rank, while 168 drop, 23 remain stable and 11 are new. This gives the US one of the highest percentages of previously ranked universities to drop down the ranking.New York University (NYU) climbs up to 20th place this year, its best ever result, and Boston University rises to 40th position, entering the top 50 for the first time. Three US universities enter the top 100, including the University of Massachusetts Amherst in 86th, Washington University in St Louis, rising five places to 97th, and the University of Connecticut up to 99th.The United States remains home to several of the world’s strongest universities for environmental education, with UC Berkeley, Stanford, and MIT jointly holding the global top position.Yet the country’s scientific strength may face headwinds, according to a QS press release that references asurvey byNatureearlier this year which reported that of the 1,600 US-based scientists who responded, nearly 1,200 – about three-quarters – said they were considering leaving the country, often for posts in Europe or Canada.Despite Canada’s exceptional concentration of top-tier universities – the highest percentage of top 10 universities in the world (7%) – the country has experienced majority drops, with 51% of its previously ranked institutions dropping and 20% climbing.This is primarily due to the expansion of the rankings driving competition, including nine new Canadian institutions.However, Canada’s gains come primarily among its top-ranked institutions. Of its nine universities in the top 100, five have improved, including McGill University (ninth, +6), Western University (24th, +6), Université de Montréal (53rd, +12), Queen’s University at Kingston (59th, +34) and McMaster University (92nd, +1).AsiaSeoul National University in Korea becomes Asia’s top-performing university in this edition, overtaking last year’s leader, the University of Tokyo. Its success is driven by improvements in Employability and Outcomes, Equality, Environmental Sustainability and Environmental Research. With Korea University rising six places to enter the top 100 at 95th, this is the first year Korea has three universities in the top 100.China is the second most represented country in the rankings, with 163 universities featured. It has added more institutions than any other higher education system, with 49 new entries – nearly double that of its closest competitor, India, which adds 26.This growth reflects improved environmental, social and governance (ESG) strategies and greater data transparency and availability across Chinese universities. Although more institutions have dropped in rank than risen, China’s drop rate of -27% is moderate and aligns closely with the average among all systems experiencing declines in this highly competitive edition.According to a QS press release, China’s main challenges are in Social Impact, particularly Equality, Impact of Education and Health and Wellbeing, in which no universities place in the top 200. Knowledge exchange, however, is a strength for China, with five universities among the top 100 in this lens – the joint-highest in Asia, alongside South Korea.Singapore has two universities in the top 100, with the National University of Singapore (NUS) dropping six places to 69th and Nanyang Technological University, Singapore (NTU Singapore) falling to 99th. These universities excel in Environmental Research with each tied in first place in this indicator.Japan’s top 100 entry is The University of Tokyo at 48th, and National Taiwan University (NTU) is Taiwan’s only top 100 entry, appearing at 72nd. Mainland China’s best-performing institution is Fudan University at 140th, while the best university in Hong Kong is The University of Hong Kong at 137th.India’s top-performing institution, the Indian Institute of Technology Delhi (IITD), drops out of the top 200, placing 205th. IITs lead India domestically in the ranking, with all three of the country’s top-performing universities and seven of its top 10 belonging to this prestigious group.Thailand is among the most improved higher education systems in this year’s rankings. QS ranks 19 Thai universities, 13 of which rise, two drop and two remain stable, while two rank for the first time.This gives Thailand an overall improvement rank of 58%. The highest in the world among higher education systems with 10 or more ranked universities. Its highest-ranked institution, Chulalongkorn University, places 165th.OceaniaNew Zealand is once again one of the best-performing locations on the planet, with all of its eight universities ranked among the top 500. However, faced with heightened global competition, only one institution improved its rank this year. The University of Auckland is the highest-ranked university, placing 28th.In Australia, a quarter of the 38 ranked universities improve their rankings. One of those to improve is UNSW Sydney, which rises five places to re-enter the top 10 at 7th. In the inaugural ranking, UNSW placed fifth. Australian National University (ANU) has also seen significant improvements this year, rising into the top 20 to rank joint 16th.Australia performs exceptionally in Equality, with five universities among the top 10 in this indicator, more than any other country. The majority of Australia’s universities have climbed the table in this indicator, including four of its five top 10 placed institutions.Environmental research, on the other hand, marks a challenge for Australia, with no university among the top 200. University of Technology Sydney is the highest ranked, placing 205th.Latin AmericaWhile in the previous iteration of the ranking, four institutions across three countries in Latin America featured in the top 200, this year’s heightened competition has resulted in a shift. Universidade de São Paulo in Brazil is now the only institution from the region to rank among the top 200 at joint 90th.However, more than 50 of the 128 universities that ranked from Latin America and the Caribbean last year have improved in this edition. Universidade Federal do Rio de Janeiro has entered the top 300, rising 26 places, to rank 277th.The Federal University of Minas Gerais (UFMG), also in Brazil, has entered the top 500, ranking 316th.Ecuador and Peru are among the most improved higher education systems in this year’s rankings, with 60% of their previously ranked universities rising. Pontificia Universidad Catolica del Peru climbs 104 places to rank 411th, while Ecuador’s premier university, Universidad San Francisco de Quito USFQ, climbed 116 places to 504th.Ben Sowter, QS senior vice president, said Latin American higher education institutions were steadily embedding sustainability into governance, curricula, and campus operations – but the progress was uneven. “Nations like Brazil, Mexico, Chile, Colombia, and Argentina are gaining momentum.In Brazil and Mexico, universities frequently lead national sustainability-works and partnerships; in Chile and Colombia, innovation clusters and inclusive community initiatives are emerging; Argentina, meanwhile, is strengthening its environmental education legacy.“Nevertheless, Latin America’s higher-education contributions to global sustainability discourse are still marginal. The governance frameworks and institutional capacities needed for deep systemic transformation remain weak or fragmented, and regional disparities persist,” he said.Middle East & Western AsiaNine of the 10 top-performing institutions across Western Asia, the Middle East and North Africa improve their scores this year. All three of Bahrain’s three entries improve their rank, as do both of Qatar’s.Six of Lebanon’s seven entries improved their ranking, with the American University of Beirut (AUB) rising 29 places to enter the top 200 at 176th. Most entries from Saudi Arabia and the UAE also improved their ranking this year. King Abdulaziz University (KAU) rises 13 places to enter the top 200 at 194th. The United Arab Emirates University’s position of 318th is the institution’s best-ever result.In specific lenses, 72 universities across the Arab region improve in Social Impact (compared with 45 that drop), and 67 improve in Environmental Impact (compared with 51 that drop). Institutions perform less well in the Governance indicator, with a total of 79 dropping compared with the 39 that improve.Kamolins said universities across the Arab Region are making “outstanding improvements” in the rankings. “Governments across the region have made ambitious plans to shift their economies in the face of the climate crisis, making this ranking all the more important.”AfricaMore than 80 universities from 16 African countries appear in the ranking this year, 15 of which debut. While Egypt leads the continent with 29 ranked institutions (and three new debutants), South Africa is home to all four of Africa’s top-ranked universities. This includes the University of Cape Town, the only African institution in the top 100, ranked 59th this year – down from 45th last year.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Global|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
