Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackUNITED STATES-CHINASTINT analysis shows China tops US for research strengthJan Petter Myklebust21 November 2025TweetChina’s emergence as a dominant force in global science is not yet fully recognised in public discourse across Europe and North America, but it is nonetheless shaping the world of science and has far-reaching implications for future research cooperation and policy, according to analysis in a report by the Swedish Foundation for International Cooperation in Research and Higher Education (STINT).Authored by Dr Erik Forsberg, the STINT Representative in China and ASEAN since 2018, the reportInternationalisation Outlook No 4, 2025, presents a detailed picture of the world’s leading research universities which differs from mainstream rankings because it eschews looking at a broad range of indicators to focus purely on research strength.“In order to rank universities based solely on their research strength, we therefore need an alternative approach,” Forsberg said. “Rankings of the strongest research institutions need to be based as much on the quality as on the quantity of research done at the respective institutions.”By using data from the Centre for Science and Technology Studies (CWTS) Leiden Rankings, published by Leiden University in the Netherlands, he has been able to rank universities across six tables based on the number of highly cited publications they publish.Advances in scientific positionThe results illustrate the huge advances in China’s scientific position after the turn of the millennium and notably in the mathematics, computing and engineering fields.Forsberg toldUniversity World News: “Having worked in academia in China for the better part of the past two decades, I have witnessed first-hand the developments that have led to what many may find a surprising – even shocking – development.“I have found it increasingly concerning how little attention these changes have received in Europe and North America, where policy discussions about China’s research capacity often rest on outdated assumptions. This gap risks leading to misguided decisions built on a misperception of China’s actual scientific strength.“That is why I wrote this report: to provide a clearer picture by using metrics that isolate research impact alone. Mainstream rankings tend to obscure this dimension, yet when we look only at impact, the results reveal a very different – and far more consequential – global landscape.”In the report, Forsberg writes: “A decade ago, the scientific dominance of the United States and Europe seemed largely unquestioned. ThisOutlook... [shows] that a profound transformation has occurred during the past decade: China has emerged as a dominant force in science.”Agneta Bladh, former chair of the Swedish Research Council (2016 to 2022), who oversaw an investigation into Sweden’s increased internationalisation of universities and university colleges in 2018 to 2019, toldUniversity World News: “It is apparent that there is a huge shift in frontline research in the hard sciences.”Bladh said: “I think it is of utmost importance that STINT make us aware of this. Especially when much of the political thinking is to avoid collaboration with China and Chinese institutions. This influences universities and can be problematic in the long run.”Erik Mo Welin, an analyst at the Swedish National China Centre, which is part of the Swedish Institute of International Affairs, toldUniversity World News: “I think Forsberg’sOutlookoffers a timely and thorough analysis that highlights China’s remarkable advances in scientific research. Although this trend has been underway for some time, policymakers and higher education institutions are only now beginning to recognise its implications.”Data tables document shiftA series of tables in the report documents the shift in standing.The first table presents all leading universities worldwide according to the top 10% most cited publications across all scientific disciplines (CWTS Leiden Rankings) from 2009 to 2012 and compares this list to 2019 to 2020.The tables reveal a substantial change during the intervening years: while there were no Chinese universities among the global top 20 for all sciences in the period 2009 to 2012, 12 Chinese universities were in the top 20 10 years later, comprising four of the top five. These were Zhejiang University, Shanghai Jiaotong University, Tsinghua University and Huazhong University of Science and Technology.The second table displays leading universities according to the 10% most cited publications in biomedical and health sciences (CWTS) and compares 2009 to 2012 with 2019 to 2020.It finds that in biomedical and health sciences, the shift is less pronounced, with seven Chinese universities ranking in the top 20 in the period 2019 to 2022, the highest being Shanghai Jiaotong University at number 7. There were no Chinese universities on the list from 2009 to 2012, partly reflecting a more recent focus on funding in these disciplines in China.Table 3 compares leading universities worldwide by the top 10% most cited publications in mathematics and computer science (CWTS) in the periods 2009 to 2012 and 2019 to 2022, while Table 4 compares leading universities according to the top 10% most cited publications in physical sciences and engineering (CWTS) across the same two time periods.They show that in the fields of mathematics and computer science, as well as physical sciences and engineering, the number of Chinese universities among the global top 20 rose dramatically from five to 19 and from two to 19, respectively.From 2009 to 2012, Tsinghua University was number 5 and Shanghai Jiaotong University number 8 in mathematics and computer science, while from 2019 to 2022 Chinese universities held all 20 places except for Singapore’s Nanyang Technological University, which held number 13. The three highest-ranking universities were: the University of Electronic Science & Technology of China, Tsinghua University and Xidian University.From 2009 to 2012, Tsinghua University held rank number 6 on the list in physical science and engineering, and Zhejiang University held rank number 13. From 2019 to 2022 only Massachusetts Institute of Technology was a non-Chinese university of the 20 highest-ranked universities, with rank 19. The three highest-ranked universities were Tsinghua University, Shanghai Jiaotong University and Zhejiang University.Different story in social sciencesChinese universities remain absent from the top global rankings in the social sciences and humanities both from 2009 to 2012 and from 2019 to 2022, with comparatively limited funding and political and ideological constraints as contributing factors.Both the two sets of years are dominated by US and UK universities, with Canada having two universities on the 2009 to 2012 list (Toronto number 12 and the University of British Columbia number 18). From 2019 to 2022 Toronto held rank number 4, the University of Amsterdam rank 8 and Utrecht University rank 20, the three non-UK/US universities.Table 6, showing leading research universities worldwide ranked by publication output in high-impact journals (Nature index), 2014 and 2024, presents a comparison of the most recent Nature Index data with corresponding figures from a decade earlier (Nature Index, n.d.).While there are some variations, the results are consistent with those based on the CWTS University Ranking, as presented in tables 1 to 5.In the 2024 Nature Index ranking, four of the top five research institutions (with Harvard University as number 2) and 13 of the top 20 were Chinese, while in 2014 China only held the number one rank on the list: the Chinese Academy of Science.Aside from the different methods used by the CWTS University Ranking and the Nature Index, they also differ in that the CWTS only lists universities.By contrast, the Nature Index includes all research-conducting institutions, which is why the Chinese Academy of Sciences and the Max Planck Society, for instance, appear in the Nature Index rankings but not in the CWTS University Ranking.Forsberg then brings in a bibliographic analysis by the Australian Strategic Policy Institute (ASPI), which examined millions of scientific publications.TheASPI Critical Technology Trackerranks China as the leader in 57 of the 64 technologies it tracked in the period 2019-2023, while the United States leads in the remaining fields.China, according to ASPI, has established “a sometimes stunning lead in high-impact research across the majority of critical and emerging technology domains”. This is in sharp contrast to the period 2003-2007, when China led in only three of the 64 technologies, while the US led in 60.China now ‘overwhelming leader’“China and the United States have effectively switched places as the overwhelming leader in research,” ASPI isquoted as saying in one paper.The ASPI tracker also analyses the degree to which countries have secured a technology monopoly and defines “high monopoly risk” as a situation in which the leading country possesses eight or more of the top 10 institutions and has at least a threefold lead in high-impact research output compared to its next competitor.“Of the 64 technologies tracked, 24 were at high risk of a Chinese monopoly in 2024, up from 14 in 2023,” Forsberg wrote.Ole Petter Ottersen, professor emeritus of Oslo University and a former rector of both Oslo University and the Karolinska Institutet in Stockholm, who has been a spokesperson on research and higher education collaboration with the Global South, toldUniversity World News: “During my time at Karolinska Institutet, we faced the difficult decision to scale down collaboration with China and eventually terminate the agreement with the China Scholarship Council. This was not a step taken lightly.“The backdrop was a growing lack of transparency regarding conditions for Chinese students and researchers, combined with concerns about how knowledge might be misused in an increasingly authoritarian context.“These developments made it impossible to uphold our values of academic freedom and integrity while maintaining the agreement.”He said the dilemma remains acute.“Science thrives on openness and exchange, and the pandemic reminded us how essential global collaboration is – even with researchers in countries governed by authoritarian regimes. Cutting ties wholesale risks isolating science and weakening our collective ability to address global challenges.”Bladh said Sweden is dependent on both importing academic knowledge and collaborating with the best academic institutions in the world, as it has more than 2% of the world’s publications.“Even though Sweden has a very good academic standing, there is a necessity to collaborate in order to be at the forefront.”However, Ottersen recognised that new legislation in China and political interference “create real risks, particularly around dual-use technologies and surveillance applications”.He said the way forward requires nuanced, case-by-case assessments rather than blanket bans.“European institutions need competence in geopolitics and ethics to navigate these waters, avoiding both naivety and excessive fear.”Welin said the study shows the importance of developing deeper knowledge about how to engage and collaborate with China in scientific research.“As Forsberg emphasises, it is imperative to maintain a clear-eyed view of the global scientific landscape, and the analysis suggests that understanding China’s scientific system – including its relationship with the party-state – will be one of the most important tasks in the near future.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New HE partnership a ‘milestone’ towards all-island system
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New HE partnership a ‘milestone’ towards all-island systemGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksIRELANDMicroCreds drive flexible learning in innovative IrelandUNITED KINGDOMCall for more student places, to avert ‘brain drain’IRELANDIrish government split on increase in HE tuition feesIRELANDGovernment launches bid to lure US researchers to IrelandIRELANDUnified tertiary education and research system a ‘game changer’Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsIRELANDNew HE partnership a ‘milestone’ towards all-island systemJohn Walshe21 November 2025TweetA cross-border partnership involving Queen’s University Belfast in Northern Ireland and the Dundalk Institute of Technology (DkIT) in Ireland has been agreed following weeks of confidential talks. It will result in DkIT becoming a university college of Queen’s.The Irish Finance Minister and Tanaiste (deputy prime minister) Simon Harris said the announcement represented a really important milestone in developing an all-island education system. "I have always said we must use our education and health systems to get to know each other better. Through these solid foundations, we will develop a shared island for all.”The North’s Minister for the Economy, Dr Caoimhe Archibald, a member of Sinn Fein, said the partnership marked a major step in north-south collaboration and added that: “By investing in shared knowledge and talent, both institutions are shaping a future where excellence thrives through co-operation.”The surprise move follows an approach to Dundalk by Queen’s nearly two months ago,University World Newshas learnt. The Irish government was advised of the outcome of the talks on 18 November, after which the governing body of DkIT and the senate of Queen’s were told of the arrangements and approved them.Students enrolling from September 2026 in Dundalk will graduate with a Queen’s degree or postgraduate qualification. The partnership will see the Dundalk institution embedded within Queen’s academic and quality assurance frameworks.DkIT will retain its financial, staffing and governance autonomy, consistent with the collegiate model through which St Mary’s University College and Stranmillis University College operate in partnership with Queen’s.Delivery of apprenticeshipsDundalk will continue to play a vital role as part of the technological sector in Ireland, to include the delivery of essential educational provisions such as apprenticeships. It has already collaborated with Queen’s in a number of academic ventures. Although separated by an open border, they are only 90 kilometres (56 miles) apart.As part of the new arrangements, both institutions will work to establish a Joint Research and Innovation Centre (hub) on the Dundalk campus, with yet-to-be-agreed lead projects in areas such as health and life sciences, and energy and sustainability, and create new opportunities for student mobility, skills development and shared learning across the island.Both institutions will also support enhanced research collaboration, industry partnerships and access to new funding opportunities, and contribute to the competitiveness and strategic development of the Dublin-Belfast Corridor.Dr Diarmuid O’Callaghan, president of DkIT said: “Our collaboration with Queen’s University has spanned decades, and together we have the potential to have a profound impact in the region. The combined strengths of this relationship will enhance educational opportunities and research and innovation in our communities from Dundalk to Belfast and far beyond.”The working title of the new institution is Dundalk University College, and primary legislation will be needed in the Oireachtas (Irish parliament) to copper-fasten the changed arrangements.All but one other of the original 13 institutes of technology in the Republic have merged and become technological universities. The remaining one without the word “university” is the Dun Laoghaire Institute of Art, Design and Technology.Shared ambitionsProfessor Sir Ian Greer, president and vice-chancellor of Queen’s University Belfast, said that the two institutions shared a strong commitment to widening access, enhancing research, and supporting regional growth.“Dundalk University College will allow us to deliver high-quality education to more people and strengthen our shared ambition for the wider region. We look forward to signing the Heads of Agreement in December and to welcoming the first cohort of students in 2026,” Greer said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: STINT tools help gauge risk and gains in HE collaboration
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: STINT tools help gauge risk and gains in HE collaborationGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSWEDENDefence University tapped to host research security nodeSWEDENAcademics wary over centralised approach to security risksNORWAYReport puts academics at core of security-related decisionsSWEDENUniversities oppose guidelines on internationalisationSWEDENGlobal research cooperation must continue, but ‘carefully’SWEDENSecurity vs openness: Towards responsible internationalisationNORDIC COUNTRIESUniversities urged to embrace global values in partnershipsSWEDENInvestigator proposes more autonomy for universitiesSWEDENCall for national HE internationalisation strategyInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackGLOBAL-SWEDENSTINT tools help gauge risk and gains in HE collaborationJan Petter Myklebust20 November 2025TweetA new report offers ‘exploratory tools’ to gauge risk and benefits in international collaborations in higher education and research that can be adapted to different contexts and decision-making – from individual researchers and lecturers to academic leadership, research funders, and policy-makers.Thereport,Risk and opportunity assessment for international collaboration and activities, was written by Albin Gaunt from the Swedish Foundation for International Cooperation in Research and Higher Education (STINT) and Tommy Shih (Lund University and KTH Royal Institute of Technology), both of whom have worked with internationalisation of research and higher education for more than two decades.According to the authors, the latest report can be read as a follow-up to STINT’sResponsible Internationalisation: Guidelines for Reflection on International Academic Collaborationreport published in 2020.“While the earlier report laid the groundwork for responsible internationalisation, this present report also focuses on opportunities and value creation. This is justified, as in the current climate, dominated by risks and threats, which may mean that value-creating partnerships are discontinued or never initiated,” the preface states.A STINTwebinaron 6 November, titled “Weighing the risks and opportunities of international collaborations”, which discussed practical methods and tools to support responsible internationalisation, drew on the new report to show how institutions can identify risks and balance them against the benefits of international cooperation.“Internationalisation is a prerequisite for scientific quality, relevance and renewal. At the same time, the world has become more unpredictable. Researchers, higher education institutions and funding bodies more often face difficult considerations due to geopolitical tensions, new regulatory frameworks and contradictory or insufficient information,” the report states.These circumstances mandate a working method that not only identifies risks but also highlights benefits.“The aim is to offer exploratory tools that can be adapted to different contexts and decision-making levels.“The report is intended to contribute to a nuanced, constructive discussion of internationalisation in Swedish higher education and research and offers a framework for reflection and dialogue that may support well-founded decision-making,” it states.“No general solutions or templates are proposed. Each international collaboration is unique and must be assessed in its specific context.”Immediate vs long-term effectsShih and Gaunt first discuss how value may arise, both as direct effects of the particular activity and as more long-term or indirect results. The more immediate effects include, for instance, the opportunity to gain access to unique research data, material, or infrastructures that would otherwise not have been accessible.They find that long-term and indirect value from international collaboration may include enhancing educational environments, knowledge and innovation systems, long-term knowledge development, and the building of strategic relationships.Such value-creating effects may occur at various levels at the university: individual, institutional and societal. Such effects may be unequally distributed between the different parties to a collaboration, which might create specific challenges.The risks associated with international cooperation are seldom clear or direct, they argue, since the risks are often based on contextual factors, indications, or potential chains of events.They also say that the ability to perceive and interpret risks may vary, depending, for example, on organisational level, access to contextual analysis, research field, and prior experience.Some of these risks arise at the individual level, while others are institutional (for example, risks to reputation) or systemic risks. Risks may be asymmetrically distributed between parties to a collaboration and those bearing the greatest risk do not always have the most influence over decisions, they argue.Foundational principlesWhen mapping risks, it is important to reflect on the foundational principles of academia.“Ethics, data security, legislation, geopolitics, and academic quality are examples of areas where risks and opportunities should be mapped,” the report states. “Matrices that combine effects and probability provide a clear overview and support prioritisation and decision-making.”The report argues that the aim is to provide documentation that supports sound decision-making; completeness should not always be the goal. An analysis should be proportionate and practical.It states: “Risks and opportunities often manifest at different times. Long-term effects and the difficulty of withdrawing should be considered,” and notes that “risk management may itself generate new risks for other actors.”The next step in the report’s analysis concerns how these risks and opportunities are sorted out in an organisational context between the participating parties in internationalisation and how upstaffing or other organisational measures might be needed.“Higher education institutions may establish advisory groups, create fora for dialogue, or build capacity through training for researchers, administrators and academic leaders. Higher education institutions and other organisations may also embark on joint initiatives in this area,” the report notes.“A risk and opportunity assessment does not only concern the activity itself but also organisational capacity to manage it. One way of providing support in this area is by formalising the view on expected effects and obtaining a common understanding of risks,” it states.In the final chapter the authors argue that student mobility and the internationalisation of higher education may require specific considerations.“High-risk destinations may offer outbound students unique learning opportunities – for example, access to specialised expertise, cultural immersion, or valuable language skills. Inbound mobility may give students important perspectives as well as networks benefitting both the mobile students and the students they meet at their higher education institution, while problems may arise with visas and residence permits, for instance.”A ‘central tension’Erik Forsberg, the STINT Representative in China and ASEAN, toldUniversity World Newsthe report offered a clear and useful breakdown of the opportunities and risks inherent in international academic cooperation.He said in doing so it “highlights a central tension that higher education institutions, security authorities, and policy-makers all approach, albeit from different angles: that an overly forceful securitisation of research partnerships risks undermining the long-term benefits such collaborations bring to the research community, academic institutions, and society.”Professor William Brustein, former vice-president for global strategies and international affairs at West Virginia University in the United States and a member of the STINT advisory board, toldUniversity World Newsthe report was “significant and timely”.“I found the report well-balanced in terms of laying out the expected costs and benefits for individual researchers as well as for institutions engaging in international collaborations,” he said.“My many years of experience have taught me that successful international partnerships require a realistic assessment of what added value the potential partnership is likely to produce versus not proceeding with the partnership.”Brustein said the report reminds its readers that institutions also need to consider how to end or “sunset” an ongoing partnership.However, he notes he would like to have seen more attention given to “the intra-institutional back and forth where different constituencies within the university debate the risks and opportunities of a given partnership”.He said in his experience, an academic unit may see these costs and benefits quite differently from the institution's legal and financial offices.“Yes, an advisory board, as the report states, can advise, but who or what body makes the final determination regarding proceeding and sunsetting the partnership? Nonetheless, I find much to like in this STINT report on risks and opportunities for international collaboration in higher education and research,” Brustein said.Nuanced understanding neededAsked to comment on the implications of universities staffing up security functions to work on internationalisation, Ole Petter Ottersen, professor emeritus of Oslo University and a former rector of both Oslo University and the Karolinska Institutet in Stockholm, said hiring security personnel to manage responsible internationalisation could become a “slippery slope”.“It risks creating more bureaucracy, internal tensions, and – perhaps most critically – eroding the sense of responsibility that researchers themselves must uphold as ethical actors in a complex international research landscape,” he toldUniversity World News.He added that the complexity of the issue was enormous, spanning generic knowledge (ethics, academic freedom, etcetera), regional expertise, and discipline-specific insights.“Navigating responsible internationalisation requires the kind of nuanced understanding that researchers in the field possess, and this cannot simply be outsourced to a new administrative layer,” he said.Instead, knowledge of responsible internationalisation should become “second nature” for every researcher and an integral part of research practice rather than something delegated or outsourced to a bureaucratic intermediary.“Ethical judgement by researchers, supported by clear guidelines, training, and continual competence building, is the key to navigating an increasingly challenging and dynamic international landscape,” Ottersen said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Egypt needs 65 new universities to meet rising demand – Report
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Egypt needs 65 new universities to meet rising demand – ReportGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksEGYPTNew international academy aims to shape sustainable citiesEGYPTNew university branches to target region’s skills gapsAFRICAEntrepreneurial HE alliance aims to create millions of jobsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackEGYPTEgypt needs 65 new universities to meet rising demand – ReportWagdy Sawahel19 November 2025TweetEgypt needs at least 65 new universities by 2030 to meet the demand for higher education amid a rapidly growing university-age population. The institutions should align with future economic needs, according to a market research report.This is one of the main findings of thereport,Higher Education Market in Egypt 2024-25, published by the investment management company Colliers on 10 November 2025.Egypt has 27 public universities and at least 40 private higher education institutions, according to the report. It indicates that more than 70% of enrolled students attend public higher education institutions, while private universities and higher education institutes account for the remaining share.“The public sector maintains market dominance, largely due to its more affordable tuition fees compared to the private sector,” the market research report reads.Population growth driving demandThe report indicates that, with a population that had reached approximately 106 million in 2024, Egypt is considered North Africa’s largest country. The country’s population accounted for 1.3% of the world’s total population.By 2030, Egypt’s population is expected to reach 120 million at an average growth rate of 2% and an average household size of four people. This is expected to continue driving demand for higher education services.As of 2024, people aged 18-24 represented 9.2% of the population, a figure projected to rise to 10.8% by 2030, resulting in an additional 3.3 million people in the university-age group.The report indicated that, with student enrolment projected to surge from 3.76 million in 2024 to over five million by 2030, based on an average of 20,000 students per university, they will need an estimated 65 additional universities.Specialisation shiftThe most common fields of study among Egypt’s graduates in the 2024-25 academic year are commerce, arts and humanities, law, education and medical studies, according to the report. However, demand for higher education is expected to shift gradually from conventional majors to emerging specialisations such as artificial intelligence, robotic sciences and nuclear and renewable energy.“This substantial need presents significant opportunities for the private sector to invest in and expand specialised programmes across high-demand fields,” according to the report.Higher education reform under wayThe report pointed out that rapid population growth, coupled with the government’s Vision 2030 reforms, is reshaping the country’s education landscape.Higher education stands as a core pillar of Egypt’s Vision 2030, which is anchored in three key goals: enhancing Egypt’s global ranking in the Global Competitiveness Index through education reforms focused on quality and graduate outcomes; reducing the unemployment rate among university graduates relative to the total unemployment; and increasing the number of Egyptian universities ranked among the world’s top 500 institutions.From 2020 to 2024, several foreign branch universities have opened, primarily in Egypt’s New Administrative Capital (or The New Capital), with more expected to launch in the coming years to further attract international students and talent.The report points out that the targeted outcomes of the strategic goals of Egypt’s Vision 2030 include increasing the higher education enrolment rate among the 18-22 age group to 45% and doubling the proportion of international students from the current 3% to 6% by 2030.The report noted that student enrolment rates increased between 2022 and 2023, reflecting “positive momentum” in the higher education sector.Urgent call for actionHamed Ead, professor of chemistry at Cairo University’s faculty of science and the director of the Science Heritage Centre, toldUniversity World News: “My reaction to this report is one of sober recognition of a monumental challenge, coupled with strong optimism about the potential for transformative change.“The scale of the demand is staggering. The projection that Egypt needs approximately 65 new universities in just five years is a clear and urgent call to action.”He said that the report paints a picture of a country at a crossroads, “facing a demographic imperative that, if met with strategic and quality-focused investment, can become a powerful engine for economic growth and global competitiveness”.Reshaping the HE landscapeTo constructively respond to the report’s findings, Egypt must avoid a ‘quantity over quality’ trap, Ead emphasised. “Building 65 new universities is a physical challenge, but ensuring they are effective is the real goal, which could be achieved by adopting specific actions.”According to Ead, the actions include prioritising quality and relevance in new programmes through future-proofing the curriculum. This requires close collaboration with industry leaders to ensure the curricula are practical and meet real-world market needs.“Because the public sector, alone, cannot bear the burden of offering higher education opportunities for all people, strategic partnerships and investment through creating a conducive environment for private investment must be fostered.”Ead further suggested active recruitment of more top-tier international universities to establish branches for boosting quality, attracting international students, and fostering a competitive environment.“Egypt should also consider implementing a differentiated university model and leveraging technology and innovative models,” he said. “Not all 65 new institutions need to be traditional, comprehensive universities.“Egypt should consider a mix of fewer research universities focused on post-graduate studies and research and development, more universities of applied sciences dedicated to practical, career-oriented degrees in engineering, technology and healthcare, with strong internship components, and specialised institutes focused exclusively on high-priority fields like nuclear energy or data science.”The goal of reducing graduate unemployment must be central through robust career services, mandatory work placements for students, and continuous feedback loops between universities and employers to adjust programmes as the job market changes, he said.Cultural, social campus impactMagdi Tawfik Abdelhamid, a professor at Cairo’s National Research Centre, supports Ead’s innovative suggestions as the way forward for reshaping the higher education sector to face demographic and economic challenges.“However, ways to navigate the negative social impact of the high tuition fees of private universities and foreign branch campuses must be considered, especially for poor families,” Abdelhamid said.Nearly 60% of Egyptians live below or near the poverty line, a fact intricately linked to the wave of inflation, which hit 35.7% in February 2024.“To safeguard the quality of university education, merit-based admissions systems must be applied in private universities. These currently have lower minimum admission marks compared to public universities and a long-standing policy to provide broader access to higher education. This could result in low-quality graduates,” Abdelhamid said.“Also, ways to preserve cultural and traditional values while studying at foreign branch campuses must be applied to producing skilled human resources with a protected Egyptian identity to drive sustainable development,” he added.Abdelhamid further said: “We need universities that protect cultural identity, accelerate achieving sustainable development and build a knowledge society as well as contributing to social development without exacerbating inequality and class division.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

埃及需新建65所大學以應對需求激增

根據Colliers於2025年11月10日發表的報告，埃及需在2030年前建設至少65所新大學，以應對不斷增長的高等教育需求。報告指出，埃及目前擁有27所公立大學和至少40所私立高等教育機構，70%以上的學生就讀於公立院校。隨著到2030年大學年齡人口預計增長至330萬，這一需求將進一步上升。報告強調，高等教育改革是埃及2030願景的核心，旨在提升教育質量及畢業生就業率，並鼓勵私營部門在高需求領域投資，創建更多專業課程。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities secure deal with major research publisherGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH AFRICAOpen access and research outputs: Who owns our knowledge?GLOBALThe future of knowledge and who should control itNEW ZEALANDUniversities body calls for fewer ‘tweaks’ and more fundingNEW ZEALANDNew Zealand universities face severe financial challengesNEW ZEALANDHE budget increased, but universities still under pressureSOUTH AFRICAOpen access directory added to accredited journal listInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackAUSTRALIA-NEW ZEALANDUniversities secure deal with major research publisherJohn Gerritsen18 November 2025TweetUniversities in Australia and New Zealand have negotiated a landmark licensing deal with one major research publisher but paused talks with another as they seek to reduce their multi-million-dollar spend on journal access.Staff at 47 institutions across the two nations were last week told of an in-principle agreement with publishers Taylor & Francis but the looming loss of access to Elsevier publications.The Council of Australasian University Librarians (CAUL) said the Taylor & Francis deal reached by the council, Universities Australia (UA) and Universities New Zealand (UNZ) was the first to be secured under a new negotiation framework.“The agreement means that all Australian and New Zealand university research published by Taylor & Francis can be freely accessed by members of the public,” it stated.The council’s Open Access Negotiation Strategy Committee Chair and Deakin University Vice-Chancellor Professor Iain Martin said universities were trying to save money and give the public access to their research.“The growing costs of both library subscriptions and open access publishing have become unaffordable for universities. The partnership between CAUL, UA and UNZ is all about ensuring that we are delivering fair value for the taxpayer and can continue to prioritise the research and learning that matters,” he said.Elsevier talks pausedThe council said it was also negotiating with Wiley and Springer Nature but had “paused” talks aimed at creating a similar deal with Elsevier.“CAUL has paused discussions with Elsevier after both parties were unable to reach agreement on major commercial terms, including pricing, agreement structure and inclusion of gold open access journals,” it said in a statement.Martin said the universities hoped to reach an agreement with Elsevier in the future.“Our sector, and indeed, our community should have confidence that our agreement with one of the world’s largest and most profitable publishers is fair and reasonable. We remain hopeful of achieving that,” he said.In a joint letter to staff at all eight of New Zealand’s universities, the country’s vice-chancellors said they spent NZ$30 million (US$16.98 million) a year collectively on licensing agreements with the four publishers.“That figure doesn’t include the unpaid cost of our researchers serving on editorial boards, writing and peer-reviewing content,” the letter said.“We are deeply disappointed by the position taken by Elsevier,” it said.“Cost is part of the issue. The payment to Elsevier is likely one of the largest signed by each vice-chancellor to any commercial entity annually. The bill for Elsevier makes up about half of the annual cost of all four of these major publishers,” the letter noted.“However, other crucial factors include securing fair access, improving transparency, and supporting a healthy publishing ecosystem that works for Australia and New Zealand. These matters are being reflected in negotiations with the other publishers,” the letter stated.The letter said Australasian universities would temporarily lose some degree of access to Elsevier’s, 1659 journals when the current agreement expired on 31 December 2025.Negotiations to take ‘months’“Negotiations will possibly take months. In similar overseas experiences, negotiations have taken six to 18 months,” it said.“We acknowledge that this will likely create some disruption and uncertainty and will impact our university communities. However, we believe this is an opportunity to continue to advocate for new models that align more closely with the needs of our sector and the public who fund and benefit from our work.”Universities New Zealand chief executive Chris Whelan said the effect on access to Elsevier publications would vary between institutions.“It’s highly likely that some universities will be losing access to Elsevier journals from the start of the new year; others will have, as part of their agreement, continued access on some sort of basis,” he said.Whelan said journal access was an important investment.“Obviously we want to make sure we’re getting the best possible return. All eight of our universities are public universities. They’re taxpayer-funded, and we really have an obligation to make sure we're getting the best value possible for the money that we spend,” he said.Whelan said the universities wanted free public access to the research taxes they had funded. He said universities were tied into the journal system.“We have a mutually beneficial relationship with these publishers. We need them to be able to publish and for other researchers to be able to access knowledge and build on it, so they play an important part in the research ecosystem,” he said.“But on the flip side, their research is hidden behind paywalls. If you’re a member of a university community, your library will have a licence to access it. But members of the public generally can't see behind those payrolls. So there's a lot of research that ultimately has been paid for by New Zealand."Elsevier said in a statement it had been in constructive discussions with CAUL to establish a new agreement that supported the research communities of Australia and New Zealand.“As these negotiations are currently on pause, we appreciate CAUL’s message to institutions to work directly with us to ensure continued reading and publishing access into next year.“As with all our customers, we remain committed to finding sustainable solutions that meet their needs. We value our long-standing partnership with CAUL and look forward to continuing to work with them in the future.”Universities need savingsNew Zealand Association of Scientists co-president Professor Troy Baisden said it didn’t make sense for universities to spend as much as they did on journal access.“The cost of library subscriptions to these major journal houses is one of the most broken issues that universities face,” he said.He said New Zealand universities were particularly motivated to find savings because they were among the most cost-constrained in the developed world.“Universities are going to play hardball,” he said.“They’ve had a long-term plan to try to get these costs under control.”Baisden said loss of access to Elsevier publications might not be a big deal for many researchers because they could find other ways to access and publish research.“A lot of people outside of major institutions haven't had access to Elsevier or haven't had access to a particular journal,” he said.“There are a lot of different ways to get access... All the universities already have the system whereby things that they publish can go into a repository which is either available immediately for free or possibly within some months after initial publication.”Meanwhile, CAUL Content Procurement Committee Chair Hero Macdonald said the Taylor & Francis agreement was “a monumental step forward” in the transition to open access for universities in Australia and New Zealand.“Comprehensive publishing across Taylor & Francis’ suite of journals, no matter their type, greatly simplifies the options for authors,” Macdonald said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Academics hail launch of new US$2.8bn basic research fund
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Academics hail launch of new US$2.8bn basic research fundGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksDENMARKReport proposes a fivefold increase in defence budget for R&DDENMARKMinisters announce ambitious funding for green innovationDENMARKResearch explores how AI might change knowledge productionEUROPE-DENMARKUniversities drive participation in Horizon Europe researchDENMARKCan the Danish research ‘miracle’ be resuscitated?DENMARKSectorally mobile researchers are ‘change agents’DENMARKNew research strategy has an eye on the Nobel PrizeDENMARKCan the Danish miracle in world-class research endure?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyDENMARKAcademics hail launch of new US$2.8bn basic research fundJan Petter Myklebust18 November 2025TweetUniversities, academics and trade unions have welcomed the announcement by the Danish Ministry of Higher Education and Science of a new fund of DKK18.4 billion (US$2.8 billion) for basic research that will lay the foundation for scientific breakthroughs and innovation to be distributed in a multi-year agreement over the period 2026 to 2029.The funds are the product of anagreementreached on 6 November between the government and a broad majority in the parliament, and aim to strengthen research within the areas of health, safety and climate, according to a ministerialpress release.Because the funds are given as basic funds for research, they will form part of the universities' general finances.The first amount of NOK500 million is to be allocated in 2026, and this will continue annually for the period 2027 to 2029. In addition, Denmark's Independent Research Fund will be strengthened by NOK120 million in 2026, so the total grant will reach DKK 1.2 billion.“A rapidly changing world, international technological competition and a challenged competitiveness place greater demands on our research and innovation efforts.“Now, a broad majority among the parties in the Danish Parliament is setting a clear and multi-year direction for what should be prioritised in the coming years,” the ministry said in its statement.More room for risk-takingMinister of Higher Education and Science Christina Egelund said: “I am incredibly happy and proud that there is now a broad majority behind a new political direction for Danish research and innovation.“This means that we can really strengthen areas that are absolutely central to the safety and health of Danes, and we can further increase the green transition and our ability to compete in a global economy.”Egelund said the government was “creating greater room for risk-taking for wild ideas and giving universities even better opportunities to attract talent and top researchers, so that Denmark can continue to conduct excellent research”.The ministertoldUniversity Postthe longer-term funding provided stability, which would influence how research projects are planned. She said that “significantly increasing the universities’ non-earmarked basic funding” would ease the pressure on research communities that are dependent on writing grant applications to public and private foundations.“Because this means you can focus on what really matters – research – and not chasing the next round of funding. It creates resilience and stability in university research communities, which I think is really important,” she added.She said she hoped more researchers could move from temporary project positions into permanent employment.The agreement also includes funding to strengthen academic freedom in Denmark, which, according to the minister, is under pressure.“We now agree that the principles and values that safeguard researchers’ freedom and independent thought should be politically protected. I think that sends a strong signal from Denmark at a time when such principles are under attack in other parts of the world,” Egelund is reported to have said.“Everything is a matter of priorities – and we have the funds we have. We raised it by half a billion last year – for one year – and now we’re increasing it to a full billion, initially for a period of four years. So I believe this is a major – and much-needed – boost to basic funding,” Egelund toldUniversity Post.A ‘good day’ for Danish researchReacting to the announcement, Brian Bech Nielsen, chair of the Rectors’ Conference at Universities Denmark, called it “a particularly good day for Danish research”.“The fact that such a large majority in the parliament agrees that the funds from the research reserve should be used over several years in this way is a vote of confidence in Danish research. It is very satisfying that there is such broad support for casting a long light on research,” he stated in apress release.“It’s really something we can feel at the universities when the parliament gives higher priority to basic funding. It gives us the opportunity both to further support free and curiosity-driven research and to prioritise innovation of new, clever solutions, products and technologies,” he said.Aalborg University Rector Per Michael Johansen also welcomed the agreement, saying: it managed “to balance consideration for long-term free research with the strategic investments, which are absolutely necessary in a world characterised by unrest and rapid technological development.“We are in the middle of a time where technologies change over weeks and months – but where progress rests on research that takes many years. It is a complex reality, and that is precisely why it is so important that we invest wisely and for the long term.”He said research and innovation had never been more important given the challenge to solve Denmark’s and Europe’s challenges regarding safety, green transition and health.“At Aalborg University, we look forward to contributing with research, education and innovation so that we can future-proof Denmark’s welfare, safety and competitiveness,” Johansen said.Peace researchAccording toUniversity Post, the new agreement specifically earmarks DKK6.9 billion for research into security, critical technologies and defence.The Socialist People’s Party (SF) entered the negotiations seeking to prioritise peace research. In this, they succeeded, as the agreement sets aside DKK25 million for this from 2026 to 2029.SF youth education, higher education and research spokesperson Sofie Lippert told theUniversity Postmany of Denmark’s neighbours have peace research centres that study wars as they may have looked 20 years ago.“I believe Denmark is well-positioned to investigate what peace might look like in a new era of hybrid and cyber warfare and all kinds of other things we didn’t know about 20 years ago.“We have huge potential to contribute to this globally. That’s why I think it’s only natural that DIIS [the Danish Institute for International Studies] should set up a strong research community in this field,” Lippert stated.The Conservative Party succeeded in their bid to have nuclear power research funded via the agreement with an allocation of DKK40 million.“This is a chance to counter fear with facts, and we’d like to go even further. We want to lift the ban on nuclear power, because we know this is one of the ways to address the green transition,” Lise Bertelsen, Conservative Party spokesperson for research, toldUniversity Post.Frede Blaabjerg, distinguished professor of energy technology at Aalborg University and chair of the Danish Council for Research and Innovation Policy (DFIR), toldUniversity World Newsthe agreement “is a breakthrough that the Minister of Higher Education and Science has been able to make a four-year agreement for Danish research and innovation policy – normally it is done year by year where it is decided which areas are of key importance to support”.Blaabjerg said in the recent past there has been an imbalance between “basic funds”, or direct university funding, and “competitive funds”.Against this backdrop the agreement “has given more long-term basic money to the universities, which can help them plan more strategically”, Blaabjerg said, adding that the significant support also given to innovation could be a “game changer in many universities”.A ‘welcome shift’ in policyAssociate Professor Brian Arly Jacobsen, based in the Department of Cross-Cultural and Regional Studies at the University of Copenhagen and chair of DM University, a union for academic professionals, said the agreement was an important and welcome shift in Danish research policy.“Long-term funding and strengthened basic research grants give universities the stability they need to plan ambitiously, attract talent and maintain high scientific quality.“For many years, Danish researchers have been operating in a system where short funding cycles and a heavy dependence on external grants have created insecurity and slowed down the kind of exploratory, curiosity-driven research that often leads to real breakthroughs.“Increasing the basic funding is therefore not just an economic adjustment – it is an investment in academic freedom and in the integrity of the research process itself,” he said.Arly Jacobsen said he was also pleased to see a “dedicated focus on research visibility, trust in research/knowledge and the protection of academic freedom”.He said in a time marked by misinformation and political pressure on universities in many countries, it was crucial that Denmark “sends a clear signal that independent research is a cornerstone of a democratic society”.“This agreement does not solve every challenge facing the sector, but it represents a significant step in the right direction. It provides a more resilient foundation for Danish research communities and strengthens our ability to contribute meaningfully to global scientific and societal challenges,” Arly Jacobsen said.Rune Boelsmand Bak, vice-chair of academic matters at the National Union of Students in Denmark (DSF), toldUniversity World News: “In short, we agree with all the praise that this change has received.“Giving the research institutions a longer, strategic base for funding their work is essential and, honestly, long overdue. The Danish university sector has been subjected to 29 reforms over 21 years, all of which have shaken the universities.“Viewing them together, they show little to no collective direction, and they all have or had rather short implementation deadlines. As such, strategic thinking within the institutions has been lacking.“This change, however, is in the opposite direction. It prioritises time and common understanding in the institutions, rather than quick fixes. We applaud the responsible minister, Christina Egelund, for providing this small spot of sunshine on our universities.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary:
丹麥推出280億美元基礎研究基金

丹麥高等教育與科學部近日宣布，將設立一項總額為184億丹麥克朗（約280億美元）的基礎研究基金，預計在2026至2029年期間逐步撥款。該基金旨在加強健康、安全及氣候等領域的研究，並為丹麥的科學突破和創新奠定基礎。

此政策獲得丹麥議會的廣泛支持，並將為大學提供更穩定的資金來源，減輕對外部資助的依賴，促進自由和探索性研究。專家指出，這一變革將增強丹麥在全球競爭中的研究能力與創新。
