https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Voting rights course links students to a global struggle
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Voting rights course links students to a global struggleGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESTrump poses ‘existential threat’ to HE and democracyGLOBALHow virtual colleges are keeping the academic project aliveGLOBALMeet the scholars working with real people on real problemsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredGLOBALVoting rights course links students to a global struggleNathan M Greenfield18 November 2025Tweet“A naive idea.” That’s what Christian Baobab Mukanirwa thought when he first encountered the concept of youth voting rights at the2024 Get Engaged Conferencein Berlin, sponsored by the Global Higher Education Alliance for the 21st Century, which brought together students from 23 institutions from 20 countries to develop leadership skills and work on civic engagement projects.“I was 23 years old, so I was [an eligible] age to vote in my country. But voting was something that most young people like me, who had never been exposed to their rights or the idea of civic education, didn’t think about. So, I really didn’t think the presentation was something worth listening to.“But that was last year,” said Mukanirwa, who fled violence and poverty in the Democratic Republic of the Congo (DRC) and has been living in the Kakuma Refugee Camp in Northwest Kenya since 2018.“That was before I took the ‘Student Voting: Power, Politics and Race in the Fight for American Democracy’ (Student Voting) course offered by Bard College as part of my freshman year as a student at Parami University,” Mukanirwa toldUniversity World News.Founded in Rangoon, Myanmar, in 2017, Parami is now a virtual university in exile based in Washington DC.In addition to being taught at Bard’s main campus in Annandale-on-Hudson, NY, a hundred miles north of New York City, Student Voting was taught synchronically with three historically black colleges and universities (HBCUs): Tuskegee University (Alabama), Prairie View A&M University (PVAMU, Texas) and North Carolina Agricultural & Technical State University (NCATSU, Greensboro).Last year, each section in the United States had approximately 20 students and a similar number of international virtual students, including Mukanirwa.“Internationally, the United States portrays itself and is presented as the home of democracy. The course queries that claim, said Simon Gilhooley, a political science assistant-professor, who co-teaches Student Voting at Bard.“To see the kind of struggles [for voting rights] that have taken place, and how they are still taking place in spaces such as Bard [where students who claim it as their residence continue to fight for the right to vote locally] is something international students ‘get’.’”Student voting rights suppressedThe backbone of Student Voting are the essays in the book,Youth Voting Rights: Civil Rights, the Twenty-Sixth Amendment, and the Fight for American Democracy on College Campuses (Youth Voting Rights, De Gruyter), published on 17 November and co-edited by Jonathan Becker, vice-president of Bard, and Yael Bromberg, a constitutional lawyer and law professor at American University Washington College of Law.Its nine chapters are written by different scholars. These case studies tell the story of the systematic efforts of voting officials in the United States to deny students the right to vote from the campuses on which they live – and the fight to secure these rights decades after the passage of the 26th Amendment to the US Constitution, which lowered the voting age from 21 to 18, in 1971.The words “civil rights” in the book’s title do double duty: they refer to the civil rights of the 18-year-old students who are eligible to vote after the passage of the 26th Amendment; at the same time, and, perhaps even more importantly, they refer to the fight waged by HBCUs and their students to vote before 1971, when the voting age was 21 – and their students were being denied the right to vote by the racist Jim Crow legal regimen that followed the end of the Reconstruction period (1867 to 1877) after the American Civil War.It’s these battles, the authors of the case studies show, that were the first concerted efforts to fight for student voting rights.Alabama’s 1901 constitution is a paradigmatic example of how white supremacists put legal impediments in the way of black people seeking to vote, as Professor Lisa Bratton shows in her chapter on Tuskegee University (Alabama), where she teaches history. This constitution disenfranchised black voters by, among other means, putting in place literacy tests, grandfather clauses and poll taxes.“Literacy tests were administered by white registrars in Macon County who allowed illiterate whites to register with little hindrance but prevented the voter registration of the county’s black citizens, including those with PhDs,” writes Bratton.When black voters who lived on the Tuskegee campus went to register, they encountered numerous tactics designed to prevent them, including finding that the registrars were absent or the rule that “only two Negro applicants [were allowed] in the registration room at the same time,” which, of course, slowed down the registration process.Blacks, not whites, were required, Bratton writes, to “read and transcribe articles from the constitution” – and as they were writing, the registrars spoke to them, often causing errors that then disqualified the resident seeking to register.Another tool used to erase student voting rights is gerrymandering, both of municipal boundaries and districts, and, more famously, of congressional districts.In 1957, Alabama State Senator Sam Englehardt, executive secretary of the White Citizen’s Council for Alabama, sponsored a bill that was passed into law under the nameAct 140. It “effectively redrew the boundaries of the City of Tuskegee,” writes Bratton, “from a rectangle to a 28-sided shape that resembled a sea dragon”, that excluded 99% of the blacks who had voted in the city as well as Tuskegee itself.The racially inspired geometricians ensured, however, that none of the 600 white voters in the former district were removed from the city’s voting rolls. In rulingAct 140unconstitutional in November 1960, the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) said the law “was not an ordinary geographical redistricting measure even with the familiar abuses of gerrymandering” because the “legislation was solely concerned with segregating white and coloured [sic] voters by sending Negro citizens out of town so as to deprive them of their pre-existing municipal vote”.Gerrymandering: Alive and wellGerrymandering is alive and well in the US – and is the subject of investigation in courses on democracy taught at the Central European University, which is affiliated with Bard, in Vienna.At the behest of President Donald J Trump, earlier this year, the Republican government of Texas redrew its congressional district map in an effort to ensure that no Democrat would be elected, even in such staunchly Democratic cities as Houston and Austin.In response, the government of Californiaproposed to its voters a new mapthat would significantly lessen the chances of a Republican being elected; this map was approved in the election earlier this month. Other states are considering redrawing their congressional maps to favour one or the other party.Though he has not worked with Becker or Gilhooley, Dr Felix Butzlaff, a postdoctoral fellow in Central European University’s (Vienna) Department of Public Policy, is aware of their work on student voting rights.Recently, in his courses on social movements and contemporary forms of activism, he has led his students through an investigation of the moral questions surrounding California governor Gavin Newsom’s plan, which had been approved in a referendum, to gerrymander his state’s map to counter gerrymandering in Texas.To the question: “How far can we take it [gerrymandering] to defend democracy?” Butzlaff said he finds his students “less dogmatic than I have seen them in the past. They are very much aware of what it is that needs to be defended, especially in the case of gerrymandering.”A second curriculum in TexasProfessors Alexander Goodwin and Michael J Nojeim’s chapter inYouth Voting Rightsdiscusses voter suppression of students and gerrymandering of congressional districts that pertain to the campus of their institution, PVAMU.Claiming he was fighting voter fraud, in the early 1970s, LeRoy Symm, the tax assessor-collector for Waller County, Texas, where PVAMU is located, decreed that PVAMU students – and only PVAMU students – had to fill out a questionnaire of his devising to determine their residency for the purposes of being entered on the voting rolls.The questions devised by Symm included: “Do you intend to reside in Waller indefinitely? Do you have a job or position in Waller County? [Do you] own any home or other property in Waller County? [Do you] Belong to a church, club, or some Waller County organisation, other than college related? Are you married to someone who has resided in Waller County?”In March 1972 five students went to court to force Symm to place them on the voters’ list. PVAMU was not a party to the suit.Yet, as a historically black institution, PVAMU “deliberately exposed [its students] to a second curriculum” which “championed the ideals of democracy and citizenship and believed in these sacrosanct values despite being denied them [students] on a daily basis”, as historian Jelani Favors writes in the chapter about NCATSU.The trial judge, Justice James Noel, ruled against the students. A series of court cases ensued and Symm continued to use his questionnaire to restrict the number of black students enrolled as voters – until, in 1979, the Supreme Court of the United States (SCOTUS) issued a four word ruling: “The judgment is affirmed,” making a lower court ruling against Symm the law of the land.Goodwin’s and Nojeim’s chapter did not, however, end with the SCOTUS decision, for, scarcely a year later, nine years after the voting age was lowered to 18, state election officials sought to dilute the black majority who attended PVAMU by dividing the campus into three separate districts.“The gerrymander was so severe,” they write, “that if students moved across the street from one dormitory to another, they were placed in different voting districts,” which necessitated re-registering to vote, something most students were unaware of. (A decade would pass before, with the help of the US Department of Justice, the university was included in one precinct.)Shattered worldviewThis and other examples of gerrymandering based on race, a South Asian student who, to protect her identity, will be called Lin, toldUniversity World News: “basically shattered my worldview because it made me aware that race is such a big factor in American politics”.The impact of this realisation did not, Lin continued, remain confined to her view of America, where she has lived for three years, but altered her view of “global politics” and how it too is impacted by racial thinking.In one discussion about gerrymandering and the fragility of voting rights, Gilhooley toldUniversity World News, a student at Bard “had this kind of gut reaction and said: ‘That’s so un-American’. And then there was a collective pause”.Gilhooley broke the silence by saying: “Well, we’ve been looking at history where this is repeatedly taking place. What does it mean to be American in this sense? What is that claim about? What does it involve?”In the discussion that ensued, students at Bard and those joining viaZoomfrom the three HBCUs, and international students from outside the US, situated the struggle in a global context and in specifics of countries where, of course, while the means may be different, the result is the same: voter suppression.Lin spoke to this point when she told me that in her country the formal right to vote is rendered nugatory when local officials use the voter lists to tell voters whose fidelity to the ruling party is doubted that their polling places have changed – and that transportation to the poll is not available.It is during such a discussion that the importance of international students becomes evident because they bring a different, perhaps better, wider, “perspective” to the course, said Gilhooley.For example, a student in exile from Afghanistan spoke about her mother, “who had fought incredibly hard to be able to vote during the brief window when the Taliban were not in control. And now she’s lost that vote, although she’s still fighting for it”.“Even for American students who conceive of the struggle to vote as a hard struggle, there can be a kind of moment of shock, of the incompatibility between these two experiences,” he stated.Instead of shutting down conversation, Gilhooley continued, the students become “very interested in kind of forging a connection with the students in different situations, and they come to see how maybe this is part of a kind of a global struggle.“The students see the same pressures to deny people [the] vote, the same urgency for people to be able to express themselves and participate politically, the same kind of loss of control of their lives that leads from not being able to do so”.Bard College strugglesNo doubt readers ofYouth Voting Rightswill be surprised by the book’s eighth chapter, which Becker, who is also director of the Center for Civic Engagement (Bard) and vice-chancellor for the Global Higher Education Alliance for the 21st Century, gave the pugnacious, but fitting, title “Bard College: Jim Crow in Dutchess County”.Fitting because Dutchess County officials used many of the Jim Crow tactics to prevent students at both Bard and its near neighbour, Vassar, one of the “Seven Sisters” (so-called female Ivies when the schools were segregated by gender), from voting.When the struggle for the right to vote from the campus began in Dutchess County, both Bard’s and Vassar’s student bodies were more than 90% white, and, since both were expensive schools to attend, most students were either wealthy or upper middle class.Still, local election officials refused to register students to vote, with one Republican county commissioner saying, as Becker quoted: “We can’t have Bard students running the Town of Red Hook,” which Bard is in.After two students sued successfully to be able to vote in 1976, the Republican election commission for the county stole a march on Waller County, Texas, and put in place a questionnaire asking such questions as “Is your present residence claimed as a student?”“What is your parents’ home address?” and “To what extent do your parents contribute to your support?” Court decisions against this practice were flouted until June of 2000, when the local Board of Elections agreed to register Bard and Vassar students.Meanwhile at Vassar, county officials took a page from Waller County’s playbook and gerrymandered the campus, splitting it into three parts to dilute student votes in municipal elections. The consequence of this was, writes Becker, that “some students who had changed dormitories would also be obliged to re-register to vote from their new address and vote at a different polling place.”The campus remains divided between two districts today.Becker relates other legal battles, including a successful bid to have polling stations on Bard’s campus.The ‘raw nature of oppression’Lin comes from a country where dissent is discouraged, including by the state using media to “desensitise” people to “all information” and convincing them that “horrible things are an inherent part of living. So just sit there and accept it.”It’s notable, therefore, how energised about the impact of race on American politics she was when we spoke and how much interest she took in the history of gerrymandering; it goes back to 1812 when Massachusetts governor Elbridge Gerry redrew a congressional district to look like a salamander that would reliably vote for the Jeffersonian Democratic-Republican party.When, as part of the Student Voting course, she went to NCATSU for Democracy Day, she found herself realising “how horrible all this [racism in American politics] is”. Months later, she toldUniversity World News: “I still, honestly, I still, cannot wrap my head around just how open and honest everyone was about the raw nature of oppression.”For his part, Mukanirwa spoke of how he knew about American racism from the news and social media, but, like Lin, had not realised it was all-pervasive, as he learnt in the course and through the case studies inVoting Rights.Given that context, which he compared to the DRC and other African countries where he believed voting was considered unimportant (and ineffective), he was inspired and “shocked” by “how much the HBCUs and the leadership of those colleges and universities and the students were fighting, despite the fact that they were sidelined”.Mukanirwa, who proudly identifies himself, not by his legal status, refugee, but as a “global citizen” intent on working in the field of civic education, toldUniversity World Newsthat learning how black American students have overcome “restrictions and repressive institutional structures” is inspiring.Commenting on the situation in Africa, Mukanirwa said: “We are very far behind, and it will take a lot of time to catch up with where they are [US campuses] right now. If we could get to the point where colleges and universities we studied are now, that would be a very, very great achievement.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Global|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

投票權課程連結學生與全球鬥爭

巴德學院開設的“學生投票：權力、政治與美國民主的鬥爭”課程，讓來自20個國家的學生深入探討投票權益，特別是青年投票權的歷史與挑戰。學生們在課程中學習到美國歷史上針對投票權的種族歧視和系統性壓制，並與歷史悠久的黑人學院合作，分享各自國家在民主參與方面的經驗。來自剛果的學生穆卡尼瓦表示，這堂課讓他對美國的種族問題和投票權鬥爭有了更深入的理解，並啟發他成為全球公民，致力於公民教育。這樣的跨文化交流強調了全球對於投票權的共同鬥爭。
```

interest in US masters degrees falls by 61%UNITED STATES-GLOBALTrump is ‘catalyst, not cause’ of HE decline – PasquerellaGLOBALThree of ‘Big Four’ see steep drop in postgrad enrolmentUNITED STATESInterest in US for postgrad study has plummeted, data showsUNITED STATESInterest in studying in US dropped 42% in JanuaryGLOBALDecline in interest in ‘Big Four’ study destinationsUNITED STATES5% decline in first-year students reverses years of gainsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED STATES-GLOBALInternational student enrolment in US down 17% in 2025Nathan M Greenfield18 November 2025TweetThe number of new international students coming to the United States fell by 17% in 2025, according to areportby NAFSA (Association of International Educators), titledFall 2025 International Student Enrollment Snapshot and Economic Impact.The decline in new international students has reduced the total number of international students on America’s campuses from 1,177,766 to 1,168,520.“A close reading of the enrolment figures from last year and this fall shows that the pipeline of global talent to the United States is in a precarious position,” said Fanta Aw, executive director of the Washington, DC-based NAFSA, in a press release.“If you exclude those international students that were engaged in post-graduation Optional Practical Training last year – which at 25% [of visa holders], is the largest share of total enrolment ever – and this fall, there are alarming declines that we ignore at our peril,” stated Aw.NAFSA’s analysis was conducted by the Bloomington, Indiana-based JB International; the data was drawn from the US government sources, including the Student Exchange and Visitor Information Service (SEVIS) and theOpen Doors 2025report (which is partially funded by the US Department of State) published on 17 November 2025.Data reflects lack of US study interestThe decline in real numbers in NAFSA’s report is in line with the steep decline in interest in studying in the United States that Studyportals began reporting in March, two months after President Donald J Trump returned to office, after which the media was filled with stories about violent arrests of thousands of undocumented immigrants and hundreds of international students – as well as with the US banning students from 19 countries.During this period the Trump administration froze billions of dollars in research funds and launched a number of suits against Columbia, Harvard and other elite universities, ostensibly to fight antisemitism on their campuses.In March, Studyportals reported a decline in interest in studying in the US of40%. By early October, the figure had risen to61%.“The latest snapshot confirms the trend that the US is losing ground in a fiercely competitive global education landscape,” Edwin van Rest, co-founder and president of Studyportals, wrote in an email toUniversity World News.Studyportalsis an online portal based in the Netherlands that has been used by 55 million prospective international students from 240 countries to search for information about international programmes and to apply to them.“A drop in new international students doesn’t just affect students and universities. It impacts local economies, local jobs, availability of programmes for domestic students and the country’s long-term competitiveness, Van Rest explained in his email.“In a global market where students have real alternatives, other countries will gain exactly where the US loses.”Professor Chris R Glass, director of the Executive Doctor of Education in Higher Education programme at Boston College and an expert in international student flows, sounded an even more alarming note in an email toUniversity World News.“The -17% new enrolment this year signals structural contraction, but context matters: total enrolment has been essentially flat for a decade. In fact, there are fewer enrolled students in the US in 2024 and 2025 (883,513) than in 2015 and 2016 (896,341). Strip out OPT, and degree-seeking enrolment has been flat for a decade.”“Without policy intervention, the US appears to be shifting from a decade of enrolment stagnation to a period of enrolment decline for the foreseeable future,” wrote Glass.‘Pipeline’ risk in STEM fieldsThe 12% decline in the number of graduate students has caused concern for what’s called “the pipeline” in the STEM fields. Graduate student researchers do most of the work in university laboratories but are in “the pipeline” to become senior researchers and professors in the future.How this links to the decline in international students is because they make upalmost halfof the STEM students in the US.“This report affirms what we’ve known, which is that we are seeing a decline in international enrolments, and so, the pipeline is softening,” Aw toldUniversity World News.Aw further explained that the pipeline is likely to be weaker next year based on what NAFSA is hearing from its partners and preliminary data about declining interest in studying in the US and the fact that the bulk of the enrolment had already taken place prior to Trump returning to the White House.“The real litmus test will be next fall because students will have had an entire year to decide whether or not they’re even going to apply to universities. On the ground there has been a decline in interest in the US, which will translate into a decline in applications coming to the US,” stated Aw.Indian and Chinese students are the two largest cohorts, comprising in theOpen Doors 2025 report, 31% and 23% of the international students, respectively. However, the number of Indian graduate students coming to the US declined by 9.5% to 177,892 between 2023 and 2024 and 2024 and 2025.The number of Chinese graduate students in the US declined by 2.1% to 120,229. In total, the number of international students coming to the US for graduate studies fell from 502,291 to 488,481.In June, an organisation not usually concerned with university laboratory staffing, the Council on Foreign Relations, warned that reducing international students threatened both the American economy and the success of its university-based research system.“International students play a vital role in the US higher education system, with more than one million enrolled during the 2023 to 2024 academic year.“They contribute tens of billions of dollars to the economy each year, support job creation, and advance cutting-edge research and innovation efforts,” anarticletitled “International Students are Critical for US Innovation. Why are they at risk?” stated.According to Grass, the “modest growth at the PhD level” in the 2024 and 2025Open Doorsdata (+2.5%) and the September 2025 SEVIS data (+2.7% vs September 2024) obviates the worry of “immediate TA/RA (teaching assistant/research assistant) shortages”.“The pipeline risk,” however, “is real,” he wrote.“Any restrictions on H-1B [visa] or OPT would immediately impact PhD programme competitiveness, and both programmes are likely to see regulatory changes in the coming academic year that have the potential to impact PhD enrolments,” he said.For his part, after noting the importance of international students for American research universities, Van Rest widened his focus and underscored how, irrespective of whether the programmes are significantly staffed by international students, successful STEM programmes attract domestic students into STEM fields, thus further strengthening the programmes.“We know international students make up the majority of full-time graduate enrolments in critical fields like AI, engineering, and computer science.“We also know that strong international student communities lift domestic participation in STEM, as universities invest more in research and STEM capacity,” he wrote.Decline in international spendingThis is the second straight year that both the financial contribution of international students and the number of jobs their spending creates have declined.In 2023-24, the 1,126,690 international students contributed US$43.8 billion to the US economy, which, according to NAFSA, supported 378,175 jobs. Last year, a slightly larger number of international students, 1,177,766, contributed almost a billion dollars less (US$42.9 billion) to the American economy,spendingthat supported 355,736 or 23,439 fewer jobs.This year, NAFSA’s Snapshot shows that the 1,168,520 international students will contribute US$41.8 billion, or 4.57% fewer dollars than international students contributed just two years ago. The decline in the number of jobs international students’ spending supports between 2023-24 and this year is even more striking: the decline from 378,175 to 333,051 is almost 13%.Among the five states that are taking the largest economic hit, four are staunchly Democratic states: California (-US$162 million year-over-year), New York (-US$152 million), Massachusetts (-US$92 million) and Illinois (-US$62 million).The only Republican state in this group is Texas, which lost US$65 million as compared to 2024-2025. The next group of five includes Pennsylvania, Florida, Michigan, New Jersey and Arizona, which together lost US$179 million.The states that were least affected, Alaska, Wyoming, Montana, South Dakota, North Dakota, are reliably Republican states and lost a total of US$4.9 million.‘Policy and narrative matters’Predicting the future is, of course, difficult. However, as Van Rest stated: “It’s difficult to see, as the US universities still welcome students and have so much to offer. What we see in this report, and in the behaviour of students on our global platforms, is that policy and narrative matters.“When visa uncertainty increases or mobility becomes harder, students choose other destinations. Protecting OPT and ensuring predictable, student-friendly rules are essential if the US wants to remain a top destination for global talent.”For its part, conscious that “other countries are creating effective incentives to capitalise on our mistakes”, as Aw put it in the press release, NAFSA has put forward a number of proposals that “the United States must adopt if it is to attract and retain the world’s best and brightest”.These proposals address the teacher-researcher pipeline problem by recognising that “post-study work opportunities are essential to our standing as the top destination for global talent.NAFSA calls for the US government to improvevisa availabilityand processing for all F (academic study) and M (vocational) visas and J (cultural exchange) visas, which run counter to policies instituted since the inauguration of President Donald J Trump.For example, in May and June, usually the busiest period for visa issuance, waiting times from India ballooned to months after the US paused interviews and then, after restarting them, began the lengthy process of investigating applicants’ social media accounts.Wait times also drastically increased forChinese visa applicants. NAFSA has also called for the administration to reverse the ban on students from 19 countries, including Chad, the Republic of Congo, Haiti, Iran, Libya and Somalia, from coming to study in the US, “while maintaining background checks and vetting required for visa issuance”.NAFSA also calls on the US government to reverse the decision taken last October that put a cap of four years on how long student visas last; this programme replaced the“Duration of Stay” policy.Additionally, NAFSA urges Congress to pass the Keep STEM Talent Act and provisions that extend dual interest and create a path to a green card [permanent residency] for F-1 students included in theDignity Act.Aw is under no illusion that convincing the US government will be easy, for, she noted: “They should have understood what the policies have done, because they were very explicit in saying that they were aiming to, if not stop the flow, then to suddenly reduce the flow of international students coming to the US.”Aw continued, saying that the government gave a number of arguments, one being to “focus on domestic students” and the other being national security; this later was used to revoke visas as well as for the pause in processing visas. The government took these actions “understanding fully that they would have ramifications in the flow and, frankly, it would certainly send a chilling effect to international students”.After noting that onFox NewsTrump spoke about the need for international students in economic terms [to keep colleges and universities from going bankrupt] but also in terms of research and innovation, Aw said that one of NAFSA’s strategies “is to continue to provide evidence-based information – because facts actually do matter, even in a climate when people think they don’t actually”.Among the data points NAFSA wants to impress on both the administration and the American public is what immigration economists like George Mason University (Fairfax, Northern Virginia) Professor Michael A Clemens have found.“Clemens has talked about the long-term effect of over ten years of GDP in the United States. A one-third decline in graduates from US universities would lead to GDP declines of between US$240 and US$480 billion each year,” said Aw.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Hong Kong reaches for the stars in new astronomy driveGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINAGlobal cooperation to be key part of new space science planCHINASpace ambitions boost research cooperationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHONG KONGHong Kong reaches for the stars in new astronomy driveYojana Sharma17 November 2025TweetWith China’s ambition to become a space power, the city of Hong Kong wants to ride its coat-tails, positioning itself as a centre in Asia for astronomy and space research.The new Hong Kong Institute for Astronomy and Astrophysics (HKIAA), which held its official inauguration ceremony on 3 November, ratchets up Hong Kong’s role as a centre for ground-based fundamental research into galaxies, the universe, high-energy physics such as black holes, and types of stars billions of light years away.The HKIAA is based at Hong Kong University (HKU).Its launch is happening at a time of significantly increased Chinese funding and expansion in the field, involving major missions in space which provide enormous opportunities for research and engineering projects in university departments.The new HKIAA represents a dramatic and upgraded comeback after HKU scrapped the city’s only undergraduate astronomy major in 2017, citing lack of student interest. The move was heavily criticised at the time, coming as it did in the wake of China announcing ambitious space plans.The founding director of HKIAA, China-born astronomer Professor Bing Zhang, chair of astrophysics at HKU, was enticed to Hong Kong from a tenured position at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas. Zhang is known for his theoretical work on powerful gamma-ray bursts in distant galaxies.“He has relocated from America to here [Hong Kong] because the University of Hong Kong promised him substantial funds to start a new group,” John Peacock, professor of cosmology at the Institute for Astronomy, University of Edinburgh, United Kingdom, toldUniversity World Newsin Hong Kong.“It is because the universities here want to build astronomy up,” said Peacock, who was attending the Hong Kong Laureate Forum, from 3 to 7 November, which honours winners of the prestigious Shaw Prize awarded to eminent scientists in the fields of astronomy, life sciences and mathematics. Peacock won the Shaw Prize in 2014.Zhang also represents a major win for HKU at a time when US-China rivalry and cuts to science budgets in the US, coupled with attacks on universities there have led to China stepping in with generous offers to prominent academics looking to leave the US, particularly those of Chinese origin.Hong Kong is part of this strategy, and its universities have access to all telescopes in China and those with which China has international agreements.“When I made a decision to relocate from the US to Hong Kong earlier this year, I saw tremendous opportunities to further advance astronomy and astrophysics at HKU and in Hong Kong in general,” Zhang said at the inauguration event.He stressed Hong Kong’s role in China’s plans. “Recently, Hong Kong astronomers, including many of our HKIAA members, were invited to participate in the scientific planning and instrumental design of a future space mission. This involvement exemplifies how HKIAA will play a more prominent role in China’s fast-growing astronomical enterprise in the future,” he said.Role of Hong Kong“Hong Kong punches above its weight, particularly at the tertiary [education] level,” said Quentin Parker, director of HKU’sLaboratory for Space Research(HK LSR), a separate HKU facility set up in 2017 to focus on space missions, the new space economy and space sustainability, or the reduction of space debris.Some global rankings agencies, depending on their methodology, have listed five Hong Kong universities in the global top 100. “We’ve got to leverage that as a city and make the most of it,” Parker toldUniversity World News. The new astronomy institute will “help augment and improve our profile”.However, cautioned Parker, Hong Kong cannot think it can be a global leader in the field.“There are probably 2,500 astronomers in mainland China and less than 100 in Hong Kong,” he estimated. “You can always pick on an area and recruit into it and get a critical mass in the [particular] area … But astronomy is an incredibly diverse field. You can't cover all the bases.”“Only if you have very large departments with, like, 20-odd people in them – and that's just the academics – and then maybe 40 or 50 postdocs and PhDs … Then you can have a world-leading presence at a very fundamental level,” he said, referring to large groups in China at the Kalvi Institute at Peking University in Beijing, Nanjing University, and Sun Yat-sen University, Zhuhai – all with significant numbers of astronomers.HKIAA currently brings together nine faculty members from HKU’s departments of Physics and of Earth and Planetary Sciences and has 14 affiliated members from other universities in the city.An HKU spokesperson said: “With plans for significant recruitment and a new, spacious facility … set to open in early 2026, the institute aims to be an international centre of research excellence, a platform for global collaboration, and a central hub for astronomers across Hong Kong and the Greater Bay Area.”The Greater Bay Area refers to Hong Kong, Macau and a cluster of cities in China’s southern Guangdong province.HKIAA’s Zhang said: “Basic science depends on global communication and collaboration. Hong Kong’s unique position as a gateway between China and the world makes it ideally suited to facilitate international dialogue and partnerships in fundamental science – bridging China, the United States, Western countries, and the Asia-Pacific region.”“Most importantly, China has enjoyed rapid progress in astronomy and astrophysics, now operating world-class telescopes and facilities across various wavelengths.“In my own research field in transient astrophysics, many of the leading telescopes I work with are based in China,” he said, naming the FAST radio telescope, the Einstein Probe X-ray telescope, the SVOM gamma-ray/multi-wavelength telescopes and the world’s largest TeV observatory, LHAASO.Government grantsSeveral universities in the city have received grants from the Hong Kong government’sInnovation and Technology Support Programme(ITSP), a major initiative for aerospace R&D.In May, around HK$100 million (US$12.86 million) over three years was awarded by ITSP to universities. “Three HKU groups submitted bids, and ours was successful, and we got one-third of the money for HKU LSR,” Parker said. “We are very proud that we got the lion’s share of the money.”Other universities that benefit from the fund include the Chinese University of Hong Kong (CUHK), the Hong Kong University of Science and Technology, the Hong Kong City University and the Hong Kong Polytechnic University, which specialises in space engineering and has developed instruments for China’s Chang’e-3, Chang’e-4, andChang’e-6lunar exploration missions, as well as analysing lunar samples from the Chang’e-5 and Chang’e-6 missions – the latter launched in May 2024.Hong Kong is also participating in Chang’e-7, which is going to the moon in November 2026.Parker explained: Chang’e-7 “will land on the Moon in the south polar region near the Shackleton crater, and there’s asmall wide-field telescope on boarddesigned by HKU LSR in collaboration withILOA[International Lunar Observatory Association, Hawaii] to which the international mission was awarded.”Hong Kong also has an advantage in being more transparent and trusted on certain technologies than China, at least for now.“We got that mission because the Americans would not allow Canada to export the sensing camera [to China], even though it is a Canadian company,” Parker said, referring to US export controls on surveillance technologies, including equipment that may have US components, sold to China.“That gave the opportunity for HKU LSR to come on board, and we designed a camera along with our collaborators, and Beijing Institute for Science and Mechanical Electricity (BISME) helped build it,” said Parker, noting that HKU had collaborated with BISME in the past.“It’s about a 9 million yuan (US$1.26 million) project to build and design the camera, but, of course, it takes hundreds of millions of dollars to get to the Moon, so we’ve got a free lift on Chang’e-7.”Philanthropic fundingHong Kong universities are also benefiting from major philanthropic funding, notably the Hong Kong Jockey Club charities’ HK$500 million (US$64.2 million) JC STEM Lab initiative, which supports a government scheme to nurture STEM talent in Hong Kong, develop science research and technologies with social impact, and expand the city’s innovation and technology talent pool.It included funding to set up the JC STEM lab of Astronomical Instrumentation in 2023 at CUHK. CUHK currently employs the largest number of astronomers in the city, with astronomy groups seeking to carve out a niche in designing and developing innovative astronomical instruments.The JC lab, headed by CUHK professor of physics Li Huabai, has built an innovative sub-millimetre polarimeter (measuring light polarisation) camera, known as ROGer, or Remote Observing from Greenland, which can more effectively cancel out sky noise.The main goal is to study star formation, using ROGer in collaboration with theGreenland telescopeto gather data about magnetic fields in stellar birth, he said.The Greenland telescope is jointly operated by the US Centre for Astrophysics at the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory and Harvard, in collaboration with the Academica Sinica Institute of Astronomy and Astrophysics, Taiwan.Sub-millimetre cameras are crucial instruments for investigating open questions such as fragmentation of galaxies into dense clouds and stars, he said.ROGer “will put the Hong Kong flag on the world map of astronomy” within two to three years, Li told the media in November. Researchers previously had to apply for telescope time from other countries with sub-millimetre cameras located in remote areas like the South Pole, Chile, and Hawaii.“This is the first sub-millimetre camera of China and the very first professional astronomical instrumentation locally brewed by Hong Kong. Even before we have finished [building it], it is attracting quite a bit of attention with the general public and high school students,” Li said, noting this summer China just started to build a new 15-metre sub-millimetre telescope on the Qinghai plateau in Southwestern China.Innovative instrumentationAnother group at the JC STEM lab is aiming to map the interstellar region in the Milky Way and nearby galaxies using spectroscopy, under its AMASE-D (Affordable Multiple Aperture Spectroscopy Explorer) project.The project team has developed an innovative method to map galaxies in the intermediate region 1-55 light years away from Earth, using spectroscopy, or analysis of light. The project uses optical fibres to achieve integral field spectroscopy that obtains a spectrum for every point in an image.Project head, CUHK’s Professor Yan Renbin, who returned to Hong Kong in 2021 after working for 20 years in Canada and the US, including UC Berkeley, noted that current instruments either observe very close objects in detail – “you see only a tiny portion of it. You cannot see the whole picture very quickly. You see the trees, but not the forest” – or observe objects very far away, beyond 55 light years – “so we see the forest, but not the trees”.“There is a gap,” he explained. “A few light years away, we don’t have much data, but that scale is the critical scale for the feedback of star formation. Our instruments can sample this intermediate physical scale very efficiently and much better than previous generations of instruments.”It would take too long for normal spectroscopy to sample every little point, he said. However, spectroscopic instruments being developed at CUHK can be attached to small telescopes and can cover a very large field of view compared to other instruments.At the same time, “the hardware cost is relatively cheap – about 10 times cheaper than other advanced spectrographs, but our light-scattering power is only one-fifth of other spectroscopy light-scattering power, so in terms of cost per photon, we are twice as cost-effective. And with the same … budget, we can do a survey twice as fast as other people can do.”He noted it is difficult to do huge sky surveys with limited funding. They normally require very large telescopes and large, expensive structure labs to analyse the light. “So we are trying to push the limit of how much we can see in the universe.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Bill passed that could jail lecturers for sexual harassmentGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNIGERIAStudents want professor sacked over alleged sexual harassmentNIGERIAReport reveals widespread sexual harassment in universitiesKYRGYZSTANResearch reveals scale of sexual harassment in universitiesRWANDAFemale lecturer speaks out about alleged sexual harassmentZIMBABWESexual harassment to be criminalised to protect studentsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNIGERIABill passed that could jail lecturers for sexual harassmentHussain Wahab14 November 2025TweetNigeria’s Senate has approved a law imposing up to 14 years’ imprisonment for lecturers found guilty of sexually harassing students in tertiary education institutions, a shift in the country’s decades-long struggle against sex-for-grades scandals.The Sexual Harassment of Students (Prevention and Prohibition)Billwas approved earlier in November during the senate plenary after it was passed by the Nigerian House of Representatives earlier. It aims to tackle sexual exploitation and abuse of authority in the country’s higher education system, where sex-for-grades scandals have long raised concerns.It mandates tertiary institutions to establish an Independent Sexual Harassment Prohibition Committee with investigative powers, while institutional heads who ignore verified complaints risk fines of up to NGN5 million (about US$3,480).During the session, Oyelola Ashiru, the deputy senate leader, who presented the bill, said it provided a clear framework to protect students and uphold the sanctity of the academic environment.New sense of protection for studentsThe legislation has been widely welcomed by academics, experts and student leaders as a turning point for higher education in Africa’s most populous country.Dr Asiya Imam, a senior lecturer at Usmanu Danfodiyo University Sokoto, toldUniversity World Newsthat the law will help redress entrenched power imbalances between students and lecturers.“This law will put a barrier in the lecturer-student relationship so that female students can feel safe in the knowledge that the law exists to protect them,” she said. “It will also make lecturers more professional in the discharge of their duties and restore mutual respect in the classroom. This isn’t just about punishing offenders; it’s about restoring integrity to the university system and ensuring every student can learn without fear,” Imam said.Imam, who has long been involved in women’s support initiatives at her university, said the legislation would empower victims to report incidents without fear. “In my institution, we have a female support group that helps students report harassment and ensures they don’t feel isolated. With this new law, such initiatives will gain even more legitimacy,” she added.‘It sends a strong message’Similarly, Professor Asiya Bagudo, another academic voice from northern Nigeria, said the 14-year jail term represents a clear warning to would-be offenders and a crucial shift from past practices where such cases were often handled quietly.“For a long time, many female students have felt unsafe because of inappropriate advances or threats from those who were supposed to be their mentors. Knowing there’s now a serious consequence sends a strong message that such behaviour will no longer be ignored,” she said.Bagudo emphasised that, while legislation was critical, it had to be complemented by institutional reforms. “Laws alone are not enough. Universities must build environments that support victims – confidential reporting systems, free counselling services, and independent panels that treat cases with sensitivity and fairness.”She added that promoting women’s leadership in academia could play a long-term role in prevention. “When more women occupy decision-making positions, they bring empathy and balance. Empowerment begins with opportunity, and when young women lead, they inspire others to do the same,” she toldUniversity World News.Ahmad Murtadha, a law lecturer at the College of Arabic and Islamic Legal Studies, Ilorin, said: “Law is considered a social instrument ... guiding the interactions of people.” He supports the law, citing religious and moral grounds. He said having sex with someone who is not your wife is prohibited, even in the Penal Code of Northern Nigeria. Regarding consent, he emphasised: “It doesn’t matter; if you’re not legally married, sexual intercourse is not allowed.”Murtadho acknowledged that some lecturers exploit their position, but also advised female students to dress modestly, saying they should “be decent in their dress as some do purposely target or arouse males”.He stressed the need for law enforcement agencies to implement the law, noting that many laws in Nigeria exist mostly on paper and lack implementation.“Issues of sexual harassment are extremely difficult to establish because of the secrecy involved,” he said, adding that some cases may be “set-ups”. However, despite enforcement challenges, Murtadho believes the law’s existence will deter male lecturers from illicit acts. “The making of this law will go a long way in preventing such acts; it will serve as a deterrent.”Students’ leaders: enforcement and awarenessStudent leaders across Nigerian campuses are optimistic, although they stress that the success of the law will depend on strict enforcement and cultural change.Jeariogbe Islamiyyah Adedoyin, a final-year geology student and student vice president of the school of physical sciences at the Federal University of Technology, Minna, described the law as a powerful step in the right direction.“It will make female students feel more protected and encourage victims to speak up. But universities still need to create supportive environments: awareness campaigns, confidential reporting systems and counselling can make a huge difference,” she toldUniversity World News.Adedoyin added that student leaders have a role to play in sustaining awareness. “We must keep conversations about respect and consent alive. I also encourage female students to take up leadership positions; when we lead, we help shape a culture of inclusivity.”Abdulrahman Soliudeen, a final-year law student at Ahmadu Bello University, Zaria, said the law aligns with broader efforts to uphold women’s rights. “It’s a welcome development. As student leaders, we must advocate for the recognition of women’s voices and ensure equality in campus structures.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

尼日利亞通過對性騷擾的嚴厲法律 

尼日利亞參議院最近通過了一項法律，對性騷擾學生的講師處以最高14年的監禁，標誌著該國在解決性交易醜聞方面的重要進展。該法案要求高等教育機構成立獨立的性騷擾禁止委員會，並對忽視投訴的機構負責人處以高達500萬奈拉的罰款。學者及學生領袖普遍歡迎這項立法，認為它將為學生提供更安全的學習環境，並促使講師在職業行為上更加專業。成功的關鍵在於法律的嚴格執行和文化變革，以支持受害者並提高意識。這一法律改變有望在非洲高等教育系統中產生深遠影響。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: PhD programmes to be resumed, says re-elected president
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: PhD programmes to be resumed, says re-elected presidentGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAFRICA7,000 new PhD graduates in 10 years is all about innovationCAMEROONPhD admission ban: Academics call for better infrastructureCAMEROONMinister suspends PhD admissions in all state universitiesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsCAMEROONPhD programmes to be resumed, says re-elected presidentElias Ngalame14 November 2025TweetCameroon’s re-elected 93-year-old president, Paul Biya, who has ruled the country for 43 years, has pledged to chart a new path in the country’s higher education system, beginning with the resumption of PhD programmes in state universities this academic year. His plans have triggered mixed reactions among academics and students.In his address to the nation after being sworn in earlier this month, Biya promised to address burning issues plaguing the higher education system.“I have just ordered the resumption of doctoral studies in universities during this academic year. I have also decided to restart competitive exams in teachers’ training colleges. I would like to extend a hand to everyone, especially our youth, to solve the disturbing problems of graduate unemployment,” headded.The Cameroon government had, in 2023, suspended PhD admissions and halted exams to enter teachers’ training colleges on the grounds of putting order into the sector.‘Results-oriented measures’Elaborating on the president’s pledge, Jacques Fame Ndongo, the minister of higher education, said Biya envisaged in his next seven-year mandate to address issues plaguing the higher education sector, such as providing the enabling environment to promote more sciences in universities with adapted infrastructure, and to encourage disabled students to enter higher education.“President Paul Biya has given instructions for results-oriented measures that will improve higher education studies in both state and private institutions in the country. We are already implementing these orders,” the minister said on 9 November over state televisionCRTV.He said more attention will be placed on accelerating infrastructural projects, especially in the new state universities created in 2023, with building student hostels, staff houses, laboratories and information technology service centres and by providing financial support to young graduates with entrepreneurial projects for self-employment.The ministry also announced the allocation of CFA420 million (about US$745,000) to support about 22 young state university graduate entrepreneurs with innovative incubation projects in various sectors such as agro-industry, digital technology and construction.“The allocated amounts range from CFA5 million to CFA45 million, depending on the project’s nature and potential. By empowering young university student entrepreneurs, the government aims to promote economic growth, job creation and national development, aligning with the country’s Vision 2035 and the National Industrial Development Plan,” a ministerial news release onFacebooksaid.Mixed response from the sectorSome vice-chancellors and rectors of the 11 state universities, attending a meeting in Yaoundé on 12 November to strategise on the re-launch of doctoral programmes, have hailed the decision as a booster to research and innovation.“This is very good news for us. Our PhD students have been stranded for two years since the suspension. This has been very demotivating, even to the lecturers and supervisors,” Professor Magloire Ondoa, the rector of the University of Douala, toldUniversity World News.However, university heads said they have expressed the need for adapted infrastructure and support funds to facilitate research for these PhD students for quality results.“The government needs to invest more in equipping university laboratories to facilitate research for students – especially in the sciences. These are some of the proposals we have made to the minister of higher education,” Ondoa said at the close of the meeting.Other university heads who have acknowledged the need for more PhD holders in the country, called for improvements in teaching and learning.“University lecturers are supposed to be better skilled and equipped to prepare students as job creators. This requires skills in transformative education,” Professor Theresia Nkuo Akenji, vice-chancellor of the University of Bamenda, told journalists at the close of the meeting in Yaoundé.For university student leaders, the pledge signalled a good start to the academic year, although some student concerns remained.“It is good news for those preparing for this terminal degree. But the general student body will be more happy to see reforms that touch all students, like the renovating of old structures, improving on the students’ welfare with good toilets, equipped libraries, and university hospitals and others,” Clarkson Forsab Kedju Mbahwe, the faculty president of social and management sciences, University of Buea, toldUniversity World News.PhD supervision as money-making ventureBut Professor Julius Ngoh, the registrar at the GreenSprings Digital University Institute, a private university in Yaoundé, and former registrar of the University of Buea, a public institution, noted that the announcement should be taken with a lot of caution.“The announcement of the resumption of PhD programmes in state universities surely excited potential PhD candidates and those whose research projects have been put on hold or slowed down. But the taste of the pudding is in the eating,” he toldUniversity World News.He expressed concern that the multitude of problems which led to the suspension of PhD admissions in the first place have yet to be solved.“Where will the qualified professors and associate professors come from to handle the piled up number of candidates for PhDs? The government should eradicate the disease common in some disciplines where some professors and associate professors think they can supervise anything and everything, regardless of their specialisation and research interest principally, because of the expected financial gains,” he advised.He said the government should ensure that supervisors did not continue looking at the academic exercise, PhD supervision, as mainly a money-making business at the expense of academic quality and standard.“Our universities need the minimum adequate and equipped libraries and laboratories, and also to ensure that administrative bottlenecks don’t hold back students for years after they have submitted their theses with the required pre-defence report from their supervisors. The government and universities should also encourage the public and private sectors to promote research and exploit the results of PhD findings,” Ngoh recommended.Other academics said quality PhD studies were not the responsibility of the university institutions alone. They required interrelated efforts for good results.“Our students need the enabling environment with good internet connections, research funding support by both the government and the private sector, non-interference of politics, and all the needed elements as obtained elsewhere. Many of our potential PhD students are migrating because they lack access to advanced research opportunities at home and, once abroad, they find better resources and professional environments that encourage them to stay permanently,” Professor Jean Bahebeck of the University of Yaoundé I toldUniversity World News.Students also expressed the need for the government and universities to lighten the financial burden of potential PhD candidates.“The financial payments to the supervisors and members of the jury should be the exclusive responsibility of the university concerned and they should be paid promptly; students should not be asked to pre-finance their defence,” Aminateh Nkemngu, a PhD applicant in the journalism department of the University of Buea, toldUniversity World News.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Young climate scientists push Africa-led action at COP30
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Young climate scientists push Africa-led action at COP30GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCAMEROONYoung forestry researchers step out into green classroomAFRICAScholars forge ‘African blueprint’ ahead of climate talksAFRICAHE, research key to developing home-grown climate solutionsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFRICAYoung climate scientists push Africa-led action at COP30Elias Ngalame10 November 2025TweetAs COP30 opened with a precursor event on 6 November 2025 in Belém, Brazil, with the Summit of Heads of State and Government, young African scientists, researchers and youth organisations prepared to join world leaders and other actors to drive innovative Africa-led, locally driven climate change solutions in the global fight against the climate crisis.This year’s United Nations (UN) Climate Change Conference (COP30), runs from 10-21 November under the broad topic, ‘Collective efforts to address climate change’.The opening session saw a funding boost that will help African researchers advance climate action over the next five years, with donor countries backing the Belém Call to Action for the Congo Basin Forests initiative. The initiative is led by France and Gabon, and supported by Germany, Norway, Belgium and Britain, according to astatementthe Congo Basin Forest Partnership released on 6 November.“Donors are committed to raising more than US$2.5 billion over the next five years, in addition to the domestic resources that countries of central Africa will mobilise to support the protection and sustainable management of the Congo Basin forests,” the release reads.The signatories also pledged support for reducing deforestation through technology, training and strategic partnerships, a move that environmental researchers say will help them further their research activity in the field.Call for prompt action“The financial support for training and partnership announced by donor organisations at COP30 is a big booster to research efforts and training of experts to save the Congo Basin forests,” Dr Abdon Awono, a senior forest scientist at CIFOR-ICRAF (Centre for International Forestry Research and World Agroforestry) Cameroon, toldUniversity World News.The announced financial support for conserving the Congo Basin forests aligns with UN Secretary-General António Guterres’ call for prompt action to alleviate the climate crisis, especially for vulnerable communities in Africa.“It’s no longer time for negotiation. It’s time for implementation. Developing countries must leave Belém equipped with a climate justice package that delivers equity, dignity and opportunity. That means a concrete plan to close the adaptation finance gap,” Guterres said during the opening of the conference.Young Africans passionately committedYoung African researchers and scientists at COP30 say they are set to play their part as climate actors. Their efforts to drive innovative Africa-led, locally driven climate change are unified under calls for transparent climate financing, the use of science-based adaptation strategies, and the integration of local knowledge into international policy in line with resolutions reached at the Second Africa Climate Summit (ACS2) in September in Addis Ababa, Ethiopia, they attest.“African youths and researchers at the Africa Climate Summit took the commitment to join forces to forge Africa-led solutions to climate change. We are here at COP30 to, not only share knowledge, but also to learn more to improve our skills and better contribute to the global fight against the climate crisis,” Geofrey Sunday, founder and executive coordinator of Support Humanity Cameroon, toldUniversity World News.A commitment was also made to fund 1,000 African climate innovation or adaptation projects each year, including infrastructure for young scientists and researchers and training programmes for university students. This means that multiple stakeholders will be raising US$50 billion annually from now until 2030 to make this possible.Media reports say that, in line with ACS2’s youth empowerment spirit, multiple actions across different countries were undertaken to prepare for COP30.Promoting youth leadershipIn Ethiopia, more than 200 young volunteers, including students, researchers and scientists, took part in areforestation effortin Addis Ababa. This led to the planting of 300 fruit tree saplings on the campus of the Addis Ababa Science and Technology University.The activity was driven by the COP30 Youth Task Force, an initiative that promotes youth leadership in the global climate agenda. A similar training and tree-planting project in Cameroon was organised by the African Forest Forum in partnership with the Swedish University of Agricultural Sciences and the Kenya Forestry Research Institute to better equip young African forest researchers and students in forestry institutions with the skills and knowledge to drive the green transition and sustainable forest management on the continent.The knowledge equipping project, dubbed ‘AfricanYouth4Forests’, represents a dynamic intersection between Africa’s verdant forests and its youth, the organisers said.One of the key trainers, environment and conservation expert Dr Peter Mbile, toldUniversity World Newsthat the training discussions continued online, as many of the youths are taking part and exchanging the knowledge they have acquired at COP30.“Most of the acquired knowledge activities will be presented during side events as country NDCs (national developed contributions) in the fight against climate change,” he said.The young scientists, researchers, students and activists will highlight critical climate issues, such as the importance of indigenous land stewardship and nature-based solutions, and will display their talents in greenhouse innovations, climate-smart agroforestry and ecotourism, Mbile said.Elevating young scientistsEnvironment experts say COP30 provides multiple opportunities for young researchers and students.“These young researchers and students participate in side events and panel discussions to learn, share experiences and meet people to enhance their work,” said Dr Linus Mofor, senior environmental affairs officer for energy, infrastructure and climate change at the UN Economic Commission for Africa.At the heart of the COP30 Presidency agenda are efforts to strengthen social participation, promote intergenerational equity, and train young scientists to empower and elevate them to better play their role in advancing practical solutions to adapt to and mitigate the climate crisis.“Young people are at the heart of the COP30 Action Agenda and at the forefront in the fight for climate justice,” Mahmoud Ali Youssouf, chair of the African Union Commission, noted at the opening of the ACS2. He emphasised the need for continuous training and skills upgrading of young scientists and researchers to foster Africa-led innovative climate solutions.It is in this spirit that a research training school was launched in Djibouti on 28 October,Ecofin Agencyreported.This is the first research training school in Africa that gathered 15 young researchers from 10 countries on the continent ahead of COP30.The young researchers are receiving a month of online training by university experts in Djibouti to conduct fieldwork in the Grand and Petit Bara regions to study local socio-productive systems.The organisers said the initiative is part of efforts by the French Institute of Research for Development and its partners, the Centre for Studies and Research of Djibouti and the Doctoral Alliance for Climate Adaptation, to establish research observatories and networks across Africa, according to theEcofin Agencyreport.According to the African Development Bank, training qualified young researchers and scientists in Africa is to create scientific job opportunities, promote locally tailored solutions, and drive the region’s economic development.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal
