https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Micro-credentials: From lifelong learning to lifelong recognition
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Micro-credentials: From lifelong learning to lifelong recognitionGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsHow do we recognise micro-credentials in the modern world? Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksEUROPEEU pushes micro-credentials but recognition lags behindGLOBALRecognition of credentials – The next big challengeGLOBALEnhancing the learning ecosystem with micro-credentialsASIATraditional HE opens up to the micro-credential revolutionCANADAFramework proposes trademark for micro-credentials protectionIRELANDMicroCreds drive flexible learning in innovative IrelandInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyGLOBALMicro-credentials: From lifelong learning to lifelong recognitionKaren MacGregor28 November 2025TweetToday’s story about micro-credentials is really about their recognition, says Simone Ravaioli, a leading global credentials expert. “If we spent the last 30 years working on learning, the next 30-plus years will be focused on recognition. In a sense, the narrative is shifting from lifelong learning to lifelong recognition.”Ravaioli was speaking at aUniversity World News-ABET webinar on “Recognising micro-credentials: Lessons from the world’s best”, held on 20 November. It was the fourth in a series of webinars that are combining high-level thinking with hands-on advice that will help people to navigate higher education in a time of change and uncertainty.The other expert speakers were: Dr Borhene Chakroun, director of the Division for Policies and Lifelong Learning Systems at UNESCO; Dora Smith, senior director of Future Workforce and Academic Strategy at Siemens Digital Industries Software; and Jessica Silwick, chief financial officer and chief operating officer at non-profit quality assurance and accreditation agency ABET.Micro-credentials create both benefits and challenges across policy, technology and practice, argued Ravaioli, director of global academic innovation at edtech company Instructure.He also co-chairs the Verifiable Credentials for Education Task Force for W3C (the World Wide Web Consortium) – the international non-profit that develops open standards to keep the web accessible and interoperable – and chairs an expert advisory group of Credential Engine, a non-profit that runs a global credential registry and develops open data standards.Ravaioli is intrigued by a global trend of “policy as data”, with countries developing frameworks that specify the data elements needed to describe micro-credentials clearly. Australia, Europe, Latin America, Africa and others are creating such policies.Credentials policies are being written in the form of structured data requirements rather than just guidelines. Every credential must include standardised information – such as learning outcomes, level, workload, assessment, recognition and quality assurance. By giving consistent descriptors, policies help align technology and make micro-credentials more transparent and comparable.Ravaioli argued the need to go beyond the definition of micro-credentials, “down into the semantic layer where the meaning is, the data elements particularly. We have guidance from policies, and the technology and open standards are ready.”If countries and institutions use common data standards and policies, micro-credentials will become easier to recognise and trust across systems, making international recognition much smoother.The webinarThe webinar followed a special report on “Micro-credentials: Shaping higher education around the world”, published under our partnership with ABET on 2 November, in whichUniversity World Newsexplored how micro-credentials are driving changes to learning, qualifications and collaboration with industry.Moderated by Editor-in-Chief Brendan O’Malley, the webinar was attended by more than 260 people and is available towatch on YouTube.The development of micro-credentials has been phenomenal in recent years. Among the reasons are rising demand among employers and learners seeking skills for a rapidly evolving world of work, the development of massive online learning platforms and a pandemic that accelerated digital and short-course learning.Today, recognition of micro-credentials is the new frontier, as universities, employers, countries and regions work to make micro-credentials matter.Looking to the futureRavaioli’s comments were made in response to a question asking the experts what actions universities, employers, countries or regions might take to ensure that micro-credentials achieve value, recognition, and learner and employer trust over the next decade?Dr Borhene Chakroun – the director of policies and lifelong learning systems at UNESCO, who has led policy reviews and global initiatives on skills development and lifelong learning – highlighted key challenges and opportunities going forward.First, he pointed out that more than 100 countries have ratified UNESCO regional or global conventions that are advancing better recognition of learning, learning outcomes, partial studies and micro-credentials.Second, “multilateralism is ensuring that we have common understanding, common definitions, common principles of quality assurance across the board”.UNESCO has a global network of information centres – national and regional bodies that collect, verify and exchange qualifications information – that is critical to improving understanding of credentials and ensuring fair, transparent and reliable recognition across borders.Third is the major contribution of UNESCO’s work in mapping the global credentials landscape, developing tools and methodological resources. “And we are developing guidance on the recognition of micro-credentials through our work in Latin America and the Caribbean.”Chakroun argued that the future landscape for micro-credentials should follow four principles: portability, interoperability, stackability and accountability, or PISA – a play on the OECD student test.These elements are essential for creating a coherent and shared global ecosystem, with UNESCO helping to drive international alignment and implementation.Both Chakroun and Dora Smith – who leads Siemens’ global future workforce and academic strategy, including partnerships with thousands of universities worldwide – pointed to the elephant in the room: artificial intelligence. AI could disrupt quality assurance systems, and “universities must be prepared”, Chakroun warned.Partnerships are keyGoing forward, said Smith: “First and foremost, you have to have a commitment to quality. If you do, you should be pursuing recognition in some form.” Recognition will also help clear pathways to credential ‘stackability’ and learning towards degree, or post-degree, qualifications.On 25 September, ABET announced the launch of a Credential Recognition System and the recognition of 16 micro-credentials developed by Siemens; the University of Colorado, Boulder; Purdue University; and the American Society of Civil Engineers.The United States-based quality assurance agency in the STEM fields currently accredits 4,773 degree programmes that graduate more than 200,000 students annually from 930 institutions worldwideFor Smith, in the increasingly formalised world of micro-credentials, partnerships will be key. “We didn’t go at this alone. We developed our first ABET-recognised micro-credential in collaboration with a university, and there are now more schools we’re starting to develop credentials with. Both sides learnt so much.“Now we have more hard work, integrating recognised credentials in a company’s learning and development programmes. These aren’t standalone extracurricular activities.”What can be done to integrate micro-credentials in university settings to drive curriculum change? Smith wondered. “It’s what we’re doing today – coming together and sharing lessons we’re learning. Everything continues to evolve so fast. We’ve got to create a blueprint so that many more can repeat this and reach a much wider set of learners.”Jessica Silwick, the ABET CEO who has helped guide the agency’s expansion from traditional degree accreditation into new areas, agreed that partnerships will be critical.For ABET, that means forging partnerships in industry and particularly with universities, “so that they understand that in the coming years, to ensure long-term value and recognition, there needs to be a shift from the credentialised course as a product to credentials as an outcome of design.“Universities will increasingly design micro-credentials backwards from either learner or workforce needs,” Silwick said.“ABET will continue to create long-term value and recognition in the credentials space because it’s essential, not only for lifelong learning but also for providing the individuals we’re going to need in very specific careers to drive global society forward.”The first batch of recognised micro-credentials is the beginning of a long learning process. “We will continue to evolve,” said Silwick. “We think our processes are nimble enough for a rapidly evolving landscape.”The evaluation is fully virtual, recognition application windows are opened four times a year, and the process lasts about four months – against years for ABET programme accreditation.For their part, professional and industry organisations will need to develop “crosswalks” that connect micro-credentials to professional licensing, continuing education and workforce apprenticeship models, said Silwick.Additionally, public policies are needed to support learners and the recognition of credentials, “so that individuals can rest assured that they’re investing their time and money in a credential that will create real value and mobility for them”.What’s driving micro-credentials?Borhene Chakroun shared a global perspective on some of the forces driving the micro-credentials movement, focusing on three areas.First is the global sustainability agenda, related to the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and particularly SDG4: Quality education.“Micro-credentials are a way for us to advance flexible learning opportunities, to have a learner-centred approach, and to link education and training, and higher education, with SDGs and the sustainable development agenda.”Second, there are forces – such as demographic pressures, youth unemployment, conflicts in some regions and rapid changes in technology and labour markets – that are creating mismatches between the skills employers need and the skills people have.Everyone will need to update their skills, and so education systems must become more agile. “Micro-credentials offer opportunities for much more personalised and flexible learning pathways,” Chakroun said.A third factor is more political. “There is political support for and a narrative around the importance of micro-credentials in the context of responding to challenges that the world is facing, be it learning, the skills mismatch, or the need for everybody to acquire higher new skills.”ABET’s Jessica Silwick told the webinar: “Over the past decade, we’ve watched an explosion of micro-credential certificates and non-degreed pathways in STEM, driven by upskilling, reskilling and workforce shortages, but with very uneven quality signals. Three or four years ago, the market hit a tipping point where choice outpaced trust in micro-credentials.“One of the gaps we saw in the global quality assurance landscape, which we are responding to, is fragmented or missing standards. Most countries and regions either have no dedicated quality assurance standards for micro-credentials or they’re being piloted, or just recently launched. We saw a need within society to be able to offer this quality assurance.”ABET also saw weak transparency and truth in advertising; limited external review beyond the issuing institution; lack of a third-party independent mechanism, where there were some quality assurance standards in place; an unclear link to workforce and qualification frameworks; and lack of portability and recognition across borders and sectors.Silwick said ABET is in a position to forge a clear link between workforce and qualifications frameworks because it provides quality assurance at a specific skill set level. With micro-credentials, the quality assurance is at the credential level, not the programme or the institutional level. ABET can validate a specific credential with a specific set of skills.For many employers, said Dora Smith of Siemens, micro-credentials have become an “essential currency” in the skills economy. “There is an ongoing, compelling and urgent need to bridge the widening gap between academic preparation and the evolving demands of industry.”Employers see micro-credentials as game changers. “They provide clear, verifiable indicators of specific skills. We think they’re crucial for building the robust talent pipeline we need to keep innovating and growing. We’re leaning into micro-credentials as on-ramps and off-ramps to new education and career pathways.”When co-developing an ABET-recognised micro-credential around the circular economy, the University of Colorado, Boulder, wanted to ensure it could be credit-bearing for students.For Siemens: “Our overall strategy is to proactively shape the future of work by addressing a decade-long challenge around both the quantity and quality of engineering graduates.”Micro-credentials do indeed matter, she said: “We’re at this intersection when companies are shifting rapidly to skills-based hiring.” At the same time: “Academia is increasingly looking to integrate industry and third-party validated credentials – that is new."The pace of digital transformation means that longer cycles of education simply cannot keep up. “Micro-credentials offer a way to evolve curricula quickly, and increasingly institutions see that industry can help in developing micro-credentials by bringing in subject matter expertise. This brings hope that the future workforce can stay ahead and not behind.”Three key decisionsFor Simone Ravaioli, the world of micro-credentials faces three decisions, all of them about common data language.First, micro-credentials must be built on open data standards to make them meaningful. If organisations create their own closed standards, credentials cannot be compared and “are just souvenirs. This first big decision speaks to the meaning-making layer of credentials – their expressiveness and the content they carry.”The second decision concerns anchoring credentials into existing frameworks. “This is where we can help the comparability of credentials,” said Ravaioli. Both qualification and competence frameworks provide opportunities to make credentials understandable and transparent, and therefore portable.“The third decision would be around designing verifiability from the get-go. Verification becomes the new accreditation.” If a credential can be verified through a trustworthy digital infrastructure, “recognition is a relatively low-friction decision.”Micro-credentials are about understanding and trust, Ravaioli argued.On understanding, he noted the need to distinguish digital credentials from micro-credentials. “A digital credential is a token of recognition of learning that you get at the end of a learning experience, whereas the micro-credential is the whole thing – the design, the delivery, the assessment, and the recognition.”Trust can break down into two main questions an employer might ask. One is, can I trust the signal? And the other is, does it have useful information for me?Digital credentials provide information on provenance, security, the issuing body, alignment to qualification frameworks, skills learnt and so on. They now also allow evidence artefacts – proof of learning such as projects, videos, designs or assessments – to be attached.“If we think of skills as currency, adding evidence adds liquidity to that currency. It makes it more portable.”Credential mobility has been a major challenge. In academia, quality is “at rest”, documented through assurance frameworks. “But it’s when credentials begin to move that value kicks in. The world of work understands value in motion,” Ravaioli said.In the future, people won’t stop asking where someone studied, “but we’ll start asking more how far their skills travel”. For this, it is essential to connect learning management systems, credentialing platforms and HR systems and to use common data standards.Ravaioli also emphasised ‘stackability’ – micro-credentials should never be one-offs or dead ends but part of a learning pathway. Again, academia and employers think differently.Universities see stacking as building towards a degree; employers view it as adding skills on top of an existing qualification. “So there is no question about the ‘degree’ going, by the way.”Email Karen MacGregor at macgregor.karen@gmail.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackISRAEL-EUROPEAcademic boycotts are intensifying against Israel – ReportWagdy Sawahel28 November 2025TweetThe number of academic boycott cases against Israeli universities and researchers has doubled since March this year, and the increase, particularly from Europe, shows no signs of abating in response to the latest ceasefire in the war in Gaza declared in October, according to a new Israeli report.The publication,The Fight Against the Academic Boycott, prepared by the Academic Boycott of Israel Monitoring Team, created by the Committee of University Presidents in Tel Aviv (VERH), reveals that reports of academic boycotts against Israeli institutions, including private reports by Israeli researchers, have now risen to 1,000 – twice the number of reports in March, which totalled around 500.The findings of the report, written in Hebrew, were published byThe Marker, the economic version of the Hebrew newspaperHaaretz.According to the report, the current state of the academic boycott reflects “a worrying academic-political trend that begins with the exclusion of individual researchers, expands to a boycott at the institutional level, moves on to a boycott of activities within a professional academic association, and even reaches large-scale international programmes”.Ceasefire violationsIt notes that “contrary to predications and hopes”, the end of the fighting that attended the ceasefire and the multi-stage process led by the United States have not led to a halt in boycott cases in Europe.Meanwhile, Agnès Callamard, secretary general of Amnesty International,warned this weekthat the ceasefire in Gaza “risks creating a dangerous illusion that life in Gaza is returning to normal.But while Israeli authorities and forces have reduced the scale of their attacks and allowed limited amounts of humanitarian aid into Gaza, the world must not be fooled. Israel's genocide is not over,” she said.The warning comes against the backdrop of Amnesty figures that show that 374 Palestinians, including 136 children, have been killed in Israeli attacks since the ceasefire in Gaza on 10 October in clearviolation of the ceasefire agreement.In response to these violations, United Nations experts have urged the international community to step up pressure to make the ceasefire effective and save lives.The Israeli monitoring group report, however, argues: “It seems that the negative image of Israel in Europe has become so entrenched that political moves alone are not enough to change the existing consciousness … So far, the cessation of hostilities does not seem to be leading to a decrease in the intensity of the boycott and may even be the opposite.”Spike in ItalyPublished on 24 November, it puts the number of boycott cases reported thus far at around 1,000 – up from 500 in March, with a quarter of all reports being made in the last three months alone and concentrated in Western Europe.In Italy there has been “a significant increase” in the number of boycott reports. “The boycott discourse in Italy has risen to the top of the agenda around the recent flotilla to Gaza, in which two Italian members of parliament participated.”It says that 57% of cases involve individual researchers (mainly being excluded from research groups), 22% are institutional severances, 7% are from professional associations, and 14% relate to halted student exchanges or postdoctoral partnerships.The year 2025 also saw a drop in research grants awarded to Israeli scholars by the EU’s Horizon Europe fund, the main source of scientific research financing for Israel, the report says.About 20 Israeli partnerships are currently excluded through boycotts from Horizon-related projects, mainly in Spain, Belgium and Italy, according to the report. The report also points to “quiet” boycotts that continue to take place in the background.On its website, Academics for Palestine, an organisation that supports Palestinian higher education and campaigns for an academic boycott of Israel, puts at 51 the number of universities worldwide to have cut ties with Israeli institutions, as of the middle of June 2025.‘Dangerous isolation’The Academic Boycott of Israel Monitoring Team report argues that various groups are working harder to deepen the pressure on Israel, even after the fighting has ended. “For many, the ceasefire is seen as only a first step and not as the end of the conflict,” it notes.It warns that the expanding forms of academic boycott could push Israeli higher education into “dangerous isolation that poses a real strategic threat to its international standing”.The report identifies other potential consequences, including a drop in EU Horizon Europe research grants for Israeli scholars, exclusion from international cooperation projects, and a breakdown in collaborative programmes.It estimates that the “boycott phenomenon” will accompany Israeli academia for a long time to come and will fade only as a result of “profound regional and geopolitical changes”.The report makes several recommendations, including the development of a long-term strategy focused on strengthening collaborations with those institutions and colleagues in Europe that are still supportive of Israel and continuously investing in public diplomacy and establishing outreach systems.It suggests the establishment of a forum for coordination between academic institutions and relevant government ministries to manage the campaign against the boycotts and establish a unified policy. It calls for more student and academic exchange programmes and scholarships for young researchers to promote academic collaborations.The report also calls for the formulation of a dedicated strategic campaign to strengthen Israel's academic presence in the international arena.‘Stop violating the law’Neve Gordon, professor of international law and human rights at Queen Mary University of London, toldUniversity World Newsthe boycott of Israeli academia was not “a principle, but a strategy deployed until Israel stops violating international law and the academic freedom of Palestinians living under its control and until the universities stop supporting the military and security complexes that carry out these violations”.“One would have expected they [Israeli universities] would stand firm for the academic freedom of colleagues in Bir Zeit and Al Najah universities.“As it stands, they are fighting for the freedom of the privileged and unwilling to even acknowledge the violations carried out on a daily basis against Palestinian academics,” he said.Professor David Harel, president of the Israel Academy of Sciences and Humanities and a professor in the Department of Computer Science and Applied Mathematics at the Weizmann Institute of Science in Israel, toldUniversity World News: “Without directly relating to [the] report, I have repeatedly voiced my opinion that the main reason for the boycotts against Israeli science is the war in Gaza and the horrendous results thereof, despite it coming in the wake of the terrible Hamas massacre of October 7.”On 17 July this year, Harel issued anopen lettercalling on the Israeli government to protect civilian lives in the Gaza Strip, to alleviate human suffering there, and to reinstate basic health and life infrastructures.“The incidents of boycotting are not likely to disappear so fast, not unless Israel leaves Gaza and shows honest and clear signs of working towards a peaceful resolution in the entire area,” Harel said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Now what?GERMANYInternational students’ mixed experience in GermanyGERMANYFirst-year international students drive enrolment growthGERMANY8% rise in international students puts Germany in top fourInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGERMANYSurvey tracks 6% rise in international student enrolmentMichael Gardner28 November 2025TweetOverall international student numbers in Germany are continuing to rise, while first-year student figures peaked in 2024 and 2025, according to a new survey. Dropout rates among international students are substantially lower than expected.According toWissenschaft weltoffen 2025(Science Open to the World), including doctoral students, around 402,000 students were enrolled at German universities in the 2024 to 2025 winter semester, a nearly 6% increase compared to 379,900 the previous year.The Federal Statistical Office DeStatis puts overall student numbers at 2.87 million for the last winter semester, compared to 2.86 million in the 2023 to 2024 winter semester. The report further notes that first-year international students peaked at 116,600 in the 2024 and 2025 winter semester.India is leading country of originPublished jointly by the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD) and the German Centre for Higher Education and Research Studies (DZHW),Wissenschaft weltoffen 2025also looks at students’ countries of origin.The Asia-Pacific region accounts for 33% of international students, followed by North Africa and the Near East (19%) and Western Europe (15%). With just below 59,000 students enrolled in Germany, representing an increase of 20% compared to last year, India is now the leading country of origin, followed by China at around 38,600 students. China has seen a decline in international student numbers in Germany since the 2019 and 2020 winter semester.Most international students seeking a degree in Germany are enrolled in the engineering sciences (43%) and economics, law and social sciences (25%). International students are particularly strongly represented in masters (26%) and doctoral (28%) programmes.Based on course of studies data from DeStatis, for the first time, this year’sWissenschaft weltoffenpresents detailed evaluations of international students dropping out of programmes in Germany. They reflect that the dropout rate among international students is only slightly higher than that for German students, contrasting sharply with previous estimates.Relating to the first three semesters of study, the dropout rate among international students of the 2020 cohort was at 16% for bachelor programmes and 9% for masters programmes, compared to 13% and 6%, respectively, among German students.Relating to 2022, around 138,000 German students were enrolled at institutions abroad, representing a moderate increase compared to the pre-COVID level of 2019 at 137,900. The most popular host countries were Austria (around 37,800), the Netherlands (22,600), Switzerland (12,500) and the United Kingdom (9,600). The figures for the first three countries saw an increase since 2019, whereas the UK numbers fell by around 32%.Key to science and innovation“The more than 400,000 international students and postgraduates bear out Germany’s status as the most important non-English-speaking host country,” said DAAD President Joybrato Mukherjee, adding that the new, low dropout rates are a positive signal.Mukherjee notes that given the country’s lack of skilled labour and with an ageing population, international students staying on after graduating are “key to Germany as a location for science and innovation”.DZHW scientific director Marcus Beiner emphasised the importance of structural developments backing the internationalisation of German higher education.“For example, numbers of international staff in higher education management have strongly increased over the last few years,” Beiner pointed out.“The vast majority of universities in Germany now have an international office. And nearly all institutions are now running at least one study programme in English. International collaborative programmes are on the increase, too,” he noted.Michael Gardner can be reached at michael.gardner@uw-news.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Global|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Fixed levy on foreign tuition fees gets lukewarm welcome
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Fixed levy on foreign tuition fees gets lukewarm welcomeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED KINGDOMTuition fees to rise in Labour bid to fix higher educationUNITED KINGDOMEnglish universities lose international student levy fightUNITED KINGDOMAlarms as high-tariff universities grab UK student marketUNITED KINGDOMWarnings on ‘unsustainable’ international tuition fee hikesUNITED KINGDOMBudget receives mixed response from science, HE sectorsUNITED KINGDOMUniversity chiefs partly to blame for funding woes – ExpertsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOMFixed levy on foreign tuition fees gets lukewarm welcomeNic Mitchell28 November 2025TweetAfter months of intense lobbying against a government proposal to levy a new tax on international student fees, many university leaders in England appeared relieved that the long-anticipated Autumn Budget fixed the sum at a flat rate of £925 (US$1,200), rather than the widely mooted original idea of charging 6% on top of foreign tuition fees.Higher education institutions will have to start paying the levy from August 2028 – the start of the 2028 to 2029 academic year – in a move which the Labour government billed as a way to raise extra funds to help to subsidise higher education and skills provision in England and allow for the return of maintenance grants for poorer home students.However, the maintenance grants will be very modest, at a maximum of £1,000 per year for students from households with an income below £25,000 per year from 2028 to 2029. That's just above the minimum wage for a full-time worker.The full grant will only be available for those studying a narrow range of priority subjects, linked to the government's industrial strategy. The policy changes only affect universities in England, as education is a devolved matter in the UK.Meanwhile, home tuition fees for UK students studying at English universities will go up with inflation, from the current level of £9,535 to £9,700 in 2026 to 2027 and break through the £10k mark in 2027 to 2028.A softer blow for elitesDespite speculation that universities were going to be hit by a 6% tax on international student fees,asUniversity World Newsreported, and intense lobbying, which led to a front-page headline in thei-newspaperon Monday 24 November, talking about a £600 million raid on already unstable university finances, the government’s Autumn Budget that followed on the Wednesday softened the blow by announcing a flat-rate fee on foreign student fees of £925.This is better news than expected for the elite Russell Group of mainly research-intensive universities, where foreign tuition fees can easily top £30,000 per year. A 6% levy would have meant paying a tax of £1,800 per year on that kind of fee, double the amount they will now have to pay.An engineering degree at the University of Cambridge, for example, carries a tuition fee ofjust over £44,000 per yearfor international students.However, the flat-rate levy is less welcome by lower-ranked universities, where international tuition fees are much lower and the extra cost cannot be simply passed on to overseas students without hitting demand for places.At the University of Suffolk, for example, international tuition students start at around £15,000.WonkHE, which describes itself as “the home of the UK higher education debate”,conducted an analysis after the budgeton the impact of a flat fee, as opposed to a 6% levy.Exemption for first 220 studentsDespite institutions being allowed to escape paying the levy on the first 220 foreign students they recruit in a bid to protect smaller specialist institutions,WonkHEfound that the University of Suffolk would lose nearly 14% of international fee income to the levy (on 750 students) compared to the University of Cambridge, which they estimate will lose just 2.69% (on 7,315 international students).The likely impact of the levy can also be seen in the reaction from the mission groups at either end of the spectrum, with elite Russell Group chief executive Dr Tim Bradshaw, responding to the Autumn Budget, by saying: “The international student levy will have a significant impact on universities’ ability to invest in teaching, research and communities, but a flat rate fee should avoid the complexities of a percentage-based model and limit the potential for gaming the system.“We will now look to work closely with government through the consultation process to avoid unintended consequences and minimise administrative burden.”Meanwhile, Dr Brooke Storer-Church, chief executive officer of GuildHE (which represents vocational and technical higher education providers), toldUniversity World News: "While we welcome an exemption for institutions with lower numbers of international students from the international student levy, the arbitrary figure of 220 international students leaves too many of our specialist, innovative and world-leading institutions exposed to payments which function as taxes on their attractiveness and high-quality offers.“This is especially true for our arts and creative education specialist institutions.“We will be asking the government to introduce an incremental system that will avoid crippling our smaller-scale, specialist and vocational institutions at a time the government has underlined the need to protect and preserve them.”Return of maintenance grantsOn the return of maintenance grants, Storer-Church said a grant of £1,000 for the poorest students will do little to boost access, and GuildHE will be lobbying the government for “stronger financial support for those who need it most”.Professor Malcolm Press, vice-chancellor at Manchester Metropolitan University and president of Universities UK (UUK), which represents vice-chancellors and rectors at 142 universities in England, Scotland, Wales and Northern Ireland, warned that the levy on international fees would either reduce cross-subsidies that support teaching and research and/or raise international fees still further.“This has the potential to drive down international numbers, ultimately limiting our ability to support UK students,” he said.However, Press said he was “encouraged that the government has responded in part to our representations regarding the levy”, and he welcomed the reintroduction of maintenance grants for the most disadvantaged students.In an interview withTimes Higher Educationreporter Helen Packer, the UUK president said the flat-fee levy is “on balance ... probably a better outcome than many people would have anticipated”.Government modelling suggests the policy will generate £445 million in revenue in its first year of operation and not the £600 million speculated before the budget. It is expected to lead to a net loss of income of about £270 million to the sector, given some of the costs will be passed on to students.‘Stop arguing about it’Press urged the sector to stop arguing “vociferously and vehemently about the issue” because it would cause damage to the UK's appeal as a destination choice for international students.This remark caused some UK higher education experts to worry about the messaging from some university leaders.Dr Diana Beech, director of the Finsbury Institute at City St George’s University in London and former government adviser on higher education, toldUniversity World News: “Yes, I know it could have been a lot worse, but if we’re seen to have no backbone as a sector and welcome the pain, then that's an invitation for future governments (this one or the next) to walk all over us.“While we shouldn’t complain for the sake of it – and yes, I have witnessed a fair bit of that in the sector in my time – it is right we push back when policies will inflict damage on our sector and could pave the way to even worse decisions in the future.”Beech said she wouldn't call the budget “an outright win for the Russell Group” and said: “The international student levy, even at a flat-fee rate, adds significant cost pressure at a time when many institutions are already running deficits.“Although a flat charge is preferable to a percentage levy on overseas fee income for large, research-intensive universities, it still reduces flexibility to invest in world-class research and facilities.”Ongoing pressure on universitiesBeech said the chancellor's decision not to announce the levy at the despatch box (in parliament) also raises serious questions. “Why undermine one of the UK’s strongest export sectors without even gaining political credit for it – whether by presenting it as a tough stance on immigration or as a boost for disadvantaged students by reinvesting levy proceeds into maintenance grants for disadvantaged students?“Publishing the details quietly in the Treasury Red Book means the policy will simply tax legitimate, highly skilled migration under the radar, while piling further pressure on financially distressed universities.“Worse still, fixing the levy at £925 per student risks hitting institutions least able to absorb the cost, given the lack of price elasticity outside the elite end of the sector.”Meanwhile, lost in all the speculation and reaction to the actual budget, the UK government also this week reaffirmed its long-termcommitment to R&D– “doubling investment in critical technologies, such as AI and quantum, and maintaining the £22.6 billion target by 2029 and 2030”, said Beech, who added: “However, these ambitions sit alongside measures that will continue to squeeze university finances in England in the short term.”Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs atwww.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

英國固定外國學費徵稅獲冷淡反應

英國政府近期公布的秋季預算案確定對國際學生學費徵收固定稅額925英鎊（約1,200美元），而非原先提議的6%稅率。此舉旨在為高等教育提供額外資金，並重新引入對低收入家庭的生活補助金，然而補助金每年僅為1,000英鎊，且僅適用於特定科目。

此政策只影響英格蘭的高等教育機構，雖然精英大學如劍橋受益較多，但低排名大學則擔心稅負會影響國際生招生需求。專家警告，這將進一步加劇大學的財政壓力，並可能影響其教學和研究投資能力。

Tenure TrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackINDIAAl-Falah University faces multiple probes after fort blastShuriah Niazi28 November 2025TweetAl-Falah University, a private institution on a 70-acre campus in Faridabad in India’s Haryana state known for its medical college and engineering programmes, has come under intense scrutiny as multiple agencies launch investigations into its accreditation claims, financial dealings, and links to individuals involved in recent terror incidents.Fifteen people were killed and over a dozen injured when a car allegedly driven by Umar-un-Nabi, a Kashmiri doctor affiliated with Al-Falah University, exploded near Delhi’s historic Red Fort on 10 November.Anxious students and their parents have been seeking clarity about the institution’s future as investigation agencies, including India’s main counter-terrorism National Investigation Agency (NIA), are constantly visiting the university.The institution, which is near Delhi, runs a hospital and a medical college offering MBBS degrees and has been in the news for allegedly being a centre of “terrorist modules”, as described by police, including modules involving three university staff members: a pharmacologist, surgeon, and assistant professor of medicine Dr Umar, the alleged Red Fort blast perpetrator who died in the explosion.The university management has assured undergraduate medical (MBBS) students and their parents the institute will not be shut down, despite an investigation by the Enforcement Directorate (ED), which investigates financial fraud cases, a show-cause notice from the National Assessment and Accreditation Council (NAAC), and other regulatory probes.The assurance came after around 25 parents, worried about the future of their children pursuing medical and other academic programmes, visited the campus on 23 November to submit a memorandum to the university’s management.Allegations against the institutionAmong the reasons for their concerns is that the NAAC issued a show-cause notice to the university on 13 November for displaying “absolutely wrong and misleading” information – including an “A Grade” for its engineering college dating back to 2013 and accreditation for its teacher education school dating back to 2011.In its notice, NAAC said the accreditation of both the colleges of engineering and teacher education expired in 2016 and 2018, respectively, and that neither college took action to renew the NAAC assessment and accreditation (A&A) process.Two FIRs (first information reports) filed by the Delhi Police Crime Branch also accuse the university of misleading students with false or outdated accreditation claims.A faculty member toldUniversity World Newsthat university officials had responded to the NAAC’s claims concerning outdated accreditation details displayed on its website. The university described it as an “oversight” and a “design error”, noting the information had been removed.Parents of medical students are now worried the National Medical Commission (NMC) could withdraw its accreditation. The Association of Indian Universities (AIU) has alreadysuspendedthe institution.No links to perpetratorsDespite the ongoing scrutiny, university officials have insisted that academic operations, including MBBS classes, teaching hospital services, and exams, will continue without interruption, and students should not fear an imminent shutdown.Al-Falah University Vice-Chancellor Bhupinder Kaur Anand has clarified that the institution has no link to the doctors arrested in the Faridabad terror module and the Red Fort blast beyond their official roles at the university and refuted allegations that the university’s laboratories were being used to store materials that could be used for explosives.In a statement issued earlier this month, she said: “We strongly condemn and categorically deny all such false and defamatory allegations. No such chemical or material, as alleged by certain platforms, is being used, stored, or handled within the university premises.”She added: “The university laboratories are used solely and exclusively for the academic and training requirements of MBBS students and other authorised courses.”Sources say that in response to parents’ concerns, Al-Falah faculty members and Kaur Anand told them not to worry “until an official order comes”, and that Kaur Anand told students and their parents that investigating agencies were doing their work and “whatever happens will be fair”.A professor at Al-Falah, speaking on condition of anonymity, toldUniversity World News: “The university will not close. We are fully cooperating with the authorities and will ensure that the students' interests are protected.”Minority institution statusMeanwhile, the National Commission for Minority Educational Institutions (NCMEI) issued a show-cause notice to Al-Falah University on 23 November, asking why its minority status should not be revoked at a time when two of its doctors are under investigation for their role in the 10 November blast.Minority institution status confers certain administrative and admission-related privileges, for example, the right to reserve seats and manage admissions for the religious or linguistic minority community that established the institution, and some regulatory exemptions on governance and appointments.Losing that status can affect control over admissions, reservations and governance, and may invite additional regulatory scrutiny.If Al-Falah loses its minority status, it could have a profound impact on students. Many of them, who rely on government scholarships and grants to fund their studies, could lose them.However, if the university fails to provide the extensive documents demanded by NCMEI, such as the trust deed, funding details, and audited accounts, strict action is likely. A hearing in this case is scheduled for 4 December, when NCMEI will decide about launching a formal probe and-or amending or cancelling the university's minority status.According to the ED, Al-Falah University, Faridabad, falsely claimed University Grants Commission (UGC) recognition under the UGC Act, with the objective of defrauding aspirants, students, parents, guardians, stakeholders, and the general public in order to gain wrongfully and cause wrongful loss to them.The UGC has clarified that Al-Falah University is only recognised as a State Private University under Section 2(f), making UGC recognition and notices relevant, but it does not qualify for public funding under Section 12(B).The university did not respond when approached byUniversity World Newsfor an official comment on all allegations against it.Investigation into fundsThe ED is also investigating an alleged diversion of funds within the Al-Falah Group. Investigators claim that millions of rupees were given to entities linked to the family of the group's chairman, Jawad Ahmed Siddiqui, with construction and catering contracts repeatedly awarded to family-run firms.The ED arrested the chairman under the Prevention of Money Laundering Act (PMLA) on 18 November, following a detailed investigation and analysis of evidence gathered during searches conducted at premises related to the Al-Falah group.ED officials said they seized more than INR4.8 million (US$53,600) in cash, along with digital devices and documents which they claimed indicated that shell companies (a formally registered entity that has no significant assets, staff, or active business operations) were allegedly used to execute “financial irregularities”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

阿法拉大學面臨多項調查引發關注 近日，印度哈里亞納邦的阿法拉大學因與德里紅堡爆炸事件相關的調查而受到嚴格審查。該事件造成15人喪生，調查機構包括國家反恐調查局(NIA)和執法局(ED)正在針對大學的認證聲明及財務交易進行調查。大學管理層已向醫學本科生及其家長保證，學校不會關閉，並強調將持續提供教學服務。然而，因為過期的認證問題及與恐怖活動的連結，家長們對學校的未來充滿焦慮。教育部門正在考慮撤回其少數民族機構身份，這將影響許多學生的獎學金和入學權益。

阿法拉大學面臨多項調查引發關注 近日，印度哈里亞納邦的阿法拉大學因與德里紅堡爆炸事件相關的調查而受到嚴格審查。該事件造成15人喪生，調查機構包括國家反恐調查局(NIA)和執法局(ED)正在針對大學的認證聲明及財務交易進行調查。大學管理層已向醫學本科生及其家長保證，學校不會關閉，並強調將持續提供教學服務。然而，因為過期的認證問題及與恐怖活動的連結，家長們對學校的未來充滿焦慮。教育部門正在考慮撤回其少數民族機構身份，這將影響許多學生的獎學金和入學權益。 
```

Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyNIGERIAUniversities and schools close amid spike in kidnappingsAbdulganiyu Abdulrahman Akanbi27 November 2025TweetAt least 10 higher education institutions in Kebbi and Bauchi states in Nigeria have been closed indefinitely owing to escalating security challenges in the country that have included the abduction of hundreds of civilians during November alone.Based on media reports in Nigeria, between 300 and 400 school children have been kidnapped in November across 19 of the country’s 36 states, giving rise to concern about the safety of tertiary students, who have also been targets in these attacks in the past as recently as September and October.The latest incidents increase figures that were released earlier this year. In July, the National Human Rights Commission said 2,266 people were killed and 857 were abducted in first half of 2025,reportedPunch.Security experts sayarmed banditsmasterminding kidnapping attacks do that for economic purposes, often through ransom demands.Ambali Abdulkabeer, an academic and quality education advocate based in Nigeria, toldUniversity World News: “Terrorists see schools as soft places to attack to get the attention of people in power and, sometimes, [they] see the students as vulnerable … So [these attacks are] for financial gain, [the attackers] understand that attacking schools would stir up conversations and force the government to react.”Higher education closuresThe recent insecurity surge in Nigeria has, at first, forced state governments across the northern parts of the country, affecting 19 of the country’s 36 states, to close elementary and high schools, but directives ordering tertiary institutions to also close followed.On 23 November Kebbi State, in the north-west, shut down all tertiary institutions, following a statement by Issa Abubakar-Tunga, the Kebbi commissioner for higher education,reportedPunch.The affected tertiary institutions include the State Polytechnic, Dakingari; Kebbi State University of Science and Technology, Aliero; College of Health Sciences and Technology, Jega; and the College of Education, Argungu, according to the commissioner’s statement.Following this directive, the management of Abdullahi Fodio University of Science and Technology, in Aliero, Kebbi State, also ordered the closure of the university indefinitely, Maimaro Alhaji Tilli, the university’s registrar, said in a statement on 23 November.“All students (postgraduate, undergraduate, diploma, remedial and [pre-degree candidates] from the Interim Joint Matriculation Board) are to immediately leave the university campus within an hour from this notice,” the statement, obtained byUniversity World News, directed. Students also had to leave off-campus hostels.Tilli toldUniversity World Newsthat the institution was aware of concerns about the short notice given to the students to vacate, the necessity of the closure, and questions on when academic activities will resume, but that the institution only acted accordingly to the impromptu directives received from the state government.The management of Adamu Augie College of Education also ordered the immediate closure of the institution based on insecurity issues.“All staff and students are to note that all the academic activities are hereby suspended till further notice and required students to vacate their hostels immediately,” a circular, seen byUniversity World Newsstated.On 24 November, the management of the Federal Polytechnic Bauchi also suspended all academic activities, following a directive issued by the Bauchi State government in response to the worsening insecurity in the region, according to a circular obtained byUniversity World News.Ibrahim Yahya, a final-year architecture student at the polytechnic, toldUniversity World News: “We have just finished exams and some of us are still writing up research projects. We are not really happy with the security situation and the closure will disrupt all our academic activities, but we believe that [not] a living soul can go to school. How do you expect us to be happy when we are on the verge of graduation and everything stopped? We pray the government is able to restore peace in the state soon so that academic activities can continue.”Mustapha Muhammad Marafa, the president of the student union at the institution, supported the management decision.He noted that the union has not received any formal complaint from the student community because only a few students were affected as the university had just completed its second round of semester examinations.Meanwhile, President Bola Tinubu hasdirectedBello Matawalle, the minister of state for defence, to relocate to Kebbi over the abduction of schoolgirls in the state.He alsoannouncedon 24 November that 38 worshippers in Kwara State and 51 of the abducted 303 pupils from St Mary’s Catholic School in Niger State were rescued.Support for closure of institutionsAbdulkabeer, the quality expert, supports the government’s decision and commends the compliance of tertiary institutions. He said all levels of government had to ensure the safety of lives and properties in the country and highlighted some of the implications of a long shutdown of institutions of learning.“The closure of universities and other tertiary institutions in Kebbi, of course, has far-reaching implications for the education sector, students and the broader community. In the short term, it would disrupt academic activities, potentially affecting students’ academic progress and future plans.“The long-term implications include declining enrolment rates as students seek safer alternatives. It is also possible that the closure leads to brain drain, as talented students and academics may seek opportunities elsewhere. Above all, it widens educational inequality, particularly in northern Nigeria, where access to quality education is already limited.“There is also an economic impact on local communities that rely on universities for business and employment,” Abdulkabeer toldUniversity World News.Joshua Biem, a security expert and senior policy analyst for consultancy firm Nextier, backs the closure in the face of the security situation in Nigeria.“Closing higher institutions is a temporary measure. The government needs to do everything to ensure protection of lives and properties so that schools can resume. The concerned authorities need to be sincere and look critically into the socio-economic factors contributing to security challenges in the country,” he toldUniversity World News.Biem further said the government needs to also identify the terror groups as threats in order to deal with them militarily, and develop early warning and response mechanisms as well as working intelligence to improve the security architecture of the country and foil looming attacks.Kidnappings: A historyThe first kidnapping in Nigeria were reported in 2014 when 276 girls from Chibok town were kidnapped from their school by Boko Haram, an armed sectarian group.But, as the security experts pointed out, these attacks are now seen as criminally, rather than ideologically, driven.In November, various attacks were reported. On 17 November gunmen invaded the Kebbi State’s Government Girls’ Comprehensive Secondary School which is located in the Maga, Danko-Wasagu Local Government Area.They reportedly killed the school’s vice principal, Hassan Makuku, and abducted 25 female students, while three of the students managed to escape.The state government and police had confirmed this, according toPremium Times.Before this attack, the government of Niger State, also in northern Nigeria,confirmedtoVanguardthe abduction of the pupils and staff from St Mary’s School, of whom some were rescued.Similarly, about 50 schools in four local government areas were closed on 19 November in Kwara State following the abduction of the 38 worshippers, who were also rescued,reportedPunch.Higher education students have also been targeted. On 22 September 2025, 30 students were kidnapped from the Federal University of Gusau, Zamfara State,reportedHumAngle.Also, the public Mustapha Agwai Polytechnic in Lafia, was invaded on 26 September 2025. Three people sustained gun injuries and one was abducted,reportedVanguard.In addition, five female students werekidnappedfrom their hostels in the Federal University Dutsin-ma, located in Katsina, on 4 October.These incidents increase figures that were reported in September 2024. TheDaily Trustwrote that 28 undergraduate students have been killed and 78 others kidnapped from various tertiary institutions across Nigeria between January 2023 and August 2024, excluding the abduction of 20 medical and dental students of the universities of Maiduguri and Jos, who were abducted in Benue State, but were later released.University staff members have also been targeted in attacks. In mid-2024University World Newsreportedabout bandits killing a deputy vice-chancellor in Katsina State.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News
