https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: State Department to impose Trump agenda on scholarships
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: State Department to impose Trump agenda on scholarshipsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsThe shift of scholarship programmes from Education to State raises questions over growing ideological oversight in international academic funding. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksCHINA-UNITED STATESThe US needs to restart the Fulbright programme in ChinaUNITED STATESAnalysing Fulbright’s legacy offers a learning moment for IEAFGHANISTANDismay as US suspends Fulbright scholarships for AfghansInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUNITED STATESState Department to impose Trump agenda on scholarshipsNathan M Greenfield04 December 2025TweetThe transfer of 12 international scholarship programmes from the all-but-defunct United States Department of Education (DoE) to the Department of State (State) is set to increase ideological control over what disciplinary areas receive funding, affecting both Americans studying outside the US and international scholars entering the country.The 12 programmes include four from the Fulbright-Hays programme – a component of the well-known Fulbright Program – such as the F-H Doctoral Dissertation Research Abroad Fellowship, as well as eight other programmes, including the Business and International Education Program and the Foreign Language and Area Studies Fellowship.The DoE’spress release of 18 November, couched in bureaucratic language, says the move “leverages the State Department’s global reach and international education and public diplomacy expertise to advance US national security and America’s foreign policy priorities”.Thewebsite of the Fulbright-Hays programmeexplains that grants are “to individual US K-14 pre-teachers, teachers and administrators, pre-doctoral students and postdoctoral faculty, as well as to US institutions and organisations. The programme supports research and training efforts overseas, which focus on non-Western foreign languages and area studies”.However,the fact sheetpublished at the same time lists five grant abstracts from the Fulbright-Hays programme as exemplars of what will no longer be approved; though the fact sheet does not reference any of the executive orders made by President Donald J Trump after returning to power in late January, they are clearly informed by the ethos of the orders that banned diversity, equity and inclusion from federally funded activities and stripped LGBTQ and trans people of protections in federally funded activities and regulated industries.The fact sheet lists an award of US$37,000 to a doctoral student for a dissertation “on the relation between collective movement, like boxing, walking, and dancing, and systemic conditions of anti-trans violence” in Bogotá, Colombia’s red-light district, and Los Angeles’ MacArthur Park as an example of a project that would not be funded.Another award that exemplifies what the US administration does not want studied is one that provided US$27,000 to a doctoral student “to investigate non-binary and trans francophones’ linguistic attitudes and ideologies toward inclusive French [for example, non-binary pronouns] in Montréal, Canada”.These limitations, Rachel Rinaldo, a sociology professor and director of the Center for Asian Studies at the University of Colorado (Boulder), toldUniversity World News: “are in line with what we’ve already seen from the Trump administration’s executive orders, so it’s no surprise that they would include them in the memo”.Jonathan Friedman, Sy Syms managing director of the US Free Expression Programs (PEN America), strongly criticised the transfer of the scholarships to the Department of State.“The transfer of international education and foreign language programmes from the Department of Education to State comes on the heels of some of these vital programmes already being cancelled this academic year,” he said.“It also appears [to be] part of the administration’s broader playbook of trying to impose total ideological control over academia, threatening funding to force compliance.“For decades, federal government funding has been vital in helping to train future diplomats and experts with deep knowledge of diverse cultures and societies. In the long run, undermining the academic freedom of students and scholars in these crucial programmes will be detrimental to their effectiveness.”While he could not rule out that it has happened before, Michael S Roth, president of Wesleyan University (Middletown, Connecticut), toldUniversity World News: “I can’t remember another administration describing the kind of grants that have been given out by previous administrations that would no longer be funded.“This is part of the Trump administration’s efforts to coordinate across the various branches of the government ideological preferences over the mission-specific agendas of the departments in question, and that would be the case in deciding to review vaccine policies because of the ideological proclivities of Robert Kennedy Jr or deciding that some Fulbrights that were completed in the past were not worth funding: now all funding would be in accord with the ‘President's priorities’.”Roth said the phrase “this grant is no longer in accord with the President's priorities” is now seen often. “And that's … really extraordinary.”“What I worry about is that people have gotten used to that, and by coordinating the Fulbright programmes under State, it gives the administration a greater opportunity to use ideological litmus tests for what should not be ideological programmes. And that, I think, should concern everybody,” said Roth.Cosmopolitan dimensionSince the establishment of the main Fulbright programme in 1946, named for its congressional sponsor, the late Senator J William Fulbright, more than 380,000 “Fulbrighters” from 160 countries have received funds to either study in the US or, if Americans, to study or work in education elsewhere in the world.Last year, roughly 1,900 US students, 4,000 foreign students, 1,200 US scholars, and 900 visiting scholars received awards, in addition to several hundred teachers and professionals, according to theprogramme’s webpage. Thousands of other scholars and students have received funding from the 12 programmes being relocated to State.“The Fulbright provides the opportunity, especially for young scholars, to bring their American education and stress test it in a different cultural environment, whether it’s Madagascar, southern France or in South America,” said Roth.“I use the word stress test because [of] what happens when you're in another context … You discover things about yourself and your own education that help you continue to learn.”While Americans on Fulbrights are not icons of the country, Roth said, you are seen as someone who is a product of the American educational system; indeed, one of the questions on the application is: “Will you be a good representative of your country wherever you go?”The programme has “allowed us to send some of our really bright young scholars and teachers into places in the world where they, I think, improve the reputation of the United States, and at the same time, they return home with a different perspective on the world, because it’s informed by the culture of the country they were in. For a pretty modest investment of US resources, I think it did a lot of good for individuals and for our country’s reputation,” said Roth.Roth explained what Fulbright scholars from other countries bring to campuses like his in Middletown, Connecticut.“The great thing about the people coming to our campus from outside is they bring their own values. We don’t want them mimicking whoever is the US president. You don’t want them trying to distort their own perspective. You want them to bring their values here so we can learn from them.“At Wesleyan, I’ve seen people who are coming to work on history or a foreign language, or in other areas of the university, who come with a very different perspective on education, politics and daily life – it’s informed by their own background and institution.“The Fulbright is an effective way of bringing, often younger, scholars to our campus who are filled with energy and ambition and add kind of another cosmopolitan dimension to the culture of the campus,” said Roth.One such recipient of a Fulbright is Pavlo Fedorchenko-Kutuev, a sociologist in Ukraine who for more than a decade taught sociology at the Igor Sikorsky Kyiv Polytechnic Institute (and with whom this author team-taught two courses on the sociology of war).Courtesy of a Fulbright fellowship, Fedorchencko-Kutuev spent the 2002 academic year as a senior fellow doing research at the Robert Wagner Graduate School of Public Service at New York University (NYU).Fedorchencko-Kutuev toldUniversity World News: “I’m interested in the comparative historical political sociology of the modern nation state, its birth, its rise, its evolution and its potential demise, or at least its falling into crisis.”His research project was a comparison of bureaucratic cultures in the United States and Ukraine using American bureaucracy as a kind of ideal against which Ukrainian bureaucracy and state institutions had to be compared.Fedorchencko-Kutuev recalled that studying at NYU was an unparalleled experience. It had “everything you needed, like research facilities, library computer facilities, faculty and … the advantage of being in New York City [with] lots of other educational and research and cultural institutions. I was lucky to take advantage of The New School for Social Research and Columbia University” (both of which are in Manhattan).“Washington DC is not far away and neither is Boston. So I could crisscross to these cities, visiting colleagues, giving talks, attending lectures, seminars, exchanging ideas, tapping into that stream of ideas and scholarship which is extremely vibrant at American universities”.Fedorchencko-Kutuev also spoke about New York City’s Fulbrighters’ community.“The Fulbright office helped us form a close-knit community of Fulbrighters based in New York City. There were quite a few of us from different countries … They organised events for us or arranged for us to attend concerts, talks and conferences,” he said.Today, as Ukraine must rely on the US for its very survival, and as US politics under Trump has taken an authoritarian turn, I asked how his experience studying there two decades ago informs his understanding of the US.He replied that living there had given him a “more subtle, more nuanced understanding of the country. You realise that it’s extremely diverse, vivacious, culturally heterogeneous, so extremely complex and sometimes fragile, despite appearing as this mighty Leviathan or mighty behemoth.”Resignation of Fulbright boardConcern about American international programmes becoming one more victim of Trump’s drive to reshape the ideology of American higher education was telegraphed last June when 11 of 12 members of the Fulbright Foreign Scholarship Board resigned, charging political interference in the awarding of fellowships.In astatement posted on Substackon 11 June, the resignees wrote, “The current administration has usurped the authority of the Board and denied Fulbright awards to a substantial number of individuals who were selected for the 2025 to 2026 academic year.“The administration is also currently subjecting an additional 1,200 foreign Fulbright recipients to an unauthorised review process and could reject more. We believe these actions not only contradict the statute but are antithetical to the Fulbright mission and the values, including free speech and academic freedom, that Congress specified in the statute,” that established the first Fulbright.The statement further said, “We have raised these legal issues and our strong objections with senior administration officials on multiple occasions, including in writing. The officials have refused to acknowledge or respond to the Board, failing to even attempt good faith efforts to course correct and operate the Programme in accordance with the statute.”In astatementshared with National Public Radio(NPR)on 12 June, the State Department described the board members as “partisan political appointees” of former president Joe Biden and said their claim that the 1961 Act “affords exclusive and final say over Fulbright applications to the board is false” – even though at the time, asNPRreported, the policy brief on the Department of State’s website said that the act entrusted to the board the “final responsibility for the choice of all participants in educational exchange programmes".A statement from the Department of State toldNPR: “It’s ridiculous to believe that these members would continue to have [the] final say over the application process, especially when it comes to determining academic suitability and alignment with President Trump's Executive Orders.”For her part, White House spokesperson Anna Kelly toldNPR: “President Trump, not the Fulbright Board, was elected by the American people to ensure all foreign policy initiatives align with our national interests."The resignees’ letter did not mention Darren Beattie, who back in March had been acting undersecretary for Public Diplomacy and Public Affairs at the State Department, which later in the year appointed him the senior bureau official at the Bureau of Educational and Cultural Affairs, the division responsible for the main Fulbright programme (and now the 12 other programmes).In 2018, during the first Trump Administration, Beattie wasfired from his position as speechwriterfor attending a gathering of white supremacists. His appointment to the State Department so concerned the Democrats on the House Foreign Affairs Committee that theywrote in protestto Secretary of State Marco Rubio.Beattie’s “white nationalist loyalties and public glorification of our adversaries’ authoritarian systems make him unqualified to serve,” the Democrats wrote.He “persistently traffics in white nationalist ideology that is hostile to Jewish Americans, women, and people of colour. The Anti-Defamation League [of B’nai Brith] . . . has highlighted his long history of involvement with ‘notorious racists, antisemites, and white supremacists … Beattie has advocated onXfor starkly racist and fascist social policies, arguing that “low-IQ, low impulse control populations lack higher reasoning and moral faculties [and, therefore] require corporal punishment and the threat of violence to function properly in society”.He has also advocated for the sterilisation of‘feral populations’ and lamented the absence of population reduction policies because ‘all I see is trash multiplying’,” said the letter.The representatives’ letter goes on to voice concerns about Beattie’s admiration of “China’s and Russia’s authoritarian systems and repressive policies as [being] superior to American democracy”.According to the website theRaw Story, when the 11 members of the Fulbright board resigned in June, some 200 scholarships that had already been awarded to US researchers had been cancelled (presumably by Beattie) “purely on the administration’s ideological objections to research topics”.That 1,200 scholarships that had already been awarded were placed under review sent “shockwaves through the international academic community and damaging America’s reputation for world-class research,” theRaw Storywrites.‘Tool of censorship’Concern about the impact on America’s reputation was shared by Alana Deluty, president of the Rhode Island chapter of the Fulbright Association, whotoldBloomberg News: “The fact that we’re alienating the smartest, most accomplished people in the world, who would normally be flocking to the US – it’s a huge loss for this country.”Effenus Henderson, co-director of the Washington State-based Institute for Sustainable Diversity and Inclusion,wrote on LinkedInthat political appointees at the State Department were turning a “programme built to foster critical inquiry and global cooperation” into a “tool of censorship” by flagging projects using phrases like “critical theory”, “refugees with disabilities”, or even for noting that “boys and girls have different perspectives on climate change”.“This isnot about national security or fiscal prudence. It is about erasing intellectual work that challenges dominant narratives,” he wrote.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Mobility barriers hinder Africa-Europe research collaboration
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Mobility barriers hinder Africa-Europe research collaborationGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyAFRICA-EUROPEMobility barriers hinder Africa-Europe research collaborationNic Mitchell04 December 2025TweetCollaboration between African and European universities may be moving in the right direction – but barriers to mobility for African academics and students continue to hold back equal cooperation between the two continents in education and research, according to a higher education leader from Africa.While partnerships between the European Union and Africa are evolving from a ‘donor-recipient’ model towards a strategic vision of ‘equal’ collaboration and a shared quality assurance framework, African higher education institutions face major obstacles to operational parity.Among the biggest barriers are the “significant visa hurdles” frequently faced by African academics trying to visit their European counterparts – and the same is true of student exchanges, Professor Olusola Oyewole, the secretary-general of the Association of African Universities (AAU), toldUniversity World News.AU-EU SummitOyewole made his remarks following the Seventh African Union-European Union (AU-EU) Summit held in Luanda, the capital of Angola, from 24-25 November 2025.The summit produced a jointdeclaration, which celebrated the 25th anniversary of the AU-EU partnership inaugurated at a summit in Cairo in 2000.The joint communique spoke about “resilience in the face of past and present geopolitical challenges” and reaffirmed “closer cooperation and collective action for the mutual benefit of the peoples of Africa and Europe” including in education, research, science, technology and skills development.Visa hurdlesHowever, Oyewole said: “While European partners can often travel to Africa with relative ease, our academics frequently face significant visa hurdles to visit their European counterparts.“True equality requires fluid intellectual exchange in both directions. Overcoming these barriers is essential for the partnership to reach its full potential.”Nevertheless, Oyewole was one of a number of African and European university leaders stressing the “strategic value of the EU-Africa relationship” at a time of increasing uncertainty about scientific relations between the developing world and the United States under the Trump administration.President Donald Trump has already closed down the United States’ Agency for International Development, merging USAID with the State Department, and cut its programme, asUniversity World Newsreportedin February 2025, under the headline, ‘Trump’s USAID shutdown impacts universities worldwide’.Other Western countries, like the United Kingdom, Germany and the Netherlands, have also scaled back their overseas development funding.Strategic value of AU-EU relationship“The recent global funding shifts have underscored the strategic value of the EU-Africa relationship,” said Oyewole, with EU framework programmes such as Horizon Europe and dedicated Africa initiatives providing “a crucial layer of stability”.Oyewole toldUniversity World News: “This disruption hasn’t so much changed the relationship [between the AU and the EU] as highlighted its importance.”He emphasised the Joint Africa-EU Strategy, the African-European Partnership, and the Joint Vision for 2030 among the programmes collectively shaping the mechanisms that enable African researchers and innovators to access funding.“The challenge now is for the EU and Africa to jointly advocate for and ensure that the effective frameworks are adequately funded and made more accessible to African institutions.“African universities are also encouraged to strengthen their internal funds-generation mechanism to reduce over-dependency on external sources of funds,” said Oyewole.Research capacityHis comments were echoed by Professor John O Gyapong, the secretary-general of the African Research Universities Alliance, or ARUA, who toldUniversity World News: “We welcome the renewed AU-EU partnership and the shift towards more balanced collaborations, as seen in many joint initiatives like the AU-EU Innovation Agenda and the (EU’s) Global Gateway programme, which enhance Africa’s research capacity.“With decreased funding from traditional partners such as the US, EU support through many flagship programmes has become increasingly vital.“For ARUA, we believe that investing in education and research is crucial for peace and stability – a key theme of the AU-EU Summit – especially in meeting the needs of Africa’s growing youth population.“This renewed partnership signifies a positive move towards equal, sustainable cooperation that promotes knowledge, innovation, and security across the continent.”Filling the gapFrom an institutional level, Dr Vincent Ogutu, the vice-chancellor of Strathmore University in Nairobi, Kenya, predicted that, as major funding to African higher education institutions from the US and some countries from the Global North declines, “an opportunity arises for other players to take up these projects”.He toldUniversity World News: “At Strathmore, many of the cases we have been working on through USAID went through a very rigorous vetting process and have high-quality outcomes, and any new partner coming to fill the gap would be inheriting viable projects, with the teams on the ground already trained and ready to go.”Ogutu foresaw an upsurge in collaboration between the Global North, via European universities and research institutions and their counterparts in Africa and the rest of the Global South “including by institutions in the very countries that have cut funding” at a national government level.“Whenever an African higher education institution is seen to have successfully run North-South projects well in the past, it continues to attract partnerships from peer institutions, even from the very countries that might have cut funding.“While governments may not see the big global picture and think they are doing the right thing by diverting resources to address domestic needs, collaboration with African higher education institutions could put those Global North countries back in the driving seat, allowing them to prepare good candidates to hire, while also lessening the need for people to emigrate, if their own countries [in the Global South] are developing as a result of these collaborative projects,” said Ogutu.So, the withdrawal of funds is an invitation for other players, whether bilateral or institutional, to fill this gap and we would welcome partnerships for mutual benefit that are aligned to our values and vision for Africa,” he added.Italian-African initiativeOne European university showing the way that Western institutions can develop a mutually beneficial approach to cooperation with African countries is Milan’sUniversità Cattolica del Sacro Cuore, or Catholic University of the Sacred Heart, in Italy.University World Newsreportedearlier this year how African entrepreneurs were being supported by a pioneering Italian initiative – E4Impact partnership – involving about 30 universities across 26 African countries.Following the AU-EU Summit in Luanda and the call to strengthen ties between Africa and Europe, Mario Molteni, a professor of corporate strategy at the Milan-based university, toldUniversity World News: “Our Global MBA in Impact Entrepreneurship and initiatives such as Circular Labour Migration are in line with the Africa-EU partnership vision and with [the] Italian government’s Mattei Plan to foster strategic cooperation and sustainable development with African countries ... We see our collaborations with African universities as a concrete model of genuine shared-growth and future cooperation.”Progress since last summitBefore last month’s AU-EU Summit in Luanda, the AAU and the European University Association (EUA) issued a jointstatementwhich outlined hopes and aspirations for taking forward cooperation and collaboration between African and European research and higher education.This set out three priorities:• Recognise the transversal importance of university cooperation and continue to invest in it;• Enable interregional exchange and cooperation to develop higher education and research systems; and• Focus on values-based partnerships.It also highlighted the progress made since the last AU-EU summit held in Brussels in 2022, including Horizon Europe’s Africa Initiatives to help address health, the green transition, digital transformation, and science capacity-building, aligned with the joint AU-EU Innovation Agenda and the Youth Mobility for Africa Global Gateway Flagship initiative.The AAU-EUA statement said there had also been more than 50,000 exchanges of students and staff between Europe and Africa, as well as 240 capacity-building projects involving higher education institutions through the Youth Mobility for Africa Flagship.It welcomed the shift towards more equitable and balanced partnerships, with an enhanced role for African higher education institutions in co-designing and leading projects and initiatives, which helped to build trust and understanding and paved the way for mutual learning.There has also been better mutual understanding and use of recognition and quality assurance instruments, based on the work of HAQAA III (Harmonisation, Accreditation and Quality Assurance in African Higher Education) and ACQF II (the African Continental Qualifications Framework).These initiatives have contributed to making Africa a key dialogue partner for the Global Dimension of theBologna Process, which is aimed at bringing about coherence in higher education systems in Europe.The AAU-EUA statement recommended stronger Africa-centric measures in the EU’s next long-term budget (2028-34), including reinforcing Horizon Europe’s Africa Initiatives, Erasmus+ capacity building, international mobility, and the Intra-Africa Academic Mobility Scheme.It also called for support for new mutual partnerships such as the Study and Research in Africa Initiative, launched in 2025 to boost Africa’s attractiveness as a study and research destination while strengthening European-African academic careers on the continent.View from EuropeMichael Gaebel, the director of Higher Education Policy at the EUA, toldUniversity World News: “The EUA has been working in collaboration with African colleagues for more than two decades, including on the first-ever Erasmus Mundus project between Europe and Africa, 15 years ago, with the AAU and other partners.“African and European universities have evolved in their internationalisation and there is more funding provided through European, national and international initiatives, and also much policy attention.“At European level, we now have the Global Gateway and the Youth Mobility flagship, enabling dialogue, cooperation and resource pooling among different actors and donors.“We also all feel the impetus to continue and enhance this in view of the specific priorities of the AU-EU relationship, but also shared global challenges and achieving the SDGs [Sustainable Development Goals].“What concerns us is that higher education and research is not yet fully acknowledged in the larger [AU-EU] collaboration frameworks and might just be seen as a tool to advance certain priorities in a rather technical and utilitarian fashion.”Professor Jan Palmowski, the secretary-general of The Guild of European Research-Intensive Universities, toldUniversity World Newsthat they welcomed the explicit commitment towards research, education, science, technology and skills in the joint declaration from the AU-EU Summit.“I am delighted to see the commitment to existing schemes of exchange and important encouragement to the international dimension of Erasmus+ as the EU plans for the next Multiannual Financial Framework from 2028,” he said.“Especially in the context of our initiative to develop Clusters of Research Excellence jointly with ARUA, the text also foresees the strengthening of partnerships between universities and research organisations.“Finally, there is a clear commitment to investing in education and research to overcome brain drain, in the context of the AU-EU Innovation Agenda. That agenda has only just begun to be implemented, and it is important that the declaration recognised its work.“Given that the declaration also mentioned many areas for which research collaboration is crucial – health, data and biodiversity/climate to name just a few – this declaration is an important affirmation of the importance of collaboration in research and education as an essential pillar of the AU-EU partnership,” said Palmowski.• TheGlobal Gatewayis the €306 billion (about US$356 billion) European strategy launched in 2021 to boost smart, clean and secure links in digital, energy and transport sectors, while also strengthening health, education and research systems across the world.It is fully aligned with the United Nations Agenda 2030 and its Sustainable Development Goals and a first milestone was the Africa-Europe Investment Package, with approximately €150 billion of investment dedicated to strengthening partnerships with Africa.Nic Mitchell is a UK-based freelance journalist and PR consultant specialising in European and international higher education. He blogs at www.delacourcommunications.com.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Students ask for international help amid detentions after coup
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Students ask for international help amid detentions after coupGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNIGERCoup disruptions thwart students due to study in FranceWEST AFRICAStudents concerned about coups and decline of democracyMYANMARHigher education sector under systemic attack since coupInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsGUINEA-BISSAUStudents ask for international help amid detentions after coupAndreia Nogueira04 December 2025TweetUniversity students are asking for international help to restore democracy in West Africa’s Guinea-Bissau after a military coup on 26 November, fearing returning to universities amid reports of missing and detained students.On 1 December, the Consortium of Academic Associations of Higher Education in Guinea-Bissau (Consórcio das Associações Académicas do Ensino Superior da Guiné-Bissau), which represents student associations for nine of the 31 higher education institutions in the country, published an open letter demanding the publication of the electoral results and the immediate release of all individuals detained during this crisis.“We call on the international community to monitor the situation closely, and support the restoration of democratic normality in Guinea-Bissau. As young academics and leaders, we reject any solution that does not involve unrestricted respect for the popular will and democratic institutions,” said the letter.Following the presidential election on 23 November, both incumbent President Umaro Sissoco Embaló, in power since 2020, and the main opposition candidate, Fernando Dias da Costa, claimed to have won, the BBCreported.But, three days later, military officers appeared onstate televisionannouncing they had seized power to stop an alleged plot to destabilise the country and manipulate electoral results – a move they claimed had been “coordinated by some national politicians with the participation of a well-known drug lord” and other domestic and foreign nationals, without providing further details.The military then detained several politicians and other prominent citizens. That, initially, included Embaló, but he has since fled and is now sheltering in the Republic of the Congo (Congo-Brazzaville).In a statement released on 27 November, a High Military Command for the Restoration of National Security and Public Order (Alto Comando Militar para a Restauração da Segurança Nacional e Ordem Pública), just one day after the coup, officially lifted immediate restrictions on movement and a curfew, ordering the reopening of institutions on 28 November, but forbidding protest marches, demonstrations and strikes.Fearful students still protest on the streetsHowever, Juviecson Nuno Correia, a member and former president of the Amílcar Cabral University (Universidade Amilcar Cabral) Students’ Association, toldUniversity World Newsthat shootings heard during the coup on 26 November, when students were returning to classes, created panic, leading people, including students and university staff, to leave the capital, Bissau.Such nervousness is widespread in a country of two million people that recalls a civil war in 1998 and which has suffered at least nine coups or attempted coups over the past five decades, since obtaining independence from Portugal in 1974.So, for the time being, said Correia, classes are not taking place in both private and public higher education institutions due to fears of violence, since the situation has not yet returned to normal, adding: “Civil society organisations called for disobedience, a general strike, from 1-5 December for us to reclaim our right to vote.“Fellow students and young people, who were demanding the immediate and unconditional release of people kidnapped by the security forces [during the coup] ... have been subjected to beatings,” he reported, explaining that some protesters had been kidnapped during demonstrations or for posting on social media asking for the results of the elections to be announced and for power to be given to the winners.“We have received reports from their families – they have been kidnapped and, to this day, nobody knows where these young people are,” he said, mentioning six people.Meanwhile, theLiga Guineense dos Direitos Humanos(Guinean League of Human Rights – LGDH)toldPortuguese news agencyLusathat about 30 young people were arrested and brutally beaten, and denounced dozens of “illegal and arbitrary” detentions.Sceptical about the diplomatic impact of pressure from the international community and disappointed that the elections have, thus far, not led to a peaceful handover of power to the opposition from a government in place since 2020, Correia called for international sanctions to be imposed on the new military rulers: “We have to prepare to confront this regime with force ... when possible.”He called on students to be brave, work with other civil society organisations in opposing the new regime, rebuking any young leaders invited to join the new cabinet, formed to be in place for a year, to isolate the government, which has several links to the deposed president.Indeed, these connections have led senior African figures, such as former Nigerian president Goodluck Jonathan, to call the coup a staged event designed to protect the past government from wholesale replacement by the opposition following the elections. Senegalese Prime Minister Ousmane Sonko and Rwandan President Paul Kagame have made similar claims.Students fear for the future of higher educationMeanwhile, Correia predicts that the instability will harm already challenging higher education work: “Considering the crises and the inherent lack of materials, the constant difficulty of studying in their own country, and having this added instability, [it] will interfere with the completion of the curriculum and will also affect student learning,” he said.“In social sciences, when discussing human rights, it will be very hard to publish a scientific article that reflects the real situation of the country. The author risks being imprisoned,” he warned.Correia does not know Mamadú Badji, the new minister of national education, higher education and scientific research and former minister of agriculture, but stressed that the education department faces many difficulties, including late pay.Likewise, Abulai Djaura, president of the National Network of Youth Associations of Guinea-Bissau (Rede Nacional das Associações Juvenis), toldUniversity World Newsthat retaining former finance minister, Ilídio Vaz Té in his old job and also appointing him prime minister, suggests that the coup will see existing policies continue.He is very concerned with the current situation, adding that all students condemned the coup: “The level of distrust and hatred between politicians and the population, itself, has grown,” he said.Djaura argued that higher education students play a crucial role now: “All of this needs analysis, but an analysis that is not based on trends, that is not based on ideological, ethnic or tribal affiliation, but an analysis based on the logic of things.”Still, Djaura hopes that the freedom to protest will be restored soon, even to help the government to better understand civic concerns. Yet, he noted: “The previous regime was always criticised regarding freedom of expression and demonstration, and the new authorities have also expressed this intention to further forbid it.”Djaura also criticises the fact that the African Union has suspended Guinea-Bissau, emphasising that it will be the people, who behaved well during elections and respected the democracy, who will suffer. The Economic Community of West African States, or ECOWAS, has also suspended Guinea-Bissau’s membership. He also asked the international community, investors and private universities to stay in the country.Meanwhile, Diamantino Lopes, political analyst, sociologist and lecturer at the private universities Lusophone University of Guinea (Universidade Lusófona da Guiné) and Colinas de Boé University (Universidade Colinas de Boé), hopes the international community will put enough pressure on the new regime to swiftly restore constitutional order and complete the electoral process.But if this fails, Lopes added, a countercoup may be launched, or popular uprisings might intensify. His hopes may be dashed by the electoral commissionclaimingit cannot publish the election results as armed men had destroyed electoral records.A state of insecurityFor now, said Lopes: “This scenario creates insecurity for any student and teacher.” This is particularly noted at the Colinas de Boé University, since around 150 to 200 of its students have classes in the same building as the campaign headquarters of opposition presidential candidate Fernando Dias da Costa, now granted asylum in Nigeria.Moreover, Lopes sees much confusion, with demonstrations organised by groups of students from various higher education institutions – a single WhatsApp group called ‘Young Revolutionaries’ has 1,500 students – with law enforcement controlling them with tear gas and threats of shooting by firearms. According to him, a university student was beaten during a march.Lopes claimed the takeover was masterminded by Embaló himself: “On the eve of the elections, the whole fraud was already set up. They handpicked the candidates who could participate ... Seeing this whole scenario, there was a collective awareness here. The people decided to vote for [the independent candidate] Fernando Dias,” he said, stressing that many people have the records that prove Dias’ victory.Lopes even questioned how Embaló was detained, since he was able to give interviews, while still speaking as the head of state: “They carried it [the coup] out because they were clearly aware of their defeat.”But he was not sure the military will prevail: “We’ve had this experience before. But the popular will isn’t the same ... We’re living in a context in which the flow of information is so vast that all of this could influence a popular revolt if the popular will isn’t respected,” he warned, adding that young people, who make up the overwhelming majority of the population, are the ones demonstrating.“The difference between Guinea-Bissau and many other societies is that, here, community-based associations play a decisive role in the social process. There isn’t a single neighbourhood that doesn’t have an association,” he noted, adding that they have a clear vision and if they focus on the same goal, it will be impossible to stop them. Although, he said, Embaló has tried to divide people with ethnic and religious narratives.Meanwhile, in the past few days, Guinean communities residing in Portugal, France, Brazil, Senegal and other countries havestageda wave of protests against what they describe as a “falsecoup d’état”.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

學生呼籲國際援助以應對政變後的拘留事件

在幾內亞比索於11月26日發生軍事政變後，當地大學學生向國際社會尋求幫助，以恢復民主，並要求立即釋放被拘留的學生。代表九所高等教育機構的幾內亞比索高等教育學術協會近日發表公開信，呼籲國際社會密切關注局勢，支持民主恢復。政變後，學生們因害怕暴力而停止上課，並在街頭抗議，要求公開選舉結果。專家擔心，持續的不穩定將進一步影響本已困難的高等教育環境，並損害學生的學習進度。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Science journalists confront social justice, disinformation
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Science journalists confront social justice, disinformationGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsSouth Africa’s Minister of Science, Technology and Innovation, Professor Blade Nzimande, at a news conference after his address at the World Conference of Science Journalists, Image: WCSJ 2025Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSOUTH AFRICA-GLOBALSouth Africa to host two international science meetingsGLOBALMapping (but not solving) the science communication crisisAFRICAScholars join forces to enhance climate change communicationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyAFRICA-GLOBALScience journalists confront social justice, disinformationDesmond Thompson03 December 2025TweetThe World Conference of Science Journalists (WCSJ2025) opened in Pretoria, South Africa, earlier this week, marking a significant shift for the profession. It is the first time in the event’s 33-year history that the flagship gathering has taken place on African soil.Hosted at the Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) International Convention Centre, the 13th edition of the conference brought together delegates from around the globe. The event, which has run biennially since its 1992 launch in Tokyo, convened under a theme that reflected the urgent political climate: ‘Science journalism and social justice – Journalism that builds understanding and resilience’.This year’s gathering was organised by the South African Science Journalists’ Association (SASJA) and the Science Diplomacy Capital for Africa (SDCfA), an initiative of the country’s Department of Science, Technology and Innovation (DSTI).Throughout the week, the discourse moved beyond the mechanics of reporting to confront inequality, democratic fragility and the eroding trust in empirical evidence.It also underscored how deeply the scientific enterprise depends on universities – as producers of research, training grounds for future scientists and journalists, and anchors of public trust in knowledge.Fourth Estate under pressureSASJA President Mandi Smallhorne reflected on the tension between the wonder of scientific discovery and the grit required to report on it honestly.“Most of us who end up doing this work stay because we love it,” she said in her welcoming address. “We are awed by the questions science asks and the worlds it opens up.”However, she cautioned that fascination cannot be an anaesthetic against scrutiny. “Science and scientists have their flaws,” she noted, positioning science journalists firmly within the Fourth Estate.“It is because we care about science – and value its role – that we dig deep to uncover misconduct or fraud, and that we fight false messaging.”This sentiment was echoed by South Africa’s Minister of Science, Technology and Innovation, Professor Blade Nzimande. Opening the conference, just more than a week after South Africa concluded the continent’s firstG20presidency, Nzimande emphasised that scientific progress is mute without dissemination.“You can do good science, but if it is not known, it is as good as dead science,” he said.He warned delegates against becoming “praise singers” for the science, urging them to remain critical guardians of the public interest. “Science has often also been used for destruction. We must remain vigilant to ensure it serves humanity.”In a news conference after his address, he said that higher education – a sector he previously oversaw before it was split from the rest of his portfolio – is still the backbone of research and innovation in South Africa: “Universities remain at the centre of our knowledge-production system.”Conference Director Engela Duvenage underlined the practical pressures facing science journalism. “The challenges we face with misinformation require a global, collaborative response,” she said in a statement, noting conference sessions that showcased tools journalists are using to counter falsehoods affecting public health and environmental policies.Nervous system for the planetIn a stirring keynote, environmental lawyer and author Advocate Cormac Cullinan – winner of the 2025 ShackletonMedal– challenged the audience to bridge the widening chasm between scientific reality and legal frameworks.“The law is lagging dangerously far behind scientific knowledge,” he warned, noting that, while science demands an end to fossil fuel reliance, legal systems continue to permit the destruction of the biosphere. He argued that humanity requires a cultural transformation as profound as a caterpillar’s metamorphosis into a butterfly, rather than mere incremental adjustments.Cullinan traced the root of the crisis to the mechanistic worldview inherited from the Enlightenment, which views the universe as a ‘giant clock’ and reduces nature to objects for human manipulation. He described this human exceptionalism as “eco-apartheid” – a form of separateness whereby one species claims superiority over the entire community of life.To counter this, he called for a shift toward ‘Earth jurisprudence’, a legal philosophy that recognises the universe as a “communion of subjects, not a collection of objects”. Illustrating this in practice, Cullinan announced the launch of the Antarctic Alliance, a global campaign to recognise the continent as a legal entity.“The question is not ‘how do we manage Antarctica?’ It is: ‘what is our relationship with Antarctica, and how do we repair it?’ ” he said, proposing that the continent be granted the standing to represent its own interests in international courts and UN climate processes.Cullinan urged the media to expand their concept of objectivity. He framed the profession as an active organ of planetary self-protection: “Science journalists are part of Earth’s nervous system – sensing danger, transmitting signals, and helping society respond,” he said. “We are not protecting nature. We are nature protecting herself.”Bongani Baloyi, a South African science and technology journalist, posing a question to Minister Blade Nzimande, Image: Desmond ThompsonFrom the microscopicWCSJ2025 also served as a platform for significant scientific announcements.In a briefing that drew parallels to a well-known scientific milestone of the 1990s, Professor Thomas Hartung of Johns HopkinsUniversityunveiled the Human ExposomeProject. He described the global initiative as an effort to map the “chemistry of your life story” – a counterpart in ambition to the Human GenomeProject.“That project taught us a great deal about our genetic blueprint,” Hartung told delegates. “But genetics explains only a fraction of chronic disease. Today, we know that over 80% of chronic human diseases are driven by environmental exposures – everything we inhale, ingest, touch or absorb.”The Human Exposome Project aims to map these exposures from conception to old age. Hartung explained that the convergence of three technologies has finally made this possible:• Ultra-sensitive mass spectrometry: Capable of detecting traces of thousands of chemicals in a single drop of blood or urine;• Organ-on-chip systems: Allowing researchers to simulate human biology and toxicology without animal testing; and• AI-driven modelling: Essential for integrating the massive datasets required to track the totality of exposures.“Ten years ago this would have been unthinkable,” Hartung noted.Crucially for a conference hosted in the Global South, he emphasised that the project is not merely an academic exercise, but a tool for equity. To ensure validity, he called for harmonised data from all over the world. “Inclusion is not optional – it is essential to the science,” he said.The project has already secured backing from several international bodies and leading scientists. Hartung outlined that the immediate focus now is on formalising a global consortium and advancing discussions with governments and funders.To the cosmicThe vast capabilities of African astronomy were highlighted in a briefing by the South African Radio Astronomy Observatory (SARAO) regarding theMeerKATradio telescope, which comprises 64 interlinked dishes and is already one of the world’s most sensitive instruments.PhD candidate Isaac Magolego of theUniversityof the Witwatersrand announced that the telescope has detected the most distant ultra-steep-spectrum radio halo ever observed.Located in a galaxy cluster seven billion light-years away, the discovery represents a look back in time through more than half the universe’s estimated 13.8 billion-year history – by far the furthest such an object has ever been spotted.Magolego explained that a radio halo is not a single ring, but a colossal “weather system” of turbulence and magnetic fields caused by the collision of galaxy clusters. These collisions accelerate electrons to near light-speed, creating a diffuse cloud of radio emission that permeates the cluster’s centre.The discovery was made possible by MeerKAT’s location in the semi-desert Karoo region. The area was chosen for its radio silence – a lack of interference from mobile phones and terrestrial broadcasts that allows the instrument to pick up the faint, ghostly signals of distant objects in the universe.The finding demonstrates the sheer power of MeerKAT, whose capabilities are set to expand exponentially as it is integrated into the larger Square Kilometre Array (SKA), the global mega-telescope currently under construction in the same region.PhD candidate Isaac Magolego explaining the radio halo observation, Image: Desmond ThompsonMilestone of substanceWCSJ2025 stands as a milestone, not just for its location, but for its substance. By placing social justice at the heart of the agenda, the conference asserted that science journalism is not merely a craft of explanation, but a civic duty essential for navigating the political and environmental pressures of the 21st century.The next World Conference of Science Journalists is set to take place in London in 2027.•Onlineaccess to live streaming can be bought at about US$30 per day. 南非科學記者大會聚焦社會正義與虛假資訊 本週在南非比勒陀利亞舉行的世界科學記者會（WCSJ2025）標誌著該職業的重大轉變，首次在非洲舉辦。會議主題為「科學新聞與社會正義」，強調在當前政治氣候下，科學報導的責任與挑戰。與會者探討了如何對抗不平等與民主脆弱，並強調科學與大學在知識生產中的核心角色。南非科技創新部長Nzimande強調，科學的進步需要有效的傳播，並呼籲記者在報導中保持批判性，捍衛公共利益。此外，會議還介紹了人類外部暴露計劃，旨在映射環境對健康的影響，並強調全球數據的包容性。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Satellite data boosts offshore fishing in local communities
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Satellite data boosts offshore fishing in local communitiesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksEGYPTEgypt’s satellite launch to advance climate researchKENYANanosatellite to help monitor security on Lake VictoriaAFRICASpace technology to measure, monitor climate change impactAFRICA-EUROPESpace sciences could help with Sustainable Development GoalsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsTANZANIASatellite data boosts offshore fishing in local communitiesClemence Manyukwe03 December 2025TweetTanzania’s fishing communities are increasingly relying on Earth observation (EO) data to identify profitable offshore fishing areas, thanks to an initiative that uses satellite data to improve fishery techniques as part of a programme aimed at developing African space capabilities.TheGlobal Monitoring for Environment and Security in Africa(GMES and Africa) is jointly implemented by the African Union and the European Union to enhance Africa’s capacity to utilise EO technologies for sustainable development, environmental governance and climate change mitigation.The programme’s key components are: water and natural resources management, focusing on monitoring water resources, land degradation and wetland management; marine and coastal areas management, addressing marine and coastal issues, including monitoring of ocean health and coastal erosion; and capacity building through training and resource provision for African institutions and stakeholders.Within the GMES and Africa framework, several initiatives have been created to manage marine and coastal areas using EO.One such initiative is the East Africa Marine Consortium, led by the Mauritius Oceanography Institute (MOI), which includes the Kenya Marine and Fisheries Research Institute, the Institute of Fisheries and Marine Sciences of Madagascar, the Seychelles Meteorological Authority and the Western Indian Ocean Marine Science Association.Positive livelihood changesIn Tanzania, tapping into the consortium’s work, some fishing communities are now using satellite navigation to track local fishing grounds via a dedicated mobile phone application that provides coordinates of potential fishing zones and data on water quality and temperature.In an interview withUniversity World News, Mauritius Oceanography Institute spokesperson Boyjoonauth Ranjeeta said the MOI and its partners have been able to improve access to EO data, engage in technology transfer and build capacity at both institutional and individual levels in the Southwest Indian Ocean region.She said by providing tools for informed decision-making, the initiative has brought positive change to the livelihoods of local communities.“The Tanzania Fisheries Research Institute (TAFIRI), one of the MOI’s partners in the GMES and Africa project, has been a pioneer in the region in the use of GMES and Africa marine and coastal service data to identify and locate profitable fishing areas, widely known as potential fishing zones (PFZ) for the pelagic fish species.“The main objective of using this technology is to reduce fishing pressure in coastal waters by encouraging small-scale fishers to venture offshore to fish for tuna and tuna-like species.“This is in alignment with Tanzania’s National Development Vision 2025 and the UN’s 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development and the Blue Economy, which acknowledge the role of science, technology and innovation to attain sustainable socioeconomic development,” said Ranjeeta.“Fishers have benefited from GPS (global positioning system) mobile phones and training for locating fishing grounds and collection of fish catch data.“The use of this innovative fishing technique not only allows an increase in fish catches but also reduces time and fuel spent looking for fishing grounds using traditional methods, therefore reducing fishing costs. This has considerably boosted the revenues of local fishers and improved the livelihoods of hundreds of Tanzanian fishermen.”Ranjeeta said one key component of the GMES and Africa project is capacity building and training.She said the programme has significantly invested in human and technical capacity development through training workshops, provision of technical resources and tools, and the development of local expertise in the use of EO data for marine and coastal management.“The aim is to empower local communities, government institutions, and decision-makers in the region to manage their own marine resources sustainably,” she said.“Furthermore, the students and young scientists have had the opportunity to benefit from scholarships, thus fostering a new generation of African experts in the field of Earth observation.”Land degradation monitoringThe North African region coordinator of the GMES and Africa project, Louis Evence Zoungrana, toldUniversity World Newsthat his organisation, the Sahara and Sahel Observatory (OSS), where he is the project expert, leads a grouping called the North African Consortium, which is responsible for coordinating a land degradation monitoring initiative.Zoungrana said the North African Consortium brings together the African Regional Centre for Space Science and Technology based in Morocco; the Regional Centre for Remote Sensing of North African States and the National Centre of Mapping and Remote Sensing, both based in Tunisia; the Desert Research Centre in Egypt; the Libyan Centre for Remote Sensing and Space Sciences; and the University of Nouakchott in Mauritania.He said that under the initiative a continental land degradation service named MISLAND-Africa (Monitoring Integrated Service for Land Degradation-Africa) has been created and a Network of Experts for Joint Monitoring of Land Degradation in Africa formed to support theinitiative.“The continental-scale land degradation monitoring service … is a decision support system (DSS) utilising EO data to deliver information, promote awareness, and support the decision-making process towards realising land degradation neutrality (LDN) in Africa.The core system provides information to monitor SDG indicator 15.3.1 – proportion of land that is degraded over the total land area – through three sub-indicators that, according to UNCCD, represent a minimum that should be complemented and enhanced by national (or sub-national) indicators for capturing a more accurate picture of land degradation,” he said.“To improve the understanding and multi-faceted nature of the active processes behind land degradation, MISLAND-Africa also provides a rich kit of harmonised indicators for land degradation monitoring, well adapted to North Africa.”Zoungrana said the indicators include vegetation loss or gain hotspots, forest fire risk assessment, vulnerability to desertification and vulnerability to water, wind and coastal erosion.He said components of the MISLAND-Africa service include a “geo-services dashboard allowing the time-series EO data collection, processing, and analysis to produce, analyse, and visualise interactively the land degradation-related products; MapoGraphics from MISLAND-Africa, an innovative and appealing physical medium for understanding the diversity of land degradation and communicating related statistics in an easily accessible and understandable format, making it a valuable tool for decision-making, awareness-raising, and information dissemination; and a mobile application linked to MISLAND-Africa, which serves land degradation products in a user-friendly way.”He said the services and products developed by the project have already been used successfully by national and local institutions in Tunisia, Libya and Egypt to map and monitor land degradation and to help decision-making and orient fieldwork.“From March 2022 to November 2025, the North African Consortium, led by the Sahara and Sahel Observatory, has trained more than 1,770 beneficiaries (about 35% are women),” he added.In a recentstatementSouth Africa’s Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR) said it was playing a central role as the technical lead for the GMES and Africa programme in Southern Africa, particularly through its leadership of the Africa Marine and Coastal Operations for Southern Africa and the Indian Ocean consortia and projects.Climate resilienceIt said it was working with regional partners such as the Southern African Science Service Centre for Climate Change and Adaptive Land Management, the Southern African Development Community and the African Union to align EO services with Africa’s development goals.The CSIR said GMES and Africa has provided real-time satellite data, supported thousands of trainees, and empowered many African countries with decision-making tools in climate resilience, disaster response and marine resource management.The organisation said it develops and deploys EO technologies to monitor marine and coastal environments and uses satellite data to track oceanography variables, pollution, ship traffic and climate change impacts.“We provide capacity building and training: by training students and stakeholders in the Southern and West Indian Ocean regions of Africa in EO applications for marine and coastal resource management and by hosting workshops to bridge the gap between science and policy to ensure EO data informs decision-making and impacts livelihoods,” the organisation said.In what is described as a “landmark moment” for continental space development, the GMES and Africa programme has officially become an initiative under the African Space Agency (AfSA).“This strategic integration marks a new chapter in Africa’s journey towards harnessing space science and technology for sustainable development, environmental resilience and policy innovation,” the organisation said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal 衛星數據助力坦尚尼亞離岸漁業發展 願景2030計劃近日報導，坦尚尼亞的漁業社區正逐漸依賴地球觀測數據，以識別有利的離岸漁區。這一計劃由非洲聯盟與歐洲聯盟共同實施，旨在增強非洲利用衛星技術進行可持續發展和環境治理的能力。透過衛星導航和專用手機應用程序，當地漁民能夠追蹤潛在的漁區，並獲得水質及溫度數據，從而提高捕撈效率並減少傳統捕魚成本。這一創新技術不僅增加了漁獲量，還改善了漁民的生計，預示著坦尚尼亞漁業的可持續發展將大幅提升。
