Professor TenureTrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredJAPANGovernment expands support for online degree programmesSuvendrini Kakuchi09 December 2025TweetThe Japanese government is strengthening support for online four-year college degrees in a move that marks a significant change from the past as a way to bring in more flexible curricula and provide more degree opportunities for students in regional areas.Iori Nakajima, an official at the University Promotion Division of Japan’s Ministry of Education, Culture, Sports, Science and Technology (MEXT), pointed to a growing government commitment to expanding online degrees.“Based on surveys and other data, the future of Japan’s college education will be more [heavily] based on a hybrid degree system that implements both face-to-face classrooms and online study, with the emphasis on the latter,” he toldUniversity World News.The COVID-19 pandemic completely transformed learning in universities, demonstrating the merits of remote study imposed at the time to meet isolation regulations.More recently, the focus has moved away from classroom instruction to student learning managed by faculties and students managing their learning trajectory using digital tools, he explained.“Flexible curriculums through remote instruction are replacing traditional face-to-face classrooms, a key change,” he said, referring to higher education goals under Japan’s Society 5.0 policy adopted in 2016 as part of the country’s Fifth Science and Technology Basic Plan.That plan, for a “super-smart society”, goes beyond Industry 4.0 towards “an integrated system of cyberspace and physical space”, according to MEXT.For higher education, Society 5.0 seeks to put in place a system where “everyone can learn anytime and anywhere”, the ministry said.Digitalised world“Endorsing online study as a degree [programme] is a given in the digital era. The government response is aimed at catching up with a digitalised world,” said Daisuke Fujita, an associate professor in the Division of Health and Safety Sciences Education, Osaka Kyoiku University.Fujita is a member of the Central Council for Education Committee that is advising MEXT on a reorganisation of the Lifelong Learning subdivision aimed at improving learning opportunities.The ministry has tried to ease existing labyrinthine regulations to permit colleges to offer newly designed curriculums for certified online degrees. For example, the 2014 Revision of Standards for University Correspondence Education no longer requires classroom facilities as part of its standards if education can be delivered “without hindrance”.This was followed by a 2022 revision of theUniversity Establishment Standards Act, which previously restricted sharing via the internet of printed materials.The new regulation permits 60 certified credits, or half the number of credits required for a degree, to be earned through courses that use audio and video as an integral part of the curriculum. Discussions are pending on the method of calculating credits with a view to including in-class and remote learning if the university accepts that such instruction is effective.Zen UniversityZen Universityis a fully online university launched in April 2025 with a grant from the philanthropicNippon Foundation. It is a collaboration with Dwango, an information technology giant that designed the institute’s online study system.Its four-year undergraduate degree is offered solely online and via on-demand classes, which allows students to access classes in their own time. Online learning is accompanied by fieldwork conducted locally and overseas, as well as internships during the academic year.The university, which also offers Japanese language classes, is the first to offer a full degree through digital learning and already has 4,000 students enrolled, according to the university’s chair, Shinichi Yamanaka.Without a physical campus, a Zen University degree costs JPY380,000 (US$2,400) annually – a big difference compared to private university tuition fees that hover between JPY800,000 and JPY1 million a year.“The goal of our university is the concept of equality in higher education,” Yamanaka toldUniversity World News. The student body hails from a mix of urban and regional areas, with 30% from outside Tokyo. “These students can access top-quality university education without having to pay for expensive urban living costs,” he noted.Another new online degree provider is Sak University, an academic venture launched in May this year by Japanese software developer SIE (Secure Information Exchange)in collaboration with United Kingdom-based Essex University.SIE’s IT education arm, Systems Architecture Knowledge, or SAK, already delivers industry-driven training through hybrid instruction. Now, Sak’s Tokyo innovation campus also offers an IT engineering degree.SAK Chief Executive Masayuki Tou, in a statement in May, described the degree’s blended approach: “SAK has developed a bespoke curriculum tailored to align with local industry requirements, which is validated by the University of Essex to ensure academic quality and coherence.This will be complemented by core modules from the University of Essex’s curriculum, which will be directly franchised to enhance academic rigour and provide students with exposure to globally recognised expertise.”New regulations for regional universitiesThe march towards incorporating digital technology took another step forward in July this year when MEXT permitted regional universities to introduce joint online courses delivered by selected regional universities collaborating together.According to MEXT’s Nakajima, the development will provide access to high-quality higher education in regional areas that will bear the brunt of the predicted decline in the student-age population in Japan.The current university student population of 630,000 enrolled in the first year is expected to dwindle to 460,000 by 2040, according to the ministry.“By enabling multiple universities to make use of each other’s strengths, the aim is to improve the quality of higher education offered in regional universities that will work with the local community to support regional development,” Nakajima said.Warning on over-relianceHowever, some experts warn of over-relying on online learning. Assistant Professor Masaaki Yamada from the Department of Epidemiology at the University of Toyama is critical of forcing a shift to online degrees without taking mental and physical health implications into consideration.“Curricula must not focus too heavily on online instruction,” he said. Referring to his own research, he said: “Data captured in 2020 when pandemic-related online teaching was the norm showed that almost 75% of answers of respondents to a survey wrote that ‘paper is better’ for study,” he toldUniversity World News. Students mostly reported problems with concentration and memory retention, and 80% also complained about eye strain.In response to a MEXT national student survey on remote learning published in 2022, 72% supported being able to study at their own pace. However, the downside is that 44% found it difficult to interact with instructors, while 40% said it was difficult to interact with other students.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

日本政府擴大在線學位課程支持 為了提供更多靈活的課程和學位機會，日本政府決定加強對在線四年制大學學位的支持。教育文化體育科學技術省（MEXT）官員指出，未來的日本高等教育將更加倚賴混合學位體系，以適應數字化時代的需求。

新政策允許地區大學共同開設在線課程，以提高教育質量，特別是在預期學生人數下降的地區。此外，Zen University 和 Sak University 等新興在線大學已經推出，提供有競爭力的在線學位，旨在促進教育公平。然而，專家警告，過度依賴在線學習可能會影響學生的心理和生理健康，需謹慎對待在線課程的設計與實施。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Universities in Africa are failing to produce quality leaders
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Universities in Africa are failing to produce quality leadersGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsExperts argue that failures to confront corruption in universities have consequences far beyond campus. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALWhat a crisis of integrity teaches you about leadershipLATIN AMERICAToday, more than ever, the quality of HE leadership mattersSOUTH AFRICAFault line: Student protests and the burden of leadershipAFRICAFellowship to play ‘transformative’ science leadership roleInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsAFRICAUniversities in Africa are failing to produce quality leadersMaina Waruru09 December 2025TweetThe higher education system in Africa has been guilty of producing corrupt future leaders who have failed the continent, denying millions of people, including the youth, hopes for a better future while, at the same time, failing to fight for good governance.Instead, Africa’s universities must fight for much-needed good governance by producing upright leaders who can be counted on to deliver development for the people.Across the continent, universities, themselves, have also not been spared from bad governance resulting from leadership failure and a loss of good values, said Dr Obiageli Ezekwesili, senior economic adviser at the Africa Economic Development Policy Initiative and founder president of Human Capital Africa. She was speaking during theACU Congress 2025, the flagship conference of the Association of Commonwealth Universities which took place in Nairobi, Kenya, recently.It was not possible to transform a “struggling” higher education on the continent without “deep domestic” reforms on governance, so as to strengthen opportunities for investment, the economic policy expert and activist noted.“Universities have a duty to mould African leaders who work for the common good as opposed to those who work to undermine the common good,” she said.At the same time, the institutions have the capacity to create a class of citizens who demand meaningful change from their leaders by advocating better governance, she added.“A transactional political class such as is prevalent in Africa at the moment has disdain for the poor. Poverty and lack of voice are related,” she noted.Currently, universities on the continent faced “significant” challenges related to funding and governance, but which cannot be seen in isolation from leadership problems faced in other spheres of life, Ezekwesili explained.The youth bulgeAccording to Ezekwesili, Africa’s huge youth demographic should be at the centre of any debate on the continent’s higher education, noting that the demographic will account for more than 85% growth in population by the year 2035.Therefore, she said, all development strategies should have the youth and human capital development at their core, adding that education should be viewed in a holistic manner without ignoring foundational learning.In many countries at the moment, inequality was fast spreading, locking many children out of education. “Only children of the middle class are making it,” remarked Ezekwesili, a former minister of education in Nigeria under President Olusegun Obasanjo.“In Nigeria, children are being abducted from schools, and this is something we predicted would happen by 2025, resulting from a mass of angry, disaffected youth if education failed as it has,” she revealed.Delivering a keynote address on a session titled, ‘Focus on Africa and what’s next for Africa?’, she suggested setting up schools of governance to try to “fix Africa’s politics”, noting that the populace on the continent had allowed the “political class to subvert public good to their own advantage”.Research had shown that the majority of Africa’s youth prefer democracy to authoritarianism, even as the governance concept continued to evade the continent, she told attendees during the 26-28 November event.“Even if democracy has been eluding Africa, it must be made to work. We have a duty to turn youth disillusionment into organised civic energy, and set standards for good governance,” she said.HE has much to offerLouise Haxthausen, the director of the UNESCO Regional Office for Eastern Africa in Nairobi, Kenya, said higher education held a huge potential to unlock economic growth in Africa and to help the continent face its various challenges.The sector was instrumental in ensuring peace and stability in a continent undergoing “rapid democratic change”, but faced challenges that included climate change and a huge youth population, among others.The future of Africa, Haxthausen said, will be determined by how the continent “educates its youth”, calling for the need of, not just provision of higher education, but quality higher education. African universities were against authoritarianism and favoured freedom of expression, she added.UNESCO was working hard to ensure that Sustainable Development Goal Four on universal education was attained in the region, appreciating that the goal was key to attainment of all the other SDGs.One of the initiatives the UN body was implementing in the region is theCampus Africa Programme, a Priority Africa flagship, dedicated to strengthening higher education, research and innovation across Africa.The initiative was, among other aims, supporting doctoral training and research, targeting underrepresented regions of the continent, she said.Besides the campus, UNESCO was advocating for digital inclusion of Africa’s education system on realisation that the “digital inclusion gap” was threatening access to university education.“While rapid spread of AI tools is necessary in Africa, it must be equitable and support access to education while reflecting African realities and using African languages,” Haxthausen told the delegates.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and humanities places by 24%DENMARKIndustry: ‘No need for more graduates in the humanities’DENMARKIs interest in language courses in critical freefall?DENMARKHumanities 'massacre' causes widespread protestsDENMARKSmaller studies including unique Eskimology face axeDENMARKSmall languages under threat as cuts biteInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank PositionsDENMARKMixed response to university funding for European languagesJan Petter Myklebust08 December 2025TweetThe Danish government has received a mixed response to its announcement that it will allocate DKK68 million (US$10.6 million) to universities for the purpose of sharpening students' European language skills and equipping them for regional cooperation in a new world order.Announcing the funding in apress releaseon 25 November, the Ministry of Higher Education and Research said: “The new global situation has made Europe more important for Denmark's international cooperation and has emphasised the importance of having strong cooperation and understanding for each other on the European continent.”News of the ministry’s allocation came on the heels of theannouncementearlier last month that the National Centre for Foreign Languages (NCFF) would be closed down at the end of the year due to a lack of state funding.It noted that the national strategy for foreign languages in higher education strategy was being redirected “to individual grants for individual areas” and said it had been “precisely the purpose” of the NCFF to cast a “long, unifying and comprehensive look at the entire area with the sole aim of promoting foreign languages, but in the contexts in which they now find themselves and must operate – as full competences or additional competences”.Funding breakdownThe government’s latest language package is made up of a DKK26 million allocation to increase the number of hours for practical language training in German, French, Spanish and Italian university programmes.A total of DKK11 million will be allocated to increase the number of hours for practical language training in the German and French subjects of teacher training, while DKK4 million will be taken in the form of a special grant for the establishment of small French classes in teacher training.An amount of DKK24 million will be allocated for strengthening opportunities for languages as additional skills at universities and DKK3 million will go towards strengthening the universities' development of subject didactics within foreign languages.Minister of Higher Education and Research Christina Egelund was quoted in the official press release as saying: “We find ourselves in a new geopolitical reality where we must safeguard European cooperation. More than ever, we need to be able to talk to each other, understand each other and trade with each other across languages, cultures and borders.“With the language package, we are allocating more money to language subjects than before, and we are doing away with the notion that language is only about grammar and belongs in the classroom. Language is about people, society, culture – and is important for business, international trade and diplomacy.”She was quoted byUniversity Postas saying it was not sustainable to only speak English if we want to be a cultured society.“Especially nowadays, it is crucial that we are able to communicate, understand each other, and trade across borders and cultures. I would go so far as to say that language skills are part of our European identity.”High demand for language skillsUnni From Andreasen, head of the School of Communication and Culture at Aarhus University who was the chair of a working group looking into the future of foreign languages at universities, which delivered itsreportearlier this year, toldUniversity World Newsit was “very positive” that the government has decided to invest in language education in Denmark.“Together with a number of great colleagues from our faculty, the Faculty of Arts, I have had the pleasure mapping out central directions of how our future language programmes should be developed.“We are very aware that these programmes must continue to ensure that graduates possess very strong written and oral language skills, but also that knowledge of communication, interculturality, and language technologies are central to their competencies,” From Andreasen noted.From Andreasen said the demand for skilled teachers and industry employees who have mastered one or more languages remains high. “And this needs to be addressed through the development of language programmes and by continuing to offer graduates from other disciplines the opportunity to strengthen their language skills.”Jesper Langergaard, director of the peak universities body Universities Denmark, also welcomed the news.“We are very pleased with the initiative to strengthen our language studies across the board.“In recent years we have experienced a drop in the number of applicants for our language studies. And concurrently, humanities do not get the same amount of external funding as other research areas, so these funds represent both an important moral and fiscal boost to our language studies.”Closure of NCFFThe directors of the NCFF, Mette Skovgaard Andersen and Hanne Wacher Kjærgaard, were less enthusiastic about the funding.“The Danish National Centre for Foreign Languages, NCFF, is happy for the universities but probably not as excited about the funding as the universities,” they said.They said the funding did not represent an increase in special funding for the languages area over the last 10 years.“In 2018, the NCFF was established as the most significant element in the former Danish National Strategy for Foreign Languages from 2017 and got funding of DKK100 million. And in 2021, the universities and the teacher training institutions got DKK40 million.“This totals 140 million over eight years, that is, DKK17.5 million per year. The DKK68 million is supposed to last for four years, that is, DKK17 million per year. In fact: it’s not actually an investment.”They went on to say that while universities and teacher training institutions get funding from the new initiative, the funding is primarily earmarked for oral and written proficiency often taught in separate courses.“Although language proficiency is a challenge in higher education, according to our analyses, it is not the most pressing problem. The most pressing problem is the constantly decreasing number of applicants to the language programmes. This can probably be explained by the content and structure of teacher education programmes. Higher language proficiency will not solve this,” they argued.They described the funding as a case of “robbing Peter to pay Paul”.“The investment in the NCFF as a knowledge centre with a comprehensive and holistic approach risks having been in vain since, at the same time, the NCFF will not receive new funding but will be closed down in December of this year.“The same goes for the National Strategy, which no longer exists. In our view, a long-term and comprehensive investment in foreign language education has therefore been exchanged for a short-term and particular investment,” Skovgaard Andersen and Wacher Kjærgaard said.“Based on our analyses, the general language problem in Denmark is systemic and structural, and major change is needed in most areas, not only the ones addressed by the DKK68 million.”New approaches are neededProfessor Hanne Leth Andersen, rector of Roskilde University and chair of the Danish Universities’ Education Policy Committee, said she supported the vision expressed by the minister around the need for a broader understanding of language use and teaching.“It is time to do something substantial about the language crisis which is well-known in many countries, and which is unreasonably aggravated by AI,” she said. “Language is the structure of our knowledge and the tool of our human relations,” she said.Leth Andersen said while children are fascinated by language, and they have a great capacity to learn, too many lose their motivation.“This calls for new approaches, with more focus on people and society – and on the way a second and third foreign language is transformative for the individual and its potential in general.”She argued it was essential to develop “not only language didactics as an important area for further research and development, but also different types of content and language integrated learning where language learning is integrated in other programmes.“Language should never be treated as only form. It is about content, relations, and cognition,” she said.She described the closure of the NCFF as “unfortunate ... now that expertise has been built up with many concrete projects in schools and colleges and as the centre was becoming a meeting place across languages and levels”.She said the closure stood in stark contrast to the fundamental need for a common platform for gathering knowledge about language teaching and for developing a coherent and coordinated approach to foreign languages throughout the education system.“I do not see another agency that can fulfil this role,” said Leth Andersen.What about other languages?Associate Professor Brian Arly Jacobsen at the Department of Cross-Cultural and Regional Studies at the University of Copenhagen toldUniversity World Newsthe minister’s announcement about strengthening language education in Denmark was welcome.“We all share the need for renewed investment in linguistic and cultural competence,” he said.“However, it is somewhat misleading to speak of ‘the language programmes’ in general when the initiative only includes the major European languages, except English.“A real language lift should also support Arabic, Turkish, Hebrew, Persian, Russian, Chinese, Japanese, Korean, Hindi, Portuguese and Greenlandic – languages that are equally vital for understanding a world marked by geopolitical tensions, migration, and global interdependence,” Arly Jacobsen said.Addressing the closure of the NCFF, he said the centre had provided invaluable knowledge and coordination across the entire foreign-language ecosystem.“Its closure risks weakening the long-term strategic foundation for language education in Denmark precisely at a moment when global and intercultural competencies are more needed than ever,” he noted.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand 丹麥政府宣布將撥款6800萬丹麥克朗（約1060萬美元）以增強大學的歐洲語言教學，旨在提升學生的語言能力以適應新的國際合作需求。此計劃包括增加德語、法語、西班牙語和義大利語的實踐課程時數，並強調語言在商業、國際貿易及外交中的重要性。

然而，該決策引發了混合反應。部分學者表示該資金對於語言教育的長期發展仍不足，並擔憂國家外語中心的關閉會削弱語言教學的整體策略。專家呼籲需要更全面的語言教育政策，以應對當前的語言危機和全球化挑戰。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: AI can help HE assess research more efficiently – Report
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: AI can help HE assess research more efficiently – ReportGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALAI won’t save higher education. It will further divide itGLOBALAI in HE: Assessment at risk or curriculum rethink needed?GLOBALHow AI is reshaping integrity, assessment in universitiesGLOBALRethinking student assessment in the age of AIGLOBALThe end of AI and the future of higher educationInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOMAI can help HE assess research more efficiently – ReportDorothy Lepkowska08 December 2025TweetNational guidelines and regulations are needed on the use of generative AI (GenAI) by universities in the United Kingdom to assess the quality of research for the allocation of public funding, found a study published on 1 December.Measures must also be taken to prevent the tools being used for research assessment from falling into the hands of external parties, including ‘tech bros’, who might capitalise on the information and data contained in academic research, the report’s author said.The study, led by the University of Bristol and funded by Research England, is the first to examine how higher education institutions are using AI to evaluate research quality and how AI might be used to save universities time and money.It focused on the Research Excellence Framework (REF), whose outcomes influence how £2 billion (US$2.7 billion) in public funding is allocated for research. The REF is criticised for being overly burdensome and costly for universities.The report,REF-AI: Exploring the potential of generative AI for REF2029, found some enthusiasm but also some scepticism among academics and professionals working in the sector about how AI should be used, highlighting the need for better oversight.Lead authorRichard Watermeyer, professor of higher education at the University of Bristol, said: “GenAI could be a game-changer for national-level research assessment, helping to create a more efficient and equitable playing field.“Although there is a lot of vocal opposition to the incorporation of it into the REF, our report uncovers how GenAI tools are nevertheless being widely – if currently, quietly – used, and that expectation of their use by REF panellists is high.”The last REF happened in 2021, and, following a review, changes to guidance for the next one,REF2029, are expected to be announced shortly. The total costs of REF2021 were estimated to be around £471 million (US$628), with an average spend of £3 million in each participating higher education institution. The costs for REF2029 are expected to be much higher.What academic and professional staff thinkThe study included a survey of nearly 400 academics and professional services staff and asked how they felt about GenAI tools being used for various aspects of REF2029.Watermeyer toldUniversity World Newsthat there is never a time in higher education institutions “when academics are not, in some shape or form, thinking about the next exercise and the review process.“Universities need to consider how AI can alleviate some of the work and what job it can do,” he said. “Can it help to review, or at least help to score research against the evaluation criteria set out with the REF guidelines for output and impact, for example?“Another dimension is how we can use the tools to do a more accurate job in helping to predict how the panellists, who do the formal assessments, will judge our research submissions.“I don’t think there was a university that we consulted whose staff didn’t think that the panellists themselves wouldn’t be utilising the [AI] tools because of the sheer enormity of the task and because it was good value for money and saved time.“Do we really want to take away our best academics and scholars from their current research to review work they’ve already completed?”He added that AI might even allow research evaluation to take place on an ongoing basis, providing a current and up-to-date view of the UK’s research ecosystem.However, Watermeyer said, there existed variations in the way AI was being used within and between institutions for the purpose of REF evaluations.Some universities were using the tools to gather evidence of their research impact in the wider world and to help craft related stories. In others, there was evidence of in-house tools being developed to streamline REF processes or GenAI being used to review, assess and score their research.Pro vice-chancellors’ viewsThe study used focus groups and interviews with 16 university pro vice-chancellors.Some were positive and regarded AI usage as inevitable: “This is the future…We need to lead into it…We’ve got to understand it and experiment a bit”. And, “just to put our heads in the sand and say ‘it’s not going to happen’ or ‘not on our watch’, I think, is very limiting of what the future might look like…I think there’s a lot of moral panic”.However, other feedback expressed doubt and mistrust and raised the possible limitations of AI: “I do just wonder if we’re just in a bit of an AI bubble at the moment, where we think everything’s going to be driven by AI, and suddenly over the next six, seven years, actually, we’re going to have a much greater clarity of what the limitations of AI are.”Another respondent commented: “We don’t trust them enough in order to really start backing away from conventional tools and supplementing them with AI tools…there are still very large numbers of administrators and researchers who have had no real experience of AI yet.”Watermeyer said: “Our findings show that opposition to the tools is concentrated among certain academic disciplines, chiefly arts and humanities and social sciences, and non-users. Professional services staff tend to be much more enthusiastic about the potential of GenAI for REF, as are those in post-92 institutions, which have considerably less resources to devote to REF processes.”He toldUniversity World Newsthat the study signalled the “first step to pull the use of AI out of the shadows”, even though concerns remained about aspects such as ethics and transparency.“We know AI is being used for this purpose, but it’s not being used in regulated ways, and there is no clear-cut governance around it,” he added.“Institutions are largely operating in the dark, and while they might have codes of practice statements, there is no requirement for them to disclose their AI use.“What we really need now is engagement within the sector, with funders, regulators, the research councils and all key stakeholders. This is really quite urgent.“We can’t roll back from this technology. We can’t put it back in a box or switch it off. So, there is a need to consider whether we need a standardised tool that every institution can plug into.”Eliminating need for consultantsWatermeyer said the use of AI would remove the need for adjunct industries, such as consultants, “who feed off universities” during the REF process. The money saved could be reinvested into research. “But you would need acceptance and trust in the tools, and we don’t have that yet,” he added.This was something the sector needed to develop and own. “There is a real concern that if this were to fall into an external party, including the tech bros, then questions around trust and acceptance become harder, when there are already low levels of this within some institutions.“There’s also a moral dimension here of organisations capitalising on things like data privacy and security, exploitation and manipulation of data.”The report made a host of recommendations, including that all universities should establish and publish a policy on the use of GenAI for research purposes, encompassing REF; that relevant staff should receive full training on the responsible and effective use of AI tools; and that appropriate security and risk management measures should be implemented.It also called for robust national oversight, comprising sector-wide guidance on usage for REF29 and a comprehensive REF AI Governance Framework. To help achieve equitable access to the technology among all higher education institutions, it advised that a shared, high-quality AI platform for REF could be developed and made accessible to all institutions.Co-author Tom Crick, professor of digital education at Swansea University, said: “The current disparate use of these tools needs clearer co-ordination into a standardised framework through which the sector can adopt and develop open, transparent and responsible practice.“This will be critical to mitigating inequitable practice by higher education institutions and across disciplines, and for even possibly levelling the playing field for this and future national research assessment exercises.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2025 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Scholars cautiously excited about drive to bridge research gap
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Scholars cautiously excited about drive to bridge research gapGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIANew plan to woo diaspora scientists amid US policy shiftGLOBALInternational higher education is not just an exportGLOBALHE should recognise community music’s role in developmentInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackNIGERIAScholars cautiously excited about drive to bridge research gapAfeez Bolaji10 January 2026TweetNearly six months after Nigeria’s Federal Ministry of Education launched the Bridging Research, Innovation, Development and Global Engagement (BRIDGE) initiative, the diaspora platform has begun to record progress.Nigerian scholars and professionals abroad in various fields have been delivering lectures and seminars via Zoom, exchanging ideas with their colleagues and students at home, Folasade Biriowo, the ministry’s director of press and public relations, toldUniversity World News.The seminars are run in series spanning four to five months, and participants get certificates of participation at the end of the programme.Biriowo said the level of participation has been “encouraging and will get better as awareness increases”, pledging that the ministry “will aggressively pursue” the BRIDGE objectives in 2026 by engaging more academics and professionals in the diaspora.The Minister of Education, Dr Maruf Alausa, alongside Nigerian Vice President Kashim Shettima, and the Executive Secretary of the National Universities Commission, Professor Abdullahi Yusuf Ribadu, among other critical players, launched BRIDGE at a hybrid event in Abuja on 29 July 2025 to connect Nigerian academics and professionals in the diaspora with universities and research centres across the country to promote stronger collaboration in scholarship, research capacity, teaching methodologies and industry relevance.BRIDGE aims to close gaps in learning and research through targeted solutions to enhance institutional capacity, improve learning outcomes and drive impactful research. The initiative aims to increase research output and global rankings of Nigerian tertiary institutions by increasing the number of globally indexed publications, thereby enhancing the nation’s academic reputation.The platform also targets more industry-academic partnerships by strengthening links between research and industry in Nigeria to drive greater commercialisation of innovations. It advocates capacity development and improved STEM-M (science, technology, engineering, mathematics, and medicine) education quality to foster a more skilled workforce and increased employability.Scholars abroad express measured hopeThough several Nigerian academics and professionals in the diaspora who spoke toUniversity World Newsapplauded BRIDGE, they were not convinced it would be implemented effectively, citing similar programmes in the past crippled by poor commitment on the part of the government.Farooq Kperogi, a Nigerian-American professor of journalism and emerging media at Kennesaw State University in Georgia in the United States (US), is excited about the initiative, but fears a lack of political will might undermine its goals. He said Nigeria has some of the most talented and accomplished academics and professionals in the diaspora. Still, institutions back home have historically lacked systematic channels to access expertise.“It is, on paper at least, a promising and long-overdue step toward closing the gap between Nigeria’s vast intellectual capital abroad and the pressing developmental needs at home. Many in the diaspora will hesitate if the programme falls into the familiar traps that have plagued past initiatives: red tape, inconsistent funding, poor follow-through, and the politicisation of appointments.“If participants feel their expertise is being used symbolically rather than substantively, or if institutional inertia makes collaboration frustrating, enthusiasm will quickly dissipate. Sustained interest depends on trust, professionalism, and demonstrable impact,” Kperogi said.Dr Esther Folarin, a research associate at the University of Warwick in the United Kingdom (UK), said BRIDGE signalled a significant step towards improving Nigeria’s higher education sector and positioning local institutions competitively globally.However, she is concerned about the culture of ineffective government policy implementation, which could discourage professionals from participating in the initiative.“There often seems to be a lack of accountability, with no one held responsible for following through on their commitments. This raises a critical question: Will people be willing to invest their time, resources, and knowledge into this initiative if they do not see tangible results from their efforts? Historically, a lack of visible outcomes has been a significant discouragement for many,” she said.Luring industry experts to classroomsIn avideoposted on X, a fellow of the Higher Education Academy and senior management consultant in the UK, Dr Dipo Awojide, enthused about BRIDGE’s potential to allow professionals like him to teach and contribute to research in universities and research institutes in Nigeria.“I have been following the diaspora BRIDGE programme created by the Ministry of Education in Nigeria, and I must say it is a very good one. I spent over a decade in academia teaching strategic management, international business, innovation, and enterprise before I switched to management consulting. For people like me, a former academic, it is an opportunity to connect with Nigerian universities and research institutes.“I could get the opportunity to now teach at the University of Lagos, Obafemi Awolowo University, University of Abuja, Osun State University, Ahmadu Bello University Zaria ... even if it is teaching a module for one academic year. As someone who has also supervised two PhD students and been an internal examiner for a PhD graduate, this is an opportunity to contribute to research in Nigeria,” Awojide said.Lessons from CADFPAcademics say BRIDGE can draw valuable lessons from the success stories of related programmes such as the Carnegie African Diaspora Fellowship Programme (CADFP), which enables African-born scholars in the US or Canada to collaborate with African institutions on projects, teach or mentor, and co-develop curricula. Since its inception in 2013, CADFP has sponsored 749 African scholars in the diaspora to travel to the continent.A 2024 Carnegiearticlerevealed that, through CADFP, Mojúbàolú Okome, a professor of political science at the City University of New York, assisted her alma mater, the University of Ibadan in Nigeria, to develop an interdisciplinary political science curriculum. Another fellow, Professor Kefa Otiso from Bowling Green State University in Ohio, developed a geography curriculum at Kisii University in Kenya, his native country.The fellows’ collaboration with their home country institutions did not end after the programme. Okome agreed to continue academic exchanges between the University of Ibadan and the City University of New York. At the same time, Otiso secured an agreement between Kisii University and his home institution to research water and sanitation issues, comparing Lake Erie in Ohio and Lake Victoria in Kenya.A six-month post-fellowshipsurveyshowed that 78% of the fellows were still engaged in academic activities with their host institutions in Africa. A one-year alumni survey of 58 fellows indicated that 84% communicated with scholars and administrators at their host institutions at least once or twice a month, and 41% visited the host institutions after the initial project visit for professional reasons. It was also found that technology enabled sustained collaboration at no or low cost.Making BRIDGE workTo attract more professionals to BRIDGE, Folarin noted that participants needed assurance it would not be business as usual.“If participants are assured of the initiative’s commitment to active results and accountability, it will encourage more individuals – especially those who might previously have been discouraged – to get involved. Building this trust is essential for fostering greater participation and ensuring the success of BRIDGE,” she said.According to Folarin, it is crucial to identify academics in the diaspora who are eager to apply their expertise to improve Nigeria’s education system. “When we engage the right people, their enthusiasm will help drive the initiative forward. I am genuinely impressed by the potential impact of BRIDGE and look forward to seeing how it will contribute to the advancement of our educational landscape.”If implemented with transparency, sustained funding, and institutional buy-in, Kperogi believes BRIDGE could help catalyse innovation, strengthen research output, and foster a culture of collaboration that benefits both Nigeria’s higher education system and the global community.He said: “Beyond the open invitation, there should be trust, clear structures, and mutual benefit. Nigerian institutions must demonstrate that collaborations will be professionally rewarding, logistically feasible, and free of bureaucratic bottlenecks.“This means transparent governance of the programme, clear expectations for engagement, streamlined processes for joint research and teaching, and concrete support such as funding for travel, research infrastructure, and administrative assistance. Equally important is cultivating respect for diaspora expertise while ensuring partnerships are reciprocal rather than extractive.”Leveraging technology for seamless communication, actively publicising success stories, and creating a sense of shared national purpose can inspire more academics to see BRIDGE, not just as a government scheme, but as a collective project to strengthen Nigeria’s intellectual and developmental capacity, Kperogi said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News

尼日利亞推動BRIDGE計劃促進學術合作 尼日利亞聯邦教育部於2025年推出的“橋接研究、創新、發展與全球參與”（BRIDGE）計劃，旨在連結海內外學者，以促進學術合作和提升研究能力。該計劃已開始進行線上講座和研討會，並獲得參與者的積極反響。儘管專家對計劃的潛力表示期待，但也擔心過去類似計劃的實施效果不佳會重演。BRIDGE計劃的成功依賴於透明的管理、持續的資金支持及參與者的信任。學者們希望此舉能加強尼日利亞高等教育的全球競爭力，並促進行業與學術界的合作。

尼日利亞推動BRIDGE計劃促進學術合作 尼日利亞聯邦教育部於2025年推出的“橋接研究、創新、發展與全球參與”（BRIDGE）計劃，旨在連結海內外學者，以促進學術合作和提升研究能力。該計劃已開始進行線上講座和研討會，並獲得參與者的積極反響。儘管專家對計劃的潛力表示期待，但也擔心過去類似計劃的實施效果不佳會重演。BRIDGE計劃的成功依賴於透明的管理、持續的資金支持及參與者的信任。學者們希望此舉能加強尼日利亞高等教育的全球競爭力，並促進行業與學術界的合作。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: New international university to produce market-ready graduates
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: New international university to produce market-ready graduatesGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsPrime Minister Mostafa Madbouly’s proposal to establish a new Egyptian international university was approved on 21 November. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksEGYPTEgypt needs 65 new universities to meet rising demand – ReportEGYPTNew international academy aims to shape sustainable citiesEGYPTNew university branches to target region’s skills gapsAFRICAEntrepreneurial HE alliance aims to create millions of jobsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEGYPTNew international university to produce market-ready graduatesWagdy Sawahel09 January 2026TweetEgypt plans to establish an international university linked to global scientific and research networks as part of efforts to position the country as a regional hub for knowledge, innovation and high-quality higher education, capable of producing graduates able to compete in regional and global labour markets.Called the International University of Science, Arts, and Technology (IUSAT) and located in the New Administrative Capital, a draft presidential decree for the new institution was approved by the cabinet at its 21 November meeting chaired by Prime Minister Mostafa Madbouly, according to a statement posted on the officialFacebook pageof the Egyptian Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research.The ministry described IUSAT as a “pioneering project” that will support government efforts to establish universities with a global identity, combining theoretical foundations with applied sciences.IUSAT is also a flagship project under Egypt’s NationalStrategyfor Higher Education and Scientific Research, launched in 2023. The strategy prioritises international accreditation, global partnerships, innovation, entrepreneurship and institutional transformation.The new university further supportsEgypt’s Vision 2030and the United Nations’ 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.Its establishment aligns with the findings of a November 2025 market researchreporttitled,Higher Education Market in Egypt 2024-25, which indicated that Egypt needs at least 65 new universities by 2030 to meet economic needs and rising demand for higher education.Expansion of Egypt’s university sectorAs of August 2025, Egypt has128 universitiesacross several tracks, including:• 28 public universities;• 32 national universities;• 37 private universities;• 12 technological universities;• Nine branches of foreign universities; and• 10 universities operating under various agreements.The rapid expansion reflects both demographic pressures and the state’s strategy to enhance competitiveness and modernise higher education.International academic model and institutional structureIUSAT is expected to adopt internationally benchmarked academic standards and connect to global scientific and research networks. It will provide an integrated educational environment to foster creativity and innovation, with the aim of strengthening Egypt’s competitiveness and preparing graduates for regional and global labour markets.The university will offer undergraduate and postgraduate programmes across science, arts and technology. It will comprise 16 faculties grouped under four international academies, with a fifth relating to architecture and urban planning:• International Medical Academy: medicine, dentistry, pharmacy, physical therapy, nursing;• Academy of Engineering, Science, and Information Technology: engineering, science, computer science, IT and artificial intelligence;• Academy of Humanities and Social Sciences: media, languages and translation, law, education;• Academy of Economics and Management: business, tourism and hospitality; and• International Academy of Architecture and Urban Planning: architecture and urban planning, arts and design.The university will also be permitted to establish additional specialised colleges, higher institutes and research units after completing the required legal procedures.Educational, economic and cultural significanceProfessor Morad Ahmed Morad of the faculty of medicine at Tanta University toldUniversity World Newsthat having a globally oriented institution like IUSAT would be significant for students, the academic community and the country.“It enhances the quality of local higher education, provides globally competitive degrees and offers students a more affordable way to obtain an international education, along with enhancing career opportunities in a globalised world,” he said.He added that the institution could attract international students, boost the economy, create jobs and strengthen Egypt’s position as a regional educational hub.Morad noted that IUSAT could also serve as a form of cultural diplomacy, enhancing soft power, fostering cross-cultural understanding and supporting international research collaboration, thereby contributing to the development of a knowledge-based economy.According to theGlobal Soft Power Index, which evaluates countries’ soft power based on indicators including higher education and science capabilities, Egypt ranked 38th out of 193 states worldwide in 2025 – the highest position among African nations, followed by South Africa (41), Morocco (50), Nigeria (77) and Algeria (78).Challenges aheadMorad cautioned that IUSAT may face challenges, including regulatory complexity and the need to develop curricula that produce analytical, creative and technology-oriented skills suited to modern labour markets.“These challenges need strategic policymaking and collaboration to improve visibility and streamline operations,” he said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Graduates share unique insights into doctoral experience
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Graduates share unique insights into doctoral experienceGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksSWEDENNew report shows how hard it is to be a foreign PhD studentSWEDENIndustry calls for action over loss of doctoral graduatesSWEDENRules eased for third-country PhD students and researchersSWEDENLengthy visa delays may be deterring student talent – AuditSWEDENBusiness criticises migration rules for foreign graduatesSWEDENData on foreign PhD graduates staying on to work revealedSWEDEN‘Largest’ investment by a university in gender equalitySWEDENEmployment status given to nearly all PhD candidatesInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredSWEDENGraduates share unique insights into doctoral experienceJan Petter Myklebust09 January 2026TweetA new collection of essays written by local and international former PhD students at Swedish universities sheds light on their experiences of doctoral study. It also contributes to a broader understanding of what motivates international graduate students to leave or remain after graduation.ThePhD Wanderer: Essays on Doctoral Studies in Swedenis written in English and edited by Cristina Ghita and Martin Stojanov from Uppsala University.In addition to Ghita and Martin Stojanov, contributors include Yunchen Sun, Aya Rizk, Per Fors, Emil Ahlström, Paulina Rajkowska, Shruti Kashyap, Niki Chatzipanagiotou, Olof Wadell, Chelsey Jo Huisman, Naghmeh Aghaee and Blerim Emruli. There are also interviews with two senior academics: Professor Christina Keller and Professor Thomas Taro Lennerfors.The writers explore what is termed “the human dimensions of becoming a scholar" through the metaphor of the PhD wanderer: “an early-career academic venturing into unknown terrain, sometimes becoming lost, at other times finding exciting directions, committing to new routes, and encountering new travellers along the way’.Ghita said she and her co-editor, Stojanov, were motivated to compile the collection after realising that the struggles and joys they experienced as PhD students were also shared by many of their peers.“Once the dust had settled and we had both successfully defended our dissertations, we began to discuss more seriously the idea of collecting reflections on the doctoral experience in Sweden from academics who have recently graduated.“Our hope was that these accounts would reach both prospective and current doctoral students who might otherwise feel alone in their struggles,” she noted.A non-linear processGhita said it was important for them to highlight the “non-linear nature" of their experiences, hence the imagery of wandering, which evokes exploration and playfulness rather than a straightforward path from point A to point B.“I believe the book would not have been possible without the institutional support and advice we received along the way from senior colleagues, some of whom also appear in the book in two interviews.“This, I believe, is also true of the PhD experience more broadly (in Sweden and elsewhere): support from senior academics and institutions matters deeply and can significantly shape the success of a doctoral project," Ghita said.In the chapter “To learn or not to learn Swedish: On balancing learning investments during PhD studies”, Aya Rizk, a senior lecturer and researcher at the Information Systems and Digitalisation department at Linköping University in Sweden, who grew up in Cairo, reflects on her challenges in learning Swedish.As the introduction to the collection notes, although “clear expectations for international PhD students to learn Swedish are rarely explicitly stated, there are existent assumptions that they should achieve a sufficient level of proficiency during their doctoral years.“Aya Rizk offers a reflection that provides insight into the experience of an international PhD student who is motivated to learn Swedish, actively taking steps towards reaching this goal.”In a chapter titled “Flying the interdisciplinary flag in doctoral projects”, Ghita, who defended her PhD thesis,Technology in Absentia: A New Materialist Study of Digital Disengagementat Uppsala University in 2022, “revisits her diaries from the doctoral period, reflecting on the entry in a new discipline and the eventual integration of theories and elements from other fields”.The introduction goes on to note: “While interdisciplinary projects are becoming increasingly accepted and encouraged in research, they remain risky territory for many PhD students, as expectations still exist to make contributions within a specific discipline.”Support systemsPaulina Rajkowska, who holds a PhD in human-computer interaction from Uppsala University, reflects on personal challenges and their impact on her doctoral work in a chapter, “Stranded at sea: Navigating crisis, responsibility, and activism in academia”.According to the introduction, the chapter goes beyond “a mere recounting of turbulent times, offering a portrayal of how struggles can remain invisible to those around.For these reasons, Paulina Rajkowska illustrates the importance of support systems and the crucial role trade unions play by sharing her motivations, involvement, and current full-time work in the Swedish Association of University Teachers and Researchers”.In the chapter “We, nomad: Towards an ethical mode of becoming in Swedish academia”, Shruti Kashyap, now a tenured senior lecturer at Örebro University School of Business in Sweden and a researcher at the Royal Swedish Academy of Sciences, offers a “powerful and evocative account of the challenges faced by international PhD students, particularly women of colour”.In a chapter on “Striking the balance between independence and academic integration during PhD studies”, Naghmeh Aghaee and Blerim Emruli, both senior lecturers and researchers at Lund University’s School of Economics and Management (LUSEM), Department of Informatics, discuss the potential social isolation that PhD students may experience.The authors reflect on the challenges of finding a balanced level of independence while navigating academic social structures and offer practical advice on how this can be improved.Role of supervisorsOne of the most discussed elements of PhD studies, aside from the writing process – supervision and the relationship between supervisor and candidate – is taken up by Niki Chatzipanagiotou, then a senior lecturer and researcher in the Department of Informatics at Lund University in Sweden.In a chapter titled “Wandering through change: The role of supervisors and support networks in the doctoral journey”, Chatzipanagiotou credits her supervisors with being supportive and encouraging.Now about to enter a permanent position at a Swedish university, Chatzipanagiotou is an example of a successful PhD candidate having taken up scientific work in one of the prioritised STEM fields in Sweden.Her research focuses on the intersection of computer-supported cooperative work and human-computer interaction. Grounded primarily in ethnographic methods, her work explores the use of information systems in information-intensive organisations and broader societal contexts, with studies conducted in Sweden, Australia, and Greece.Chatzipanagiotou toldUniversity World Newsher doctoral journey as an international PhD student was “academically demanding and personally transformative”.“What made success possible for me was not only the formal structure of the doctoral programme. Supportive and trusting supervision, combined with strong academic and social networks, played a decisive role,” Chatzipanagiotou noted.She said her supervisors combined high academic expectations with empathy, guidance, trust, and encouragement, helping her to navigate uncertainty, mistakes, and moments of self-doubt. “They actively supported my integration into Swedish academia by involving me early in teaching, supervision, and research activities,” she said.This helped her to develop professional confidence, a sense of belonging, and an identity as an academic colleague rather than a temporary guest, she said.Treated as a ‘whole person’“Equally important was the broader academic and social environment. Informal peer networks, research school communities, and opportunities to engage internationally helped counteract the isolation many international PhD students experience. Being treated as a whole person, not just a researcher, mattered greatly, especially during personal challenges,” she stated.Chatzipanagiotou said in her experience, when international doctoral students are given space to grow, trusted with responsibility, and supported both academically and emotionally, they are more likely to envision a future in Sweden.“These human and relational factors are, in my opinion, critical if a country’s doctoral education system wants not only to attract international doctoral talent but also to retain it.”Pointing to areas that could be strengthened, she said. “While doctoral training prepares candidates well for research, there is often limited guidance on what comes next: how academic career paths function in Sweden, how academic ranks and expectations differ from other countries, and which strategic steps are important immediately after graduation.”Chatzipanagiotou said clearer communication about academic career structures, encouragement to develop a realistic three-to-five-year career plan after graduation, and access to senior mentors alongside previous supervisors could significantly strengthen doctoral education.“Such measures would not only help PhD graduates navigate academia more confidently but could also increase the likelihood that internationally trained researchers choose to remain and contribute to Swedish higher education in the long term,” Chatzipanagiotou noted.Growth in international PhD candidatesThe essay collection stands alongside recent findings of an investigation commissioned by the Confederation of Swedish Enterprises which suggest that the number of international doctoral candidates is outstripping local candidates in four key STEM fields of: artificial intelligence semiconductor technology, quantum technology and nanotechnology.Thereport,Where do the researchers go? A mapping out of researchers’ career paths within four strategically important technologies for Swedenfound that 70% of those graduating in the four fields are from countries outside Sweden. The highest proportion is within AI, where 80% of doctoral candidates come from other countries.The report notes that 40% of those graduating in the four fields examined in 2022 had left Sweden three years after graduation – a trend that was expensive and was weakening Sweden’s competence base. It also found that the business sector dominated as an employer compared with the academic sector.The confederation’s report recommended that Swedish authorities increase the number of Swedish doctoral students. However, it also called for the government to strengthen the country’s attractiveness to international researchers and for incentives to encourage them to stay on after graduation.The report also called for research milieus to have strong industrial relevance in order to facilitate the translation of research into innovations and competence in the business sector.“It is positive and a strength that Sweden is attracting research students from all over the world. Without international competence we would not be able to fill our doctorate places. At the same time, the state is funding the research training to supply the society with research competence.“If a large proportion are leaving the country after graduation, we are losing out on an important aspect of our higher education,” Emil Görnerup, who is responsible for research and innovation in the Confederation of Swedish Enterprise, said in an article about the report.“Over time we risk that the Swedish competence base is eroding when the recruitment of Swedish doctorates is declining,” Görnerup wrote.Gender and nationalityOn 17 December 2025,Curie, the newsletter of the Swedish Research Council, carried anarticleby Frida Lawenius, the director of the Union of Natural Sciences and the Steering Group of the National Junior Faculty, and others, headlined “Good conditions are required for researchers to choose Sweden”.In it Lawenius wrote: “National Junior Faculty, which is a Swedish umbrella organisation supporting junior researchers, has identified systematic differences in career options based on country of origin and gender at six Swedish higher education institutions. This is not related only to salaries but also to access to services and research funding.“There is a clear pattern. Women researchers and international researchers are in the majority on the basic level, but as higher up in the career ladder they come, the fewer they are.”Sylvia Schwaag Serger, a professor at the School of Economics at Lund University, president of the Royal Swedish Academy of Engineering Sciences (IVA) and former head of international collaboration at Vinnova, the Swedish Innovation Agency, toldUniversity World Newsin order to stay at the technology forefront, Sweden needed to work “more ambitiously and systematically” in attracting and retaining international talents and competence.“This includes, for example, effective startup visas for entrepreneurs and universities working with enterprises in recruiting world-leading technology expertise to Sweden, but also recognising and valuing skills, qualifications and competence acquired abroad,” she said.“Currently, researchers recruited from abroad often have to contend with somewhat inward-looking systems, rules and norms that are not suited to people who have been educated or worked abroad.“Overall, it requires sending clear and consistent signals to globally mobile talents that Sweden welcomes and needs them and provides an attractive environment for them to flourish in,” Schwaag Serger said.Pernilla Wittung-Stafshede, former chair of the leadership group of GENIE (Gender Initiative for Excellence) at Chalmers University of Technology and now Charles W Duncan, Jr Welch Professor of Chemistry at Rice University, Houston, Texas, toldUniversity World Newsit had become harder and harder for international students to stay in Sweden after graduation.“I do not think the solution is to lure more Swedes to become PhD students. We want the best people, whatever nationality they have,” she said , agreeing with Schwaag Serger’s argument that Sweden should become more attractive to international talent and actively recruit them.No immediate solutionsOn the recently published collection of essays by PhD students, Topias Tolonen-Weckström, outgoing chairperson of the Doctoral Committee at the Swedish National Union of Students, toldUniversity World Newsa key contribution of the essay anthology was to remind the Swedish academia, policymakers, and those wishing to pursue a PhD that there are no identical journeys in pursuing a PhD: doctoral students not only come from vastly different backgrounds and all over the world, but they also participate in compiling their individual thesis based on their individual efforts and interests.“The essays appear to analyse prevalent topics among PhD studies in Sweden for which no immediate solutions have been found, such as language barriers, other difficulties faced by international students, and the necessity of support structures.“Difficulties faced by international students are particularly awkward for Sweden. Every international PhD that moves from Sweden reduces the amount and depth of highly skilled labour in Sweden.“Moreover, it is known that fields of STEM occupy the highest share of international students: combatting losing these experts should be of the utmost importance to every government that wishes to pursue advances in technology and innovation,” he said.Associate Professor Andreas Nord at Lund University, who is the science policy spokesperson for the Young Academy of Sweden, said it was important to “facilitate newly graduated foreign PhD graduates’ entry into the Swedish job market.“Meanwhile, Swedish universities should ensure that doctoral education remains an attractive career choice for a broad range of students in Sweden and beyond and that bachelor and masters programmes align with and evolve in response to the needs of research and technology,” Nord said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News
