Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIAIndia is primed to be a key player in global student marketsINDIAIndia’s journey to HE superpower is a shared responsibilityINDIAModi’s third term: Growth in internationalisation expectedINDIACould foreign branch campuses keep more students in India?INDIAMore effort is needed to internationalise higher educationINDIAIndia’s higher education is opening up. But is it ready?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsINDIAReport signals India’s ambition to be global talent magnetShuriah Niazi09 January 2026TweetIndia must make a strategic shift from being primarily a supplier of global talent to becoming a magnet for it, according to a landmark policy report published by India’s apex public policy think tank, NITI Aayog.The report explores how India can move away from an over-reliance on outbound student mobility towards a more resilient and inclusive model of “internationalisation at home”.Thereport, titledInternationalisation of Higher Education in India: Prospects, potential, and policy recommendations, was published last month and outlines how the country’s national roadmap for global engagement in higher education as envisaged under the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020 can be implemented.Developed by a consortium led by the Indian Institute of Technology (IIT) Madras, with Acumen and the Association of Indian Universities as knowledge partners, the report is among the most comprehensive policy exercises on higher education globalisation to emerge from the Global South.It draws on survey responses from 160 higher education institutions across 24 states, deliberations at a national workshop hosted at IIT Madras with 140 Indian and international participants, and interviews with experts from 30 global institutions in 16 countries.‘Unsustainable’ talent flow imbalanceThe recalibration of internationalisation comes at a critical moment. Student flows to traditional destinations such as the United States, the United Kingdom, Canada and Australia are under growing pressure from tightening visa regimes, geopolitical uncertainty and rising costs.India currently sends more than 1.3 million students abroad annually but hosts only around 50,000 international students – a stark imbalance the report describes as economically and strategically unsustainable.For every international student coming to India, nearly 28 Indian students go overseas.According to Dr Pankaj Mittal, secretary general of the Association of Indian Universities, the report identifies both India’s comparative strengths and persistent gaps.“India has the intellectual depth and institutional capacity to reimagine itself as a global higher education destination,” she said, pointing to cost-effective education, English-medium instruction, ICT capabilities, and internationally recognised expertise in engineering, management and professional disciplines.“The Indian Knowledge System (incorporating an indigenous knowledge framework), she added, brings “cultural and interdisciplinary richness”.However, she noted that India still lags behind global education hubs in areas such as research collaboration, global visibility, infrastructure, student experience and international branding.“From global models, India can learn research-led internationalisation, coordinated partnerships, clear post-study pathways, competitive scholarships, and strong mobility and credit-recognition frameworks, while retaining its distinct knowledge and cultural strengths,” Mittal toldUniversity World News.Speakers at the report’s release backed the intention to shift India towards being a magnet, rather than an exporter, of talent and argued that internationalisation must be embedded within India’s long-term development vision of becoming a developed nation by 2047.System-level reformsProfessor Raghunathan Rengaswamy, dean (global engagement) at IIT Madras and a core contributor from the institute’s knowledge partner team for the NITI Aayog report, toldUniversity World Newsthe report confronts a structural gap in India's higher education landscape: the absence of a coherent, outcomes-orientated strategy for internationalisation.Despite India’s scale and academic depth, he noted that global engagement has remained piecemeal, mobility-driven and weakly aligned with national objectives.The report, he said, reframes internationalisation as a system-level reform agenda, integrating student and faculty mobility, research collaboration, regulation, financing and institutional strategy.Rengaswamy argued that regulatory complexity, limited funding instruments, weak global branding and the lack of formal faculty mobility pathways have constrained India's global competitiveness.Flagship proposals such as the Vishwa Bandhu Scholarship and Fellowship, streamlined regulatory processes and alumni-led global outreach, he added, are designed to reposition India from a predominantly student-sending country to an active global education hub.Ambitious targetsAmbitious targets underscore this intended shift, including the goal of hosting 100,000 international students in central and state universities by 2030.The report recommends that each of the 54 central universities be designated to host a centre of excellence for a specific country, backed by dedicated funding and staffing support.“This will enable the University to emerge as the host institution and nodal point for the said country, develop expertise on that country through multidisciplinary research, and align thematic or disciplinary strengths (for example, technology, humanities, agriculture) of the said country with India’s requirements,” the report says.The report also anticipates the launch of more than 15 international branch campuses (IBCs) in India by September 2026.Rather than viewing IBCs as substitutes for studying abroad, the report frames them as complementary pathways that can help retain Indian talent, attract global faculty, and deliver international curricula at a significantly lower cost.Rahul Choudaha, professor and chief operating officer at the University of Aberdeen’s Mumbai campus – the first international campus of a Scottish university in India – toldUniversity World Newsthat the report arrives at a moment of global disruption.“Given that traditional student mobility, along with many research engagements, is under stress in the US and beyond, this report encourages stakeholders to find alternative modes of engagement without losing the essence of internationalisation,” he said.Choudaha noted that the report makes a compelling case for “internationalisation at home” as a more inclusive and relevant model in the current geopolitical and economic context.“It offers a grounding in how internationalisation at home can be more relevant than ever, embedding global perspectives into everyday academic practice rather than depending solely on physical mobility,” he said.Beyond standalone campuses, the report proposes flexible embedded models that allow foreign universities to operate within Indian institutions through rental or partnership arrangements.Such models, it argues, could enhance financial sustainability, extend global-quality education beyond metropolitan centres, and support the creation of specialised global education hubs in areas such as artificial intelligence, biotechnology, climate science and STEAM – expanding STEM to include arts, medicine and management. These hubs could draw inspiration from ecosystem-based models such as GIFT City.Implementation is keyHowever, the report is explicit that policy intent alone will not suffice. Its impact will depend on implementation.Key recommendations include simplifying visa and mobility rules for students and faculty, easing eligibility norms for foreign universities beyond narrow global ranking criteria, digitising approval processes, and providing clarity on taxation and foreign contribution regulations.In terms of funding, the proposed Bharat Vidya Kosh – a US$10 billion national research fund supported equally by government contributions and diaspora matching funds – is highlighted as potentially transformative. If realised and combined with global fellowships, joint research funds and a new Indian alumni network, it could significantly strengthen India’s research ecosystem.Equity also features prominently. Financial assistance mechanisms such as the proposed Vishwa Bandhu Scholarship aim to diversify international classrooms in India while providing Indian students – particularly those outside elite institutions – with meaningful global exposure at home.Choudaha emphasised that the expansion of international branch campuses should be seen not as a replacement for traditional mobility, but as part of a blended ecosystem.“This is about widening choices and expanding opportunities for collaboration, impact and growth,” he said, adding that such campuses could also deepen engagement with employers and the research community.Academics involved in the consultation process said success will depend on coordinated action between government, regulators, public universities and the rapidly expanding private sector.If sustained, they argue, India’s internationalisation strategy could help stem brain drain, reduce the estimated US$30 to US$40 billion spent annually on overseas education, and generate high-value jobs in research, innovation and education technology.The report marks a decisive attempt to reposition India not just as a participant but as a shaper of the global higher education landscape.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEUROPEHE sector demands €60 billion in EU funding for Erasmus+Brendan O’Malley08 January 2026TweetEuropean higher education sector organisations have issued a joint statement calling on member states, the European Parliament and the European Commission to ensure an allocation of at least €60 billion (US$70 billion) for Erasmus+ from 2028 to 2034 during the ongoing negotiations on the European Union’s next long-term budget or multiannual financial framework.Thestatementsays, that as highlighted in theDraghi report, over the coming decade Europe needs “significantly more talent that can work together across borders, particularly in strategic sectors and at high skill levels”.“In this context, investing in people – through higher education exchange and collaboration – is central to Europe’s future prosperity, competitiveness and resilience, as reflected in the European Education Area, the Union of Skills and the Competitiveness Compass.”The statement warns that underinvestment in education would undermine the EU’s own political objectives, and Europe can only achieve its ambitions in education, skills and talent if Erasmus+ is “ambitiously resourced”.“Therefore, we welcome the European Commission’s efforts, under the leadership of Executive Vice-President Roxana Mînzatu, to secure an increased budget for Erasmus+ in a highly constrained financial environment.”The organisations say the proposed allocation of €40.8 billion for 2028 to 2034 represents a “step in the right direction, recognising the programme’s value and political importance”.But the European Parliament’s call to triple the Erasmus+ budget compared to the period 2021 to 2027, as well as the sector’s experience from the current programme and a wide range of new challenges and ambitions articulated at the EU level, “demonstrate that a higher level of investment (at least €60 billion) is needed”.The statement is signed by 17 organisations, between them representing thousands of higher education institutions and millions of students.They include the European University Association, CESAER (non-profit association of universities of science and technology), the Coimbra Group, the Erasmus Student Network, the European Students’ Union, the European Association for the Applied Sciences in Higher Education (EURASHE), the Guild of European Research-Intensive Universities, the Academic Cooperation Association, the AURORA network, the European Association for International Education, the European Consortium of Innovative Universities, the European University Foundation, the German Academic Exchange Service (DAAD), the League of European Research Universities, the Network of Universities from the Capitals of Europe (UNICA), the Mediterranean Universities Union (UNIMED) and the Young European Research Universities Network.Proven impactThey say Erasmus+ is one of Europe’s most tangible success stories, and its contribution to citizens’ lives and to Europe’s future needs investment that matches its proven impact.“For nearly 40 years, this popular flagship programme has empowered millions of learners, strengthened institutional cooperation, deepened European integration and fostered global outreach.“At a time of heightened geopolitical tensions, Erasmus+ delivers long-term returns in skills, employability, innovation capacity and civic engagement.“Few EU programmes combine such broad public support with such clear and measurable societal impact. Erasmus+ contributes directly to developing a highly skilled, mobile and adaptable workforce, strengthens Europe’s knowledge base and enhances societal cohesion and democratic resilience.”The organisations warn that the proposed Erasmus+ budget put forward on 16 July 2025 by the European Commission would barely maintain the level of activities in 2027, once inflation and rising costs, the integration of the European Solidarity Corps and additional responsibilities are taken into account.“In practice, this would leave very limited scope to expand access, improve quality and deliver on newly agreed priorities.”These priorities include mobility targets endorsed by member states in the council recommendation “Europe on the Move”, alongside commitments to inclusive learning mobility and talent attraction under the Union of Skills agenda.They also include the consolidation of the European Universities Initiative and the Centres of Vocational Excellence, plus enhanced inclusion of learners with fewer opportunities, and new actions such as scholarships in strategic fields.“Erasmus+ is one of the EU’s most cost-effective instruments. In a context of competing policy priorities, it stands out as a programme where relatively modest additional investment delivers long-term returns across multiple EU objectives, from competitiveness and skills to cohesion, inclusion and global engagement,” the statement says.The organisations argue that a budget of at least €60 billion would allow Erasmus+ to serve Europe’s geopolitical priorities by promoting it as an attractive destination for talent and building sustainable partnerships with institutions worldwide, in close interplay with the Global Europe programme.‘Concerted investment’ neededWelcoming the sector’s clear call for investment in the programme ahead of a key debate in the European Parliament’s Committee on Budgets on 15 January, EUA Secretary General Amanda Crowfoot said that when all factors, including inflation and new priorities, are taken into account, the proposed Erasmus+ budget for 2028-2034 would at best allow the programme to continue as it is.“It would not be able to fund enhanced and additional activities to underpin the Union of Skills and the European Education Area, as proposed by the European Commission.She said this means that there will not be enough to pay for more inclusive learning mobility nor properly funded university alliances, let alone for the new scholarships in strategy priority fields.“Education can make an invaluable contribution to the EU’s competitiveness agenda, but this requires concerted investment.”Jan Palmowski, secretary-general of The Guild, said at a time when Europe is increasingly challenged economically and geopolitically, the EU must invest in future generations by boosting one of its most successful programmes, Erasmus+.“This is critical not just for our competitiveness. Our economic position is also intertwined with the international partners we can rely on, which Erasmus+ strengthens in unique ways, not least through strong interconnections with the Global Europe programme that extends its impact in lower- and middle-income countries.”Ivana Didak, The Guild’s head of higher education policy, said: “Providing high-quality education by scaling up pedagogical innovation will be one of the key tasks of the sector in the coming years. And if we really want ‘Erasmus+ for all’ in the long term, we must increase the funding for the core features of the programme.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News

Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksAUSTRALIAGovernment policy has put universities into survival modeAUSTRALIARisks and opportunities for universities from policy shiftAUSTRALIAUniversities call for funding policy overhaul as cuts biteAUSTRALIAWho is to blame for the loss of trust in universities?AUSTRALIADon’t be ‘dodgy’: How to build trust in higher educationAUSTRALIAWhat does the budget fine print say about higher education?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsHuman-Computer Interaction - Associate/Full Professor TenuredPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyAUSTRALIAUniversities back senate’s call for stronger governanceShadi Khan Saif07 January 2026TweetUniversities and stakeholders have welcomed the contents of the Australian Senate report into the quality of university governance, published in December.The report calls for stronger institutional oversight at the nation’s universities, restoration of the primacy of teaching and research functions and greater emphasis on the needs of staff, students and communities.Universities Australia chief executive officer Luke Sheehy toldUniversity World Newsstrong governance was essential to the health of the sector.“Strong governance is critical because it builds trust and transparency and puts students, staff and communities at the centre of everything we do,” he said.The Group of Eight chief executive Vicki Thomson toldUniversity World Newsthat Australia’s research-intensive universities were “absolutely committed to strengthening governance and delivering for Australia”, stressing that universities are “national assets – essential to the nation’s prosperity, security, and global standing”.Thomson said the committee’s recognition of universities’ public good mission and the need for robust, transparent governance aligned closely with the Go8’s longstanding advocacy for a sector that delivers for students, the nation and the world.The National Tertiary Education Union also welcomed the final report as a watershed moment for the sector. National President Dr Alison Barnes said the inquiry had finally placed long-running issues such as casualisation, wage theft and toxic workplace cultures firmly on the national agenda.Barnes said the report’s eight recommendations provided a clear blueprint for reform but warned that their impact would depend entirely on whether governments chose to implement them in full.Decades of corporatisationWhen the Senate Education and Employment Legislation Committee tabled its long-awaited final report into the quality of university governance on 11 December 2025, it landed as more than another policy document.For many staff, students and sector insiders, it marked a pivotal moment in a growing reckoning over how Australia’s public universities are run, who they serve, and whether their public mission has been eroded by decades of corporatisation.The inquiry, which drew on more than 300 submissions and five public hearings, concluded that many Australian universities have drifted away from their core purpose of advancing public education and research.Instead, senators found that governance structures increasingly resemble corporate boards, prioritising financial performance, managerial control and executive autonomy over academic quality, transparency and accountability.Evidence presented to the committee went well beyond abstract governance theory. Staff and students described lived experiences of insecurity, exclusion and declining morale.Casualisation emerged as one of the most consistent and troubling themes, with evidence showing that in some institutions up to 60% of undergraduate teaching is delivered by casual or insecure staff.Many described being employed on short-term contracts with limited protections and little input into decisions that directly affect teaching quality and student outcomes.Students told senators they felt sidelined by strategic decisions driven primarily by budget pressures rather than educational needs.Senate committee chair Marielle Smith said the inquiry had exposed governance failures that were undermining trust in public institutions. She said universities were established for the public good and should be governed accordingly, warning that corporate-style models had weakened accountability and distanced decision-makers from staff and students.Prioritisation of teaching and researchThe eight recommendations are broad in scope but tightly focused on restoring the primacy of teaching and research. They call on state and territory governments to review university-establishing legislation to ensure public education and research are explicitly prioritised.University councils would be required to have duties and performance assessments clearly tied to those missions. The report also urges the Tertiary Education Quality and Standards Agency (TEQSA) to strengthen governance guidance, develop new monitoring frameworks for course quality and staffing, and issue a statement of expectations on academic governance.Other recommendations include mandatory public reporting on casual and insecure employment, annual reviews of teaching quality and staffing balance, and stronger oversight of student interests and institutional reputation.Thomson said the Go8 strongly backed the committee’s focus on student experience, noting the group has consistently argued that students must be central to university policy and practice. “Every reform must be measured by its impact on student experience and outcomes,” she toldUniversity World News.She also welcomed the committee’s emphasis on research, saying a number of recommendations reinforced the primacy of research as a core university function. Research, she said, must be treated as a national priority rather than an optional extra, with sustained and strategic investment critical to Australia’s innovation capacity, resilience and global leadership.Sheehy said the Senate committee’s work complemented existing reform efforts already underway, including the Expert Council on University Governance.“This report complements the Expert Council on University Governance’s work and various other initiatives to strengthen governance in our sector,” he said, adding that Universities Australia would continue to engage with governments and sector leaders.“It’s important work, and we’ll continue to engage with the government, the University Chancellors Council and others to improve and strengthen governance for the good of those we serve – our students, staff and communities.”Growing political and public concernsThe recommendations build on the committee’s interim findings earlier in 2025, which included proposals to cap vice-chancellor salaries, improve transparency around consultant spending and strengthen disclosure of executive remuneration.Together, the findings reflect growing political and public concern about executive excess and opaque decision-making at publicly funded institutions.Several universities have already become flashpoints in the wider governance debate, most notably the Australian National University. Former vice-chancellor Professor Genevieve Bell resigned in September 2025 after months of controversy over restructures, job losses and cost-cutting measures.Her departure followed sustained staff protests and criticism of the university’s governance culture, with TEQSA commencing a compliance review.Interim Vice-Chancellor Professor Rebekah Brown has pledged to rebuild trust, but staff representatives say the ANU case illustrates deeper systemic failures of council oversight.Governance tensions have also surfaced at other institutions, including the University of Technology Sydney, where staff delivered an overwhelming vote of no confidence in Andrew Parfitt, vice-chancellor. The NTEU has cited such cases as evidence that governance failures are widespread rather than isolated.Funding models review neededThe governance inquiry also intersects with broader debates about university funding. Thomson said the Go8 acknowledged the committee’s identification of the urgent need to review and reform funding models, pointing to declining public investment and the structural distortions created by the Job-Ready Graduates scheme.She said the group continued to call for a sustainable funding framework that enables universities to deliver on their public mission.She also noted that independent senator David Pocock had highlighted the critical importance of research funding and the need to refocus universities on their public good role, aligning with the Go8’s position that funding reform must sit alongside governance change.According to Thomson, the committee’s report represents an important step towards addressing deep structural problems driven by Australia’s distorted funding model.However, she said genuine reform would require governments to enable better use of existing resources, fund the full cost of research, cut unnecessary red tape that diverts resources away from students, staff and communities, and abolish the Job-Ready Graduates scheme as a priority.With the report now tabled, attention has shifted to whether governments will act. State and territory governments have been urged to review the legislation underpinning public universities, while at the federal level proposed changes to TEQSA’s standards will depend on consultation outcomes and political will.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: UK agrees deal to rejoin EU study, train abroad programme
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: UK agrees deal to rejoin EU study, train abroad programmeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED KINGDOMHopes rise that UK is edging towards rejoining Erasmus+UNITED KINGDOM-EUROPEThe challenge of replacing Erasmus+ with the Turing programmeUNITED KINGDOM-EUROPEUK to stay in Horizon Europe but drop out of Erasmus+UNITED KINGDOM-EUROPEWhy has the UK ditched participation in Erasmus+?Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUNITED KINGDOM-EUROPEUK agrees deal to rejoin EU study, train abroad programmeBrendan O’Malley17 December 2025TweetThe United Kingdom has reached agreement with the European Union to rejoin the Erasmus+ programme from 2027, widening opportunities for young people from all backgrounds, learners, educational, youth and sport staff to study and train abroad.The agreement to join Erasmus+ in 2027 fulfils a key commitment made at the UK-EU Summit in May and will open up study and training abroad opportunities for young people across the country, as well as staff.Erasmus+ offers opportunities around education, training, youth, culture and sport for participants of all ages. More than 100,000 people in the UK could benefit from the scheme in the first year alone. Data shows that studying abroad can boost the career prospects of students, especially those from disadvantaged backgrounds.In a joint statement, the EU’s Commissioner for Trade and Economic Security Maroš Šefovi and EU Relations Minister Nick Thomas-Symonds MP said the agreed financial terms “represent a fair balance between the UK’s contributions and the benefits the programme offers and pave the way for United Kingdom participation in the programme in 2027”.The UK's EU Relations Minister Nick Thomas-Symonds said: “Joining Erasmus+ is a huge win for our young people, breaking down barriers and widening horizons to ensure everyone, from every background, has the opportunity to study and train abroad.He said this is about more than just travel: it’s about future skills, academic success, and giving the next generation access to the best possible opportunities.Responding to the Erasmus+ announcement today, Vivienne Stern MBE, chief executive of Universities UK, said the decision is “fantastic news for the UK, and for students and universities here and across Europe”.“The agreement marks a huge step forward in our relationship with the EU and will offer life-changing opportunities for thousands of students”.Josep M Garrell, president of the European University Association (EUA), said: "As the voice of European universities, we have worked closely with our UK members to advocate for their return to Erasmus+."By restoring bridges between our universities, this will support student and staff mobility, cooperation between universities (including through the European Universities alliances) and joint policy development. The benefits of Erasmus+ go far beyond financial support, promoting mutual understanding, respect and freedom – shared values that are more vital than ever in today’s world.”Bid to improve EU-UK relationsThe decision by the Labour government follows from the UK-EU Summit agreement to strengthen cooperation through a new strategic partnership, which represented a significant attempt to improve EU-UK relations.Post-Brexit the previous Conservative government did agree to association with Horizon, the EU’s flagship research programme, but refused to rejoin Erasmus+, claiming it was poor value for money.Instead, it set up the Turing programme in 2021, supporting only outbound mobility from the UK and geared only to short stays of up to three to four weeks. It was also aimed at widening access to study abroad.In contrast, previous Erasmus+ participation paid for reciprocal arrangements and funded both inward and outward mobility of young people between the UK and EU before the Conservative government of Boris Johnson decided to leave the EU scheme as part of the EU withdrawal agreement of 31 December 2020.Today the Labour government stressed that the Erasmus+ programme will create educational and training opportunities for British apprentices, further education students and adult learners, as well as those in higher education.The government says it will work closely with institutions and young people to maximise take-up – particularly among disadvantaged groups.Opportunities available on Erasmus+ include further education students and apprentices going on work placements in leading European companies.Talks acceleratedLast week, EU Relations Minister Nick Thomas-Symonds met with EU Trade Commissioner Maroš Šefcovi in Brussels to instruct teams to step up talks before the end of the year.Thomas-Symonds said: “Today’s agreements prove that our new partnership with the EU is working. We have focused on the public’s priorities and secured a deal that puts opportunity first.”UK Minister for Skills, Baroness Jacqui Smith, said: “Erasmus+ will open doors for thousands of students and staff right across the country in universities, schools, colleges and adult education.“This is about breaking down barriers to opportunity, giving learners the chance to build skills, confidence and international experience that employers value.”UK Minister for Sport, Tourism, Civil Society and Youth Stephanie Peacock said Erasmus+ will play an important role in “ensuring young people from all backgrounds can access transformative experiences”.The government said the financial terms include a 30% discount compared to the default terms under the Trade and Cooperation Agreement, ensuring value for UK taxpayers while guaranteeing full participation benefits.The UK contribution for 2027 will be approximately £570 million (US$762.5 million). This covers the 2027 and 2028 academic year. Any participation in Erasmus+ in the next Multiannual Financial Framework will need to be agreed upon in the future and be based on a fair and balanced contribution.Supporting 100,000 students compares favourably withnumbersin the programme pre-Brexit. In 2018 EU Erasmus+ funds for the UK covered 49,000 students (17,000 British outgoing and 32,000 coming in) and over 7,000 staff, at a cost of around €160 million (US$194.4 million).In a statement, the EUA said: "Erasmus has long been a cornerstone of international collaboration in higher education, and the UK rejoining will help rebuild and deepen partnerships that are vital for research, mobility and cultural exchange."For Universities UK, Stern said the UK government and the EU deserve credit for moving these complex negotiations forward at pace and reaching a mutually agreeable deal.“We look forward to working with our universities and our partners in Europe to ensure that the programme plays a pivotal role in rebuilding our international partnerships and delivers the best possible outcomes for the UK.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleSCinet’s tagline is: ‘Immensely Powerful and Insanely Fast’IEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleImpact Stories 2025: A Collective Journey of Transformation in African HEThe Education Collaborative teamSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: How private universities are challenging the HE status quo
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: How private universities are challenging the HE status quoGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksASIANew Asia ranking to recognise private, public universitiesGHANAFour more public universities envisaged as demand growsSOUTH AFRICAPrivate HE is an indispensable part of the larger ecosystemInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredUGANDAHow private universities are challenging the HE status quoAulah Najjuuka17 January 2026TweetFor decades, Makerere University and Kyambogo University dominated Uganda’s higher education landscape, with private institutions often seen as ‘plan B’ for students. But that perception is shifting rapidly.Today, universities such as Kampala International University (KIU), Uganda Christian University (UCU), Ndejje University and the Islamic University (IUIU) are emerging as competitive centres of learning, research and innovation, challenging the long-standing public university monopoly.Rising tide of charters, recognitionOne sign of the shift is the growth in chartered private universities. According to the National Council for Higher Education (NCHE), Uganda now has 19 chartered private universities, up from 11 two years earlier. A charter gives institutions the authority to award fully recognised degrees, run postgraduate programmes, and compete for greater academic visibility.For Ndejje University, which received its charter recently, the recognition marked a turning point. Vice-Chancellor Dr Olivia Nassaka Banja described the charter as a “measure of confidence” in the university’s development. “The charter strengthens our legitimacy, but our true mission is to produce graduates grounded in competence and character,” she said.KIU, UCU and IUIU are already chartered and hold strong reputations. The 2025 uniRANK listings place KIU first among private universities, followed by UCU, Ndejje, IUIU and others. Their presence in these rankings indicates greater local and regional credibility.Rankings no longer public monopolyThe Webometrics 2025 global rankings also revealed a notable shift. While Makerere remains Uganda’s top university, KIU ranked second nationally and first among private institutions, placing 1,516th globally. For many students, this signals that academic quality and visibility are no longer exclusive to public institutions.“I applied to KIU because I wanted a university that offered flexibility and global connectivity,” Michael Matovu, a second-year public administration student, said. “Its ranking gave me confidence in the degree I’ll graduate with.”Strategies powering the risePrivate universities are investing in research capacity, digital infrastructure and partnerships. This reflects a broader understanding that visibility and competitiveness matter in the global education ecosystem.UCU, for instance, promotes a holistic model that combines academic excellence with spiritual formation, e-learning options and strong visibility in sports. At IUIU, academic relevance and student performance earned it national recognition in 2019 as Best Private University by the Visionaries of Uganda Consortium.At KIU, administrators attribute their ranking success to deliberate investment in research, innovation and partnerships. “We are immensely proud of this consistent recognition from Webometrics,” Professor Muhammed Ngoma, vice-chancellor of KIU, said. “Maintaining our leadership reflects our strategic investments in digital infrastructure, cutting-edge research, and a vibrant online presence that makes our academic output accessible globally.”What does this mean for students?For many learners, private universities offer an accessible pathway into higher education, particularly for those who narrowly miss government admission. Flexible entry requirements, multiple campuses, and a more personalised environment make these institutions attractive alternatives.“I didn’t join through public sponsorship to a public university, but UCU believed in my potential,” Sarah Nakitto, a second-year law student, said. “The academic support here has helped me grow in ways I didn’t expect.”At Ndejje University, students like Grace Nakimuli say the charter reassured them of quality. “I wanted a university that recognised my potential,” she said. “Ndejje gives me that.”For others, the rise of private institutions strengthens their career confidence. Muhammad Rajab, a final-year ICT student at KIU, said: “Having a private university with global rankings behind me gives confidence when I think of regional job markets.”However, the NCHE has cautioned that some private universities risk becoming “glorified high schools” if they fail to invest in research, community engagement, and postgraduate training. Quality assurance remains essential for long-term credibility.Challenges and sustainabilityGrowth brings challenges. Scaling infrastructure, hiring qualified staff, maintaining academic rigour, and securing funding require strategic planning. Some private institutions struggle to balance affordability with sustainability. Others still face employer scepticism that favours public university graduates.“Students today seek relevance. Private universities must innovate to keep up and many are doing that, which is a good thing,” said Geoffrey Ssenoga, a senior lecturer at UCU.A major policy shift is on the horizon. Starting in July 2025, the government will manage admissions to both public and private universities through the new National Higher Education Admission System, or NHEAS. While the intention is fairness and standardisation, some stakeholders fear it may reduce the flexibility that has long made private universities appealing.Shift widens accessUganda’s youth population is growing rapidly, and public universities cannot absorb all qualified applicants. Private institutions play a crucial role in widening access, diversifying academic programmes and supporting national development.By strengthening research output, enhancing digital learning, and building industry-linked programmes, private universities can help address Uganda’s skills gaps in fields such as ICT, health sciences, renewable energy and entrepreneurship.Uganda’s higher-education landscape is evolving, and private universities are at the centre of that transformation. KIU, UCU, Ndejje, IUIU and others are no longer viewed as fallback options, but are emerging as dynamic institutions, capable of shaping the country’s academic future.The challenge now is sustaining quality. If private institutions continue to invest in research, innovation and student support while regulatory bodies enforce standards, Uganda’s surge in private universities will not just change the status quo; it will redefine what higher education in the country can be.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. This one understands itKhalifa University staffSponsored ArticleThe demand for well-trained software engineers is soaringIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleHolistic support is key to empowering students to succeedDeresh RamjugernathSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: ‘Catastrophic’ year for US academic freedom and autonomy
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: ‘Catastrophic’ year for US academic freedom and autonomyGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsFaculty and students silenced by widening restrictions, as US government pressure reshapes the boundaries of academic freedom and autonomy. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESWhat Columbia University’s deal means for higher educationUNITED STATESHarvard fights back as antisemitism battle escalatesUNITED STATESGovernment unfreezes, refreezes NIH funding to ColumbiaUNITED STATESHarvard stands firm against Trump’s ‘retaliatory’ threatsLATIN AMERICAHarvard shock throws the spotlight on university autonomyGLOBALScientists self-censor as Trump axes research fundsUNITED STATESDeSantis’ tech bros take aim at Florida’s universitiesUNITED STATESFlorida’s case against HE accreditors fails in federal courtInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyUNITED STATES‘Catastrophic’ year for US academic freedom and autonomyNathan M Greenfield16 January 2026TweetMore than 50% of America’s 16.2 million college and university students now attend institutions in states where there is at least one law, or school or state policy that restricts what can be taught.In 2025, at least 15 state legislatures – in all parts of the country – put in place gag orders or indirect censorship restrictions, despite constitutionally recognised rights to freedom of speech and a number of Supreme Court decisions defining and defending academic freedom.Secretary of War Pete Hegseth ordered the federally controlled military academies and schools on military bases to cull hundreds of books that deal with race or gender diversity issues from their library shelves; a federal judge ordered that the books be returned to the school’s shelves. In the meantime, the military academies have eliminated majors and removed modules that dealt with these issues from their curricula.These and hundreds of other assaults on colleges and universities by state governments and the Federal government led by President Donald J Trump, such as the 19 executive orders aimed at higher education institutions and the revocation of more than 6,000 international student visas, led the authors of PEN America’s report,Expanding the Web of Control: America’s censored campuses 2025(Web of Control), published on 15 January, to characterise 2025 as a “catastrophe”.The report documents a trend that alarms international observers, who openly compare the United States to authoritarian countries like Hungary.“America’s politicians have made a full-scale campaign against colleges and universities, with a concerted focus on speech. The toll is immense. Fear among faculty members and administrators is widespread. Self-censorship in teaching and research is rampant,” it states.Web of Controldescribes the “direct forms of censorship – educational gag order laws thatdirectlyrestrict teaching about such topics as race, gender, American history, and LGBTQ+ identities – as well as indirect forms of censorship, meaning, state laws and policies that attack the practices and institutions that enable academic freedom to thrive, therebyindirectlychilling the climate for free speech.”Parallels with dictatorshipsLiviu Matei, head of the School of Education, Communication and Society at King’s College London, who was provost of Central European University when Hungarian Prime Minister Viktor Orbán’s attacks on it forced the university to move from Budapest to Vienna, toldUniversity World Newsvia email thatWeb of Control“is an extraordinary report documenting the unprecedented threats to academic freedom in the US.“Moreover, the report makes it plain that this is a time of existential threats to science itself and to advanced education, which have helped to build the foundation for making the US the greatest country in the world for more than a century. If science and education are now treated as in a primitive dictatorship, the country can only become akin to a primitive dictatorship – socially, politically, and also economically.”Matei sees troubling comparisons between the actions of US federal and state administrations and Viktor Orbán’s moves to seize control of universities and other actions that have brought Hungary, “once a Central European tiger, to the brink of collapse [and] into a deep moral, economic, and political state of marasmus”.There are also, Matei said, “troubling parallels” with communist Romania.“The report talks about vengeance and retaliation as a policy tool in education and science. In communist Romania, the wife of Nicolae Ceauescu, the communist dictator, knew how to get her revenge against science, scientists and higher education.“Comrade Elena Ceauescu was semi-illiterate, never studied anything, but wanted to be recognised as a genius chemical engineer and leader in communist science policy. The regime bought national and international prizes for her, and a Nobel in Chemistry would not have upset her either,” he noted.“What did upset her was when researchers at the Institute of Psychology in Bucharest told her to her face, after a talk she gave, that she was not a scientist and should stop pretending she was. The very next day, there was no institute of psychology in Bucharest, and all study of psychology in Romanian universities was forbidden until the end of the communist regime, for almost 20 years,” he stated.“That was primitive vengeance and retaliation. But is it very different from what is happening now in the US? Or maybe similarly eager leaders who cannot get the Nobel for peace would be willing to settle for one in chemistry?” he concluded.(By coincidence, several hours before this author received Liviu’s email, Venezuelan Opposition leader María Corina Machado met with Trump at the White House and gifted him the Nobel Peace Prize medal awarded to her last year, an act which, as the Nobel Peace Prize committeestated last week, does not equal Trump receiving the prize: “Once a Nobel Prize is announced, it cannot be revoked, shared, or transferred to others. The decision is final and stands for all time.”)Academic freedom: A contested pastAmerica’s famous tradition of academic freedom is not, the report shows, uncontested – and, indeed, many of the criticisms made today by right-wing politicians and organisations are more than a century old. During the First World War, critics charged professors with being unpatriotic and enthralled to both Marxism and anarchism. In the 1950s, during the McCarthy period, professors were hounded for being communists, which led to the destruction of careers and self-censorship.At the same time, conservatives like William F Buckley, who in 1951, the year after he graduated with his bachelor’s degree from Yale University, publishedGod and Man at Yale: The Superstitions of ‘Academic Freedom’, argued, according to the report, that professors “had no sense of truth or beauty, having traded eternal truths for passing fashions”.Buckley, who went on to found the in-house journal of mid-20th-century conservatism, theNational Review, was central to the burgeoning “populist critique of higher education” that wanted to restore “to the academy the simple virtues of patriotism, individualism and love of God”.American higher education grew enormously in the following decades, and the federal government became a major funder of biomedical research, engineering, physics, and area studies and foreign languages, “and many other disciplines deemed critical during the Cold War”.Despite this, the critique Buckley midwifed “would prove remarkably adaptive”, says the report. It was “seemingly able to diagnose every prevailing anxiety of the day, whether from the civil rights or anti-[Vietnam] war activism, feminism, decolonisation or political correctness run amok”.By 2010, support for higher education began dropping among Republicans. This, combined with the high cost of tuition (which led to the student debt crisis) and the “declining number of conservatives in the professoriate”, led many conservatives to question the value of college degrees.In addition to drawing on critiques like Buckley’s, conservatives also drew on the philosopher James Burnham, whom the report characterises as having advanced a “‘burn it all down’ nihilism” visible in some of the MAGA movement’s more vicious attacks on the higher education sector”.In 1941, inThe Managerial Revolution, Burnham warned that as the economy became more complex, “power would become concentrated in the hands of unelected and unaccountable experts and credentialed elites” – which fed the conservatives’ accusation in the early 2000s that the higher education sector was “an incubator for harmful ideas run by out-of-touch elies more interested in coddling students [who had come to be seen as customers] rather than educating them.”Between 2021 and 2023, Republican politicians introduced over 300 gag orders targeting their states’ K-12 systems as well as publicly funded state universities; almost all restricted “teaching about race, gender and sexuality”.AsUniversity World Newshas reported, Florida’s (Republican) governor Ron DeSantis was among the most active, declaring on 8 November 2022 in hissecond election victory speechthat “Florida is where woke goes to die”.The campus unrest that followed Hamas’ 7 October 2023 attack on Israel and Israel’s subsequent war on Hamas and the “devastating toll on Gazan civilians and infrastructure” has, the report says, changed the “cultural and political dynamics on campuses”, with “instances of antisemitic, anti-Arab and anti-Muslim hate and violence.”Trampling on shared governanceA year before Trump was inaugurated at the end of January 2025, Harvard University president Claudine Gay and University of Pennsylvania president Liz Magill appeared before the House of Representatives’ Committee on Education and the Workforce and, the report writes, were “forced to answer antagonistic questions and respond to lawmakers’ grandstanding while testifying under oath” (about the encampments and charges of rabid antisemitism on their campuses).“And it seems likely that there was a link between the disastrous testimony of Columbia’s Minouche Shafik before the Committee on April 17, 2024, and her decision to end negotiations with student protestors one week later”.To drive home the Republicans’ ire, Representative Mike Johnson, the House Speaker, went to Columbia’s campus in New York and publicly demanded Shafik resign; she did a few months later.This set the stage for a series of federal actions that have been discussed in these pages, including executive orders, thefreezing of hundreds of millions of dollars in research fundsand other actions that affectedHarvard,Columbiaand several other universities.“Under enormous pressure from the federal government,” saysWeb of Control, “both Columbia and Harvard rewrote their student discipline policies in ways that trample shared governance. The obvious trigger was the wave of pro-Palestinian protests in 2024. In Columbia’s case, the university moved authority over student misconduct from the University Judicial Board, on which faculty have a prominent place, to the Office of the Provost.This change was one of the Trump administration’s key demands and a condition of releasing Columbia’s frozen federal grants. It also happened without either the knowledge or the consent of Columbia’s faculty senate, an alarming and potentially illegal attack on shared governance.”Harvard too centralised the disciplinary process in its president’s office.Under the precept of fostering “viewpoint diversity”, Columbia has begun “the process of placing the Middle East, South Asian, and African Studies department under academic receivership for a minimum of five years”, a move that PEN America believes is less about diversity than “censoring of certain viewpoints”, such as those of Joseph Massad, a professor of modern Arab history who criticised Israel after it was attacked by Hamas.In July, Columbia entered into a formal legal settlement with the federal government, under which it must ensure that courses about the Middle East are “comprehensive and balanced”. The agreement runs contrary to more than a century of shared governance tradition and a number of Supreme Court cases that said that faculty control the curriculum.For, as the report says: “The Columbia settlement makes the threat explicit: Should the federal government conclude that Columbia has failed to meet these obligations (and notwithstanding language in the settlement insisting that none of its provisions ‘shall be construed as giving the United States authority to dictate faculty hiring’), it may seek a binding injunction from a federal judge.”According to PEN America, Linda McMahon, the secretary of education, took what amounted to a victory lap, saying that the agreement was a “template for other universities around the country”.Notable gag ordersMore than 80 educational gag order bills were introduced into America’s 50 state legislatures. PEN America has singled out several, including Ohio’s Senate Bill 1 (SB 1) – a 42-page behemoth that “mandates” that every course demonstrates “intellectual diversity, while also forbidding evenvoluntarydiversity, equity and inclusion training”.Intellectual diversity is defined as “multiple, divergent, and varied perspectives on an extensive range of public policy issues”. Further, SB 1 “specifies that the exercise of professional judgement may not be ‘misused’ when determining how to ‘accomplish intellectual diversity’ or on whether to endorse the ‘consensus or foundational beliefs of an academic discipline’”.The requirement that “student learning outcomes” must demonstrate the university’s commitment to intellectual diversity must leave more than a few professors scratching their heads; in a course on the history of Christian theology, for example, must at least one assignment be cut from the clothWinston Churchillwove when he said: “If Hitler invaded Hell, I would make at least a favourable reference to the Devil in the House of Commons”?Ohio’s legislators provided for on-site policing: “Students, student groups, and faculty members may file complaints alleging a violation of the prohibitions and requirements or that an administrator, faculty member, staff, or even a student has interfered with the enumerated ‘intellectual diversity rights’ of ‘another’.”The day beforeWeb of Controlwas published (15 January), in an article about the banning of Plato’sThe Symposiumat Texas A&M, these pages showed the practical reality of Texas’ “sweeping educational gag order policies that are quite explicit about their censorial aims.Faculty members and academic departments are now restricted in how they can discuss race or ‘race ideology’, gender identity, and sexual orientation without special permission from university leaders. These sorts of policies don’t carry the imprimatur of law, but they can cut off access to speech for tens of thousands of faculty and students,” says the report.Texas’s SB 37 does not list subjects and issues that areverboten. Rather, as PEN America shows, like bills introduced in other states, for example, Indiana, Utah, and West Virginia, it increases state control by undermining shared governance.Shortly after, SB 37 terminated faculty senates and councils, and replaced them with “toothless advisory bodies” on which the university president appoints up to half the members, and the governor appoints another member, a statewide ombudsman. These bodies are “prohibited by law from wielding ‘final decision-making authority on any matter’.”One week after this root and branch alternation to Texas A&M’s governance, the university “fired Melissa McCoul, an English instructor, for discussing gender and sexuality in the classroom” (prohibited by SB 17 and directives issued by the regents). Two days later, “Texas State University fired a tenured history professor for discussing the hypothetical overthrow of the US governmentat an academic conference” (emphasis added).Model billsNo other country has the ecosystem of right-wing organisations – many funded in part by the right-wing libertarian oil man Charles Koch and foundations he controls – that seek, sometimes individually, sometimes together, to influence state legislators.TheGoldwater Instituteand the American Legislative Exchange have numerous links, even as both produce “model mills” that “claim to offer general prescriptions for free expression on campus, including mandating free speech education” but will, in fact, regulate it. These bills are sent to thousands of legislators in all 50 states, which explains why the language of bills that limit academic freedom is so similar.In an email, Jonathan Friedman, PEN America’s Sy Syms managing director of US free expression programmes, toldUniversity World News: “Spurred by the onslaught of model bills pushed by conservative think tanks, state legislatures are setting record levels of censorship as they mix and match confusing and often contradictory provisions into law. Despite the clear attacks on academic freedom and public opposition, these laws are fuelling censorship and spreading across state lines like wildfire."In December 2025, the Goldwater Institute unveiled its model bill titled “The American Higher Education Restoration Act”.In language that Buckley, who strongly supported the Republican nominee for the presidency in 1965, Republican Senator Barry Goldwater, could have penned, the bill’s authors say, “the academic research process is broken. At best, faculty research is trivial; at worst, downright un-American … high-profile journals, which ought to prioritise the pursuit of truth, are making publication decisions on the basis of identity politics and political activism.”Their solution, explained PEN America, is to divide universities into three parts: STEM faculty and those who teach “Americanism and Western Civilisation” courses would teach fewer courses and have an “expedited path to tenure”. (The authors ofWeb of Controldid not find it necessary to comment on what the model bill’s authors took for granted: a Bucklerian, or, to use a present day reference, Trump’s resurrection of American exceptionalism, as well as the fact that Western Civilization is the subordinate term, meaning it should be taught through Americanist eyes.)The third faculty grouping includes departments of social sciences, humanities, and professional studies, as well as fine and performing arts. Professors in these fields would have a heavier teaching load, and governing boards would have the final say on new hires. Special committees – “dominated by politicians and political appointees, to oversee a fund for state-sponsored research of ‘public value’” – would rule on requests for time for research.And then there was the stick: were a university to grant time for research to a member of this third group without approval, “the state attorney generalor any other taxpayercould file for an injunction in court”.The Goldwater Institute says that these restructured universities – from which, incidentally, a preference would be given to faculty publishing in “open-access venues” rather than peer-reviewed journals – would end wasting tax dollars on “corrupt academic research”. PEN America’s analysis, by contrast, is that “it would furnish politicians with one more tool to decide what gets taught, who gets hired, which topics are researched, and who gets tenure. In other words, it is about political and ideological control.”‘Sloppy’ and ‘extreme’ lawsSome of the laws analysed inWeb of Controlare drafted so badly they approach incoherence. Mississippi’s House Bill 1193, which has been enjoined by a federal judge, requires instructors from kindergarten through to university to teach that there are only two sexes: male and female.“But the bill alsoprohibitseducators from offering a class that ‘increaseawareness or understanding of issues related to race, sex, colour, gender identity, sexual orientation or national origin’.”The language of an Arizona law that banned encampments that passed after the Trump administration complained that the state’s universities were “coddling antisemites” was “so sloppy and extreme that it would seem to prohibit Jewish students from celebrating Sukkot”, a holiday during which dinners are eaten in temporary structures.Texas’ SB 2972 banned “anyexpressive activityanywhereon campus byanyonebetween the hours of 10pm and 8am”. The law went on to define “express activity” as “expression protected by the First Amendment” (which should have been the first clue something was wrong, since state laws cannot override the Federal Bill of Rights).“This means,” the report says, “no after-hours journalism by the student newspaper, no early-morning prayer circle in the campus chapel, no theatre rehearsal the night before the big show”, for all are expressive activities that come under the First Amendment. The bill was enjoined by a federal judge in a case led by the Fellowship of Christian University Students at the University of Texas Dallas. Judge Alan Ezra stated flatly: “The First Amendment does not have a bedtime of 10pm.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea.
