https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: India and Germany announce ambitious joint HE roadmap
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: India and Germany announce ambitious joint HE roadmapGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsIndian Prime Minister Narendra Modi. Image: Wikimedia CommonsJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksINDIAIndian students abroad: Numbers fall after three-year surgeGERMANYSurvey tracks 6% rise in international student enrolmentGLOBALSocial media influencers can widen access to study abroadINDIAEurope emerges as top choice for study abroad studentsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackINDIA-GERMANYIndia and Germany announce ambitious joint HE roadmapShuriah Niazi16 January 2026TweetIndia and Germany have placed higher education at the heart of their strategic partnership by adopting a new India-Germany Comprehensive Roadmap for Higher Education, announced by German Federal Chancellor Friedrich Merz during a visit to India from 12 to 13 January 2026.“The comprehensive roadmap on higher education that we have developed today will give a new direction to our partnership in the field of education. I invite German universities to open their campuses in India.“I express my gratitude to Chancellor Merz for announcing visa-free transit for Indian citizens,” the prime minister said, according toANI.The roadmap, released after talks between Indian Prime Minister Narendra Modi and Merz, marks a decisive shift from symbolic academic cooperation to an institutional, policy-driven engagement that could reshape how universities, students and researchers in both countries interact.At a time when India seeks to globalise its higher education system and Germany is responding to skills shortages and pressing research needs in areas such as advanced manufacturing, green technologies, artificial intelligence and applied industrial innovation, this roadmap positions university and talent mobility as strategic assets rather than elements of diplomacy.What sets the new roadmap apart is its stress on institutional depth, especially Modi’s invitation to leading German universities to establish campuses in India under the framework of the National Education Policy (NEP) 2020.While India and Germany have long collaborated through student exchanges, joint research projects, and dual degree programmes, the invitation for German universities to set up physical campuses signals a more ambitious phase of engagement.For India, this aligns with its broader policy of attracting high-quality foreign higher education institutions, with Delhi-National Capital Region (Delhi-NCR) and Chennai emerging as key hubs.Under that policy 12 foreign universities have been approved to establish campuses in India under the University Grants Commission (UGC) regulations, while another four have received approval to set up campuses at Gujarat International Finance Tec-City (GIFT City) under the International Financial Services Centres Authority (IFSCA) framework.According to official data, Mumbai will have the highest number of foreign higher education institution campuses, with six planned. Bengaluru will have two campuses, while Gurugram, Delhi-NCR, Greater Noida, and Chennai will each have one.The joint statement highlighted the significant increase in the number of Indian students choosing Germany for higher education, as well as the growing network of institutional partnerships and dual degree programmes.Vast student populationFor German institutions, campuses in India offer access to one of the world’s largest student populations, a rapidly growing research ecosystem, and closer alignment with Indian industry and innovation priorities.Instead of relying solely on outward mobility, German universities can establish themselves within India's academic system, co-create curricula, and participate directly in research and workforce development.“The Indo-German Higher Education Roadmap marks a shift from short-term exchanges to deep institutional partnerships, aligning with NEP 2020’s push for meaningful internationalisation of Indian higher education,” said Dr Anil Sahasrabudhe, chair of the National Assessment and Accreditation Council.Sahasrabudhe toldUniversity World News: “India’s regulatory and accreditation framework is already operational and prepared to handle foreign university campuses. We now have 16 globally top-ranked universities approved to establish campuses in India – one has already begun operations in the NCR region, and several others are expected to start from the coming academic year.“This demonstrates that the regulatory processes-application scrutiny, quality assessment, and approvals are firmly in place,” he said.Academics said the roadmap could significantly alter the cost, accessibility, and structure of international education for Indian students. Local campuses of German universities would allow students to obtain international qualifications without the financial and logistical hurdles of studying abroad.At the same time, these campuses could serve as gateways for further mobility, including semester exchanges, joint supervision of doctoral research, and pathways into the German job market, they said.Broader reformsProfessor Harsh V Pant, vice-president for studies and foreign policy at the Observer Research Foundation, an independent global think tank based in India, said: “India has a strong demand for good-quality higher education, while Germany requires talent and skills that many Indians can provide. The roadmap reflects this convergence of interests and attempts to institutionalise it.”Pant toldUniversity World News: “This initiative should be seen as part of a broader attempt to reform India's higher education system. On its own, it is not sufficient, but together with other measures, such as opening the system to foreign universities, it has the potential to create meaningful change.”Germany has already become a preferred destination for Indian students, thanks to its strong STEM programmes, comparatively affordable education, and post-study work opportunities.The joint statement noted the growing number of Indian students in Germany and the expanding network of joint and dual-degree programmes. This roadmap seeks to strengthen and expand these trends through structured institutional linkages rather than ad hoc arrangements.While visa-free transit for Indian passport holders through Germany – also announced during the visit – is not a student visa reform, it does ease travel difficulties and reinforces Germany's openness to Indian mobility.The roadmap for higher education is intertwined with skilling and workforce collaboration, reflecting the growing synergy between universities, industry, and migration policies.Indo-German renewable energy centreA key outcome of the visit was the signing of a joint declaration to establish an Indo-German Centre of Excellence for skilling in renewable energy. This centre will focus on curriculum development, industry collaboration, and trainer training, linking academic learning with labour market needs in both countries.The initiative reflects Germany’s emphasis on applied education and India’s focus on employability. It also connects higher education to strategic sectors like renewable energy, where both countries are investing heavily. Universities are expected to play a pivotal role not only as teaching institutions but also as hubs for research, innovation, and skills development.The roadmap also builds on existing collaborations between Indian institutes of technology and German technical universities, which have become important platforms for joint research and doctoral training. By formalising these relationships under a government-supported framework, the roadmap provides greater predictability and scale.More broadly, leaders affirmed that people-to-people connections – students, researchers, skilled professionals, artists, and young people – are a vital pillar of the India-Germany strategic partnership. Education is seen as a long-term investment in these relationships, with impacts extending far beyond individual cohorts of students.The increasing prominence of higher education on the bilateral agenda is noteworthy. The prominent inclusion of higher education in these outcomes reflects recognition that universities are fundamental to innovation, economic competitiveness, and geopolitical influence.“India could significantly benefit from Germany’s experience in vocational and skills education, particularly through universities of applied sciences that have successfully strengthened industry–university linkages,” said Professor Furqan Qamar, former secretary general of the Association of Indian Universities.He toldUniversity World News: “Many Indian universities – especially central universities, institutions of national importance, and institutions of eminence – are well prepared and keen to collaborate with German partners.”However, he said, so far, no German university is in the race in this regard. “It would be interesting to see if they want to take advantage of the facilitation promised in the roadmap. German universities have generally been selective in establishing offshore campuses. The medium of instruction could pose some barriers.”Modi described the roadmap as giving a “new direction” to educational ties, emphasising that academic collaboration is no longer a minor component of trade or diplomacy. Instead, it is being treated as a policy instrument that supports broader goals, from clean energy transition to skilled migration and technological leadership.On preventing inequality in higher education with the establishment of foreign institutions, Sahasrabudhe said: “Inequality in higher education stems mainly from limited access to quality institutions for economically weaker students and must be addressed through scholarships, fee waivers, and education loans.“International universities in India are being encouraged to offer fee concessions or waivers and reserve seats for meritorious but disadvantaged students, ensuring that talented learners are not excluded as India expands its international higher education engagement.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 印度和德國發布雄心勃勃的高等教育路線圖 印度總理納倫德拉·莫迪與德國聯邦總理弗里德里希·梅茲在2026年1月12日至13日的會議中發布了《印度-德國高等教育綜合路線圖》。此路線圖標誌著兩國在教育領域的合作邁向更深層次的政策驅動參與，並強調德國大學在印度設立校區的邀請。這一措施不僅有助於印度全球化其高等教育體系，還將滿足德國在先進製造、綠色技術及人工智慧等領域的人才需求。專家指出，這一路線圖可能會顯著改變印度學生的國際教育結構，提供更便利的學習機會和國際資格。同時，兩國還簽署了建立印度-德國可再生能源技能中心的協議，進一步加強了在教育和產業間的合作。

印度和德國發布雄心勃勃的高等教育路線圖 印度總理納倫德拉·莫迪與德國聯邦總理弗里德里希·梅茲在2026年1月12日至13日的會議中發布了《印度-德國高等教育綜合路線圖》。此路線圖標誌著兩國在教育領域的合作邁向更深層次的政策驅動參與，並強調德國大學在印度設立校區的邀請。這一措施不僅有助於印度全球化其高等教育體系，還將滿足德國在先進製造、綠色技術及人工智慧等領域的人才需求。專家指出，這一路線圖可能會顯著改變印度學生的國際教育結構，提供更便利的學習機會和國際資格。同時，兩國還簽署了建立印度-德國可再生能源技能中心的協議，進一步加強了在教育和產業間的合作。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: UAE cuts scholarships to UK, warms HE ties with IsraelGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative JobsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUAE-UNITED KINGDOM-ISRAELUAE cuts scholarships to UK, warms HE ties with IsraelWagdy Sawahel15 January 2026TweetThe United Arab Emirates (UAE), which provides its high-achieving students generous grants to study abroad, has removed United Kingdom (UK) universities from the list of institutions eligible for state scholarships, it has emerged in the past week.However, on 12 January the UAE held talks with Israel seeking closer academic ties, and continues to include universities in Israel on the list, despite the context of Israel’s war in Gaza.Although no dated formal statement was issued by the UAE, this was first reported by theFinancial Timeson 8 January, which indicated that in June 2025, the UAE Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research (MoHESR) published a list of global universities for which scholarships would be approved and qualifications certified.These universities are located in 42 countries in Asia, Europe, Latin America and the Caribbean, Oceania, Northern America and Africa (South Africa and Uganda), according to thelist of academic programmes and universitiesposted on the MoHESR website.The impact of excluding UK universities from governmental funding was evident inUK home office statistics, which showed that in the year ending September 2025 there were 213 students from the UAE granted visas to study at UK universities, a decline of 27% from a year earlier and a 55% drop from the year ending September 2022.The absence of UK universities from the UAE’s approved list also means credentials from them are unrecognised and of lesser value in the UAE employment market, but it is not a ban on Emirati students going to the UK for university education using self-funding.Israeli tiesThe removal of the UK from the list has raised eyebrows internationally, with observers noting that Israel remains on the list despite widespread opposition in the region to Israel’s war in Gaza, which included deliberate destruction of universities in the territory.The list includes several academic programmes at Israeli universities, namely, the Hebrew University of Jerusalem and Tel Aviv University.These academic programmes include agriculture, biological sciences, chemistry, mathematics, medicine, nursing, computer sciences, and physics and astronomy, as well as archaeology, classic and ancient history, law, communication and media studies, economics and econometrics, politics, psychology, and sociology.In a relevant development, on 12 January,UAE-Israel discussionswere held aimed at developing academic partnerships, especially in scientific research and innovation, and promoting the exchange of best practices and expertise in education to support a knowledge-based economy and advance sustainable development.This UAE move towards funding Emirati students to study at Israeli universities comes at a time when several universities worldwide have been severing ties with Israeli universities in response to the war in Gaza.On 28 November 2025,University World Newsreported on an Israeli publication, “The Fight Against the Academic Boycott”, which revealed that the number of academic boycott cases against Israeli universities and researchers has doubled since March 2025, and the increase, particularly from Europe, shows no signs of abating in response to the latest ceasefire in the war in Gaza declared in October 2025.UAE's reasons behind the moveUniversity World Newsreached out to theUAE’s Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Researchto ask for reasons behind the removal of United Kingdom universities from the official list but received no response.Neve Gordon, professor of international law and human rights at Queen Mary University of London, toldUniversity World Newsthat he understands the UAE said it reached the decision because the UK government has not banned the Sunni Islamist organisation, the Muslim Brotherhood, but he believes there are several other issues at play.“These issues include disagreements about a UAE-backed bid to buy the UK’sDaily Telegraphnewspaper in 2023; corruption allegations against the UAE-owned English Premier League soccer team Manchester City; and the UAE’s alleged backing for the Rapid Support Forces, which have committed atrocities in a civil war against Sudan’s Muslim Brotherhood-influenced military,” he noted.On 11 January, Emirati expert in strategic and political affairs Amjad Taha, who advocates conflict resolution and the normalisation of relations with Israel, wrote onsocial media platformX: "We do not want our students from the UAE to be held hostage by British Islamist jihadists.”Heallegedthat the Muslim Brotherhood is “operating inside British and Irish universities, recruiting and influencing your children”.“Your kids are being turned into hostages of Islamist jihadism. You pay tuition fees, only to see your child radicalised,” he warned.Heaccused the UKof being a society which “tolerates extremism in its universities”, citing as an example the invitation by the Muslim Brotherhood Society at Birmingham University of a Muslim Brotherhood leader to campus, where he spoke in support of Hamas.“When the state refuses to draw moral boundaries, chaos fills the vacuum. History teaches us this lesson again and again,” he wrote.“The UAE chooses a different path. We protect minds before they are poisoned. We safeguard students before they are radicalised. Caring for people sometimes means saying no, even when others look away.”Unlike the UAE, the UK has not officially designated the Muslim Brotherhood as a terrorist group becausea comprehensive UK government reviewinto the Muslim Brotherhood in December 2015 found that the group had not been linked to terrorist-related activity in and against Britain.By contrast, on 13 January, the administration of US President Donald TrumpdesignatedMuslim Brotherhood branches in Lebanon foreign terrorist organisations – the most severe of the labels, which makes it a criminal offence to provide material support to the group.The Jordanian and Egyptian branches were listed as specially designated global terrorists for providing material support to Hamas.Diplomatic engagementSpeaking toUniversity World News,Dr Moran Zaga, a researcher of the Gulf States at the University of Haifa in Israel, said: “The UAE’s decision reflects a widely shared international position that recognises Israeli universities as legitimate academic institutions that contribute to science, innovation, and global knowledge.“From the Emirati perspective, conflict resolution is pursued through dialogue rather than academic boycotts. This is a principle embedded in the spirit of the Abraham Accords and its preference for diplomatic engagement,” said Zaga.The Abraham Accords are a set of agreements that established diplomatic normalisation between Israel and several Arab states, including a 2020peace agreementwith the UAE.The first Emirati student to study at an Israeli university wasMansoor Bin Shamekh Al Marzooqi, who enrolled at the Interdisciplinary Center Herzliya private college (now called Reichman University) in 2021, following the Abraham Accords.“Israeli universities constitute a vital critical agent vis-à-vis the government. Boycotting them effectively silences internal critique and undermines the capacity to generate social and political change within Israel,” said Zaga, who is the author of a 2021papertitled “The Culture of Sciences: Bridging the Gaps Between the Emirati and Israeli Research Systems”.“While the Hebrew University of Jerusalem operates dedicated programmes for Palestinian students from East Jerusalem, including scholarships and tailored support frameworks, the social sciences and humanities faculties at Tel Aviv University are widely regarded as liberal, committed to social pluralism, supportive of ending the conflict, and openly critical of government policies,” Zaga said.Higher education must be ‘dislodged from politics’Arshin Adib-Moghaddam, a professor in global thought and comparative philosophies at the School of Oriental and African Studies (SOAS), University of London, toldUniversity World Newshigher education policies “should not be geared to political agendas, as universities can only be incubators for innovation if they are dislodged from politics”.But Gordon toldUniversity World News: “The UAE move demonstrates once more that higher education is not some island separated from politics, and that countries have always used it to advance both a domestic and international political agenda.”Adib-Moghaddam said: “Including Israeli universities in the list of institutions eligible for the UAE’s scholarships is clearly a political move and quite an affront to the Palestinians whose educational system has been systematically targeted and partially destroyed by the state of Israel.”Gordon added: “I think there is something grotesque about denying UAE’s scholarships to the UK universities and paying for students to study in a country that, according to the leading human rights organisations, is an apartheid regime that discriminates against Muslims and has been carrying outgenocideagainst Palestinians.“It is a sign of moral bankruptcy,” Gordon said.According to a 6 January report bythe United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, the estimated death toll in Gaza, following Israel’s military operation on October 2023 stood at 71,391, and 171,279 people had been injured. More than 63 university buildings had been destroyed in Gaza, the report says.AReport of the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on the Occupied Palestinian Territory, including East Jerusalem, and Israel*presented to the UN in May 2025 found that in the killing of civilians sheltering in schools, Israeli security forces had committed the “crime against humanity of extermination”.UK removal is ‘self-defeating’Adib-Moghaddam said the United Kingdom has some of the best universities in the world, both in terms of the quality of research and distinctive teaching.“Restricting students from the UAE from those opportunities is largely self-defeating, as it constrains their ability to study at unique universities such as Cambridge, SOAS or Oxford,” Adib-Moghaddam said.But Zaga claimed academic institutions in the United Kingdom pose a “significant challenge for Emirati students” because of “toxic” student activism and because they allowed scholars based in political science and Middle East departments to openly engage in teaching critical narratives about the UAE.“It is difficult to accept that such figures are entrusted with shaping the knowledge and worldview of Emirati students,” Zaga said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. This one understands itKhalifa University staffSponsored ArticleThe demand for well-trained software engineers is soaringIEEE Computer SocietySponsored ArticleHow AI-powered search is reshaping discovery in researchLaura SyrettSponsored ArticleCUNY launches initiative honouring Achyuta SamantaKIIT staffSponsored ArticleMBZUAI – Where research and innovation meet opportunityMBZUAI staffSponsored ArticleProfessional women who break barriers and empower othersSponsored ArticlePatent of catalyst for natural gas reforming technologiesUAE University StaffGlobal|Africa|Asia|SDGs|Edtech/AI|Commentary|World Blog|Special Reports|Jobs in HE|Subscribe|Facebook|BookmarksAbout Us|Advertise|Sponsorship|Careers at UWN|Terms & Conditions|Privacy Policy|Cookie Policy|Contact|Mobile SiteCopyright 2026 University World News
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.summary: 阿聯酋削減對英國的獎學金，強化與以色列的高等教育關係。最近，阿聯酋政府已將英國大學從其國家獎學金名單中移除，並在與以色列的會談中尋求更緊密的學術合作，儘管以色列在加沙的戰爭情況仍然持續。這一改變可能導致前往英國留學的阿聯酋學生簽證數量大幅下降，並影響英國大學的學位在阿聯酋的認可度。專家認為，這一政策反映了阿聯酋對以色列高等教育機構的合法性認可，並可能在地區教育合作中引發更廣泛的討論。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Europe’s deep-tech potential: What role for universities?
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Europe’s deep-tech potential: What role for universities?GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksDENMARKAcademics hail launch of new US$2.8bn basic research fundDENMARKReport proposes a fivefold increase in defence budget for R&DNORWAY-NORDIC COUNTRIESRegion grapples with how to retain foreign PhD graduatesSWEDENIndustry calls for action over loss of doctoral graduatesSWEDENRules eased for third-country PhD students and researchersDENMARKMinisters announce ambitious funding for green innovationFINLANDPM’s roundtable flags plan to retain international studentsDENMARKResearch explores how AI might change knowledge productionEUROPE-DENMARKUniversities drive participation in Horizon Europe researchDENMARKCan the Danish research ‘miracle’ be resuscitated?DENMARKSectorally mobile researchers are ‘change agents’DENMARKNew research strategy has an eye on the Nobel PrizeInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsMachine Learning - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsDENMARK-EUROPEEurope’s deep-tech potential: What role for universities?Jan Petter Myklebust15 January 2026TweetEurope needs to ensure it becomes not only the birthplace of deep-tech ideas but also the place where they “grow, mature, stay and thrive”, according to the head of a leading Danish university.These sentiments, which capture an increased urgency in Europe to safeguard the region’s competitiveness, resilience and technological autonomy, were expressed by the Technical University of Denmark (DTU) President Anders O Bjarklev at the opening of the Ascend Innovation conference held late last year in Copenhagen.Hosted by DTU in collaboration with the Ministry of Higher Education and Science, and supported by the European Innovation Council and the Novo Nordisk Foundation, the gathering focused on the question of how to strengthen Europe’s competitive position in the global technology race.TheAscend Innovation conference, held in Copenhagen, was attended by over 700 innovation leaders from universities, research organisations, industry organisations and unions, together with investors and other innovation stakeholders.“We must act. And act now. To safeguard our competitiveness. Our resilience. Our technological autonomy. And for this, innovation is the key. So that we are not only the birthplace of deep-tech ideas, but also the place where they grow, mature, stay and thrive,” said Bjarklev who positioned universities as key players in the movement towards European technological sovereignty.“I believe we need to create a foundation to empower people at DTU, both staff and students, so that they have the chance to act on their ideas – if they want to,” Bjarklev said.“And I know they have ideas. Ideas to start new businesses. Businesses that can create jobs in Denmark and contribute to the prosperity we all depend on,” he noted.Minister of Higher Education and Research Christina Egelund, who participated in the panel debate with former prime minister Anders Fogh Rasmussen, now chair of Rasmussen Global, and Mads Krogsgaard Thomsen, CEO of the Novo Nordic Foundation, noted in aLinkedInpost after the conference that major societal problems required long-term solutions.She said this is why the Danish government recently made an agreement on a long-term strategic direction for research and innovation funding with a focus on strengthening free research and innovation and a strategic focus on critical technologies, defence technologies, climate and health.European basic research must be safeguarded but at the same time open up for more risk-taking research and innovation with the potential for major scientific breakthroughs, she added.She also noted that universities and other research organisations should be better able to support entrepreneurship, for example, by being able to provide facilities and services to start-ups.According to Margrethe Vestager, chair of the board of governors at DTU and a former European Commissioner for Competition, Europe’s greatest challenge is allowing others to set the rules for the next technological era. Her comments were made in the context of heavy investments in research and critical technologies by the US and China.“Europe must move deliberately, reflect on what we want and what we are building. We should not catch up. We should build differently and better,” said Vestager in her keynote.Egelund added that Europe could create something different. “It’s not just about technology but also about ethics, morality and democratic values. That is Europe’s competitive advantage,” she said.Referencing former Danish PM Anders Fogh Rasmussen, Professor Peter Heather of King’s College London, historian and author ofWhy Empires Fall, a book about the fall of the Roman Empire, said Europe needed to “be able to stand on its own two feet”.To succeed in that, he said Europe needed to “renew our institutions and invest in innovation – not attempt to recreate the past”.He drew parallels to today’s Europe: “Empires do not collapse through sudden breakdowns, but through institutional rigidity, loss of innovation, and failure to adapt.”Pre-conference report: Scaling upA pre-conference report,From Strategy to Scale: Unlocking Europe's Deep Tech Potential, based on interviews and workshops with actors across the entire innovation ecosystem, highlighted the barriers faced by Europe in creating scalable companies.“It is clear that Europe has the potential through its excellent research environments but lacks cohesion. Without a common action plan, we risk groundbreaking technologies never leaving the laboratory. Therefore, there is a task at hand to remove the barriers that are currently slowing down innovation,” Bjarklev said.According to the report, the main barriers to scaling up are a lack of capital, limited access to technical infrastructure, and regulatory obstacles across the 27 national borders of the European Union.Among other things, the report recommends the creation of a common access charter to advanced infrastructure throughout Europe. DTU Nanolab is one of the places that offers an “open access” approach for companies to manufacture micro and quantum chips.Other recommendations from the report include: positioning the Scaleup Europe Fund as a leading investor in deep tech companies; and the establishment of “regulatory sandboxes” to streamline Europe's different national regulatory frameworks and promote the testing of new solutions and faster implementation of technologies.DTU as top EU innovation universityAnother DTU initiative, the SkyFactory scaling hub, which aims to bridge the gap in the value chain that hinders growth, is making inroads here.“DTU SkyFactory is a breakthrough in the university world, putting the deep tech startups with the greatest potential into a framework that gives them full backing and support to grow,” Bjarklev said.The ambition is for every tenth DTU startup to become a growth company with international impact. According to Bjarklev, this is part of DTU’s contribution to the common European solutions highlighted in the pre-conference report.Already in 2024, DTU-based startups accounted for21% of the total venture capital invested in Denmark, while120 startups emerged from DTU.Another part of the DTU’s contribution to common European solutions involves sharing DTU’s experiences.“DTU’s work with innovation has enabled us to build up valuable knowledge that can make a difference. Our role is therefore to strengthen European cooperation by presenting a proposal for the Ascend conference, such as this one, and at the same time sharing our experiences with others,” Bjarklev said.For the third consecutive year, DTU hasranked first on EngiRank, a survey of the top technical universities in Europe. This year, EngiRank has expanded its list of universities to 239, including 15 from the United Kingdom.“This is a great recognition, not only of DTU as a university but also of our researchers, teachers, students, and staff. The first year, it was a completely new ranking, but now that we have been named Europe’s best technical university for the third consecutive year, it truly solidifies our position. It shows that our targeted efforts to combine world-class research with innovation and international collaboration have made a clear impact,” Bjarklev said.On 16 December 2025,ScienceBusinessreportedthat DTU was number one among Europan universities receiving funding for innovation from Horizon Europe.It reported that the most successful individual institutions, by number of total participations, were three technical universities from elsewhere in Europe: Technical University of Denmark, National Technical University of Athens and Delft University of Technology, with each participating in projects more than 40 times, drawing in €120 million (US$139.34 million) in funding.“But the top three represent only a small chunk of the total funding for universities in Horizon Europe innovation projects, which totals €1.46 billion across 968 projects involving 830 universities”, asScience|Businessreported.Talent retentionThe Ascend pre-conference report also recommended that universities focus on attracting and retaining talents, themes that were interrogated at the conference.One session, “How do we secure the talent needed to build global tech champions out of Europe?”, co-organised with the Danish Society of Engineers (IDA), heard presentations by Michiel Scheffer, president of the European Innovation Council; Richardo Marvao, co-founder of collaborative innovation platform Beta-I; and Georg Schäfer, research associate at the University of Bath, UK.Laura Klitgaard, president of IDA (Danish Society of Engineers), who moderated the session, listed the key challenges for European universities as follows: Too many people leave Europe due to more attractive startup environments elsewhere, especially in the US; too few students become entrepreneurs after graduating; Europe faces a decline in deep tech talent due to fewer STEM doctorate candidates (7.5% decline from 2015 to to 2023 in the EU, with 57.010 STEM doctorates graduating in 2023); EU companies striving to hire local talent; fragmented legal frameworks hinde cross-border mobility; and the talent gap is impacted by persistent gender imbalances.University World Newsasked Gregor Schäfer, who researched academic mobility and entrepreneurship in Denmark while a postdoctoral researcher at the University of Copenhagen and contributed to projects on migrant integration and academic mobility in Europe, what he sees as the most critical barrier preventing Europe from securing the talent needed to build global tech champions.Schäfer said university graduates need sufficient time to find a job or start a business after leaving university.“Many countries offer third-country graduates only a few months’ visa for job hunting. This is often not enough, as it usually takes longer for the first job or business idea to materialise.“As a result, these people are forced to leave the EU before they can enter the labour market, even though they would like to stay. Current migration discourses tend to tighten these rules even further, which does not help to secure talent,” he said.“When working professionals are attracted from abroad ... it is important to consider their families as well. Provide them with reasons to stay and grow, because talent goes where it can build a life, not just a career. The same is true of global talent and long-term retention. A recentNaturePhD surveyfound that one-third of PhD students (future global talent) are studying outside their home country.”When asked about the role of universities in Europe’s innovation landscape and how they should balance their traditional academic mission with developing entrepreneurial and innovative talent, Schäfer said: “Innovative thought is an integral part of academic work. No academic discipline is purely conservative; they are all constantly evolving and challenging existing ideas. Going beyond existing ideas is a core academic task.“To think innovatively, you need a solid foundation in every discipline. This is why we should not pit the ‘traditional academic mission’ against ‘developing entrepreneurial and innovative talent’, but rather view them as complementary.”Schäfer said in this context, embedding entrepreneurial and innovation skills more deeply in the university curriculum was a good idea, as was giving students time and space to develop and explore (which may include failure) – “because intrinsic curiosity is the best facilitator of innovation (identifying a gap or problem and thinking creatively about a possible solution)”.He noted: “Moreover, it is essential that we eliminate or minimise the frictions and barriers that exist between EU states with regard to educational experiences, migration, legislation and cultural attitudes towards entrepreneurship.”According to Schäfer, a potential problem was the rise of xenophobia and nationalism, which could deter international talent.“Although our research shows that such political rhetoric usually does not make them feel directly targeted, several participants from the UK who were interviewed and now live in Denmark coined the term ‘Brexit refugees’ to describe themselves. This demonstrates that culture, as well as policies, can change.“If Europe can present itself as a place where people want to live in the long term due to factors such as safety, culture, social life, the environment and atmosphere, it can attract global tech talent. While Dubai might be a better place to earn and save money, Europe needs to promote its high quality of life more effectively.”Reflecting on the conference, Klitgaard toldUniversity World NewsEuropean universities did not have to make a choice between academic endeavours and entrepreneurship.“On the contrary, they have to integrate the two. From the discussions at Ascend, it was clear that the excellence of research in Europe is the key to our strength. However, we are losing too much of this by not turning enough ideas, talent, and discovery into innovation and new enterprises.“A balance between these needs can only be achieved if universities are given the ability and the incentive to foster multi-track careers, where academics are able to move between universities, startups, and industry without any negative consequences for their career progression. The recognition of entrepreneurship as a component of academic merit is needed,” he noted.Klitgaard said students should also be introduced to entrepreneurial knowledge and ecosystems alongside their academic education, and innovation should become a “seamless” continuation of research and not a departure from it.“Europe’s desire to develop deep-tech leaders means our universities need to continue to be centres of excellence and also gateways to innovation, scaling, and impact,” Klitgaard said.New policy mixFrede Blaabjerg, distinguished professor of energy technology at Aalborg University and chair of the Danish Council for Research and Innovation Policy, toldUniversity World Newsthe Copenhagen conference illustrated the “urgency for Europe to develop a new policy mix of innovation and industrial policies” that would require thinking that goes across sectors and ministries.“We need to improve our ability to develop and maintain growth companies, and that will require a different policy framework.“We need to move unified in the direction of more innovation in order to keep our competitiveness in the global technology race.“It is not only about money but also talent, public procurement, market simplification and general development of the innovation ecosystem,” Blaabjerg said.Commenting on innovation at Aarhus University, Lone Ryg Olsen, director of enterprise and innovation at Aarhus, said her institution “manages to combine a high scientific level with excellent research environments and a strong administrative setup. As a result, we have achieved a position as a university that you would like to have at the head of your consortia”.She said individual academic environments had prioritised network building in Europe.“The researchers are encouraged to apply and supported by department fundraisers and external advisors. It means that our senior researchers have buit up experience in developing and conducting such projects, which increases success rates of new projects. While younger researchers are included in ongoing European projects to develop their knowledge and network,” she noted.Insights from GermanyAsked to comment on the experience of Technical University Munich (TUM) in Germany, which is ranked 13 on the ScienceBusiness Horizon ranking, Professor Dr Helmut Schönenberger, CEO of UnternehmerTUM and vice-president for entrepreneurship said: “On top of research and teaching, universities should position entrepreneurship as a core mission. At TUM, startups are not an add-on but a strategic priority.”Schönenberger said around 5% of the university budget was allocated to entrepreneurship and innovation – compared to under 1% at most other universities. “Innovation does not happen by accident; it requires long-term investment in people, infrastructure and culture,” he noted.He said TUM was inspired by global role models like Stanford University, which is now a strategic partner for entrepreneurship and sustainability.“We built UnternehmerTUM as a startup factory connecting academic excellence with real-world impact. This approach has allowed us to scale. We double our output every three years, effectively increasing it tenfold over a decade.“This demonstrates how universities can become powerful drivers of innovation when entrepreneurship is embedded at the heart of their academic mission,” Schönenberger said.A ‘bold and necessary step’Asked to comment on the significance of the Ascend conference, Rune Boelsmand Bak, vice-chair for academic matters at the National Union of Students in Denmark, toldUniversity World Newsthe gathering was a “bold and necessary” step for the EU, and as a country, Denmark “should be proud of hosting such an event”.However, the participant list had a glaring omission, he said.“There seems to be this misunderstanding in the Danish government that the brand ‘innovation stakeholders’ doesn’t include students. This makes no sense, as students are the absolute basis for the innovation ecosystem that the EU needs and must work toward.“Innovation development at higher education institutions, then, must encompass not only spin-outs and barrier breakdowns, but also education practice and knowledge flow between innovation and classroom. The first barrier for innovation in the EU is the translation between education and innovative practices, and it must be broken down,” Boelsmand Bak said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 歐洲深科技潛力：大學的角色

在最近於哥本哈根舉行的Ascend創新會議上，丹麥技術大學（DTU）校長安德斯·O·比亞克萊夫強調，歐洲不僅應是深科技想法的搖籃，更須成為這些想法成長和繁榮的地方。他指出，為了在全球技術競賽中保持競爭力，歐洲必須加強創新，並支持大學在創業和技術發展中的關鍵角色。

會議中，與會者討論了如何克服歐洲在創建可擴展企業上面臨的障礙，包括資金不足和技術基礎設施的限制。報告建議創建共同資源以促進創新，並強調大學在吸引和保留人才方面的重要性。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Academics welcome Japan’s membership of EU research scheme
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Academics welcome Japan’s membership of EU research schemeGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsThe European Commission. Image: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksJAPANIncrease in defence-related research is dividing academiaJAPANOpposition builds against national science council reformsJAPAN-EUROPEJapan-EU Horizon talks kick off amid dual-use concernsJAPANUniversities brace for more cuts as defence spending risesJAPANCrisis spurs new calls to align academic year to the WestJAPANRapid expansion of defence research divides academicsInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredEUROPEAcademics welcome Japan’s membership of EU research schemeSuvendrini Kakuchi15 January 2026TweetAcademics have welcomed a European Commission decision in December to allow Japanese universities and companies to participate in Horizon Europe, the European Union’s flagship research and innovation funding programme.They view participation in the€93.5 billion (US$108.9 billion) programmeas vital for fostering global talent exchange, creating an environment for global cooperation and strengthening technological innovation against a backdrop of ongoing geopolitical tensions.“The Horizon programme provides critical funds for Japanese researchers to develop collaborative projects with EU counterparts. The initiative also promotes and expands Japan’s cutting-edge research development on an international platform,” said Toshiharu Saiki, vice-president of research and entrepreneurial education and support at the Yagami campus of Keio University.Challenges to global orderAcademics contend that joining the Horizon programme has taken on a greater significance in the context of new challenges facing the international order.A key concern is the US Trump administration’s push to control diversity programmes in US universities and, more recently, the US military operation in Venezuela, which many observers argue breaks with international law.The United States’ withdrawal from the United Nations Framework Convention on Climate Change has also been condemned by scientists across the world.Nagoya University Vice-President Koji Yamanaka also points to the difficult climate for researchers in the US who are faced with budget cuts and a squeeze in hiring prospects that threatens the research sector’s progress and output.“The Horizon programme that extends financial support through collaboration provides a vital platform for academics to combat these growing concerns facing the international research community,” he said in an interview withUniversity World News.Nagoya University, a national higher education institute, is a leader in material science and applied energy technology and has research programmes with some of its Nobel Prize-winning alumni.According to supplied university data, the institution has already accepted over 100 researchers from a range of European countries, as well as the UK, Norway and Switzerland.Yamanaka said applications for Horizon funding will focus on the expansion of these sectors with EU counterparts.“As the outlook for academic collaborations with the United States becomes less attractive for Japanese universities, the role of the Horizon programme that fosters new partnerships in the Asian region becomes vital,” he toldUniversity World News.Japanese companies and universities, if selected, will be able to access EU funding for projects that focus on collaborations with EU counterparts affiliated with Horizon. South Korea and Canada have already decided to join.The EU is investing €93.5 billion in the Horizon programme between 2021 and 2027. For its next iteration, 2028 to 2034, the proposed budget is €175 billion.Support from university consortiumJapan’s Research University 11, a consortium of 11 higher education institutes established in 2009, has urged the Japanese government to formally participate in Horizon Europe. Japanese research organisations already have individual collaborations with Horizon-affiliated institutes but have relied on finding their own funding if located in Japan.Research topics under Horizon cover sectors such as digital and artificial intelligence, climate change, the space industry and national security.Horizon Europe also includes large-scale initiatives called “EU missions” that focus on adaptation to climate change, cancer prevention and cure, climate-neutral and smart cities, transition to healthy soil, and protecting and restoring oceans and waters.The contribution from the Japanese government is expected to be more than JPY1 billion (US$6.3 million), aimed mostly at fostering the new institutional networks with the EU by establishing systems such as legal mechanisms to foster Japanese participation.While applications for funding of concrete projects are still in the making, Saiki pointed to Keio University’s advanced research on robotics and medical fields such as longevity and ageing as some of the core areas to be pursued.Dual-use frameworkDomestically, Japan is embarking on an unprecedented expansion of the defence budget to beef up national security. Against academic resistance towards the policy, the Ministry of Defence introduced a new dual-purpose framework, and, in fiscal 2025, established a “grant-based programme” in addition to the existing “commissioned research” scheme that provides funding for research proposals initiated by researchers themselves.As a result, the number of applications rose to 340 (from 203 the previous year). Applications from universities and similar institutions, in particular, nearly tripled to 123 (from 44), according to data from the Ministry of Education.“Universities are navigating the traditional resistance towards accepting defence-related funds. This research is also critical to saving lives,” Saiki toldUniversity World News.Japan follows a pacifist mission after the country’s disastrous military defeat to the Allied Forces ending World War ll in 1945.In 2015, Japan officially embarked on a science technology diplomacy policy aimed at enhancing Japanese diplomacy through the sharing of research in areas such as agricultural innovation.Boost for internationalisationTsukuba University Vice-President Yasunori Endo, who is also executive director for research, said in an interview that Japan’s entry into Horizon Europe is a giant step to boost the institution’s international outreach.“Horizon is an opportunity for our young researchers to gain experience in collaborative work with EU counterparts. Through joint publications they will raise their global profile,” he said, adding that sharing of top technology between the two sides will enhance academic contribution to human progress.Despite top-quality infrastructure and high funding, Japan’s higher institutions have fallen out of the top 10 ranking of nations in respect of highly cited scientific papers.Japan ranked 12th with a figure of 3,780 in the average number of papers published between 2018 and 2020, according to the National Institute of Science and Technology Policy 2022 report, produced in affiliation with the Ministry of Education. The figure is less than 10% of that for China.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 日本加入歐盟研究計劃獲學界歡迎

學術界對歐洲委員會在12月決定允許日本大學和企業參與「地平線歐洲」計劃表示歡迎。該計劃預算達935億歐元，旨在促進全球人才交流、加強合作及推動技術創新。專家指出，這對於日本研究者來說，提供了關鍵的資金支持，讓他們能與歐盟同行開展合作項目。

隨著美國對國際研究環境的挑戰增加，加入地平線計劃對日本大學的合作前景至關重要，尤其是在數位和人工智慧、氣候變化等領域。日本政府預計將投入超過10億日元，以促進與歐盟的機制建立，從而進一步推動國際化。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Ban on Plato readings leaves academics asking who’s next
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Ban on Plato readings leaves academics asking who’s nextGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsA classical philosopher quietly removed from view, as academics question which ideas and texts could be targeted next. Image generated with SoraJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksUNITED STATESTrump poses ‘existential threat’ to HE and democracyUNITED STATES-GLOBALTrump is ‘catalyst, not cause’ of HE decline – PasquerellaUNITED STATESJustice Department ramps up pressure against ‘unlawful’ DEIUNITED STATESHE leaders unite over Trump’s ‘overreach and interference’GLOBALScientists self-censor as Trump axes research fundsUNITED STATES‘Stop DEI’ order lacks force of law but set to sow chaosInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackUNITED STATESBan on Plato readings leaves academics asking who’s nextNathan M Greenfield14 January 2026TweetA Texas university has been lambasted by organisations such as the American Association of University Professors (AAUP) and the Foundation for Individual Rights and Expression (FIRE) after it emerged that university authorities asked a philosophy professor to remove course readings on the ancient Greek philosopher Plato.On 6 January, Texas A&M University (TAMU) philosophy professor Martin Peterson received an e-mail from his chair, Dr Kristi Sweet, asking him either to “remove the modules on race ideology and gender ideology” from his Contemporary Modern Problems course set to begin last Tuesday at 8 am or accept a teaching reassignment.That the two one-day modules would draw the attention of the administration of the university was expected: in December, Sweet sent an email underscoring that the state board of regents had voted to implement an order that no core course “will advocate race or gender ideology, or topics related to sexual orientation or gender identity”.Less predictable was a ban (or “mitigation” in TAMU jargon) by Sweet, a Kantian philosopher, on selections of Plato’sSymposium, written 2,500 years ago.The offending part of theSymposium, a dialogue devoted to interrogating the nature of love (eros), is a speech by the comic playwright Aristophanes which, through the telling of the “Myth of Androgyny”, destabilises the idea that “male” and “female” are stable categories.On the websiteMS Nowon 10 January,Peterson wrote: “In the ‘Symposium’ Plato describes homosexuality as fully natural and suggests there are more than two genders: ‘you should learn the nature of humanity . . . in times past our nature was not the same as it is now, but otherwise. For, in the first place, there were three kinds of human beings and not two, as nowadays, male and female. No, there was also a third kind, a combination of both genders’.”Criticism in response to the university’s actions was swift. Within a daythe AAUP pointedto “serious legal concerns, including viewpoint discrimination and violations of constitutionally protected academic freedom” embodied in the move and argued that “silencing 2,500-year-old ideas of one of the world’s most influential thinkers betrays the mission of higher education”.FIRE was equally clear: “Texas A&M now believes Plato doesn’t belong in an introductory philosophy course. The philosophy department is demanding that Professor Martin Peterson remove Platonic readings because they ‘may’ touch on race or gender ideology.”He noted: “This is what happens when the board of regents gives university bureaucrats veto power over academic content. The board didn’t just invite censorship; they unleashed it with immediate and predictable consequences.”On 9 January, in an effort to counter the groundswell of criticism against the banning of Plato, which had grown to include a feature article inThe New York Times(7 January) and other national papers, Tim Eaton, TAMU’s assistant vice president for institutional reputation management, issued a statement which said the university would teach “numerous dialogues by Plato in a variety of courses this semester and will continue to do so in the future.In alignment with recent system policy, university administrators are reviewing all core curriculum courses to ensure they do not teach race or gender ideology.”The statement went on to say: “The Department Head advised the professor that he may teach the course if the modules that do not align with the new policy are removed. If the professor chooses not to comply, the section will be reassigned to another professor to ensure our students can move forward with the course they registered for without interruption.“Other sections of the same course have been approved, meet learning outcomes and include works by Plato but do not include modules on race and gender ideology.”Eaton’s words did little to stem the flood of criticisms of TAMU.PEN America’s Jonathan Friedman, Sy Syms managing director at the US free expression programme, toldUniversity World Newsthat the banning of Plato demonstrates how censorship is often about “flattening” rather than finding nuance, “reducing things to their most basic elements and saying, well it doesn’t matter that we might have an informed discussion about Plato” because the regulations say this or that.In addition to being “highly illegal”, Friedman said, the move was “deeply damaging to the notion of a liberal education that we hope American college students are being exposed to.“We’re talking about government directives rooted in a political and ideological agenda shaping how a university will teach students about our inherited civilisation. I think that this is certainly and appropriately recognised as a fundamental violation of the university, and it will do our young students a disservice,” he said.Lynn Pasquarella, president of the American Association of Colleges and Universities (AACU), toldUniversity World Newsvia email: “Plato sits at the core of the Western canon – making it striking that critics who once demanded a return to canonical texts are now calling for the exclusion of one of its most influential thinkers.“This case is a good illustration of how vague and overbroad policies can have a chilling effect or lead to overcompliance in which administrators err on the side of elimination rather than clarification.”‘There is no state law’Eaton’s claim that TAMU’s directive accords with the “system policy,” that is, last year’s decision of the board of regents, is true as far as it goes and explains whymore than 200 other coursesin the university’s College of Arts and Sciences have undergone “mitigation”.They include a course on religion and the arts, which was taken off the list of core courses, and the removal in its entirety of a course called “Introduction to Race and Ethnicity”.However, as Professor Leonard Bright, who teaches in the university’s Bush School of Government and Public Service and is president of TAMU’s AAUP chapter, explained toUniversity World News, the regents stepped in only after the Republican-controlled legislature rebuffed efforts to pass into law bans on critical race theory and gender theories being taught in university classes.“A lot of politicians are spinning a lie, essentially that they put this into state law (SB 17). There’s no state law whatsoever that bans any of these topics.“They attempted to do that. I was one of the people out there testifying against this. It didn’t pass. They never banned critical race theory or the others from our universities.“As a matter of fact, the law gives an exemption to universities to teach, to do research around diversity topics and DEI topics. All they banned were DEI offices within the university. But that did not impact our teaching,” said Bright.Having failed in the legislature, the regents, under the leadership of Charles B Creighton, put forth new regulations (ostensibly under the authority established by SB 17) banning the teaching of racial and gender ideology.“They’re backdooring it using the same language that failed in the state legislature,” said Bright.Core Trumpian beliefsThe banning of any of Plato’s works by MAGA (Make America Great Again) supporters of the increasingly authoritarian Trump presidency is in itself a surprise.Plato can hardly be accused of being ‘woke”’and he famously called inThe Republicfor poets to be banished from his mythical city of Kallipolis because they told false stories about the gods, depicted them and heroes (like Odysseus or Achilles) behaving badly, and wrote stories that destabilised people by stirring their emotions.“He’s not a left-wing extremist,” said Peterson. “He doesn’t defend democracy.”“Plato is your go-to philosopher if you are an authoritarian leader and you want to limit freedom. But nevertheless, he says things about gender issues that many conservative politicians do not agree with.”It should be noted, however, that from the beginning of his second term, Trump has used the power of the US government to shore up the traditional view that there are only two sexes: male and female.Within hours of being inaugurated on 20 January 2025, Trump signed anexecutive order, wildly cheered by his supporters, titled, “Defending Women from Gender Ideology Extremism and Restoring Biological Truth to the Federal Government”, which “recognisetwo sexes, male and female… [that] are not changeable and are grounded in fundamental and incontrovertible reality”.It also argues that “gender ideology replaces the biological category of sex with an ever-shifting concept of self-assessed gender identity, permitting the false claim that males can identify as and thus become women and vice versa, and requiring all institutions of society to regard this false claim as true.”While the executive order does not bind the Texas Board of Regents to the assertion that the United States only recognises two genders, the order reflects a core belief against transgenderism by Trump, his administration and supporters like Texas’ Republican governor Gregg Abbott, who has appointed the regents who are answerable to him.After Plato, who is next?As are Peterson and Bright, the other professors interviewed for this article are fearful of what will be banned next.James Romm, the James H Ottaway Jr Professor of Classics and director of the classical studies programme at Bard College (Annandale-on-Hudson, New York), toldUniversity World Newswhen he heard of the banning of Plato, he reacted with “horror and dismay that this obsession with gender issues could be a stumbling block to reading works that are designed to open minds, not close them”.When asked what might be banned next, Romm said he feared “putting a target onto any particular work” before pointing to Euripides’ playThe Bacchae.“In it, Dionysus is portrayed as androgynous, as having the features of a woman as well as a man. He has long hair, he has smooth cheeks, he has a sort of feminine gait. So he could be perceived as intergender or as an example of how gender binaries don’t always work.”In Aristophanes’Thesmophoriazusae, there’s cross-dressing as women dress like men “in order to constitute an assembly and behave as though they were male, assuming power in a male register. That too undermines the notion that men are men and women are women, and they should stick to their own roles,” said Romm.“Then you have the figure of Athena in many literary works. She was born from the head of her father, and doesn’t have a mother. She dresses in armour and goes to war like men. You also have the Artemis, who rides horses and hunts the way men do.“There are also the Amazons, who assume a male role in war and hunting, and disdain the domestic role of ancient women. All of these figures in myth and literature call gender binaries into question,” said Romm, who, in addition toPlato and the Tyrant(2025, WW Norton), is the author ofThe Sacred Band: Three Hundred Theban Lovers Fighting to Save Greek Freedom(2021, Scribner), which tells how 150 homosexual couples fought the Spartans beginning in 379 BC before being annihilated at the Battle of Chaeronea in 338 BC by Phillip II of Macedon and his son, the future Alexander the Great.The history covered in theSacred Bandshows his LGBTQ+ and cisgendered students that stereotypical depictions of homosexuals are ahistorical – and by doing so, challenges an important portrayal that dominant heterosexual males use to devalue homosexuals and, thus, shore up their own status.“Being gay doesn’t mean that one has to be feminised or sidelined or treated as weak. This was a band that had heroic stature in the ancient world,” said Romm.Speaking of how people in the 19th century responded to the rediscovery of the history of this band, Romm added: “[They realised] my homosexuality does not make me a lesser being, and that was a huge revelation in the 19th century when homosexual rights were first being advanced.It was the Greeks that provided the inspiration for all kinds of people whose identities might otherwise be castigated or unrecognised by society,” Romm said, referring to both the reception of these ideas two centuries ago and how they are now endangered by MAGA.For his part, after likening the attack on academic freedom and on Plato to the way, in the run-up to the American Civil War, Southern states forbade the quoting of Biblical passages that questioned slavery, Robert Blaisdell, a professor at Kingsborough Community College (Brooklyn, New York), first drew attention to the works of Sophocles.When he teaches community (two-year) college students Sophocles’Oedipus the King, he said he wants his students, (half of whom were born outside the US and many of whom are the first in their family to go to a post-secondary institution) to grasp what John Ruskin called “the root” of both drama and morality.“Everyone who has ever written a play or a screenplay,” he tells his students, “has read these works or, at least, knows them and their shocking stories. These stories can shock us, despite the fact that we know what’s going to happen.“It’s a way to connect them to history, opening up history backwards. Look, there are these issues inAntigone. I want them to see there have been bullies in government there have been tyrants. Antigone has to deal with King Creon [who will not let her bury her dead brothers].“He was very powerful. His speech is all about getting behind the state. We see where it lands him. He destroys everything,” though, he adds, unlike Trump, Creon had “both a command of language” and a conscience.Plato’s dialogues provide Blaisdell with a unique way of teaching essay writing, using them to show how an essayist has to anticipate or imagine somebody listening to their writing or answering them.“That’s part of the shame of Texas A&M. What were their politicians thinking? What were the boards of regents thinking – that students are these passive receptacles? That they’re not responding to their readings? That they can’t handle ideas that have been around for thousands of years? These are important thinkers and writers; it shows their contempt for and their misunderstanding of education and their students.”In addition to learning to grapple with millennia-old philosophical questions: “What,” I asked Peterson, “do your students who are not in the humanities gain from studying Plato?”He began by saying that engineering students, for example, like his course because it is so different from the others in their programme. But soon he was making a major pedagogical argument about how the course helps them to structure their thoughts.“Philosophy and our methods can be very precise. We can really break up arguments into smaller parts and see how they will fit together. Engineers discover that philosophy isn’t as wishy-washy as they perhaps thought, and that is something they thank me for.”Epistemic humility vs arroganceThough concerned about the banning of Plato because of gender issues, Dr Deepak Sarma, professor of Indian religions and philosophy and the Inaugural Distinguished Scholar in the Public Humanities at Case Western Reserve University (Cleveland, Ohio), provided a slightly different analysis, distinguishing between “epistemic humility” and “epistemic arrogance”.Epistemic humility, Sarma said, is what you find in Plato when, for example, Socrates encourages his students to reflect on virtue and says that he doesn’t know what it is either.At the heart of the Western philosophic enterprise is that any claim to knowledge is open to questions that challenge presupposition. By contrast, Sarma said: “The Trump administration and their MAGA minions prefer epistemic arrogance, which lauds strength and supposed ‘certainty’.”It’s not a surprise that the banning of Plato happened in Texas, which has been a sort of ground zero for epistemic arrogance.“What’s interesting about Texas is that there have been lots of moves [there] that are towards epistemic certainty or to maintain or create authority of, for example,The Bibleover all other texts in the context of educational institutions,” Sarma toldUniversity World News, referring to the fact that Texas last year mandated that the Ten Commandments be displayed in every classroom and to allow “voluntary” Bible readings, though federal judges have blocked both in some school districts.It is not difficult, however, to imagine Texas’ epistemic certainty leading to the banning of a hymn like the “Nasadiya Sukta” from theRigveda, Book 10, which, presents an agnostic version of creation which, for the evangelicals in the Texas state government, would be anathema.The “He” in the final verse of the hymn is certainly not the ‘God’ who in the first line of Genesis “created Heaven and Earth”. (“He, the first origin of this creation, whether he formed it all or did not form it, whose eye controls this world in highest heaven, he verily knows it, or perhaps he knows not.”)Beneath the religious and epistemic orthodoxy, Sarma said, is “a kind of classic fascism, an attempt to indoctrinate. Indoctrination is not about questioning the foundations of doctrines; rather, it is merely accepting them on the authority of the fascist leaders' right to claim authority despite having none.”At one level, Sarma said it's like when you ask a small child what they believe and they say it's this or that “because Mommy says so”.“To prevent the infinite regress of epistemic uncertainty, you have claims that something is self-validating, that there is a kind of epistemic foundation of self-validating, as when the other day Trump said he was limited only by his own morality,” Sarma said, referencingwidely reported commentsby Trump describing the limits of his use of military power.“Yeah, there is one thing. My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.”While Indian philosophical texts do not reflect on gender because, as Sarma averred, “it’s a fairly patriarchal system, and for the most part women are not considered as being part of the conversation”, Sarma was not sanguine about Indian philosophical practice being immune to an attack similar to that against Plato.For, while Indian philosophical practice differs greatly from its Western cousin, the core of the practice, the rigorous interrogation of your presuppositions by others looking for internal flaws, bears some resemblance to, for example, Socrates’ constant questioning.“The core issue in Indian philosophy is the question of epistemology, what we call in the Indian philosophical realm,Pramatheory; theories about how we know, what you know and what constitutes proper knowledge,” said Sarma.“The act of doing Indian philosophy is being open to or embracing epistemic humility.”This is demonstrated inVigrahavyvartan, written by the second-century Indian Buddhist philosopher Ngrjuna, in which, Sarma said, “scepticism is lauded”, which is the antithesis of Trumpiansui generisclaims to morality – and thus, constitutes a possible point of contention with the sort of epistemic certainty seen in Texas.Sarma described, in practical terms, the distinction between universities under the aegis of epistemic certainty and what he explained was the heart of the Indian philosophical tradition by pointing to the rise of the view that higher education is little more than tertiary vocational training.“Vocational training is all about epistemic certainty” in both the training of individuals and engineering products and processes that are all measurable outputs and the capitalist system.What about race?Although the emphasis has been on Plato’sSymposiumand the TAMU’s claim that it furthers “gender ideology”, TAMU’s Bright cautioned that it should not be forgotten that the university has also objected to “race ideology”, even though it was not dealt with in this dialogue.“What’s hiding underneath is race,” he said. “They are pairing them together. They are using ‘gender theories’ as a front because that’s where the lightning rod is. But they are sliding in their race, which I think is going to have an even more devastating impact on our students.”In a sense, what Bright was saying is that the attack on Plato and gender was a “dry run” to assess the feasibility of banning a singularly important intellectual figure.“They are pairing the two together in order to destroy higher education,” he concluded.A brand new courseA final e-mail between Peterson and Sweet explains what he has inserted into the course to replace the deleted Platonic readings. (Peterson declined the option to be reassigned.) It reads in part: “To comply with the new censorship requirement, I have replaced that affected module with lectures on free speech and academic freedom.”“The text for the new module is: ‘Texas A&M, Under Curriculum Limits, Warns Professor Not to Teach Plato”,The New York Times, 9 January 2026.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 德克薩斯大學禁止教授在課程中閱讀柏拉圖的作品，引發學術界的廣泛批評。德克薩斯農工大學哲學教授馬丁·彼得森收到通知，要求他刪除涉及“種族和性別意識形態”的課程模組，這一決定被認為是對學術自由的嚴重侵犯。學者們質疑這一行為可能引發的後續影響，擔心更多經典文獻可能成為禁忌。批評者指出，該校的行動反映了當前教育體系中對言論自由的威脅，並可能對學生的學習造成長遠影響。這一事件凸顯了當代教育中對思想自由的迫切需求。
