https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: A virtuous cycle for academic and community engagement
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: A virtuous cycle for academic and community engagementGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsMutual exchange between universities and communities can create a virtuous cycle of innovation, learning and social change. Image generated by Sora.Join us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALBuilding global expert capacity for water sustainabilityGLOBALDutch water researcher links rivers, risks and global solutionsTHAILANDA revolution in learning helps to transform communitiesTHAILAND‘We need to go back to interdisciplinary higher education’Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredMEXICOA virtuous cycle for academic and community engagementMandy Garner13 January 2026TweetJust outside Paso del Norte in Chihuahua state in northern Mexico, across a river and over a hill, lies an informal community. Those who live there lack access to public transport, services, shops, pharmacies and employment opportunities. They feel isolated and not fully part of the city.The journey to the main city is dangerous and arduous, particularly for women. It involves a 60-metre climb and a pedestrian bridge where robbery is common. One woman comments that even the Uber drivers are afraid to go there.The local community, which numbers around 700 people, says transport is a key concern. They have been working with academics from Tec de Monterrey on a project to convert traditional bikes to electric bikes and improve transport links with the main city.This article is part of a series on ‘Changemakers: Higher education institutions for societal transformation and planetary health’, published byUniversity World Newsin partnership withABET.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.The project began back in the early COVID days of 2020 with a small research grant of US$5,000 for Tec’s School of Architecture, Art and Design to design and organise online activities for vulnerable communities. In 2022 they won a bigger grant to understand how urban design might reduce the digital divide that was growing larger during COVID.“In the most vulnerable communities children could not join classes and lost a couple of years of education. Technology is so important nowadays and we wanted to find out how to introduce it to vulnerable communities,” says Professor Emanuele Giorgi, director of research at the School of Architecture, Art and Design at Tec de Monterrey.The Design for Vulnerables project interprets technology in a broad way. Working in multidisciplinary teams, led by the School of Architecture, Art and Design, they brought the skills of engineers, medics and business people as well as climate change experts to bear in four communities in Chihuahua in northern Mexico, including Paso del Norte.They also partnered with international universities, including Columbia University’s School of Social Work and Tongji University in Shanghai.Giorgi says the main challenge of the project was getting people from different disciplines, ranging from the School of Engineering to the School of Humanities and Education, to work together.“Everyone spoke a different disciplinary language, brought a different perspective and understood vulnerability in a different way,” he says.But the benefits of multiple perspectives have been crucial and have brought new ideas. In 2023, the academics started to gather information from the different communities. In each community they conducted weekly semi-structured interviews and workshops for a year to develop the projects, focusing on their needs and limitations.Combining what they heard with scientific evidence, they mapped out six projects per community area. These included a project to introduce carbon-based water filters in an area where the water was contaminated with arsenic and fluoride and the tech and mobility project – involving bikes – in Paso del Norte.Understanding community needsThe bike project was designed with a professor of mechanical engineering and showcased the benefits of applied research as well as developing local skills and entrepreneurship.Anette Díaz Gómez is one of the architecture students working on the project. “It gave me the chance to get to know a community and understand how technology could be useful to them,” she says.“It was clear that we needed to be hands-on. Talking to vulnerable communities and taking the time to understand what they need and not just what you think they need was one of the key takeaways for me. It transformed my way of understanding architecture.”She adds that they could see that the solutions to problems facing the communities could not be dealt with by one discipline alone, that they were not hard to solve and that technology could play a vital role in addressing them.Anette has really enjoyed working in the community and seeing how they have embraced and shared what they have learnt. “It has broadened my world and my understanding of how architecture is not limited to bricks and mortar,” she says.After setting up the project, the academic team have been checking in regularly with the local community to document how they are using the project’s eight bikes and where they need help to modify the project, for instance, if the batteries run out. It has also allowed time for the community to take ownership of the project.Visible impact on mobilityVirginia Aceves is the leader of Accionética, an association which works with the community, acting as a bridge between the academics and community members. She says: “The people who have received the electric bikes have seen an improvement in their mobility without incurring transport costs. Some mothers even use the bikes to transport their young daughters.”The project has changed over time. Its initial goal was to create a bike-sharing system, but it became clear that was too complex because it needed special locks. The team worked with the community group and decided to give the bikes to specific families to share with their family members.“The bikes project has the most visible impact on the community of all our projects. You can see people out and about with their bikes,” says Giorgi.One local woman, Armida Rosas Pompa, says: “It has helped me a lot in terms of mobility and saving time.”Aceves states: “I hope to expand the project into a second phase by seeking additional funding to acquire more electric bicycles; to spread awareness of the benefits these bicycles have provided to families so similar initiatives can emerge; and eventually to set up a workshop to convert conventional bicycles into electric ones.“I am frequently asked whether more electric bicycles will be available for other families, especially for people who would use them primarily to commute to work.”One woman who has benefited from the scheme and has learnt how to convert traditional bikes to electric ones is keen to set up a workshop for women to help them get around more easily.Rethinking how architecture is taughtGiorgi adds that it was very important to involve students in the project and says it has changed the way architecture is taught at Tec de Monterrey. It has also brought international attention. This year the architecture team was selected to take part in the Venice Biennale for the first time following an open call.“It was the first biennale where architecture was not represented in the traditional way. This project represents an evolution in architecture. It’s not just about building spaces, but the way we live in those spaces,” he states.Giorgi hopes that their success can be an example to governments and philanthropic organisations looking to support similar projects and that these can raise vulnerable communities’ awareness of their rights and change mindsets.“It’s about regeneration more than sustainability. I hope it can broaden people’s horizons and force them to expect more from their governments and to realise there are solutions and that technology can play a role.”He adds: “These projects show that working with communities does work and that we have to try it. These things are doable and we have to trust communities to receive help and take ownership of them.”Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 科技與社區合作促進教育改革

墨西哥北部查胡瓦州的Paso del Norte社區，因缺乏交通和服務而感到孤立。當地居民與德克薩斯科技大學合作，開展將傳統自行車轉換為電動自行車的項目，以改善交通連結。該計劃從2020年開始，最初獲得5000美元的研究資助，並在2022年獲得更大獎助金，旨在減少因疫情擴大的數位差距。

參與的學生表示，這一實踐經驗不僅增強了他們對社區需求的理解，還改變了他們對建築學的看法。項目已顯著改善社區居民的流動性，並激發了社區對於科技和創新的新期待。教育者希望這能成為其他社區的典範，促進社會變革和可持續發展。

https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Tec de Monterrey: ‘SDGs are a cornerstone of our vision’
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Tec de Monterrey: ‘SDGs are a cornerstone of our vision’GlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksGLOBALBuilding global expert capacity for water sustainabilityGLOBALDutch water researcher links rivers, risks and global solutionsTHAILANDA revolution in learning helps to transform communitiesTHAILAND‘We need to go back to interdisciplinary higher education’Institutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNatural Language Processing and Speech - Open Rank FacultyMEXICOTec de Monterrey: ‘SDGs are a cornerstone of our vision’Mandy Garner13 January 2026TweetTecnológico de Monterrey, a private university, was established in 1943 close to Saddle Mountain in Monterrey, the capital of Nuevo León state in northeastern Mexico, but now has 20 campuses located across 20 states nationwide.These are supported by civil associations made up of numerous groups of outstanding leaders from all over the country who are committed to quality in higher education.Their board members meet every year to define the goals that will guide Tec in its efforts to drive the development of communities and the nation.Tec has placed the Sustainable Development Goals at the heart of its strategic plans and is currently updating its curriculum to ensure sustainability is integrated across all disciplines and becomes a design principle in all of its undergraduate programmes.This article is part of a series on ‘Changemakers: Higher education institutions for societal transformation and planetary health’, published byUniversity World Newsin partnership withABET.University World Newsis solely responsible for the editorial content.The university defines its general purpose as transforming lives and communities through education and by working with communities. The Sustainable Development Goals lie at the foundation of its three main pillars.“Tec focuses on excellence in education, applied research and social impact projects,” says Juan Pablo Murra Lascurain, rector at Tec de Monterrey, adding that sustainability is key toits 2030 Strategic Plan.The ‘Ruta Azul’ planTec’sRuta Azul,the university’s Sustainability and Climate Change Plan, was launched in 2021 and is interdisciplinary, presenting an integrated vision of sustainable development thinking for the university, from encouraging research in sustainability to changing the culture.The university’s approach to sustainability includes its award-winning Monterrey Innovation District, where researchers work with industry to address global challenges, its teaching, its carbon footprint, water usage and much more.The SDGs are “the cornerstone of our vision”, Lascurain says.That vision has seen the university climb the global rankings, particularly in relation to sustainability. In theTHEImpact Rankings 2025, the university shares the top spot in Latin America with three other universities, including the National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), with which it also ties for first place nationally.Since 2019, Tec has been moving towards making it obligatory for all undergraduate students to do at least one course related to the SDGs, with SDG 13 on climate change being the first focus. It has also implemented a core course on sustainable development for graduate students.For 2026 it is updating the curriculum to ensure sustainability is integrated across all disciplines and becomes a design principle in each of its 44 undergraduate programmes.It is also working on a competency assessment model addressing transversal competencies across the curriculum, including a “future thinking competency”. The aim is to incorporate at least one of the SDGs into every undergraduate curriculum from the outset and not as an add-on.It’s a complex operation because Tec is a complicated organisation. It brings together Tec Salud – a medical school which does applied research; Tec de Monterrey, with its focus on AI, health, talent and excellence; and Tecmilenio, which is centred on employability, flexibility, personalised education, remote models and outreach.Tec’s 20 campuses operate on different scales of student numbers, from 1,000 to 20,000 students. The university is also multi-level, ranging from high school students to those embarking on postdoctoral studies, and has over 90,000 students enrolled across its programmes.“That complexity creates challenges and opportunities when it comes to the way that we organise our sustainability efforts,” says Lascurain.Embedding the SDGsSo what is the framework for ensuring the SDGs penetrate every area of the university? Tec has a sustainability office headed by Paola Visconti, director of sustainable development and outreach. The office has a mandate to define the plan for embedding the SDGs and to coordinate the different teams responsible for different elements of the plan. Different team members are responsible for areas ranging from operations to research and cultural shift.“In the end it’s about changing hearts and minds,” says Lascurain. “It’s not just about tasks but about a long-term fundamental vision for individuals and institutions to leave a better world for our children and build a more sustainable model.”He adds that the cultural element of moving to a more sustainable future can be hard to measure. For that reason, the university has developed a sustainability culture index which assesses students’ and academics’ attitudes, behaviours, ideologies and knowledge about sustainability and climate change through a survey taken by students, faculty and those who work at Tec.The first survey took place in 2022/23. “We measure if we are shifting perceptions in a positive way when it comes to things such as personal responsibility,” says Lascurain.“We are all aware of the damage that is being done to the environment, but it is hard to translate that into what it means for each individual as well as for the collective. We are making progress, but it is slower than we would like.”Initially, the index was a centrally coordinated national survey, but now each campus has their own index so they can track how they are improving.Lascurain says initially there was a leadership team and committee to drive the launch of the sustainability plan, but since then bridges have been built between different areas of the university, and a more organic process of collaboration is emerging.“It’s now more pull than push,” says Lascurain.Mobilising studentsStudents – both undergraduate and high school – play an important part in the SDGs plan. The university has a student government group on each campus. Each group has its own sustainability committee, and there are student interest groups at the five high schools in Monterrey which are affiliated with the university.These address issues ranging from clean air to hunger, fairness and health. In Mexico students have to do community service as part of their course and have been matched with applied projects linked with the SDGs.When undergraduate students graduate, they are surveyed intermittently about what they are doing. The next survey of alumni will include a question on how they are incorporating the knowledge they have gained about sustainability in their lives.Community actionAt the heart of Tec de Monterrey’s sustainability work are local community groups. Around seven years ago, it helped launch Alliances for Climate Action, a key initiative promoted by Tec de Monterrey since 2019 as part of its participation in the global campaign Race to Zero for Universities and Colleges. The campaign aims to halve global emissions by 2030, and Tec’s participation acted as a catalyst forRuta Azul.Along with UNAM, Tec is host of the Mexican chapter of the Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN), a network that brings together academics from Tec and UNAM, other Mexican universities, industry, NGOs and government to work on initiatives to address challenges on sustainable development issues.Also, in collaboration with SDSN Mexico and GIZ Germany, Tec created an SDGs Platform for Local Action in 2023 to showcase local efforts for sustainable development, such as projects linked to the circular economy. Its Youth Research and Community Festivals also support its sustainability efforts.In addition, the university has createda Sustainable Development Initiatives Reportwhich, in its 2024 edition, identified more than 850 initiatives done in collaboration with local communities between 2022 and 2023 that have touched on at least one SDG.Most are focused on SDG 4 – ensuring inclusive and equitable quality education and promoting lifelong learning opportunities for all – and health, but there are initiatives that draw on all the SDGs.Tec has itself had a long tradition of increasing access to education across communities, with 55% of its undergraduate students receiving some form of financial support from the university. Remote learning has been part of Tec’s DNA for many decades. It has been using satellite-based education since the late 1980s and early 1990s. That was because it needed to teach a range of courses across its 20 campuses when it might not have the right faculty on every campus.“We were one of the first universities in the world to use satellite-based education,” says Lascurain.Most of its graduate programmes have a distance learning element, and it is looking to help Mexico reach a target of at least 200,000 new people online – many from marginalised communities – to boost economic development. Indeed, it is hosting the Insights for the Future of Education Conference on education innovation in January 2026.International linksWhile it is focused on the local, Tec also has international reach. It is the only Latin American institution at the European Consortium of Innovative Universities – an organisation committed to innovations in both teaching and learning – and at Universitas 21. It is also the only Mexican university to be enrolled in the Association of Pacific Rim Universities.Tec collaborates with several universities globally; for instance, it partners with two other Latin American universities, the University of Los Andes in Colombia andPontificia Universidad Católica de Chile, as part of “La Triada”. The collaboration was launched in 2018 and it has since worked on over 90 joint initiatives, many around sustainability, for instance, biodiversity, conservation and economic development.Tec also collaborated with other Latin American universities for COP30 in Brazil. As part of these efforts, Tec de Monterrey hosted the Higher Education for Climate Action Pavilion along with the University of Campinas and 14 other institutions to highlight the critical role of higher education in advancing climate action and sustainable development. This was in response to a request from the Pontifical Commission for Latin America under the last Pope.Lascurain says that Latin American countries share many common challenges and features. They are highly biodiverse; they have very diverse climates, ranging from deserts to jungles. They also tend to have very large cities and also a close connection with nature through their indigenous populations.And poverty is never far from their doors – Tec’s main campus is near the community of La Campana, which is characterised by low-income jobs, precarious working conditions and a lack of adequate infrastructure. That proximity brings with it a sense of responsibility, says Lascurain.Fully incorporating the SDGsOne of the main challenges when it comes to embedding the SDGs is ensuring all teaching staff are on board with the need to fully incorporate the SDGs, rather than just tack them on as a separate topic, despite them being overloaded with demands.“The challenge is how we make sure the faculty understand and believe in and can teach these issues as part of their own disciplines,” says Lascurain. “That means we need to do more to develop faculty. It all goes back to culture.”Guidelines have been developed to that end, and the university has been training lecturers and leaders in Education for Sustainable Development.Lascurain acknowledges that the difficult global economic outlook of the past couple of years has meant the percentage of time given to talking about sustainability generally has decreased, but that means, he says, that universities with a longer-term focus “have a responsibility to ensure it is part of the conversation”.Funding has been a perennial issue, particularly when it comes to research, with Mexico investing less than 0.4% of GDP as a country in research and development.“We are always fighting for scarce resources. That is a challenge,” says Lascurain, adding that Tec now places greater emphasis on applied research and has three interdisciplinary research institutes, including one on sustainable manufacturing.“We have to fight to make sustainability a priority amid so many short-term challenges, but we are determined to do so,” he says.Earlier instalments of our Changemaker series, including audio reports, are available below:IHE Delft – Engineering water for sustainable developmentThailand – Universities for transforming society and planetary healthThe UN Sustainable Development Solutions Network (SDSN) operates under the auspices of the UN Secretary-General, mobilising the world’s largest knowledge network to drive action on the Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) and the Paris Agreement on Climate Change. The SDSN works at the intersection of science, policy, and development practice, maximising SDG achievements across global, regional, national, and local levels. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Mental health issues a key cause of study delays – Survey
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Mental health issues a key cause of study delays – SurveyGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNETHERLANDSAmid housing shortage, students give up on finding a roomNETHERLANDSPsychological support is key to PhD students’ well-beingNETHERLANDSUnion concerned about student loneliness, depressionInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Assistant Professor Tenure TrackEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputer Vision - Associate/Full Professor TenuredHuman-Computer Interaction - Open Rank FacultyComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsNETHERLANDSMental health issues a key cause of study delays – SurveyLiz Newmark12 January 2026TweetStress, depression and other mental health issues are key factors which explain why 37% of students at Dutch universities and universities of applied sciences are falling behind in their studies, according to research by the Dutch National Student Association (ISO –Interstedelijk Studenten Overleg).The finding, contained ina reportreleased on 8 December 2025, followed an ISO survey completed by 7,542 students.The data showed illness, particularly psychological conditions such as stress and depression, was the most common reason for such delays, cited by 22% of respondents.Other factors included other emotional issues such as a lack of motivation (18%). Students facing delays were also more likely to report emotional exhaustion and lower resilience.Sarah Evink, ISO chair, called on the government to make mental health part of curriculums in higher education. She stressed how the report noted that “83% of students have anxiety and/or depressive feelings”, which she says can stem from performance pressure, with the Dutch system “very much focused on results”.She said universities can help by having good support systems, but these resources are not always available.Financial and academic pressureMeanwhile, Dutch students are being placed under increased stress because of financial and academic pressures, she said.Students receive a grant of €350 (US$408) a month, which is "not sufficient to get by”, especially as the average price of a room in Amsterdam is €900/month. Evink noted that the Dutch grant is lower than grants in Scandinavia and in Germany.These countries also have far lower tuition fees than in the Netherlands, where students will pay €2,694 for the 2026 and 2027 year, more than twice as much as in neighbouring Belgium, where it costs less than €1,000 to go to university.Evink said these factors mean many Dutch students must “work a lot as well as study”.She also criticised government guidance,Bindend Studie Advies(BSA), which prevents students from advancing if they do not earn enough credits in their first year, as piling on the stress.She recommended taking the “B’ [binding] out of the BSA”, arguing it is “a fake solution for preventing delays”, as 40% of students needing to quit a programme will go on to study the same kinds of subjects in other universities – but in the meantime, suffer pressure and unhappiness.Evink added thatEUROSTUDENT research datashow similar figures on student wellbeing across the European Union. Some 19% of students reported “low wellbeing” in this report.Four-pillar programmeThe Dutch Ministry of Education, Science and Culture (Ministerie van Onderwijs, Cultuur en Wetenschap, or OCW) toldUniversity World Newsit is taking steps to tackle the problem.A spokesperson said educational institutions receive €15 million a year to implement aNational Framework for Student Welfare(Landelijk Kader Studentenwelzijn, 2023-2030 – LKr), which aims to reduce student stress.It consists of four pillars: working on prevention (early recognition of symptoms); increasing a “sense of belonging”; strengthening knowledge and skills of educational professionals on student welfare; and improving collaboration at the local level (education, healthcare, municipality).Evink welcomed this initiative as “a great start to improving mental health”, adding that “indirectly, it might help prevent study delay”.In addition, the spokesperson said aStijnsupport programmefrom the Netherlands-based Trimbos Institute, which focuses on mental health and addiction problems, and is subsidised by the ministry, was established in 2023 to support educational institutions in work on student wellbeing.“Educational institutions offer various support options ... including financial aid through the student support fund or more practical or emotional support,” she added.However, the ministry spokesperson insisted that the BSA – “a valuable tool for closely monitoring study progress” – would not be abolished or even adjusted. At the same time, specific attention is being given to student dropout rates, with a special policy dedicated to tackling this and study delay.Improvement in student mental healthJolien Dopmeijer, project lead of the student wellbeing programme at the Trimbos Institute, is hopeful that initiatives on mental and emotional health are making a difference.Indeed, he said thelatest figures(2025) from the National Institute for Public Health and the Environment at the Ministry of Health, Welfare and Sport (the RIVM orRijksinstituut voor Volksgezondheid en Milieu) showed a slight improvement in student mental health compared to 2021 and 2023.He toldUniversity World Newsthat as well as having their own support systems, universities must collaborate with external partners, such as municipalities, healthcare services, and even housing organisations. They should also actively involve the students themselves in ways to boost mental health and create a safe learning environment.“Support should also include targeted help for students with ADHD, anxiety or burnout, such as study skills support, additional time or counselling,” Dopmeijer said. He added that a BSA course advancement block should not be issued automatically if study delay is due to mental health problems.Implementing the LKr programme should contribute to better student wellbeing and therefore prevent study delays but would not “completely eliminate” the problem, he said. External factors like “poor financial status and housing perspective” also play a role.A dual approachRuben Puylaert, spokesperson for Universities of the Netherlands (Universiteiten van Nederlandor UNL), the association representing the country's 14 research universities, agreed that mental health is associated with and influenced by multiple factors, such as personal characteristics, the social environment and study climate.“UNL recognises that multiple interactive pressures are increasing, such as the shortage in student housing and financial worries," he said, noting that universities cannot "solve" student wellbeing alone.Puylaert also emphasised that universities were committed to working on student wellbeing through a dual approach: prevention and a safe study climate and targeted support towards students requiring assistance, "including those with a disability, personal circumstances or neurodiversity”.He toldUniversity World Newsthat the BSA in its current form was beneficial for study success and should not be weakened or changed.“The BSA does not lead to more dropout in general but to earlier dropout ... preventing a bigger and more costly delay,” he argued. Moreover, most students are “either neutral or positive” to it.Student housing investmentMember institution Eindhoven’s University of Technology (TU/e) has taken steps to ease the high cost of living that creates a “heavy burden on students’ mental health” by building a high-rise “student village” with affordable housing, Rector Silvia Lenaerts toldUniversity World News: “Serious investments in student housing” are also expected in the Brainport region, 10 minutes” walk from the city centre, where TU/e is situated.TU/e university advocates a broad approach to accommodating students with mental health issues, she said, with a “clear and very accessible support landscape and matching individual solutions … In addition, a close-knit community where students can feel at home is essential, just like peer support”.This is why TU/e has “a more personalised approach with specific attention for neurodiverse students... and personalised learning paths”, Lenaerts said.At Breda’s University of Applied Sciences (BUas), Jan Willem Besselaar, director of marketing, communication and student community, noted social pressure, increased partly by social media, and the need for a stable situation, with accommodation and financial means, as key influencers of students' mental health.BUas’ support measures – informative, preventive and reactive – include housing aid, study support and social events, such as a student wellbeing-focused “GoodMood” week in November “encouraging students to get to know the city, experience culture and meet each other”, said Besselaar.There are also more than 30 BUas clubs, such as cooking, board games, acting and several sports, which “really contributes to a high sense of belonging”, he added.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.title: Ministry announces tighter regulation of HE institutions
https://www.universityworldnews.com/post.content: Ministry announces tighter regulation of HE institutionsGlobalHomeNewsCoronavirus CrisisWorld round-upCommentaryWorld BlogFeaturesTransformative LeadershipAcademic FreedomSpecial ReportsStudent ViewLettersSubscribe NewsletterAfricaHomeAfrica NewsCoronavirus AfricaAfrica CommentaryAfrica FeaturesAfrica Student ViewSubscribe NewsletterSpecial ReportsAsiaSDGsEdtech/AICommentaryInDepthGlobal Special ReportsAfrica Special ReportsTrump's Impact on HECoronavirus CrisisCoronavirus AfricaHE JobsPartnershipsCreative ServicesAdvertisingSearchAdvanced SearchArticles by CountryGlobal Newsletter ArchivesAfrica Newsletter ArchivesAbout UsAbout UWNSubscribe NewsletterAdvertisingSponsorshipTerms & ConditionsPrivacy PolicyCookie PolicyContact UsImage: iStockJoin us onFacebookFollow us onTwitterRelated LinksNEPAL-UAEGovernment acts on bogus UAE colleges after complaintsUAEEnrolment in private higher education sees record increaseInstitutional ProfilesFeatured HE JobsComputer Science - Open Rank Faculty PositionsComputational Biology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsPersonalized Medicine - Open Rank Faculty PositionsStatistics and Data Science - Associate/Full Professor TenuredArtificial Intelligence in Action - Open Rank Faculty PositionsEpidemiology - Open Rank Faculty PositionsMachine Learning - Open Rank FacultyComputer Vision - Assistant Professor TenureTrackRobotics - Open Rank Faculty PositionsUAEMinistry announces tighter regulation of HE institutionsWagdy Sawahel12 January 2026TweetSweeping new quality control measures that will clamp down on higher education institutions operating without adequate regulation, including those in so-called ‘free zones’, in the United Arab Emirates (UAE), have been welcomed by sector stakeholders who say they will help to safeguard the country’s international reputation and protect student interests.The quality control regulations were included in a newfederal decree lawon higher education and scientific research governance, issued under the authority of the federal government on 31 December and overseen by the Ministry of Higher Education and Scientific Research (MoHESR).The newlawwill introduce a unified legal framework for higher education institutions across the UAE, including those operating in free zones, updating a2021 lawon higher education which only applied to public educational institutions, with the exception of those operating in the free zones. The new law now applies to public and private institutions.Under the new law, institutions cannot be established and programmes cannot be promoted without the provision of financial guarantees.The new law includes special regulatory provisions for higher education institutions operating in free zones, where a number of international branch campuses are located, including Dubai Knowledge Park and Dubai International Academic City. Previously, the2021 federal law on higher educationdid not apply to free zones.All institutions in free zones will now be required to obtain local authorisation as well as federal licensure and programme accreditation, with clear coordination mechanisms between local authorities and the ministry to implement institutional control and inspection procedures.The new law also supports the system for recognising academic and professional qualifications inside and outside the country, which contributes to ensuring quality, protecting the academic and professional path of students, and enhancing societal confidence in the national higher education system.The new law also imposes penalties of imprisonment and a fine of up to AED10 million (US$2.7million) or one of these two penalties on anyone who establishes and operates a higher education institution or provides programmes and services in the country for the purpose of granting a qualification or promoting or advertising its services before obtaining programme and institutional accreditation from the ministry.The same penalties also apply in the event of refusing to provide the data or information requested by the MoHESR or providing incorrect data or information.Reports of violationsThe UAE has recently experienced issues with unaccredited educational institutions, which are often training centres operating from small offices that falsely market themselves as degree-granting universities with extensive facilities. These entities typically operate from free zones, where business registration is easier but academic oversight limited.For example, in November 2025University World Newscarried an article about complaints from Nepali students about colleges and universities in the UAE that appeared to be accepting enrolments for degrees they were not qualified to deliver.The following month, on 8 December, the MoHESRrevokedrecognition of Midocean University after inspections revealed major violations, including unaccredited programmes, flawed online courses, record discrepancies and non-compliance with national laws.According to the university’s website, it is “accredited and licensed by the Ministry of Education in the United Republic of the Comoros”.According to the UAE’s Commission for Academic Accreditation (CAA) website, there are 103 active higher education institutions in the UAE. At 39, the UAE has the second-highest number of international campuses in the world after China (with 50), according to the November 2025 Cross-Border Education Research Team (C-BERT) website, which tracks trends and developments in cross-border higher education.Key issues of the lawThe new law focuses on regulating the higher education sector, strengthening governance and effective management of education institutions and aligning educational outcomes with labour-market requirements.It requires all higher education institutions to operate within national standards for licensing, classification, programme accreditation, and quality assurance, overseen by the MoHESR. It applies to all forms of education, including traditional, online, and blended learning models.The law sets out clear rules for licensing higher education institutions, which include the provision of financial guarantees, fulfilment of local permits, and compliance with health, safety, and security standards.It regulates the validity and renewal of licences, additional grace periods, and the measures resulting from non-compliance. It also defines the controls governing the transfer or cancellation of licences, while ensuring appropriate measures to safeguard the status of enrolled students.The law also introduces periodic classification and evaluation of higher education institutions and publishes classification results, enhancing transparency and strengthening competitiveness.Greater coherenceProfessor Abhilasha Singh, dean of the College of Business Administration at American University in the Emirates and formerly its provost/vice-president of academic affairs, toldUniversity World News: “Having spent nearly two decades in UAE higher education, I view this law as a timely and necessary reform that marks the sector’s transition from rapid growth to sustained excellence.”“It brings much-needed coherence to governance and quality assurance across all providers, while reinforcing alignment with national priorities, workforce readiness, and global academic standards,” she said.“I also think that the law presents a rare opportunity to recalibrate the higher education system towards impact, relevance, and trust.“Its success will lie not only in regulation but in thoughtful implementation that values institutional maturity, encourages innovation, and supports a culture of continuous improvement rather than procedural compliance only.”Removal of grey areasDr Lokman Meho, a professor and university librarian at the American University of Beirut and developer of the Research Integrity Risk Index that assesses universities’ exposure to research integrity risks, toldUniversity World Newsthe new law is a “landmark shift towards centralised oversight".“Its primary significance lies in the total removal of ‘regulatory grey areas’ for institutions in free zones, which must now obtain federal licensure and programme accreditation from the MoHESR,” Meho said.“By implementing a risk-based framework and mandatory public classification, the law directly addresses the fragmentation that previously allowed unaccredited providers to operate, thereby safeguarding the UAE's international academic reputation and protecting student rights,” he added.Meho said in terms of governance the law introduces “teeth” to quality assurance through severe financial penalties and imprisonment for non-compliance.“This transition is essential for the UAE to move from a regional education market to a credible, transparent, and globally competitive education hub,” said Meho, who is the author of a November 2025study, “Gaming the metrics: Bibliometric anomalies in global university rankings and the research integrity risk index (RI2)”, which suggested that some UAE higher education institutions have exhibited patterns pointing to research output inflation and metric-driven publication and citation strategies aimed at boosting ranking positions.For example, the University of Sharjah increased its research output by 268%, compared with a national average of 169%.EnforcementMeho said that while the law formally establishes a national framework for scientific research, its success will depend on how aggressively the standards are enforced.“For example, while the decree links licensing to research and scientific activity, there is still room for more explicit mandates regarding research impact and innovation output.“Without rigorous, research-linked key performance indicators in the upcoming executive regulations, there remains a risk that many institutions may prioritise a low-cost ‘teaching-only’ model.“To fulfil the UAE's long-term human capital goals, the regulatory framework must ensure these institutions do not just deliver degrees but actively contribute to the national innovation pipeline,” Meho said.Tim Rottleb, a postdoctoral fellow at Brandenburg University of Technology Cottbus-Senftenberg in Germany, toldUniversity World Newsthat the new law regulating higher education marks another step in the UAE government’s aim for the country to become a global hub for education and research.“In my view, it makes sense to have a coherent national legal framework that streamlines the higher education landscape across the various federal emirates, and particularly seeks to ensure quality and relevance of programmes,” said Rottleb, who is the author of a 2024 study, “Offshore campus development and the worlding of Dubai as an international education hub”.Protection for studentsRottleb said if successful, the initiative will mean students will be less prone to becoming victims of predatory and dubious higher education providers.However, he said it was unclear what the new law will mean for the myriad international branch campuses in the country, particularly for those located in Dubai’s and Ras al-Khaimah’s transnational education zones.This would need to be clarified in the upcoming executive regulations governing the implementation of the new law, said Rottleb.“For many of these universities, it is key to their business model to offer the programmes of their home university, unchanged by local standards, and the zones enable exactly that,” Rottleb noted.“The new law might jeopardise this business model. Additional reporting requirements might generate additional costs for the universities.“More importantly, if the new law requires them to adapt their programmes to an Emirati framework, they can no longer claim that a degree from their branch campus is exactly the same as one from their main campus. This would challenge the very core of the selling promise of their service,” Rottleb said.Receive email updates from UWNGlobal newslettersAfrica newslettersOther(other includes related events and webinars)Data will be processed according to our standardterms & conditionsand ourPrivacy Policy.Sponsored ArticleMost underwater robots record the sea. 阿聯酋高等教育機構新法案加強監管 阿聯酋高等教育和科研部（MoHESR）於12月31日發布的新法律，加強了對高等教育機構的規範，特別是自由區內的學校。新法要求所有高等教育機構獲得地方授權及聯邦認證，並提供財務擔保，旨在提升教育質量及保護學生權益。這項法規還對未獲得認證的機構施加高達1000萬阿聯酋迪拉姆的罰款或監禁，旨在消除過去的監管灰色地帶。專家表示，這標誌著阿聯酋高等教育向可持續卓越邁進的重要轉變，並強調了與國際標準對接的必要性。

阿聯酋高等教育機構新法案加強監管 阿聯酋高等教育和科研部（MoHESR）於12月31日發布的新法律，加強了對高等教育機構的規範，特別是自由區內的學校。新法要求所有高等教育機構獲得地方授權及聯邦認證，並提供財務擔保，旨在提升教育質量及保護學生權益。這項法規還對未獲得認證的機構施加高達1000萬阿聯酋迪拉姆的罰款或監禁，旨在消除過去的監管灰色地帶。專家表示，這標誌著阿聯酋高等教育向可持續卓越邁進的重要轉變，並強調了與國際標準對接的必要性。 
```
